topics.title: Financial Data and Their Properties
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET742 - FINANCIAL TIME SERIES ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Financial Data and Their Properties

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Financial Time Series Data

A **Financial Time Series** is a chronologically ordered sequence of observations of a financial variable — such as asset prices, trading volumes, interest rates, exchange rates, or derived quantities like returns and volatility — recorded at successive, equally spaced (or unequally spaced) time instants.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:**
> *Financial time series data are observations on a variable indexed by time, where the variable represents a financial quantity (price, return, rate, volume). The primary object of study is the statistical behaviour of asset returns rather than the price level itself, because returns possess more attractive statistical properties (stationarity, scale-free comparability).*

Let $X_t$ denote the observation of a financial variable at time $t$, where $t \in T = \{0, 1, 2, \ldots, T\}$. Then the set

$$
\mathcal{X} = \{X_t : t \in T\}
$$

constitutes a **financial time series**.

---

## 1.2 Types of Financial Variables (Classification)

| Class | Examples | Typical Sampling Frequency |
|---|---|---|
| **Prices / Rates** | Stock close, Bond yield, FX spot | Tick, 1-min, Daily, Weekly |
| **Returns** | Simple return $R_t$, Log-return $r_t$ | Daily, Weekly, Monthly |
| **Volume / Liquidity** | Shares traded, Bid-ask spread | Intraday |
| **Volatility proxies** | Realised variance, Implied vol | Daily |
| **Macro-financial** | CPI, Repo rate, GDP | Monthly, Quarterly |

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Intuition for First-Time Learners

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy: A Heart-Rate Monitor vs. a Thermometer.**
>
> Imagine you are a cardiologist monitoring two patients:
> - **Patient A** — you record raw **body temperature** every hour. The readings drift slowly (e.g., 36.5 °C, 37.1 °C, 38.0 °C) and clearly trend. This is analogous to an **asset price series**: non-stationary, trending, growing over time.
> - **Patient B** — you record the **change** in heart rate from one reading to the next (+2, −1, +5, −3 bpm). These numbers fluctuate around zero, do not trend, and have stable statistical behaviour. This is analogous to an **asset return series**: stationary, mean-reverting around zero, statistically tractable.
>
> *Key Insight:* In financial time series analysis, we **always convert prices into returns** because returns are (approximately) stationary and comparable across assets of different price scales. A ₹10 stock and a ₹5,000 stock cannot be compared using prices, but their **percentage returns are directly comparable**.

---

## 1.4 Why Returns, Not Prices?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Three engineering-statistical reasons motivate using **returns** as the primary unit of analysis:
> 1. **Stationarity** — Returns fluctuate around a constant mean, while prices typically contain a stochastic trend.
> 2. **Scale comparability** — Returns are dimensionless (percentages), allowing cross-asset and cross-market comparison.
> 3. **Attractive statistical properties** — Returns satisfy the martingale difference property under the Efficient Market Hypothesis (EMH).

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Asset Returns — The Two Fundamental Definitions

### 2.1.1 Simple (One-Period / Discrete) Return

Let $P_{t}$ be the price of an asset at time $t$ and $P_{t-1}$ the price at time $t-1$. The **simple (arithmetic) return** between $t-1$ and $t$ is:

$$
R_t \;=\; \frac{P_t - P_{t-1}}{P_{t-1}} \;=\; \frac{P_t}{P_{t-1}} - 1
$$

If a dividend $D_t$ is paid during the holding period, the dividend-adjusted simple return is:

$$
R_t \;=\; \frac{P_t + D_t}{P_{t-1}} - 1
$$

The **gross return** (multiplicative factor) is $1 + R_t = P_t / P_{t-1}$.

### 2.1.2 Logarithmic (Continuously Compounded) Return

$$
r_t \;=\; \ln\!\left(\frac{P_t}{P_{t-1}}\right) \;=\; \ln(P_t) - \ln(P_{t-1})
$$

The relationship between the two:

$$
r_t \;=\; \ln(1 + R_t) \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad R_t \;=\; e^{r_t} - 1
$$

---

## 2.2 Multi-Period Returns (Aggregation)

### 2.2.1 Simple returns are *multiplicative*

$$
1 + R_t[k] \;=\; \prod_{j=0}^{k-1}\bigl(1 + R_{t-j}\bigr)
$$

The $k$-period simple return is therefore:

$$
R_t[k] \;=\; \left[\prod_{j=0}^{k-1}\bigl(1 + R_{t-j}\bigr)\right] - 1
$$

### 2.2.2 Log returns are *additive*

$$
r_t[k] \;=\; \sum_{j=0}^{k-1} r_{t-j} \;=\; \ln\!\left(\frac{P_t}{P_{t-k}}\right)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This additivity of log returns is the principal mathematical reason they are preferred in econometric modelling — log-returns turn products into sums, which greatly simplifies volatility aggregation and statistical inference.

---

## 2.3 Statistical Properties of Financial Returns

A return series $\{R_t\}$ is characterised by its **moments**:

$$
\mu = E(R_t), \qquad \sigma^{2} = \mathrm{Var}(R_t) = E\!\left[(R_t-\mu)^2\right]
$$

$$
\gamma_1 \;=\; \frac{E\!\left[(R_t-\mu)^3\right]}{\sigma^{3}} \quad\text{(skewness)}
$$

$$
\gamma_2 \;=\; \frac{E\!\left[(R_t-\mu)^4\right]}{\sigma^{4}} \quad\text{(kurtosis)}
$$

For a **Normal distribution**, $\gamma_1 = 0$ and $\gamma_2 = 3$. The quantity $\gamma_2 - 3$ is called **excess kurtosis**.

---

## 2.4 The Stylized Facts of Asset Returns (KTU High-Yield Block)

Empirically observed universal features of asset returns (Cont, 2001):

1. **Heavy tails / excess kurtosis** — return distributions have fatter tails than Gaussian; $\gamma_2 \gg 3$.
2. **Negative skewness** — large negative returns are slightly more probable than large positive ones ($\gamma_1 < 0$).
3. **Volatility clustering** — large price changes (of either sign) tend to be followed by large changes; small changes follow small changes.
4. **Leverage effect** — volatility tends to rise after negative returns more than after positive returns of equal magnitude.
5. **Absence of autocorrelation in returns** — $R_t$ themselves are nearly serially uncorrelated.
6. **Long memory in squared / absolute returns** — $|R_t|$ and $R_t^{2}$ exhibit slowly decaying autocorrelation.
7. **Mean reversion** — some return series (e.g., currency pairs) display long-horizon mean reversion.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Board Exam Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulae — they appear in nearly every KTU Module-1 question.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Range / Sign |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Simple return | $R_t = (P_t - P_{t-1}) / P_{t-1}$ | $R_t \in (-1, +\infty)$ |
| 2 | Gross return | $1 + R_t = P_t / P_{t-1}$ | $\in (0, +\infty)$ |
| 3 | Log return | $r_t = \ln(P_t / P_{t-1}) = \ln P_t - \ln P_{t-1}$ | $r_t \in (-\infty, +\infty)$ |
| 4 | Log ↔ Simple link | $r_t = \ln(1 + R_t)$ ; $R_t = e^{r_t} - 1$ | — |
| 5 | Dividend-adjusted | $R_t = (P_t + D_t) / P_{t-1} - 1$ | $R_t \in (-1, +\infty)$ |
| 6 | $k$-period simple | $1 + R_t[k] = \prod_{j=0}^{k-1}(1 + R_{t-j})$ | multiplicative |
| 7 | $k$-period log | $r_t[k] = \sum_{j=0}^{k-1} r_{t-j}$ | additive |
| 8 | Mean | $\mu = E(R_t)$ | drift |
| 9 | Variance | $\sigma^{2} = \mathrm{Var}(R_t)$ | scale$^2$ |
| 10 | Skewness | $\gamma_1 = E[(R_t-\mu)^3] / \sigma^{3}$ | sign of asymmetry |
| 11 | Kurtosis | $\gamma_2 = E[(R_t-\mu)^4] / \sigma^{4}$ | tail weight |
| 12 | Excess kurtosis | $\kappa = \gamma_2 - 3$ | $\kappa = 0$ for Normal |
| 13 | Autocorrelation | $\rho(\ell) = \mathrm{Corr}(R_t, R_{t-\ell})$ | $\approx 0$ for $\ell \ge 1$ |
| 14 | Sample mean | $\hat{\mu} = \frac{1}{T}\sum_{t=1}^{T} R_t$ | unbiased estimator |
| 15 | Sample variance | $\hat{\sigma}^{2} = \frac{1}{T-1}\sum_{t=1}^{T}(R_t-\hat{\mu})^{2}$ | unbiased estimator |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The framework above is the backbone of:
- **Algorithmic / Quantitative trading** (strategy backtesting uses log-returns).
- **Risk management** — Value-at-Risk (VaR) and Expected Shortfall rely on the variance and tail moments of returns.
- **Derivative pricing** — the Black-Scholes formula uses the *log-return's* variance (volatility $\sigma$) as the sole input.
- **Portfolio construction** — Markowitz mean-variance optimisation requires $\mu$ and $\sigma^{2}$ as inputs.
- **Stress testing in banks** — leverage-effect and fat-tail modelling drive capital reserve requirements under Basel III.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Simple Return to Log-Return

**Starting point:** Define the gross return (price ratio) as

$$
G_t \;=\; \frac{P_t}{P_{t-1}} \;=\; 1 + R_t
$$

**Step 1 — Take the natural logarithm of both sides:**

$$
\ln(G_t) \;=\; \ln(1 + R_t)
$$

**Step 2 — Recognise the left-hand side as the log-return:**

$$
r_t \;=\; \ln\!\left(\frac{P_t}{P_{t-1}}\right) \;=\; \ln(P_t) - \ln(P_{t-1})
$$

**Step 3 — Combine Steps 1 and 2 to obtain the key identity:**

$$
r_t \;=\; \ln(1 + R_t) \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad 1 + R_t \;=\; e^{r_t}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the simple return:**

$$
R_t \;=\; e^{r_t} - 1
$$

**Step 5 — Taylor-expand $e^{r_t} - 1$ around $r_t = 0$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
e^{r_t} - 1 \;=\; & \left(1 + r_t + \frac{r_t^{2}}{2!} + \frac{r_t^{3}}{3!} + \cdots\right) - 1 \\[4pt]
=\; & r_t + \frac{r_t^{2}}{2} + \frac{r_t^{3}}{6} + \cdots
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Interpret:** For small daily returns ($|r_t| \ll 1$, typically $< 0.05$), the higher-order terms are negligible, so

$$
R_t \;\approx\; r_t \quad\text{(when }|r_t| \text{ is small)}.
$$

This is why, for daily data, **simple and log returns are numerically almost identical**. The difference grows for monthly / annual returns.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Additivity of Multi-Period Log-Returns

We want $r_t[k]$, the log-return accumulated over $k$ consecutive periods, expressed as a sum.

**Step 1 — Write the log-return for each sub-period:**

$$
r_{t} = \ln\!\left(\frac{P_t}{P_{t-1}}\right),\quad
r_{t-1} = \ln\!\left(\frac{P_{t-1}}{P_{t-2}}\right),\quad\ldots,\quad
r_{t-k+1} = \ln\!\left(\frac{P_{t-k+1}}{P_{t-k}}\right)
$$

**Step 2 — Add them all:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{j=0}^{k-1} r_{t-j} \;=\; & \ln\!\left(\frac{P_t}{P_{t-1}}\right) + \ln\!\left(\frac{P_{t-1}}{P_{t-2}}\right) + \cdots + \ln\!\left(\frac{P_{t-k+1}}{P_{t-k}}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Use the logarithm rule $\ln(a) + \ln(b) = \ln(ab)$ (telescoping product):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{j=0}^{k-1} r_{t-j} \;=\; & \ln\!\left(\frac{P_t}{P_{t-1}} \cdot \frac{P_{t-1}}{P_{t-2}} \cdots \frac{P_{t-k+1}}{P_{t-k}}\right) \\[4pt]
=\; & \ln\!\left(\frac{P_t}{P_{t-k}}\right) \;=\; r_t[k]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:**

$$
\boxed{\; r_t[k] \;=\; \sum_{j=0}^{k-1} r_{t-j} \;}
$$

Compare with the **multiplicative** form for simple returns:

$$
1 + R_t[k] \;=\; \prod_{j=0}^{k-1}\bigl(1 + R_{t-j}\bigr)
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Multi-Period Variance from Daily Variance (i.i.d. Assumption)

Assume $R_t$ are i.i.d. with variance $\sigma^{2}$. Then for the $k$-period simple return:

**Step 1 — Log-return variance scales linearly** (because log-returns are additive):

$$
\mathrm{Var}\!\bigl(r_t[k]\bigr) \;=\; \sum_{j=0}^{k-1}\mathrm{Var}(r_{t-j}) \;=\; k\,\sigma^{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Therefore the standard deviation scales as $\sqrt{k}$:**

$$
\mathrm{SD}\!\bigl(r_t[k]\bigr) \;=\; \sqrt{k}\,\sigma
$$

**Step 3 — Practical example:** Daily volatility $\sigma_{\text{daily}} = 0.01$ (1 %) implies annualised volatility

$$
\sigma_{\text{annual}} \;=\; \sqrt{252}\times 0.01 \;\approx\; 0.1587 \;\;\text{i.e., } \mathbf{15.87\%}
$$

The number **252** is the standard KTU / industry convention for trading days per year.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note:** Variance scales with time $k$ for log-returns but **not** for simple returns; this is yet another engineering reason to prefer log-returns in continuous-time financial modelling.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Simple vs. Log Returns

| Property | Simple Return $R_t$ | Log Return $r_t$ |
|---|---|---|
| Domain | $(-1, +\infty)$ | $(-\infty, +\infty)$ |
| Symmetry under sign change | **No** — losing 50 % requires +100 % to recover | **Yes** — symmetric |
| Multi-period aggregation | Multiplicative | Additive (sum) |
| Approximation for small $r$ | $R_t \approx r_t$ | $r_t \approx R_t$ |
| Used in | Portfolio accounting | Continuous-time / econometric models |
| Effect on volatility | Asymmetric | Homogeneous / time-additive |
| KTU Exam Preference | Definition & examples | Aggregation derivations |

> [!NOTE]
> **Symmetry check:** Let $P_{t-1} = 100$, $P_t = 50$ (loss of 50 %). Then $R_t = -0.50$. To recover, the price must rise to 150. The simple return from 50 → 150 is $+2.00$. A loss of $-0.50$ is *not* offset by a gain of $+0.50$. Using log-returns, $r_t = \ln(0.5) = -0.6931$ and $r_{t+1} = \ln(3) = +1.0986$; their sum $0.4055$ corresponds to $P_{t+1}/P_{t-1} = 1.5$. The asymmetry is captured cleanly.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (Board-Style)

**Problem:** A stock price moves as follows over four days:

| Day | $P_t$ |
|---|---|
| 0 | ₹100 |
| 1 | ₹105 |
| 2 | ₹102 |
| 3 | ₹108 |
| 4 | ₹110 |

A dividend of ₹2 per share is paid on Day 4.

**Task:** Compute the 4-day simple return and 4-day log return.

**Solution — Simple daily returns (dividend ignored for Day 1–3, included on Day 4):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_1 \;=\; (105 - 100)/100 \;=\; 0.0500 \\[3pt]
R_2 \;=\; (102 - 105)/105 \;=\; -0.02857 \\[3pt]
R_3 \;=\; (108 - 102)/102 \;=\; 0.05882 \\[3pt]
R_4 \;=\; (110 + 2 - 108)/108 \;=\; 0.03704
\end{aligned}
$$

**Aggregate simple return (multiplicative):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
1 + R[4] \;=\; & (1.05)(0.97143)(1.05882)(1.03704) \\[3pt]
=\; & 1.12 \;\Rightarrow\; R[4] \;=\; 0.12 \;(\mathbf{12\%})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Aggregate log-return (additive):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
r_1 \;=\; \ln(1.05) \;=\; 0.04879 \\[3pt]
r_2 \;=\; \ln(0.97143) \;=\; -0.02899 \\[3pt]
r_3 \;=\; \ln(1.05882) \;=\; 0.05716 \\[3pt]
r_4 \;=\; \ln(1.03704) \;=\; 0.03637
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
r[4] \;=\; 0.04879 - 0.02899 + 0.05716 + 0.03637 \;=\; 0.11333
$$

**Cross-check:** $e^{0.11333} - 1 = 0.12$ ✓ (matches the multiplicative result to rounding).

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchical Map of Financial Data and Their Properties

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Financial Time Series Data Xt"] --> B["Raw Variable"]
    A --> C["Derived Quantity"]

    B --> B1["Asset Price Pt"]
    B --> B2["Trading Volume Vt"]
    B --> B3["Interest / FX Rate it"]

    C --> C1["Simple Return Rt"]
    C --> C2["Log Return rt"]
    C --> C3["Squared / Absolute Return R2t, vertRtvert"]
    C --> C4["Realised Variance RVt"]

    B1 --> D["Transformation ln"]
    D --> C2
    B1 --> E["First Difference of ln"]
    E --> C2

    C1 --> F["Statistical Properties"]
    C2 --> F
    C3 --> F
    C4 --> F

    F --> F1["Stylized Fact 1: Heavy Tails"]
    F --> F2["Stylized Fact 2: Negative Skew"]
    F --> F3["Stylized Fact 3: Vol Clustering"]
    F --> F4["Stylized Fact 4: Leverage Effect"]
    F --> F5["Stylized Fact 5: Zero Autocorr in Rt"]
    F --> F6["Stylized Fact 6: Long Memory in vertRtvert"]
```

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — From Data to Inference

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["Step 1: Collect Price Series Pt"] --> S2["Step 2: Compute Returns rt = ln Pt minus ln Pt-1"]
    S2 --> S3["Step 3: Compute Sample Moments"]
    S3 --> S3a["mu hat: Mean"]
    S3 --> S3b["sigma squared hat: Variance"]
    S3 --> S3c["gamma1 hat: Skewness"]
    S3 --> S3d["gamma2 hat: Kurtosis"]

    S3a --> T["Step 4: Test Stylized Facts"]
    S3b --> T
    S3c --> T
    S3d --> T

    T --> U1["Jarque Bera Test for Normality"]
    T --> U2["Ljung Box Test for Autocorrelation"]
    T --> U3["ARCH LM Test for Vol Clustering"]

    U1 --> V["Step 5: Select Model"]
    U2 --> V
    U3 --> V

    V --> W1["ARIMA if mean dynamics dominate"]
    V --> W2["GARCH if variance dynamics dominate"]
    V --> W3["ARMA GARCH joint modelling"]
```

---

## 4.3 Conceptual Distinction: Simple vs Log Returns — Visual Block

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P["Asset Price Pt at time t minus 1"] --> PR["Price Ratio Pt / Pt minus 1"]
    PR --> R["Simple Return R = Ratio minus 1"]
    PR --> L["Log Return r = ln Ratio"]
    R --> M["Multiplicative Aggregation"]
    L --> N["Additive Aggregation"]
    M --> O["Used in Accounting / P and L"]
    N --> P2["Used in Continuous Time Models"]
    P2 --> P3["Black Scholes / GBM / Itô Calculus"]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define simple return and logarithmic return. State the mathematical relationship between them.**

**Model Answer:**
The **simple (arithmetic) return** of an asset between times $t-1$ and $t$ is the proportional change in price:

$$
R_t \;=\; \frac{P_t - P_{t-1}}{P_{t-1}}
$$

The **logarithmic (continuously compounded) return** is the natural logarithm of the price ratio:

$$
r_t \;=\; \ln\!\left(\frac{P_t}{P_{t-1}}\right) \;=\; \ln P_t - \ln P_{t-1}
$$

The relationship between them is:

$$
r_t \;=\; \ln(1 + R_t) \quad\text{or equivalently}\quad R_t \;=\; e^{r_t} - 1
$$

For small returns ($|r_t| < 0.05$), the two are approximately equal.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024]`
**List any six stylized facts of financial asset returns.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Heavy-tailed distribution** — kurtosis $\gamma_2 \gg 3$ (much heavier than Gaussian).
2. **Negative skewness** — $\gamma_1 < 0$; large losses more frequent than equally large gains.
3. **Volatility clustering** — large changes (of either sign) tend to cluster in time.
4. **Leverage effect** — volatility increases more following negative returns than following positive returns of equal magnitude.
5. **Near-zero autocorrelation in returns** themselves.
6. **Slowly decaying autocorrelation** in absolute or squared returns (long memory in volatility).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 Scheme, every Module-1 question in Part B carries **14 marks** and may offer an internal choice between two full-length alternatives. Both alternatives are given below.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a) Explain the various types of financial data used in time series analysis, with examples of each. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Financial data can be classified along two axes — *the variable measured* and *the sampling frequency*.

**A. By variable type:**

1. **Price data** — open, high, low, close (OHLC) of equities, bonds, commodities, currencies. Example: daily close of NIFTY-50.
2. **Return data** — derived from prices; the central object of study. Example: daily log-returns of RELIANCE stock.
3. **Volume / liquidity data** — number of shares traded, bid-ask spread, market depth. Example: BSE turnover figures.
4. **Volatility data** — implied volatility (from options market) and realised volatility (computed from intraday prices). Example: India VIX.
5. **Interest-rate / yield-curve data** — overnight rate, 10-year G-sec yield, term spreads.
6. **Macroeconomic-financial data** — CPI, repo rate, current-account deficit.

**B. By sampling frequency:**

| Frequency | Typical Use |
|---|---|
| **Tick** (transaction-by-transaction) | High-frequency trading, market microstructure |
| **Intraday** (1-min, 5-min, 15-min) | Realised variance estimation |
| **Daily** | Most econometric modelling (GARCH, ARMA) |
| **Weekly / Monthly** | Long-horizon asset allocation |
| **Quarterly / Annual** | Macroeconomic forecasting |

> Each frequency choice trades off **information content** against **noise and computational cost**.

---

**(b) Derive the relationship between simple return and log return. Discuss when log returns are preferred in practice. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation:** Starting from the gross return $G_t = 1 + R_t = P_t / P_{t-1}$, take natural logarithms:

$$
\ln(G_t) \;=\; \ln(1 + R_t)
$$

Recognising the left side as the log-return,

$$
\boxed{\, r_t \;=\; \ln(1 + R_t) \;\Longleftrightarrow\; R_t \;=\; e^{r_t} - 1 \,}
$$

Taylor expansion gives:

$$
r_t \;=\; R_t - \frac{R_t^{2}}{2} + \frac{R_t^{3}}{3} - \cdots
$$

For $|R_t| \ll 1$, the higher-order terms vanish and $r_t \approx R_t$.

**When log returns are preferred:**

1. **Multi-period aggregation** — log returns *add*, simple returns *multiply*. Aggregating $k$ periods is one summation instead of a $k$-factor product.
2. **Time-additive variance** — for i.i.d. returns, $\mathrm{Var}(r_t[k]) = k\sigma^{2}$, enabling the standard $\sqrt{T}$ volatility scaling used in Black-Scholes.
3. **Continuous-time modelling** — Geometric Brownian Motion and Itô calculus are defined on log-prices.
4. **Symmetry under sign changes** — a loss of $-50\%$ followed by a gain of $+100\%$ restores the original capital under log-returns ($r_1 + r_2 = \ln 0.5 + \ln 1.5 = \ln 0.75 \ne 0$; under simple returns $R[2] = -0.5 + 1.0 = +0.5$). The log-return captures *price-relative* changes correctly.
5. **Normality of log-returns** corresponds to log-normal price distribution, a modelling convenience.

**When simple returns are preferred:** in **portfolio accounting**, P&L statements, and regulatory capital reporting, where actual money amounts matter.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Supplementary Model]`

**(a) Discuss the distributional properties of financial returns — mean, variance, skewness and kurtosis — with reference to the normality assumption. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Assume $\{R_t\}$ is a strictly stationary series with finite fourth moment.

**1. Mean (Drift):** $\mu = E(R_t)$ — measures the average per-period gain.

$$
\hat{\mu} \;=\; \frac{1}{T}\sum_{t=1}^{T} R_t
$$

For daily equity returns, $\hat{\mu} \approx 0.0005$ (≈ 12 % annualised).

**2. Variance (Squared Volatility):** $\sigma^{2} = \mathrm{Var}(R_t)$ — measures dispersion.

$$
\hat{\sigma}^{2} \;=\; \frac{1}{T-1}\sum_{t=1}^{T}(R_t - \hat{\mu})^{2}
$$

Daily $\hat{\sigma} \approx 0.015$ for a typical large-cap Indian stock (≈ 24 % annualised).

**3. Skewness:** $\gamma_1 = E[(R_t - \mu)^{3}]/\sigma^{3}$. Empirical equity returns exhibit $\gamma_1 < 0$ (negative skew): occasional large losses.

**4. Kurtosis:** $\gamma_2 = E[(R_t - \mu)^{4}]/\sigma^{4}$. Empirical $\gamma_2 \in [5, 30]$, far exceeding the Gaussian value of $3$. The **excess kurtosis** $\kappa = \gamma_2 - 3$ is a measure of tail-heaviness.

**Normality Assumption:** Many classical models (Black-Scholes, basic CAPM) assume

$$
R_t \;\sim\; \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^{2})
$$

Under this assumption, $\gamma_1 = 0$, $\gamma_2 = 3$, and joint normality holds across assets.

**Why normality fails empirically:**

- **Fat tails** — extreme events (2008 crisis, COVID-19 crash) occur with probability much higher than Gaussian.
- **Asymmetry** — equity returns are negatively skewed.
- **Implications for risk models** — VaR computed under normality systematically *under*-estimates true tail risk.

The **Jarque-Bera test** statistic

$$
JB \;=\; \frac{T}{6}\!\left(\hat{\gamma}_1^{2} + \frac{(\hat{\gamma}_2 - 3)^{2}}{4}\right) \;\sim\; \chi^{2}_{2}
$$

rejects normality for essentially every liquid asset at conventional levels.

**Remedies:** Student-$t$, generalised error distribution, skewed-$t$ distributions and stable Paretian distributions are used to capture fat tails.

---

**(b) Explain volatility clustering and the leverage effect as stylized facts of financial time series. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Volatility Clustering (Mandelbrot, 1963):**

> "Large changes tend to be followed by large changes, of either sign, and small changes tend to be followed by small changes."

Mathematically, while $\mathrm{Corr}(R_t, R_{t-\ell}) \approx 0$ for $\ell \ge 1$, the **autocorrelation of squared returns**

$$
\rho_{2}(\ell) \;=\; \mathrm{Corr}(R_t^{2}, R_{t-\ell}^{2})
$$

is **significantly positive** and decays slowly (often hyperbolically).

**Implication:** Returns are *conditionally* heteroskedastic — their variance evolves through time. This is the empirical foundation of **ARCH** (Engle, 1982) and **GARCH** (Bollerslev, 1986) models.

**Statistical signature:**

$$
\rho_{1}(\ell) \approx 0, \qquad \rho_{|\cdot|}(\ell) > 0 \;\;\text{for } \ell \le 50 \text{ (slow decay)}.
$$

**2. Leverage Effect (Black, 1976):**

> "Negative returns tend to increase future volatility more than positive returns of the same magnitude."

Intuition: A price decline reduces the firm's equity value (while debt is approximately fixed), raising the **debt-to-equity ratio** and hence financial risk. Lenders demand a higher risk premium, which manifests as higher volatility.

**Empirical quantification:** The correlation

$$
\mathrm{Corr}(R_{t-\ell}, \sigma^{2}_{t})
$$

is **negative** for small $\ell$ (typically $\ell = 1, 2, \ldots, 10$).

**Modelling implication:** Asymmetric GARCH models — **GJR-GARCH**, **EGARCH** — explicitly incorporate this effect:

$$
\sigma_t^{2} \;=\; \omega + \alpha R_{t-1}^{2} + \gamma R_{t-1}^{2}\,\mathbb{1}_{\{R_{t-1}<0\}} + \beta\sigma_{t-1}^{2}
$$

where $\gamma > 0$ captures the leverage effect.

**3. Joint implication for risk management:**

- Option **pricing** must use asymmetric volatility (puts become more expensive than under symmetric GARCH).
- **VaR models** must distinguish upside vs. downside shocks — a one-day $-5\%$ shock carries a larger variance forecast than a $+5\%$ shock.
- **Stress testing** protocols explicitly model the leverage effect to estimate crisis-period volatility spikes.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing $R_t$ and $r_t$** in multi-period aggregation. Log returns ADD; simple returns MULTIPLY. Mixing them loses **3 to 4 marks** instantly.
> 2. **Forgetting to include the dividend** $D_t$ in the dividend-adjusted return formula on Day-of-payment — common mistake worth **2 marks**.
> 3. **Using $\sigma$ instead of $\sigma^{2}$** when stating the variance scaling property of log-returns. Correct statement: *variance* scales linearly with $k$, *standard deviation* scales with $\sqrt{k}$.
> 4. **Citing "$\gamma_2 > 3$"** without the qualifier "**excess** kurtosis = $\gamma_2 - 3$" loses partial credit.
> 5. **Writing "returns are normally distributed"** as a definitive statement — the correct KTU phrasing is *"the normality assumption is rejected empirically but retained as a working approximation."*
> 6. **Omitting the autoregressive structure** when defining volatility clustering — must mention that squared/absolute returns are autocorrelated, not the returns themselves.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Financial Data and Their Properties**
>
> - **Primary object of study is the return series, not the price series.** Prices are non-stationary; returns are (approximately) stationary.
> - **Simple return:** $R_t = (P_t - P_{t-1})/P_{t-1}$.
> - **Log return:** $r_t = \ln(P_t/P_{t-1}) = \ln(1 + R_t)$.
> - **For $|r_t| \ll 1$**, $r_t \approx R_t$.
> - **Multi-period simple returns** are products (multiplicative); **multi-period log returns** are sums (additive).
> - **Dividend-adjusted** simple return: $(P_t + D_t)/P_{t-1} - 1$.
> - **Variance of $k$-period log return** = $k\sigma^{2}$ (i.i.d. case) — the **$\sqrt{T}$ volatility scaling rule**.
> - **Six to seven stylized facts** are universally observed: heavy tails, negative skew, volatility clustering, leverage effect, no return autocorrelation, long memory in $|R_t|$ or $R_t^{2}$.
> - **Normality is rejected empirically** — Jarque-Bera test fails for every liquid asset; fat-tail distributions (Student-$t$, GED) are used instead.
> - **Sample skewness and kurtosis** formulas are board-exam favourites; remember the **bias correction** ($\frac{1}{T-1}$ in variance).
> - **Log returns** are preferred for: continuous-time models (GBM/Itô), option pricing, multi-period aggregation.
> - **Simple returns** are preferred for: accounting P&L, portfolio aggregation in monetary terms.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Financial Time Series and Their Characteristics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET742 - FINANCIAL TIME SERIES ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Financial Time Series and Their Characteristics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **financial time series** is an ordered, chronologically indexed sequence of observations on a financial variable — typically recorded at equally spaced time intervals (daily closing price, minute-by-minute tick, monthly index level, etc.). Formally, it is a realization of a stochastic process $\{X_t : t \in T\}$ where the index set $T$ represents time and $X_t$ denotes the value of the financial variable (asset price, exchange rate, interest rate, volatility index, trading volume, or a derived quantity such as return) at time $t$.

The most common objects of study in financial time series analysis are:

- **Price series**: $P_t$ — the transaction price or index level of a financial asset at time $t$.
- **Return series**: $R_t$ (simple return) or $r_t$ (log-return / continuously compounded return).
- **Volatility series**: $\sigma_t$ — a measure of the conditional second moment of returns.
- **Volume series**: $V_t$ — the trading volume at time $t$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Definition (Examination Standard):**
> A *financial time series* is a sequence of observations on a financial variable ordered in time, whose probabilistic properties (mean, variance, autocorrelation, distributional shape) are central to modeling, forecasting, and risk-management decisions in modern finance.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition (Plain-English Analogy):**
> Imagine you are standing at the edge of a wide, fast-flowing river. The water level at every second is your *time series*. You cannot predict the exact next height with certainty, but you can observe patterns: the river rises and falls, has moments of calm and moments of turbulence, and large floods tend to be followed by large floods (volatility clustering). A *financial time series* behaves similarly — stock prices flow unpredictably in the short term, but they exhibit recognizable statistical "weather patterns" over time. The job of a financial time series analyst is exactly like that of a hydrologist — to measure the water, describe its patterns, build a model of its behavior, and forecast (within probabilistic limits) where it will go next.

---

## 1.3 Why Financial Time Series Are Special

Unlike series observed in physics or engineering, financial time series exhibit a set of well-documented **stylized facts** that distinguish them from the Gaussian, linear, stationary world of classical Box–Jenkins models:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Five Pillars of Stylized Facts (must-know for KTU exams):**
> 1. **Non-stationarity of price levels** — prices wander without a fixed mean.
> 2. **Stationarity and near-zero autocorrelation of returns** — today's return barely predicts tomorrow's.
> 3. **Fat (heavy) tails** — extreme events occur far more often than a Gaussian model predicts.
> 4. **Volatility clustering** — large (small) absolute returns tend to be followed by large (small) absolute returns.
> 5. **Leverage effect** — negative returns raise future volatility more than positive returns of equal magnitude.
> 6. **Asymmetry / negative skewness** — stock returns typically have a left-skewed distribution.

These facts motivate specialized models (ARCH, GARCH, stochastic volatility, jump-diffusion) that go beyond the classical ARMA framework.

---

## 1.4 Returns: The Preferred Working Variable

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Although raw prices are easy to observe, **returns** are the fundamental modeling object. Two reasons:
> 1. Returns are approximately **scale-free** (a 10-point move in a ₹100 stock and a 10-point move in a ₹10,000 stock are not comparable; their returns are).
> 2. Returns are more likely to be **stationary**, which is a prerequisite for classical inference.

**Simple return** (over period $t-1 \to t$):

$$
R_t \;=\; \frac{P_t - P_{t-1}}{P_{t-1}} \;=\; \frac{P_t}{P_{t-1}} - 1
$$

**Log-return (continuously compounded return)**:

$$
r_t \;=\; \ln\!\left(\frac{P_t}{P_{t-1}}\right) \;=\; \ln P_t - \ln P_{t-1}
$$

For small movements, $r_t \approx R_t$, and the log-return is **additive over time**:

$$
r_t + r_{t+1} + \cdots + r_{t+k} \;=\; \ln\!\left(\frac{P_{t+k}}{P_t}\right)
$$

which makes multi-period aggregation algebraically trivial.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Probabilistic Descriptors of a Time Series

Let $\{X_t\}$ be a discrete-time stochastic process with $E(X_t^2) < \infty$.

- **Mean function**: $\mu_t = E(X_t)$.
- **Variance function**: $\sigma_t^2 = \text{Var}(X_t) = E\!\left[(X_t - \mu_t)^2\right]$.
- **Autocovariance function**: $\gamma(s,t) = E\!\left[(X_s - \mu_s)(X_t - \mu_t)\right]$.
- **Autocorrelation function (ACF)**: $\rho(s,t) = \dfrac{\gamma(s,t)}{\sqrt{\gamma(s,s)\,\gamma(t,t)}}$.
- **Cross-correlation** between two series $X_t, Y_t$: $\rho_{XY}(s,t) = \dfrac{\gamma_{XY}(s,t)}{\sqrt{\gamma_X(s,s)\,\gamma_Y(t,t)}}$.

For a *stationary* series these depend only on the **lag** $k = \vert t - s \vert$, giving:

- $\mu$ (constant)
- $\gamma_k = \gamma(t, t+k)$
- $\rho_k = \gamma_k / \gamma_0$

## 2.2 Strict (Strong) vs. Weak (Second-Order) Stationarity

A process is **strictly stationary** if the joint distribution of $(X_{t_1},\dots,X_{t_n})$ is identical to that of $(X_{t_1+k},\dots,X_{t_n+k})$ for every $k$ and every $n$.

A process is **weakly stationary** (or *second-order / covariance stationary*) if:

$$
E(X_t^2) < \infty, \quad E(X_t) = \mu \;(\text{constant}), \quad \gamma(s,t) = \gamma(\vert t-s\vert)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Strict stationarity $\Rightarrow$ Weak stationarity only when second moments exist. The reverse is false: weak stationarity does not imply strict stationarity (e.g., when the distribution is non-Gaussian).

## 2.3 White Noise and Random Walk — Two Cornerstone Processes

### 2.3.1 White Noise $\{a_t\}$

A sequence $\{a_t\}$ is **white noise** if:

$$
E(a_t) = 0, \qquad \text{Var}(a_t) = \sigma_a^2, \qquad \text{Cov}(a_s, a_t) = 0 \;\; \text{for} \;\; s \neq t
$$

If in addition the $a_t$ are i.i.d. **Gaussian**, it is denoted **Gaussian white noise**, written $a_t \sim \text{WN}(0, \sigma_a^2)$ or $a_t \sim \text{i.i.d. } N(0, \sigma_a^2)$.

### 2.3.2 Random Walk $\{S_t\}$

$$
S_t = S_{t-1} + a_t, \qquad S_0 = 0, \qquad \{a_t\} \sim \text{WN}(0, \sigma_a^2)
$$

Equivalently:

$$
S_t = a_1 + a_2 + \cdots + a_t = \sum_{i=1}^{t} a_i
$$

The random walk has:

$$
E(S_t) = 0, \qquad \text{Var}(S_t) = t\,\sigma_a^2
$$

Because variance grows linearly with $t$, the random walk is **non-stationary**. Differencing once returns a stationary white-noise process.

### 2.3.3 Random Walk with Drift $\{X_t\}$

$$
X_t = X_{t-1} + \delta + a_t \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; X_t = X_0 + \delta t + \sum_{i=1}^{t} a_i
$$

with $E(X_t) = X_0 + \delta t$ and $\text{Var}(X_t) = t\,\sigma_a^2$. The drift $\delta$ creates a deterministic linear trend superimposed on the stochastic random walk.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Empirical fact**: A stock price $P_t$ closely resembles a **random walk with drift**; its log-return $r_t = \ln P_t - \ln P_{t-1} \approx \delta + a_t$ is approximately **stationary white noise** (with stylized-fact deviations).

## 2.4 The Stylized Facts in Detail

### 2.4.1 Fat (Heavy) Tails

Returns have **excess kurtosis** far greater than zero:

$$
\kappa = \frac{E\!\left[(X_t - \mu)^4\right]}{\sigma^4} - 3 \;\gg\; 0
$$

Typical daily equity returns have kurtosis $\kappa \approx 5$ to $50$, while a Gaussian distribution has $\kappa = 0$. This implies that extreme events (crashes, rallies) occur with probability orders of magnitude higher than the Gaussian prediction.

### 2.4.2 Volatility Clustering

$$
\text{Corr}(r_t^2, r_{t+k}^2) > 0 \quad \text{for several lags } k
$$

Large absolute returns tend to be followed by large absolute returns, and small by small. Formally, $\vert r_t \vert$ exhibits positive, slowly decaying autocorrelation — even though $r_t$ itself has near-zero autocorrelation.

### 2.4.3 Leverage Effect

Negative returns increase *future* volatility more than positive returns of the same magnitude:

$$
\text{Cov}(r_t^2, r_{t-k}) \;<\; 0 \quad \text{for small positive } k
$$

Economically: a price drop raises the debt-to-equity ratio of a firm, increasing its riskiness.

### 2.4.4 Negative Skewness

$$
\gamma_1 = \frac{E\!\left[(X_t - \mu)^3\right]}{\sigma^3} \;<\; 0
$$

Equity returns are left-skewed — large losses are more frequent than equally large gains.

### 2.4.5 Slow Decay of Autocorrelations of Absolute/Squared Returns

The ACF of $\vert r_t \vert$ or $r_t^2$ decays hyperbolically rather than exponentially, suggesting long-memory dependence in volatility.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates every formula you must memorize for the KTU board exam on this topic. **No vertical-pipe symbols are used inside table cells** (use $\vert$ or $\mid$ instead).

| # | Concept | Mathematical Expression | Boundary / Condition |
|---|---------|------------------------|----------------------|
| 1 | Mean function | $\mu_t = E(X_t)$ | Finite expectation required |
| 2 | Variance function | $\sigma_t^2 = E\!\left[(X_t - \mu_t)^2\right]$ | Finite second moment |
| 3 | Autocovariance | $\gamma(s,t) = E\!\left[(X_s-\mu_s)(X_t-\mu_t)\right]$ | $\gamma(s,s) \geq 0$ |
| 4 | Autocorrelation (ACF) | $\rho(s,t) = \dfrac{\gamma(s,t)}{\sqrt{\gamma(s,s)\,\gamma(t,t)}}$ | $\vert \rho(s,t)\vert \leq 1$ |
| 5 | Lag-k ACF (stationary) | $\rho_k = \gamma_k / \gamma_0$ | $\rho_0 = 1,\; \vert \rho_k\vert \leq 1$ |
| 6 | Simple return | $R_t = \dfrac{P_t - P_{t-1}}{P_{t-1}}$ | Multi-period additive only after log |
| 7 | Log-return | $r_t = \ln P_t - \ln P_{t-1}$ | $r_t \approx R_t$ for small $R_t$ |
| 8 | Cumulative log-return | $\sum_{i=1}^{k} r_{t+i} = \ln P_{t+k} - \ln P_t$ | Additive property |
| 9 | Weak stationarity | $\mu_t = \mu,\; \gamma(s,t)=\gamma(\vert s-t\vert)$ | Three conditions simultaneously |
| 10 | White noise mean | $E(a_t) = 0$ | Constant |
| 11 | White noise variance | $\text{Var}(a_t) = \sigma_a^2$ | Constant |
| 12 | White noise covariance | $\text{Cov}(a_s, a_t) = 0,\; s \neq t$ | Uncorrelated |
| 13 | Random walk recursion | $S_t = S_{t-1} + a_t$ | $a_t \sim$ WN |
| 14 | Random walk mean | $E(S_t) = S_0$ | Constant |
| 15 | Random walk variance | $\text{Var}(S_t) = t\,\sigma_a^2$ | Linear in $t$ — non-stationary |
| 16 | RW autocovariance | $\gamma(s,t) = \sigma_a^2 \cdot \min(s,t)$ | Function of $\min(s,t)$, not lag alone |
| 17 | RW with drift | $X_t = X_0 + \delta t + \sum_{i=1}^{t} a_i$ | $E(X_t) = X_0 + \delta t$ |
| 18 | Excess kurtosis | $\kappa = \mu_4/\sigma^4 - 3$ | $\kappa = 0$ for Gaussian |
| 19 | Skewness | $\gamma_1 = \mu_3/\sigma^3$ | $\gamma_1 = 0$ for symmetric |
| 20 | Sample ACF (estimator) | $\hat{\rho}_k = \dfrac{\sum_{t=k+1}^{T}(x_t-\bar x)(x_{t-k}-\bar x)}{\sum_{t=1}^{T}(x_t-\bar x)^2}$ | $\vert\hat\rho_k\vert \leq 1$ |
| 21 | AR(1) ACF | $\rho_k = \phi^{\vert k\vert}$ | $\vert\phi\vert < 1$ for stationarity |
| 22 | First-differencing | $\nabla X_t = X_t - X_{t-1}$ | Removes random-walk trend |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering & Financial Utility

- **Portfolio risk management**: Quantification of variance and tail-risk is the input to VaR and Expected Shortfall models.
- **Algorithmic & high-frequency trading**: Microstructure-aware return models exploit volatility clustering to set dynamic bid-ask spreads.
- **Derivative pricing**: Models such as Heston and SABR use the stylized facts to construct realistic volatility surfaces.
- **Macroeconomic forecasting**: Central-bank rate paths and exchange rates are modeled as financial time series with drift + random-walk components.
- **Anomaly / fraud detection**: The Gaussian reference is replaced by heavy-tailed distributions when monitoring extreme events.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following derivations are the **highest-yield numerical/algebraic problems** for KTU board examinations. Each transition is shown explicitly with the underlying mathematical logic.

---

## 3.1 Derivation of the Mean and Variance of a Random Walk

**Problem Setup.** Let $\{S_t\}_{t=0}^{T}$ be a random walk defined recursively by:

$$
S_t = S_{t-1} + a_t, \qquad S_0 = 0, \qquad \{a_t\} \sim \text{WN}(0,\sigma_a^2)
$$

**Step 1 — Unroll the recursion.** Apply the recurrence repeatedly:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_1 &= S_0 + a_1 = a_1 \\[4pt]
S_2 &= S_1 + a_2 = a_1 + a_2 \\[4pt]
S_3 &= S_2 + a_3 = a_1 + a_2 + a_3 \\[4pt]
&\;\;\vdots \\[4pt]
S_t &= a_1 + a_2 + \cdots + a_t = \sum_{i=1}^{t} a_i
\end{aligned}
$$

The cumulative form is what we will use for all moments.

**Step 2 — Compute the mean.** Take expectations on both sides, using linearity of $E[\cdot]$ and $E[a_i] = 0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E(S_t) &= E\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^{t} a_i\right] = \sum_{i=1}^{t} E(a_i) = \sum_{i=1}^{t} 0 = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

So $E(S_t) = 0$ for all $t$ (the random walk is **mean-stationary** even though it is not variance-stationary).

**Step 3 — Compute the variance.** Use the variance-of-a-sum identity with the white-noise independence assumption:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Var}(S_t) &= \text{Var}\!\left(\sum_{i=1}^{t} a_i\right) \\[6pt]
&= \sum_{i=1}^{t} \text{Var}(a_i) \;+\; 2 \!\!\sum_{1 \leq i < j \leq t}\!\! \text{Cov}(a_i, a_j) \\[6pt]
&= \sum_{i=1}^{t} \sigma_a^2 \;+\; 2 \!\!\sum_{1 \leq i < j \leq t}\!\! 0 \\[6pt]
&= t \,\sigma_a^2
\end{aligned}
$$

Because $\text{Var}(S_t)$ depends on $t$ (it grows linearly), the random walk **violates weak stationarity**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the derivation:** Line 1 unrolls the recursion into a sum (key trick — converting a recursive definition into an analytical closed form). Line 2 uses linearity and the zero-mean assumption. Line 3 uses the *general variance-sum identity* and exploits the zero cross-covariance of white noise.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Autocovariance of a Random Walk

**Problem.** Show that the autocovariance function of the random walk $S_t = \sum_{i=1}^{t} a_i$ is:

$$
\gamma(s,t) \;=\; \sigma_a^2 \cdot \min(s,t)
$$

**Step 1 — Express $S_s$ and $S_t$ as sums.** Assume WLOG $s \leq t$:

$$
S_s = \sum_{i=1}^{s} a_i, \qquad S_t = \sum_{j=1}^{t} a_j = \sum_{i=1}^{s} a_i + \sum_{i=s+1}^{t} a_i
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the autocovariance definition.** With $\mu_s = \mu_t = 0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\gamma(s,t) &= E\!\left[(S_s - 0)(S_t - 0)\right] = E(S_s \, S_t) \\[6pt]
&= E\!\left[\left(\sum_{i=1}^{s} a_i\right)\!\left(\sum_{j=1}^{t} a_j\right)\right] \\[6pt]
&= E\!\left[\left(\sum_{i=1}^{s} a_i\right)\!\left(\sum_{i=1}^{s} a_i + \sum_{i=s+1}^{t} a_i\right)\right] \\[6pt]
&= E\!\left[\left(\sum_{i=1}^{s} a_i\right)^{\!2}\right] + E\!\left[\left(\sum_{i=1}^{s} a_i\right)\!\left(\sum_{i=s+1}^{t} a_i\right)\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate each piece.** The first term is $\text{Var}(S_s) = s\sigma_a^2$. The second term is zero because the two sums index disjoint shocks (and white-noise cross-covariances vanish):

$$
E\!\left[\left(\sum_{i=1}^{s} a_i\right)\!\left(\sum_{i=s+1}^{t} a_i\right)\right] = \sum_{i=1}^{s}\sum_{j=s+1}^{t} E(a_i a_j) = 0
$$

So for $s \leq t$:

$$
\gamma(s,t) = s\,\sigma_a^2
$$

By symmetry, for $s > t$ we obtain $\gamma(s,t) = t\,\sigma_a^2$. Combining:

$$
\gamma(s,t) = \sigma_a^2 \cdot \min(s,t)
$$

**Step 4 — Show non-stationarity.** Note that $\gamma(s,t)$ depends on $\min(s,t)$, *not* on the lag $\vert t-s\vert$ alone. If $s=5, t=10$ then $\gamma=5\sigma_a^2$; if $s=100, t=105$ then $\gamma=100\sigma_a^2$, even though the lag is identical. Therefore the random walk fails the second weak-stationarity condition.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the ACF of an AR(1) Process

**Problem Setup.** Consider the stationary AR(1) process:

$$
r_t = \phi\, r_{t-1} + a_t, \qquad \{a_t\} \sim \text{WN}(0,\sigma_a^2), \qquad \vert \phi\vert < 1
$$

Derive the autocorrelation function $\rho_k$.

**Step 1 — Stationary mean.** Iterate expectations:

$$
E(r_t) = \phi\, E(r_{t-1}) + E(a_t) = \phi\, E(r_t) \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; E(r_t)(1-\phi) = 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; E(r_t) = 0
$$

**Step 2 — Stationary variance.** Multiply the recursion by $r_t$ and take expectations:

$$
E(r_t^2) = \phi\, E(r_t r_{t-1}) + E(r_t a_t)
$$

Since $r_t a_t = \phi r_{t-1} a_t + a_t^2$, and $E(r_{t-1} a_t) = 0$ (because $a_t$ is uncorrelated with the past), and $E(a_t^2) = \sigma_a^2$:

$$
\gamma_0 = \phi\, \gamma_1 + \sigma_a^2
$$

**Step 3 — General lag-$k$ autocovariance.** Multiply $r_t = \phi r_{t-1} + a_t$ by $r_{t-k}$ and take expectations (for $k \geq 1$):

$$
\gamma_k = \phi\, \gamma_{k-1} + E(a_t r_{t-k}) = \phi\, \gamma_{k-1}
$$

because $a_t$ is uncorrelated with $r_{t-k}$ for $k \geq 1$.

**Step 4 — Solve the recursion.** With $\gamma_0$ as the initial value:

$$
\gamma_1 = \phi\,\gamma_0,\quad \gamma_2 = \phi\,\gamma_1 = \phi^2\gamma_0,\quad \ldots\quad \gamma_k = \phi^{\vert k\vert}\,\gamma_0
$$

Dividing by $\gamma_0$:

$$
\boxed{\;\rho_k = \phi^{\vert k\vert}\;}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The AR(1) ACF decays **geometrically** at rate $\phi$. This is the canonical contrast with volatility clustering, where ACFs of $\vert r_t\vert$ or $r_t^2$ decay hyperbolically.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Annualized Statistics from Daily Data

**Problem.** Suppose the daily log-returns of an asset have:

$$
\bar r = 0.0006, \qquad s_r = 0.015, \qquad s_{\vert r\vert} = 0.012, \qquad \hat\kappa = 6.4, \qquad \hat\rho_1(r^2) = 0.32
$$

with $T = 250$ trading days.

**(a) Annualized mean return and standard deviation.**

$$
\bar r_{\text{ann}} = 250 \times 0.0006 = 0.15 \;=\; 15\%
$$

$$
s_{\text{ann}} = \sqrt{250}\,\times\, 0.015 = 15.811\,\times\, 0.015 \approx 0.237 \;=\; 23.7\%
$$

**(b) Excess kurtosis comment.** $\hat\kappa = 6.4 \gg 0$, confirming **fat tails**.

**(c) Interpretation of $\hat\rho_1(r^2) = 0.32$.** Squared returns are positively autocorrelated at lag 1, indicating **volatility clustering** — today's squared return explains ~32% of the variance of tomorrow's squared return.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Summary of Key Results

| Process | Mean $E(X_t)$ | Variance $\text{Var}(X_t)$ | ACF $\rho_k$ | Stationary? |
|---------|--------------|---------------------------|--------------|-------------|
| White noise $a_t$ | $0$ | $\sigma_a^2$ | $0$ for $k\geq 1$ | ✅ Yes |
| Random walk $S_t$ | $0$ | $t\,\sigma_a^2$ | $\sqrt{\min(t-k,t)/\min(t,t-k)}$ shape | ❌ No |
| RW with drift | $\delta t$ | $t\,\sigma_a^2$ | Not lag-dependent | ❌ No |
| AR(1) $|\phi|<1$ | $0$ | $\sigma_a^2/(1-\phi^2)$ | $\phi^{\vert k\vert}$ | ✅ Yes |
| MA(1) $|\theta|<1$ | $0$ | $\sigma_a^2(1+\theta^2)$ | $\theta/(1+\theta^2)$ at $k=1$, else $0$ | ✅ Yes |

---

## 3.6 Standardization of Returns and the Role of Skewness/Kurtosis

For a return $r_t$ with mean $\mu$ and standard deviation $\sigma$, define the **standardized return**:

$$
z_t \;=\; \frac{r_t - \mu}{\sigma}
$$

Then:

$$
E(z_t) = 0, \qquad \text{Var}(z_t) = 1
$$

The third and fourth standardized moments are:

$$
\text{Skewness:}\;\; \gamma_1 = E(z_t^3), \qquad \text{Excess Kurtosis:}\;\; \kappa = E(z_t^4) - 3
$$

For a Gaussian distribution, $\gamma_1 = 0$ and $\kappa = 0$. Empirical daily equity returns routinely yield $\gamma_1 \approx -0.5$ and $\kappa \approx 5$–$30$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**
Define a **financial time series**. Explain why *returns* are preferred over *price levels* as the primary object of statistical modeling.

**Model Answer:**

> A financial time series is an ordered sequence of observations on a financial variable indexed by time, $X_t$ for $t = 1, 2, \dots, T$, typically recorded at equally spaced intervals.
>
> Returns are preferred over price levels for two main reasons:
>
> 1. **Scale-free comparison** — A 10-rupee move in a ₹100 stock and a 10-rupee move in a ₹10,000 stock represent different economic magnitudes, but their *returns* (10% and 0.1%) are directly comparable across assets and time.
> 2. **Stationarity** — Price levels typically exhibit a random-walk behavior (variance grows with $t$, hence non-stationary). Returns, being first differences of log-prices, are approximately stationary, satisfying the pre-condition for applying classical ARMA / GARCH inference.

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**
List and briefly explain **any four stylized facts** of financial return series.

**Model Answer:**

> 1. **Fat tails** — The empirical distribution of returns has excess kurtosis much greater than zero, so extreme events occur more frequently than predicted by a Gaussian model.
> 2. **Volatility clustering** — Large absolute returns tend to be followed by large absolute returns; the squared or absolute return series has positive, slowly-decaying autocorrelation.
> 3. **Negative skewness** — Stock returns are slightly left-skewed, indicating that large negative moves are more likely than equally large positive moves.
> 4. **Leverage effect** — Negative returns increase future volatility more strongly than positive returns of the same magnitude, evidenced by negative cross-correlation between $r_t^2$ and lagged $r_t$.
>
> *(Other acceptable facts: near-zero autocorrelation of raw returns; mean reversion in volatility; long-memory in squared returns.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern:** Each Part-B question carries **14 marks** with internal structure (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. You are required to attempt **ONE** of the two choices provided.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style, Adapted]**

**(a) Define a random walk with drift. Starting from the recursion $X_t = X_{t-1} + \delta + a_t$, derive expressions for the mean, variance, and autocovariance function of the process. Show explicitly that the process is non-stationary.** **(7 Marks)**

**(b) The daily log-returns of an asset over $T = 252$ days have sample mean $\bar r = 0.0008$ and sample standard deviation $s = 0.012$. Compute the annualized mean return and annualized standard deviation. The first five sample autocorrelations of the squared returns are $\hat\rho_1 = 0.41,\; \hat\rho_2 = 0.32,\; \hat\rho_3 = 0.27,\; \hat\rho_4 = 0.22,\; \hat\rho_5 = 0.18$. Comment on the implications.** **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**Solution to (a):**

**Definition.** A random walk with drift is:

$$
X_t = X_{t-1} + \delta + a_t, \qquad \{a_t\} \sim \text{WN}(0,\sigma_a^2), \qquad X_0 \text{ given}
$$

**Unrolling the recursion:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
X_1 &= X_0 + \delta + a_1 \\
X_2 &= X_1 + \delta + a_2 = X_0 + 2\delta + a_1 + a_2 \\
&\;\;\vdots \\
X_t &= X_0 + \delta\,t + \sum_{i=1}^{t} a_i
\end{aligned}
$$

**Mean.** Using $E(a_i) = 0$:

$$
E(X_t) = X_0 + \delta t
$$

**Variance.** Using $\text{Var}(a_i) = \sigma_a^2$ and independence of shocks:

$$
\text{Var}(X_t) = \text{Var}\!\left(\sum_{i=1}^{t} a_i\right) = t\,\sigma_a^2
$$

**Autocovariance.** For $s \leq t$, mimicking Section 3.2:

$$
\gamma(s,t) = E[(X_s - E(X_s))(X_t - E(X_t))] = E\!\left[\left(\sum_{i=1}^{s} a_i\right)\!\left(\sum_{i=1}^{t} a_i\right)\right] = s\,\sigma_a^2
$$

By symmetry, $\gamma(s,t) = \sigma_a^2\,\min(s,t)$.

**Non-stationarity check.**

- Condition 1 (constant mean): $E(X_t) = X_0 + \delta t$ depends on $t$ → **violated**.
- Condition 2 (lag-dependent autocovariance): $\gamma(s,t) = \sigma_a^2\min(s,t)$ depends on $\min(s,t)$, not just $\vert t-s\vert$ → **violated**.

Hence the process is **not weakly stationary**. ∎

---

**Solution to (b):**

**Annualization (assuming 252 trading days/year).** Daily log-return mean $\bar r = 0.0008$:

$$
\bar r_{\text{ann}} = 252 \times 0.0008 = 0.2016 \approx 20.16\%
$$

Daily standard deviation $s = 0.012$:

$$
s_{\text{ann}} = \sqrt{252} \times 0.012 = 15.8745 \times 0.012 \approx 0.1905 \approx 19.05\%
$$

**Commentary on autocorrelations of squared returns:**

The autocorrelations $\hat\rho_k(r_t^2)$ are *all large and positive*, decaying only gradually (from $0.41$ at lag 1 to $0.18$ at lag 5). This is the empirical signature of **volatility clustering**: large squared returns (large movements, in either direction) are followed by large squared returns, and small by small. In addition, the slow, non-geometric decay hints at **long-memory** dependence in volatility, which motivates **GARCH-family** models rather than plain ARMA.

The persistence of $r_t^2$ autocorrelation also implies that a constant-variance assumption is inadequate — the conditional variance $\sigma_t^2$ must itself be modeled as a time-varying quantity.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style, Adapted]**

**(a) Define weak stationarity of a time series. State and briefly justify the three conditions. Explain with a counter-example why weak stationarity does NOT imply strict stationarity.** **(7 Marks)**

**(b) Define white noise and a random walk. Prove that the random walk is non-stationary by showing that its variance depends on $t$ and its autocovariance is not a function of the lag alone. Hence, conclude that first-differencing a random walk recovers stationarity.** **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**Solution to (a):**

**Definition.** A stochastic process $\{X_t\}$ is **weakly stationary** (second-order / covariance stationary) if its first two moments are finite and time-invariant:

1. $E(X_t^2) < \infty$ for all $t$ (finite second moment exists).
2. $E(X_t) = \mu$ is constant (does not depend on $t$).
3. $\text{Cov}(X_s, X_t) = \gamma(\vert t-s\vert)$ depends only on the lag $\vert t-s\vert$, not on the individual times.

**Justifications.** Condition (1) is needed for variance to be defined. Condition (2) ensures the mean function is constant. Condition (3) ensures the second-moment structure is shift-invariant.

**Counter-example to the converse.** Consider a stationary sequence $X_t$ taking values $+1$ or $-1$ with equal probability, but with **non-Gaussian** marginal distribution such that the joint distribution is *not* shift-invariant in distribution. A weakly stationary process that is Gaussian *is* strictly stationary (by the closure of the multivariate normal under linear combinations). But weak stationarity says nothing about higher moments or the full distribution; an i.i.d. Bernoulli sequence with $p \neq 0.5$ has a constant mean? — actually it does not satisfy mean constancy if $p$ changes, so the cleanest counter-example is a *non-Gaussian* second-order stationary process whose higher-order joint densities are lag-dependent. Such a process can have $E(X_t)=\mu$ constant and $\gamma(\vert t-s\vert)$ lag-dependent yet **not** be strictly stationary. ∎

---

**Solution to (b):**

**White noise.** $\{a_t\}$ is WN$(0,\sigma_a^2)$ if $E(a_t)=0$, $\text{Var}(a_t)=\sigma_a^2$, and $\text{Cov}(a_s,a_t)=0$ for $s\neq t$.

**Random walk.** $S_t = S_{t-1} + a_t$, $S_0 = 0$, $a_t \sim$ WN.

**Cumulative form (derived in Section 3.1):**

$$
S_t = \sum_{i=1}^{t} a_i
$$

**Variance:** $\text{Var}(S_t) = t\,\sigma_a^2$. Since this depends on $t$, condition (2) of weak stationarity is **violated**.

**Autocovariance:** As shown in Section 3.2, $\gamma(s,t) = \sigma_a^2\min(s,t)$. Setting $s = t-k$ for fixed $k$:

$$
\gamma(t-k,t) = \sigma_a^2\,(t-k)
$$

which depends on $t$ even when the lag $k$ is held constant. So condition (3) is **violated**.

**Differencing recovers stationarity.** Define:

$$
Y_t = S_t - S_{t-1} = a_t
$$

Then $\{Y_t\}$ is white noise, which is stationary. Differencing is therefore the standard remedy for random-walk non-stationarity (Box-Jenkins $d=1$). ∎

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students commonly lose marks on this topic:**
>
> 1. **Confusing strict and weak stationarity.** Many students write "strict stationarity implies weak stationarity" correctly but then fail to give a counter-example for the converse (a non-Gaussian weakly stationary process). Examiners allocate 2 marks specifically for this distinction — leaving it blank costs those marks.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the cross-covariance terms when expanding variance of a sum.** The expansion $\text{Var}(X+Y) = \text{Var}(X) + \text{Var}(Y) + 2\text{Cov}(X,Y)$ requires the student to **explicitly write the cross term and then justify it as zero** using the white-noise independence assumption. Skipping this justification costs 1–2 marks.
>
> 3. **Annualization formula misuse.** Use $\sqrt{T}$ for standard deviation but $T$ for mean. Writing $T \cdot s$ for annualized standard deviation is a frequent error (cost: 1 mark).
>
> 4. **Confusing "random walk" with "random walk with drift".** The mean of the random walk is *zero*, while the mean of the random walk with drift is *$\delta t$*. Examiners routinely test this distinction.
>
> 5. **Stating stylized facts without justification.** Saying "returns have fat tails" alone is incomplete; you must connect it to excess kurtosis or cite an empirical value (e.g., $\kappa \approx 5$ to $30$).
>
> 6. **Skipping the linearity of expectations step in the random-walk derivation.** Always write $E(\sum a_i) = \sum E(a_i)$ explicitly.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (must memorize before KTU exam):**

- [ ] A **financial time series** is a time-ordered sequence of a financial variable $X_t$.
- [ ] **Returns** (not prices) are the primary modeling object because they are scale-free and approximately stationary.
- [ ] **Weak stationarity** requires (i) finite second moment, (ii) constant mean $\mu$, (iii) autocovariance depending only on the lag $\vert t-s\vert$.
- [ ] **Strict stationarity** requires the full joint distribution to be shift-invariant; weak stationarity does *not* imply strict stationarity for non-Gaussian processes.
- [ ] **White noise** $\{a_t\} \sim \text{WN}(0,\sigma_a^2)$ has zero mean, constant variance $\sigma_a^2$, and zero autocovariance for $s \neq t$.
- [ ] **Random walk** $S_t = S_{t-1} + a_t$ has $E(S_t)=0$, $\text{Var}(S_t)=t\sigma_a^2$, and $\gamma(s,t)=\sigma_a^2\min(s,t)$ — hence **non-stationary**.
- [ ] **Random walk with drift** $X_t = X_0 + \delta t + \sum a_i$ has mean $X_0 + \delta t$ and variance $t\sigma_a^2$ — also non-stationary.
- [ ] **First-differencing** a non-stationary random walk produces a stationary white-noise process.
- [ ] **AR(1)** ACF decays geometrically: $\rho_k = \phi^{\vert k\vert}$, $|\phi| < 1$ for stationarity.
- [ ] **Simple return** $R_t = (P_t - P_{t-1})/P_{t-1}$; **log-return** $r_t = \ln P_t - \ln P_{t-1}$; log-returns are **additive over time**.
- [ ] **Annualization**: mean $\times T$, standard deviation $\times \sqrt{T}$ (with $T$ = trading days per year, typically 252).
- [ ] **Excess kurtosis** $\kappa = \mu_4/\sigma^4 - 3$; Gaussian has $\kappa = 0$; equity returns have $\kappa \gg 0$.
- [ ] **Five stylized facts**: (1) fat tails, (2) volatility clustering, (3) leverage effect, (4) negative skewness, (5) slowly decaying ACF of squared returns.
- [ ] **Volatility clustering** is the empirical motivation for **GARCH-family** models.
- [ ] For the random walk, $\gamma(s,t)$ depends on $\min(s,t)$ — *not* on $\vert t-s\vert$ — confirming non-stationarity.
- [ ] Always state and **justify** (not just write) stationarity conditions — examiners reward reasoning, not just statements.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Means
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET742 - FINANCIAL TIME SERIES ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Means

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Financial Time Series Analysis**, the term **"Means"** refers to a family of descriptive statistical measures of central tendency used to summarize the typical value, expected level, or central location of a stochastic financial process. Under the KTU 2024 syllabus, Means are foundational estimators that form the first conceptual step in characterizing the distributional properties of asset returns, prices, and volatilities.

Formally, the **population mean** (expectation) of a discrete random variable $X_t$ representing a financial time series is defined as:

$$\mu = \mathbb{E}[X_t] = \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i \cdot P(X_t = x_i)$$

where $P(X_t = x_i)$ is the probability of occurrence of value $x_i$. The corresponding **sample mean** computed from an observed realization of length $n$ is:

$$\bar{x} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{t=1}^{n} x_t$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In financial contexts, the variable $x_t$ typically represents **log-returns**, **simple returns**, **prices**, or **log-prices**. The choice of mean estimator is dictated by the multiplicative/additive nature of the underlying process. For example, the **geometric mean** is preferred over the arithmetic mean when averaging compounded returns.

## 1.2 Intuition & Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are a portfolio manager monitoring the daily closing price of a stock for 30 days. You have 30 numbers staring back at you, and you want to summarize them with a **single representative value**. That representative value is a *mean*. But which one?

- **Arithmetic Mean**: The "fair share" — if you distributed the total price equally across all 30 days, what would each day look like? Analogy: Sharing a birthday cake — total slices ÷ number of guests.
- **Geometric Mean**: The "compounded growth rate" — if your wealth grew by varying percentages each year, what single constant rate would have produced the same final wealth? Analogy: Average speed of a car over a journey with traffic — you cannot simply average speeds naively.
- **Exponentially Weighted Mean (EWMA)**: The "memory-decay mean" — recent observations matter more, just like humans remember yesterday's stock price more vividly than last year's. Analogy: A cooking recipe where the chef tastes the current dish and only slightly adjusts the seasoning, gradually forgetting the earlier oversalting.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction for KTU Examinations:**
> - Arithmetic mean preserves **additive** properties ($\sum$).
> - Geometric mean preserves **multiplicative** properties ($\prod$).
> - Exponential mean preserves **recency-weighted** properties.

## 1.3 Classification of Means (KTU-Aligned Taxonomy)

The following taxonomy is standard across KTU 2024 scheme question papers:

| Category | Specific Mean | Best Use-Case in Finance |
|----------|--------------|--------------------------|
| Unweighted | Arithmetic Mean | Simple averaging of prices |
| Unweighted | Geometric Mean | Average compounded returns |
| Unweighted | Harmonic Mean | Price-Earnings (P/E) ratios |
| Weighted | Weighted Arithmetic Mean | Portfolio weighted returns |
| Weighted | Exponentially Weighted Mean | Volatility (EWMA), GARCH inputs |
| Robust | Trimmed Mean | Outlier-robust estimation |
| Robust | Winsorized Mean | Bounded-influence estimation |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Primary Means — Operational Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Arithmetic Mean (AM)
The arithmetic mean is the most elementary estimator. It is the **Best Linear Unbiased Estimator (BLUE)** of the population mean under the Gauss-Markov assumptions (homoscedasticity, no autocorrelation, finite variance).

**Logic Steps:**
1. Sum all observed values $x_t$ from $t=1$ to $n$.
2. Divide the sum by the total number of observations $n$.
3. The result is the unique value $\bar{x}$ that minimizes $\sum_{t=1}^{n}(x_t - \bar{x})^2$ — i.e., it is the least-squares estimator of the location parameter.

**Why?** It is mathematically the projection of the data vector onto the constant subspace, minimizing Euclidean distance.

**How?** It produces a unique scalar summary used as input to variance, covariance, and regression computations.

### 2.1.2 Geometric Mean (GM)
Critical for financial time series because financial growth compounds **multiplicatively**, not additively.

**Logic Steps:**
1. Take the natural logarithm of each observation: $\ln(x_t)$.
2. Compute the arithmetic mean of these log values: $\frac{1}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n} \ln(x_t)$.
3. Exponentiate the result to return to the original scale: $\exp\!\left(\frac{1}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n} \ln(x_t)\right)$.

**Why?** Returns compound. The geometric mean is the only mean that respects the multiplicative structure: $X_{t} = X_{0} \cdot \prod_{t=1}^{n} (1 + r_t)$.

**How?** It is equivalent to the arithmetic mean in the log-domain, making it additive-friendly for log-returns.

### 2.1.3 Harmonic Mean (HM)
Used when the data represent **rates or ratios**. In finance, it is the correct way to average P/E ratios across stocks.

**Logic Steps:**
1. Invert each observation: $1/x_t$.
2. Take the arithmetic mean of the inverted values.
3. Invert the result.

### 2.1.4 Weighted Mean (WM)
Generalizes the arithmetic mean by assigning unequal importance to observations.

**Logic Steps:**
1. Assign weight $w_i$ to each $x_i$ where $\sum w_i = 1$.
2. Compute $\sum w_i x_i$.
3. In portfolio theory, $w_i$ represents capital allocation weights.

### 2.1.5 Exponentially Weighted Mean (EWM / EWMA)
The cornerstone of modern volatility modeling (RiskMetrics, GARCH pre-filtering).

**Logic Steps:**
1. Initialize $\mu_0 = x_1$.
2. Apply recursive update: $\mu_t = \lambda \mu_{t-1} + (1 - \lambda) x_t$.
3. The decay parameter $\lambda \in (0, 1)$ controls memory — larger $\lambda$ means longer memory.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The exponentially weighted mean is the **forward-pass input** for computing EWMA variance in the RiskMetrics methodology, which the KTU 2024 syllabus uses as the bridge between Module 1 (descriptive means) and Module 2 (volatility models).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE:** The vertical bar symbol $\vert$ is rendered using `\vert` to preserve Markdown table integrity.

| # | Mean Type | Formula | Domain Restriction | Bias Property |
|---|-----------|---------|--------------------|---------------|
| 1 | Arithmetic Mean | $\bar{x} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n} x_t$ | All real $x_t$ | Unbiased estimator of $\mu$ |
| 2 | Geometric Mean | $G = \left(\prod_{t=1}^{n} x_t\right)^{1/n}$ | $x_t > 0 \; \forall t$ | Biased downward for small $n$ |
| 3 | Harmonic Mean | $H = \frac{n}{\sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{1}{x_t}}$ | $x_t \neq 0 \; \forall t$ | Biased estimator |
| 4 | Weighted Mean | $\bar{x}_w = \sum_{t=1}^{n} w_t x_t$ | $\sum w_t = 1$ | Unbiased if weights correct |
| 5 | EWM (Recursive) | $\mu_t = \lambda \mu_{t-1} + (1-\lambda) x_t$ | $\lambda \in (0,1)$ | Asymptotically unbiased |
| 6 | EWM (Explicit) | $\mu_t = (1-\lambda)\sum_{j=0}^{\infty} \lambda^{j} x_{t-j}$ | Infinite history | Asymptotically unbiased |
| 7 | Trimmed Mean | $\bar{x}_{\alpha} = \frac{1}{n-2\lfloor \alpha n \rfloor} \sum_{t \in S} x_t$ | Sorted data $S$ | Approximately unbiased |
| 8 | Population Mean | $\mu = \mathbb{E}[X_t] = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x f(x)\, dx$ | Continuous case | Definition |

### 2.3 Inequality Hierarchy (AM-GM-HM)

For any strictly positive dataset $\{x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n\}$, the following **strict inequality** holds unless all $x_i$ are identical:

$$H \leq G \leq \bar{x}$$

with equality $\Leftrightarrow x_1 = x_2 = \ldots = x_n$. This inequality is a frequent KTU 14-mark question.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Quantitative Finance:** EWMA is the computational heart of RiskMetrics volatility forecasting (J.P. Morgan, 1996).
- **Algorithmic Trading:** Geometric mean of returns yields the **Compound Annual Growth Rate (CAGR)** — a KTU-favorite metric.
- **Risk Management:** Trimmed means are used in **Expected Shortfall (ES)** computations to suppress outlier influence.
- **Signal Processing:** The recursive EWM is mathematically identical to a **first-order Infinite Impulse Response (IIR) low-pass filter** with smoothing coefficient $\alpha = 1 - \lambda$.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Arithmetic Mean as Least-Squares Estimator

**Statement:** The arithmetic mean $\bar{x}$ minimizes the sum of squared deviations $S(\theta) = \sum_{t=1}^{n}(x_t - \theta)^2$.

**Proof:**

$$\begin{aligned}
S(\theta) &= \sum_{t=1}^{n}(x_t - \theta)^2 \\
\frac{dS(\theta)}{d\theta} &= -2 \sum_{t=1}^{n}(x_t - \theta) = 0 \\
\sum_{t=1}^{n} x_t - n\theta &= 0 \\
n\theta &= \sum_{t=1}^{n} x_t \\
\theta^{*} &= \frac{1}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n} x_t = \bar{x}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step Explanation:**
- Line 1: Define the objective function — sum of squared errors.
- Line 2: Take the first derivative with respect to the unknown $\theta$.
- Line 3: Set the derivative to zero (necessary condition for a minimum).
- Line 4: Algebraic rearrangement.
- Line 5: Solve for $\theta^{*}$, recovering the arithmetic mean. $\blacksquare$

> **Second-derivative test:** $\frac{d^2 S}{d\theta^2} = 2n > 0$, confirming a global minimum.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Geometric Mean from Multiplicative Compounding

**Statement:** Given $n$ successive gross returns $R_t = 1 + r_t$, the geometric mean $G$ is the constant gross return that yields the same cumulative wealth.

**Proof:**

Let $W_0$ be initial wealth. After $n$ periods of returns $R_1, R_2, \ldots, R_n$, the final wealth is:

$$\begin{aligned}
W_n &= W_0 \cdot R_1 \cdot R_2 \cdots R_n \\
W_n &= W_0 \cdot \prod_{t=1}^{n} R_t
\end{aligned}$$

We seek a constant $G$ such that $W_n = W_0 \cdot G^n$. Setting these equal:

$$\begin{aligned}
W_0 \cdot G^n &= W_0 \cdot \prod_{t=1}^{n} R_t \\
G^n &= \prod_{t=1}^{n} R_t \\
G &= \left(\prod_{t=1}^{n} R_t\right)^{1/n}
\end{aligned}$$

Taking logarithms of $G$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\ln G &= \frac{1}{n} \sum_{t=1}^{n} \ln R_t \\
G &= \exp\!\left(\frac{1}{n} \sum_{t=1}^{n} \ln R_t\right)
\end{aligned}$$

This is the closed-form geometric mean. $\blacksquare$

**Step Explanation:**
- Lines 1–2: Express the multiplicative wealth evolution.
- Lines 3–4: Impose equivalence to a constant compounded path.
- Lines 5–6: Solve for $G$ via $n$-th root.
- Lines 7–8: Transform to the additive log-domain, showing $G$ equals the exponential of the mean of log-returns.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Unbiasedness and Variance of the Sample Mean

**Statement:** Under i.i.d. assumptions with $\mathbb{E}[X_t] = \mu$ and $\text{Var}(X_t) = \sigma^2$:

(a) $\mathbb{E}[\bar{x}] = \mu$ (unbiasedness)

(b) $\text{Var}(\bar{x}) = \sigma^2 / n$

**Proof (a):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\mathbb{E}[\bar{x}] &= \mathbb{E}\!\left[\frac{1}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n} X_t\right] \\
&= \frac{1}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n} \mathbb{E}[X_t] \quad \text{(linearity of expectation)} \\
&= \frac{1}{n} \cdot n \cdot \mu = \mu \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}$$

**Proof (b):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Var}(\bar{x}) &= \text{Var}\!\left(\frac{1}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n} X_t\right) \\
&= \frac{1}{n^2}\sum_{t=1}^{n}\sum_{s=1}^{n} \text{Cov}(X_t, X_s) \\
&= \frac{1}{n^2}\sum_{t=1}^{n} \text{Var}(X_t) \quad \text{(zero cross-covariance, i.i.d.)} \\
&= \frac{1}{n^2} \cdot n \cdot \sigma^2 = \frac{\sigma^2}{n} \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: EWMA as Infinite Weighted Sum

**Statement:** The recursive EWMA $\mu_t = \lambda \mu_{t-1} + (1-\lambda) x_t$ equals the explicit form:

$$\mu_t = (1-\lambda) \sum_{j=0}^{\infty} \lambda^{j} x_{t-j}$$

**Proof by Infinite Back-Substitution:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\mu_t &= \lambda \mu_{t-1} + (1-\lambda) x_t \\
&= \lambda \bigl[\lambda \mu_{t-2} + (1-\lambda) x_{t-1}\bigr] + (1-\lambda) x_t \\
&= \lambda^2 \mu_{t-2} + (1-\lambda)(\lambda x_{t-1} + x_t) \\
&\;\;\vdots \\
&= \lambda^{k} \mu_{t-k} + (1-\lambda)\sum_{j=0}^{k-1} \lambda^{j} x_{t-j}
\end{aligned}$$

As $k \to \infty$, since $\vert \lambda \vert < 1$, the term $\lambda^{k}\mu_{t-k} \to 0$, yielding:

$$\mu_t = (1-\lambda) \sum_{j=0}^{\infty} \lambda^{j} x_{t-j} \quad \blacksquare$$

**Verification of weights:** The coefficients $(1-\lambda), (1-\lambda)\lambda, (1-\lambda)\lambda^2, \ldots$ form a geometric series summing to 1:

$$\sum_{j=0}^{\infty} (1-\lambda)\lambda^{j} = (1-\lambda) \cdot \frac{1}{1-\lambda} = 1$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Property | Arithmetic Mean | Geometric Mean | Harmonic Mean | EWMA |
|----------|----------------|----------------|----------------|------|
| Additive consistency | Yes | No | No | No |
| Multiplicative consistency | No | Yes | No | No |
| Outlier sensitivity | High | High (in log-space, moderate) | Very High | Moderate |
| Recency weighting | No | No | No | Yes ($\lambda$-controlled) |
| Computation cost | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ per update |
| Standard error | $\sigma/\sqrt{n}$ | Approximate | Approximate | $\sigma \sqrt{(1-\lambda)/(1+\lambda)}$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Taxonomy of Means (KTU-Aligned Mind Map)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Means in Financial Time Series] --> B[Unweighted Means]
    A --> C[Weighted Means]
    A --> D[Robust Means]

    B --> B1[Arithmetic Mean]
    B --> B2[Geometric Mean]
    B --> B3[Harmonic Mean]

    C --> C1[Weighted Arithmetic Mean]
    C --> C2[Exponentially Weighted Mean]
    C --> C3[Kernel Weighted Mean]

    D --> D1[Trimmed Mean]
    D --> D2[Winsorized Mean]
    D --> D3[Median]

    B1 --> E1[BLUE under i.i.d.]
    B2 --> E2[CAGR / Multi-period returns]
    B3 --> E3[P/E ratio averaging]
    C1 --> E4[Portfolio expected return]
    C2 --> E5[RiskMetrics volatility]
    C3 --> E6[Local regression smoothing]

    D2 --> E8[Bounded influence estimator]
    D3 --> E9[Distribution-free central value]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — EWMA Computation Pipeline

The Mermaid diagram below illustrates the recursive computational flow of the Exponentially Weighted Mean, which is the most exam-relevant weighted mean under the KTU 2024 scheme.

```mermaid
graph LR
    inputX[Input Observation x_t] --> mulA[Multiply by 1 minus lambda]
    prevMu[Previous Mean mu_t minus 1] --> mulB[Multiply by lambda]
    mulA --> sum[Adder Node]
    mulB --> sum
    sum --> newMu[New Mean mu_t]
    newMu --> store[Memory Register]
    store --> prevMu
    newMu --> outVar[Output to Variance Calculator]
    outVar --> ewmVar[EWMA Variance]
    ewmVar --> sigma[Square Root to get Sigma_t]
    sigma --> riskOut[RiskMetrics Daily Volatility]
```

## 4.3 Comparative Decision Flow — Which Mean to Use?

```mermaid
graph TD
    Q1{Data Type?} --> Q1a[Prices / Levels]
    Q1 --> Q1b[Returns / Growth Rates]
    Q1 --> Q1c[Ratios / Rates]

    Q1a --> P1{Need outlier robustness?}
    P1 --> P1a[No] --> A1[Use Arithmetic Mean]
    P1 --> P1b[Yes] --> A2[Use Trimmed Mean]

    Q1b --> P2{Compound or Simple?}
    P2 --> P2a[Multi-period] --> A3[Use Geometric Mean]
    P2 --> P2b[Single-period] --> A4[Use Arithmetic Mean]

    Q1c --> A5[Use Harmonic Mean]

    A1 --> fin[Final Choice]
    A2 --> fin
    A3 --> fin
    A4 --> fin
    A5 --> fin
```

## 4.4 Information Decay Curve — EWMA Weighting

The figure below shows how the EWM assigns exponentially decaying weights to past observations. The half-life is $t_{1/2} = \ln(0.5) / \ln(\lambda)$. For RiskMetrics standard $\lambda = 0.94$:

$$\begin{aligned}
t_{1/2} &= \frac{\ln(0.5)}{\ln(0.94)} \\
&= \frac{-0.6931}{-0.0619} \\
&\approx 11.2 \text{ days}
\end{aligned}$$

```mermaid
graph LR
    obsT[Observation at time t] --> w0[Weight w sub 0 equals 1 minus lambda]
    obsT1[Observation at time t minus 1] --> w1[Weight w sub 1 equals 1 minus lambda times lambda]
    obsT2[Observation at time t minus 2] --> w2[Weight w sub 2 equals 1 minus lambda times lambda squared]
    obsT3[Observation at time t minus 3] --> w3[Weight w sub 3 equals 1 minus lambda times lambda cubed]
    w0 --> sumDecay[Sum of Decaying Weights]
    w1 --> sumDecay
    w2 --> sumDecay
    w3 --> sumDecay
    sumDecay --> normCheck[Normalization Equals 1]
```

> **Note:** The diagram intentionally labels nodes without subscripts due to Mermaid's strict label-rendering constraints; mathematically, the weights form a geometric series with ratio $\lambda$.
