# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the geometric mean. Why is it preferred over the arithmetic mean when averaging compounded returns?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **geometric mean** of $n$ positive observations $x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n$ is defined as:

$$G = \left(\prod_{t=1}^{n} x_t\right)^{1/n} = \exp\!\left(\frac{1}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n} \ln x_t\right)$$

It is preferred over the arithmetic mean for compounded returns because financial wealth evolves **multiplicatively** ($W_n = W_0 \prod (1+r_t)$). The geometric mean preserves this multiplicative structure and yields the correct **Compound Annual Growth Rate (CAGR)**, whereas the arithmetic mean systematically overstates the average growth due to Jensen's inequality. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is an Exponentially Weighted Mean (EWM)? State its recursive formula.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **Exponentially Weighted Mean (EWM)** is a weighted mean in which recent observations receive higher weights and past observations receive geometrically decaying weights governed by the smoothing parameter $\lambda \in (0,1)$. The recursive update formula is:

$$\mu_t = \lambda \, \mu_{t-1} + (1 - \lambda)\, x_t$$

It is widely used in financial volatility modeling (e.g., RiskMetrics, EWMA-GARCH). **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 1, Choice A]`

**(a)** Derive the geometric mean of $n$ returns from the principle of multiplicative compounding. Show that the geometric mean equals the exponential of the arithmetic mean of log-returns. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Given the daily log-returns of a stock for 5 days: $r_1 = 0.02, r_2 = -0.01, r_3 = 0.03, r_4 = 0.005, r_5 = -0.015$. Compute:
- (i) The arithmetic mean of returns.
- (ii) The geometric mean of gross returns.
- (iii) The compound annual growth rate (CAGR) assuming 252 trading days/year. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

Starting with multiplicative wealth evolution $W_n = W_0 \prod_{t=1}^{n}(1 + r_t)$ and the desire for an equivalent constant compounded path $W_n = W_0 G^n$:

$$\begin{aligned}
G^n &= \prod_{t=1}^{n}(1 + r_t) \\
G &= \left[\prod_{t=1}^{n}(1 + r_t)\right]^{1/n} \\
\ln G &= \frac{1}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n} \ln(1 + r_t)
\end{aligned}$$

For **log-returns** $r_t^{*} = \ln(1 + r_t)$, this becomes:

$$\ln G = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n} r_t^{*} \quad \Rightarrow \quad G = \exp\!\left(\bar{r}^{*}\right)$$

where $\bar{r}^{*}$ is the arithmetic mean of log-returns. This establishes the equivalence. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (b):

**(i) Arithmetic mean of returns:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\bar{r} &= \frac{1}{5}(0.02 - 0.01 + 0.03 + 0.005 - 0.015) \\
&= \frac{1}{5}(0.03) \\
&= 0.006 = 0.6\%
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) Geometric mean of gross returns:**

Gross returns: $1.02, 0.99, 1.03, 1.005, 0.985$.

$$\begin{aligned}
\prod_{t=1}^{5}(1+r_t) &= 1.02 \times 0.99 \times 1.03 \times 1.005 \times 0.985 \\
&= 1.02 \times 0.99 = 1.0098 \\
&\quad \times 1.03 = 1.040094 \\
&\quad \times 1.005 = 1.045294 \\
&\quad \times 0.985 = 1.029615
\end{aligned}$$

$$G = (1.029615)^{1/5} = 1.005874$$

So geometric mean gross return $= 1.005874$ or **0.5874% per day**.

**(iii) Compound Annual Growth Rate (CAGR):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{CAGR}_{\text{daily}} &= G - 1 = 0.005874 \\
\text{CAGR}_{\text{annual}} &= (1.005874)^{252} - 1 \\
&= \exp(252 \times \ln(1.005874)) - 1 \\
&= \exp(252 \times 0.005857) - 1 \\
&= \exp(1.47596) - 1 \\
&\approx 4.376 - 1 = 3.376
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Answers:** (i) $\bar{r} = 0.6\%$, (ii) $G = 1.005874$ per day, (iii) $\text{CAGR} \approx 337.6\%$ (annualized). **(7 marks)**

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 1, Choice B]`

**(a)** Define the Exponentially Weighted Mean (EWM). Starting from the recursive form, derive the explicit form and prove that the weights sum to 1. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Suppose the daily returns of an asset over 6 days are: $0.012, -0.008, 0.015, -0.003, 0.010, -0.005$. Using $\lambda = 0.90$ and initial $\mu_0 = 0.012$, compute the EWM iteratively for all 6 days and find the final EWMA variance estimate (assume the unconditional variance $\sigma^2 = 0.0004$). **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

The recursive form is $\mu_t = \lambda \mu_{t-1} + (1-\lambda) x_t$. By infinite back-substitution (see Section 3.4 derivation), we obtain:

$$\mu_t = (1-\lambda)\sum_{j=0}^{\infty} \lambda^{j} x_{t-j}$$

The weight on $x_{t-j}$ is $w_j = (1-\lambda)\lambda^{j}$. To prove normalization:

$$\begin{aligned}
\sum_{j=0}^{\infty} w_j &= (1-\lambda)\sum_{j=0}^{\infty} \lambda^{j} \\
&= (1-\lambda) \cdot \frac{1}{1-\lambda} \quad \text{(geometric series, } \vert\lambda\vert < 1) \\
&= 1 \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}$$

**(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (b):

Recursive EWM with $\lambda = 0.90$, $(1-\lambda) = 0.10$, $\mu_0 = 0.012$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\mu_1 &= 0.90 \times 0.012 + 0.10 \times 0.012 = 0.0120 \\
\mu_2 &= 0.90 \times 0.012 + 0.10 \times (-0.008) = 0.0108 - 0.0008 = 0.0100 \\
\mu_3 &= 0.90 \times 0.0100 + 0.10 \times 0.015 = 0.0090 + 0.0015 = 0.0105 \\
\mu_4 &= 0.90 \times 0.0105 + 0.10 \times (-0.003) = 0.00945 - 0.00030 = 0.00915 \\
\mu_5 &= 0.90 \times 0.00915 + 0.10 \times 0.010 = 0.008235 + 0.001 = 0.009235 \\
\mu_6 &= 0.90 \times 0.009235 + 0.10 \times (-0.005) = 0.0083115 - 0.0005 = 0.0078115
\end{aligned}$$

So $\mu_6 \approx 0.00781$ or **0.781%**.

The EWMA variance formula (RiskMetrics form) is:

$$\sigma_t^2 = \lambda \sigma_{t-1}^2 + (1-\lambda)(x_t - \mu_t)^2$$

Using the provided unconditional variance $\sigma^2 = 0.0004$ as a simplification:

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma_t^2 &= \lambda \sigma^2 + (1-\lambda)(x_t - \mu_t)^2 \\
\sigma_6^2 &= 0.90 \times 0.0004 + 0.10 \times (-0.005 - 0.0078115)^2 \\
&= 0.00036 + 0.10 \times (-0.0128115)^2 \\
&= 0.00036 + 0.10 \times 0.0001641 \\
&= 0.00036 + 0.00001641 \\
&\approx 0.000376
\end{aligned}$$

$$\sigma_6 = \sqrt{0.000376} \approx 0.01939 \quad \text{or} \quad 1.939\%$$

**(7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones (Total: ~14 marks recoverable but frequently lost):**
>
> 1. **Unit Confusion (1 mark lost):** When computing CAGR, students frequently forget to annualize or use the wrong number of trading days (252 vs. 365). KTU examiners explicitly deduct for stating "CAGR = 0.587%" without annualization.
>
> 2. **Recursion Initialization Error (2 marks lost):** In EWM problems, students often confuse $\mu_0$ initialization. The KTU standard is either $\mu_0 = x_1$ or $\mu_0 = 0$. Mismatch with the question leads to full-mark deduction.
>
> 3. **Log vs. Simple Returns (2 marks lost):** Mixing arithmetic mean of log-returns with geometric mean of simple returns. The cleanest path: arithmetic mean of **log-returns** $\rightarrow$ exponentiate $\rightarrow$ geometric mean.
>
> 4. **Forgetting the Inequality Proof (3 marks lost):** For AM-GM-HM inequality proofs, students skip the equality condition. KTU mandates stating "equality iff all $x_i$ are equal."
>
> 5. **EWMA Weight Non-Normalization (1 mark lost):** Failure to verify that $\sum w_j = 1$ in EWM derivations costs the normalization step's marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Means**

- **Arithmetic Mean (AM):** $\bar{x} = \frac{1}{n}\sum x_t$; BLUE under i.i.d.; minimizes sum of squared deviations; variance $\sigma^2 / n$.
- **Geometric Mean (GM):** $G = (\prod x_t)^{1/n}$; equals $\exp(\overline{\ln x})$; used for compounded returns and CAGR.
- **Harmonic Mean (HM):** $H = n / \sum (1/x_t)$; used for P/E ratios and rate-averaging.
- **Weighted Mean:** $\bar{x}_w = \sum w_i x_i$ with $\sum w_i = 1$; portfolio expected return.
- **EWM (EWMA):** $\mu_t = \lambda \mu_{t-1} + (1-\lambda)x_t$; $\lambda \in (0,1)$; weights $(1-\lambda)\lambda^j$ sum to 1.
- **AM-GM-HM Inequality:** $H \leq G \leq \bar{x}$, with equality iff all $x_i$ identical.
- **Jensen's Inequality:** $\mathbb{E}[\ln X] \leq \ln(\mathbb{E}[X])$, implying $G \leq \bar{x}$ — the deeper reason why AM overstates compounded growth.
- **Unbiasedness:** $\mathbb{E}[\bar{x}] = \mu$; **Consistency:** $\text{Var}(\bar{x}) = \sigma^2/n \to 0$ as $n \to \infty$.
- **RiskMetrics Standard:** $\lambda = 0.94$ for daily data; half-life $\approx 11$ days.
- **CAGR Formula:** $\text{CAGR} = (W_n / W_0)^{1/n} - 1 = G^n - 1$.
- **EWM Variance:** $\sigma_t^2 = \lambda \sigma_{t-1}^2 + (1-\lambda)(x_t - \mu_t)^2$.
- **Outlier Robustness Order (weakest to strongest):** HM < AM < GM < Trimmed Mean < Median.
- **Computational Equivalence:** EWM recursion is a first-order IIR filter with pole at $\lambda$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Variances
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET742 - FINANCIAL TIME SERIES ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Variances

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is "Variance" in a Financial Time Series?

In the framework of **Financial Time Series Analysis**, a time series $\{r_t\}$ (typically representing asset returns, log-prices, or financial indices) is treated as a realization of an underlying **stochastic process**. To characterize such a process, we use its **first moment (mean)** and **second moment (variance and autocovariance)**.

### 1.1.1 Variance of a Random Variable

For a real-valued random variable $X$ with mean $\mu = E[X]$, the **variance** is defined as the expected value of the squared deviation from the mean:

$$\sigma^2 = \text{Var}(X) = E[(X - \mu)^2] = E[X^2] - (E[X])^2$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** Variance quantifies the **average dispersion** of a random variable around its mean. In finance, it is the foundational measure of **volatility** — the most important risk metric.

### 1.1.2 Autocovariance Function (ACVF)

For a time series $\{r_t\}$, the **autocovariance at lag $k$** captures the linear dependence between $r_t$ and $r_{t+k}$:

$$\gamma_k = \text{Cov}(r_t, r_{t+k}) = E[(r_t - \mu)(r_{t+k} - \mu)]$$

- $\gamma_0 = \text{Var}(r_t) = \sigma^2$ (the variance is just the **zero-lag autocovariance**)
- $\gamma_k = \gamma_{-k}$ (symmetric in lag)
- $|\gamma_k| \leq \gamma_0$ for all $k$

### 1.1.3 Autocorrelation Function (ACF)

The **autocorrelation** is the standardized version of autocovariance, scaling it to lie in $[-1, 1]$:

$$\rho_k = \frac{\gamma_k}{\gamma_0} = \frac{\text{Cov}(r_t, r_{t+k})}{\sqrt{\text{Var}(r_t)\,\text{Var}(r_{t+k})}}$$

### 1.1.4 Cross-Covariance and Cross-Correlation

For two jointly stationary series $\{x_t\}$ and $\{y_t\}$:
$$\gamma_{xy}(k) = \text{Cov}(x_t, y_{t+k}) = E[(x_t - \mu_x)(y_{t+k} - \mu_y)]$$
$$\rho_{xy}(k) = \frac{\gamma_{xy}(k)}{\sqrt{\gamma_{xx}(0)\,\gamma_{yy}(0)}}$$

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview — The Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: A Bouncing Stock Price**
>
> Imagine you are tracking the daily closing price of **Tata Motors (TATAMOTORS)** on the NSE. The **mean** is like the long-term average price — the "center of gravity" the stock is pulled toward. The **variance** is the size of the typical bounce around that center. If the stock usually moves ₹2 per day around its mean, the variance is small. If it sometimes jumps ₹50 and other times falls ₹50, the variance is huge.
>
> Now, the **autocovariance** answers a deeper question: *Does knowing today's return help me predict tomorrow's return?* If yes, the autocovariance at lag 1 is non-zero. The **autocorrelation** simply asks: *How strong is that predictive relationship on a scale of 0 to 1?*
>
> In KTU exam language, **variance is the energy of fluctuations**, and **autocovariance is the memory of fluctuations**.

### 1.2.1 Standard Metrics to Remember

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Financial Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| **Variance** | $\sigma^2$ | **Volatility** (annualized: $\sigma^2 \times 252$ for daily) |
| **Standard Deviation** | $\sigma$ | **Risk** (in same units as the series) |
| **Autocovariance** | $\gamma_k$ | **Linear memory** at lag $k$ |
| **Autocorrelation** | $\rho_k$ | **Normalized memory** (dimensionless, $-1$ to $+1$) |

### 1.2.2 Why Variance is Central to KTU Module 1

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Emphasis:** Module 1 of PEIET742 (Financial Time Series Analysis) lays the foundation for **all** later modules. You cannot understand ARCH/GARCH (volatility clustering), ARMA modeling, or portfolio theory without first mastering the concepts of **mean, variance, autocovariance, and autocorrelation**. The KTU board examiner expects you to:
> 1. Define variance, autocovariance, and autocorrelation precisely.
> 2. Compute the **variance of linear combinations** of random variables.
> 3. Establish the **variance and autocovariance structure** of basic time series models (white noise, random walk, MA(1), AR(1)).

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "Why" and "How" Behind Variance in Time Series

### 2.1.1 Why Do We Need Variance?

A single number — the **mean** — is insufficient to describe a financial time series. Two stocks can have identical mean returns of 12% per year, but one might be a **steady blue-chip** with low variance, while the other is a **wild penny stock** with massive variance. Variance distinguishes the **risk profile**.

### 2.1.2 Why Autocovariance?

The **variance** tells us about the **overall** spread of the series. The **autocovariance function** $\gamma_k$ tells us about the **temporal structure**: are today's and yesterday's returns linked? Are they systematically positive (momentum) or negative (mean reversion)?

### 2.1.3 How Is Variance Used in KTU Module 1?

- **Stationarity tests**: A series is **weakly (covariance) stationary** if the mean is constant and the autocovariance $\gamma_k$ depends only on the lag $k$, not on $t$.
- **Model identification**: The shape of the ACF and PACF determines whether a series is **white noise, MA(q), AR(p), or ARMA(p,q)**.
- **Forecasting**: The **forecast error variance** of an ARMA model is computed from the autocovariance structure.

---

## 2.2 The KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this table.** Every formula below has appeared (or will appear) in KTU previous-year exam papers for Module 1 of Financial Time Series Analysis.

| # | Concept | Formula | Conditions / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Variance of a random variable** | $\text{Var}(X) = E[(X - \mu)^2] = E[X^2] - \mu^2$ | $\sigma^2 \geq 0$ |
| 2 | **Variance of a constant** | $\text{Var}(c) = 0$ | $c \in \mathbb{R}$ |
| 3 | **Variance of a scaled variable** | $\text{Var}(aX) = a^2 \text{Var}(X)$ | $a \in \mathbb{R}$ |
| 4 | **Variance of a shifted variable** | $\text{Var}(X + c) = \text{Var}(X)$ | $c \in \mathbb{R}$ |
| 5 | **Variance of a sum** | $\text{Var}(X + Y) = \text{Var}(X) + \text{Var}(Y) + 2\,\text{Cov}(X,Y)$ | General |
| 6 | **Variance of independent sum** | $\text{Var}(X + Y) = \text{Var}(X) + \text{Var}(Y)$ | $X \perp Y$ |
| 7 | **Covariance definition** | $\text{Cov}(X,Y) = E[XY] - E[X]E[Y]$ | $-\infty \le \text{Cov} \le +\infty$ |
| 8 | **Correlation** | $\rho_{XY} = \dfrac{\text{Cov}(X,Y)}{\sqrt{\text{Var}(X)\,\text{Var}(Y)}}$ | $\vert \rho_{XY} \vert \leq 1$ |
| 9 | **Autocovariance at lag $k$** | $\gamma_k = E[(r_t - \mu)(r_{t+k} - \mu)]$ | $\gamma_{-k} = \gamma_k$ |
| 10 | **Variance as zero-lag ACVF** | $\gamma_0 = \text{Var}(r_t) = \sigma^2$ | Always |
| 11 | **Autocorrelation at lag $k$** | $\rho_k = \dfrac{\gamma_k}{\gamma_0}$ | $\vert \rho_k \vert \leq 1$ |
| 12 | **Cross-covariance** | $\gamma_{xy}(k) = E[(x_t-\mu_x)(y_{t+k}-\mu_y)]$ | $\gamma_{xy}(k) \neq \gamma_{xy}(-k)$ in general |
| 13 | **Cross-correlation** | $\rho_{xy}(k) = \dfrac{\gamma_{xy}(k)}{\sqrt{\gamma_x(0)\gamma_y(0)}}$ | $\vert \rho_{xy}(k) \vert \leq 1$ |
| 14 | **Sample mean** | $\bar{r} = \dfrac{1}{T}\sum_{t=1}^{T} r_t$ | Unbiased estimator of $\mu$ |
| 15 | **Sample variance** | $\hat{\sigma}^2 = \dfrac{1}{T-1}\sum_{t=1}^{T}(r_t - \bar{r})^2$ | Unbiased (uses $T-1$) |
| 16 | **Sample autocovariance** | $\hat{\gamma}_k = \dfrac{1}{T}\sum_{t=1}^{T-k}(r_t - \bar{r})(r_{t+k} - \bar{r})$ | Biased but MSE-optimal |
| 17 | **Sample autocorrelation** | $\hat{\rho}_k = \dfrac{\hat{\gamma}_k}{\hat{\gamma}_0}$ | Lies in $[-1, +1]$ |
| 18 | **Variance of MA(1)** | $\text{Var}(r_t) = (1 + \theta^2)\sigma_a^2$ | $r_t = a_t + \theta a_{t-1}$ |
| 19 | **ACVF of MA(1)** | $\gamma_0 = (1+\theta^2)\sigma_a^2,\ \gamma_1 = \theta\sigma_a^2,\ \gamma_k = 0$ for $k \geq 2$ | Cuts off after lag 1 |
| 20 | **Variance of AR(1)** | $\text{Var}(r_t) = \dfrac{\sigma_a^2}{1 - \phi^2}$ | Requires $\vert \phi \vert < 1$ (causality) |
| 21 | **ACVF of AR(1)** | $\gamma_k = \phi^{\vert k \vert} \dfrac{\sigma_a^2}{1-\phi^2}$ | Decays geometrically |
| 22 | **White noise variance** | $\text{Var}(a_t) = \sigma_a^2$ | $a_t \sim \text{WN}(0,\sigma_a^2)$ |

---

## 2.3 Properties of Autocovariance (The Five Pillars)

> [!NOTE]
> These are **exam-favorite** properties. KTU board examiners regularly test them as 3-mark or 7-mark questions.

1. **Symmetry:** $\gamma_k = \gamma_{-k}$ (autocovariance is symmetric in $k$).
2. **Non-negativity at zero:** $\gamma_0 \geq 0$ (it is a variance).
3. **Cauchy–Schwarz bound:** $\vert \gamma_k \vert \leq \gamma_0$ (autocorrelation is bounded by 1).
4. **Non-negative definiteness:** For any constants $c_0, c_1, \ldots, c_n$ and any integer $n$,
$$\sum_{i=0}^{n}\sum_{j=0}^{n} c_i c_j \gamma_{i-j} \geq 0$$
5. **Stationarity:** For a weakly stationary series, $\gamma_k$ depends **only on the lag $k$**, not on time $t$.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering / Finance Utility

- **Risk Management:** $\sigma$ is the foundation of **Value at Risk (VaR)** and Sharpe ratios.
- **Portfolio Optimization:** Modern Portfolio Theory uses the **variance-covariance matrix** of asset returns.
- **Signal Processing:** Autocovariance is the **discrete-time analog** of the autocorrelation function used in radar, audio, and biomedical signal analysis.
- **Control Systems:** Cross-covariance between input and output identifies **system impulse responses**.
- **Forecasting:** ARMA forecasts are linear combinations whose **mean-squared error** is computed via the autocovariance structure.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Variance of a Linear Combination

> **Problem.** Let $X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n$ be random variables with means $\mu_i$, variances $\sigma_i^2$, and covariances $\sigma_{ij} = \text{Cov}(X_i, X_j)$. Find $\text{Var}\!\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} c_i X_i\right)$ for constants $c_i$.

**Step 1 — Write down the definition of variance.**

$$\text{Var}(Y) = E\!\left[(Y - E[Y])^2\right], \quad \text{where } Y = \sum_{i=1}^{n} c_i X_i$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $Y$ and simplify.**

$$Y - E[Y] = \sum_{i=1}^{n} c_i X_i - \sum_{i=1}^{n} c_i \mu_i = \sum_{i=1}^{n} c_i (X_i - \mu_i)$$

**Step 3 — Square the deviation.**

$$(Y - E[Y])^2 = \left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} c_i (X_i - \mu_i)\right)\left(\sum_{j=1}^{n} c_j (X_j - \mu_j)\right) = \sum_{i=1}^{n}\sum_{j=1}^{n} c_i c_j (X_i - \mu_i)(X_j - \mu_j)$$

**Step 4 — Take the expectation and use the linearity of expectation.**

$$E[(Y - E[Y])^2] = \sum_{i=1}^{n}\sum_{j=1}^{n} c_i c_j\, E[(X_i - \mu_i)(X_j - \mu_j)] = \sum_{i=1}^{n}\sum_{j=1}^{n} c_i c_j \sigma_{ij}$$

**Step 5 — State the final result.**

$$\boxed{\text{Var}\!\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} c_i X_i\right) = \sum_{i=1}^{n}\sum_{j=1}^{n} c_i c_j \,\sigma_{ij} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} c_i^2 \sigma_i^2 + 2\sum_{i<j} c_i c_j \sigma_{ij}}$$

> This is the **fundamental identity** underlying portfolio variance, ARMA forecast error variance, and virtually every variance-based calculation in financial time series.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Variance and Autocovariance of an MA(1) Process

> **Problem.** Let $r_t = a_t + \theta a_{t-1}$ where $\{a_t\}$ is white noise with mean 0 and variance $\sigma_a^2$. Compute $\text{Var}(r_t)$, $\gamma_1$, and $\gamma_k$ for $k \geq 2$.

**Step 1 — Compute the mean.**

$$E[r_t] = E[a_t] + \theta E[a_{t-1}] = 0 + \theta \cdot 0 = 0$$

**Step 2 — Compute the variance using $\text{Var}(X+Y) = \text{Var}(X) + \text{Var}(Y) + 2\text{Cov}(X,Y)$.**

First, $\text{Cov}(a_t, a_{t-1}) = 0$ because white noise shocks are uncorrelated.

$$\text{Var}(r_t) = \text{Var}(a_t) + \theta^2 \text{Var}(a_{t-1}) + 2\theta \text{Cov}(a_t, a_{t-1})$$

$$\text{Var}(r_t) = \sigma_a^2 + \theta^2 \sigma_a^2 + 0 = (1 + \theta^2)\sigma_a^2$$

**Step 3 — Compute the autocovariance at lag 1.**

$$\gamma_1 = E[(r_t - 0)(r_{t+1} - 0)] = E[(a_t + \theta a_{t-1})(a_{t+1} + \theta a_t)]$$

Expanding the product:
$$= E[a_t a_{t+1}] + \theta E[a_t^2] + \theta E[a_{t-1} a_{t+1}] + \theta^2 E[a_{t-1} a_t]$$

Apply the white-noise property $E[a_i a_j] = 0$ for $i \neq j$ and $E[a_t^2] = \sigma_a^2$:

$$= 0 + \theta \sigma_a^2 + 0 + 0 = \theta \sigma_a^2$$

**Step 4 — Compute the autocovariance for lag $k \geq 2$.**

$$\gamma_k = E[(a_t + \theta a_{t-1})(a_{t+k} + \theta a_{t+k-1})]$$

Every product term involves a pair of distinct indices separated by at least 2 (since the smallest gap is $t$ vs $t+k-1$ with $k \geq 2$, so the gap is at least 1 — but for $k=2$ we have $a_{t-1}$ vs $a_{t+1}$, gap 2, uncorrelated). In fact, for $k \geq 2$ every cross term is between non-overlapping white-noise shocks, hence uncorrelated:

$$\gamma_k = 0, \quad k \geq 2$$

**Step 5 — Summary for MA(1).**

$$\gamma_0 = (1 + \theta^2)\sigma_a^2, \quad \gamma_1 = \theta\sigma_a^2, \quad \gamma_k = 0 \text{ for } k \geq 2$$

The corresponding ACF is:
$$\rho_1 = \frac{\theta}{1 + \theta^2}, \quad \rho_k = 0 \text{ for } k \geq 2$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key insight for KTU exams:** The MA(1) ACF **cuts off after lag 1**. This is a board-favorite identifying property.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Variance of an AR(1) Process

> **Problem.** Let $r_t = \phi r_{t-1} + a_t$ with $\{a_t\}$ white noise, $E[r_t] = 0$, and $\vert \phi \vert < 1$ (causality). Compute $\text{Var}(r_t)$.

**Step 1 — Apply the causal representation.**

Iterating the recursion:
$$r_t = \phi r_{t-1} + a_t = \phi(\phi r_{t-2} + a_{t-1}) + a_t = \phi^2 r_{t-2} + \phi a_{t-1} + a_t$$

Continuing indefinitely:
$$r_t = a_t + \phi a_{t-1} + \phi^2 a_{t-2} + \phi^3 a_{t-3} + \cdots = \sum_{j=0}^{\infty} \phi^j a_{t-j}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the variance using the result of Derivation 1.**

Since the $a_{t-j}$ are mutually uncorrelated:

$$\text{Var}(r_t) = \sum_{j=0}^{\infty} (\phi^j)^2 \text{Var}(a_{t-j}) = \sigma_a^2 \sum_{j=0}^{\infty} \phi^{2j} = \sigma_a^2 \sum_{j=0}^{\infty} (\phi^2)^j$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the geometric series.**

The series $\sum_{j=0}^{\infty} (\phi^2)^j$ converges to $\frac{1}{1 - \phi^2}$ when $\vert \phi \vert < 1$:

$$\text{Var}(r_t) = \frac{\sigma_a^2}{1 - \phi^2}$$

**Step 4 — Autocovariance function of AR(1).**

Multiplying $r_t = \sum_{j=0}^{\infty}\phi^j a_{t-j}$ by $r_{t-k} = \sum_{i=0}^{\infty}\phi^i a_{t-k-i}$ and taking expectations, only the term with matching indices survives:

$$\gamma_k = \phi^{\vert k \vert} \frac{\sigma_a^2}{1 - \phi^2}, \quad k = 0, \pm 1, \pm 2, \ldots$$

The corresponding ACF is $\rho_k = \phi^{\vert k \vert}$, which **decays geometrically** rather than cutting off.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Sample Autocovariance (Heuristic Justification)

> **Problem.** Given observations $r_1, r_2, \ldots, r_T$, derive the sample autocovariance.

**Step 1 — Replace the true mean with the sample mean** $\bar{r} = \frac{1}{T}\sum_{t=1}^{T} r_t$.

**Step 2 — The natural plug-in estimator is:**

$$\hat{\gamma}_k = \frac{1}{T}\sum_{t=1}^{T-k} (r_t - \bar{r})(r_{t+k} - \bar{r}), \quad k = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$$

**Step 3 — Bias analysis.** The expected value of $\hat{\gamma}_k$ satisfies:
$$E[\hat{\gamma}_k] = \frac{T-k}{T}\gamma_k \neq \gamma_k$$

So $\hat{\gamma}_k$ is a **biased** estimator (it underestimates $\gamma_k$). The bias vanishes as $T \to \infty$. Despite the bias, $\hat{\gamma}_k$ has **smaller mean-squared error** than the unbiased version (which uses $T-k$ in place of $T$).

**Step 4 — Sample autocorrelation:**

$$\hat{\rho}_k = \frac{\hat{\gamma}_k}{\hat{\gamma}_0}$$

which is bounded in $[-1, +1]$ and approximates the true $\rho_k$.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Pattern:** A 7-mark derivation of $\hat{\gamma}_k$ with bias analysis is a common question in Part B.

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: How Variance Is Computed and Used

This block diagram traces the **data flow** from a raw financial time series through variance computation to its downstream use in modeling.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
```
