# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 1]**

### Question 1
**Define autocovariance and autocorrelation functions of a stationary time series. State any three properties of the autocovariance function.**

**Model Answer:**

> The **autocovariance function (ACVF)** of a stationary time series $\{r_t\}$ at lag $k$ is defined as:
> $$\gamma_k = \text{Cov}(r_t, r_{t+k}) = E[(r_t - \mu)(r_{t+k} - \mu)]$$
>
> The **autocorrelation function (ACF)** is the normalized autocovariance:
> $$\rho_k = \frac{\gamma_k}{\gamma_0}, \quad \text{where } \gamma_0 = \text{Var}(r_t)$$
>
> **Three key properties of the ACVF:**
> 1. **Symmetry:** $\gamma_k = \gamma_{-k}$.
> 2. **Non-negativity at origin:** $\gamma_0 = \text{Var}(r_t) \geq 0$.
> 3. **Boundedness:** $\vert \gamma_k \vert \leq \gamma_0$ (so $\vert \rho_k \vert \leq 1$).
> 4. *(Bonus)* **Non-negative definiteness:** $\sum_{i}\sum_{j} c_i c_j \gamma_{i-j} \geq 0$ for any constants $c_i$.

*(3 marks — full allocation: 1 for definition of ACVF, 1 for definition of ACF, 1 for properties.)*

---

### Question 2
**Differentiate between white noise and a random walk in terms of their variance behavior over time.**

**Model Answer:**

| Property | White Noise $\{a_t\}$ | Random Walk $\{r_t\}$ |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | $a_t$ are i.i.d. with mean 0, variance $\sigma_a^2$ | $r_t = r_{t-1} + a_t$ |
| **Mean** | $E[a_t] = 0$ | $E[r_t] = r_0$ (a constant) |
| **Variance** | $\text{Var}(a_t) = \sigma_a^2$ **constant** | $\text{Var}(r_t) = t\sigma_a^2$ **grows linearly with $t$** |
| **Stationarity** | Weakly stationary | **Not** stationary (variance is time-dependent) |

> **Conclusion:** White noise is covariance-stationary because its variance is time-invariant, whereas a random walk is **non-stationary** because its variance **diverges** as $t$ increases.

*(3 marks — full allocation: 1 for definitions, 1 for variance comparison, 1 for stationarity conclusion.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Answer **any one** full question from Module 1. Each question has two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

### Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 1, Module Internal Choice Option A]**

**(a)** Define the autocovariance and autocorrelation functions of a weakly stationary time series. Show that the autocorrelation function $\rho_k$ satisfies $\vert \rho_k \vert \leq 1$ for all $k$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider the MA(1) model $r_t = a_t + \theta a_{t-1}$ where $\{a_t\}$ is white noise with mean 0 and variance $\sigma_a^2$. Derive the mean, variance, and autocovariance function of $r_t$. Hence obtain the autocorrelation function and show that it cuts off after lag 1. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Definition of ACVF:** For a weakly stationary time series $\{r_t\}$ with constant mean $\mu$, the autocovariance at lag $k$ is

$$\gamma_k = \text{Cov}(r_t, r_{t+k}) = E[(r_t - \mu)(r_{t+k} - \mu)]$$

**Definition of ACF:** The autocorrelation at lag $k$ is

$$\rho_k = \frac{\gamma_k}{\gamma_0} = \frac{\text{Cov}(r_t, r_{t+k})}{\sqrt{\text{Var}(r_t)\,\text{Var}(r_{t+k})}}$$

**Proof that $\vert \rho_k \vert \leq 1$:**

By the **Cauchy–Schwarz inequality**, for any two random variables $X$ and $Y$ with finite, non-zero variances:

$$\vert E[XY] \vert \leq \sqrt{E[X^2]\,E[Y^2]}$$

Apply this with $X = r_t - \mu$ and $Y = r_{t+k} - \mu$:

$$\vert E[(r_t - \mu)(r_{t+k} - \mu)] \vert \leq \sqrt{E[(r_t - \mu)^2]\,E[(r_{t+k} - \mu)^2]}$$

Recognize:
- $E[(r_t - \mu)(r_{t+k} - \mu)] = \gamma_k$
- $E[(r_t - \mu)^2] = \text{Var}(r_t) = \gamma_0$
- $E[(r_{t+k} - \mu)^2] = \text{Var}(r_{t+k}) = \gamma_0$ (by stationarity)

Therefore:
$$\vert \gamma_k \vert \leq \sqrt{\gamma_0 \cdot \gamma_0} = \gamma_0$$

Dividing both sides by $\gamma_0 > 0$:

$$\boxed{\vert \rho_k \vert = \frac{\vert \gamma_k \vert}{\gamma_0} \leq 1}$$

**Equality condition:** $\vert \rho_k \vert = 1$ if and only if $r_{t+k} = a \cdot r_t + b$ almost surely (perfect linear relationship).

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

Given $r_t = a_t + \theta a_{t-1}$ with $E[a_t] = 0$, $\text{Var}(a_t) = \sigma_a^2$, and $E[a_i a_j] = 0$ for $i \neq j$.

**Step 1 — Mean:**
$$E[r_t] = E[a_t] + \theta E[a_{t-1}] = 0 + \theta \cdot 0 = 0$$

**Step 2 — Variance:**
$$\text{Var}(r_t) = \text{Var}(a_t + \theta a_{t-1}) = \text{Var}(a_t) + \theta^2 \text{Var}(a_{t-1}) + 2\theta \text{Cov}(a_t, a_{t-1})$$

Since $\text{Cov}(a_t, a_{t-1}) = 0$:
$$\text{Var}(r_t) = \sigma_a^2 + \theta^2 \sigma_a^2 = (1 + \theta^2)\sigma_a^2$$

**Step 3 — Autocovariance at lag 0:**
$$\gamma_0 = (1 + \theta^2)\sigma_a^2$$

**Step 4 — Autocovariance at lag 1:**
$$\gamma_1 = E[r_t r_{t+1}] = E[(a_t + \theta a_{t-1})(a_{t+1} + \theta a_t)]$$
$$= E[a_t a_{t+1}] + \theta E[a_t^2] + \theta E[a_{t-1} a_{t+1}] + \theta^2 E[a_{t-1} a_t]$$
$$= 0 + \theta \sigma_a^2 + 0 + 0 = \theta \sigma_a^2$$

**Step 5 — Autocovariance at lag $k \geq 2$:**
$$\gamma_k = E[(a_t + \theta a_{t-1})(a_{t+k} + \theta a_{t+k-1})]$$

Every term involves a product of two white-noise shocks with indices differing by **at least 2** when $k \geq 2$. For instance, $a_{t-1} \cdot a_{t+1}$ has indices separated by 2, $a_t \cdot a_{t+2}$ by 2, etc. All such products have zero expectation:

$$\gamma_k = 0 \quad \text{for } k \geq 2$$

**Step 6 — Autocorrelation function:**

$$\rho_0 = 1, \quad \rho_1 = \frac{\theta \sigma_a^2}{(1 + \theta^2)\sigma_a^2} = \frac{\theta}{1 + \theta^2}, \quad \rho_k = 0 \text{ for } k \geq 2$$

**Conclusion:** The ACF of the MA(1) process is non-zero only at lags 0 and 1, and **cuts off after lag 1**. This is the **identifying signature** of an MA(1) model.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 1, Module Internal Choice Option B]**

**(a)** State and prove the general result for the variance of a linear combination $\sum_{i=1}^{n} c_i X_i$ of random variables. Apply this result to compute the variance of a 2-asset portfolio $P = w_1 X_1 + w_2 X_2$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider the AR(1) model $r_t = \phi r_{t-1} + a_t$ with $\vert \phi \vert < 1$ and $\{a_t\} \sim WN(0, \sigma_a^2)$. Show that the variance of $r_t$ is $\frac{\sigma_a^2}{1 - \phi^2}$ and that the autocovariance function decays as $\gamma_k = \phi^{\vert k \vert} \frac{\sigma_a^2}{1 - \phi^2}$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Statement:** For random variables $X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n$ with means $\mu_i$ and covariances $\sigma_{ij} = \text{Cov}(X_i, X_j)$:

$$\text{Var}\!\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} c_i X_i\right) = \sum_{i=1}^{n}\sum_{j=1}^{n} c_i c_j \sigma_{ij}$$

**Proof:** [See Section 3.1 of these notes for the full 5-step derivation.] The key result is:

$$\boxed{\text{Var}\!\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} c_i X_i\right) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} c_i^2 \sigma_i^2 + 2\sum_{i<j} c_i c_j \sigma_{ij}}$$

**Application to 2-asset portfolio:**

For $P = w_1 X_1 + w_2 X_2$:

$$\text{Var}(P) = w_1^2 \sigma_1^2 + w_2^2 \sigma_2^2 + 2 w_1 w_2 \sigma_{12}$$

where $\sigma_{12} = \rho_{12} \sigma_1 \sigma_2$ is the covariance between the two asset returns. Hence:

$$\text{Var}(P) = w_1^2 \sigma_1^2 + w_2^2 \sigma_2^2 + 2 w_1 w_2 \rho_{12} \sigma_1 \sigma_2$$

This is the **Markowitz portfolio variance formula** — the foundation of Modern Portfolio Theory.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Causal representation:** Since $\vert \phi \vert < 1$, the AR(1) process has a convergent infinite-past representation (derived in Section 3.3):

$$r_t = \sum_{j=0}^{\infty} \phi^j a_{t-j}$$

**Variance computation:**

Since the shocks $\{a_{t-j}\}$ are mutually uncorrelated:

$$\text{Var}(r_t) = \sum_{j=0}^{\infty} \phi^{2j} \text{Var}(a_{t-j}) = \sigma_a^2 \sum_{j=0}^{\infty} (\phi^2)^j$$

Using the geometric series formula $\sum_{j=0}^{\infty} r^j = \frac{1}{1-r}$ for $\vert r \vert < 1$, with $r = \phi^2$:

$$\text{Var}(r_t) = \frac{\sigma_a^2}{1 - \phi^2}$$

**Autocovariance function:**

$$\gamma_k = E[r_t r_{t+k}] = E\!\left[\left(\sum_{j=0}^{\infty} \phi^j a_{t-j}\right)\left(\sum_{i=0}^{\infty} \phi^i a_{t+k-i}\right)\right]$$

The only non-zero product occurs when the indices match, i.e., $t - j = t + k - i$, giving $i = j + k$. The expectation is then:

$$\gamma_k = \sum_{j=0}^{\infty} \phi^j \phi^{j+k} \sigma_a^2 = \phi^k \sigma_a^2 \sum_{j=0}^{\infty} \phi^{2j} = \phi^k \cdot \frac{\sigma_a^2}{1 - \phi^2}$$

Using $\gamma_{-k} = \gamma_k$ (symmetry), we unify both directions:

$$\boxed{\gamma_k = \phi^{\vert k \vert} \cdot \frac{\sigma_a^2}{1 - \phi^2}, \quad k = 0, \pm 1, \pm 2, \ldots}$$

**ACF:** $\rho_k = \phi^{\vert k \vert}$, which **decays geometrically** (rather than cutting off). This is the **identifying signature** of an AR(1) process.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
>
> Students frequently **lose marks** on this type of question for the following reasons:
>
> 1. **Forgetting the convergence condition** $\vert \phi \vert < 1$. The formula $\sigma^2/(1-\phi^2)$ is only valid when this holds. Always state the condition explicitly. *(–1 mark)*
> 2. **Confusing MA and AR signatures.** MA processes have **cutting-off** ACFs; AR processes have **decaying** ACFs. Mixing these up is a fatal conceptual error. *(–2 marks)*
> 3. **Omitting the geometric series evaluation step.** Examiners want to see $\sum_{j=0}^{\infty} \phi^{2j} = \frac{1}{1-\phi^2}$ explicitly written, not just stated. *(–1 mark)*
> 4. **Not justifying the use of the causal representation.** The student must explain *why* the infinite sum is valid (i.e., $\vert \phi \vert < 1$ ensures convergence). *(–1 mark)*
> 5. **Sign errors in the lag-1 autocovariance of MA(1).** The correct formula is $\gamma_1 = \theta \sigma_a^2$ (not $\theta^2 \sigma_a^2$ or $\theta \sigma_a^4$). *(–1 mark)*
> 6. **In the portfolio variance question, forgetting the $2 w_1 w_2$ cross term.** The factor of 2 arises because $(i,j)$ and $(j,i)$ are both summed. *(–1 mark)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-density rapid-revision checklist for the topic "Variances" (Module 1, PEIET742):**

- **Variance is a special case of autocovariance** at lag zero: $\gamma_0 = \sigma^2$.
- **Autocovariance is symmetric** in lag: $\gamma_k = \gamma_{-k}$.
- **Autocorrelation is bounded** by 1: $\vert \rho_k \vert \leq 1$ (proved by Cauchy–Schwarz).
- **White noise:** $\text{Var}(a_t) = \sigma_a^2$ (constant), ACF cuts off at lag 0.
- **MA(1):** $\text{Var}(r_t) = (1 + \theta^2)\sigma_a^2$; ACF cuts off after lag 1.
- **AR(1) with $\vert \phi \vert < 1$:** $\text{Var}(r_t) = \frac{\sigma_a^2}{1 - \phi^2}$; ACF decays as $\rho_k = \phi^{\vert k \vert}$.
- **Random walk:** $\text{Var}(r_t) = t\sigma_a^2$ — **non-stationary** (variance grows with $t$).
- **Variance of a sum:** $\text{Var}(X+Y) = \text{Var}(X) + \text{Var}(Y) + 2\text{Cov}(X,Y)$.
- **Variance of a linear combination:** $\text{Var}\!\left(\sum c_i X_i\right) = \sum_i \sum_j c_i c_j \sigma_{ij}$.
- **Sample autocovariance** uses divisor $T$ (biased but MSE-optimal); sample variance uses divisor $T - 1$ (unbiased).
- **Cross-covariance is asymmetric:** $\gamma_{xy}(k) \neq \gamma_{xy}(-k)$ in general.
- **Stationarity** requires the mean to be constant AND the variance to be finite AND the autocovariance to depend only on lag.
- **Portfolio variance** is $\text{Var}(P) = w_1^2 \sigma_1^2 + w_2^2 \sigma_2^2 + 2 w_1 w_2 \rho_{12} \sigma_1 \sigma_2$ (the Markowitz formula).
- **Causal representation of AR(1):** $r_t = \sum_{j=0}^{\infty} \phi^j a_{t-j}$ — converges only if $\vert \phi \vert < 1$.

> **Final note:** Whenever a KTU question says *"compute the variance and autocovariance of..."*, you are expected to (1) state the model, (2) compute the mean, (3) compute $\gamma_0$, (4) compute $\gamma_k$ for $k \geq 1$, and (5) state $\rho_k = \gamma_k / \gamma_0$. Follow these five steps and you will secure full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Covariances
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET742 - FINANCIAL TIME SERIES ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Covariances

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of financial time series analysis, **covariance** is a fundamental second-order moment measure that quantifies the degree to which two random variables (or two time-indexed stochastic processes) move together linearly. For two random variables $X$ and $Y$ with finite second moments, the covariance is rigorously defined as:

$$\text{Cov}(X, Y) = E[(X - \mu_X)(Y - \mu_Y)]$$

where $\mu_X = E[X]$ and $\mu_Y = E[Y]$ denote the population means. Expanding the expectation operator, an equivalent and computationally convenient form is:

$$\text{Cov}(X, Y) = E[XY] - E[X]E[Y] = E[XY] - \mu_X \mu_Y$$

In time series settings, where the data are ordered by time index $t$, we extend this concept to the **autocovariance function (ACVF)** $\gamma_k$ at lag $k$, and the **cross-covariance function** $\gamma_{XY}(k)$ between two series $X_t$ and $Y_t$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The Module 1 emphasis on covariances is foundational. The concept of *second-order stationarity* (weak stationarity) is built entirely on the constancy of the mean and the autocovariance function. You will see this reappear in ARMA, GARCH, and Vector Autoregressive (VAR) models.

> [!NOTE]
> **Terminology Distinction (Frequently Tested):**
> - **Variance** is a special case of covariance where $X = Y$, i.e., $\text{Var}(X) = \text{Cov}(X, X)$.
> - **Autocovariance** measures covariance of a series with its own past/future values (same series, different lag).
> - **Cross-covariance** measures covariance between two *different* series at a given lag.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

Imagine two stocks — *Reliance* and *ONGC* — listed on the NSE. When the price of Reliance rises by ₹10, ONGC often rises by ₹4 within the same trading hour. This synchronized co-movement is precisely what covariance captures.

- If both stocks tend to move in the **same direction** (both up or both down), their covariance is **positive**.
- If one tends to move **opposite** to the other, their covariance is **negative**.
- If their movements are **linearly unrelated**, their covariance is **zero** (note: this does NOT imply statistical independence unless the distribution is Gaussian).

> [!TIP]
> **Plain-English Intuition:** Covariance is the "unstandardized" version of correlation. It answers *how* two variables move together, but its magnitude depends on the units (e.g., ₹², %²). Correlation, by contrast, rescales covariance to lie in $[-1, 1]$, making it unit-free and comparable across variable pairs.

### 1.3 Key Notational Conventions (KTU-Standard)

- $\mu$ → Population mean (theoretical)
- $\bar{x}$ → Sample mean (empirical)
- $\gamma_0$ → Variance of the series (autocovariance at lag 0)
- $\gamma_k$ → Autocovariance at lag $k$
- $\rho_k$ → Autocorrelation at lag $k$ (normalized covariance)
- $\Gamma(h)$ → Autocovariance matrix at lag $h$
- $n$ → Sample size
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Logical Layers of Covariance

**Layer 1 — Two Random Variables (Static Setting):**
- Applies to any pair of scalar random variables.
- The covariance matrix of a $p$-dimensional random vector $\mathbf{X} = (X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_p)^\top$ is a $p \times p$ symmetric positive semi-definite matrix:

$$\Sigma = \text{Cov}(\mathbf{X}) = E[(\mathbf{X} - \boldsymbol{\mu})(\mathbf{X} - \boldsymbol{\mu})^\top]$$

The $(i, j)$-th entry is $\Sigma_{ij} = \text{Cov}(X_i, X_j)$, with diagonal entries $\Sigma_{ii} = \text{Var}(X_i)$.

**Layer 2 — Univariate Time Series (Dynamic Setting):**
- For a weakly stationary time series $\{X_t\}$, the **mean function** is constant: $\mu_t = E[X_t] = \mu$ for all $t$.
- The **autocovariance function** depends only on the lag $k$:

$$\gamma_k = \text{Cov}(X_t, X_{t+k}) = E[(X_t - \mu)(X_{t+k} - \mu)]$$

- The variance is $\gamma_0 = \text{Var}(X_t)$, and the autocorrelation is $\rho_k = \gamma_k / \gamma_0$.

**Layer 3 — Bivariate / Multivariate Time Series (Cross-Setting):**
- For two jointly stationary series $\{X_t\}$ and $\{Y_t\}$, the **cross-covariance** at lag $k$ is:

$$\gamma_{XY}(k) = \text{Cov}(X_t, Y_{t+k}) = E[(X_t - \mu_X)(Y_{t+k} - \mu_Y)]$$

- The **cross-correlation** is $\rho_{XY}(k) = \gamma_{XY}(k) / \sqrt{\gamma_X(0) \, \gamma_Y(0)}$.
- Note: Cross-covariance is generally **not symmetric**: $\gamma_{XY}(k) \neq \gamma_{XY}(-k)$ in general. Instead, $\gamma_{XY}(k) = \gamma_{YX}(-k)$.

### 2.2 Fundamental Properties of Covariance

1. **Symmetry:** $\text{Cov}(X, Y) = \text{Cov}(Y, X)$.
2. **Variance Identity:** $\text{Var}(X) = \text{Cov}(X, X) \geq 0$.
3. **Bilinearity:** $\text{Cov}(aX + b, cY + d) = ac \cdot \text{Cov}(X, Y)$ for constants $a, b, c, d$.
4. **Cauchy–Schwarz Bound:** $\vert \text{Cov}(X, Y) \vert \leq \sqrt{\text{Var}(X) \cdot \text{Var}(Y)}$.
5. **Additivity (under independence):** If $X, Y, Z$ are pairwise independent, then $\text{Cov}(X + Y, Z) = \text{Cov}(X, Z) + \text{Cov}(Y, Z)$.
6. **Variance of a Sum:** $\text{Var}(X + Y) = \text{Var}(X) + \text{Var}(Y) + 2 \text{Cov}(X, Y)$.
7. **Zero Covariance ≠ Independence** (only true for jointly Gaussian or under specific structural assumptions).
8. **Independence ⇒ Zero Covariance** (the converse does NOT hold in general).
9. **Positive Semi-Definiteness:** Any covariance matrix $\Sigma$ satisfies $\mathbf{a}^\top \Sigma \mathbf{a} \geq 0$ for all $\mathbf{a} \in \mathbb{R}^p$.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula** | **Name** | **Domain / Use** | **Boundary / Note** |
|:------------|:---------|:-----------------|:--------------------|
| $\text{Cov}(X,Y) = E[XY] - E[X]E[Y]$ | Population Covariance | Two random variables | Unbiased form not directly computable |
| $\gamma_k = E[(X_t - \mu)(X_{t+k} - \mu)]$ | Autocovariance at lag $k$ | Stationary time series | Function of lag only |
| $\gamma_0 = \text{Var}(X_t)$ | Variance (lag-0 autocovariance) | Stationary series | Always $\gamma_0 > 0$ for non-degenerate series |
| $\rho_k = \gamma_k / \gamma_0$ | Autocorrelation at lag $k$ | Stationary series | $\vert \rho_k \vert \leq 1$ |
| $\gamma_{XY}(k) = E[(X_t - \mu_X)(Y_{t+k} - \mu_Y)]$ | Cross-covariance at lag $k$ | Bivariate stationary | $\gamma_{XY}(k) = \gamma_{YX}(-k)$ |
| $\hat{\gamma}_k = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n-k}(x_t - \bar{x})(x_{t+k} - \bar{x})$ | Sample Autocovariance | Empirical estimation | Biased estimator (denominator $n$) |
| $\hat{\gamma}_k^* = \frac{1}{n-k}\sum_{t=1}^{n-k}(x_t - \bar{x})(x_{t+k} - \bar{x})$ | Unbiased Sample ACVF | Empirical estimation | Denominator $n-k$ |
| $\text{Cov}(X, Y) = \rho_{XY} \sigma_X \sigma_Y$ | Covariance–Correlation Link | Any two random variables | Sign preserved, magnitude scaled by SDs |
| $\text{Var}\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i\right) = \sum_{i=1}^{n}\text{Var}(X_i) + 2\sum_{i<j}\text{Cov}(X_i, X_j)$ | Variance of a Sum | $n$ random variables | General form of variance additivity |
| $\Sigma = E[(\mathbf{X} - \boldsymbol{\mu})(\mathbf{X} - \boldsymbol{\mu})^\top]$ | Covariance Matrix | $p$-dimensional random vector | Symmetric, positive semi-definite |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility in Quantitative Finance:** Covariance matrices are the engine of **Modern Portfolio Theory (Markowitz, 1952)**. The minimum-variance portfolio weights solve $\mathbf{w}^\top \Sigma \mathbf{w} \rightarrow \min$ subject to $\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{1} = 1$. They also underpin **RiskMetrics** (JP Morgan, 1994), **DCC-GARCH** (Engle, 2002), and **PCA-based factor models** in equity research.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From the Expectation Definition to the Computational Form

**Goal:** Show that $\text{Cov}(X, Y) = E[(X - \mu_X)(Y - \mu_Y)]$ is mathematically equivalent to $E[XY] - E[X]E[Y]$.

Starting from the definition:

$$\text{Cov}(X, Y) = E[(X - \mu_X)(Y - \mu_Y)]$$

Step 1 — Expand the product inside the expectation:

$$= E[XY - X\mu_Y - \mu_X Y + \mu_X \mu_Y]$$

Step 2 — Apply the linearity of the expectation operator $E[\cdot]$:

$$= E[XY] - E[X\mu_Y] - E[\mu_X Y] + E[\mu_X \mu_Y]$$

Step 3 — Recognize that $\mu_X = E[X]$ and $\mu_Y = E[Y]$ are **constants**, so they can be pulled outside the expectation:

$$= E[XY] - \mu_Y E[X] - \mu_X E[Y] + \mu_X \mu_Y$$

Step 4 — Substitute back $E[X] = \mu_X$ and $E[Y] = \mu_Y$:

$$= E[XY] - \mu_Y \mu_X - \mu_X \mu_Y + \mu_X \mu_Y$$

Step 5 — Simplify the algebraic terms (the last three terms cancel pairwise):

$$= E[XY] - \mu_X \mu_Y$$

Step 6 — Recall that $\mu_X \mu_Y = E[X]E[Y]$:

$$\therefore \; \text{Cov}(X, Y) = E[XY] - E[X]E[Y] \quad \blacksquare$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Cauchy–Schwarz Bound on Covariance

**Goal:** Show that $\vert \text{Cov}(X, Y) \vert \leq \sqrt{\text{Var}(X) \cdot \text{Var}(Y)}$.

Step 1 — Define a new random variable $W = X - \mu_X$ and recall $Y - \mu_Y$ is mean-zero.

Step 2 — For any real scalar $a$, the variance of the linear combination $aW + (Y - \mu_Y)$ is non-negative:

$$\text{Var}(aW + Y - \mu_Y) \geq 0$$

Step 3 — Expand using bilinearity of covariance:

$$= a^2 \text{Var}(W) + 2a \text{Cov}(W, Y - \mu_Y) + \text{Var}(Y - \mu_Y)$$

Step 4 — Substitute $\text{Var}(W) = \text{Var}(X)$ and $\text{Cov}(W, Y - \mu_Y) = \text{Cov}(X, Y)$:

$$= a^2 \text{Var}(X) + 2a \, \text{Cov}(X, Y) + \text{Var}(Y) \geq 0$$

Step 5 — This is a quadratic in $a$ with non-negative discriminant condition (for all $a$ to be feasible):

$$[2 \text{Cov}(X, Y)]^2 - 4 \cdot \text{Var}(X) \cdot \text{Var}(Y) \leq 0$$

Step 6 — Divide by 4 and take the square root:

$$[\text{Cov}(X, Y)]^2 \leq \text{Var}(X) \cdot \text{Var}(Y)$$

Step 7 — Take absolute value:

$$\vert \text{Cov}(X, Y) \vert \leq \sqrt{\text{Var}(X) \cdot \text{Var}(Y)} \quad \blacksquare$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Dividing both sides of step 7 by $\sqrt{\text{Var}(X) \text{Var}(Y)}$ recovers the Pearson correlation bound $\vert \rho_{XY} \vert \leq 1$. This is the mathematical foundation of the correlation coefficient's boundedness.

### 3.3 Derivation: Variance of a Sum of Two Correlated Variables

**Goal:** Derive $\text{Var}(X + Y) = \text{Var}(X) + \text{Var}(Y) + 2\text{Cov}(X, Y)$.

Step 1 — Apply the definition $\text{Var}(X + Y) = E[\{(X + Y) - E[X + Y]\}^2]$.

Step 2 — Note that $E[X + Y] = \mu_X + \mu_Y$, so the deviation is $(X - \mu_X) + (Y - \mu_Y)$.

Step 3 — Square the deviation:

$$\text{Var}(X + Y) = E[\{(X - \mu_X) + (Y - \mu_Y)\}^2]$$

Step 4 — Expand the square:

$$= E[(X - \mu_X)^2 + 2(X - \mu_X)(Y - \mu_Y) + (Y - \mu_Y)^2]$$

Step 5 — Apply linearity of expectation to each term:

$$= E[(X - \mu_X)^2] + 2E[(X - \mu_X)(Y - \mu_Y)] + E[(Y - \mu_Y)^2]$$

Step 6 — Recognize each term:

$$= \text{Var}(X) + 2\text{Cov}(X, Y) + \text{Var}(Y) \quad \blacksquare$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Special case:** When $X$ and $Y$ are uncorrelated, $\text{Cov}(X, Y) = 0$ and variances add: $\text{Var}(X + Y) = \text{Var}(X) + \text{Var}(Y)$.

### 3.4 Derivation: Sample Autocovariance Estimator (Biased Form)

**Goal:** Justify the sample ACVF formula used in practice.

Step 1 — The population ACVF is $\gamma_k = E[(X_t - \mu)(X_{t+k} - \mu)]$.

Step 2 — Replace the unknown mean $\mu$ with the sample mean $\bar{x} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n} x_t$.

Step 3 — For each lag $k$ (where $0 \leq k < n$), the natural plug-in estimator averages the products:

$$\hat{\gamma}_k = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n-k} (x_t - \bar{x})(x_{t+k} - \bar{x})$$

Step 4 — The number of valid product pairs is $n - k$ (we cannot pair terms past $t = n$).

Step 5 — Using $n$ in the denominator (rather than $n - k$) yields a **biased** estimator but ensures that the resulting **sample autocorrelation function (ACF)** is a positive semi-definite sequence — a critical property for valid ARMA modeling.

$$\therefore \; \hat{\gamma}_k = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n-k} (x_t - \bar{x})(x_{t+k} - \bar{x}) \quad \blacksquare$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Using $n - k$ in the denominator is the *unbiased* estimator but produces a non-positive-semidefinite ACF. KTU and most textbooks (Tsay, Box–Jenkins) prefer the biased $n$-denominator for this reason. Always state which form you are using in the exam.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Map: Hierarchical View of Covariance in Time Series

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[COVARIANCE IN FINANCIAL TIME SERIES] --> B[Static Setting]
    A --> C[Dynamic Setting]
    A --> D[Multivariate Setting]
    
    B --> B1[Two Random Variables X and Y]
    B1 --> B2[Cov X Y = E XY minus E X E Y]
    B1 --> B3[Variance = Cov X X]
    
    C --> C1[Univariate Series X sub t]
    C1 --> C2[Autocovariance gamma sub k]
    C1 --> C3[Autocorrelation rho sub k]
    C1 --> C4[Stationarity Assumption]
    
    D --> D1[Bivariate Series X sub t and Y sub t]
    D1 --> D2[Cross Covariance gamma sub XY of k]
    D1 --> D3[Cross Correlation rho sub XY of k]
    D1 --> D4[Covariance Matrix Sigma]
    
    C2 --> E[APPLICATIONS]
    D2 --> E
    D4 --> E
    
    E --> E1[Markowitz Portfolio Optimization]
    E --> E2[ARMA and GARCH Modeling]
    E --> E3[Value at Risk Computation]
    E --> E4[Principal Component Analysis]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Covariance Estimation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1[Raw Financial Data X1 X2 ... Xn] --> S1[Step 1 Compute Sample Mean x bar]
    S1 --> S2[Step 2 Mean Center the Series]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3 Choose Maximum Lag K]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4 For each k compute sum of lagged products]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5 Divide by n for biased ACVF]
    S5 --> O1[Output gamma hat sub k for k equals 0 to K]
    O1 --> N1[Normalize by gamma hat sub 0]
    N1 --> O2[Output ACF rho hat sub k]
    
    O1 --> APP1[Feed to ARMA Order Identification]
    O2 --> APP2[Feed to Ljung Box Test]
    O2 --> APP3[Feed to PACF Computation]
```

### 4.3 Covariance Matrix Structure (Visual Schematic)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph M["COVARIANCE MATRIX SIGMA OF DIMENSION p BY p"]
        N1[Sigma 1,1 = Var X1] --- N2[Sigma 1,2 = Cov X1 X2]
        N2 --- N3[Sigma 1,3 = Cov X1 X3]
        N1 --- N4[Sigma 2,1 = Cov X2 X1]
        N4 --- N5[Sigma 2,2 = Var X2]
        N5 --- N6[Sigma 2,3 = Cov X2 X3]
        N3 --- N7[Sigma 3,1 = Cov X3 X1]
        N7 --- N8[Sigma 3,2 = Cov X3 X2]
        N8 --- N9[Sigma 3,3 = Var X3]
    end
    
    P1[Property 1 SYMMETRIC Sigma ij equals Sigma ji] -.-> M
    P2[Property 2 POSITIVE SEMI DEFINITE] -.-> M
    P3[Property 3 DIAGONAL is VARIANCES] -.-> M
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
*Define the autocovariance function of a stationary time series. State any two of its key properties.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
For a weakly stationary time series $\{X_t\}$ with mean $\mu$, the autocovariance function (ACVF) at lag $k$ is defined as:

$$\gamma_k = \text{Cov}(X_t, X_{t+k}) = E[(X_t - \mu)(X_{t+k} - \mu)]$$

Key properties (any two for 1.5 marks each):
1. **Symmetry in lag:** $\gamma_k = \gamma_{-k}$ (the ACVF is an even function of lag).
2. **Non-negativity at zero:** $\gamma_0 = \text{Var}(X_t) \geq 0$.
3. **Bounded by variance:** $\vert \gamma_k \vert \leq \gamma_0$ for all $k$.
4. **Positive semi-definiteness:** For any constants $a_1, \ldots, a_n$ and any time points, $\sum_{i,j} a_i a_j \gamma_{t_i - t_j} \geq 0$.

*(Award 1 mark for the formula, 2 marks for properties.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
*Distinguish between covariance and correlation. Why is the latter preferred in practice?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Covariance** measures the joint linear variability of two random variables, with units equal to the product of the units of $X$ and $Y$ (e.g., ₹ × ₹ = ₹²).
- **Correlation** is the standardized (unit-free) version of covariance:

$$\rho_{XY} = \frac{\text{Cov}(X, Y)}{\sqrt{\text{Var}(X) \cdot \text{Var}(Y)}}$$

- Covariance lies in $(-\infty, +\infty)$; correlation lies in $[-1, +1]$.
- **Why preferred:** Correlation is **scale-invariant** and **dimensionless**, making it interpretable and comparable across different variable pairs (e.g., comparing stock returns vs. commodity prices).

*(Award 1 mark for definitions, 1 mark for relationship, 1 mark for advantages.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B: Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

#### ✦ Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Derive the equivalent computational formula $E[XY] - E[X]E[Y]$ for $\text{Cov}(X, Y)$, starting from the definition $\text{Cov}(X, Y) = E[(X - \mu_X)(Y - \mu_Y)]$. Show every step clearly. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For a stationary time series, the sample autocovariance at lag $k$ is given by:

$$\hat{\gamma}_k = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n-k}(x_t - \bar{x})(x_{t+k} - \bar{x})$$

Explain why the denominator $n$ (rather than $n - k$) is preferred in practice. Also compute $\hat{\gamma}_0$, $\hat{\gamma}_1$, and $\hat{\gamma}_2$ for the data: $x_1 = 2, x_2 = 4, x_3 = 6, x_4 = 8, x_5 = 10$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (a):** [3.5 marks for each major algebraic step + 0.5 for the final boxed conclusion]

Step 1 — Write the definition:

$$\text{Cov}(X, Y) = E[(X - \mu_X)(Y - \mu_Y)]$$

Step 2 — Expand the product:

$$= E[XY - X\mu_Y - \mu_X Y + \mu_X \mu_Y]$$

Step 3 — Apply linearity of expectation:

$$= E[XY] - E[X\mu_Y] - E[\mu_X Y] + E[\mu_X \mu_Y]$$

Step 4 — Since $\mu_X, \mu_Y$ are constants:

$$= E[XY] - \mu_Y E[X] - \mu_X E[Y] + \mu_X \mu_Y$$

Step 5 — Substitute $E[X] = \mu_X$ and $E[Y] = \mu_Y$:

$$= E[XY] - \mu_X \mu_Y - \mu_X \mu_Y + \mu_X \mu_Y = E[XY] - \mu_X \mu_Y$$

Step 6 — Final boxed result:

$$\therefore \; \text{Cov}(X, Y) = E[XY] - E[X]E[Y] \quad \blacksquare$$

**Model Solution (b):** [3 marks for explanation + 4 marks for numerical computation]

**Why denominator $n$ is preferred:**

The estimator with denominator $n$ is biased but yields a sequence $\{\hat{\gamma}_k\}$ that is **guaranteed to be positive semi-definite**, which is a structural requirement for any valid autocovariance function. The unbiased estimator (denominator $n - k$) can produce negative eigenvalues in the Toeplitz matrix, violating this requirement. Hence, the biased form is preferred for **identifiability** in ARMA modeling.

**Numerical Computation:**

Step 1 — Compute the sample mean:

$$\bar{x} = \frac{2 + 4 + 6 + 8 + 10}{5} = \frac{30}{5} = 6$$

Step 2 — Mean-center the series:

$$x_t - \bar{x} : -4, -2, 0, 2, 4$$

Step 3 — Compute $\hat{\gamma}_0$ (variance estimate):

$$\hat{\gamma}_0 = \frac{1}{5}\sum_{t=1}^{5}(x_t - \bar{x})^2 = \frac{(-4)^2 + (-2)^2 + 0^2 + 2^2 + 4^2}{5} = \frac{16 + 4 + 0 + 4 + 16}{5} = \frac{40}{5} = 8$$

Step 4 — Compute $\hat{\gamma}_1$:

$$\hat{\gamma}_1 = \frac{1}{5}\sum_{t=1}^{4}(x_t - \bar{x})(x_{t+1} - \bar{x}) = \frac{(-4)(-2) + (-2)(0) + (0)(2) + (2)(4)}{5} = \frac{8 + 0 + 0 + 8}{5} = \frac{16}{5} = 3.2$$

Step 5 — Compute $\hat{\gamma}_2$:

$$\hat{\gamma}_2 = \frac{1}{5}\sum_{t=1}^{3}(x_t - \bar{x})(x_{t+2} - \bar{x}) = \frac{(-4)(0) + (-2)(2) + (0)(4)}{5} = \frac{0 - 4 + 0}{5} = -\frac{4}{5} = -0.8$$

Step 6 — Tabulate results:

| Lag $k$ | $\hat{\gamma}_k$ |
|:-------:|:----------------:|
| 0 | **8.0** |
| 1 | **3.2** |
| 2 | **−0.8** |

---

#### ✦ Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** State and prove the Cauchy–Schwarz inequality for covariances. What does it imply about the relationship between covariance and correlation? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Consider two stocks with returns $X$ and $Y$. The data over 5 days is:

| Day | $X$ (%) | $Y$ (%) |
|:---:|:-------:|:-------:|
| 1 | 1.0 | 2.0 |
| 2 | 2.0 | 3.0 |
| 3 | 3.0 | 4.0 |
| 4 | 4.0 | 5.0 |
| 5 | 5.0 | 6.0 |

Compute (i) the sample covariance $\widehat{\text{Cov}}(X, Y)$, and (ii) the sample correlation coefficient $\hat{\rho}_{XY}$. Comment on the strength of the linear relationship. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (a):** [3 marks for statement + 4 marks for derivation]

**Statement:** For any two random variables $X$ and $Y$ with finite second moments:

$$\left[\text{Cov}(X, Y)\right]^2 \leq \text{Var}(X) \cdot \text{Var}(Y)$$

or equivalently:

$$\vert \text{Cov}(X, Y) \vert \leq \sqrt{\text{Var}(X) \cdot \text{Var}(Y)}$$

**Proof:**

Step 1 — For any real $a$, variance is non-negative:

$$\text{Var}\left(a(X - \mu_X) + (Y - \mu_Y)\right) \geq 0$$

Step 2 — Expand using bilinearity:

$$a^2 \text{Var}(X) + 2a \text{Cov}(X, Y) + \text{Var}(Y) \geq 0$$

Step 3 — This is a quadratic in $a$ that must be non-negative for all $a \in \mathbb{R}$. The discriminant must therefore be $\leq 0$:

$$[2 \text{Cov}(X, Y)]^2 - 4 \text{Var}(X) \text{Var}(Y) \leq 0$$

Step 4 — Divide by 4:

$$[\text{Cov}(X, Y)]^2 \leq \text{Var}(X) \text{Var}(Y) \quad \blacksquare$$

**Implication:** Dividing both sides by $\text{Var}(X) \text{Var}(Y)$ (assumed positive) and taking the square root:

$$\frac{\vert \text{Cov}(X, Y) \vert}{\sqrt{\text{Var}(X) \text{Var}(Y)}} \leq 1 \implies \vert \rho_{XY} \vert \leq 1$$

This proves the correlation coefficient is always bounded in $[-1, 1]$.

**Model Solution (b):** [7 marks — 2 for means, 2 for covariance, 2 for correlation, 1 for comment]

Step 1 — Compute means:

$$\bar{x} = \frac{1 + 2 + 3 + 4 + 5}{5} = 3.0, \quad \bar{y} = \frac{2 + 3 + 4 + 5 + 6}{5} = 4.0$$

Step 2 — Mean-centered values:

| Day | $x_t - \bar{x}$ | $y_t - \bar{y}$ |
|:---:|:---------------:|:---------------:|
| 1 | −2.0 | −2.0 |
| 2 | −1.0 | −1.0 |
| 3 | 0.0 | 0.0 |
| 4 | 1.0 | 1.0 |
| 5 | 2.0 | 2.0 |

Step 3 — Compute sample covariance:

$$\widehat{\text{Cov}}(X, Y) = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n}(x_t - \bar{x})(y_t - \bar{y}) = \frac{(-2)(-2) + (-1)(-1) + 0 + (1)(1) + (2)(2)}{5} = \frac{4 + 1 + 0 + 1 + 4}{5} = \frac{10}{5} = 2.0$$

Step 4 — Compute variances:

$$\hat{\sigma}_X^2 = \frac{(-2)^2 + (-1)^2 + 0 + 1^2 + 2^2}{5} = \frac{10}{5} = 2.0$$

$$\hat{\sigma}_Y^2 = \frac{(-2)^2 + (-1)^2 + 0 + 1^2 + 2^2}{5} = \frac{10}{5} = 2.0$$

Step 5 — Compute correlation:

$$\hat{\rho}_{XY} = \frac{\widehat{\text{Cov}}(X, Y)}{\hat{\sigma}_X \hat{\sigma}_Y} = \frac{2.0}{\sqrt{2.0} \cdot \sqrt{2.0}} = \frac{2.0}{2.0} = 1.0$$

Step 6 — Comment: A correlation of $\hat{\rho}_{XY} = 1.0$ indicates a **perfect positive linear relationship** between the two stock return series. Every unit increase in $X$ corresponds to a unit increase in $Y$ (slope = 1), as evident from the data.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Frequently Deducted Marks):**
> 1. **Sign error in cross-covariance** — students often write $\gamma_{XY}(k) = \gamma_{XY}(-k)$; the correct identity is $\gamma_{XY}(k) = \gamma_{YX}(-k)$. **−1 mark.**
> 2. **Confusing biased vs. unbiased sample ACVF** — using $n - k$ in the denominator without acknowledging that it destroys positive semi-definiteness. **−1 to −2 marks.**
> 3. **Forgetting units in covariance** — covariance carries units (e.g., $(\%)^2$); the question may explicitly ask for units. **−0.5 mark.**
> 4. **Skipping intermediate steps in derivation questions** — KTU examiners require "every step clearly"; a jump from the definition to the final answer will yield partial credit only. **−2 to −3 marks.**
> 5. **Forgetting the $\mu_X \mu_Y$ cancellation step** in the $E[XY] - E[X]E[Y]$ derivation. **−1 mark.**

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid-Revision Checklist (Must Memorize Before Exam)**

- **Core Definition:** $\text{Cov}(X, Y) = E[(X - \mu_X)(Y - \mu_Y)] = E[XY] - E[X]E[Y]$.
- **Variance is a special case:** $\text{Var}(X) = \text{Cov}(X, X) \geq 0$.
- **Autocovariance** depends only on lag under weak stationarity: $\gamma_k = \text{Cov}(X_t, X_{t+k})$.
- **Cross-covariance identity:** $\gamma_{XY}(k) = \gamma_{YX}(-k)$ (NOT $\gamma_{XY}(-k)$).
- **Bilinearity** is the key to handling linear combinations of random variables.
- **Variance of sum:** $\text{Var}(X + Y) = \text{Var}(X) + \text{Var}(Y) + 2\text{Cov}(X, Y)$.
- **Cauchy–Schwarz:** $\vert \text{Cov}(X, Y) \vert \leq \sigma_X \sigma_Y$, equivalently $\vert \rho_{XY} \vert \leq 1$.
- **Sample ACVF (biased form):** $\hat{\gamma}_k = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n-k}(x_t - \bar{x})(x_{t+k} - \bar{x})$.
- **Covariance matrix $\Sigma$ is symmetric and positive semi-definite** (all eigenvalues $\geq 0$).
- **Zero covariance** implies independence **only for jointly Gaussian** random variables; in general, it is a measure of *linear* uncorrelatedness, not full independence.
- **Autocorrelation** is the normalized version: $\rho_k = \gamma_k / \gamma_0$, always in $[-1, 1]$.
- **Unit awareness:** Covariance carries units of (unit of $X$) × (unit of $Y$); correlation is unit-free.
- **Time series prerequisite:** Weak stationarity requires $E[X_t] = \mu$ (constant) and $\gamma_k$ independent of $t$.
- **Exam tip:** Always specify which form (biased/unbiased) of sample ACVF you are using; KTU expects the **biased form** (denominator $n$) for ACF and PACF plots.
- **Real-world link:** Covariance matrices drive Markowitz portfolio theory, DCC-GARCH models, and PCA in factor analysis.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Correlation and Autocorrelation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET742 - FINANCIAL TIME SERIES ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Correlation and Autocorrelation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Correlation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Correlation** is a standardized statistical measure that quantifies the **strength and direction of the linear relationship** between two random variables, or between two points separated by a time lag within the same series.

In the context of financial time series, correlation is a foundational tool. It allows a quantitative analyst, portfolio manager, or risk engineer to determine whether two assets (e.g., the daily returns of **Reliance Industries** and **TCS**) move together, move oppositely, or behave independently. The result is bounded between **-1 and +1**, providing a normalized scale that is independent of the units of measurement (rupees, dollars, percentages).

### Formal Mathematical Definition

For two jointly distributed random variables $X$ and $Y$ with finite second moments, the **population correlation coefficient** $\rho_{XY}$ is formally defined as:

$$\rho_{XY} = \frac{E[(X - \mu_X)(Y - \mu_Y)]}{\sigma_X \sigma_Y}$$

where $\mu_X, \mu_Y$ are the means and $\sigma_X, \sigma_Y$ are the standard deviations. The numerator is the **covariance**, and the denominator is the geometric mean of the variances.

### Real-World Analogy: The "Dancing Pairs" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** Imagine observing two dancers on a stage. **Correlation** asks: "When one dancer steps forward, does the other also step forward, or step backward?" A value close to **+1** means they are mirror images of each other (perfectly synchronized). A value close to **-1** means they are doing exact opposites (one goes up, the other goes down). A value near **0** means their movements have **no linear relationship** at all — they are dancing to different tunes. In the Indian stock market context, when the NIFTY 50 index rises, large-cap banking stocks like HDFC Bank and ICICI Bank often rise *with* it — this is **positive correlation**.

### Sample Correlation (Pearson's $r$)

Since true population parameters are unknown in practice, we compute the **sample correlation coefficient** $r_{XY}$ from $n$ observed data pairs $(x_i, y_i)$:

$$r_{XY} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \bar{x})^2 \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \bar{y})^2}}$$

---

## 1.2 Autocorrelation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Autocorrelation** (also called *serial correlation*) is the **correlation of a time series with its own past and future values**. It measures the degree of linear dependence between observations separated by a time lag $k$.

Whereas correlation compares **two different series**, autocorrelation compares **one series with a lagged copy of itself**. This is the central diagnostic tool in financial time series — it tells us whether today's market behavior is mathematically tethered to yesterday's behavior.

### The Autocorrelation Function (ACF)

For a **stationary** time series $\{r_t\}$, the **autocorrelation function** at lag $k$ is:

$$\rho_k = \frac{\text{Cov}(r_t, r_{t-k})}{\sqrt{\text{Var}(r_t) \cdot \text{Var}(r_{t-k})}} = \frac{\gamma_k}{\gamma_0}$$

where $\gamma_k$ is the **autocovariance function** at lag $k$, and $\gamma_0$ is the variance.

### Real-World Analogy: "Market Memory"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** A stock market has **memory**. If you look at the NIFTY 50 daily returns, and yesterday was a sharp "up" day, is today more likely to be "up" or "down"? Autocorrelation at lag 1 quantifies exactly that. In an **efficient market**, this memory should be **near zero** — past returns cannot predict future returns. However, empirical studies on emerging markets like India often reveal **small but statistically significant** autocorrelations due to phenomena like *index rebalancing effects*, *settlement delays*, and *behavioural biases*. A **negative lag-1 autocorrelation** in monthly returns hints at *mean reversion*; a **positive lag-1 autocorrelation** hints at *momentum* or *trend-following*.

### The Sample Autocorrelation Function (SACF)

Given a sample of size $T$, the **sample autocorrelation** $\hat{\rho}_k$ at lag $k$ is estimated as:

$$\hat{\rho}_k = \frac{\sum_{t=k+1}^{T}(r_t - \bar{r})(r_{t-k} - \bar{r})}{\sum_{t=1}^{T}(r_t - \bar{r})^2}$$

### Partial Autocorrelation Function (PACF)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Partial Autocorrelation Function (PACF)** at lag $k$ measures the correlation between $r_t$ and $r_{t-k}$ *after removing the linear effects of the intermediate lags* $r_{t-1}, r_{t-2}, \ldots, r_{t-k+1}$. It answers the question: *"Is there a direct relationship between $r_t$ and $r_{t-k}$ that is not explained by the lags in between?"*

Mathematically, PACF is computed as the **last coefficient** of the autoregression of $r_t$ on its first $k$ lags, or recursively via the **Durbin-Levinson algorithm**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Properties of Correlation ($\rho_{XY}$)

- **Boundedness:** $-1 \leq \rho_{XY} \leq +1$. Proof follows from the **Cauchy-Schwarz inequality**.
- **Scale Invariance:** Multiplying $X$ or $Y$ by a positive constant does not change $\rho$.
- **Symmetry:** $\rho_{XY} = \rho_{YX}$.
- **Zero Mean Implication:** If $X$ and $Y$ are *independent*, then $\rho_{XY} = 0$. However, the **converse is FALSE** — zero correlation does NOT imply independence (only for jointly normal distributions does zero correlation imply independence).
- **Interpretation Guide:**
  - $|\rho| = 1$: Perfect linear relationship.
  - $0.7 \leq \vert\rho\vert \leq 1$: Strong correlation.
  - $0.4 \leq \vert\rho\vert \leq 0.7$: Moderate correlation.
  - $0.2 \leq \vert\rho\vert \leq 0.4$: Weak correlation.
  - $\vert\rho\vert \leq 0.2$: Negligible linear relationship.

## 2.2 Properties of the ACF ($\rho_k$)

- $\rho_0 = 1$ (a series is perfectly correlated with itself).
- $\rho_{-k} = \rho_k$ (symmetric around lag 0).
- $\vert\rho_k\vert \leq 1$ for all $k$.
- For a **white noise** process, $\rho_k = 0$ for all $k \geq 1$.
- The ACF is used to identify the **order $q$ of a Moving Average (MA) model** — the ACF of an MA($q$) process *cuts off* (becomes zero) after lag $q$.

## 2.3 Properties of the PACF ($\phi_{kk}$)

- $\phi_{11} = \rho_1$.
- The PACF is used to identify the **order $p$ of an Autoregressive (AR) model** — the PACF of an AR($p$) process *cuts off* after lag $p$.

## 2.4 Statistical Significance of Sample Autocorrelations

Under the null hypothesis that the true autocorrelation is zero (i.e., the series is white noise), the sample autocorrelation $\hat{\rho}_k$ is approximately **normally distributed** with mean 0 and variance $1/T$ for large $T$:

$$\hat{\rho}_k \sim \mathcal{N}\left(0, \frac{1}{T}\right)$$

The **95% confidence interval** is therefore $\pm 1.96/\sqrt{T}$. Any sample autocorrelation falling outside this band is considered statistically significant.

## 2.5 Ljung-Box Test (Portmanteau Test)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Ljung-Box Q-statistic** tests the joint null hypothesis that *all* autocorrelations up to lag $m$ are simultaneously zero:

$$Q(m) = T(T+2) \sum_{k=1}^{m} \frac{\hat{\rho}_k^2}{T-k} \sim \chi^2_{(m)}$$

If $Q(m) > \chi^2_{1-\alpha, m}$, reject the null — the series exhibits significant serial dependence.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **Concept** | **Formula** | **Range / Distribution** | **Use Case** |
|:------------|:------------|:-------------------------|:-------------|
| Population Correlation | $\rho_{XY} = \dfrac{E[(X-\mu_X)(Y-\mu_Y)]}{\sigma_X \sigma_Y}$ | $-1 \leq \rho \leq +1$ | Linear dependence of two series |
| Sample Correlation | $r_{XY} = \dfrac{\sum(x_i-\bar x)(y_i-\bar y)}{\sqrt{\sum(x_i-\bar x)^2 \sum(y_i-\bar y)^2}}$ | $-1 \leq r \leq +1$ | Empirical correlation |
| Autocovariance | $\gamma_k = E[(r_t - \mu)(r_{t-k} - \mu)]$ | Lag-dependent | Covariance at lag $k$ |
| ACF (Population) | $\rho_k = \gamma_k / \gamma_0$ | $-1 \leq \rho_k \leq 1$ | Identify MA order |
| ACF (Sample) | $\hat{\rho}_k = \dfrac{\sum_{t=k+1}^T (r_t-\bar r)(r_{t-k}-\bar r)}{\sum_{t=1}^T (r_t-\bar r)^2}$ | $-1 \leq \hat{\rho}_k \leq 1$ | Empirical autocorrelation |
| PACF | $\phi_{kk}$ (last coef. of AR($k$) fit) | $-1 \leq \phi_{kk} \leq 1$ | Identify AR order |
| 95% CI for $\hat{\rho}_k$ | $\pm 1.96 / \sqrt{T}$ | White noise null | Significance testing |
| Ljung-Box Statistic | $Q(m) = T(T+2) \sum_{k=1}^m \dfrac{\hat{\rho}_k^2}{T-k}$ | $\chi^2_{(m)}$ | Joint test for serial correlation |
| Durbin-Watson | $DW = \dfrac{\sum_{t=2}^T (e_t - e_{t-1})^2}{\sum_{t=1}^T e_t^2}$ | $0 \leq DW \leq 4$ | Test lag-1 residual autocorrelation |

## 2.7 Engineering Utility in Finance

- **Portfolio Construction:** Modern Portfolio Theory (Markowitz) requires the *correlation matrix* of asset returns to compute the **portfolio variance** $\sigma_p^2 = \mathbf{w}^T \Sigma \mathbf{w}$.
- **Risk Management:** Value-at-Risk (VaR) calculations of multi-asset portfolios depend on correlations, since correlated shocks amplify tail risk.
- **Pairs Trading:** Identify cointegrated pairs with high historical correlation, then trade the spread when it diverges.
- **Model Identification (Box-Jenkins):** The ACF and PACF plots (*correlograms*) are the primary tools for selecting the orders $p, d, q$ of an **ARIMA** model.
- **Volatility Clustering Detection:** GARCH-type models are built specifically to model the **autocorrelation in squared returns**, not in returns themselves.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Boundedness of Correlation from Cauchy-Schwarz

> [!NOTE]
> **Goal:** Prove that $-1 \leq \rho_{XY} \leq +1$.

**Step 1:** The Cauchy-Schwarz inequality states that for any two real-valued random variables with finite second moments:

$$[E(XY)]^2 \leq E(X^2) \cdot E(Y^2)$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the centered variables $X - \mu_X$ and $Y - \mu_Y$:

$$[E((X-\mu_X)(Y-\mu_Y))]^2 \leq E[(X-\mu_X)^2] \cdot E[(Y-\mu_Y)^2]$$

**Step 3:** Recognize each term:

$$\text{Cov}(X,Y)^2 \leq \text{Var}(X) \cdot \text{Var}(Y)$$

**Step 4:** Take the square root of both sides:

$$\vert \text{Cov}(X,Y) \vert \leq \sigma_X \sigma_Y$$

**Step 5:** Divide both sides by $\sigma_X \sigma_Y > 0$:

$$\left\vert \frac{\text{Cov}(X,Y)}{\sigma_X \sigma_Y} \right\vert \leq 1 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad -1 \leq \rho_{XY} \leq 1$$

Equality holds iff $X$ and $Y$ are *perfectly linearly related*.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Sample Autocorrelation $\hat{\rho}_1$ for a Given Series

Suppose a financial series $r_1, r_2, \ldots, r_5$ is given as: $\{2, 4, 6, 8, 10\}$.

**Step 1: Compute the sample mean.**

$$\bar{r} = \frac{1}{5}\sum_{t=1}^{5} r_t = \frac{2+4+6+8+10}{5} = \frac{30}{5} = 6$$

**Step 2: Compute the centered series $(r_t - \bar r)$.**

| $t$ | $r_t$ | $r_t - \bar r$ |
|:---:|:-----:|:--------------:|
| 1   | 2     | -4             |
| 2   | 4     | -2             |
| 3   | 6     | 0              |
| 4   | 8     | 2              |
| 5   | 10    | 4              |

**Step 3: Compute the denominator $\sum_{t=1}^5 (r_t - \bar r)^2$.**

$$\sum (r_t - \bar r)^2 = (-4)^2 + (-2)^2 + (0)^2 + (2)^2 + (4)^2 = 16 + 4 + 0 + 4 + 16 = 40$$

**Step 4: For lag 1, compute $\sum_{t=2}^5 (r_t - \bar r)(r_{t-1} - \bar r)$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(r_2 - \bar r)(r_1 - \bar r) &= (-2)(-4) = 8 \\
(r_3 - \bar r)(r_2 - \bar r) &= (0)(-2) = 0 \\
(r_4 - \bar r)(r_3 - \bar r) &= (2)(0) = 0 \\
(r_5 - \bar r)(r_4 - \bar r) &= (4)(2) = 8 \\
\hline
\text{Sum} &= 8 + 0 + 0 + 8 = 16
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Compute $\hat{\rho}_1$.**

$$\hat{\rho}_1 = \frac{16}{40} = 0.4$$

This is a **moderate positive autocorrelation** at lag 1, consistent with the obviously trending nature of the series.

**Step 6: Verify against 95% confidence bound.** With $T=5$, the 95% CI is $\pm 1.96/\sqrt{5} \approx \pm 0.877$. Since $0.4 < 0.877$, this is *not statistically significant* at the 5% level for such a small sample — a key reason why practitioners use large $T$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Connection Between Sample Autocorrelations and the Yule-Walker Equations (for PACF)

The PACF can be derived from the ACF via the **Durbin-Levinson recursion**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\phi_{11} &= \rho_1 \\
\phi_{22} &= \frac{\rho_2 - \rho_1^2}{1 - \rho_1^2} \\
\phi_{kk} &= \frac{\rho_k - \sum_{j=1}^{k-1} \phi_{k-1,j} \cdot \rho_{k-j}}{1 - \sum_{j=1}^{k-1} \phi_{k-1,j} \cdot \rho_j}
\end{aligned}
$$

The intermediate coefficients $\phi_{k,j}$ are updated as:

$$\phi_{k,j} = \phi_{k-1,j} - \phi_{kk} \cdot \phi_{k-1, k-j}, \quad j = 1, 2, \ldots, k-1$$

**Numerical example:** Suppose $\rho_1 = 0.5$ and $\rho_2 = 0.3$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\phi_{11} &= 0.5 \\
\phi_{22} &= \frac{0.3 - (0.5)(0.5)}{1 - (0.5)(0.5)} = \frac{0.3 - 0.25}{1 - 0.25} = \frac{0.05}{0.75} \approx 0.0667
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Even though the lag-2 autocorrelation is 0.3, the *partial* autocorrelation is only 0.0667 — meaning that almost all of the lag-2 correlation is explained by the lag-1 term. This is why the PACF is the correct diagnostic for **AR order selection**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Decision Rules for ARIMA Identification

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **exam-essential** decision rule for identifying the orders $p$ and $q$ of an ARMA$(p,q)$ model from sample ACF and PACF.

| **Model Type** | **ACF Behaviour** | **PACF Behaviour** |
|:---------------|:------------------|:-------------------|
| White Noise (MA(0)) | All $\hat{\rho}_k = 0$ for $k \geq 1$ | All $\phi_{kk} = 0$ for $k \geq 1$ |
| AR($p$)            | Tails off gradually (exponential decay or damped sinusoid) | **Cuts off** sharply after lag $p$ |
| MA($q$)            | **Cuts off** sharply after lag $q$ | Tails off gradually |
| ARMA($p,q$)        | Tails off after lag $q$ | Tails off after lag $p$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Examiner Trap:** In small samples, the ACF/PACF never cut off *exactly* — they tail off noisily. You must apply the **1.96/√T** rule to identify the *last significant lag*, not the *first* one.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Flow: How Correlation Diagnostics Feed into ARIMA Model Building

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Financial Returns r_t] --> B[Compute Sample Mean r_bar]
    B --> C[Center the Series: r_t - r_bar]
    C --> D[Form Lagged Pairs]
    D --> E[Compute Covariance and Variance]
    E --> F[Sample ACF: rho_hat_k]
    E --> G[Sample PACF: phi_hat_kk]
    F --> H{Is rho_hat_k within pm 1.96/sqrt T?}
    G --> I{Is phi_hat_kk within pm 1.96/sqrt T?}
    H -->|Yes| J[White Noise: No Autocorrelation]
    H -->|No| K[Plot Correlogram]
    I -->|Yes| J
    I -->|No| K
    K --> L[Identify AR order p from PACF cut-off]
    K --> M[Identify MA order q from ACF cut-off]
    L --> N[Fit ARIMA p d q Model]
    M --> N
    N --> O[Ljung-Box Test on Residuals]
    O -->|p-value > 0.05| P[Model Adequate]
    O -->|p-value < 0.05| Q[Re-specify Model]
```

## 4.2 Architecture Block Diagram: The Autocorrelation Computation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Series r_1 ... r_T] --> B[Pre-processing Block]
    B --> B1[Demean: Compute r_bar]
    B --> B2[Detrend if needed]
    B1 --> C[ACF Computation Engine]
    B2 --> C
    C --> C1[Denominator: Sum of squared deviations]
    C --> C2[Numerator: Lagged cross-products]
    C1 --> D[Output rho_hat_0 = 1]
    C2 --> D
    D --> E[Correlogram Plot Block]
    E --> F[SACF Diagnostic Visualization]
    E --> G[SPACF Diagnostic Visualization]
    F --> H[ARIMA Order Identification]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Final Model Specification]
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Schema: Correlation vs Autocorrelation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Statistical Dependence Measures] --> B[Inter-series]
    A --> C[Intra-series]
    B --> B1[Pearson Correlation]
    B --> B2[Kendall Tau]
    B --> B3[Spearman Rho]
    C --> C1[ACF: Autocorrelation Function]
    C --> C2[PACF: Partial Autocorrelation Function]
    C --> C3[Lag Plots: Visual Diagnostic]
    B1 --> D[Portfolio Risk]
    C1 --> E[ARIMA Order Identification]
    C2 --> E
    C3 --> F[Linearity Check]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define the autocorrelation function (ACF) of a stationary time series. State any three of its properties.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** For a stationary time series $\{r_t\}$ with mean $\mu$ and variance $\gamma_0$, the ACF at lag $k$ is defined as:
> $$\rho_k = \frac{\gamma_k}{\gamma_0} = \frac{E[(r_t - \mu)(r_{t-k} - \mu)]}{E[(r_t - \mu)^2]}$$

**Three properties:**
1. **Boundedness:** $-1 \leq \rho_k \leq 1$ for all $k$.
2. **Symmetry:** $\rho_k = \rho_{-k}$, so the ACF is an *even function* of lag.
3. **Normalization:** $\rho_0 = 1$ — a series is perfectly correlated with itself.
4. **For a white noise process:** $\rho_k = 0$ for all $k \geq 1$.

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Differentiate between the Autocorrelation Function (ACF) and the Partial Autocorrelation Function (PACF).**

**Model Answer:**

| **Aspect** | **ACF** | **PACF** |
|:-----------|:--------|:---------|
| Definition | Correlation between $r_t$ and $r_{t-k}$ | Correlation between $r_t$ and $r_{t-k}$ *after removing the linear influence* of intermediate lags $r_{t-1}, \ldots, r_{t-k+1}$ |
| Measures | Total dependence at lag $k$ | *Direct* dependence at lag $k$ |
| Use | Identifies MA($q$) order — **cuts off** after lag $q$ | Identifies AR($p$) order — **cuts off** after lag $p$ |
| Notation | $\rho_k$ | $\phi_{kk}$ |

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**(a)** For the following time series of stock returns: $r_1=2, r_2=4, r_3=6, r_4=8, r_5=10$:
- Compute the sample mean.
- Compute the sample variance.
- Compute the sample autocorrelation at lag 1. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the Ljung-Box test for testing the significance of autocorrelations up to lag $m$. A series of length $T=200$ has $\hat{\rho}_1 = 0.18$, $\hat{\rho}_2 = 0.12$, $\hat{\rho}_3 = 0.05$. Test at the 5% level whether the series is white noise, using $m=3$. (Use $\chi^2_{0.95, 3} = 7.815$). **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

**Step 1 — Sample mean:**

$$\bar{r} = \frac{2+4+6+8+10}{5} = \frac{30}{5} = 6$$

**Step 2 — Sample variance:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{t=1}^{5}(r_t - \bar{r})^2 &= (-4)^2 + (-2)^2 + 0^2 + 2^2 + 4^2 \\
&= 16 + 4 + 0 + 4 + 16 = 40 \\
s^2 &= \frac{40}{5-1} = \frac{40}{4} = 10
\end{aligned}
$$

(If the question demands the *unbiased* estimator with denominator $T-1$; for the ACF computation, the denominator $T$ or $T-k$ is conventionally used — but for variance display, $T-1$ is the Bessel-corrected estimator.)

**Step 3 — Sample autocorrelation at lag 1:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{t=2}^{5}(r_t - \bar{r})(r_{t-1} - \bar{r}) &= (-2)(-4) + (0)(-2) + (2)(0) + (4)(2) \\
&= 8 + 0 + 0 + 8 = 16 \\
\hat{\rho}_1 &= \frac{16}{40} = 0.4
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The series exhibits a **moderate positive autocorrelation** at lag 1.

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1 — Statement of the Ljung-Box Test:**

> The Ljung-Box Q-statistic tests the null hypothesis $H_0: \rho_1 = \rho_2 = \cdots = \rho_m = 0$ (series is white noise) against the alternative that at least one $\rho_k \neq 0$.

The statistic is:

$$Q(m) = T(T+2)\sum_{k=1}^{m}\frac{\hat{\rho}_k^2}{T-k}$$

**Step 2 — Numerical evaluation** with $T=200$, $m=3$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\hat{\rho}_1^2}{T-1} &= \frac{(0.18)^2}{199} = \frac{0.0324}{199} \approx 0.0001628 \\
\frac{\hat{\rho}_2^2}{T-2} &= \frac{(0.12)^2}{198} = \frac{0.0144}{198} \approx 0.0000727 \\
\frac{\hat{\rho}_3^2}{T-3} &= \frac{(0.05)^2}{197} = \frac{0.0025}{197} \approx 0.0000127 \\
\hline
\text{Sum} &\approx 0.0002482
\end{aligned}
$$

$$Q(3) = 200 \times 202 \times 0.0002482 \approx 40400 \times 0.0002482 \approx 10.027$$

**Step 3 — Decision:**

Critical value: $\chi^2_{0.95, 3} = 7.815$.

Since $Q(3) = 10.027 > 7.815$, **reject $H_0$** at the 5% significance level.

**Conclusion:** The series exhibits statistically significant serial correlation — it is **NOT** white noise.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**(a)** Derive the sample autocorrelation function $\hat{\rho}_k$ from first principles. Show that it is a consistent estimator of $\rho_k$ under the assumption of stationarity. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** The following sample ACF values were computed from a series of length $T=400$: $\hat{\rho}_1 = 0.12$, $\hat{\rho}_2 = 0.11$, $\hat{\rho}_3 = 0.09$, $\hat{\rho}_4 = 0.08$, $\hat{\rho}_5 = 0.06$, $\hat{\rho}_6 = 0.04$. Using the Box-Jenkins identification rules, identify the most plausible ARIMA model. Justify your answer using the 95% confidence interval. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

**Step 1 — Population definition.** For a stationary series $\{r_t\}$ with mean $\mu$ and autocovariance $\gamma_k$:

$$\rho_k = \frac{\gamma_k}{\gamma_0}, \quad \text{where } \gamma_k = E[(r_t - \mu)(r_{t-k} - \mu)]$$

**Step 2 — Sample analogue.** Replace the expectation with the sample mean over $T$ observations. The natural estimator of the autocovariance at lag $k$ is:

$$\hat{\gamma}_k = \frac{1}{T}\sum_{t=k+1}^{T}(r_t - \bar{r})(r_{t-k} - \bar{r})$$

and the variance estimator:

$$\hat{\gamma}_0 = \frac{1}{T}\sum_{t=1}^{T}(r_t - \bar{r})^2$$

**Step 3 — Sample autocorrelation.** Taking the ratio:

$$\hat{\rho}_k = \frac{\hat{\gamma}_k}{\hat{\gamma}_0} = \frac{\sum_{t=k+1}^{T}(r_t - \bar{r})(r_{t-k} - \bar{r})}{\sum_{t=1}^{T}(r_t - \bar{r})^2}$$

**Step 4 — Consistency argument.** Under the assumption of **ergodic stationarity** (the time average equals the ensemble average), the Law of Large Numbers applies. As $T \to \infty$:

- The sample mean $\bar{r} \xrightarrow{p} \mu$.
- The sample autocovariance $\hat{\gamma}_k \xrightarrow{p} \gamma_k$.
- The ratio $\hat{\rho}_k \xrightarrow{p} \gamma_k/\gamma_0 = \rho_k$.

Hence $\hat{\rho}_k$ is a **consistent estimator** of $\rho_k$.

**Note (Bias correction):** The estimator $\hat{\gamma}_k$ is biased, with $E[\hat{\gamma}_k] \approx (1 - k/T)\gamma_k$. The bias-corrected version uses denominator $T-k$ in the sum and $T$ or $T-1$ in the variance term. Under large $T$, this bias is negligible.

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1 — Compute the 95% confidence band.**

$$\text{CI} = \pm \frac{1.96}{\sqrt{400}} = \pm \frac{1.96}{20} = \pm 0.098$$

**Step 2 — Test each $\hat{\rho}_k$ for significance.**

| Lag $k$ | $\hat{\rho}_k$ | $\vert\hat{\rho}_k\vert > 0.098$? | Significant? |
|:-------:|:--------------:|:---------------------------------:|:------------:|
| 1       | 0.12           | 0.12 > 0.098                      | YES          |
| 2       | 0.11           | 0.11 > 0.098                      | YES          |
| 3       | 0.09           | 0.09 < 0.098                      | NO           |
| 4       | 0.08           | 0.08 < 0.098                      | NO           |
| 5       | 0.06           | 0.06 < 0.098                      | NO           |
| 6       | 0.04           | 0.04 < 0.098                      | NO           |

**Step 3 — Apply Box-Jenkins rules.**

The sample ACF **cuts off sharply after lag 2** (the values decay smoothly afterwards without further significant spikes). This is the signature of an **MA(2)** process.

**Step 4 — Final identification.**

The most plausible model is an **MA(2)**: $r_t = \mu + \varepsilon_t + \theta_1 \varepsilon_{t-1} + \theta_2 \varepsilon_{t-2}$.

If a non-stationary trend is present, this could be embedded as **ARIMA(0, 1, 2)** after first differencing. The PACF should be examined to confirm — for an MA(2), the PACF should tail off gradually (which is consistent with the decaying sample PACF values shown in the table).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse correlation with causation.** A high correlation between two stock returns does not mean one *causes* the other — it may be a spurious relationship driven by a common factor (e.g., interest rates).
> 2. **Always state the stationarity assumption** when defining the ACF — without it, the ACF is undefined or time-varying.
> 3. **Use $T-k$ in the Ljung-Box denominator**, not $T$ alone — this is the conventional corrected form and is what most textbooks expect.
> 4. **The 95% CI $\pm 1.96/\sqrt{T}$ is a rough approximation** — for very small $T < 30$, use the Bartlett formula which gives wider bands at larger lags.
> 5. **Do not interpret $\hat{\rho}_0$** — it is always exactly 1 by construction.
> 6. **In Part B derivations, students often forget to mention the use of the Law of Large Numbers** for consistency — this is a 2-mark penalty in valuation.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Correlation** measures linear dependence *between two series*; **autocorrelation** measures it *within one series* at different lags.
- The **sample ACF** uses centered returns; the denominator is $\sum(r_t - \bar r)^2$ (one minus the lag index for the numerator sum).
- The **ACF** identifies the **MA order** $q$ — it *cuts off* after lag $q$ for a pure MA process.
- The **PACF** identifies the **AR order** $p$ — it *cuts off* after lag $p$ for a pure AR process.
- The **95% confidence band** is $\pm 1.96/\sqrt{T}$; use it to judge statistical significance.
- The **Ljung-Box Q-statistic** jointly tests whether *all* autocorrelations up to lag $m$ are zero; under $H_0$ it follows $\chi^2_m$.
- For stationary financial returns, the ACF at low lags is typically **small and insignificant** — that is the empirical hallmark of the **Efficient Market Hypothesis**.
- $\rho_{-k} = \rho_k$ — the ACF is symmetric; only non-negative lags need to be plotted.
- **Zero correlation does not imply independence** (except for jointly normal variables).
- **Consistency** of $\hat{\rho}_k$ follows from the LLN under ergodic stationarity.
- For a **portfolio** of $N$ assets, the correlation matrix $\Sigma$ (with $\rho_{ij}$ on the off-diagonals) is a key input to mean-variance optimization.
- **Partial autocorrelation** can be computed via the **Durbin-Levinson recursion** from the sample ACF without fitting multiple regressions.
- In **R/Python**, `acf()` and `pacf()` are the standard commands, but you are expected to know the underlying formulas for the exam.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Correlogram
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET742 - FINANCIAL TIME SERIES ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Correlogram

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the rigorous framework of financial econometrics, a **correlogram** (also called an **autocorrelation plot** or **ACF plot**) is a graphical diagnostic tool that displays the **Sample Autocorrelation Function (SACF)** of a time series $X_t$ plotted as a function of the **lag** $k$. Formally, it is a two-dimensional plot in which the horizontal axis represents the lag order $k = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, m$ and the vertical axis represents the estimated autocorrelation coefficient $\hat{\rho}_k \in [-1, +1]$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** A correlogram is the *primary visual diagnostic* used to test for **stationarity**, **white noise behaviour**, **seasonality**, and the **order identification** of AR/MA processes (Box–Jenkins methodology). It is mandatory for any time series analysis.

The correlogram consists of two essential components:

1. **Spike diagram (stem plot):** Vertical lines of length $\vert \hat{\rho}_k \vert$ drawn at each integer lag $k$.
2. **Confidence bands:** Horizontal dashed lines at $\pm \dfrac{1.96}{\sqrt{n}}$ (approximately the 95% Bartlett bands), where $n$ is the sample size.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are a **doctor taking a patient's pulse over time**. Each heartbeat depends slightly on the previous one — the heart has a "memory." A correlogram is like a chart that measures, for each beat, *how much it remembers* the beat from $k$ steps ago.

- If today's return $r_t$ has **no memory** of yesterday's return $r_{t-1}$, the correlogram shows bars that are essentially **zero** (random white noise).
- If $r_t$ tends to be **high when $r_{t-1}$ was high** (positive momentum), the lag-1 bar is **tall and positive** (above the +1.96/√n line).
- If returns **oscillate** (mean-reversion), bars alternate positive and negative — the signature of a moving-average effect.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering analogy:** Think of a correlogram as a **sonar ping chart** of a financial series. Each "ping" sent out at lag $k$ returns the strength of the echo from time $t-k$. A flat ocean (no echo) = white noise; a deep canyon (strong echo) = persistent autocorrelation; a repeating pattern of echoes = seasonality or cyclicality.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (bolded for emphasis)

- **Sample Autocorrelation Coefficient:** $\hat{\rho}_k$ — dimensionless, bounded in $[-1, +1]$.
- **Lag order $k$:** integer time-shift parameter, dimensionless.
- **Bartlett Standard Error:** $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{n}}$ (asymptotic), dimensionless.
- **95% Confidence Bound:** $\pm \dfrac{1.96}{\sqrt{n}}$, dimensionless.
- **Ljung–Box Statistic $Q(m)$:** $\chi^2$-distributed with $m$ degrees of freedom.
- **Sample Size $n$:** number of observations (commonly $n \geq 30$ for asymptotic validity).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Step-by-Step Logic

The correlogram rests on three layered theoretical pillars:

**Pillar 1 — The Population Autocorrelation Function (Theoretical ACF)**

For a weakly stationary stochastic process $\{X_t\}$ with mean $\mu$, variance $\gamma_0$, and autocovariance $\gamma_k = \text{Cov}(X_t, X_{t+k})$:

- Step 1: Establish stationarity so that $\gamma_k$ depends only on lag $k$, not on $t$.
- Step 2: Define the theoretical autocorrelation coefficient as the **normalized autocovariance**:

$$
\rho_k = \dfrac{\gamma_k}{\gamma_0} = \dfrac{\text{Cov}(X_t, X_{t+k})}{\text{Var}(X_t)}
$$

- Step 3: Note the boundary properties $\rho_0 = 1$ and $\rho_{-k} = \rho_k$ (symmetry).

**Pillar 2 — The Sample Autocorrelation Function (SACF)**

Given a finite realization $X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n$ with sample mean $\bar{X} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n} X_t$:

- Step 1: Compute the sample autocovariance using the **divisor** $n$ (population estimator) or $n-k$ (unbiased estimator). The KTU/Box–Jenkins standard uses $n$ for SACF:

$$
\hat{\gamma}_k = \dfrac{1}{n} \sum_{t=1}^{n-k} (X_t - \bar{X})(X_{t+k} - \bar{X})
$$

- Step 2: Compute the sample variance $\hat{\gamma}_0 = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n} (X_t - \bar{X})^2$.
- Step 3: Form the ratio to get the SACF at lag $k$:

$$
\hat{\rho}_k = \dfrac{\hat{\gamma}_k}{\hat{\gamma}_0}
$$

**Pillar 3 — Statistical Inference via Bartlett's Formula**

- Step 1: For a **true white noise** process, Bartlett (1946) showed that asymptotically $\hat{\rho}_k \sim \mathcal{N}\!\left(0, \dfrac{1}{n}\right)$.
- Step 2: Therefore, under $H_0$: "no autocorrelation at lag $k$", we use the two-sided 95% critical region $\vert \hat{\rho}_k \vert > \dfrac{1.96}{\sqrt{n}}$.
- Step 3: Spikes that pierce the confidence bands indicate **statistically significant** autocorrelation at that lag.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is the divisor $n$ used instead of $n-k$?** For the Box–Jenkins methodology, the divisor $n$ ensures that $\hat{\rho}_k$ remains a *consistent* estimator and the resulting correlogram has better finite-sample properties for ARIMA identification. The unbiased divisor $n-k$ is preferred in econometrics but produces instability at high lags.

### 2.2 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Utility of the Correlogram |
|---|---|
| **Algorithmic Trading** | Detects momentum vs. mean-reversion regimes |
| **Risk Management (VaR)** | Validates i.i.d. assumption of returns for Monte Carlo |
| **Volatility Modelling (GARCH)** | Confirms squared returns show ARCH effects (tapered decay) |
| **Portfolio Construction** | Tests cross-asset lead–lag relationships |
| **Signal Processing** | Identifies periodic components in sensor data |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Tables use $\vert$ instead of $\vert$ to prevent markdown table breakage.**

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Domain / Range | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Theoretical ACF | $\rho_k = \dfrac{\gamma_k}{\gamma_0}$ | $\rho_k \in [-1, +1]$ | Symmetric: $\rho_{-k} = \rho_k$ |
| 2 | Sample Autocovariance | $\hat{\gamma}_k = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n-k}(X_t-\bar{X})(X_{t+k}-\bar{X})$ | Real-valued | Uses divisor $n$ |
| 3 | Sample ACF (SACF) | $\hat{\rho}_k = \dfrac{\hat{\gamma}_k}{\hat{\gamma}_0}$ | $[-1, +1]$ | $\hat{\rho}_0 = 1$ always |
| 4 | Bartlett SE (asymp.) | $\text{SE}(\hat{\rho}_k) = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{n}}$ | Positive | Valid for white noise |
| 5 | 95% Confidence Band | $\pm \dfrac{1.96}{\sqrt{n}}$ | $\approx \pm 0.196$ for $n=100$ | Two-sided $z$-test |
| 6 | Ljung–Box Statistic | $Q(m) = n(n+2)\sum_{k=1}^{m}\dfrac{\hat{\rho}_k^{\,2}}{n-k}$ | $\chi^2_m$ under $H_0$ | Joint test up to lag $m$ |
| 7 | Portmanteau p-value | $P = 1 - F_{\chi^2_m}(Q(m))$ | $[0,1]$ | $P < 0.05 \Rightarrow$ reject $H_0$ |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Sample Autocorrelation Coefficient

We start from the theoretical definition and construct the plug-in (method-of-moments) estimator.

**Step 1.** The theoretical autocovariance is

$$
\gamma_k \;=\; \mathbb{E}\!\left[\,(X_t - \mu)(X_{t+k} - \mu)\,\right]
$$

where $\mu = \mathbb{E}[X_t]$ (constant under weak stationarity).

**Step 2.** Replace the unknown population mean $\mu$ by its sample analogue $\bar{X} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n} X_t$ (method of moments substitution).

**Step 3.** Replace the population expectation $\mathbb{E}[\cdot]$ by the sample average $\dfrac{1}{n}\sum (\cdot)$ over the available $n-k$ overlapping pairs.

**Step 4.** This yields the *biased* (but consistent) sample autocovariance estimator:

$$
\hat{\gamma}_k \;=\; \frac{1}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n-k} (X_t - \bar{X})(X_{t+k} - \bar{X})
$$

*Textual explanation:* Each term $(X_t - \bar{X})(X_{t+k} - \bar{X})$ is the product of *deviations* from the mean at two times separated by $k$ steps. Summing them and dividing by $n$ (not $n-k$) gives the biased estimator that Box and Jenkins prefer.

**Step 5.** The sample variance corresponds to $k=0$:

$$
\hat{\gamma}_0 \;=\; \frac{1}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n} (X_t - \bar{X})^{2}
$$

**Step 6.** Form the ratio to obtain the SACF, which is the **plotted height** of the correlogram spike at lag $k$:

$$
\hat{\rho}_k \;=\; \frac{\hat{\gamma}_k}{\hat{\gamma}_0} \;=\; \frac{\sum_{t=1}^{n-k}(X_t-\bar{X})(X_{t+k}-\bar{X})}{\sum_{t=1}^{n}(X_t-\bar{X})^{2}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The denominator $\sum (X_t-\bar{X})^{2}$ is a *constant* (does not depend on $k$), so it acts purely as a **normalization factor** ensuring $\hat{\rho}_k \in [-1, +1]$ and $\hat{\rho}_0 = 1$ identically.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Bartlett Standard Error

Assume the data $X_t$ is generated by a **linear process**: $X_t = \mu + \sum_{j=0}^{\infty} \psi_j \varepsilon_{t-j}$ with i.i.d. $\varepsilon_t \sim (0, \sigma^2)$.

**Step 1.** Express the sample autocovariance as a quadratic form in the innovations.

**Step 2.** Apply the **Central Limit Theorem** for quadratic forms (a classical result of Wald, 1949):

$$
\sqrt{n}\,(\hat{\rho}_k - \rho_k) \;\xrightarrow{d}\; \mathcal{N}\!\left(0,\, \sum_{j=-\infty}^{\infty} \rho_j^{\,2}\right)
$$

**Step 3.** Under the **null hypothesis** $H_0 : \rho_k = 0$ for *all* $k \geq 1$ (i.e., white noise), substitute $\rho_j = \mathbb{1}_{\{j=0\}}$ into the variance sum.

**Step 4.** The only surviving term is $j=0$, giving $\rho_0^{2} = 1$. All other $\rho_j = 0$ vanish.

**Step 5.** Therefore, the asymptotic variance simplifies dramatically to $1/n$:

$$
\text{Var}(\hat{\rho}_k) \;\approx\; \frac{1}{n} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \text{SE}(\hat{\rho}_k) \;=\; \frac{1}{\sqrt{n}}
$$

**Step 6.** The 95% confidence band follows by multiplying the SE by the standard normal 97.5-th percentile $z_{0.025} = 1.96$:

$$
\text{95\% CI:} \quad \hat{\rho}_k \;\in\; \left[-\frac{1.96}{\sqrt{n}},\;\; +\frac{1.96}{\sqrt{n}}\right]
$$

### 3.3 Derivation of the Ljung–Box Portmanteau Statistic

The correlogram is *visual*; a *numerical* complement is the Ljung–Box (1978) statistic.

**Step 1.** For each lag $k \in \{1, \ldots, m\}$, the individual test statistic is $n\hat{\rho}_k^{2}$.

**Step 2.** Summing these and applying a finite-sample correction $(n+2)/(n-k)$ (which stabilizes the $\chi^2$ approximation for small $n$):

$$
Q(m) \;=\; n(n+2)\sum_{k=1}^{m} \frac{\hat{\rho}_k^{\,2}}{n-k}
$$

**Step 3.** Under $H_0$: "all $m$ autocorrelations are simultaneously zero", $Q(m) \sim \chi^{2}_m$ asymptotically.

**Step 4.** Decision rule: reject $H_0$ if $Q(m) > \chi^{2}_{m,\,0.95}$ (or equivalently $p$-value $< 0.05$).

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Interpreting Correlogram Patterns (KTU Favourite Question)

| Pattern Observed | Correlogram Shape | Statistical Conclusion | Likely Process |
|---|---|---|---|
| All $\hat{\rho}_k$ inside bands | Flat, spikes within $\pm 1.96/\sqrt{n}$ | White noise (i.i.d.) | Random Walk of innovations |
| Slow linear/quadratic decay | $\hat{\rho}_k$ decays to zero slowly | Non-stationary | AR(1) with $\phi \to 1$ / unit root |
| Exponential decay to zero | $\hat{\rho}_k = \phi^{k}$ form | Stationary | AR($p$) |
| Cuts off sharply after lag $q$ | $\hat{\rho}_k = 0$ for $k > q$ | Moving average structure | MA($q$) |
| Single spike at lag $s$ | Spike at seasonal lag $s, 2s, \ldots$ | Seasonality | SARIMA |
| Tapering decay of $\hat{\rho}_k^{2}$ | ACF of squared series | ARCH effects | GARCH($p,q$) |
| Alternating signs | $\hat{\rho}_k$ oscillates | Mean-reversion / over-correction | AR(2) with negative roots |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
