## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper PEIET742]**
*Define a correlogram. What does it signify in time series analysis?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A correlogram is a plot of the **sample autocorrelation function (SACF)** $\hat{\rho}_k$ against the lag $k$. In time series analysis, it serves three primary purposes:
1. Tests for **white noise** (randomness) of a series.
2. Identifies the **order** of autoregressive (AR) and moving-average (MA) components.
3. Detects **non-stationarity** and **seasonality**.

It is constructed by plotting vertical spikes of length $\vert \hat{\rho}_k \vert$ at each lag $k$ together with **95% Bartlett confidence bands** at $\pm 1.96/\sqrt{n}$.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Supplementary]**
*State and explain the Bartlett formula for the standard error of the sample autocorrelation coefficient.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Under the null hypothesis that the series is white noise, Bartlett (1946) showed that the sample autocorrelation $\hat{\rho}_k$ is asymptotically normally distributed with

$$
\hat{\rho}_k \;\sim\; \mathcal{N}\!\left(0,\; \frac{1}{n}\right)
$$

Thus the standard error is $\text{SE}(\hat{\rho}_k) = 1/\sqrt{n}$, and the 95% confidence band is $\pm 1.96/\sqrt{n}$. Any spike exceeding this band is **statistically significant** at the 5% level.

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Descriptive Questions (with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Every Part B question in End-Semester Examinations (ESE) carries **internal choice**. You must answer either Option A **or** Option B in full. Each part (a) is worth **7 marks** and part (b) is worth **7 marks**.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks):** *Correlogram Construction and Inference*

**[KTU University Exam — Sample Question, Module 1, PEIET742]**

**(a)** Derive the sample autocorrelation function $\hat{\rho}_k$ from first principles. Explain the role of the **divisor $n$** in its construction and why Box–Jenkins prefer it over the unbiased divisor $n-k$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A financial analyst observes 100 daily log-returns of a stock. The computed SACF values are:

| Lag $k$ | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 | 8 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $\hat{\rho}_k$ | 0.21 | 0.18 | 0.04 | -0.02 | 0.03 | -0.01 | 0.02 | -0.05 |

The 95% Bartlett band for $n=100$ is $\pm 0.196$. Identify the significant lags, comment on the stationarity, and suggest a tentative model. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):** *(7 marks — full derivation)*

1. *Start with theoretical definition* of $\gamma_k = \mathbb{E}[(X_t-\mu)(X_{t+k}-\mu)]$ — 1 mark.
2. *Replace $\mu$ by $\bar{X}$* and $\mathbb{E}[\cdot]$ by the sample mean over $n-k$ pairs — 2 marks.
3. *Arrive at* $\hat{\gamma}_k = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n-k}(X_t-\bar{X})(X_{t+k}-\bar{X})$ — 2 marks.
4. *Normalize* to obtain $\hat{\rho}_k = \hat{\gamma}_k / \hat{\gamma}_0$ — 1 mark.
5. *Discuss divisor choice:* using $n$ gives a *biased but consistent* estimator with $\hat{\rho}_0 = 1$ exactly and better finite-sample behaviour for ARIMA identification. The unbiased divisor $n-k$ becomes numerically unstable at high lags (variance blows up) and is therefore avoided in Box–Jenkins. — 1 mark.

**Model Solution (b):** *(7 marks — data-driven inference)*

1. *Compute the critical band:* $\pm 1.96/\sqrt{100} = \pm 0.196$. (1 mark)
2. *Identify significant lags:* Lags 1 (0.21) and 2 (0.18 — borderline). Lag 1 clearly exceeds 0.196 in absolute value; lag 2 marginally above. (2 marks)
3. *Pattern interpretation:* Significant short-run positive autocorrelation at lag 1 suggests **momentum / persistence**. Absence of significant autocorrelation beyond lag 2 suggests the dependence dies out quickly. (2 marks)
4. *Stationarity:* No slow decay ⇒ **stationary series** (not a unit root). (1 mark)
5. *Model suggestion:* Tentative **AR(1) or AR(2)** with positive coefficient. (1 mark)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks):** *Ljung–Box Test and White Noise Diagnostics*

**[KTU University Exam — Sample Question, Module 1, PEIET742]**

**(a)** State and derive the Ljung–Box portmanteau statistic $Q(m)$. Explain how it complements the correlogram. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a series of $n = 250$ daily returns, the first 10 sample autocorrelations are given as:

$$
\hat{\rho}_1 = 0.08,\ \hat{\rho}_2 = 0.05,\ \hat{\rho}_3 = 0.06,\ \hat{\rho}_4 = 0.04,\ \hat{\rho}_5 = 0.03
$$
$$
\hat{\rho}_6 = 0.07,\ \hat{\rho}_7 = 0.04,\ \hat{\rho}_8 = 0.02,\ \hat{\rho}_9 = 0.05,\ \hat{\rho}_{10} = 0.03
$$

Compute $Q(10)$ and test $H_0$ (white noise) at the 5% level, given $\chi^2_{10,\,0.95} = 18.31$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):** *(7 marks — theory)*

1. *Definition of $H_0$:* $H_0: \rho_1 = \rho_2 = \cdots = \rho_m = 0$ (white noise). (1 mark)
2. *Box–Pierce precursor:* $Q_{\text{BP}}(m) = n\sum_{k=1}^{m}\hat{\rho}_k^{2}$ (1 mark)
3. *Limitation of Box–Pierce:* poor small-sample approximation to $\chi^2_m$. (1 mark)
4. *Ljung–Box correction:* multiply each term by $(n+2)/(n-k)$ to yield $Q(m) = n(n+2)\sum_{k=1}^{m}\hat{\rho}_k^{2}/(n-k)$. (2 marks)
5. *Asymptotic distribution:* Under $H_0$, $Q(m) \sim \chi^2_m$. (1 mark)
6. *Complementarity with correlogram:* While the correlogram is *visual* and lag-wise, $Q(m)$ is a *single numerical statistic* that aggregates evidence across $m$ lags. Used together in residual diagnostics. (1 mark)

**Model Solution (b):** *(7 marks — numerical computation)*

**Step 1.** List the squared autocorrelations:

$$
\hat{\rho}_1^{2} = 0.0064,\ \hat{\rho}_2^{2} = 0.0025,\ \hat{\rho}_3^{2} = 0.0036,\ \hat{\rho}_4^{2} = 0.0016,\ \hat{\rho}_5^{2} = 0.0009
$$
$$
\hat{\rho}_6^{2} = 0.0049,\ \hat{\rho}_7^{2} = 0.0016,\ \hat{\rho}_8^{2} = 0.0004,\ \hat{\rho}_9^{2} = 0.0025,\ \hat{\rho}_{10}^{2} = 0.0009
$$

**Step 2.** Sum of squared autocorrelations: $S = 0.0253$ (1 mark)

**Step 3.** Compute the correction factor $n(n+2) = 250 \times 252 = 63000$. (1 mark)

**Step 4.** Form the Ljung–Box statistic:

$$
Q(10) = 63000 \times \sum_{k=1}^{10} \frac{\hat{\rho}_k^{2}}{n-k}
$$

**Step 5.** Evaluate each term $\hat{\rho}_k^{2}/(n-k)$:

| $k$ | $\hat{\rho}_k^{2}$ | $n-k$ | $\hat{\rho}_k^{2}/(n-k)$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0.0064 | 249 | 0.0000257 |
| 2 | 0.0025 | 248 | 0.0000101 |
| 3 | 0.0036 | 247 | 0.0000146 |
| 4 | 0.0016 | 246 | 0.0000065 |
| 5 | 0.0009 | 245 | 0.0000037 |
| 6 | 0.0049 | 244 | 0.0000201 |
| 7 | 0.0016 | 243 | 0.0000066 |
| 8 | 0.0004 | 242 | 0.0000017 |
| 9 | 0.0025 | 241 | 0.0000104 |
| 10 | 0.0009 | 240 | 0.0000038 |

Sum of all terms $\approx 1.033 \times 10^{-4}$. (2 marks)

**Step 6.** Final calculation:

$$
Q(10) = 63000 \times 1.033 \times 10^{-4} \approx 6.51
$$

(1 mark)

**Step 7.** Comparison with critical value: $Q(10) = 6.51 < 18.31 = \chi^2_{10,\,0.95}$. **Fail to reject $H_0$.** (1 mark)

**Step 8.** Conclusion: The series is consistent with **white noise** at the 5% level. No ARIMA structure is detectable. (0.5 mark)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the divisor:** Writing $\hat{\gamma}_k$ with $n-k$ instead of $n$ in Box–Jenkins context → 1-mark penalty.
> 2. **Confusing ACF with PACF:** ACF = $\hat{\rho}_k$ vs. PACF = $\hat{\phi}_{kk}$. The correlogram plots ACF only.
> 3. **Misreading the Bartlett band:** Using $1/\sqrt{n}$ instead of $1.96/\sqrt{n}$ for the *95%* band. The 68% band is $\pm 1/\sqrt{n}$.
> 4. **Skipping units / signs:** Always report $\hat{\rho}_k$ with its sign and state whether it lies *inside* or *outside* the band.
> 5. **Ljung–Box miscalculation:** Forgetting the $(n+2)$ factor, which is the *crucial small-sample correction* that distinguishes Ljung–Box from Box–Pierce. Examiners explicitly test this.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** A correlogram is a plot of $\hat{\rho}_k$ (SACF) vs. lag $k$, used for diagnosing time series structure.
- **Formula:** $\hat{\rho}_k = \frac{\sum_{t=1}^{n-k}(X_t-\bar{X})(X_{t+k}-\bar{X})}{\sum_{t=1}^{n}(X_t-\bar{X})^{2}}$ — divisor $n$ (Box–Jenkins).
- **Range:** $\hat{\rho}_k \in [-1, +1]$, and $\hat{\rho}_0 = 1$ always.
- **Bartlett SE:** $\text{SE}(\hat{\rho}_k) = 1/\sqrt{n}$ under white noise $H_0$.
- **95% Confidence Band:** $\pm 1.96/\sqrt{n}$ (≈ ±0.196 for $n=100$).
- **Ljung–Box Statistic:** $Q(m) = n(n+2)\sum_{k=1}^{m}\frac{\hat{\rho}_k^{2}}{n-k} \sim \chi^2_m$ under $H_0$.
- **Pattern Recognition:** *Slow decay* ⇒ non-stationary; *exponential decay* ⇒ AR($p$); *cutoff after lag $q$* ⇒ MA($q$); *spike at multiples of $s$* ⇒ seasonal.
- **White Noise Test:** All spikes inside band **and** $Q(m) < \chi^2_{m,\,0.95}$.
- **Distinguish:** Correlogram ≠ Periodogram. Correlogram = time-domain ACF; Periodogram = frequency-domain spectral density.
- **Golden Rule:** Always check the correlogram *after* achieving stationarity (differencing if needed); a non-stationary correlogram is **uninformative** for ARMA order identification.
- **Engineering Use:** Risk management (VaR i.i.d. check), algorithmic trading (momentum/mean-reversion), GARCH (ARCH effect in squared returns ACF).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stationarity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET742 - FINANCIAL TIME SERIES ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Stationarity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Stationarity?

In the context of financial time series analysis, a stochastic process $\{X_t, t \in T\}$ is called a **time series**. Stationarity is the cornerstone assumption of nearly every classical time series model (AR, MA, ARMA, ARIMA). Intuitively, a time series is stationary if its **statistical properties do not change over time**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (Strict Stationarity)**
> A time series $\{X_t\}$ is **strictly stationary** if, for any positive integer $k$ and any set of time points $t_1, t_2, \ldots, t_k$, the joint distribution of $(X_{t_1}, X_{t_2}, \ldots, X_{t_k})$ is identical to the joint distribution of $(X_{t_1+h}, X_{t_2+h}, \ldots, X_{t_k+h})$ for every integer $h$ (i.e., the joint distribution is invariant under time shifts).

However, strict stationarity is a very strong requirement that requires knowledge of the entire joint distribution. In practice, we almost always relax this to a more useful concept.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (Weak / Covariance / Second-Order Stationarity)**
> A time series $\{X_t\}$ is said to be **weakly stationary** (or second-order stationary) if the following three conditions are satisfied:
> 1. The mean function $\mu_X(t) = E(X_t)$ is **constant** and independent of time, i.e., $E(X_t) = \mu$ for all $t \in T$.
> 2. The variance function $\sigma_X^2(t) = \text{Var}(X_t)$ is **finite and constant**, i.e., $E(X_t^2) < \infty$ and $\text{Var}(X_t) = \sigma^2$ for all $t$.
> 3. The autocovariance function depends **only on the time lag** $k = \vert s - t \vert$, not on the actual time $s$ or $t$.

### 1.2 The "Lakeside" Analogy — Intuition for Students

Imagine you are standing beside a **calm lake** on a windy afternoon. The water surface is always moving — ripples go up, ripples go down — but:
- The **average water level** stays the same throughout the day.
- The **height of ripples** (variance) is the same at 9 AM, 12 PM, and 5 PM.
- The **pattern of correlation** between today's wave and tomorrow's wave is identical to the pattern between Monday's and Tuesday's waves.

That lake is **stationary**. The water level fluctuates around a constant mean with a constant variance, and its memory structure (autocovariance) is time-invariant.

> [!TIP]
> **Non-Stationary Analogy — The Mumbai Monsoon River**
> Consider the water level of a river during a monsoon. In June the river is low, in July it rises dramatically, and by September it begins to fall. The mean level, the variance, and the dynamics all change with time. This is a **non-stationary** series. The same equation that worked in June will not work in September.

### 1.3 Why Stationarity Matters in Finance

- Most financial econometric models (AR, MA, ARMA, GARCH) are derived assuming a **stationary underlying data-generating process**.
- Stationary series have **mean-reverting behavior** — extreme values tend to revert to the long-run mean, making forecasting meaningful.
- Non-stationary series can lead to **spurious regression**, where two unrelated trending variables appear highly correlated.
- The famous saying: *"If a series is non-stationary, we can model the differences but rarely the levels."*

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Misconception:** A series is NOT non-stationary simply because it fluctuates. Even violently oscillating series (e.g., white noise with high variance) are stationary as long as the statistical properties remain time-invariant.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Logical Breakdown of Weak Stationarity

The three conditions of weak stationarity can be broken down as follows:

**Step 1 — Constant Mean**
For a time series $\{X_t\}$, the mean function is defined as:

$$
\mu_X(t) = E(X_t)
$$

Weak stationarity requires $\mu_X(t) = \mu$ for every $t$. This means the **expected value does not drift** over time. In a stationary AR(1) process $X_t = \phi X_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t$, the mean is $\mu = 0$ (assuming $\varepsilon_t$ has zero mean) **only if** $\vert \phi \vert < 1$.

**Step 2 — Constant Finite Variance**
The variance is $\sigma_X^2(t) = E[(X_t - \mu)^2] < \infty$. A finite second moment is essential because it guarantees that the autocovariance and autocorrelation are well-defined quantities.

**Step 3 — Lag-Dependent Autocovariance**
The autocovariance between $X_s$ and $X_t$ is:

$$
\gamma_X(s, t) = E[(X_s - \mu_X(s))(X_t - \mu_X(t))]
$$

Weak stationarity demands $\gamma_X(s, t) = \gamma_X(\vert s - t \vert) = \gamma_X(k)$ where $k$ is the time lag.

### 2.2 The Autocorrelation Function (ACF)

The **autocorrelation function** normalizes the autocovariance to lie in the range $[-1, 1]$:

$$
\rho_X(k) = \frac{\gamma_X(k)}{\gamma_X(0)} = \frac{\gamma_X(k)}{\sigma_X^2}, \quad k = 0, \pm 1, \pm 2, \ldots
$$

This is one of the most useful **diagnostic tools** in time series analysis. A slowly decaying ACF indicates non-stationarity, while a rapidly decaying ACF suggests stationarity.

### 2.3 Joint Stationarity

When analyzing relationships between two (or more) time series $\{X_t\}$ and $\{Y_t\}$, we require **joint stationarity**:

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (Joint Stationarity)**
> Two time series $\{X_t\}$ and $\{Y_t\}$ are **jointly stationary** if:
> 1. Both $\{X_t\}$ and $\{Y_t\}$ are individually weakly stationary.
> 2. The cross-covariance function $\gamma_{XY}(s, t) = E[(X_s - \mu_X)(Y_t - \mu_Y)]$ depends only on the lag $k = \vert s - t \vert$.

Joint stationarity is critical when building models like Vector Autoregression (VAR) and Transfer Function models.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formula Reference Table for KTU Examinations**

| **Concept** | **Mathematical Expression** | **Stationarity Condition** |
|-------------|---------------------------|---------------------------|
| Mean Function | $\mu_X(t) = E(X_t)$ | Must be constant $\mu$ for all $t$ |
| Variance Function | $\sigma_X^2(t) = E[(X_t - \mu)^2]$ | Must be finite and constant $\sigma^2$ |
| Autocovariance | $\gamma_X(s,t) = E[(X_s - \mu)(X_t - \mu)]$ | Must depend only on lag $\vert s - t \vert$ |
| Autocorrelation | $\rho_X(k) = \gamma_X(k) / \gamma_X(0)$ | $\vert \rho_X(k) \vert \leq 1$ for all $k$ |
| Cross-Covariance | $\gamma_{XY}(s,t) = E[(X_s - \mu_X)(Y_t - \mu_Y)]$ | Function of lag only for joint stationarity |
| Lag-$k$ ACF Estimate | $\hat{\rho}_k = \dfrac{\sum_{t=k+1}^{n}(X_t - \bar{X})(X_{t-k} - \bar{X})}{\sum_{t=1}^{n}(X_t - \bar{X})^2}$ | Sample ACF (Bartlett formula for SE) |
| Differencing Operator | $\nabla X_t = X_t - X_{t-1}$ | Used to convert non-stationary to stationary |

### 2.5 Properties of Autocovariance $\gamma(k)$ for Weakly Stationary Series

For any weakly stationary series, the autocovariance function $\gamma(k)$ must satisfy:

1. **Non-negativity at zero:** $\gamma(0) = \sigma^2 \geq 0$
2. **Bounded by variance:** $\vert \gamma(k) \vert \leq \gamma(0)$ for all $k$
3. **Symmetry:** $\gamma(k) = \gamma(-k)$
4. **Positive semi-definiteness:** For any $a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_n \in \mathbb{R}$ and $t_1, t_2, \ldots, t_n \in T$:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{n}\sum_{j=1}^{n} a_i a_j \, \gamma(t_i - t_j) \geq 0
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Utility in Engineering & Finance**
> - **Forecasting:** Mean reversion in stationary series allows reliable long-horizon forecasts.
> - **Risk Management:** Constant variance of stationary returns enables stable Value-at-Risk (VaR) calculations.
> - **Signal Processing:** Autocovariance functions are used in radar, speech recognition, and biomedical signal analysis (e.g., ECG monitoring) to identify periodic structures.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: $\gamma(0) = E(X_t^2) - \mu^2$ (Variance Identity)

Starting from the definition of autocovariance at lag $k = 0$:

$$
\gamma(0) = E[(X_t - \mu)(X_t - \mu)] = E[(X_t - \mu)^2]
$$

Expanding the squared term:

$$
\gamma(0) = E[X_t^2 - 2\mu X_t + \mu^2]
$$

Applying the linearity of expectation:

$$
\gamma(0) = E(X_t^2) - 2\mu E(X_t) + \mu^2
$$

Since $E(X_t) = \mu$ under weak stationarity:

$$
\gamma(0) = E(X_t^2) - 2\mu^2 + \mu^2 = E(X_t^2) - \mu^2
$$

Rearranging:

$$
\boxed{E(X_t^2) = \gamma(0) + \mu^2}
$$

This identity is the **foundation** of mean-square analysis in stationary processes.

### 3.2 Derivation: $\vert \gamma(k) \vert \leq \gamma(0)$ (Cauchy–Schwarz Inequality)

By the **Cauchy–Schwarz inequality** for two random variables $U$ and $V$ with finite second moments:

$$
\bigl\vert E[UV] \bigr\vert \leq \sqrt{E[U^2] \cdot E[V^2]}
$$

Let $U = (X_t - \mu)$ and $V = (X_{t+k} - \mu)$. Then:

$$
E[UV] = E[(X_t - \mu)(X_{t+k} - \mu)] = \gamma(k)
$$

And:

$$
E[U^2] = E[V^2] = E[(X_t - \mu)^2] = \gamma(0)
$$

Substituting into Cauchy–Schwarz:

$$
\bigl\vert \gamma(k) \bigr\vert \leq \sqrt{\gamma(0) \cdot \gamma(0)} = \gamma(0)
$$

Hence:

$$
\boxed{\bigl\vert \gamma(k) \bigr\vert \leq \gamma(0) \quad \text{for all } k}
$$

Dividing both sides by $\gamma(0)$:

$$
\bigl\vert \rho(k) \bigr\vert \leq 1
$$

This proves the bounded nature of the autocorrelation function.

### 3.3 Derivation: Mean, Variance, and ACF of an AR(1) Process

Consider the AR(1) process $X_t = \phi X_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t$ where $\varepsilon_t \sim WN(0, \sigma_\varepsilon^2)$ and $\vert \phi \vert < 1$.

**Step 1 — Finding the Mean**

Taking expectations of both sides:

$$
E(X_t) = \phi E(X_{t-1}) + E(\varepsilon_t) = \phi E(X_{t-1}) + 0
$$

Let $\mu = E(X_t) = E(X_{t-1})$ (constant by stationarity). Therefore:

$$
\mu = \phi \mu \implies \mu(1 - \phi) = 0 \implies \mu = 0
$$

> **Note:** The mean of a causal AR(1) process is zero. If there were a non-zero intercept, e.g., $X_t = c + \phi X_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t$, the mean would be $\mu = c / (1 - \phi)$.

**Step 2 — Finding the Variance**

$$
\text{Var}(X_t) = E(X_t^2) = E[(\phi X_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t)^2]
$$

Expanding:

$$
E(X_t^2) = \phi^2 E(X_{t-1}^2) + 2\phi E(X_{t-1}\varepsilon_t) + E(\varepsilon_t^2)
$$

Since $\varepsilon_t$ is white noise and independent of $X_{t-1}$, the cross-term vanishes:

$$
E(X_{t-1}\varepsilon_t) = E(X_{t-1}) \cdot E(\varepsilon_t) = 0
$$

So:

$$
\gamma(0) = \phi^2 \gamma(0) + \sigma_\varepsilon^2
$$

Solving:

$$
\gamma(0)(1 - \phi^2) = \sigma_\varepsilon^2 \implies \boxed{\gamma(0) = \frac{\sigma_\varepsilon^2}{1 - \phi^2}}
$$

**Step 3 — Finding the Autocovariance $\gamma(k)$**

Multiplying the AR(1) equation by $X_{t-k}$ (for $k \geq 1$) and taking expectations:

$$
E(X_t X_{t-k}) = \phi E(X_{t-1} X_{t-k}) + E(\varepsilon_t X_{t-k})
$$

Since $\varepsilon_t$ is independent of past values, $E(\varepsilon_t X_{t-k}) = 0$ for $k \geq 1$. Thus:

$$
\gamma(k) = \phi \, \gamma(k-1), \quad k \geq 1
$$

This is a first-order linear recurrence whose solution is:

$$
\boxed{\gamma(k) = \phi^{\,k} \, \gamma(0) = \frac{\phi^{\,k} \, \sigma_\varepsilon^2}{1 - \phi^2}, \quad k \geq 0}
$$

**Step 4 — Autocorrelation Function**

Dividing by $\gamma(0)$:

$$
\boxed{\rho(k) = \phi^{\,k}, \quad k \geq 0}
$$

This exponential decay confirms that the AR(1) process is **stationary only when** $\vert \phi \vert < 1$.

### 3.4 Derivation: Properties of the ACF for MA(1) Process

Consider the MA(1) process $X_t = \varepsilon_t + \theta \varepsilon_{t-1}$ where $\varepsilon_t \sim WN(0, \sigma_\varepsilon^2)$.

**Step 1 — Mean**

$$
E(X_t) = E(\varepsilon_t) + \theta E(\varepsilon_{t-1}) = 0 + 0 = 0
$$

**Step 2 — Variance**

$$
\gamma(0) = E(X_t^2) = E[(\varepsilon_t + \theta \varepsilon_{t-1})^2]
$$

Expanding (cross-term is zero because $\varepsilon_t$ and $\varepsilon_{t-1}$ are independent):

$$
\gamma(0) = E(\varepsilon_t^2) + \theta^2 E(\varepsilon_{t-1}^2) = \sigma_\varepsilon^2(1 + \theta^2)
$$

**Step 3 — Autocovariance at Lag 1**

$$
\gamma(1) = E(X_t X_{t-1}) = E[(\varepsilon_t + \theta \varepsilon_{t-1})(\varepsilon_{t-1} + \theta \varepsilon_{t-2})]
$$

Expanding:

$$
\gamma(1) = E(\varepsilon_t \varepsilon_{t-1}) + \theta E(\varepsilon_t \varepsilon_{t-2}) + \theta E(\varepsilon_{t-1}^2) + \theta^2 E(\varepsilon_{t-1} \varepsilon_{t-2})
$$

Only one term survives (the $E(\varepsilon_{t-1}^2)$ term):

$$
\boxed{\gamma(1) = \theta \, \sigma_\varepsilon^2}
$$

**Step 4 — Autocovariance at Lag $k \geq 2$**

$$
\gamma(k) = E[(\varepsilon_t + \theta \varepsilon_{t-1})(\varepsilon_{t-k} + \theta \varepsilon_{t-k-1})] = 0, \quad k \geq 2
$$

**Step 5 — Autocorrelation Function**

$$
\boxed{\rho(0) = 1, \quad \rho(1) = \frac{\theta}{1 + \theta^2}, \quad \rho(k) = 0 \text{ for } k \geq 2}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Significance:** The MA(1) ACF **cuts off after lag 1** — a sharp diagnostic signature that distinguishes MA processes from AR processes (whose ACF decays gradually).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Hierarchical Classification of Stationarity

```mermaid
graph TD

    B --> B2[Invariant under time shift h]
    B --> B3[Very strong assumption]
    B --> B4[Rarely verifiable in practice]

    C --> C1[Constant mean E Xt = mu]
    C --> C2[Constant variance Var Xt = sigma squared]
    C --> C3[Autocovariance depends only on lag]
    C --> C4[Foundation of AR MA ARMA models]

    D --> D1[Two or more series Xt and Yt]
    D --> D2[Both individually stationary]
    D --> D3[Cross covariance depends only on lag]
    D --> D4[Required for VAR and Transfer Function]

    E --> E2[Normal or stable distributions]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** Define **weak stationarity** of a time series. State the three conditions that must be satisfied.

> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> A time series $\{X_t\}$ is said to be **weakly stationary** if:
> 1. The **mean function** $E(X_t) = \mu$ is **constant** for all $t$.
> 2. The **variance** $\text{Var}(X_t) = \sigma^2$ is **finite and constant** for all $t$.
> 3. The **autocovariance function** $\gamma(s, t) = E[(X_s - \mu)(X_t - \mu)]$ depends only on the **time lag** $k = \vert s - t \vert$ and not on the actual time $s$ or $t$.
>
> *(1 mark for proper definition, 1 mark for first two conditions, 1 mark for autocovariance condition.)*

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** State any **four properties** of the **autocovariance function** of a weakly stationary series.

> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> For a weakly stationary series with autocovariance $\gamma(k)$:
> 1. $\gamma(0) = \sigma^2 \geq 0$ — non-negative variance.
> 2. $\gamma(k) = \gamma(-k)$ — symmetry.
> 3. $\vert \gamma(k) \vert \leq \gamma(0)$ — bounded by the variance.
> 4. $\gamma(k)$ is **positive semi-definite**, i.e., $\sum_i \sum_j a_i a_j \gamma(t_i - t_j) \geq 0$ for any real constants $a_i$.
>
> *(0.75 mark each. Writing "all four in a single numbered list" earns full credit.)*

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### ✅ **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a)** Define **strict stationarity** and **weak stationarity**. Show that strict stationarity, together with the assumption of finite second moments, implies weak stationarity. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider the **MA(1) process** $X_t = \varepsilon_t + 0.6\,\varepsilon_{t-1}$, where $\varepsilon_t \sim WN(0, 1)$. Compute the **mean**, **variance**, **autocovariance function $\gamma(k)$**, and **autocorrelation function $\rho(k)$**. Comment on the stationarity of the process. **(7 marks)**

#### 📝 Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — Definitions and Proof (7 Marks):**

**Definition 1 (Strict Stationarity):** A series $\{X_t\}$ is strictly stationary if, for any integer $k \geq 1$ and any time points $t_1, t_2, \ldots, t_k$ and any shift $h$:

$$
P(X_{t_1} \leq x_1, \ldots, X_{t_k} \leq x_k) = P(X_{t_1+h} \leq x_1, \ldots, X_{t_k+h} \leq x_k)
$$

for all $x_1, \ldots, x_k \in \mathbb{R}$. *(2 marks)*

**Definition 2 (Weak Stationarity):** A series $\{X_t\}$ is weakly stationary if $E(X_t) = \mu$ (constant), $\text{Var}(X_t) = \sigma^2 < \infty$ (constant), and $\gamma(s, t) = \gamma(\vert s - t \vert)$ depends only on the lag. *(2 marks)*

**Proof that Strict + Finite Second Moment $\Rightarrow$ Weak:**

Let $\{X_t\}$ be strictly stationary with $E(X_t^2) < \infty$. For $k = 1$:

$$
P(X_t \leq x) = P(X_{t+h} \leq x) \quad \text{for all } h
$$

This implies the **marginal distribution** of $X_t$ does not depend on $t$, so $E(X_t) = \mu$ is a constant, and $\text{Var}(X_t) = \sigma^2$ is finite and constant. *(1 mark)*

For $k = 2$, the joint distribution of $(X_s, X_t)$ equals that of $(X_{s+h}, X_{t+h})$:

$$
\gamma(s, t) = E[(X_s - \mu)(X_t - \mu)] = E[(X_{s+h} - \mu)(X_{t+h} - \mu)] = \gamma(s+h, t+h)
$$

Thus the autocovariance depends only on the lag $k = \vert s - t \vert$. *(2 marks)*

> $\blacksquare$

**Part (b) — MA(1) Process Analysis (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Mean:**

$$
E(X_t) = E(\varepsilon_t) + 0.6\,E(\varepsilon_{t-1}) = 0 + 0 = 0
$$

*(1 mark)*

**Step 2 — Variance (lag-0 autocovariance):**

$$
\gamma(0) = E(X_t^2) = E[(\varepsilon_t + 0.6\,\varepsilon_{t-1})^2]
$$

$$
\gamma(0) = E(\varepsilon_t^2) + 2(0.6)E(\varepsilon_t \varepsilon_{t-1}) + 0.36 E(\varepsilon_{t-1}^2)
$$

Since $\varepsilon_t$ and $\varepsilon_{t-1}$ are independent, $E(\varepsilon_t \varepsilon_{t-1}) = 0$:

$$
\gamma(0) = 1 + 0.36 = 1.36
$$

*(2 marks)*

**Step 3 — Lag-1 Autocovariance:**

$$
\gamma(1) = E(X_t X_{t-1}) = E[(\varepsilon_t + 0.6\,\varepsilon_{t-1})(\varepsilon_{t-1} + 0.6\,\varepsilon_{t-2})]
$$

Expanding — only the cross term $\theta E(\varepsilon_{t-1}^2) = 0.6(1) = 0.6$ survives:

$$
\gamma(1) = 0.6
$$

*(2 marks)*

**Step 4 — Lag-$k \geq 2$ Autocovariance:**

$$
\gamma(k) = 0 \quad \text{for } k \geq 2
$$

*(1 mark)*

**Step 5 — Autocorrelation Function:**

$$
\rho(0) = 1, \quad \rho(1) = \frac{0.6}{1.36} \approx 0.441, \quad \rho(k) = 0 \text{ for } k \geq 2
$$

*(1 mark)*

**Conclusion:** The MA(1) process is **weakly stationary for any value of $\theta$** (no restriction on $\theta$ for stationarity, unlike AR processes).

---

#### ✅ **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** What is **joint stationarity**? Explain with reference to the **cross-covariance function** between two time series $X_t$ and $Y_t$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Suppose $X_t = 0.7 X_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t$, where $\varepsilon_t \sim WN(0, 4)$ is white noise. Show that the process is **weakly stationary** by deriving the **mean**, **variance**, and **autocorrelation function** $\rho(k)$. **(7 marks)**

#### 📝 Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — Joint Stationarity (7 Marks):**

**Definition:** Two time series $\{X_t\}$ and $\{Y_t\}$ are **jointly stationary** if:
- Both $\{X_t\}$ and $\{Y_t\}$ are individually weakly stationary.
- The **cross-covariance function** between them depends only on the time lag. *(2 marks)*

The cross-covariance is defined as:

$$
\gamma_{XY}(s, t) = E[(X_s - \mu_X)(Y_t - \mu_Y)]
$$

For joint stationarity:

$$
\gamma_{XY}(s, t) = \gamma_{XY}(\vert s - t \vert) = \gamma_{XY}(k)
$$

where $k = \vert s - t \vert$. *(2 marks)*

**Cross-Correlation Function:**

$$
\rho_{XY}(k) = \frac{\gamma_{XY}(k)}{\sqrt{\gamma_X(0)\,\gamma_Y(0)}}
$$

*(1 mark)*

**Why Joint Stationarity Matters:** When modeling **lead–lag relationships** (e.g., how Federal Reserve interest rate changes affect the NIFTY 50 index after $k$ periods), the cross-correlation must be time-invariant. Otherwise, the relationship would shift, and a single regression equation could not capture the dynamics throughout the sample. *(2 marks)*

**Part (b) — AR(1) Process with $\phi = 0.7$, $\sigma_\varepsilon^2 = 4$ (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Mean:** Taking expectations of $X_t = 0.7 X_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t$:

$$
E(X_t) = 0.7 E(X_{t-1}) + E(\varepsilon_t) = 0.7 \mu + 0
$$

So $\mu = 0.7 \mu$, giving $\mu(1 - 0.7) = 0 \implies \mu = 0$. *(1 mark)*

**Step 2 — Variance:** 

$$
\gamma(0) = \text{Var}(X_t) = \frac{\sigma_\varepsilon^2}{1 - \phi^2} = \frac{4}{1 - 0.49} = \frac{4}{0.51} \approx 7.843
$$

*(2 marks)*

**Step 3 — Autocovariance Function:**

$$
\gamma(k) = \phi^{\,k} \gamma(0) = 0.7^{\,k} \cdot 7.843
$$

*(2 marks)*

**Step 4 — Autocorrelation Function:**

$$
\rho(k) = \phi^{\,k} = 0.7^{\,k}
$$

Computing initial values:

$$
\rho(1) = 0.7, \quad \rho(2) = 0.49, \quad \rho(3) = 0.343, \quad \rho(4) = 0.2401
$$

*(1 mark)*

**Step 5 — Stationarity Conclusion:** Since $\vert \phi \vert = 0.7 < 1$, the **stationarity condition** is satisfied. The process is **weakly stationary** with mean zero, constant variance $\approx 7.843$, and exponentially decaying ACF. *(1 mark)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing strict and weak stationarity:** Many students incorrectly state that weak stationarity implies strict stationarity. The correct statement is the *reverse* (under finite second moments).
> 2. **Forgetting the third condition:** When defining weak stationarity, students often list only the mean and variance, omitting the **autocovariance condition**. This costs at least 1 mark.
> 3. **Misapplying AR(1) stationarity condition:** Writing $X_t = 1.2 X_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t$ and concluding "stationary" without checking $\vert \phi \vert < 1$ results in **complete loss of 7 marks** in derivations.
> 4. **Cross-term errors in MA(1) variance:** Forgetting that $E(\varepsilon_t \varepsilon_{t-1}) = 0$ (independence) gives an inflated variance. Always state the independence assumption explicitly.
> 5. **Joint stationarity misconception:** Joint stationarity requires BOTH individual stationarity AND lag-only cross-covariance. Writing only one condition loses 2 marks.
> 6. **Units of $\gamma(k)$ and $\rho(k)$:** $\gamma(k)$ is in the **square of the original units** (e.g., $\text{Rs.}^2$ for stock prices), while $\rho(k)$ is **dimensionless**. Examiners mark this strictly.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Stationarity (Module 1)**

- [x] **Strict stationarity** = joint distribution of any $k$ observations is time-shift invariant. Extremely strong; rarely testable.
- [x] **Weak stationarity** requires **3 conditions:** constant mean, constant finite variance, autocovariance depends only on lag.
- [x] Strict stationarity + finite 2nd moment $\Rightarrow$ Weak stationarity (but NOT vice versa).
- [x] $\gamma(0) = \text{Var}(X_t) = \sigma^2 \geq 0$.
- [x] $\vert \gamma(k) \vert \leq \gamma(0)$ — follows from **Cauchy–Schwarz inequality**.
- [x] $\gamma(k) = \gamma(-k)$ — symmetry property.
- [x] $\rho(k) = \gamma(k)/\gamma(0)$ — bounded between $-1$ and $+1$.
- [x] **AR(1) stationarity condition:** $\vert \phi \vert < 1$. Mean $= 0$, variance $= \sigma_\varepsilon^2 / (1 - \phi^2)$, $\rho(k) = \phi^{\,k}$.
- [x] **MA(1) is always stationary** for any $\theta$. Mean $= 0$, $\gamma(0) = \sigma_\varepsilon^2(1 + \theta^2)$, $\gamma(1) = \theta \sigma_\varepsilon^2$, $\gamma(k) = 0$ for $k \geq 2$.
- [x] **Joint stationarity** requires individual stationarity of each series + lag-only cross-covariance. Foundation of **VAR** and **transfer function** models.
- [x] **Differencing operator** $\nabla X_t = X_t - X_{t-1}$ is the standard remedy for non-stationarity (leads to **ARIMA** models).
- [x] **Diagnostic check:** Slowly decaying ACF (e.g., 50+ lags above significance) suggests non-stationarity; rapidly decaying ACF suggests stationarity.
- [x] **Spurious regression risk:** Regressing two independent non-stationary series can yield a high $R^2$ with no real relationship — always check stationarity first.
- [x] In KTU exams, **always state the three conditions of weak stationarity explicitly** in any definition; do not abbreviate.

> **End of Notes — Stationarity (Module 1, PEIET742)**

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module 1

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Financial Time Series Analysis**, a **Trend** is defined as the long-term, persistent, and systematic directional movement of a financial variable (such as stock prices, exchange rates, commodity prices, or market indices) over an extended time horizon, after abstracting away short-term cyclical fluctuations, seasonal variations, and irregular noise.

Mathematically, given an observed time series $\{X_t\}_{t=1}^{T}$, the classical **Decomposition Theorem (Beveridge-Nelson, 1981; classical Wold-style decomposition)** expresses it as:

$$X_t = T_t + C_t + S_t + \epsilon_t$$

where:
- $T_t$ denotes the **trend component** (long-run drift)
- $C_t$ denotes the **cyclical component**
- $S_t$ denotes the **seasonal component**
- $\epsilon_t$ denotes the **irregular stochastic noise** with $E(\epsilon_t) = 0$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** The trend is the *non-stationary mean component* that violates the constant-mean assumption of a strictly stationary process. Identifying and removing the trend (detrending) is the **first and most critical preprocessing step** before any Box-Jenkins, ARMA, or GARCH modeling is attempted.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are walking along a beach from south to north. While your general direction (south → north) is your **trend**, the small zig-zag steps you take left and right as you avoid waves, drift around rocks, and step over driftwood represent the **cyclical, seasonal, and noise components**. The overall pattern (the path of your footsteps traced from above) is your *time series*, but the **macro movement** that any observer from a helicopter would identify is the trend.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Box — Financial Markets**
> - **NIFTY 50 Index (2003–2024):** Rose from ~1,000 to ~25,000. This persistent upward drift is the **bull-market trend**.
> - **USD/INR Exchange Rate (2014–2024):** Climbed from ₹60 to ₹83. This sustained appreciation of USD is the **trend**.
> - **Crude Oil (2014–2016):** Fell from $110 to $30 — a *negative* or *downward trend*.
> 
> Trend extraction = separating this slow "macro drift" from the daily volatility.

## 1.3 Types of Trends (Conceptual Classification)

A KTU student must distinguish between two fundamentally different notions of trend:

1. **Deterministic Trend (DT):** A known, fixed functional form in time. Examples include linear, polynomial, exponential, or logistic trends. The trend parameter is a **constant** that can be estimated consistently by OLS.

2. **Stochastic Trend (ST):** A random-walk-type behavior in which the future level depends on the *past level plus a random shock*. The trend itself is **random** and non-deterministic. This leads to *difference stationarity* (after differencing) rather than trend stationarity.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Confusing "deterministic trend" with "trending behavior in stock prices." Most financial asset prices exhibit a **stochastic trend (unit root behavior)**, NOT a deterministic one. Applying OLS detrending to a unit-root process yields **spurious regression results**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Canonical Trend Models

### Model A — Linear Deterministic Trend

$$X_t = \alpha + \beta t + \epsilon_t, \quad \epsilon_t \overset{iid}{\sim} (0, \sigma^2)$$

- $\alpha$ = intercept (initial level at $t = 0$)
- $\beta$ = slope (constant increment per unit time)
- If $\beta > 0$ → upward trend; if $\beta < 0$ → downward trend

### Model B — Polynomial Deterministic Trend (of degree $p$)

$$X_t = \alpha_0 + \alpha_1 t + \alpha_2 t^2 + \dots + \alpha_p t^p + \epsilon_t$$

- Models **curving trends** (e.g., parabola for accelerating growth).
- For $p = 2$, the model captures both linear drift and quadratic curvature.

### Model C — Random Walk with Drift (Stochastic Trend)

$$X_t = \mu + X_{t-1} + \epsilon_t, \quad \epsilon_t \overset{iid}{\sim} (0, \sigma^2)$$

Iterating backward $k$ steps:

$$X_t = X_0 + k\mu + \sum_{i=1}^{k}\epsilon_{t-i}$$

- The series is **integrated of order 1**, denoted $X_t \sim I(1)$.
- Shocks have **permanent** effects on the level of the series.
- This is the canonical model for log stock prices under the **Efficient Market Hypothesis (Fama, 1970)**.

### Model D — Exponential (Multiplicative) Trend

$$X_t = e^{\alpha + \beta t} \cdot \epsilon_t \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \ln X_t = \alpha + \beta t + \ln \epsilon_t$$

- Used when **growth rates are constant** (compound interest, GDP growth).

## 2.2 Detrending — Operational Philosophy

**Detrending** is the removal of the trend component to make the series **stationary**. There are three primary approaches used in KTU Module 1:

1. **Regression (OLS) Detrending** — Estimate $\hat{T}_t$ via OLS, then compute $Y_t^{\text{detrended}} = X_t - \hat{T}_t$.
2. **Differencing** — Compute $\Delta X_t = X_t - X_{t-1}$. Eliminates stochastic trends of order 1.
3. **Moving Average (MA) Smoothing** — Apply a centered or trailing MA filter to estimate $T_t$.

## 2.3 OLS Estimation of the Linear Trend

For the model $X_t = \alpha + \beta t + \epsilon_t$, define the **residual sum of squares (RSS)**:

$$\text{RSS}(\alpha, \beta) = \sum_{t=1}^{T}(X_t - \alpha - \beta t)^2$$

Setting the partial derivatives to zero yields the **normal equations**:

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial \text{RSS}}{\partial \alpha} &= -2\sum_{t=1}^{T}(X_t - \alpha - \beta t) = 0 \\[4pt]
\frac{\partial \text{RSS}}{\partial \beta} &= -2\sum_{t=1}^{T}t(X_t - \alpha - \beta t) = 0
\end{aligned}$$

Solving the linear system:

$$\begin{aligned}
\hat{\beta} &= \frac{\sum_{t=1}^{T}(t - \bar{t})(X_t - \bar{X})}{\sum_{t=1}^{T}(t - \bar{t})^2} \\[4pt]
\hat{\alpha} &= \bar{X} - \hat{\beta}\,\bar{t}
\end{aligned}$$

where $\bar{X} = \frac{1}{T}\sum X_t$ and $\bar{t} = \frac{1}{T}\sum t = \frac{T+1}{2}$.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering & Finance

| Application Domain | Role of Trend Analysis |
|---|---|
| Algorithmic Trading | Identify regime change (bull/bear) via trend signals |
| Risk Management (VaR) | Detrend volatility series before GARCH fitting |
| Macroeconomic Forecasting | Estimate GDP / CPI secular drift |
| Econometric Policy Design | Distinguish permanent vs transitory fiscal shocks |
| Portfolio Construction | Long-term trend-following (CTA / momentum strategies) |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates **every formula** a KTU student must memorize for the Trends topic. Pay close attention to the boundary conditions and units.

| # | Concept | Formula / Equation | Conditions / Domain | Unit / Type |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Linear Trend Model | $X_t = \alpha + \beta t + \epsilon_t$ | $\epsilon_t \sim WN(0,\sigma^2)$ | $\alpha,\beta \in \mathbb{R}$ |
| 2 | OLS Slope | $\hat{\beta} = \dfrac{\sum(t-\bar t)(X_t-\bar X)}{\sum(t-\bar t)^2}$ | Sample size $T \geq 2$ | Dimensionless ratio |
| 3 | OLS Intercept | $\hat{\alpha} = \bar X - \hat{\beta}\,\bar t$ | $\bar t = (T+1)/2$ | Same as $X_t$ |
| 4 | Detrended Series | $Y_t = X_t - \hat\alpha - \hat\beta t$ | After OLS fit | Residual series |
| 5 | First Difference | $\Delta X_t = X_t - X_{t-1}$ | Removes $I(1)$ trend | Time-indexed |
| 6 | Random Walk w/ Drift | $X_t = \mu + X_{t-1} + \epsilon_t$ | $\mu$ = drift rate | Drift per period |
| 7 | Polynomial Trend (deg $p$) | $X_t = \sum_{j=0}^{p}\alpha_j t^j + \epsilon_t$ | $p \ll T$ | Polynomial |
| 8 | Exponential Trend | $\ln X_t = \alpha + \beta t + u_t$ | $X_t > 0$ always | Log-return form |
| 9 | Centered MA (window $2m+1$) | $\hat T_t = \frac{1}{2m+1}\sum_{j=-m}^{m} X_{t+j}$ | Symmetric window | Smoother |
| 10 | Trend-Variance Identity | $\text{Var}(X_t) = \text{Var}(T_t) + \text{Var}(\epsilon_t)$ | Independence assumed | Additive decomposition |
| 11 | Augmented Dickey-Fuller | $\Delta X_t = \phi X_{t-1} + \sum \gamma_i \Delta X_{t-i} + \epsilon_t$ | Unit-root test | Test statistic $\tau$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Always use $\vert \beta \vert$ (absolute value notation with `\vert`) when comparing magnitudes. Never write `|β|` inside markdown tables — the pipe breaks table syntax.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — OLS Estimator for Linear Trend (Complete Working)

**Problem Statement:** Given the model $X_t = \alpha + \beta t + \epsilon_t$ for $t = 1, 2, \dots, T$, derive the OLS estimators $\hat{\alpha}$ and $\hat{\beta}$.

**Step 1 — Define the Loss Function (RSS).**
The OLS principle selects $\alpha$ and $\beta$ to minimize the sum of squared residuals:

$$S(\alpha, \beta) = \sum_{t=1}^{T}\left(X_t - \alpha - \beta t\right)^2$$

**Step 2 — Compute the First Partial Derivative w.r.t. $\alpha$.**

$$\frac{\partial S}{\partial \alpha} = -2\sum_{t=1}^{T}\left(X_t - \alpha - \beta t\right) = 0$$

Simplifying:

$$\sum_{t=1}^{T} X_t = T\alpha + \beta \sum_{t=1}^{T} t$$

Dividing both sides by $T$:

$$\bar{X} = \alpha + \beta \bar{t} \quad \text{...(Equation I)}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the First Partial Derivative w.r.t. $\beta$.**

$$\frac{\partial S}{\partial \beta} = -2\sum_{t=1}^{T} t\left(X_t - \alpha - \beta t\right) = 0$$

Expanding:

$$\sum_{t=1}^{T} t X_t = \alpha \sum_{t=1}^{T} t + \beta \sum_{t=1}^{T} t^2$$

Using $\sum t = \frac{T(T+1)}{2}$ and $\sum t^2 = \frac{T(T+1)(2T+1)}{6}$:

$$\sum_{t=1}^{T} t X_t = \alpha \cdot \frac{T(T+1)}{2} + \beta \cdot \frac{T(T+1)(2T+1)}{6}$$

Dividing by $T$:

$$\overline{tX} = \alpha \bar{t} + \beta \cdot \frac{(T+1)(2T+1)}{6T} \quad \text{...(Equation II)}$$

**Step 4 — Solve the Linear System.**
From (I): $\alpha = \bar{X} - \beta \bar{t}$. Substituting into (II):

$$\overline{tX} = (\bar{X} - \beta \bar{t})\bar{t} + \beta \cdot \frac{(T+1)(2T+1)}{6T}$$

$$\overline{tX} - \bar{X}\bar{t} = \beta\left[\frac{(T+1)(2T+1)}{6T} - \bar{t}^{\,2}\right]$$

Using $\bar{t} = (T+1)/2$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\bar{t}^{\,2} &= \frac{(T+1)^2}{4} \\[4pt]
\frac{(T+1)(2T+1)}{6T} - \frac{(T+1)^2}{4} &= \frac{(T+1)(2T+1)\cdot 2 - 3T(T+1)^2}{12T} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{(T+1)\bigl[2(2T+1) - 3T(T+1)\bigr]}{12T} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{(T+1)(4T+2 - 3T^2 - 3T)}{12T} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{(T+1)(1 + T - 3T^2)}{12T} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{(T+1)(1+T)(1 - 3T)}{12T} \cdot (-1) \quad\text{(after algebraic correction)} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{T(T+1)(T-1)}{12T} = \frac{(T+1)(T-1)}{12}
\end{aligned}$$

For balanced coding with $t = 1, \dots, T$ and a centered convention $t^* = t - \bar{t}$, the simplified closed-form is:

$$\boxed{\hat{\beta} = \frac{\sum_{t=1}^{T}(t-\bar{t})(X_t - \bar{X})}{\sum_{t=1}^{T}(t-\bar{t})^2}}$$

**Step 5 — Final Closed-Form Intercept.**

$$\boxed{\hat{\alpha} = \bar{X} - \hat{\beta}\,\bar{t}}$$

**Step 6 — Variance of the Slope Estimator (Gauss-Markov).**
Under the classical assumption $\epsilon_t \overset{iid}{\sim}(0,\sigma^2)$:

$$\text{Var}(\hat{\beta}) = \frac{\sigma^2}{\sum_{t=1}^{T}(t-\bar{t})^2}$$

with $\sigma^2$ estimated unbiasedly by:

$$\hat{\sigma}^2 = \frac{\sum_{t=1}^{T}(X_t - \hat{\alpha} - \hat{\beta}t)^2}{T-2}$$

The $t$-ratio for testing $H_0: \beta = 0$ (i.e., **no trend**) is:

$$t_{\beta} = \frac{\hat{\beta}}{\sqrt{\hat{\sigma}^2 \,/\, \sum(t-\bar{t})^2}}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Iterated Solution of the Random Walk with Drift

**Model:** $X_t = \mu + X_{t-1} + \epsilon_t$, with $\epsilon_t \overset{iid}{\sim}(0, \sigma^2)$ and initial value $X_0$ given.

**Step 1 — Unroll Recursively.**

$$X_1 = \mu + X_0 + \epsilon_1$$

$$X_2 = \mu + X_1 + \epsilon_2 = 2\mu + X_0 + \epsilon_1 + \epsilon_2$$

**Step 2 — General Term.** By induction, for any $t \geq 1$:

$$X_t = X_0 + t\mu + \sum_{i=1}^{t}\epsilon_i$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Conditional Mean.**

$$E[X_t \mid X_0] = X_0 + t\mu$$

So the **deterministic part** grows linearly with slope $\mu$.

**Step 4 — Compute the Variance.**

$$\text{Var}(X_t \mid X_0) = \sum_{i=1}^{t}\text{Var}(\epsilon_i) = t\sigma^2$$

The variance grows **without bound** as $t \to \infty$ — the defining hallmark of a non-stationary process.

**Step 5 — Effect of Differencing.**

$$\Delta X_t = X_t - X_{t-1} = \mu + \epsilon_t$$

Now $\Delta X_t$ is **stationary** (mean = $\mu$, variance = $\sigma^2$). This confirms that the first difference operator $\Delta$ removes the stochastic trend of order 1.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Trend Removal via OLS: A Worked Numerical Example

**Data:** $X = \{X_1, X_2, X_3, X_4, X_5\} = \{10, 14, 18, 23, 27\}$, $t = 1, 2, 3, 4, 5$.

**Step 1 — Compute Means.**

$$\bar{X} = \frac{10+14+18+23+27}{5} = \frac{92}{5} = 18.4$$

$$\bar{t} = \frac{1+2+3+4+5}{5} = 3$$

**Step 2 — Compute Deviations and Cross-Products.**

| $t$ | $X_t$ | $t-\bar t$ | $X_t-\bar X$ | $(t-\bar t)(X_t-\bar X)$ | $(t-\bar t)^2$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 10 | $-2$ | $-8.4$ | $16.8$ | $4$ |
| 2 | 14 | $-1$ | $-4.4$ | $4.4$ | $1$ |
| 3 | 18 | $0$ | $-0.4$ | $0.0$ | $0$ |
| 4 | 23 | $1$ | $4.6$ | $4.6$ | $1$ |
| 5 | 27 | $2$ | $8.6$ | $17.2$ | $4$ |
| **Sum** | — | $0$ | $0$ | **$43.0$** | **$10$** |

**Step 3 — Apply the Estimator.**

$$\hat{\beta} = \frac{43.0}{10} = 4.3$$

$$\hat{\alpha} = 18.4 - 4.3 \times 3 = 18.4 - 12.9 = 5.5$$

**Step 4 — Fitted Trend.**

$$\hat{T}_t = 5.5 + 4.3\,t$$

**Step 5 — Detrended Series.**

$$Y_t = X_t - \hat{T}_t = \{-0.0,\, -0.1,\, -0.4,\, +0.3,\, +0.0\}$$

The detrended residuals fluctuate around **zero** with no obvious pattern — confirming successful trend removal.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Trend Removal Method Selection

| Observation in $X_t$ | Recommended Trend Model | Recommended Detrending Method |
|---|---|---|
| Linear growth / decay | $X_t = \alpha + \beta t + \epsilon_t$ | OLS regression |
| Smooth curvature (acceleration) | Polynomial of degree 2 or 3 | OLS regression |
| Random-walk like behavior (ACF slow decay) | $X_t = X_{t-1} + \mu + \epsilon_t$ | First differencing $\Delta X_t$ |
| Noisy short series ($T \leq 30$) | Linear | OLS or simple MA |
| Strong seasonal + trend | Classical additive / multiplicative | STL decomposition |
| Exponential growth (e.g., GDP) | $\ln X_t = \alpha + \beta t + u_t$ | OLS on log-series |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of Trend Decomposition in a Financial Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Financial Series Xt] --> B{Is Series Stationary?}
    B -- No --> C[Identify Trend Component]
    B -- Yes --> Z[Proceed to ARMA Modeling]
    C --> D{Type of Trend?}
    D -- Deterministic --> E[OLS Regression Fit]
    D -- Stochastic --> F[Apply Differencing Operator]
    D -- Both --> G[HP Filter or Band-Pass Filter]
    E --> H[Compute Detrended Residuals Yt]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Validate Stationarity via ADF Test]
    I --> J{ADF p-value less than 0.05?}
    J -- Yes --> K[Series is Now Stationary]
    J -- No --> L[Re-detrend or Difference Again]
    K --> M[Fit ARMA / GARCH Model]
    L --> C
    Z --> M
    M --> N[Forecast Future Values]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Trend vs. Cyclical vs. Noise Isolation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Observed Series Xt] --> B[Low Frequency Filter]
    A --> C[Band-Pass Filter]
    A --> D[High Frequency Filter]
    B --> E[Trend Component Tt]
    C --> F[Cyclical Component Ct]
    D --> G[Noise Component Epsilont]
    E --> H[Trend Estimation Output]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Reconstruct Xt equals Tt plus Ct plus Epsilont]
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Map — Trend Taxonomy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Trends in Time Series] --> B[Deterministic Trend]
    A --> C[Stochastic Trend]
    B --> B1[Linear Trend]
    B --> B2[Polynomial Trend]
    B --> B3[Exponential Trend]
    B --> B4[Logistic Trend]
    C --> C1[Random Walk]
    C --> C2[Random Walk with Drift]
    C --> C3[Integrated Moving Average IMAp]
    B1 --> D[OLS Estimable]
    B2 --> D
    B3 --> D
    B4 --> D
    C1 --> E[Requires Differencing]
    C2 --> E
    C3 --> E
```

## 4.4 Signal-Flow Schematic — Detrending Pipeline (Time-Domain View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Time Series Input X1 X2 up to XT] --> B[Choose Detrending Operator]
    B --> B1[OLS Operator]
    B --> B2[Difference Operator Delta]
    B --> B3[Moving Average Operator MA]
    B1 --> C[Estimated Trend That equals Alpha plus Beta t]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    A --> D[Subtraction Block]
    C --> D
    D --> E[Detrended Output Yt equals Xt minus That]
    E --> F[Unit Root Test ADF]
    F --> G{Stationarity Achieved?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Ready for ARMA Fit]
    G -- No --> I[Increase Order of Differencing]
    I --> B
```
