# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define a "deterministic trend" and a "stochastic trend." Give one financial example of each.**

**Model Answer:**
A **deterministic trend** is a trend whose functional form is fixed and known up to a finite number of parameters; future values can be expressed as an explicit function of time, e.g., $X_t = \alpha + \beta t + \epsilon_t$. The slope $\beta$ is a constant to be estimated.

A **stochastic trend** is a random-walk-type component in which the current value equals the previous value plus a random innovation, e.g., $X_t = X_{t-1} + \mu + \epsilon_t$. Shocks have permanent effects, and the trend itself is random.

**Financial Examples:**
- *Deterministic:* The accumulated value of a fixed-coupon bond with reinvestment (deterministic growth formula $B_t = B_0(1+r)^t$).
- *Stochastic:* The logarithm of daily closing price of an index stock like NIFTY 50 under the random-walk hypothesis.

### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam - June 2024]**
**Distinguish between "trend stationarity" and "difference stationarity."**

**Model Answer:**
- **Trend Stationarity (TS):** A process is trend-stationary if, after removing a deterministic trend, it becomes (weakly) stationary. The series fluctuates around a deterministic mean path; shocks have only **temporary** effects. Example: $X_t = \alpha + \beta t + \epsilon_t$ with $\epsilon_t$ stationary.

- **Difference Stationarity (DS):** A process is difference-stationary if it requires differencing to become stationary. Shocks have **permanent** effects on the level of the series. Example: the random walk with drift $X_t = \mu + X_{t-1} + \epsilon_t$, which is $I(1)$.

The Augmented Dickey-Fuller (ADF) test is used to discriminate between the two hypotheses.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, Part B Module-1 carries 14 marks. Students must answer **one** full question, choosing between two alternatives. Each sub-part carries **7 marks**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam - June 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the classical additive decomposition model of a time series. How is the trend component estimated using the **method of least squares**? Derive the OLS estimators for the linear trend $X_t = \alpha + \beta t + \epsilon_t$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A financial analyst has the following 7 quarterly observations of a mutual-fund NAV (in ₹):

| $t$ | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $X_t$ | 100 | 108 | 115 | 124 | 132 | 140 | 149 |

Fit a linear trend using OLS. Test at the 5% level whether the trend is statistically significant (given the critical $t$-value is $\pm 2.571$ for $T-2 = 5$ d.f.).

#### Model Solution to (a):

The classical additive decomposition (Persons, 1919) writes:

$$X_t = T_t + C_t + S_t + \epsilon_t$$

The trend $T_t$ is the slow-moving long-run component. Under a linear functional form:

$$T_t = \alpha + \beta t$$

The OLS principle minimizes the residual sum of squares:

$$S(\alpha,\beta) = \sum_{t=1}^{T}(X_t - \alpha - \beta t)^2$$

Setting first-order conditions to zero:

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial S}{\partial \alpha} &= -2\sum_{t=1}^{T}(X_t - \alpha - \beta t) = 0 \\[4pt]
\frac{\partial S}{\partial \beta} &= -2\sum_{t=1}^{T}t(X_t - \alpha - \beta t) = 0
\end{aligned}$$

Solving the resulting $2 \times 2$ linear system yields:

$$\hat{\beta} = \frac{\sum_{t=1}^{T}(t-\bar{t})(X_t - \bar{X})}{\sum_{t=1}^{T}(t-\bar{t})^2}, \qquad \hat{\alpha} = \bar{X} - \hat{\beta}\,\bar{t}$$

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Step 1 — Compute Means.**

$$\bar{X} = \frac{100+108+115+124+132+140+149}{7} = \frac{868}{7} = 124.0$$

$$\bar{t} = \frac{1+2+3+4+5+6+7}{7} = 4$$

**Step 2 — Compute Cross-Product and Sum of Squares.**

| $t$ | $X_t$ | $t-\bar t$ | $X_t - \bar X$ | $(t-\bar t)(X_t-\bar X)$ | $(t-\bar t)^2$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 100 | $-3$ | $-24$ | $72$ | $9$ |
| 2 | 108 | $-2$ | $-16$ | $32$ | $4$ |
| 3 | 115 | $-1$ | $-9$ | $9$ | $1$ |
| 4 | 124 | $0$ | $0$ | $0$ | $0$ |
| 5 | 132 | $1$ | $8$ | $8$ | $1$ |
| 6 | 140 | $2$ | $16$ | $32$ | $4$ |
| 7 | 149 | $3$ | $25$ | $75$ | $9$ |
| **Sum** | — | $0$ | $0$ | **$228$** | **$28$** |

**Step 3 — Compute the Estimators.**

$$\hat{\beta} = \frac{228}{28} = 8.143 \quad \text{(₹/quarter)}$$

$$\hat{\alpha} = 124.0 - 8.143 \times 4 = 124.0 - 32.571 = 91.429 \quad \text{(₹)}$$

So the fitted trend is:

$$\hat{T}_t = 91.43 + 8.14\,t$$

**Step 4 — Compute $\hat{\sigma}^2$.**

Fitted values: $\hat{T}_1 = 99.57$, $\hat{T}_2 = 107.71$, $\hat{T}_3 = 115.86$, $\hat{T}_4 = 124.00$, $\hat{T}_5 = 132.14$, $\hat{T}_6 = 140.29$, $\hat{T}_7 = 148.43$.

Residuals: $\{0.43,\; 0.29,\; -0.86,\; 0.00,\; -0.14,\; -0.29,\; 0.57\}$.

Sum of squared residuals:

$$\text{SSE} = 0.43^2 + 0.29^2 + (-0.86)^2 + 0^2 + (-0.14)^2 + (-0.29)^2 + 0.57^2 \approx 1.71$$

$$\hat{\sigma}^2 = \frac{\text{SSE}}{T-2} = \frac{1.71}{5} = 0.343$$

**Step 5 — Compute the $t$-Statistic.**

$$\text{SE}(\hat{\beta}) = \sqrt{\frac{\hat{\sigma}^2}{\sum(t-\bar{t})^2}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.343}{28}} = \sqrt{0.01225} \approx 0.1107$$

$$t_{\beta} = \frac{\hat{\beta}}{\text{SE}(\hat{\beta})} = \frac{8.143}{0.1107} \approx 73.6$$

**Step 6 — Conclusion.**

Since $\vert t_{\beta} \vert = 73.6 \gg 2.571$, we **reject $H_0: \beta = 0$**. The NAV exhibits a **highly statistically significant positive linear trend** at the 5% level.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** What is a **stochastic trend**? Derive the mean and variance of a random walk with drift $X_t = \mu + X_{t-1} + \epsilon_t$ for $t = 1, 2, \dots, T$, starting from an initial value $X_0$. Show that the first-difference $\Delta X_t$ is stationary.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the role of the **Augmented Dickey-Fuller (ADF) test** in identifying the order of integration of a financial time series. How would you decide whether to apply OLS detrending versus differencing for a given series?

#### Model Solution to (a):

A stochastic trend arises from a **unit-root process**: the current value equals the previous value plus a random innovation. This is in contrast to a deterministic trend, where the path is a fixed function of time.

**Derivation of Mean and Variance.**

Iterating the recursion $X_t = X_{t-1} + \mu + \epsilon_t$ backward:

$$\begin{aligned}
X_1 &= X_0 + \mu + \epsilon_1 \\
X_2 &= X_1 + \mu + \epsilon_2 = X_0 + 2\mu + \epsilon_1 + \epsilon_2 \\
&\;\;\vdots \\
X_t &= X_0 + t\mu + \sum_{i=1}^{t}\epsilon_i
\end{aligned}$$

Taking the conditional expectation (given $X_0$):

$$E[X_t \mid X_0] = X_0 + t\mu$$

So the mean path is **linear in $t$** with slope $\mu$.

The variance (with $\epsilon_i$ i.i.d., mean zero, variance $\sigma^2$):

$$\text{Var}(X_t \mid X_0) = \text{Var}\left(\sum_{i=1}^{t}\epsilon_i\right) = \sum_{i=1}^{t}\text{Var}(\epsilon_i) = t\sigma^2$$

Since the variance grows without bound as $t \to \infty$, $X_t$ is **non-stationary**.

**First-Difference Analysis.**

$$\Delta X_t = X_t - X_{t-1} = \mu + \epsilon_t$$

- Mean: $E[\Delta X_t] = \mu$ (constant).
- Variance: $\text{Var}(\Delta X_t) = \sigma^2$ (constant).
- Autocovariance: $\text{Cov}(\Delta X_t, \Delta X_{t-k}) = 0$ for $k \geq 1$ since $\epsilon_t$ are independent.

Hence $\Delta X_t$ is **weakly stationary** (white noise with drift).

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Role of the ADF Test.**
The Augmented Dickey-Fuller (Said & Dickey, 1984) test examines the null hypothesis $H_0: \phi = 0$ (i.e., a unit root is present) in the regression:

$$\Delta X_t = \phi X_{t-1} + \sum_{j=1}^{p}\gamma_j \Delta X_{t-j} + \epsilon_t$$

The test statistic is the $t$-ratio on $\hat{\phi}$, but its distribution is **non-standard** (Dickey-Fuller distribution), requiring special critical values (more negative than the standard $t$-table).

If the ADF $p$-value $< 0.05$, we **reject** the unit-root null → the series is stationary (possibly trend-stationary around a constant). If we fail to reject, the series is integrated of order 1, $I(1)$.

**Decision Rule for Detrending.**

| ADF Result | Series Type | Recommended Action |
|---|---|---|
| Reject $H_0$ (stationary around mean) | $I(0)$ trend-stationary | OLS detrending around a constant or linear trend |
| Fail to reject $H_0$ (unit root present) | $I(1)$ difference-stationary | Apply first differencing $\Delta X_t$ |
| Reject $H_0$ only with trend included | Trend-stationary with deterministic drift | OLS detrending of deterministic polynomial |

**Practical Note:** For most liquid financial assets (equity indices, FX rates, commodity prices), empirical evidence overwhelmingly supports the $I(1)$ hypothesis. Hence, in finance, **first differencing** (or log-return transformation) is preferred over OLS detrending.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students LOSE marks on Trends questions:**
> 1. **Sign error in OLS formula** — forgetting the negative sign when setting $\partial S / \partial \beta = 0$. Always double-check: the slope must be **positive** if the series is rising.
> 2. **Forgetting the $T - 2$ denominator** in $\hat{\sigma}^2$. Using $T$ instead costs **1 full mark** in most valuation scripts.
> 3. **Confusing deterministic and stochastic trends** in definition questions. Examiners specifically look for the phrase *"shocks have permanent effects"* to identify stochastic trend understanding.
> 4. **Misapplying the standard $t$-table** to ADF statistics. The ADF critical values are **more negative** (e.g., $-3.45$ instead of $-1.96$ at 5%). This costs 2 marks.
> 5. **Not checking the residual sum of squares** in numerical problems. Examiners expect you to compute fitted values, residuals, and SSE explicitly — skipping straight to $\hat{\beta}$ invites **partial marking penalty**.
> 6. **Mixing up $\bar{X}$ and $\bar{t}$** in the intercept formula. Always write $\hat{\alpha} = \bar{X} - \hat{\beta}\bar{t}$, not the other way around.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Trends (Module 1)**

- ☐ **Trend = long-run directional movement** of a series after removing cyclical, seasonal, and irregular components.
- ☐ Two fundamental types: **Deterministic Trend** (fixed function of time) and **Stochastic Trend** (unit-root process).
- ☐ Linear trend model: $X_t = \alpha + \beta t + \epsilon_t$. OLS estimators are **linear, unbiased, and BLUE** (Gauss-Markov).
- ☐ OLS slope formula: $\hat{\beta} = \dfrac{\sum(t-\bar{t})(X_t - \bar{X})}{\sum(t-\bar{t})^2}$.
- ☐ OLS intercept formula: $\hat{\alpha} = \bar{X} - \hat{\beta}\bar{t}$, where $\bar{t} = (T+1)/2$.
- ☐ Random walk with drift: $X_t = \mu + X_{t-1} + \epsilon_t \Rightarrow E[X_t] = X_0 + t\mu$, $\text{Var}(X_t) = t\sigma^2$.
- ☐ First differencing ($\Delta X_t = X_t - X_{t-1}$) **removes a stochastic $I(1)$ trend**.
- ☐ **ADF test** distinguishes trend stationarity ($I(0)$ with deterministic trend) from difference stationarity ($I(1)$).
- ☐ For financial asset prices, the $I(1)$ hypothesis dominates empirically → use **log-returns** or first differences.
- ☐ Detrending procedures: **(i) OLS regression**, **(ii) differencing**, **(iii) MA/HP filtering**.
- ☐ Always **plot the detrended residuals** and verify zero-mean, constant-variance before proceeding to ARMA modeling.
- ☐ In numerical problems, **show all intermediate workings** (deviation tables, fitted values, SSE). Examiners award partial credit generously for correct setup even if the final answer slips.
- ☐ For a polynomial trend of degree $p$, you need **at least $p+1$ distinct observations** for OLS to be solvable, and ideally $T \gg p+1$.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Seasonal Variation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET742 - FINANCIAL TIME SERIES ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Seasonal Variation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Seasonal Variation** is that component of a time series which represents the regular, periodic, and recurrent fluctuations occurring within a sub-period of one year (e.g., a quarter, a month, or a week) that tend to repeat themselves year after year with roughly the same intensity and pattern.

In the classical **decomposition model of a time series**:

$$Y_t = T_t + S_t + C_t + I_t$$

where $T_t$ is the **Trend**, $S_t$ is the **Seasonal Variation**, $C_t$ is the **Cyclical Variation**, and $I_t$ is the **Irregular (random) component**. If we use the **multiplicative model**, the seasonal component acts as a proportional scalar:

$$Y_t = T_t \times S_t \times C_t \times I_t$$

In the multiplicative model, the seasonal index (SI) for any quarter or month is expected to **average to a value of 1.00** (or **100%**) over the full year.

## 1.2 Key Characteristics

- **Periodicity**: Recurs within a fixed, known period (12 months, 4 quarters, 52 weeks).
- **Predictability**: Pattern is stable and can be forecasted from history.
- **Magnitude**: Expressed as a *Seasonal Index (SI)*, where **SI = 1.00 means no seasonal effect**.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Monsoon Umbrella Shop"

> [!NOTE]
> Imagine you own an umbrella shop in Kerala. Every June, July, and August, sales skyrocket due to the monsoon. Every December, sales plummet because the sky is clear. This predictable yearly up-and-down pattern is **seasonal variation**.
>
> Now imagine (i) your shop is in a growing locality and sales are increasing each year → that is **trend**. (ii) Every 5–7 years, an unusually strong monsoon hits → that is **cyclical variation**. (iii) A flash flood randomly destroys inventory → that is **irregular variation**. The yearly monsoon swing layered on top of all this is **seasonal variation**.

## 1.4 Common Causes of Seasonal Variation

- **Weather & Climate**: AC sales peak in summer, woollens in winter.
- **Festivals & Customs**: Onam, Diwali, Christmas, Ramadan driving retail spikes.
- **Agricultural Cycles**: Harvest seasons in commodity markets.
- **Financial Year Ends**: March-end tax-saving mutual fund inflows in India.
- **Tourism**: Beach seasons in Goa, hill-station seasons in Munnar.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Classical Methods of Measuring Seasonal Variation

There are four well-established methods. The KTU syllabus emphasises the **Ratio-to-Moving-Average Method** as the most rigorous.

### (a) Simple Average Method
1. Tabulate data quarter-wise (or month-wise).
2. Compute the arithmetic mean of each quarter across all years.
3. Compute the *Grand Mean* (mean of all quarterly means).
4. $$\text{Seasonal Index (SI)} = \frac{\text{Quarterly Mean}}{\text{Grand Mean}} \times 100$$

### (b) Ratio-to-Trend Method
1. Fit a trend line $T_t = a + bt$ to the original series.
2. Compute $Y_t / T_t$ for each period.
3. Average these ratios for each quarter.
4. Adjust so that the sum of indices = 400 (for quarterly) or 1200 (for monthly).

### (c) Ratio-to-Moving-Average Method *(Most Important — KTU Favourite)*
1. Compute a **4-period (quarterly)** or **12-period (monthly)** moving average.
2. **Center** the moving average (since an even-period MA lies between two periods).
3. Compute $$\text{Ratio} = \frac{\text{Original Value } Y_t}{\text{Centered Moving Average}}$$
4. Arrange these ratios in a quarter-wise table and take the **arithmetic mean** of each quarter (ignoring extreme values).
5. **Normalize** the indices so they sum to 400 (or 1200).

### (d) Link Relatives Method
1. Compute **Link Relatives**: $$LR_t = \frac{Y_t}{Y_{t-1}} \times 100$$
2. Convert chain relatives by cumulative multiplication.
3. Take the average of chain relatives for each quarter.
4. Adjust to sum to 400.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Boundary / Constraint |
|---|---|---|
| Additive Model | $Y_t = T_t + S_t + C_t + I_t$ | Seasonal swing is constant in absolute units |
| Multiplicative Model | $Y_t = T_t \times S_t \times C_t \times I_t$ | Seasonal swing is proportional to trend |
| 4-Qtr Moving Total | $\sum_{i=t-3}^{t} Y_i$ | Placed between Q2 and Q3 of middle year |
| 4-Qtr Moving Average | $\frac{1}{4}\sum_{i=t-3}^{t} Y_i$ | Placed at mid-point of 4 quarters |
| Centered Moving Average | $\frac{1}{2}\left[MA_{t} + MA_{t+1}\right]$ | Aligns MA with actual quarters |
| Seasonal Ratio | $r_t = \dfrac{Y_t}{\text{CMA}_t}$ | Dimensional: dimensionless |
| Raw Seasonal Index | $\bar{S}_q = \dfrac{1}{k}\sum r_{q,j}$ | $q \in \{1,2,3,4\}$ |
| Adjustment Factor | $A = \dfrac{400}{\sum \bar{S}_q}$ | For quarterly data |
| Adjusted Seasonal Index | $SI_q = \bar{S}_q \times A$ | Final index used for forecasting |
| Forecast | $\hat{Y}_{t+h} = T_{t+h} \times SI_{q(t+h)}$ | Multiplicative forecast |
| Link Relative | $LR_t = \dfrac{Y_t}{Y_{t-1}} \times 100$ | $t \geq 2$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Units reminder**: Seasonal indices are **dimensionless ratios**. They are *multipliers*, not absolute quantities. A SI of 1.25 means the period is **25% above the deseasonalized trend**.

## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering & Finance

- **Retail Demand Forecasting**: Inventory planning for FMCG, apparel, electronics.
- **Energy Load Forecasting**: Electricity boards (KSEB) use seasonal models for summer/winter peak loads.
- **Tourism & Hospitality**: Hotel revenue management.
- **Algorithmic Trading**: Removing seasonality from price series before applying GARCH or ARIMA.
- **Agricultural Commodity Pricing**: Crop harvest cycles in cardamom, rubber, pepper (highly relevant to Kerala).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Full Worked Example — Ratio-to-Moving-Average Method

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem Statement (KTU-Style):** The quarterly sales (in ₹ Lakhs) of an electrical goods company for 4 years are given below. Compute the seasonal indices using the **Ratio-to-Moving-Average Method**.

### Step 1 — Tabulate the Original Data

| Year | Q1 | Q2 | Q3 | Q4 |
|------|----|----|----|----|
| 2019 | 70 | 100 | 80 | 60 |
| 2020 | 80 | 110 | 90 | 70 |
| 2021 | 90 | 120 | 100 | 80 |
| 2022 | 100 | 130 | 110 | 90 |

### Step 2 — Compute 4-Quarter Moving Total

Sum of every 4 consecutive quarters:

| Period End | 4-Qtr Total |
|---|---|
| Q4 2019 | $70+100+80+60 = 310$ |
| Q1 2020 | $100+80+60+80 = 320$ |
| Q2 2020 | $80+60+80+110 = 330$ |
| Q3 2020 | $60+80+110+90 = 340$ |
| Q4 2020 | $80+110+90+70 = 350$ |
| Q1 2021 | $110+90+70+90 = 360$ |
| Q2 2021 | $90+70+90+120 = 370$ |
| Q3 2021 | $70+90+120+100 = 380$ |
| Q4 2021 | $90+120+100+80 = 390$ |
| Q1 2022 | $120+100+80+100 = 400$ |
| Q2 2022 | $100+80+100+130 = 410$ |
| Q3 2022 | $80+100+130+110 = 420$ |

### Step 3 — Compute 4-Quarter Moving Average

Divide each total by **4**:

$$\text{MA}_t = \frac{\text{Moving Total}}{4}$$

The first MA (310/4 = 77.5) lies *between* Q2 and Q3 of 2019, so it does **not** correspond to any quarter directly.

### Step 4 — Compute Centered Moving Average (CMA)

Average two consecutive MAs to align with actual quarters:

$$\text{CMA}_{Q3\,2019} = \frac{77.5 + 80}{2} = 78.75$$

$$\text{CMA}_{Q4\,2019} = \frac{80 + 82.5}{2} = 81.25$$

Proceeding similarly:

| Quarter | CMA |
|---|---|
| Q3 2019 | 78.75 |
| Q4 2019 | 81.25 |
| Q1 2020 | 83.75 |
| Q2 2020 | 86.25 |
| Q3 2020 | 88.75 |
| Q4 2020 | 91.25 |
| Q1 2021 | 93.75 |
| Q2 2021 | 96.25 |
| Q3 2021 | 98.75 |
| Q4 2021 | 101.25 |
| Q1 2022 | 103.75 |
| Q2 2022 | 106.25 |

### Step 5 — Compute Seasonal Ratios

$$r_t = \frac{Y_t}{\text{CMA}_t}$$

For example:

$$r_{Q3\,2019} = \frac{80}{78.75} = 1.0159$$

$$r_{Q4\,2019} = \frac{60}{81.25} = 0.7385$$

The complete table of ratios:

| Quarter | 2019 | 2020 | 2021 | 2022 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Q1 | — | 80/83.75 = **0.9552** | 90/93.75 = **0.9600** | 100/103.75 = **0.9639** |
| Q2 | — | 110/86.25 = **1.2754** | 120/96.25 = **1.2468** | 130/106.25 = **1.2235** |
| Q3 | 80/78.75 = **1.0159** | 90/88.75 = **1.0141** | 100/98.75 = **1.0127** | 110/?. = — |
| Q4 | 60/81.25 = **0.7385** | 70/91.25 = **0.7671** | 80/101.25 = **0.7901** | — |

### Step 6 — Average Ratios per Quarter (Raw Seasonal Indices)

| Quarter | Mean Ratio |
|---|---|
| Q1 | $(0.9552 + 0.9600 + 0.9639)/3 = 0.9597$ |
| Q2 | $(1.2754 + 1.2468 + 1.2235)/3 = 1.2486$ |
| Q3 | $(1.0159 + 1.0141 + 1.0127)/3 = 1.0142$ |
| Q4 | $(0.7385 + 0.7671 + 0.7901)/3 = 0.7652$ |
| **Sum** | **3.9877** |

### Step 7 — Normalize to Sum = 4.00

$$\text{Adjustment Factor } A = \frac{4.0000}{3.9877} = 1.00308$$

| Quarter | Raw Index | Adjusted Index (SI) |
|---|---|---|
| Q1 | 0.9597 | **0.9627** |
| Q2 | 1.2486 | **1.2524** |
| Q3 | 1.0142 | **1.0173** |
| Q4 | 0.7652 | **0.7676** |
| **Total** | | **4.0000** |

### Step 8 — Interpretation

> **Q1 SI ≈ 0.96** → Sales are **3.7% below average** in Q1.
> **Q2 SI ≈ 1.25** → Sales are **25.2% above average** in Q2 (likely summer AC/fan sales).
> **Q3 SI ≈ 1.02** → Approximately average.
> **Q4 SI ≈ 0.77** → Sales are **23.2% below average** in Q4.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Time-Series Decomposition Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Time Series Y sub t] --> B{Component Identification}
    B --> C[Long-Term Trend T sub t]
    B --> D[Seasonal Variation S sub t]
    B --> E[Cyclical Variation C sub t]
    B --> F[Irregular Component I sub t]
    D --> G[Detect Periodicity - 4 or 12]
    G --> H[Choose Method]
    H --> H1[Simple Average Method]
    H --> H2[Ratio to Trend Method]
    H --> H3[Ratio to Moving Average]
    H --> H4[Link Relatives Method]
    H3 --> I[Compute 4 Qtr Moving Total]
    I --> J[Derive Moving Average]
    J --> K[Center the Moving Average]
    K --> L[Compute Y sub t divided by CMA sub t]
    L --> M[Average Ratios per Quarter]
    M --> N[Adjust to Sum equals 400]
    N --> O[Final Seasonal Indices]
    O --> P[Deseasonalize Series]
    P --> Q[Forecast Future Values]
```

## 4.2 Seasonal Index Interpretation Chart (Conceptual Block Diagram)

```mermaid
block-beta
    columns 5
    block:row1:1
        Q1["Q1 SI 0.96 Below Normal"]
    end
    block:row2:1
        Q2["Q2 SI 1.25 Peak Season"]
    end
    block:row3:1
        Q3["Q3 SI 1.02 Near Average"]
    end
    block:row4:1
        Q4["Q4 SI 0.77 Trough Season"]
    end
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Deseasonalization Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Raw Sales Y sub t] --> B[Compute Trend T sub t]
    B --> C[Multiply by Seasonal Index S sub q]
    C --> D[Apply Forecast Equation]
    D --> E[Deseasonalized Series]
    E --> F[ARIMA or Regression Model]
    F --> G[Final Forecast Y hat sub t plus h]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**Define seasonal variation in a time series. List any two causes.**

**Model Answer:**

> Seasonal variation is the component of a time series that represents the regular, periodic fluctuations occurring within a sub-period (quarter/month) of a year that repeat year after year with similar intensity.
>
> **Two causes:**
> (i) **Weather & Climate** — e.g., ice-cream sales rise in summer.
> (ii) **Festivals & Customs** — e.g., Onam spike in Kerala retail markets.

*(Self-explanatory; 1 mark for definition, 1 mark each for causes = 3 marks)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Why is the Ratio-to-Moving-Average method preferred over the Simple Average Method for measuring seasonal variation?**

**Model Answer:**

> (i) The Simple Average Method is biased by **trend** — rising trends inflate later years' averages.
> (ii) The Ratio-to-Moving-Average Method **removes trend and cyclical effects** by dividing actual values by the centered moving average, leaving a *pure* seasonal-irregular ratio.
> (iii) It is therefore more **accurate, robust, and theoretically sound** for non-stationary series.

*(3 marks — 1 mark per point)*

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the four classical methods of measuring seasonal variation. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** From the data below of quarterly production (in tonnes) of a sugar factory, compute the seasonal indices using the **Ratio-to-Moving-Average Method**. **(7 Marks)**

| Year | Q1 | Q2 | Q3 | Q4 |
|------|----|----|----|----|
| 2020 | 50 | 80 | 100 | 30 |
| 2021 | 60 | 90 | 110 | 40 |
| 2022 | 70 | 100 | 120 | 50 |
| 2023 | 80 | 110 | 130 | 60 |

#### Model Solution (a) — Four Methods (7 Marks)

1. **Simple Average Method** — Average each quarter, then divide by grand mean.
2. **Ratio-to-Trend Method** — Fit linear trend $T_t = a + bt$, compute $Y_t/T_t$, average per quarter, adjust.
3. **Ratio-to-Moving-Average Method** — Divide $Y_t$ by centered moving average; average ratios per quarter; normalize to sum = 400.
4. **Link Relatives Method** — Use $LR_t = (Y_t/Y_{t-1}) \times 100$, build chain relatives, average, adjust.

#### Model Solution (b) — Numerical (7 Marks)

**Step 1:** 4-Q Moving Totals:

- Q4 2020: $50+80+100+30 = 260$
- Q1 2021: $80+100+30+60 = 270$
- Q2 2021: $100+30+60+90 = 280$
- Q3 2021: $30+60+90+110 = 290$
- Q4 2021: $60+90+110+40 = 300$
- Q1 2022: $90+110+40+70 = 310$
- Q2 2022: $110+40+70+100 = 320$
- Q3 2022: $40+70+100+120 = 330$
- Q4 2022: $70+100+120+50 = 340$
- Q1 2023: $100+120+50+80 = 350$
- Q2 2023: $120+50+80+110 = 360$
- Q3 2023: $50+80+110+130 = 370$

**Step 2:** 4-Q MA (÷4): 65, 67.5, 70, 72.5, 75, 77.5, 80, 82.5, 85, 87.5, 90, 92.5

**Step 3:** Centered MA = average of two consecutive MAs:

| Quarter | CMA |
|---|---|
| Q3 2020 | 66.25 |
| Q4 2020 | 68.75 |
| Q1 2021 | 71.25 |
| Q2 2021 | 73.75 |
| Q3 2021 | 76.25 |
| Q4 2021 | 78.75 |
| Q1 2022 | 81.25 |
| Q2 2022 | 83.75 |
| Q3 2022 | 86.25 |
| Q4 2022 | 88.75 |
| Q1 2023 | 91.25 |
| Q2 2023 | 93.75 |

**Step 4:** Ratios $Y_t / \text{CMA}_t$:

| Quarter | 2020 | 2021 | 2022 | 2023 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Q1 | — | 60/71.25 = **0.8421** | 70/81.25 = **0.8615** | 80/91.25 = **0.8767** |
| Q2 | — | 90/73.75 = **1.2203** | 100/83.75 = **1.1940** | 110/93.75 = **1.1733** |
| Q3 | 100/66.25 = **1.5094** | 110/76.25 = **1.4426** | 120/86.25 = **1.3913** | — |
| Q4 | 30/68.75 = **0.4364** | 40/78.75 = **0.5079** | 50/88.75 = **0.5634** | — |

**Step 5:** Average ratios and normalize:

| Quarter | Mean | Adjusted SI |
|---|---|---|
| Q1 | 0.8601 | **0.8623** |
| Q2 | 1.1959 | **1.1989** |
| Q3 | 1.4478 | **1.4513** |
| Q4 | 0.5026 | **0.5039** |
| **Sum** | 4.0064 | **4.0164** |

$$\text{Adjustment Factor } A = \frac{4.0000}{4.0064} = 0.9984$$

*(Corrected SIs after re-normalization: Q1 ≈ 0.86, Q2 ≈ 1.20, Q3 ≈ 1.45, Q4 ≈ 0.50.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Distinguish between **additive** and **multiplicative** time-series models. When is the multiplicative model preferred? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Link Relatives Method** of measuring seasonal variation with a suitable example. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution (a) — Additive vs Multiplicative (7 Marks)

| Feature | Additive | Multiplicative |
|---|---|---|
| Equation | $Y_t = T_t + S_t + C_t + I_t$ | $Y_t = T_t \times S_t \times C_t \times I_t$ |
| Nature of Seasonal Swing | Constant in absolute units | Proportional to trend |
| Seasonal Index | Sum of SIs = 0 | Product of SIs = 1 (or sum = 400) |
| Preferred when | Seasonal variation is roughly stable over years | Seasonal swing grows with the trend |
| Example | Monthly temperature deviation from mean | Quarterly revenue of growing company |

**Prefer multiplicative when** the trend is clearly rising or falling *and* the seasonal fluctuations visibly widen/narrow over time (e.g., a startup's revenue where Q4 absolute spike grows each year).

#### Model Solution (b) — Link Relatives Method (7 Marks)

**Definition:** The Link Relative for period $t$ is the percentage change from the previous period:

$$LR_t = \frac{Y_t}{Y_{t-1}} \times 100$$

**Procedure:**

1. Compute $LR_t$ for every period.
2. Tabulate $LR_t$ values in a matrix indexed by Year × Quarter.
3. Convert to **Chain Relatives**: multiply successively within each year (each January LR set as 100% and rescale).
4. Take the average chain relative per quarter.
5. Convert averages to current month base and adjust so sum = 400.

**Example snippet** — If Jan LR = 100, Feb LR = 110, Mar LR = 120:

$$\text{Chain Rel}_{\text{Feb}} = 100 \times 1.10 = 110$$

$$\text{Chain Rel}_{\text{Mar}} = 110 \times 1.20 = 132$$

Repeating this for all quarters across all years and averaging gives the final seasonal indices.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to center the moving average**: An even-period MA (4-Qtr) falls *between* quarters. Not centering gives wrong ratios. **Loss: 2 marks.**
> 2. **Skipping normalization**: Raw seasonal indices rarely sum exactly to 400. Students forget to multiply by the adjustment factor. **Loss: 1 mark.**
> 3. **Including extreme values blindly**: Standard practice is to ignore unusually high/low ratios before averaging. **Loss: 1 mark.**
> 4. **Confusing Cyclical and Seasonal**: Cyclical > 1 year period (e.g., business cycles 5–7 years); Seasonal ≤ 1 year. **Loss: 2 marks.**
> 5. **Sign errors in additive model**: $S_t$ in additive model can be negative; index-style interpretation doesn't apply.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Seasonal Variation** = regular intra-year fluctuations (≤ 1 year period).
- Distinguish from **Cyclical** (> 1 year) and **Irregular** (random shocks).
- The **classical decomposition**:
  - Additive: $Y_t = T_t + S_t + C_t + I_t$
  - Multiplicative: $Y_t = T_t \times S_t \times C_t \times I_t$
- **Ratio-to-Moving-Average Method** steps (memorize):
  1. Compute 4-Q (or 12-M) Moving Total.
  2. Divide by 4 (or 12) → Moving Average.
  3. **Center** the MA (average two consecutive MAs).
  4. Compute $Y_t / \text{CMA}_t$.
  5. Average ratios per quarter.
  6. **Normalize** so $\sum SI = 400$ (quarterly) or $1200$ (monthly).
- In the **multiplicative model**, SI averages to **1.00** (or **100%**).
- **Link Relatives Method** uses successive period-to-period percentage changes.
- **Multiplicative** is preferred when seasonal swing grows with the trend.
- Final SI must satisfy the **sum constraint** — verification step is mandatory.
- After decomposition, the deseasonalized series can be used with **ARIMA / SARIMA** for forecasting.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Univariate and Multivariate Data
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET742 - FINANCIAL TIME SERIES ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Univariate and Multivariate Data

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Univariate Data — Formal Definition

In the context of **Financial Time Series Analysis**, a **univariate time series** is a sequential collection of observations recorded over time for a **single random variable**. Mathematically, it is represented as:

$$\{x_t : t = 1, 2, 3, \dots, T\}$$

where $x_t$ denotes the value of the single variable at time $t$, and $T$ is the total number of observations. The subscript "uni-" implies **one** dimension of variation.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Examples of univariate financial series include the daily closing price of a single stock, monthly inflation rate, or daily log-returns of one asset.

### 1.2 Multivariate Data — Formal Definition

A **multivariate (or vector) time series** is a sequential collection of observations recorded over time for **two or more related random variables**. It is represented as:

$$\{\mathbf{x}_t = (x_{1,t}, x_{2,t}, \dots, x_{k,t})' : t = 1, 2, 3, \dots, T\}$$

where $k \geq 2$ is the number of variables, and $\mathbf{x}_t$ is a $k \times 1$ column vector of observations at time $t$. The case $k=2$ is specifically called a **bivariate** time series.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Multivariate?** In real financial markets, asset prices do not move in isolation. The price of Reliance is influenced by crude oil prices, USD/INR exchange rate, and NIFTY index. To capture these **dynamic interdependencies**, multivariate models become essential.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Analogy 1 — Univariate (A Single Cardiogram):**
Imagine a doctor attaching **one** ECG electrode to a patient's chest. The machine draws a single wavy line representing the heartbeat over time. That single line is your **univariate time series** — one signal, one variable, evolving through time.

**Analogy 2 — Multivariate (An Orchestra Recording):**
Now imagine a recording engineer placing **multiple microphones** in a concert hall — one for the violin section, one for the cello, one for the piano. The engineer records the **synchronized sound waves** of all instruments over time. Each instrument is a univariate series, but the **synchronized multi-track recording is a multivariate series**. The engineer can study both individual instrument behavior AND how the violin responds when the piano plays louder (this is **cross-correlation**).

**Real-World Financial Examples:**

| Series Type | Example |
|---|---|
| Univariate | Daily closing price of TCS stock from 2015–2024 |
| Bivariate | Daily closing prices of TCS and Infosys |
| Multivariate ($k \geq 3$) | Daily closing prices of TCS, Infosys, Wipro, and HCL Tech |
| Multivariate (mixed) | Daily NIFTY index, USD/INR rate, and crude oil price |

### 1.4 Cross-Sectional vs. Time Series Data

A critical distinction the KTU syllabus expects students to identify:

- **Time Series Data:** Observations of one or more variables indexed by **time** $t$ (e.g., daily Sensex values).
- **Cross-Sectional Data:** Observations of multiple entities at the **same point in time** (e.g., closing prices of 50 NIFTY stocks on a single day).
- **Panel (Longitudinal) Data:** A hybrid — multiple entities observed over time (e.g., closing prices of 50 NIFTY stocks over 252 trading days).

> [!NOTE]
> **In PEIET742**, our primary focus is on **time series data**, but understanding cross-sectional distinctions helps in interpreting correlation structures and is foundational for later modules on VAR and VECM.

### 1.5 Why Distinguish Univariate from Multivariate?

The distinction matters because:

1. **Model Selection:** Univariate data can be modeled with AR, MA, ARMA, ARIMA. Multivariate data requires VAR, VECM, VMA, or VARMA.
2. **Computational Complexity:** A $k$-variate VAR($p$) has $k^2 p + k$ parameters vs. only $p+1$ for a univariate AR($p$).
3. **Information Content:** Multivariate models exploit **lead-lag relationships** that univariate models cannot capture.
4. **Risk Management:** Portfolio risk (variance) depends on covariances between assets — a fundamentally multivariate concept.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Mean Function (Univariate)

The **mean function** of a univariate time series $\{x_t\}$ measures the central tendency at each time $t$:

$$\mu_t = E(x_t)$$

If the series is **stationary** (statistical properties do not change with time), the mean function is a **constant**:

$$\mu_t = \mu \quad \forall t$$

### 2.2 The Variance Function (Univariate)

The **variance function** measures the dispersion around the mean at time $t$:

$$\sigma_t^2 = E[(x_t - \mu_t)^2] = \text{Var}(x_t)$$

For a stationary series, $\sigma_t^2 = \sigma^2$ (constant).

### 2.3 The Autocovariance Function (ACVF)

The **autocovariance function** measures the **linear dependence** between $x_t$ and $x_{t+k}$ separated by a time lag $k$:

$$\gamma_k = \text{Cov}(x_t, x_{t+k}) = E[(x_t - \mu)(x_{t+k} - \mu)]$$

For $k = 0$, this reduces to the variance: $\gamma_0 = \sigma^2$.

### 2.4 The Autocorrelation Function (ACF)

The **autocorrelation function** is the normalized version of the autocovariance, bounded between $-1$ and $+1$:

$$\rho_k = \frac{\gamma_k}{\gamma_0} = \frac{\text{Cov}(x_t, x_{t+k})}{\sqrt{\text{Var}(x_t)\text{Var}(x_{t+k})}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield:** Always remember $\rho_0 = 1$ and $-1 \leq \rho_k \leq 1$.

### 2.5 Extension to Multivariate Series: Cross-Covariance and Cross-Correlation

For a multivariate series $\mathbf{x}_t = (x_{1,t}, x_{2,t}, \dots, x_{k,t})'$, the pairwise dependence between two component series $x_{i,t}$ and $x_{j,t}$ is captured by the **cross-covariance function (CCVF)**:

$$\gamma_{ij}(k) = E[(x_{i,t} - \mu_{i})(x_{j,t+k} - \mu_{j})]$$

and the **cross-correlation function (CCF)**:

$$\rho_{ij}(k) = \frac{\gamma_{ij}(k)}{\sqrt{\gamma_{ii}(0)\,\gamma_{jj}(0)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Asymmetry:** Unlike the ACF, the CCF is **not symmetric**: $\rho_{ij}(k) \neq \rho_{ij}(-k)$ in general. It is anti-symmetric: $\rho_{ij}(k) = \rho_{ji}(-k)$. This asymmetry is what allows us to detect **lead-lag relationships** in financial markets.

### 2.6 The Cross-Correlation Matrix (Multivariate)

The complete dependence structure of a $k$-variate series at lag $k$ is captured in the $k \times k$ **cross-correlation matrix**:

$$\boldsymbol{\rho}(k) = \begin{bmatrix} \rho_{11}(k) & \rho_{12}(k) & \cdots & \rho_{1k}(k) \\ \rho_{21}(k) & \rho_{22}(k) & \cdots & \rho_{2k}(k) \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ \rho_{k1}(k) & \rho_{k2}(k) & \cdots & \rho_{kk}(k) \end{bmatrix}$$

The diagonal elements are the ACFs of each component; the off-diagonal elements are the CCFs.

### 2.7 Sample Estimators (From Real Data)

Given a finite sample of size $T$, the **sample mean** is:

$$\bar{x} = \frac{1}{T}\sum_{t=1}^{T} x_t$$

The **sample autocovariance** at lag $k$ is:

$$\hat{\gamma}_k = \frac{1}{T}\sum_{t=k+1}^{T}(x_t - \bar{x})(x_{t-k} - \bar{x})$$

The **sample autocorrelation** is:

$$\hat{\rho}_k = \frac{\hat{\gamma}_k}{\hat{\gamma}_0}$$

The **sample cross-correlation** between series $x_i$ and $x_j$ at lag $k$:

$$\hat{\rho}_{ij}(k) = \frac{\sum_{t=k+1}^{T}(x_{i,t} - \bar{x}_i)(x_{j,t-k} - \bar{x}_j)}{\sqrt{\sum_{t=1}^{T}(x_{i,t} - \bar{x}_i)^2 \cdot \sum_{t=1}^{T}(x_{j,t} - \bar{x}_j)^2}}$$

### 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — Must-Memorize Formula Table**

| Concept | Formula | Range / Property |
|---|---|---|
| Univariate mean | $\mu = E(x_t)$ | Constant under stationarity |
| Univariate variance | $\sigma^2 = \text{Var}(x_t)$ | $\sigma^2 \geq 0$ |
| Autocovariance | $\gamma_k = E[(x_t - \mu)(x_{t+k} - \mu)]$ | $\gamma_{-k} = \gamma_k$ (symmetric) |
| Autocorrelation (ACF) | $\rho_k = \gamma_k / \gamma_0$ | $-1 \leq \rho_k \leq 1$; $\rho_0 = 1$ |
| Cross-covariance (CCVF) | $\gamma_{ij}(k) = E[(x_{i,t}-\mu_i)(x_{j,t+k}-\mu_j)]$ | Anti-symmetric: $\gamma_{ij}(k) = \gamma_{ji}(-k)$ |
| Cross-correlation (CCF) | $\rho_{ij}(k) = \gamma_{ij}(k) / \sqrt{\gamma_{ii}(0)\gamma_{jj}(0)}$ | $-1 \leq \rho_{ij}(k) \leq 1$ |
| Sample mean | $\bar{x} = T^{-1}\sum x_t$ | Unbiased estimator of $\mu$ |
| Sample ACF | $\hat{\rho}_k = \hat{\gamma}_k / \hat{\gamma}_0$ | Used in EACF, PACF plots |
| Bartlett's Std. Error | $SE(\hat{\rho}_k) \approx 1/\sqrt{T}$ | For white noise at lag $k > 0$ |

### 2.9 Real-World Utility in Engineering and Finance

1. **Portfolio Construction (Markowitz Theory):** Requires the covariance matrix $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}$ of multiple assets — a direct application of multivariate concepts.
2. **Risk Management (Value-at-Risk):** Computing VaR for a portfolio of $k$ assets requires multivariate variance estimation.
3. **Algorithmic Trading:** Lead-lag CCF analysis identifies which asset moves first, enabling pair-trading strategies.
4. **Macroeconomic Forecasting:** Central banks use multivariate models (VAR) to model the joint dynamics of GDP, inflation, and interest rates.
5. **Signal Processing:** Univariate/Multivariate AR models are used in speech recognition, EEG/ECG analysis, and vibration monitoring of mechanical systems.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Covariance Definition to the Sample ACF

**Starting Point (Population ACF):**

The population autocorrelation at lag $k$ is defined as:

$$\rho_k = \frac{\text{Cov}(x_t, x_{t+k})}{\sqrt{\text{Var}(x_t) \cdot \text{Var}(x_{t+k})}}$$

**Step 1 — Stationarity Assumption:**
Under (weak) stationarity, $\text{Var}(x_t) = \text{Var}(x_{t+k}) = \gamma_0$ for all $t$ and $k$. Substituting:

$$\rho_k = \frac{\gamma_k}{\sqrt{\gamma_0 \cdot \gamma_0}} = \frac{\gamma_k}{\gamma_0}$$

**Step 2 — Replace Population Mean with Sample Mean:**
Since the true mean $\mu$ is unknown, we substitute the sample mean $\bar{x} = T^{-1}\sum_{t=1}^{T} x_t$. The sample autocovariance becomes:

$$\hat{\gamma}_k = \frac{1}{T}\sum_{t=k+1}^{T}(x_t - \bar{x})(x_{t-k} - \bar{x})$$

*Logic:* We sum over all valid pairs $(x_t, x_{t-k})$ where $t - k \geq 1$ and $t \leq T$, giving exactly $T - k$ pairs. We then divide by $T$ (the convention used by some textbooks; others use $T-k$ for unbiasedness).

**Step 3 — Form the Sample ACF:**

$$\hat{\rho}_k = \frac{\hat{\gamma}_k}{\hat{\gamma}_0} = \frac{\sum_{t=k+1}^{T}(x_t - \bar{x})(x_{t-k} - \bar{x})}{\sum_{t=1}^{T}(x_t - \bar{x})^2}$$

*Logic:* The denominator $\hat{\gamma}_0 = T^{-1}\sum(x_t - \bar{x})^2$ is the sample variance. Dividing by it normalizes $\hat{\rho}_k$ to lie in $[-1, +1]$.

**Step 4 — Verify Boundary at $k=0$:**

$$\hat{\rho}_0 = \frac{\hat{\gamma}_0}{\hat{\gamma}_0} = 1 \quad \checkmark$$

This confirms the ACF is **unity at lag zero** by construction.

### 3.2 Derivation: Cross-Correlation Asymmetry Property

**Claim:** $\rho_{ij}(k) = \rho_{ji}(-k)$.

**Proof:**

By definition, the cross-covariance function between series $i$ and $j$ at lag $k$ is:

$$\gamma_{ij}(k) = E[(x_{i,t} - \mu_i)(x_{j,t+k} - \mu_j)]$$

Now consider $\gamma_{ji}(-k)$:

$$\gamma_{ji}(-k) = E[(x_{j,t} - \mu_j)(x_{i,t-k} - \mu_i)]$$

Let $s = t - k$, so $t = s + k$:

$$\gamma_{ji}(-k) = E[(x_{j,s+k} - \mu_j)(x_{i,s} - \mu_i)]$$

$$\gamma_{ji}(-k) = E[(x_{i,s} - \mu_i)(x_{j,s+k} - \mu_j)] = \gamma_{ij}(k) \quad \blacksquare$$

Dividing both sides by $\sqrt{\gamma_{ii}(0)\gamma_{jj}(0)}$ yields the desired CCF identity. **Implication:** If $x_i$ leads $x_j$, we observe $\rho_{ij}(k) \neq 0$ for $k > 0$.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Computing Sample ACF

**Problem:** Given observations $x_1 = 2, x_2 = 4, x_3 = 6, x_4 = 8, x_5 = 10$, compute the sample ACF at lags $k = 0, 1, 2$.

**Step 1 — Sample Mean:**

$$\bar{x} = \frac{2+4+6+8+10}{5} = \frac{30}{5} = 6$$

**Step 2 — Deviations from Mean:**

$$x_t - \bar{x}: \quad -4, -2, 0, 2, 4$$

**Step 3 — Sample Variance (numerator at $k=0$):**

$$\hat{\gamma}_0 = \frac{1}{5}\sum_{t=1}^{5}(x_t - \bar{x})^2 = \frac{16+4+0+4+16}{5} = \frac{40}{5} = 8$$

**Step 4 — Sample Autocovariance at $k=1$:**

$$\hat{\gamma}_1 = \frac{1}{5}\sum_{t=2}^{5}(x_t - \bar{x})(x_{t-1} - \bar{x})$$

$$\hat{\gamma}_1 = \frac{(-2)(-4) + (0)(-2) + (2)(0) + (4)(2)}{5} = \frac{8 + 0 + 0 + 8}{5} = \frac{16}{5} = 3.2$$

**Step 5 — Sample Autocovariance at $k=2$:**

$$\hat{\gamma}_2 = \frac{1}{5}\sum_{t=3}^{5}(x_t - \bar{x})(x_{t-2} - \bar{x})$$

$$\hat{\gamma}_2 = \frac{(0)(-4) + (2)(-2) + (4)(0)}{5} = \frac{0 - 4 + 0}{5} = -0.8$$

**Step 6 — Sample ACFs:**

$$\hat{\rho}_0 = \frac{8}{8} = 1.000$$

$$\hat{\rho}_1 = \frac{3.2}{8} = 0.400$$

$$\hat{\rho}_2 = \frac{-0.8}{8} = -0.100$$

**Step 7 — Interpretation:** The series exhibits **strong positive autocorrelation at lag 1** (consistent with a linear trend) and slight negative correlation at lag 2, typical of an overdifferenced or oscillatory pattern.

### 3.4 Worked Example — Cross-Correlation Between Two Series

**Problem:** Two series $x_t$ and $y_t$ with $T=4$: $x = (1, 2, 3, 4)$, $y = (2, 3, 4, 5)$. Compute the sample CCF at $k = 0, 1, -1$.

**Step 1 — Sample Means:** $\bar{x} = 2.5$, $\bar{y} = 3.5$.

**Step 2 — Deviations:** $x_t - \bar{x} = (-1.5, -0.5, 0.5, 1.5)$, $y_t - \bar{y} = (-1.5, -0.5, 0.5, 1.5)$.

**Step 3 — Lag $k=0$ CCVF:**

$$\hat{\gamma}_{xy}(0) = \frac{(-1.5)(-1.5)+(-0.5)(-0.5)+(0.5)(0.5)+(1.5)(1.5)}{4} = \frac{2.25+0.25+0.25+2.25}{4} = \frac{5}{4} = 1.25$$

**Step 4 — Lag $k=1$ CCVF (using pairs $(y_t, x_{t-1})$):**

$$\hat{\gamma}_{xy}(1) = \frac{(-0.5)(-1.5)+(0.5)(-0.5)+(1.5)(0.5)}{4} = \frac{0.75 - 0.25 + 0.75}{4} = \frac{1.25}{4} = 0.3125$$

**Step 5 — Lag $k=-1$ CCVF (using pairs $(y_t, x_{t+1})$):**

$$\hat{\gamma}_{xy}(-1) = \frac{(-1.5)(-0.5)+(-0.5)(0.5)+(0.5)(1.5)}{4} = \frac{0.75 - 0.25 + 0.75}{4} = 0.3125$$

**Step 6 — CCFs (normalizing by $\sqrt{\hat{\gamma}_{xx}(0)\hat{\gamma}_{yy}(0)} = 1.25$):**

$$\hat{\rho}_{xy}(0) = \frac{1.25}{1.25} = 1.00$$

$$\hat{\rho}_{xy}(1) = \frac{0.3125}{1.25} = 0.25$$

$$\hat{\rho}_{xy}(-1) = \frac{0.3125}{1.25} = 0.25$$

**Step 7 — Interpretation:** The two series move **synchronously** (correlation at $k=0$ is 1). The small non-zero CCF at $k=\pm 1$ is due to the linear trend shared by both.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`** Define a univariate time series. Distinguish it from a multivariate time series with one financial example of each.

**Model Answer:**

A **univariate time series** is a sequence of observations $\{x_t\}_{t=1}^{T}$ recorded over time for a **single random variable**. Example: Daily closing price of HDFC Bank stock from Jan 2020 to Dec 2023.

A **multivariate time series** is a sequence of $k$-dimensional vectors $\mathbf{x}_t = (x_{1,t}, x_{2,t}, \dots, x_{k,t})'$ for $k \geq 2$. Example: Daily closing prices of HDFC Bank, ICICI Bank, and SBI captured simultaneously — a 3-variate series used to study banking-sector co-movements.

| Feature | Univariate | Multivariate |
|---|---|---|
| Dimension | $k=1$ | $k \geq 2$ |
| Dependence measure | ACF | ACF + CCF |
| Model class | AR, MA, ARIMA | VAR, VECM, VARMA |

*(3 marks — 1 mark for each definition + 1 mark for the distinction table or example)*

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`** What is the cross-correlation function (CCF)? State one key property that differentiates it from the ACF.

**Model Answer:**

The **Cross-Correlation Function (CCF)** between two series $x_{i,t}$ and $x_{j,t}$ at lag $k$ is defined as:

$$\rho_{ij}(k) = \frac{E[(x_{i,t}-\mu_i)(x_{j,t+k}-\mu_j)]}{\sqrt{E[(x_{i,t}-\mu_i)^2]\cdot E[(x_{j,t}-\mu_j)^2]}}$$

**Key differentiating property:** Unlike the ACF which is **symmetric** $\rho_k = \rho_{-k}$, the CCF is **anti-symmetric**: $\rho_{ij}(k) = \rho_{ji}(-k)$. This asymmetry is what enables lead-lag detection — if $x_i$ Granger-causes $x_j$, then $\rho_{ij}(k)$ is significantly non-zero for some $k > 0$.

*(3 marks — 1 mark formula, 1 mark property, 1 mark lead-lag implication)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`**

**(a)** For a weakly stationary time series, derive the expressions for the mean function, autocovariance function, and autocorrelation function. State and prove the symmetry property of the autocovariance function. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider two financial time series representing daily log-returns of NIFTY ($x_t$) and BANK NIFTY ($y_t$) over $T = 5$ days: $x = (0.01, -0.02, 0.03, -0.01, 0.02)$ and $y = (0.02, -0.01, 0.04, -0.02, 0.03)$. Compute the sample mean, sample variance, sample ACF at lag 1 for the NIFTY series, and the sample CCF between NIFTY and BANK NIFTY at lag 0. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question A:**

#### Part (a) — Derivation of Mean, ACVF, ACF and Symmetry Property

**Step 1 — Mean Function:**
For a weakly stationary series, the mean is constant across time:

$$\mu_t = E(x_t) = \mu \quad \forall t$$

**Step 2 — Autocovariance Function (ACVF):**
The dependence between $x_t$ and $x_{t+k}$ is measured by:

$$\gamma_k = E[(x_t - \mu)(x_{t+k} - \mu)]$$

**Step 3 — Autocorrelation Function (ACF):**
Normalizing the autocovariance by the variance:

$$\rho_k = \frac{\gamma_k}{\gamma_0}$$

This ensures $-1 \leq \rho_k \leq 1$.

**Step 4 — Symmetry Property Proof:**

**Claim:** $\gamma_k = \gamma_{-k}$.

**Proof:**

$$\gamma_{-k} = E[(x_t - \mu)(x_{t-k} - \mu)]$$

Substitute $s = t - k$, so $t = s + k$:

$$\gamma_{-k} = E[(x_{s+k} - \mu)(x_s - \mu)] = E[(x_s - \mu)(x_{s+k} - \mu)] = \gamma_k \quad \blacksquare$$

**Step 5 — Conclusion:** Under stationarity, the mean is a constant $\mu$, the variance is a constant $\gamma_0$, and the autocovariance depends only on the lag $k$, satisfying $\gamma_k = \gamma_{-k}$ and $\vert \rho_k \vert \leq 1$.

*(7 marks breakdown: 1 mark for each of the 3 definitions, 2 marks for the symmetry proof, 1 mark for stating properties)*

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Computation

**Given:** $x = (0.01, -0.02, 0.03, -0.01, 0.02)$, $y = (0.02, -0.01, 0.04, -0.02, 0.03)$, $T = 5$.

**Step 1 — Sample Mean of NIFTY ($x$):**

$$\bar{x} = \frac{0.01 - 0.02 + 0.03 - 0.01 + 0.02}{5} = \frac{0.03}{5} = 0.006$$

**Step 2 — Sample Variance of NIFTY:**

$$\hat{\sigma}_x^2 = \frac{1}{5}\sum_{t=1}^{5}(x_t - \bar{x})^2$$

Deviations: $0.004, -0.026, 0.024, -0.016, 0.014$.

Squared deviations: $0.000016, 0.000676, 0.000576, 0.000256, 0.000196$.

$$\hat{\sigma}_x^2 = \frac{0.001720}{5} = 0.000344$$

**Step 3 — Sample ACF of NIFTY at Lag 1:**

$$\hat{\gamma}_1 = \frac{1}{5}\sum_{t=2}^{5}(x_t - \bar{x})(x_{t-1} - \bar{x})$$

$$= \frac{(-0.026)(0.004) + (0.024)(-0.026) + (-0.016)(0.024) + (0.014)(-0.016)}{5}$$

$$= \frac{-0.000104 - 0.000624 - 0.000384 - 0.000224}{5} = \frac{-0.001336}{5} = -0.0002672$$

$$\hat{\rho}_1 = \frac{\hat{\gamma}_1}{\hat{\sigma}_x^2} = \frac{-0.0002672}{0.000344} = -0.7767$$

**Step 4 — Sample Mean of BANK NIFTY ($y$):**

$$\bar{y} = \frac{0.02 - 0.01 + 0.04 - 0.02 + 0.03}{5} = \frac{0.06}{5} = 0.012$$

**Step 5 — Sample CCF between NIFTY and BANK NIFTY at Lag 0:**

$$\hat{\gamma}_{xy}(0) = \frac{1}{5}\sum_{t=1}^{5}(x_t - \bar{x})(y_t - \bar{y})$$

Deviations of $y$: $0.008, -0.022, 0.028, -0.032, 0.018$.

Cross-products: $(0.004)(0.008), (-0.026)(-0.022), (0.024)(0.028), (-0.016)(-0.032), (0.014)(0.018)$

$$= 0.000032, 0.000572, 0.000672, 0.000512, 0.000252$$

$$\hat{\gamma}_{xy}(0) = \frac{0.002040}{5} = 0.000408$$

$$\hat{\sigma}_y^2 = \frac{0.000064+0.000484+0.000784+0.001024+0.000324}{5} = \frac{0.002680}{5} = 0.000536$$

$$\hat{\rho}_{xy}(0) = \frac{0.000408}{\sqrt{0.000344 \times 0.000536}} = \frac{0.000408}{\sqrt{1.8438 \times 10^{-7}}} = \frac{0.000408}{0.0004294} = 0.9502$$

**Step 6 — Final Answers:**

$$\bar{x} = 0.006, \quad \hat{\sigma}_x^2 = 0.000344, \quad \hat{\rho}_1(x) = -0.7767, \quad \hat{\rho}_{xy}(0) = 0.9502$$

**Step 7 — Interpretation:** The NIFTY series shows **strong negative autocorrelation at lag 1** (mean-reverting behavior typical of financial returns), while NIFTY and BANK NIFTY exhibit an **extremely high simultaneous correlation** ($\approx 0.95$), consistent with the banking sector dominating the index.

*(7 marks breakdown: 1 mark mean, 1 mark variance, 2 marks ACF lag 1, 2 marks CCF at lag 0, 1 mark interpretation)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`**

**(a)** Explain the concept of a multivariate time series with a suitable financial example. Define the cross-covariance matrix and the cross-correlation matrix. State and prove the anti-symmetry property of the cross-covariance function. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Given three daily return series $x_t, y_t, z_t$ over 4 days:
- $x = (1, 2, 3, 4)$
- $y = (2, 4, 5, 7)$
- $z = (3, 1, 4, 2)$

Construct the $3 \times 3$ sample cross-correlation matrix $\boldsymbol{\rho}(0)$. Interpret the off-diagonal elements. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question B:**

#### Part (a) — Multivariate Time Series, Cross-Covariance, Cross-Correlation, Anti-Symmetry

**Step 1 — Multivariate Time Series Definition:**
A $k$-variate time series is a sequence of vectors $\mathbf{x}_t = (x_{1,t}, x_{2,t}, \dots, x_{k,t})'$ for $t = 1, 2, \dots, T$, where $k \geq 2$. **Example:** The daily closing prices of three banking stocks — SBI, HDFC, and ICICI — form a 3-variate series used to study inter-bank co-movements.

**Step 2 — Cross-Covariance Matrix at Lag $k$:**

$$\boldsymbol{\Gamma}(k) = \begin{bmatrix} \gamma_{11}(k) & \gamma_{12}(k) & \cdots & \gamma_{1k}(k) \\ \gamma_{21}(k) & \gamma_{22}(k) & \cdots & \gamma_{2k}(k) \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ \gamma_{k1}(k) & \gamma_{k2}(k) & \cdots & \gamma_{kk}(k) \end{bmatrix}$$

where $\gamma_{ij}(k) = E[(x_{i,t} - \mu_i)(x_{j,t+k} - \mu_j)]$.

**Step 3 — Cross-Correlation Matrix at Lag $k$:**

$$\boldsymbol{\rho}(k) = \begin{bmatrix} \rho_{11}(k) & \rho_{12}(k) & \cdots & \rho_{1k}(k) \\ \rho_{21}(k) & \rho_{22}(k) & \cdots & \rho_{2k}(k) \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ \rho_{k1}(k) & \rho_{k2}(k) & \cdots & \rho_{kk}(k) \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Anti-Symmetry Property:**

**Claim:** $\gamma_{ij}(k) = \gamma_{ji}(-k)$.

**Proof:**

Starting from $\gamma_{ji}(-k)$:

$$\gamma_{ji}(-k) = E[(x_{j,t} - \mu_j)(x_{i,t-k} - \mu_i)]$$

Substitute $s = t - k$, so $t = s + k$:

$$\gamma_{ji}(-k) = E[(x_{j,s+k} - \mu_j)(x_{i,s} - \mu_i)]$$

Reorder (multiplication is commutative):

$$\gamma_{ji}(-k) = E[(x_{i,s} - \mu_i)(x_{j,s+k} - \mu_j)] = \gamma_{ij}(k) \quad \blacksquare$$

**Step 5 — Diagonal Property:** $\gamma_{ii}(k) = \gamma_{ii}(-k)$ (since the ACVF of a stationary series is symmetric). The CCVF is anti-symmetric off the diagonal.

*(7 marks breakdown: 2 marks definition + example, 2 marks for matrices, 2 marks for anti-symmetry proof, 1 mark for stating implication)*

---

#### Part (b) — Constructing the $3 \times 3$ Cross-Correlation Matrix

**Given:** $x = (1,2,3,4)$, $y = (2,4,5,7)$, $z = (3,1,4,2)$, $T=4$.

**Step 1 — Sample Means:**

$$\bar{x} = \frac{1+2+3+4}{4} = 2.5$$

$$\bar{y} = \frac{2+4+5+7}{4} = 4.5$$

$$\bar{z} = \frac{3+1+4+2}{4} = 2.5$$

**Step 2 — Deviations:**

$x - \bar{x} = (-1.5, -0.5, 0.5, 1.5)$

$y - \bar{y} = (-2.5, -0.5, 0.5, 2.5)$

$z - \bar{z} = (0.5, -1.5, 1.5, -0.5)$

**Step 3 — Variances (denominator inputs):**

$$\hat{\sigma}_x^2 = \frac{2.25+0.25+0.25+2.25}{4} = \frac{5.0}{4} = 1.25$$

$$\hat{\sigma}_y^2 = \frac{6.25+0.25+0.25+6.25}{4} = \frac{13.0}{4} = 3.25$$

$$\hat{\sigma}_z^2 = \frac{0.25+2.25+2.25+0.25}{4} = \frac{5.0}{4} = 1.25$$

**Step 4 — Cross-Covariances at Lag 0:**

$$\hat{\gamma}_{xy}(0) = \frac{(-1.5)(-2.5)+(-0.5)(-0.5)+(0.5)(0.5)+(1.5)(2.5)}{4} = \frac{3.75+0.25+0.25+3.75}{4} = \frac{8.0}{4} = 2.0$$

$$\hat{\gamma}_{xz}(0) = \frac{(-1.5)(0.5)+(-0.5)(-1.5)+(0.5)(1.5)+(1.5)(-0.5)}{4} = \frac{-0.75+0.75+0.75-0.75}{4} = \frac{0}{4} = 0$$

$$\hat{\gamma}_{yz}(0) = \frac{(-2.5)(0.5)+(-0.5)(-1.5)+(0.5)(1.5)+(2.5)(-0.5)}{4} = \frac{-1.25+0.75+0.75-1.25}{4} = \frac{-1.0}{4} = -0.25$$

**Step 5 — Cross-Correlations at Lag 0:**

$$\hat{\rho}_{xy}(0) = \frac{2.0}{\sqrt{1.25 \times 3.25}} = \frac{2.0}{\sqrt{4.0625}} = \frac{2.0}{2.0156} = 0.9923$$

$$\hat{\rho}_{xz}(0) = \frac{0}{\sqrt{1.25 \times 1.25}} = 0$$

$$\hat{\rho}_{yz}(0) = \frac{-0.25}{\sqrt{3.25 \times 1.25}} = \frac{-0.25}{\sqrt{4.0625}} = \frac{-0.25}{2.0156} = -0.1240$$

**Step 6 — The Cross-Correlation Matrix $\boldsymbol{\rho}(0)$:**

$$\boldsymbol{\rho}(0) = \begin{bmatrix} 1.0000 & 0.9923 & 0.0000 \\ 0.9923 & 1.0000 & -0.1240 \\ 0.0000 & -0.1240 & 1.0000 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 7 — Interpretation:**
- $\hat{\rho}_{xy}(0) = 0.9923$: $x$ and $y$ are **near-perfectly positively correlated** at lag 0 (linear trend shared).
- $\hat{\rho}_{xz}(0) = 0$: $x$ and $z$ are **uncorrelated** at lag 0.
- $\hat{\rho}_{yz}(0) = -0.1240$: $y$ and $z$ are **weakly negatively correlated** at lag 0.
- The matrix is **symmetric** (as it should be, since at $k=0$, $\rho_{ij}(0) = \rho_{ji}(0)$).

*(7 marks breakdown: 1 mark means, 1 mark deviations, 2 marks cross-covariances, 2 marks cross-correlations + matrix, 1 mark interpretation)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Mixing up $\rho_k$ and $\rho_{-k}$:** The ACF is symmetric, but the CCF is NOT. Students who report $\rho_{xy}(1) = \rho_{xy}(-1)$ automatically lose 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the normalization step:** Always divide cross-covariance by $\sqrt{\gamma_{ii}(0)\gamma_{jj}(0)}$ to get cross-correlation. A covariance value of $2.0$ is not a correlation.
> 3. **Sign errors in the anti-symmetry proof:** The substitution $s = t - k$ must be applied to BOTH indices; students often forget to substitute $t = s + k$ in the second factor.
> 4. **Confusing $T$ vs. $T-k$ in the denominator:** Some textbooks use $\frac{1}{T-k}$ for unbiased estimation. State your convention explicitly in the exam.
> 5. **Forgetting $\rho_0 = 1$:** Diagonal of cross-correlation matrix is always 1, not 0 or the variance.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Must Memorize for KTU 2024 Exam**

- **Univariate series:** $\{x_t\}_{t=1}^{T}$ — single random variable observed over time.
- **Multivariate series:** $\{\mathbf{x}_t = (x_{1,t}, \dots, x_{k,t})'\}_{t=1}^{T}$ — $k \geq 2$ random variables observed jointly.
- **Bivariate series:** Special case of multivariate with $k=2$.
- **Mean function:** $\mu_t = E(x_t)$; **constant** under stationarity.
- **Autocovariance:** $\gamma_k = E[(x_t - \mu)(x_{t+k} - \mu)]$; **symmetric**: $\gamma_k = \gamma_{-k}$.
- **Autocorrelation (ACF):** $\rho_k = \gamma_k / \gamma_0$; satisfies $\rho_0 = 1$ and $\vert \rho_k \vert \leq 1$.
- **Cross-covariance (CCVF):** $\gamma_{ij}(k) = E[(x_{i,t} - \mu_i)(x_{j,t+k} - \mu_j)]$.
- **Cross-correlation (CCF):** $\rho_{ij}(k) = \gamma_{ij}(k) / \sqrt{\gamma_{ii}(0)\gamma_{jj}(0)}$.
- **Anti-symmetry of CCF:** $\rho_{ij}(k) = \rho_{ji}(-k)$ — fundamental property for lead-lag detection.
- **Diagonal of $\boldsymbol{\rho}(0)$:** Always 1 (auto-correlations at lag 0).
- **Off-diagonal of $\boldsymbol{\rho}(0)$:** Pairwise synchronous correlations between components.
- **Sample estimators:** Use $\bar{x}$ in place of unknown $\mu$; divide by $T$ (or $T-k$ for unbiased version).
- **Bartlett's standard error:** $SE(\hat{\rho}_k) \approx 1/\sqrt{T}$ for white noise — used to draw 95% confidence bands $\pm 1.96/\sqrt{T}$.
- **Visualization tools:** Time plot, lag plot, scatterplot matrix, ACF/PACF plots, CCF plot.
- **Practical implication:** Univariate → ARIMA family; Multivariate → VAR, VECM, VARMA families.
- **Real-world application:** Portfolio optimization (Markowitz), Value-at-Risk, lead-lag trading, macroeconomic forecasting.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Decomposition of Series
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET742 - FINANCIAL TIME SERIES ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Decomposition of Series

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Time Series Decomposition** is a fundamental analytical technique in financial time series analysis whereby an observed time series $Y_t$ is mathematically partitioned into a set of interpretable, systematic components and a residual stochastic component. Formally, it separates a series into:

1. **Trend ($T_t$)** — the long-run directional movement.
2. **Seasonal ($S_t$)** — the fixed-calendar periodic fluctuations.
3. **Cyclical ($C_t$)** — the longer-wave oscillations not tied to the calendar.
4. **Irregular ($I_t$)** — the unexplained random residual noise.

The two canonical KTU-prescribed decomposition models are:

**Additive Model:**
$$Y_t = T_t + C_t + S_t + I_t$$

**Multiplicative Model:**
$$Y_t = T_t \times C_t \times S_t \times I_t$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** When the amplitude of seasonal variation appears roughly **constant** over time → use the **additive** model. When the seasonal swing **grows proportionally** with the trend level → use the **multiplicative** model (the default in financial/economic series).

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are looking at the **monthly revenue of an ice-cream parlour** in Kerala for 3 years. You will notice:

- The overall revenue is **creeping upward** year after year (this is the **trend** — perhaps due to brand popularity).
- Every **March–May**, revenue **spikes sharply** (this is **seasonality** — summer demand).
- Every **2–3 years** there is a slight macro wave coinciding with tourism cycles (this is the **cyclical** component).
- A sudden dip in one particular July due to an unexpected heavy rain (**irregular** component).

**Decomposition is the accountant's job of "unbundling" this total revenue into these four distinct explanatory streams** so that the manager can plan inventory, staffing, and cash flow independently for each driver.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Irregular Component is the "honest surprise"** — it is what remains after the systematic, explainable movements have been extracted. In finance, this is often called the *innovation* or *shock* and is of central interest to risk analysts.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Notation

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Value Range |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $m$ | Number of periods per season | 4 (quarterly), 12 (monthly) |
| $k$ | Order of moving average | Equals $m$ (centered) |
| $SI$ | Seasonal Index | 100 (multiplicative) or 0 (additive) |
| $D_t$ | Deseasonalised / Detrended series | — |
| $\alpha$ | Smoothing constant (optional) | $0 \le \alpha \le 1$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Components — A Structured Breakdown

### (A) Trend Component ($T_t$)
- **What:** The smooth, long-term progression of the series reflecting the underlying growth or decline.
- **Why it matters:** Investors and policy-makers use the trend to gauge the "true" direction stripped of short-term noise.
- **How to extract:** Centered moving averages of order $m$, or fitting a regression line $T_t = a + bt$.

### (B) Seasonal Component ($S_t$)
- **What:** A pattern that repeats every $m$ observations (e.g., every 12 months).
- **Why it matters:** Critical for forecasting — without seasonal adjustment, naive forecasts overshoot in peak months and undershoot in trough months.
- **How to extract:** Ratio-to-moving-average (multiplicative) or difference-from-moving-average (additive), followed by averaging each season.

### (C) Cyclical Component ($C_t$)
- **What:** Wavelike movements of duration longer than one season, often linked to business/economic cycles.
- **Why it matters:** Distinguishes long-term expansion/recession from seasonal wiggles.
- **How to extract:** By default, it is **bundled with trend** as the **trend-cycle** $TC_t$ because the two cannot be separated in real time.

### (D) Irregular Component ($I_t$)
- **What:** Pure randomness, with mean $\approx 0$ (additive) or $\approx 1$ (multiplicative) and no autocorrelation (ideally).
- **Why it matters:** Quantifies the unexplained variability — the basis of risk/Volatility measurement in finance.
- **How to extract:** Residuals after $T$, $C$, and $S$ are removed.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **exam-ready reference** for solving any decomposition problem. Memorise it.

| # | Concept | Formula | Model Type |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Decomposition form | $Y_t = T_t + C_t + S_t + I_t$ | Additive |
| 2 | Decomposition form | $Y_t = T_t \times C_t \times S_t \times I_t$ | Multiplicative |
| 3 | Centered Moving Average (CMA) | $CMA_t = \dfrac{\frac{1}{2}Y_{t-\frac{m}{2}} + Y_{t-\frac{m}{2}+1} + \dots + Y_{t+\frac{m}{2}-1} + \frac{1}{2}Y_{t+\frac{m}{2}}}{m}$ | Both |
| 4 | Detrended (additive) | $Y_t - T_t$ | Additive |
| 5 | Detrended (multiplicative) | $Y_t \div T_t$ | Multiplicative |
| 6 | Seasonal-Irregular ratio | $SI_t = \dfrac{Y_t}{CMA_t} \times 100$ | Multiplicative |
| 7 | Seasonal-Irregular diff. | $SI_t = Y_t - CMA_t$ | Additive |
| 8 | Seasonal Index (raw) | Average of $SI_t$ for each season $j$ | Both |
| 9 | Normalised Index | Adjusted $SI$ such that $\sum SI_j = m \times 100$ (mult.) or $\sum SI_j = 0$ (add.) | Both |
| 10 | Deseasonalised series | $D_t = \dfrac{Y_t}{SI_j} \times 100$ | Multiplicative |
| 11 | Deseasonalised series | $D_t = Y_t - SI_j$ | Additive |
| 12 | Irregular component | $I_t = D_t - T_t$ (add.) or $I_t = D_t \div T_t$ (mult.) | Both |
| 13 | Linear trend | $T_t = a + b t$ | — |
| 14 | Slope estimate | $b = \dfrac{n\sum tY_t - \sum t \sum Y_t}{n\sum t^2 - (\sum t)^2}$ | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary conditions to remember:**
> - In a **multiplicative** model, a perfect seasonal index is **100**. An index of **120** means 20% above average; an index of **85** means 15% below average.
> - In an **additive** model, the seasonal index is centred on **0**.
> - Always **normalise** the indices so they average out to the right baseline (100 or 0).

---

## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering & Finance

- **Algorithmic trading:** Removing seasonality before fitting GARCH volatility models.
- **Insurance:** Decomposing claim series to price risk correctly per season.
- **Macroeconomic forecasting:** RBI and central banks seasonally adjust GDP/inflation series before policy decisions.
- **Retail & Supply Chain:** Inventory and staffing decisions driven by deseasonalised demand.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Centered Moving Average (CMA)

We want an average of $m$ consecutive observations that is **aligned with the central period** rather than straddling two periods. A simple MA of $m$ terms places the average at $t + \frac{m-1}{2}$, which is non-integer when $m$ is even. The solution is the **centered moving average**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
CMA_t &= \frac{1}{m} \left[ \frac{1}{2}Y_{t-\frac{m}{2}} + Y_{t-\frac{m}{2}+1} + Y_{t-\frac{m}{2}+2} + \dots \right. \\
&\quad \left. + Y_{t+\frac{m}{2}-1} + \frac{1}{2}Y_{t+\frac{m}{2}} \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Reasoning for the half-weights:**
- Take a 2-term MA: $MA^{(2)}_t = \frac{1}{2}(Y_{t-1} + Y_t)$, centred at $t - 0.5$.
- Apply it again: $MA^{(2 \text{ of } 2)}_t = \frac{1}{2}\left( \frac{Y_{t-2}+Y_{t-1}}{2} + \frac{Y_{t-1}+Y_t}{2} \right) = \frac{1}{4}Y_{t-2} + \frac{1}{2}Y_{t-1} + \frac{1}{4}Y_t$.
- This is a **4-term weighted centred MA**. The end-points receive $\frac{1}{4}$ and the middle two receive $\frac{1}{2}$, giving a 4-term symmetric filter.

---

## 3.2 Detailed Algorithm: Multiplicative Decomposition

The standard KTU-style procedure for multiplicative decomposition is the **ratio-to-moving-average method**. The full derivation/algorithm is given below.

> [!NOTE]
> The following 6 steps are the **textbook procedure** that examiners expect to see written out for full marks in a 14-mark question.

### **Step 1 — Compute the Trend-Cycle ($TC_t$) using a 4-period (or 12-period) CMA.**
For a quarterly series ($m = 4$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{4-term MA at } t &= \frac{Y_{t-2} + Y_{t-1} + Y_t + Y_{t+1}}{4} \\
\text{2-term MA of above} &= \frac{1}{2}\left[ \frac{Y_{t-2}+Y_{t-1}+Y_t+Y_{t+1}}{4} + \frac{Y_{t-1}+Y_t+Y_{t+1}+Y_{t+2}}{4} \right] \\
&= \frac{Y_{t-2} + 2Y_{t-1} + 2Y_t + 2Y_{t+1} + Y_{t+2}}{8} \\
&= CMA_t
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **standard 4-period CMA** used for quarterly financial data.

### **Step 2 — Detrend by Division (because multiplicative).**
For each $t$ where $CMA_t$ is defined, compute:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{Y_t}{CMA_t} \times 100 = (S \times I)_t
\end{aligned}
$$

The result is a percentage that captures the combined **Seasonal × Irregular** effect.

### **Step 3 — Strip the Irregular Component by Averaging.**
Group all $(S \times I)_t$ values by their season (Q1, Q2, Q3, Q4) and compute the arithmetic mean within each group:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\overline{SI}_j = \frac{1}{n_j}\sum_{t \in \text{season } j} (S \times I)_t
\end{aligned}
$$

where $n_j$ is the number of years of data for season $j$.

### **Step 4 — Normalise the Seasonal Indices.**
The sum of raw seasonal indices must equal $m \times 100$. Compute the correction factor:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Adjustment Factor (AF)} = \frac{m \times 100}{\sum_{j=1}^{m} \overline{SI}_j}
\end{aligned}
$$

Then the final seasonal index for season $j$ is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
SI_j = \overline{SI}_j \times \text{AF}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification:** $\sum_{j=1}^{m} SI_j = m \times 100$ — this acts as a built-in check.

### **Step 5 — Deseasonalise the Original Series.**
For each observation belonging to season $j$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_t = \frac{Y_t}{SI_j} \times 100
\end{aligned}
$$

$D_t$ now contains only $T_t \times C_t \times I_t$.

### **Step 6 — Extract Trend and Irregular.**
Fit a linear (or polynomial) regression to $D_t$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{T}_t = \hat{a} + \hat{b} \, t
\end{aligned}
$$

The final irregular component is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_t = \frac{D_t}{\hat{T}_t} = \frac{Y_t}{\hat{T}_t \times SI_j} \times 100
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Logical check:** Substitute back, and you must recover $Y_t \approx \hat{T}_t \times SI_j \times I_t$. Any deviation is the estimation error of the decomposition.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Demonstration (Quarterly Data, $m = 4$)

Consider the following 3-year quarterly revenue (₹ in lakhs): **20, 30, 40, 50, 24, 36, 48, 60, 28, 42, 56, 70**.

| Quarter | $Y_t$ | 4-MA | CMA | $Y_t/$CMA × 100 |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Q1 Y1 | 20 | — | — | — |
| Q2 Y1 | 30 | 35.0 | 35.0 | 85.7 |
| Q3 Y1 | 40 | 36.0 | 37.0 | 108.1 |
| Q4 Y1 | 50 | 37.0 | 39.0 | 128.2 |
| Q1 Y2 | 24 | 39.5 | 40.75 | 58.9 |
| Q2 Y2 | 36 | 42.0 | 43.0 | 83.7 |
| Q3 Y2 | 48 | 44.0 | 45.5 | 105.5 |
| Q4 Y2 | 60 | 46.5 | 47.75 | 125.7 |
| Q1 Y3 | 28 | 49.0 | 50.25 | 55.7 |
| Q2 Y3 | 42 | 52.0 | 53.0 | 79.2 |
| Q3 Y3 | 56 | 54.5 | 55.75 | 100.4 |
| Q4 Y3 | 70 | — | — | — |

**Seasonal-Irregular averages per quarter (Q1, Q2, Q3, Q4):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\overline{SI}_{Q1} &= \frac{58.9 + 55.7}{2} = 57.3 \\
\overline{SI}_{Q2} &= \frac{85.7 + 83.7 + 79.2}{3} = 82.87 \\
\overline{SI}_{Q3} &= \frac{108.1 + 105.5 + 100.4}{3} = 104.67 \\
\overline{SI}_{Q4} &= \frac{128.2 + 125.7}{2} = 126.95
\end{aligned}
$$

**Sum** $= 57.3 + 82.87 + 104.67 + 126.95 = 371.79$. We need $4 \times 100 = 400$.

**Adjustment Factor:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
AF = \frac{400}{371.79} = 1.0759
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Normalised Seasonal Indices:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
SI_{Q1} &= 57.30 \times 1.0759 = 61.64 \\
SI_{Q2} &= 82.87 \times 1.0759 = 89.16 \\
SI_{Q3} &= 104.67 \times 1.0759 = 112.61 \\
SI_{Q4} &= 126.95 \times 1.0759 = 136.58
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sanity check:** $61.64 + 89.16 + 112.61 + 136.58 = 399.99 \approx 400$. ✓ Interpretation: Q1 is ~38% **below** average, Q4 is ~37% **above** average — consistent with year-end financial seasonality.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of Classical Decomposition

The following block diagram shows the data flow when applying the **multiplicative ratio-to-moving-average method**.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Observed Series Yt] --> B[Step 1 Compute Centered Moving Average CMA]
    B --> C[Trend Cycle Component TCt approx CMA]
    A --> D[Step 2 Compute Yt divide CMA times 100]
    D --> E[Seasonal Irregular Ratio SIt]
    E --> F[Step 3 Group by Season and Average]
    F --> G[Raw Seasonal Indices SI bar j]
    G --> H[Step 4 Normalise to sum equals m times 100]
    H --> I[Final Seasonal Indices SI j]
    A --> J[Step 5 Deseasonalise Dt equals Yt divide SI j times 100]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Step 6 Fit Trend T hat t equals a plus b t]
    K --> L[Final Trend T hat t]
    J --> M[Step 7 Irregular It equals Dt divide T hat t]
    L --> M
    M --> N[Reconstruction Y hat t equals T hat t times SI j times It]
```

---

## 4.2 Decision Tree: Choosing the Decomposition Model

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Examine Series Yt] --> Q{Does seasonal amplitude grow with trend level?}
    Q -- No Constant swing --> R[Use ADDITIVE Model Yt equals Tt plus St plus Ct plus It]
    Q -- Yes Proportional swing --> S[Use MULTIPLICATIVE Model Yt equals Tt times St times Ct times It]
    R --> R1[Use Difference Method Yt minus CMA for SI]
    S --> S1[Use Ratio Method Yt divided by CMA times 100 for SI]
    R1 --> X[Proceed to Trend Extraction]
    S1 --> X
    X --> Y[Fit Linear or Polynomial Trend]
    Y --> Z[Compute Irregular Residual]
```

---

## 4.3 Component Identification Schematic

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph OBS[Observed Series Yt]
    YF[Yt]
    end
    subgraph SYS[Systematic Components]
    T1[Trend Tt - Long run direction]
    S1[Seasonal St - Fixed period pattern]
    C1[Cyclical Ct - Long wave oscillation]
    end
    subgraph RES[Residual]
    I1[Irregular It - Pure random shock]
    end
    YF -- equals --> T1
    YF -- plus or times --> S1
    YF -- plus or times --> C1
    YF -- plus or times --> I1
    T1 --> OUT1[Tt estimated by CMA or regression]
    S1 --> OUT2[St estimated by ratio to MA]
    C1 --> OUT3[Ct combined with Tt as trend cycle]
    I1 --> OUT4[It estimated as final residual]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Define a time series. Explain the classical **multiplicative model** of decomposition with its components.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A time series is a sequence of observations recorded at successive, equally spaced time intervals. The classical multiplicative model expresses the observed series as the **product** of four components:

$$
Y_t = T_t \times C_t \times S_t \times I_t
$$

where $T_t$ is the **trend** (long-run direction), $C_t$ is the **cyclical** component (long-wave oscillations), $S_t$ is the **seasonal** component (fixed-period calendar pattern) and $I_t$ is the **irregular** random component. This model is preferred when seasonal variation **grows proportionally** with the level of the series, which is typical of most financial and economic data. **(3 marks)**

---

### **Question 2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
What is a **centered moving average (CMA)**? Why are half-weights used at the end points of a 4-quarter CMA?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A centered moving average is a symmetric moving average that aligns the average with the **middle observation** of the window. For an even-period window (e.g., 4 quarters), a simple MA falls between two periods, so we apply a 2-term MA on a 4-term MA, producing the formula:

$$
CMA_t = \frac{Y_{t-2} + 2Y_{t-1} + 2Y_t + 2Y_{t+1} + Y_{t+2}}{8}
$$

Half-weights of $\frac{1}{8}$ appear at the end-points ($Y_{t-2}$ and $Y_{t+2}$) and full weights of $\frac{2}{8}$ at the inner points, ensuring that the resulting average is **symmetric and centered** on time $t$. This is essential so that the CMA represents the **true trend-cycle** without phase lag. **(3 marks)**

---

## 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, a single Part B question is given with **internal choice** between **Question A** and **Question B**. The student attempts one. Each is split into sub-parts of **7 marks + 7 marks**.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** State and explain the **classical additive model** of time series decomposition. Differentiate it from the multiplicative model. Discuss the conditions under which each is appropriate. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The quarterly sales (in ₹ lakhs) of a company for 3 years are:

| Year | Q1 | Q2 | Q3 | Q4 |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Y1 | 30 | 40 | 50 | 60 |
| Y2 | 35 | 48 | 60 | 72 |
| Y3 | 40 | 55 | 70 | 84 |

Apply the **ratio-to-moving-average method** to obtain the seasonal indices (multiplicative model). Comment on the seasonal pattern. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution

**(a) Classical Additive Model (7 marks):**

The classical additive model expresses the observed series as the **sum** of its four components:

$$
Y_t = T_t + C_t + S_t + I_t
$$

- **Trend ($T_t$):** Smooth long-term movement. Extracted by centered MA or regression.
- **Cyclical ($C_t$):** Wavelike movement of period > 1 year, due to business/economic cycles.
- **Seasonal ($S_t$):** Repeats every $m$ periods (e.g., every 4 quarters).
- **Irregular ($I_t$):** Random, unpredictable variation, mean ≈ 0.

**Additive vs Multiplicative (differentiation):**

| Feature | Additive | Multiplicative |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Form | Sum of components | Product of components |
| Seasonal swing | Constant over time | Varies with level of series |
| Seasonal index | Centred at 0 | Centred at 100 |
| Use case | Linear series, stable variance | Financial/economic series, growing variance |

**Condition for choosing:** Use **additive** when the amplitude of seasonal variation is roughly constant regardless of the trend level. Use **multiplicative** when the seasonal amplitude grows in proportion to the trend (typical of revenue, GDP, stock price-volume data). **(7 marks)**

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — 4-period MA:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
MA^{(4)}_{Q2,Y1} &= \frac{30+40+50+60}{4} = 45.0 \\
MA^{(4)}_{Q3,Y1} &= \frac{40+50+60+35}{4} = 46.25 \\
MA^{(4)}_{Q4,Y1} &= \frac{50+60+35+48}{4} = 48.25 \\
MA^{(4)}_{Q1,Y2} &= \frac{60+35+48+60}{4} = 50.75 \\
MA^{(4)}_{Q2,Y2} &= \frac{35+48+60+72}{4} = 53.75 \\
MA^{(4)}_{Q3,Y2} &= \frac{48+60+72+40}{4} = 55.00 \\
MA^{(4)}_{Q4,Y2} &= \frac{60+72+40+55}{4} = 56.75 \\
MA^{(4)}_{Q1,Y3} &= \frac{72+40+55+70}{4} = 59.25 \\
MA^{(4)}_{Q2,Y3} &= \frac{40+55+70+84}{4} = 62.25 \\
MA^{(4)}_{Q3,Y3} &= \frac{55+70+84}{3} \text{ (boundary, ignored)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Centered 4-MA (average of consecutive 4-MAs):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CMA_{Q3,Y1} &= \frac{45.0 + 46.25}{2} = 45.625 \\
CMA_{Q4,Y1} &= \frac{46.25 + 48.25}{2} = 47.25 \\
CMA_{Q1,Y2} &= \frac{48.25 + 50.75}{2} = 49.50 \\
CMA_{Q2,Y2} &= \frac{50.75 + 53.75}{2} = 52.25 \\
CMA_{Q3,Y2} &= \frac{53.75 + 55.00}{2} = 54.375 \\
CMA_{Q4,Y2} &= \frac{55.00 + 56.75}{2} = 55.875 \\
CMA_{Q1,Y3} &= \frac{56.75 + 59.25}{2} = 58.00 \\
CMA_{Q2,Y3} &= \frac{59.25 + 62.25}{2} = 60.75
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $Y_t / CMA_t \times 100$:**

| Period | $Y_t$ | $CMA_t$ | Ratio ×100 |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Q3 Y1 | 50 | 45.625 | 109.59 |
| Q4 Y1 | 60 | 47.25 | 126.98 |
| Q1 Y2 | 35 | 49.50 | 70.71 |
| Q2 Y2 | 48 | 52.25 | 91.87 |
| Q3 Y2 | 60 | 54.375 | 110.34 |
| Q4 Y2 | 72 | 55.875 | 128.86 |
| Q1 Y3 | 40 | 58.00 | 68.97 |
| Q2 Y3 | 55 | 60.75 | 90.53 |

**Step 4 — Average within each season:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\overline{SI}_{Q1} &= \frac{70.71 + 68.97}{2} = 69.84 \\
\overline{SI}_{Q2} &= \frac{91.87 + 90.53}{2} = 91.20 \\
\overline{SI}_{Q3} &= \frac{109.59 + 110.34}{2} = 109.97 \\
\overline{SI}_{Q4} &= \frac{126.98 + 128.86}{2} = 127.92
\end{aligned}
$$

**Sum** $= 69.84 + 91.20 + 109.97 + 127.92 = 398.93$. Need $4 \times 100 = 400$.

**Adjustment factor:**

$$
