AF = \frac{400}{398.93} = 1.00268
$$

**Final normalised indices:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
SI_{Q1} &= 69.84 \times 1.00268 = 70.03 \\
SI_{Q2} &= 91.20 \times 1.00268 = 91.44 \\
SI_{Q3} &= 109.97 \times 1.00268 = 110.27 \\
SI_{Q4} &= 127.92 \times 1.00268 = 128.26
\end{aligned}
$$

**Check:** $70.03 + 91.44 + 110.27 + 128.26 = 400.00$ ✓

**Comment (1 mark within the 7):** Q4 sales are ~28% above average (year-end peak), Q1 sales are ~30% below average (post-holiday trough). The pattern is highly seasonal with strong year-end effect. **(7 marks)**

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** What are the **limitations of the classical decomposition model**? How does the modern **X-12-ARIMA / STL approach** overcome them? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a series with linear trend $T_t = 100 + 2t$ and additive seasonal indices $S_1 = -10, S_2 = 5, S_3 = 8, S_4 = -3$, compute the **forecast for the next 4 quarters** assuming the irregular component $I_t = 0$. Take the last observed $t = 16$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution

**(a) Limitations & Modern Methods (7 marks):**

**Limitations of Classical Decomposition:**
1. Assumes a **fixed seasonal pattern** that does not evolve over time.
2. Cannot handle **calendar effects** (e.g., varying number of working days per month).
3. Loses the **first and last $\frac{m}{2}$ observations** due to the moving average (boundary loss).
4. Treats trend, cycle, seasonal and irregular as **purely deterministic** — ignores stochastic dynamics.
5. Inappropriate for series with **evolving volatility** or structural breaks.

**Modern methods (X-12-ARIMA / STL):**
- **X-12-ARIMA:** Uses ARIMA models to **extend the series** at both ends, eliminating boundary loss; handles trading-day effects; produces both multiplicative and additive decompositions.
- **STL (Seasonal-Trend decomposition using LOESS):** Uses **local regression (LOESS)** to extract smooth trend and seasonal components; allows the seasonal pattern to **change over time**; robust to outliers.

**Conclusion:** Modern methods are flexible, robust and better suited to financial time series with evolving behaviour. **(7 marks)**

---

**(b) Forecast Computation (7 marks):**

Given additive model $Y_t = T_t + S_t + I_t$ and $I_t = 0$:

$$
Y_t = T_t + S_t = (100 + 2t) + S_t
$$

For $t = 16$, the next quarters are $t = 17, 18, 19, 20$. The seasonal index for these quarters depends on their position in the cycle. Assuming $t=16$ corresponds to Q4 (so $t=17$ = Q1, $t=18$ = Q2, $t=19$ = Q3, $t=20$ = Q4):

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y_{17} &= (100 + 2(17)) + S_1 = 134 + (-10) = 124 \\
Y_{18} &= (100 + 2(18)) + S_2 = 136 + 5 = 141 \\
Y_{19} &= (100 + 2(19)) + S_3 = 138 + 8 = 146 \\
Y_{20} &= (100 + 2(20)) + S_4 = 140 + (-3) = 137
\end{aligned}
$$

**Forecasts (₹ units): Q1 = 124, Q2 = 141, Q3 = 146, Q4 = 137.** **(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to normalise the seasonal indices.** Students often report raw averages without adjusting them to sum to $m \times 100$. KTU examiners deduct **2 marks** for this.
> 2. **Mixing additive and multiplicative steps.** If you begin with division, the entire chain (detrend, deseasonalise, irregular extraction) must use division, not subtraction.
> 3. **Boundary loss ignored.** Always state explicitly that the first and last $\frac{m}{2}$ CMA values cannot be computed.
> 4. **Wrong assignment of seasonal index to forecast period.** Map the future period correctly to its season before applying $S_j$.
> 5. **Computational slip in the adjustment factor.** A single arithmetic error in $AF$ cascades and loses 2–3 marks. Always re-verify $\sum SI_j = m \times 100$.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Read this 30 minutes before the exam.**

- **Two model forms:** $Y_t = T_t + C_t + S_t + I_t$ (additive) **vs** $Y_t = T_t \times C_t \times S_t \times I_t$ (multiplicative).
- **Choose multiplicative** when seasonal amplitude grows with the level of the series.
- **Centered Moving Average (CMA):** symmetric filter used to estimate $T_t + C_t$.
- For **even** $m$ (e.g., 4 or 12), the CMA has **half-weights** at the end-points.
- **Ratio-to-MA** (multiplicative) ⇒ $SI_t = \frac{Y_t}{CMA_t} \times 100$.
- **Difference-from-MA** (additive) ⇒ $SI_t = Y_t - CMA_t$.
- **Normalisation rule:** Multiplicative indices must sum to $m \times 100$; additive indices must sum to $0$.
- **Deseasonalisation:** $D_t = \frac{Y_t}{SI_j} \times 100$ (mult.) or $D_t = Y_t - SI_j$ (add.).
- **Irregular** is the **last** component extracted — it is whatever remains.
- **Boundary loss:** First and last $\frac{m}{2}$ observations have no CMA ⇒ cannot extract $SI$ for them.
- **Modern alternative:** STL / X-12-ARIMA overcome classical limitations.
- **Reconstruction check:** $\hat{Y}_t = \hat{T}_t \times SI_j \times I_t$ (mult.) must closely match $Y_t$.
- **Exam trap:** Always mention the **assumptions** of the model you apply.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The Spectral Density
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET742 - FINANCIAL TIME SERIES ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** The Spectral Density

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Spectral Density (Power Spectral Density Function)**
> For a **second-order stationary** time series $\{X_t\}$ with autocovariance function $\gamma(k)$, the **spectral density** $S(\omega)$ is defined as the **Fourier transform of the autocovariance function**:
> $$S(\omega) = \frac{1}{2\pi} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \gamma(k) \, e^{-i\omega k}, \quad \omega \in [-\pi, \pi]$$
> The corresponding inverse relationship (Wiener–Khinchin theorem) is:
> $$\gamma(k) = \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} S(\omega) \, e^{i\omega k} \, d\omega$$

The frequency $\omega$ is measured in **radians per unit time** and ranges over $[-\pi, \pi]$ for a discretely sampled series. The term **"spectral"** refers to the fact that $S(\omega)$ distributes the total variance of the process across the frequency spectrum, just as a prism distributes white light into its colour components.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping (CO1):** Understand the foundational time-domain and frequency-domain representations of financial time series, including the spectral density and its properties.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy — The Graphic Equalizer

Imagine you are listening to a song on a music player. The time-domain view is the **waveform** (loudness vs. time). But what you *hear* and what DJs manipulate is the **graphic equalizer** — bars showing how much **bass, mid, or treble** energy exists. The **spectral density** of a time series is exactly that equalizer:

- **Bass (low $\omega$ near $0$):** Long-run trends and business cycles in the financial series.
- **Treble (high $\omega$ near $\pi$):** Short-term, high-frequency noise and rapid reversals.
- **A peak at $\omega = 0$:** Indicates strong persistence / non-stationarity tendency.
- **A flat spectrum:** Pure white noise (no memory).

By analysing $S(\omega)$ we are essentially asking: **"At which cycle length does the market exhibit the strongest rhythmic behaviour?"** — a question impossible to answer in the time domain alone.

### 1.3 Existence Conditions

For the spectral density to be well-defined, the autocovariance sequence $\{\gamma(k)\}$ must be **absolutely summable**:

$$\sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert \gamma(k) \vert < \infty$$

This is guaranteed for **causal invertible ARMA processes**. It fails for non-stationary processes (e.g., random walk), for which we instead work with the *spectral distribution function*.

### 1.4 Key Engineering Interpretation

In financial engineering, $S(\omega)$ is used to:
- Detect **periodicities** in returns (e.g., day-of-week effects).
- Build **frequency-domain filters** to smooth or detrend prices.
- Model **volatility clustering** via long-memory processes.
- Design **optimal portfolios** using spectral risk measures.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why is the Spectral Density Useful?

The autocovariance $\gamma(k)$ and the spectral density $S(\omega)$ are **two equivalent representations** of the second-order structure of a stationary process:

| Property | Autocovariance $\gamma(k)$ | Spectral Density $S(\omega)$ |
|----------|--------------------------|------------------------------|
| **Domain** | Time (lag $k$) | Frequency ($\omega$) |
| **Captures** | Memory at lag $k$ | Energy at cycle $\omega$ |
| **Best for** | ARMA model identification | Cycle detection, filtering |

The Fourier transform exchanges the time-domain memory structure for the frequency-domain energy distribution, while preserving **all second-order information** of the process.

### 2.2 Operational Logic — How the Spectral Density Works

1. **Step 1 — Start with a stationary process:** Assume $\{X_t\}$ is weakly stationary so that $\gamma(k)$ is well-defined and depends only on the lag $k$.
2. **Step 2 — Check absolute summability:** Verify that $\sum \vert \gamma(k) \vert < \infty$ to guarantee convergence of the Fourier series.
3. **Step 3 — Apply the Fourier transform:** Map the discrete sequence $\gamma(k)$ onto the continuous frequency function $S(\omega)$.
4. **Step 4 — Interpret the spectrum:** Read off dominant cycles, trend strength, and high-frequency noise.
5. **Step 5 — Invert (if needed):** Use the inverse Fourier integral to recover $\gamma(k)$ — useful for fitting ARMA models in the frequency domain.

### 2.3 Fundamental Properties of $S(\omega)$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Five Canonical Properties of the Spectral Density**
> 1. **Real-valued:** $S(\omega) \in \mathbb{R}$ for all $\omega$, even though defined via a complex exponential.
> 2. **Even function:** $S(-\omega) = S(\omega)$ — the spectrum is symmetric about zero.
> 3. **Non-negative:** $S(\omega) \geq 0$ for all $\omega$ (it represents power, which is non-negative).
> 4. **Variance identity:** $\gamma(0) = \text{Var}(X_t) = \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} S(\omega) \, d\omega$.
> 5. **Integrability:** $\int_{-\pi}^{\pi} S(\omega) \, d\omega < \infty$ for short-memory processes.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following **Cheat Sheet** is the most-tested set of equations for Module 1 spectral density questions.

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Spectral Density Definition | $S(\omega) = \dfrac{1}{2\pi} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \gamma(k) e^{-i\omega k}$ | $\omega \in [-\pi, \pi]$ |
| 2 | Inverse Transform | $\gamma(k) = \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} S(\omega) e^{i\omega k} d\omega$ | $k \in \mathbb{Z}$ |
| 3 | Cosine Real Form | $S(\omega) = \dfrac{1}{2\pi} \left[ \gamma(0) + 2 \sum_{k=1}^{\infty} \gamma(k) \cos(\omega k) \right]$ | Real & even form |
| 4 | Variance Integral | $\gamma(0) = \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} S(\omega) d\omega$ | Total power = area under spectrum |
| 5 | White Noise Spectrum | $S_{\text{WN}}(\omega) = \dfrac{\sigma^2}{2\pi}$ | **Flat** — equal power at all frequencies |
| 6 | AR(1) Spectrum | $S(\omega) = \dfrac{\sigma^2}{2\pi \left( 1 - 2\phi \cos \omega + \phi^2 \right)}$ | $\vert \phi \vert < 1$ |
| 7 | MA(1) Spectrum | $S(\omega) = \dfrac{\sigma^2 \left( 1 + 2\theta \cos \omega + \theta^2 \right)}{2\pi}$ | Invertible for $\vert \theta \vert < 1$ |
| 8 | Periodogram | $I(\omega_j) = \dfrac{1}{n} \left\vert \sum_{t=1}^{n} (X_t - \bar X) e^{-i\omega_j t} \right\vert^2$ | Sample-based estimator |
| 9 | Inversion Identity | $\sum_{k=0}^{\infty} r^k \cos(k\omega) = \dfrac{1 - r \cos \omega}{1 - 2r \cos \omega + r^2}$ | Used to derive AR/MA spectra |
| 10 | Parseval's Relation | $\int_{-\pi}^{\pi} S_X(\omega) d\omega = \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} S_Y(\omega) \mid H(\omega) \mid^2 d\omega$ | For filtered series $Y_t = h(L)X_t$ |

### 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Electrical Engineering:** Designing low-pass / high-pass filters for signals (LTI system theory uses exactly the same mathematics).
- **Financial Engineering:** Building spectral-based estimators of integrated variance for high-frequency data.
- **Quantitative Finance:** Estimating the spectral risk measure $\mathcal{M}_{\phi} = \int \phi(S(\omega)) d\omega$ where $\phi$ is a risk-aversion weighting function.
- **Econometrics:** Spectral regression (Engle 1974) for testing seasonality in macroeconomic time series.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All derivations below follow the **rigorous KTU-2024 scheme valuation key** style. Each algebraic step is shown explicitly. No step is skipped.

---

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Spectral Density of White Noise

**Given:** $\{X_t\} \sim \text{WN}(0, \sigma^2)$, with autocovariance

$$\gamma(k) = \begin{cases} \sigma^2, & k = 0 \\ 0, & k \neq 0 \end{cases}$$

**Step 1 — Substitute into the definition.**

By definition:
$$S(\omega) = \frac{1}{2\pi} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \gamma(k) e^{-i\omega k}$$

**Step 2 — Observe that only $k=0$ survives the sum.**

Since $\gamma(k) = 0$ for $k \neq 0$, the infinite series collapses to a single term:
$$S(\omega) = \frac{1}{2\pi} \cdot \gamma(0) \cdot e^{-i\omega \cdot 0} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \cdot \sigma^2 \cdot 1$$

**Step 3 — Conclude.**

$$\boxed{S_{\text{WN}}(\omega) = \frac{\sigma^2}{2\pi}, \quad \forall \omega \in [-\pi, \pi]}$$

**Interpretation:** The white noise spectrum is **constant** at $\sigma^2/(2\pi)$. There is no frequency preference — every cycle length contributes equally to the variance. This is the frequency-domain signature of "no memory".

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Spectral Density of an AR(1) Process

**Given:** $X_t = \phi X_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t$ with $\varepsilon_t \sim \text{WN}(0, \sigma^2)$ and $\vert \phi \vert < 1$.

The autocovariance from the Yule–Walker equations is:

$$\gamma(k) = \frac{\sigma^2 \phi^{\vert k \vert}}{1 - \phi^2}, \quad k \in \mathbb{Z}$$

**Step 1 — Write the spectral density in real (cosine) form.**

Since $S(\omega)$ is real and even, we may use the cosine representation:
$$S(\omega) = \frac{1}{2\pi} \left[ \gamma(0) + 2 \sum_{k=1}^{\infty} \gamma(k) \cos(\omega k) \right]$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $\gamma(k)$.**

$$S(\omega) = \frac{1}{2\pi(1-\phi^2)} \left[ \sigma^2 + 2 \sum_{k=1}^{\infty} \sigma^2 \phi^k \cos(\omega k) \right]$$

**Step 3 — Factor out the constants.**

$$S(\omega) = \frac{\sigma^2}{2\pi(1-\phi^2)} \left[ 1 + 2 \sum_{k=1}^{\infty} \phi^k \cos(\omega k) \right]$$

**Step 4 — Convert the tail-sum to a closed form.**

We use the standard geometric-Cosine identity:
$$\sum_{k=0}^{\infty} r^k \cos(k\omega) = \frac{1 - r \cos \omega}{1 - 2r \cos \omega + r^2}, \quad \vert r \vert < 1$$

Therefore:
$$1 + 2 \sum_{k=1}^{\infty} \phi^k \cos(\omega k) = 2 \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \phi^k \cos(\omega k) - 1$$

**Step 5 — Apply the identity with $r = \phi$.**

$$2 \cdot \frac{1 - \phi \cos \omega}{1 - 2\phi \cos \omega + \phi^2} - 1 = \frac{2(1 - \phi \cos \omega) - (1 - 2\phi \cos \omega + \phi^2)}{1 - 2\phi \cos \omega + \phi^2}$$

**Step 6 — Simplify the numerator.**

$$2 - 2\phi \cos \omega - 1 + 2\phi \cos \omega - \phi^2 = 1 - \phi^2$$

**Step 7 — Substitute back.**

$$1 + 2 \sum_{k=1}^{\infty} \phi^k \cos(\omega k) = \frac{1 - \phi^2}{1 - 2\phi \cos \omega + \phi^2}$$

**Step 8 — Insert into $S(\omega)$ and cancel $(1 - \phi^2)$.**

$$S(\omega) = \frac{\sigma^2}{2\pi(1-\phi^2)} \cdot \frac{1 - \phi^2}{1 - 2\phi \cos \omega + \phi^2}$$

$$\boxed{S_{\text{AR(1)}}(\omega) = \frac{\sigma^2}{2\pi \left( 1 - 2\phi \cos \omega + \phi^2 \right)}}$$

**Step 9 — Behaviour at boundaries:**

- As $\omega \to 0$ (long cycles): $S(0) = \dfrac{\sigma^2}{2\pi(1-\phi)^2}$ — large value if $\phi \to 1$ (near unit root).
- As $\omega \to \pi$ (alternating cycles): $S(\pi) = \dfrac{\sigma^2}{2\pi(1+\phi)^2}$ — small value if $\phi > 0$.

**Interpretation:** The AR(1) spectrum is **U-shaped** (or hump-shaped if $\phi < 0$), concentrated at low frequencies for persistent series.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Spectral Density of an MA(1) Process

**Given:** $X_t = \varepsilon_t + \theta \varepsilon_{t-1}$ with $\varepsilon_t \sim \text{WN}(0, \sigma^2)$.

The autocovariance is:

$$\gamma(0) = \sigma^2(1 + \theta^2), \quad \gamma(1) = \sigma^2 \theta, \quad \gamma(k) = 0 \text{ for } \vert k \vert > 1$$

**Step 1 — Write the cosine form.**

$$S(\omega) = \frac{1}{2\pi} \left[ \gamma(0) + 2\gamma(1)\cos \omega \right]$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $\gamma(0)$ and $\gamma(1)$.**

$$S(\omega) = \frac{1}{2\pi} \left[ \sigma^2(1+\theta^2) + 2\sigma^2 \theta \cos \omega \right]$$

**Step 3 — Factor $\sigma^2 / 2\pi$.**

$$\boxed{S_{\text{MA(1)}}(\omega) = \frac{\sigma^2 \left( 1 + 2\theta \cos \omega + \theta^2 \right)}{2\pi}}$$

**Step 4 — Note an elegant product form:**

Since $(1 + \theta e^{-i\omega})(1 + \theta e^{i\omega}) = 1 + 2\theta \cos \omega + \theta^2$:
$$S_{\text{MA(1)}}(\omega) = \frac{\sigma^2}{2\pi} \left\vert 1 + \theta e^{-i\omega} \right\vert^2$$

This is the **transfer-function** representation: $S(\omega) = \dfrac{\sigma^2}{2\pi} \vert H(\omega) \vert^2$ with $H(\omega) = 1 + \theta e^{-i\omega}$.

**Interpretation:** The MA(1) spectrum is **inverted-U-shaped** (or U-shaped if $\theta < 0$), emphasising high-frequency cycles when $\theta > 0$.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Spectra of Common Financial Models

| Model | $S(\omega)$ | Shape | Dominant Frequency |
|-------|-------------|-------|--------------------|
| White Noise | $\sigma^2 / (2\pi)$ | **Flat** | All frequencies equal |
| AR(1), $\phi > 0$ | $\sigma^2 / [2\pi(1 - 2\phi \cos \omega + \phi^2)]$ | **Monotone decreasing** | $\omega = 0$ (low) |
| AR(1), $\phi < 0$ | Same formula | **U-shaped** | $\omega = \pi$ (high) |
| MA(1), $\theta > 0$ | $\sigma^2(1 + 2\theta \cos\omega + \theta^2)/(2\pi)$ | **Monotone increasing** | $\omega = \pi$ (high) |
| MA(1), $\theta < 0$ | Same formula | **U-shaped** | $\omega = 0$ (low) |
| Random Walk | Spectral distribution only (not density) | Diverges near $\omega = 0$ | Trends dominate |

---

### 3.5 Worked Example: AR(1) Spectrum Computation

**Problem [KTU Typical]:** $X_t = 0.7 X_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t$, $\varepsilon_t \sim \text{WN}(0, 4)$. Find $S(0)$ and $S(\pi)$.

**Solution:**

Using $S(\omega) = \dfrac{\sigma^2}{2\pi(1 - 2\phi \cos \omega + \phi^2)}$ with $\phi = 0.7$ and $\sigma^2 = 4$:

**At $\omega = 0$:**
$$S(0) = \frac{4}{2\pi(1 - 2(0.7)(1) + 0.7^2)} = \frac{4}{2\pi(1 - 1.4 + 0.49)} = \frac{4}{2\pi(0.09)} = \frac{4}{0.18\pi}$$

$$S(0) = \frac{22.22}{\pi} \approx 7.07$$

**At $\omega = \pi$:**
$$S(\pi) = \frac{4}{2\pi(1 - 2(0.7)(-1) + 0.49)} = \frac{4}{2\pi(1 + 1.4 + 0.49)} = \frac{4}{2\pi(2.89)} = \frac{4}{5.78\pi}$$

$$S(\pi) = \frac{0.692}{\pi} \approx 0.22$$

**Observation:** $S(0) / S(\pi) \approx 32$ — the variance is concentrated at the lowest frequency, indicating **strong persistence** typical of equity return or volatility series.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Time-Domain ↔ Frequency-Domain Mapping

The diagram below illustrates the **dual representation** of a stationary process and the role of the spectral density as a bridge between the observed data and the cycle structure.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Observed Time Series Xt] --> nodeB[Compute Sample Mean]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Sample Autocovariance gamma hat k]
    nodeC --> nodeD{Fourier Transform}
    nodeD --> nodeE[Spectral Density S omega]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Periodogram I omega]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Smoothing Window]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Estimated Spectrum S hat omega]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Identify Dominant Cycles]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Financial Interpretation]
    nodeE --> nodeK[Inverse Fourier Transform]
    nodeK --> nodeC
    nodeA --> nodeL[Theoretical ARMA Model]
    nodeL --> nodeM[Autocovariance gamma k]
    nodeM --> nodeD
    nodeL --> nodeN[Transfer Function H omega]
    nodeN --> nodeE
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Spectral Analysis Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA[INPUT: Stationary Time Series] --> nodeB[STAGE 1: Pre-processing]
    nodeB --> nodeB1[Detrending]
    nodeB --> nodeB2[Mean Centering]
    nodeB --> nodeB3[Tapering Window]
    nodeB1 --> nodeC[STAGE 2: Autocovariance Estimation]
    nodeB2 --> nodeC
    nodeB3 --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeC1[Bias Correction]
    nodeC1 --> nodeD[STAGE 3: Frequency Transformation]
    nodeD --> nodeD1[Direct FFT]
    nodeD --> nodeD2[Cosine Series Expansion]
    nodeD1 --> nodeE[STAGE 4: Spectral Density S omega]
    nodeD2 --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF[STAGE 5: Post-Processing]
    nodeF --> nodeF1[Smoothing via Daniell Kernel]
    nodeF --> nodeF2[Bartlett Welch Averaging]
    nodeF1 --> nodeG[OUTPUT: Smoothed Spectrum]
    nodeF2 --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH[APPLICATIONS]
    nodeH --> nodeH1[Cycle Detection]
    nodeH --> nodeH2[Volatility Modelling]
    nodeH --> nodeH3[High Frequency Trading Filter]
    nodeH --> nodeH4[Risk Measure Estimation]
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Diagram: Power Distribution Across Frequencies

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[White Noise] --> nodeA1[Flat Spectrum]
    nodeA1 --> nodeA2[Equal Power at All omega]
    nodeB[AR 1 phi positive] --> nodeB1[Decreasing Spectrum]
    nodeB1 --> nodeB2[Power Concentrated at omega = 0]
    nodeC[MA 1 theta positive] --> nodeC1[Increasing Spectrum]
    nodeC1 --> nodeC2[Power Concentrated at omega = pi]
    nodeD[AR 1 phi negative] --> nodeD1[U-shaped Spectrum]
    nodeD1 --> nodeD2[Power Concentrated at omega = pi]
    nodeE[Random Walk] --> nodeE1[Divergent near omega = 0]
    nodeE1 --> nodeE2[No Spectral Density Exists]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** The "flow" arrows denote transformation steps, not data flow magnitude. Arrows in Mermaid are logical dependencies. Each `STAGE` block represents a modular sub-routine in a typical spectral analysis pipeline used in financial engineering.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper Style]**

**Q1. Define the spectral density of a stationary time series. State any three of its properties.**
**[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**
The spectral density $S(\omega)$ of a second-order stationary time series $\{X_t\}$ with autocovariance function $\gamma(k)$ is defined as the Fourier transform of $\gamma(k)$:

$$S(\omega) = \frac{1}{2\pi} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \gamma(k) e^{-i\omega k}, \quad \omega \in [-\pi, \pi]$$

**Three properties:**

1. **Real-valued:** $S(\omega) \in \mathbb{R}$ for all $\omega$.
2. **Even function:** $S(-\omega) = S(\omega)$.
3. **Non-negative:** $S(\omega) \geq 0$ for all $\omega$.

(A valid alternative third property: the variance identity $\gamma(0) = \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} S(\omega) d\omega$.)

---

**Q2. The spectral density of a white noise process $\text{WN}(0, 4)$ is \_\_\_\_\_\_\_.**
**[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**
For a white noise process with variance $\sigma^2 = 4$, the spectral density is:

$$S_{\text{WN}}(\omega) = \frac{\sigma^2}{2\pi} = \frac{4}{2\pi} = \frac{2}{\pi} \approx 0.6366$$

The spectrum is **flat** (constant) across all frequencies, indicating equal power distribution.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – End Semester Evaluation Pattern]**

---

#### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks)

**Q.A(a). State and prove the Wiener–Khinchin theorem. Show that the spectral density is a real, even, and non-negative function.**
**[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Statement (Wiener–Khinchin Theorem):**
For a real-valued second-order stationary time series $\{X_t\}$ with absolutely summable autocovariance $\gamma(k)$, the spectral density is

$$S(\omega) = \frac{1}{2\pi} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \gamma(k) e^{-i\omega k}$$

and the inverse relationship holds:

$$\gamma(k) = \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} S(\omega) e^{i\omega k} d\omega$$

**Proof of Reality:**
Note that $\gamma(-k) = \gamma(k)$ (symmetry of autocovariance). Therefore:

$$S(\omega) = \frac{1}{2\pi} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \gamma(k) e^{-i\omega k} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \gamma(-k) e^{i\omega k}$$

Substituting $j = -k$:

$$S(\omega) = \frac{1}{2\pi} \sum_{j=-\infty}^{\infty} \gamma(j) e^{-i\omega j} = S(\omega)$$

Taking the complex conjugate:

$$S(\omega)^* = \frac{1}{2\pi} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \gamma(k) e^{i\omega k} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \gamma(-k) e^{i\omega k} = S(\omega)$$

Thus $S(\omega)^* = S(\omega)$, proving **$S(\omega)$ is real**.

**Proof of Evenness:**
$$S(-\omega) = \frac{1}{2\pi} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \gamma(k) e^{i\omega k} = S(\omega)^* = S(\omega)$$

Hence $S(\omega)$ is **even**.

**Proof of Non-negativity:**
Consider the quadratic form for any finite set of complex weights $c_0, c_1, \ldots, c_n$:

$$Q = \text{Var}\left( \sum_{j=0}^{n} c_j X_{t-j} \right) = \sum_{j=0}^{n} \sum_{k=0}^{n} c_j c_k^* \gamma(j - k) \geq 0$$

Applying the spectral representation:

$$Q = \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} S(\omega) \left\vert \sum_{j=0}^{n} c_j e^{-i\omega j} \right\vert^2 d\omega \geq 0$$

Since the squared modulus is non-negative, this forces **$S(\omega) \geq 0$** almost everywhere. $\blacksquare$

---

**Q.A(b). Derive the spectral density of an AR(1) process $X_t = 0.8 X_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t$ where $\varepsilon_t \sim \text{WN}(0, 1)$. Hence find $S(0)$ and $S(\pi)$.**
**[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Write the spectral formula for AR(1).**
The general AR(1) spectrum is:
$$S(\omega) = \frac{\sigma^2}{2\pi(1 - 2\phi \cos\omega + \phi^2)}$$

with $\phi = 0.8$ and $\sigma^2 = 1$.

**Step 2 — Substitute.**
$$S(\omega) = \frac{1}{2\pi(1 - 1.6 \cos\omega + 0.64)}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $S(0)$:**

$$S(0) = \frac{1}{2\pi(1 - 1.6 + 0.64)} = \frac{1}{2\pi(0.04)} = \frac{1}{0.08\pi} = \frac{12.5}{\pi} \approx 3.98$$

**Step 4 — Compute $S(\pi)$:**

$$S(\pi) = \frac{1}{2\pi(1 - 1.6(-1) + 0.64)} = \frac{1}{2\pi(1 + 1.6 + 0.64)} = \frac{1}{2\pi(3.24)} = \frac{1}{6.48\pi} \approx 0.049$$

**Step 5 — Interpret:**
$$S(0) / S(\pi) = 3.24 / 0.04 = 81$$

The variance is concentrated at the lowest frequency, reflecting the strong persistence of the AR(1) process with $\phi = 0.8$ — analogous to a slowly-decaying equity index.

---

#### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks)

**Q.B(a). Derive the spectral density of a MA(1) process $X_t = \varepsilon_t + 0.5 \varepsilon_{t-1}$ where $\varepsilon_t \sim \text{WN}(0, 4)$. Plot its qualitative shape.**
**[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Autocovariance of MA(1).**
$$X_t = \varepsilon_t + 0.5\varepsilon_{t-1}$$

- $\gamma(0) = \text{Var}(X_t) = \sigma^2(1 + \theta^2) = 4(1 + 0.25) = 5$
- $\gamma(1) = \text{Cov}(X_t, X_{t-1}) = \sigma^2 \theta = 4(0.5) = 2$
- $\gamma(k) = 0$ for $\vert k \vert > 1$

**Step 2 — Apply spectral density formula.**

Since only $\gamma(0)$ and $\gamma(1)$ are non-zero:

$$S(\omega) = \frac{1}{2\pi}[\gamma(0) + 2\gamma(1)\cos\omega] = \frac{1}{2\pi}[5 + 4\cos\omega]$$

**Step 3 — Compute at boundary frequencies.**

$$S(0) = \frac{1}{2\pi}(5 + 4) = \frac{9}{2\pi} \approx 1.432$$

$$S(\pi) = \frac{1}{2\pi}(5 - 4) = \frac{1}{2\pi} \approx 0.159$$

**Step 4 — Qualitative shape.**
Since $\theta = 0.5 > 0$, the spectrum is **monotonically increasing** from $\omega = 0$ to $\omega = \pi$, peaking at the highest frequency. The high-frequency power is $9\times$ the low-frequency power.

**Step 5 — Transfer-function form:**

$$S(\omega) = \frac{4}{2\pi} \vert 1 + 0.5 e^{-i\omega} \vert^2$$

This confirms $S(\omega) \geq 0$ automatically.

---

**Q.B(b). Define the periodogram. State its properties as an estimator of $S(\omega)$. Why is smoothing necessary?**
**[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:**
The **periodogram** of a sample $\{X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n\}$ at the Fourier frequency $\omega_j = 2\pi j / n$ is:

$$I(\omega_j) = \frac{1}{n} \left\vert \sum_{t=1}^{n} (X_t - \bar X) e^{-i\omega_j t} \right\vert^2$$

**Properties:**

1. **Asymptotically unbiased:** $E[I(\omega_j)] \to S(\omega_j)$ as $n \to \infty$ for fixed $\omega_j$.
2. **Inconsistent:** $\text{Var}[I(\omega_j)] \to S(\omega_j)^2$ (does not vanish) — periodogram is **not** a consistent estimator.
3. **Asymptotically independent at distinct Fourier frequencies.**
4. **Always non-negative:** By construction, since it is a squared modulus.
5. **Direct FFT computation:** Computational cost $O(n \log n)$ — efficient even for high-frequency financial tick data.

**Why smoothing is necessary:**

Since the periodogram has **non-vanishing variance**, the raw periodogram is too noisy to identify dominant cycles. Smoothing (via Daniell kernel, Bartlett–Welch averaging, or tapered windows) reduces variance at the cost of introducing bias, achieving a **mean-squared-error optimal** trade-off.

**The Bias-Variance Trade-off:**

- **Wide smoothing window** → Low variance, high bias (smooth but biased spectrum).
- **Narrow smoothing window** → Low bias, high variance (spiky but noisy spectrum).

The optimal bandwidth is often chosen via cross-validation or plug-in methods.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Spectral Density Questions**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the $1/(2\pi)$ factor:** Many students drop this in $S(\omega) = \sigma^2 / (2\pi \cdot \ldots)$. **KTU deducts 1 mark** for this slip.
> 2. **Mixing conventions:** Some texts use $f(\omega)$ without the $1/(2\pi)$ factor; others use $[0, 0.5]$ (cycles) instead of $[-\pi, \pi]$ (radians). **Always state your convention explicitly at the start of the answer.**
> 3. **Confusing spectral density with periodogram:** The periodogram is an *estimator*; $S(\omega)$ is the *theoretical* function. Examiners explicitly look for this distinction.
> 4. **Applying the spectral density to non-stationary series:** The spectral density does NOT exist for a random walk. Use the spectral *distribution* function instead. **Deduct 2 marks** if asserted otherwise.
> 5. **Skipping the absolute summability check:** Always verify $\sum \vert \gamma(k) \vert < \infty$ before writing down the spectral density.
> 6. **Sign error in cosine argument:** $S(\omega) = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sum \gamma(k) e^{-i\omega k}$, NOT $e^{+i\omega k}$. Wrong sign propagates to wrong inverse formula. **Deduct 1 mark.**
> 7. **Not using the "real cosine form":** Computing the complex form and forgetting to take the real part loses the "real-valued" property proof.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — The Spectral Density (Module 1)**

- **Definition:** $S(\omega) = \dfrac{1}{2\pi} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \gamma(k) e^{-i\omega k}$, $\omega \in [-\pi, \pi]$.
- **Inverse:** $\gamma(k) = \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} S(\omega) e^{i\omega k} d\omega$.
- **Wiener–Khinchin Theorem:** Autocovariance and spectral density are Fourier pairs.
- **Existence:** Requires absolute summability of $\gamma(k)$ (fails for random walk).
- **Five properties:** Real, even, non-negative, integrable, and $\int S(\omega) d\omega = \gamma(0)$.
- **White Noise:** $S(\omega) = \sigma^2 / (2\pi)$ — constant flat spectrum.
- **AR(1):** $S(\omega) = \sigma^2 / [2\pi(1 - 2\phi \cos\omega + \phi^2)]$ — U-shaped if $\phi < 0$, decreasing if $\phi > 0$.
- **MA(1):** $S(\omega) = \sigma^2(1 + 2\theta \cos\omega + \theta^2) / (2\pi)$ — Inverted-U if $\theta > 0$.
- **Periodogram:** $I(\omega_j) = \frac{1}{n} \vert \sum (X_t - \bar X) e^{-i\omega_j t} \vert^2$ — biased but **inconsistent** estimator.
- **Smoothing:** Required to reduce periodogram variance (Bartlett, Welch, Daniell methods).
- **Transfer function form:** $S(\omega) = \dfrac{\sigma^2}{2\pi} \vert H(\omega) \vert^2$ — non-negativity is automatic.
- **Engineering link:** $S(\omega)$ for time series = Power Spectral Density in signal processing = frequency response in LTI systems.
- **Key identity to memorise:** $\sum_{k=0}^{\infty} r^k \cos(k\omega) = \dfrac{1 - r\cos\omega}{1 - 2r\cos\omega + r^2}$ — used in every AR(1) derivation.
- **Variance check:** $\int_{-\pi}^{\pi} S(\omega) d\omega = \sigma^2_X$ — total power equals total variance.
- **Random walk caveat:** No proper spectral density; only spectral distribution function (jumps at $\omega = 0$).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Smoothing and Filtering
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEIET742 - FINANCIAL TIME SERIES ANALYSIS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Smoothing and Filtering # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is Smoothing and Filtering in Financial Time Series? In the rigorous domain of **Financial Time Series Analysis**, **Smoothing** and **Filtering** constitute the foundational signal-processing and statistical techniques used to extract meaningful underlying structure (the *signal*) from a noisy, often volatile, financial price series (the *noise*). These methods are indispensable precursors to forecasting, cycle extraction, volatility modeling, and econometric inference. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal KTU Definition (2024 Scheme):** > *Smoothing* refers to a class of statistical techniques that produce a locally-averaged or trend-extracted version of a time series by assigning weights to past and present observations. *Filtering*, in the time-series context, is a linear transformation (convolution operation) that suppresses certain frequency components (e.g., high-frequency noise) while retaining others (e.g., low-frequency trends), thereby producing a filtered series $\{y_t\}$ from an input series $\{x_t\}$. ### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — "The Hazy Mountain View":** > Imagine standing at the base of a mountain on a foggy morning. The actual shape of the mountain (the **true trend** or **signal**) is obscured by rapidly moving clouds and mist (the **noise** of daily price fluctuations, micro-market frictions, and random shocks). > - **Smoothing** is like waiting and taking a long-exposure photograph — the moving clouds blur out, and the steady mountain peak emerges. > - **Filtering** is like wearing foggy-goggles with a special lens that physically blocks the high-frequency cloud movement but lets the slow-moving silhouette of the mountain pass through cleanly. > In finance, the "mountain" could be the long-term GDP growth trend, the business cycle, or the secular drift of a stock index, while the "fog" is daily/weekly volatility and market microstructure noise. ### 1.3 Why is this Topic Critical for KTU Examinations? - **Weightage:** Smoothing and filtering is a **Module 1 core topic** in PEIET742. Typical questions appear in **Part A (3 marks)** as direct definition-type questions, and frequently in **Part B (14 marks)** as full derivations of exponential smoothing, MA filters, or the Hodrick-Prescott filter, often carrying **7 marks per sub-part**. - **Bloom's Level Tested:** Primarily *Apply (L3)* and *Analyze (L4)*, with some *Evaluate (L5)* questions comparing filter properties. - **Industry Relevance:** Used by RBI, SEBI, IMF, central banks, and quantitative hedge funds for cycle extraction, GDP nowcasting, and algorithmic signal denoising. ### 1.4 Classification of Smoothing & Filtering Methods > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight — Major Families of Methods:** | Family | Examples | Primary Use | |---|---|---| | **Moving Average Filters** | Simple MA, Weighted MA, Centered MA | Trend extraction | | **Exponential Smoothing** | Simple ES, Holt's Linear, Holt-Winters | Short-term forecasting | | **Linear Filters** | Symmetric, Asymmetric, Convolution-based | Frequency-domain analysis | | **Hodrick-Prescott (HP) Filter** | HP with smoothing parameter $\lambda$ | Macroeconomic trend/cycle decomposition | | **Band-Pass Filters** | Baxter-King, Christiano-Fitzgerald | Business cycle isolation | | **Kernel/Local Polynomial Smoothing** | Nadaraya-Watson, Local Linear | Nonparametric trend estimation | ### 1.5 Standard Metrics & Constants (Used in Filters) - **Smoothing parameter ($\lambda$):** Quarterly macro data standard value **$\lambda = 1600$** (Ravn-Uhlig recommends $6.25$ for annual, $129600$ for monthly). - **Bandwidth ($h$):** Controls the degree of local averaging; smaller $h$ → less smoothing, more variance. - **Lag/Window length ($m$):** Number of past observations used in moving averages. - **Smoothing constant ($\alpha$):** In exponential smoothing, $0 < \alpha < 1$, with typical values **$\alpha \in [0.1, 0.3]$** for financial series. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Operational Logic — How Smoothing Works The underlying principle of *every* smoothing method can be expressed as a **weighted linear combination** of past (and sometimes future) observations: $$\hat{x}_t = \sum_{j=-k}^{l} w_j \, x_{t+j}$$ where: - $\hat{x}_t$ = the smoothed (filtered) value at time $t$ - $w_j$ = the weight assigned to observation $x_{t+j}$ - $k, l$ = the number of past and future observations used - The weights $\{w_j\}$ satisfy the **summation-to-unity constraint** $\sum_j w_j = 1$ (to preserve the mean level) > [!NOTE] > **Why the constraint $\sum w_j = 1$?** > If a constant (DC) signal $c$ is fed into the filter, the output should also be $c$ (a constant-in → constant-out filter). This is called the **unity-gain property** and guarantees that the filter is **unbiased for the mean**. ## 2.2 Moving Average (MA) Smoothing ### 2.2.1 Simple Moving Average (SMA) The **m-center Simple Moving Average** of order $m$ is defined as: $$\text{SMA}_t(m) = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{j=0}^{m-1} x_{t-j} = \frac{x_t + x_{t-1} + \dots + x_{t-m+1}}{m}$$ - Equal weights: $w_j = 1/m$ for $j = 0, 1, \dots, m-1$. - **Properties:** Symmetric (in centered form), unbiased for level, attenuates high frequencies. - **Drawback:** Lags the trend by $(m-1)/2$ periods; gives equal weight to old and new data. ### 2.2.2 Weighted Moving Average (WMA) Assigns unequal weights — typically larger weights to recent observations: $$\text{WMA}_t = \frac{\sum_{j=0}^{m-1} (m-j) \, x_{t-j}}{\sum_{j=0}^{m-1} (m-j)} = \frac{\sum_{j=0}^{m-1} (m-j) \, x_{t-j}}{m(m+1)/2}$$ - Reduces lag compared to SMA. - Weights form an arithmetic progression $m, m-1, \dots, 1$. ## 2.3 Exponential Smoothing (ES) ### 2.3.1 Simple (Single) Exponential Smoothing The recursive update equation: $$\hat{x}_t = \alpha \, x_t + (1-\alpha) \, \hat{x}_{t-1}, \quad 0 < \alpha < 1$$ **Expanded (infinite-memory) form:** $$\hat{x}_t = \alpha \sum_{j=0}^{\infty} (1-\alpha)^j \, x_{t-j}$$ - Effective weights decay geometrically: $\alpha, \alpha(1-\alpha), \alpha(1-\alpha)^2, \dots$ - Sum of weights: $\alpha \sum_{j=0}^{\infty} (1-\alpha)^j = \alpha \cdot \frac{1}{\alpha} = 1$ ✓ (unity gain satisfied). ### 2.3.2 Holt's Linear (Double) Exponential Smoothing For series with a **linear trend**, two recursions are maintained — level $\ell_t$ and trend $b_t$: $$\ell_t = \alpha \, x_t + (1-\alpha)(\ell_{t-1} + b_{t-1})$$ $$b_t = \beta \, (\ell_t - \ell_{t-1}) + (1-\beta) \, b_{t-1}$$ $h$-step ahead forecast: $$\hat{x}_{t+h} = \ell_t + h \, b_t$$ ### 2.3.3 Holt-Winters (Triple) Exponential Smoothing Adds a **seasonal component** $s_t$ of period $m$: $$\ell_t = \alpha \frac{x_t}{s_{t-m}} + (1-\alpha)(\ell_{t-1} + b_{t-1})$$ $$b_t = \beta (\ell_t - \ell_{t-1}) + (1-\beta) b_{t-1}$$ $$s_t = \gamma \frac{x_t}{\ell_t} + (1-\gamma) s_{t-m}$$ ## 2.4 Linear Filters and Convolution A **linear time-invariant (LTI) filter** transforms input $\{x_t\}$ into output $\{y_t\}$ via convolution: $$y_t = \sum_{j=-\infty}^{\infty} \psi_j \, x_{t-j}$$ where $\{\psi_j\}$ is the **impulse response function** of the filter. In the **frequency domain** (via the $z$-transform or Fourier transform), this becomes multiplication: $$Y(z) = \Psi(z) \, X(z)$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Frequency Response (Gain Function):** > The **gain** of a filter at frequency $\omega$ is: > $$G(\omega) = \vert \Psi(e^{-i\omega}) \vert = \left\vert \sum_{j} \psi_j \, e^{-i\omega j} \right\vert$$ > - $G(0) = 1$ → preserves the trend (low frequencies). > - $G(\pi) = 0$ → eliminates high-frequency noise. ## 2.5 Hodrick-Prescott (HP) Filter Decomposes a series $x_t$ into trend $\tau_t$ and cyclical component $c_t$: $$x_t = \tau_t + c_t$$ The trend is obtained by **minimizing**: $$\min_{\{\tau_t\}} \left\{ \sum_{t=1}^{T} (x_t - \tau_t)^2 + \lambda \sum_{t=2}^{T-1} \left[ (\tau_{t+1} - \tau_t) - (\tau_t - \tau_{t-1}) \right]^2 \right\}$$ - The first term penalizes the **deviation** of trend from data (fit). - The second term penalizes the **second difference** of the trend (smoothness). - $\lambda$ is the **smoothing parameter**: larger $\lambda$ → smoother trend. - Standard $\lambda$ values: **$\lambda = 1600$ for quarterly data**, $6.25$ for annual, $129600$ for monthly. ## 2.6 Band-Pass Filters Extract cyclical components within a specific frequency band $[\omega_1, \omega_2]$. ### 2.6.1 Baxter-King (BK) Filter A symmetric finite-impulse-response (FIR) band-pass filter: $$y_t = \sum_{j=-K}^{K} \psi_j \, x_{t-j}, \quad \sum_{j=-K}^{K} \psi_j = 0, \quad \psi_j = \psi_{-j}$$ - Ideal for isolating business-cycle frequencies (period 2–8 years for quarterly data). - Loses $K$ observations at each end of the sample. ### 2.6.2 Christiano-Fitzgerald (CF) Filter A full-sample **infinite-impulse-response (IIR)** approximation to the ideal band-pass filter using a trigonometric approximation. Preserves all observations (no endpoint loss). ## 2.7 Kernel and Local Polynomial Smoothing A **nonparametric** approach: estimate the trend at time $t$ using weighted least squares within a local window. **Nadaraya-Watson Estimator (Local Constant):** $$\hat{m}(t) = \frac{\sum_{s=1}^{T} K_h(t - s) \, x_s}{\sum_{s=1}^{T} K_h(t - s)}$$ where $K_h(\cdot) = \frac{1}{h} K(\cdot/h)$ is a rescaled kernel (Gaussian, Epanechnikov, uniform). **Local Linear Estimator (more accurate at boundaries):** $$\hat{m}(t) = e_1^\top \left( \mathbf{X}_t^\top \mathbf{W}_t \mathbf{X}_t \right)^{-1} \mathbf{X}_t^\top \mathbf{W}_t \, \mathbf{x}$$ where $\mathbf{X}_t$ contains local polynomial regressors and $\mathbf{W}_t = \text{diag}(K_h(t-s))$. ## 2.8 📋 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield for Exam) > [!NOTE] > **Master these equations — they form the spine of most exam problems.** | Method | Core Equation | Key Parameters | |---|---|---| | Simple MA | $\hat{x}_t = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{j=0}^{m-1} x_{t-j}$ | Window length $m$ | | Weighted MA | $\hat{x}_t = \frac{\sum (m-j) x_{t-j}}{m(m+1)/2}$ | Linear weights | | Simple ES | $\hat{x}_t = \alpha x_t + (1-\alpha) \hat{x}_{t-1}$ | Smoothing const. $\alpha$ | | Holt's Linear | $\ell_t = \alpha x_t + (1-\alpha)(\ell_{t-1} + b_{t-1})$; $b_t = \beta(\ell_t - \ell_{t-1}) + (1-\beta)b_{t-1}$ | $\alpha, \beta$ | | HP Filter | $\min \sum (x_t - \tau_t)^2 + \lambda \sum [(\tau_{t+1} - \tau_t) - (\tau_t - \tau_{t-1})]^2$ | $\lambda$ (1600 quarterly) | | Convolution | $y_t = \sum \psi_j x_{t-j}$ | Impulse response $\psi_j$ | | Gain function | $G(\omega) = \vert \sum \psi_j e^{-i\omega j} \vert$ | Frequency $\omega$ | | Nadaraya-Watson | $\hat{m}(t) = \frac{\sum K_h(t-s) x_s}{\sum K_h(t-s)}$ | Bandwidth $h$, kernel $K$ | | BK Filter | $y_t = \sum_{j=-K}^{K} \psi_j x_{t-j}$ | Truncation lag $K$ | | Sum of weights | $\sum_j w_j = 1$ (mean preservation) | Unity-gain condition | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Utility in Real-World Finance:** > - **HP Filter:** Used by the **U.S. CBO, OECD, and IMF** to extract trend GDP and output gap for monetary policy. > - **Holt-Winters:** Deployed in **retail inventory forecasting** (e.g., Walmart demand cycles). > - **Band-pass filters:** Used in **FX carry-trade** strategy design to identify medium-term cycles. > - **Kernel smoothing:** Used in **option pricing surfaces** and **yield curve estimation**. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Exponential Smoothing as an Infinite-Memory Weighted Average > **Goal:** Show that the recursive form $\hat{x}_t = \alpha x_t + (1-\alpha)\hat{x}_{t-1}$ is equivalent to an infinite weighted sum of past observations. **Step 1 — Write the recursive form at time $t$:** $$\hat{x}_t = \alpha \, x_t + (1-\alpha) \, \hat{x}_{t-1}$$ **Step 2 — Substitute the recursion for $\hat{x}_{t-1}$:** $$\hat{x}_{t-1} = \alpha \, x_{t-1} + (1-\alpha) \, \hat{x}_{t-2}$$ **Step 3 — Plug back into the original equation:** $$\hat{x}_t = \alpha x_t + (1-\alpha) \left[ \alpha x_{t-1} + (1-\alpha) \hat{x}_{t-2} \right]$$ $$\hat{x}_t = \alpha x_t + \alpha(1-\alpha) x_{t-1} + (1-\alpha)^2 \hat{x}_{t-2}$$ **Step 4 — Continue unrolling the recursion $k$ times:** $$\hat{x}_t = \alpha \sum_{j=0}^{k-1} (1-\alpha)^j x_{t-j} + (1-\alpha)^k \hat{x}_{t-k}$$ **Step 5 — Take the limit as $k \to \infty$:** Since $0 < 1-\alpha < 1$, we have $(1-\alpha)^k \to 0$ as $k \to \infty$, so the terminal term vanishes. We obtain: $$\boxed{\hat{x}_t = \alpha \sum_{j=0}^{\infty} (1-\alpha)^j \, x_{t-j}}$$ **Step 6 — Verify the unity-gain property:** $$\sum_{j=0}^{\infty} \alpha (1-\alpha)^j = \alpha \cdot \frac{1}{1-(1-\alpha)} = \alpha \cdot \frac{1}{\alpha} = 1 \quad \checkmark$$ This confirms that the geometric weights sum to 1, hence the filter preserves a constant input. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Moving Average as a Special Case of Exponential Smoothing > **Goal:** Show that SMA corresponds to ES in the limit $\alpha = 2/(m+1)$. **Step 1 — Recall the recursive ES form:** $$\hat{x}_t = \alpha x_t + (1-\alpha) \hat{x}_{t-1}$$ **Step 2 — Assume a uniform "memory" of $m$ periods and an "equal-weight" approximation:** The optimal $\alpha$ that minimizes the in-sample MSE for a horizon-1 forecast with a constant level can be shown (via the **Brown-Silver-Mayer** correspondence) to be: $$\alpha^* = \frac{2}{m+1}$$ **Step 3 — Verify the equivalence via the impulse response:** For ES, the impulse response is $\psi_j = \alpha(1-\alpha)^j$ for $j \geq 0$. Substituting $\alpha = 2/(m+1)$ and truncating at $j = m-1$ (the remaining weights become negligibly small), we get approximately equal weights $\approx 1/m$ over $m$ lags, recovering the SMA. This shows the **Bridge Identity:** $$\text{SMA}_t(m) \;\approx\; \text{ES}_t\left(\alpha = \frac{2}{m+1}\right)$$ ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — First-Order Conditions for the Hodrick-Prescott Filter > **Goal:** Derive the matrix form of the HP filter and identify its impulse response. **Step 1 — Express the optimization problem in matrix form:** Let $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, \dots, x_T)^\top$ and $\boldsymbol{\tau} = (\tau_1, \dots, \tau_T)^\top$. Then: $$\min_{\boldsymbol{\tau}} \; (\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\tau})^\top (\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\tau}) + \lambda \, \boldsymbol{\tau}^\top \mathbf{K}^\top \mathbf{K} \, \boldsymbol{\tau}$$ where $\mathbf{K}$ is the $(T-2) \times T$ **second-difference matrix** (a banded matrix with $[1, -2, 1]$ rows). **Step 2 — Differentiate w.r.t. $\boldsymbol{\tau}$ and set to zero:** $$\frac{\partial}{\partial \boldsymbol{\tau}} \left[ (\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\tau})^\top (\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\tau}) + \lambda \boldsymbol{\tau}^\top \mathbf{K}^\top \mathbf{K} \boldsymbol{\tau} \right] = 0$$ $$-2(\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\tau}) + 2\lambda \mathbf{K}^\top \mathbf{K} \boldsymbol{\tau} = 0$$ **Step 3 — Solve the linear system:** $$(\mathbf{I} + \lambda \mathbf{K}^\top \mathbf{K}) \, \boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{x}$$ $$\boxed{\boldsymbol{\tau} = (\mathbf{I} + \lambda \mathbf{K}^\top \mathbf{K})^{-1} \, \mathbf{x}}$$ **Step 4 — Identify the implicit impulse response:** The matrix $\mathbf{S}_\lambda = (\mathbf{I} + \lambda \mathbf{K}^\top \mathbf{K})^{-1}$ acts as a **smoothing operator** whose rows give the impulse response (weights) used to compute each $\tau_t$ from the data. The smoothing increases with $\lambda$. **Step 5 — Frequency-domain gain of the HP filter:** The transfer function is: $$\Psi_\lambda(\omega) = \frac{1}{1 + \lambda \left[2 \sin(\omega/2)\right]^4} = \frac{1}{1 + 16 \lambda \sin^4(\omega/2)}$$ - At $\omega = 0$ (trend): $\Psi_\lambda(0) = 1$ → trend preserved. - At $\omega = \pi$ (highest frequency): $\Psi_\lambda(\pi) = \frac{1}{1 + 16\lambda}$ → strong attenuation. This confirms the HP filter is a **low-pass filter**. ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Optimal Smoothing Constant in ES (MSE Minimization) > **Goal:** Find the $\alpha$ that minimizes the in-sample sum of squared one-step-ahead forecast errors. **Step 1 — Define the one-step-ahead forecast error:** $$e_t = x_t - \hat{x}_{t-1}$$ **Step 2 — The sum of squared errors:** $$S(\alpha) = \sum_{t=1}^{T} e_t^2 = \sum_{t=1}^{T} (x_t - \hat{x}_{t-1})^2$$ **Step 3 — Differentiate w.r.t. $\alpha$ and set to zero:** The recursion is nonlinear in $\alpha$, so no closed-form solution exists. Numerical optimization (gradient descent or Newton-Raphson) is used: $$\alpha^* = \arg\min_{\alpha \in (0,1)} S(\alpha)$$ **Step 4 — Common practice in finance:** Set $\alpha$ via cross-validation or information criteria (AIC/BIC). For noisy financial returns, $\alpha \in [0.05, 0.2]$ is common; for low-noise macroeconomic series, $\alpha \in [0.2, 0.4]$. ## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — Holt's Linear Smoothing **Given:** A 5-point quarterly revenue series $x_t = \{100, 120, 140, 160, 180\}$. Use $\alpha = 0.4$, $\beta = 0.3$, initial level $\ell_0 = 100$, initial trend $b_0 = 20$. **Step 1 — Compute $t=1$:** $$\ell_1 = \alpha x_1 + (1-\alpha)(\ell_0 + b_0) = 0.4(100) + 0.6(100 + 20) = 40 + 72 = 112$$ $$b_1 = \beta(\ell_1 - \ell_0) + (1-\beta) b_0 = 0.3(112 - 100) + 0.7(20) = 3.6 + 14 = 17.6$$ **Step 2 — Compute $t=2$:** $$\ell_2 = 0.4(120) + 0.6(112 + 17.6) = 48 + 77.76 = 125.76$$ $$b_2 = 0.3(125.76 - 112) + 0.7(17.6) = 4.128 + 12.32 = 16.448$$ **Step 3 — Forecast at $t=2$ for $h=3$ steps ahead:** $$\hat{x}_{2+3} = \ell_2 + 3 \, b_2 = 125.76 + 3(16.448) = 125.76 + 49.344 = 175.10$$ > [!NOTE] > This forecast $\hat{x}_5 = 175.10$ compares against the actual $x_5 = 180$ — the smoothing algorithm captures the upward trend with a small lag. ## 3.6 Comparative Properties Matrix | Property | Simple MA | Exponential Smoothing | HP Filter | Band-Pass | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Direction** | Backward / Centered | Forward | Two-sided | Two-sided | | **Memory** | Finite ($m$) | Infinite (decaying) | Full sample | Finite ($K$) or full | | **Lags trend?** | Yes, by $(m-1)/2$ | Yes, minimal | No | No | | **Mean preservation** | Yes (if $m$ odd) | Yes | Yes | No (zero-sum weights) | | **Frequency behavior** | Low-pass (cosine) | Low-pass (geometric) | Low-pass (4th-order) | Band-pass | | **Optimal use** | Noise removal | Forecasting | Trend-cycle decomposition | Business cycle isolation | # 4. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > **Modeled on KTU 2024 Scheme Part A format — direct, definition/conceptual type.** --- ### Question A1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **Define exponential smoothing. Derive the recursive form and state the property of the smoothing constant $\alpha$.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** Exponential smoothing is a forecasting technique that produces a smoothed series by recursively combining the current observation with the previous smoothed value. The simple form is: $$\hat{x}_t = \alpha x_t + (1-\alpha) \hat{x}_{t-1}, \quad 0 < \alpha < 1$$ The smoothing constant $\alpha$ controls the weight given to recent observations: a higher $\alpha$ gives more weight to recent data (less smoothing, more responsive), while a lower $\alpha$ gives more weight to past data (more smoothing, more stable). The weights on past observations decay geometrically as $\alpha(1-\alpha)^j$, and sum to unity, ensuring the filter preserves the mean level. *(1 mark: definition, 1 mark: equation, 1 mark: property of α)* --- ### Question A2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **What is the Hodrick-Prescott (HP) filter? Explain the role of the smoothing parameter $\lambda$.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The HP filter decomposes a time series $x_t$ into a trend $\tau_t$ and a cyclical component $c_t$ by minimizing: $$\sum_{t=1}^{T} (x_t - \tau_t)^2 + \lambda \sum_{t=2}^{T-1} \left[ (\tau_{t+1} - \tau_t) - (\tau_t - \tau_{t-1}) \right]^2$$ The smoothing parameter $\lambda$ controls the trade-off between fit and smoothness: **larger $\lambda$ produces a smoother trend** (penalizes abrupt changes more heavily), while **smaller $\lambda$ allows the trend to follow the data more closely**. The standard value for quarterly macroeconomic data is $\lambda = 1600$, proposed by Hodrick and Prescott (1997). *(1 mark: definition/equation, 1 mark: decomposition, 1 mark: role of λ)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) > [!NOTE] > **Internal choice pattern: Question A OR Question B — both at 14 marks with 7+7 sub-parts.** --- ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **(a) [7 Marks]** Define a linear filter in the context of time series. Show that the simple $m$-period moving average is a linear filter and compute its frequency response (gain function). **(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the Hodrick-Prescott (HP) filter with its objective function. Discuss the role of the smoothing parameter $\lambda$ and derive the first-order condition for the trend component. --- ### Model Solution to Question A #### Part (a) Solution — Linear Filter and MA Gain Function **Definition (2 marks):** A linear filter transforms an input series $\{x_t\}$ into an output series $\{y_t\}$ via a linear (convolution) operation: $$y_t = \sum_{j=-\infty}^{\infty} \psi_j x_{t-j}$$ where $\{\psi_j\}$ is the **impulse response** of the filter. The filter is **time-invariant** if $\psi_j$ does not depend on $t$. **Simple MA as a linear filter (2 marks):** The simple $m$-period moving average is: $$y_t = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{j=0}^{m-1} x_{t-j} = \sum_{j=0}^{m-1} \psi_j x_{t-j}, \quad \psi_j = \frac{1}{m}$$ This is clearly a linear filter with constant weights $\psi_j = 1/m$, time-invariant, and a finite impulse response (FIR). **Frequency response derivation (3 marks):** The gain function at frequency $\omega$ is: $$G(\omega) = \left| \sum_{j=0}^{m-1} \frac{1}{m} e^{-i\omega j} \right| = \frac{1}{m} \left| \frac{1 - e^{-i\omega m}}{1 - e^{-i\omega}} \right|$$ Using $1 - e^{-i\theta} = 2i \sin(\theta/2) e^{-i\theta/2}$: $$G(\omega) = \frac{1}{m} \left| \frac{\sin(\omega m/2)}{\sin(\omega/2)} \right|$$ This is the **Dirichlet kernel**, which equals 1 at $\omega = 0$ (trend preserved) and approximately 0 at $\omega = \pi$ (noise suppressed), confirming the low-pass nature of the simple moving average. #### Part (b) Solution — HP Filter **Objective function (2 marks):** The HP filter decomposes $x_t = \tau_t + c_t$ by solving: $$\min_{\{\tau_t\}} \sum_{t=1}^{T} (x_t - \tau_t)^2 + \lambda \sum_{t=2}^{T-1} \left[ (\tau_{t+1} - \tau_t) - (\tau_t - \tau_{t-1}) \right]^2$$ The first term measures **fit**; the second penalizes the **second difference** of the trend (smoothness). **Role of $\lambda$ (2 marks):** - $\lambda = 0$ → trend equals the data itself (no smoothing). - $\lambda \to \infty$ → trend becomes a straight line (extreme smoothing). - Standard choice: $\lambda = 1600$ for quarterly data. **First-order condition (3 marks):** Differentiating w.r.t. $\tau_t$ for an interior point $t$: $$-2(x_t - \tau_t) + 2\lambda \left[ (\tau_{t+2} - 2\tau_{t+1} + \tau_t) - 2(\tau_{t+1} - 2\tau_t + \tau_{t-1}) + (\tau_t - 2\tau_{t-1} + \tau_{t-2}) \right] = 0$$ Simplifying: $$\boxed{x_t = \tau_t + \lambda \left[ \tau_{t+2} - 4\tau_{t+1} + 6\tau_t - 4\tau_{t-1} + \tau_{t-2} \right]}$$ Equivalently, in matrix form: $(\mathbf{I} + \lambda \mathbf{K}^\top \mathbf{K})\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{x}$. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain Simple Exponential Smoothing. Show that it can be expressed as an infinite weighted average and verify the unity-gain property of the weights. **(b) [7 Marks]** Describe Holt's linear (double) exponential smoothing method. Given the data $x_1 = 50, x_2 = 55, x_3 = 60, x_4 = 65$ with $\alpha = 0.3$, $\beta = 0.2$, $\ell_0 = 50$, $b_0 = 5$, compute the level and trend at $t = 4$ and forecast for $h = 2$ steps ahead. --- ### Model Solution to Question B #### Part (a) Solution — SES Derivation **Definition (2 marks):** Simple Exponential Smoothing is a recursive forecasting method: $$\hat{x}_t = \alpha x_t + (1-\alpha) \hat{x}_{t-1}, \quad 0 < \alpha < 1$$ **Unrolling the recursion (3 marks):** Iteratively substituting: $$\hat{x}_t = \alpha x_t + (1-\alpha)[\alpha x_{t-1} + (1-\alpha)\hat{x}_{t-2}]$$ $$= \alpha x_t + \alpha(1-\alpha) x_{t-1} + (1-\alpha)^2 \hat{x}_{t-2}$$ Continuing $k$ times: $$\hat{x}_t = \alpha \sum_{j=0}^{k-1} (1-\alpha)^j x_{t-j} + (1-\alpha)^k \hat{x}_{t-k}$$ As $k \to \infty$, $(1-\alpha)^k \to 0$ (since $\vert 1-\alpha \vert < 1$): $$\hat{x}_t = \alpha \sum_{j=0}^{\infty} (1-\alpha)^j x_{t-j}$$ **Unity-gain verification (2 marks):** $$\sum_{j=0}^{\infty} \alpha(1-\alpha)^j = \frac{\alpha}{1-(1-\alpha)} = \frac{\alpha}{\alpha} = 1 \quad \checkmark$$ This confirms that feeding in a constant series yields a constant output. #### Part (b) Solution — Holt's Method Computation **Recursions (2 marks):** $$\ell_t = \alpha x_t + (1-\alpha)(\ell_{t-1} + b_{t-1})$$ $$b_t = \beta(\ell_t - \ell_{t-1}) + (1-\beta) b_{t-1}$$ **Compute $t=1$ (1.5 marks):** $$\ell_1 = 0.3(50) + 0.7(50 + 5) = 15 + 38.5 = 53.5$$ $$b_1 = 0.2(53.5 - 50) + 0.8(5) = 0.7 + 4 = 4.7$$ **Compute $t=2$ (1 mark):** $$\ell_2 = 0.3(55) + 0.7(53.5 + 4.7) = 16.5 + 40.74 = 57.24$$ $$b_2 = 0.2(57.24 - 53.5) + 0.8(4.7) = 0.748 + 3.76 = 4.508$$ **Compute $t=3$ (1 mark):** $$\ell_3 = 0.3(60) + 0.7(57.24 + 4.508) = 18 + 43.223 = 61.22$$ $$b_3 = 0.2(61.22 - 57.24) + 0.8(4.508) = 0.796 + 3.606 = 4.402$$ **Compute $t=4$ (0.5 mark):** $$\ell_4 = 0.3(65) + 0.7(61.22 + 4.402) = 19.5 + 45.935 = 65.43$$ $$b_4 = 0.2(65.43 - 61.22) + 0.8(4.402) = 0.842 + 3.522 = 4.36$$ **Forecast $h=2$ steps ahead (1 mark):** $$\hat{x}_{4+2} = \ell_4 + 2 \cdot b_4 = 65.43 + 2(4.36) = 65.43 + 8.72 = \boxed{74.15}$$ --- ## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks (Read Carefully Before Exam):** > > 1. **Forgetting the unity-gain constraint:** When asked to design a custom filter, students often pick weights that do not sum to 1, which **biases the level**. Always verify $\sum w_j = 1$. *(–1 to 2 marks typical deduction)* > > 2. **Confusing centered vs. backward MA:** A centered MA uses $x_{t-m+1}$ through $x_{t+m-1}$ (symmetric), while a backward MA uses only past values. Exam questions specifying "5-day MA" usually mean backward. *(–1 mark)* > > 3. **Wrong $\lambda$ value for HP filter:** Quarterly data uses $\lambda = 1600$. Writing $\lambda = 160$ (a common slip) is incorrect and loses marks. Annual: $6.25$; Monthly: $129600$. > > 4. **Mishandling $(1-\alpha)^k \to 0$ step in ES:** Students often write "as $k \to \infty$" without justifying that $\vert 1-\alpha \vert < 1$ for $\alpha \in (0,1)$. The KTU examiner expects this explicit justification. *(–1 mark)* > > 5. **Mixing up Holt's vs. Holt-Winters:** Holt's has only level + trend; Holt-Winters adds a seasonal term. Writing the wrong recursion loses up to 2 marks. > > 6. **Numerical computation errors in Part B (b):** When computing Holt's recursions, carry at least 3 decimal places and write each intermediate result clearly. The KTU valuation scheme deducts heavily for skipped intermediate steps. --- ## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Final Read Before Exam:** - ✅ **Smoothing** = local averaging to suppress noise; **Filtering** = frequency-selective transformation. - ✅ **Simple MA**: $\hat{x}_t = \frac{1}{m}\sum_{j=0}^{m-1} x_{t-j}$; lags trend by $(m-1)/2$. - ✅ **Exponential Smoothing**: $\hat{x}_t = \alpha x_t + (1-\alpha)\hat{x}_{t-1}$; weights decay geometrically. - ✅ **Holt's Linear**: Two recursions — level $\ell_t$ and trend $b_t$ — for trending data. - ✅ **Holt-Winters**: Adds seasonal component $s_t$ for seasonal data. - ✅ **HP Filter**: Minimize $(\text{fit penalty}) + \lambda \cdot (\text{smoothness penalty})$; $\lambda = 1600$ for quarterly. - ✅ **HP matrix form**: $\boldsymbol{\tau} = (\mathbf{I} + \lambda \mathbf{K}^\top \mathbf{K})^{-1}\mathbf{x}$. - ✅ **Linear filter / convolution**: $y_t = \sum \psi_j x_{t-j}$. - ✅ **Gain function**: $G(\omega) = \vert \sum \psi_j e^{-i\omega j} \vert$ — measures frequency response. - ✅ **Unity-gain constraint**: $\sum w_j = 1$ for mean preservation. - ✅ **Band-Pass Filters**: Baxter-King (FIR, symmetric, $K$-truncated); CF (IIR, full sample). - ✅ **Kernel Smoothing**: Nadaraya-Watson estimator uses $\hat{m}(t) = \frac{\sum K_h(t-s) x_s}{\sum K_h(t-s)}$. - ✅ **Bandwidth $h$**: smaller $h$ → less smoothing (more variance, less bias). - ✅ **First-order condition for HP**: $x_t = \tau_t + \lambda[\tau_{t+2} - 4\tau_{t+1} + 6\tau_t - 4\tau_{t-1} + \tau_{t-2}]$. - ✅ **$\alpha \in (0,1)$** always; choose via cross-validation; financial returns: $\alpha \in [0.05, 0.2]$. - ✅ **Always state the choice of $\lambda$ or $\alpha$** when solving numerical exam problems. - ✅ **Sketch the gain function** for full marks in filter design questions. - ✅ **Differentiate** "forecast" (future prediction) from "smoothing" (denoised past/present). - ✅ **Know the difference** between "filters" and "smoothers" — filters use only past data, smoothers can use both past and future. - ✅ **End-of-sample and boundary behavior** matter — kernel and HP filters behave differently at series boundaries. - ✅ **Standard KTU exam trick**: ask for the "frequency response" or "transfer function" of a filter — practice computing $G(\omega)$ for SMA and HP. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final Mantra for KTU PEIET742 Module 1:** > *"Every smoother is a filter; every filter is a weighted average; and the weights must sum to one."* Memorize this and you have the conceptual backbone to answer 80% of any exam question on this topic.

topics.title: White Noise
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET742 - FINANCIAL TIME SERIES ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** White Noise

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A time series $\{\varepsilon_t\}$ is called a **White Noise (WN)** process if it is a sequence of uncorrelated random variables with **zero mean** and **constant finite variance** $\sigma^2$. Formally, the sequence $\{\varepsilon_t\}$ is white noise if:

$$E(\varepsilon_t) = 0, \quad \text{Var}(\varepsilon_t) = \sigma^2 < \infty, \quad \text{Cov}(\varepsilon_t, \varepsilon_s) = 0 \;\; \text{for} \;\; t \neq s$$

If in addition the random variables are **identically and independently distributed (i.i.d.)**, the process is referred to as **strict white noise**. When the innovations are further assumed to follow a Normal (Gaussian) distribution, the process is specifically called **Gaussian White Noise**, denoted as $\varepsilon_t \sim \text{WN}(0, \sigma^2)$ or $\varepsilon_t \sim \text{i.i.d. } N(0,\sigma^2)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** White noise is the foundational building block of all stationary time series models (AR, MA, ARMA, ARIMA). Any time series can be conceptually decomposed as a linear combination of past white noise shocks. Memorizing the three conditions (zero mean, constant variance, zero autocorrelation) is mandatory.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a person flipping a fair coin at every minute mark. The outcome at minute $t$ has no memory of the outcome at minute $t-1$, $t-2$, or any past minute. Each flip is **independent**, has an expected value of **zero** (if we code Heads = +1 and Tails = −1), and the spread (variance) of the outcome never changes. That is precisely a white noise process — it is the financial analogue of "pure randomness with no memory."

In finance, white noise can be thought of as the **unpredictable component** of asset returns after all systematic patterns have been removed. A market with no arbitrage, no momentum, no mean-reversion, and no autocorrelation is, in theory, a white noise market — a hypothesis tested rigorously in the **Efficient Market Hypothesis (EMH)**.

### 1.3 Key Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Mean:** $\mu = 0$
- **Variance:** $\sigma^2$ (finite, constant, strictly positive)
- **Standard Deviation:** $\sigma = \sqrt{\sigma^2}$
- **Autocorrelation Function (ACF) at Lag 0:** $\rho(0) = 1$
- **Autocorrelation Function at all other lags:** $\rho(k) = 0 \;\; \forall \, k \geq 1$
- **Skewness:** $\gamma_1 = 0$ (for symmetric distributions like Gaussian)
- **Excess Kurtosis:** $\gamma_2 = 0$ (for Gaussian white noise)

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic Device for Exams:** "**Z-C-Z**" — **Z**ero mean, **C**onstant variance, **Z**ero autocorrelation. This is the easiest way to recall the three pillars of a white noise process during quick-recall exam questions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Decomposition of the Definition

The white noise concept rests on **three independent pillars**, each of which must be verified before a series can be called white noise:

- **Pillar 1 — Zero Mean Condition**
  - *Why:* Ensures the process is centered around zero, simplifying theoretical derivations and allowing for cleaner covariance structures.
  - *How:* Verified empirically by checking that the sample mean $\bar{\varepsilon} = \frac{1}{T}\sum_{t=1}^{T} \varepsilon_t$ is statistically indistinguishable from zero via a t-test.
  - *Practical Implication:* If a series has a non-zero mean, it can be detrended to satisfy this condition.

- **Pillar 2 — Constant Variance (Homoscedasticity)**
  - *Why:* Guarantees that the dispersion of shocks is uniform across time, which is essential for valid inference in regression and time series models.
  - *How:* Tested using the **Ljung-Box test**, **ARCH test**, or by inspecting the **Autocorrelation Function (ACF)** of the squared series.
  - *Practical Implication:* Violation leads to **Conditional Heteroscedasticity** (ARCH/GARCH effects), a critical KTU topic in later modules.

- **Pillar 3 — Zero Autocorrelation (No Memory)**
  - *Why:* Means past shocks carry no information about future shocks — the cornerstone of unpredictability.
  - *How:* Tested using the **Ljung-Box Q-statistic**, the **Durbin-Watson test**, or the **Portmanteau test** on the ACF.
  - *Practical Implication:* If autocorrelation exists, the series is not white noise but rather an AR, MA, or ARMA process.

### 2.2 Distinction: Strict White Noise vs. Gaussian White Noise

| Property | Strict White Noise | Gaussian White Noise |
|----------|-------------------|----------------------|
| Zero Mean | ✓ | ✓ |
| Constant Variance | ✓ | ✓ |
| Uncorrelated | ✓ | ✓ |
| Independent | ✓ | ✓ (uncorrelated + Normal $\Rightarrow$ independent) |
| Normally Distributed | Not Required | **Mandatory** |
| Notation | $\varepsilon_t \sim \text{WN}(0, \sigma^2)$ | $\varepsilon_t \sim \text{i.i.d. } N(0, \sigma^2)$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** In financial engineering, Gaussian white noise is used to model the **innovation process** in stochastic differential equations (e.g., the **Geometric Brownian Motion** model for stock prices: $dS_t = \mu S_t \, dt + \sigma S_t \, dW_t$, where $dW_t$ is a white noise increment).

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Property | Mathematical Expression | Description / Condition |
|---|-------------------|-------------------------|--------------------------|
| 1 | Mean | $E(\varepsilon_t) = 0$ | Zero mean condition |
| 2 | Variance | $\text{Var}(\varepsilon_t) = \sigma^2$ | Constant finite variance |
| 3 | Autocovariance | $\gamma(k) = \text{Cov}(\varepsilon_t, \varepsilon_{t+k}) = 0, \; k \neq 0$ | Zero for all non-zero lags |
| 4 | Autocorrelation | $\rho(k) = \gamma(k)/\gamma(0) = 0, \; k \neq 0$ | Zero for all non-zero lags |
| 5 | ACF at Lag 0 | $\rho(0) = 1$ | Always equals 1 by definition |
| 6 | Standard Deviation | $\sigma = \sqrt{\text{Var}(\varepsilon_t)}$ | Always positive |
| 7 | Skewness | $\gamma_1 = E[(\varepsilon_t - \mu)^3]/\sigma^3$ | Zero for symmetric WN |
| 8 | Excess Kurtosis | $\gamma_2 = E[(\varepsilon_t - \mu)^4]/\sigma^4 - 3$ | Zero for Gaussian WN |
| 9 | Sample Mean | $\bar{\varepsilon} = \frac{1}{T}\sum_{t=1}^{T} \varepsilon_t$ | Empirical estimator of mean |
| 10 | Sample Variance | $\hat{\sigma}^2 = \frac{1}{T-1}\sum_{t=1}^{T} (\varepsilon_t - \bar{\varepsilon})^2$ | Empirical estimator of variance |
| 11 | Sample ACF | $\hat{\rho}(k) = \frac{\sum_{t=k+1}^{T}(\varepsilon_t - \bar{\varepsilon})(\varepsilon_{t-k} - \bar{\varepsilon})}{\sum_{t=1}^{T}(\varepsilon_t - \bar{\varepsilon})^2}$ | Empirical autocorrelation at lag $k$ |
| 12 | Ljung-Box Statistic | $Q(m) = T(T+2)\sum_{k=1}^{m}\frac{\hat{\rho}^2(k)}{T-k}$ | Tests joint null of white noise |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note for Table Rendering:** All absolute value notations and conditional bars in formulas use $\vert$ or $\mid$ to prevent markdown table syntax breakage during PDF conversion. Always replicate this convention in your KTU answer sheets.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Autocovariance and Autocorrelation Function of White Noise

**Step 1 — Start with the Definition of Autocovariance Function (ACVF).**

By definition, the autocovariance at lag $k$ for a stationary process is:
$$\gamma(k) = E[(\varepsilon_t - \mu)(\varepsilon_{t+k} - \mu)]$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the White Noise Mean Condition.**

For a white noise process, $\mu = E(\varepsilon_t) = 0$. Thus, the expression simplifies to:
$$\gamma(k) = E[\varepsilon_t \cdot \varepsilon_{t+k}]$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the Two Distinct Cases.**

**Case A: $k = 0$ (Lag Zero).**
$$\gamma(0) = E[\varepsilon_t \cdot \varepsilon_t] = E[\varepsilon_t^2] = \text{Var}(\varepsilon_t) + [E(\varepsilon_t)]^2$$
Since $E(\varepsilon_t) = 0$, this reduces to:
$$\gamma(0) = \sigma^2 + 0 = \sigma^2$$

**Case B: $k \neq 0$ (Any Non-Zero Lag).**
$$\gamma(k) = E[\varepsilon_t \cdot \varepsilon_{t+k}]$$
Since $\varepsilon_t$ and $\varepsilon_{t+k}$ are uncorrelated for $k \neq 0$, the expectation of the product factorizes:
$$\gamma(k) = E[\varepsilon_t] \cdot E[\varepsilon_{t+k}] = 0 \cdot 0 = 0$$

**Step 4 — Combine Both Cases into a Compact Expression Using the Kronecker Delta.**

The Kronecker delta $\delta_k$ is defined as $\delta_k = 1$ if $k = 0$, and $\delta_k = 0$ otherwise. The autocovariance function can thus be written elegantly as:
$$\gamma(k) = \sigma^2 \cdot \delta_k$$

**Step 5 — Derive the Autocorrelation Function (ACF) by Normalization.**

The ACF is defined as $\rho(k) = \gamma(k) / \gamma(0)$. Substituting the ACVF result:
$$\rho(k) = \frac{\sigma^2 \cdot \delta_k}{\sigma^2} = \delta_k$$

Therefore, the ACF of a white noise process is:
$$\rho(k) = \begin{cases} 1, & k = 0 \\ 0, & k \neq 0 \end{cases}$$

**Interpretation:** A plot of the ACF versus lag $k$ for a white noise series should show a single spike of height 1 at $k=0$ and zeros everywhere else. This is the visual fingerprint of white noise and is the basis of the **Ljung-Box Portmanteau test** for white noise.

### 3.2 Derivation: Sample Mean and Sample Variance Under White Noise

**Step 1 — Expected Value of the Sample Mean.**
$$E(\bar{\varepsilon}) = E\left[\frac{1}{T}\sum_{t=1}^{T}\varepsilon_t\right] = \frac{1}{T}\sum_{t=1}^{T} E(\varepsilon_t) = \frac{1}{T}\sum_{t=1}^{T} 0 = 0$$

**Step 2 — Variance of the Sample Mean (Key Result).**
$$\text{Var}(\bar{\varepsilon}) = \text{Var}\left(\frac{1}{T}\sum_{t=1}^{T}\varepsilon_t\right) = \frac{1}{T^2}\sum_{t=1}^{T}\text{Var}(\varepsilon_t) = \frac{1}{T^2}\sum_{t=1}^{T}\sigma^2 = \frac{\sigma^2}{T}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This result is the foundation of the **Law of Large Numbers** for white noise: as $T \to \infty$, the variance of the sample mean shrinks to zero, so $\bar{\varepsilon} \to 0$ in probability. This is why large-sample financial averages become reliable estimators under white noise assumptions.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Decision Table for White Noise Diagnostics

| Diagnostic Test | Null Hypothesis $H_0$ | Test Statistic | Decision Rule at 5% Level | If $H_0$ Rejected $\Rightarrow$ Series is... |
|-----------------|----------------------|----------------|----------------------------|----------------------------------------------|
| Ljung-Box Test | Series is WN | $Q(m) = T(T+2)\sum_{k=1}^{m}\hat{\rho}^2(k)/(T-k) \sim \chi^2(m)$ | Reject if $Q(m) > \chi^2_{0.05, m}$ | Autocorrelated (AR/MA/ARMA) |
| t-test on Mean | $E(\varepsilon_t) = 0$ | $t = \bar{\varepsilon}/(\hat{\sigma}/\sqrt{T})$ | Reject if $\vert t \vert > 1.96$ | Has a non-zero mean |
| ARCH-LM Test | No ARCH effects | $LM = TR^2 \sim \chi^2(p)$ | Reject if $LM > \chi^2_{0.05, p}$ | Heteroscedastic (ARCH/GARCH) |
| Jarque-Bera Test | Normal Distribution | $JB = \frac{T}{6}\left(S^2 + \frac{(K-3)^2}{4}\right) \sim \chi^2(2)$ | Reject if $JB > \chi^2_{0.05, 2}$ | Non-Normal (fat tails) |

where $S$ = sample skewness, $K$ = sample kurtosis, $T$ = sample size, $R^2$ = R-squared from auxiliary regression, and $m$ = number of lags considered.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: White Noise Process — Conceptual Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Random Shock Generator] --> B[White Noise Process eps_t]
    B --> C{Zero Mean: E = 0?}
    B --> D{Constant Variance: sigma^2?}
    B --> E{Zero Autocorrelation: rho k = 0?}
    C --> F[Valid WN Process]
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G[Building Block for AR MA ARMA Models]
    F --> H[Innovation Term in GARCH Volatility Models]
    F --> I[Benchmark for Efficient Market Hypothesis]
    F --> J[Monte Carlo Simulation Engine]
    K[Time Series X_t] --> L{Decomposition}
    L --> M[Systematic Component S_t]
    L --> N[Stochastic Component eps_t]
    N --> B
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: ACF Signature of White Noise

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Lag 0: rho equals 1.0] -->|Spike| B[Strong Reference Point]
    C[Lag 1: rho equals 0.0] -->|Within CI| D[No Memory]
    E[Lag 2: rho equals 0.0] -->|Within CI| D
    F[Lag 3: rho equals 0.0] -->|Within CI| D
    G[Lag k for k greater than 0] -->|Within CI| D
    D --> H[Conclusion: Series is White Noise]
    B --> H
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: White Noise in Time Series Modeling

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    input1[Raw Financial Series r_t] --> stage1[Stationarity Testing ADF KPSS]
    stage1 --> stage2{Differencing Required?}
    stage2 -->|Yes| stage3[Apply Differencing]
    stage2 -->|No| stage4[Proceed to Modeling]
    stage3 --> stage4
    stage4 --> stage5[Model Identification ACF PACF]
    stage5 --> stage6{AR MA ARMA?}
    stage6 --> stage7[Estimate Model Parameters]
    stage7 --> stage8[Residual Diagnostics]
    stage8 --> stage9{Ljung-Box on Residuals}
    stage9 -->|p greater than 0.05| stage10[Residuals are White Noise Model Acceptable]
    stage9 -->|p less than 0.05| stage11[Re-specify Model]
    stage10 --> output1[Forecasting and Inference]
    stage11 --> stage5
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation of the Architecture:** The above flowchart illustrates the canonical KTU-recommended workflow in financial time series analysis. The terminal validation step — confirming that model residuals behave as white noise — is the **goodness-of-fit criterion** for any ARMA-family model. Without this, the model is statistically invalid.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Style]**

**Q1. Define a White Noise process. List and briefly explain the three essential properties a stochastic process must satisfy to be classified as white noise. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
A stochastic process $\{\varepsilon_t\}$ is called a **White Noise** process if it consists of uncorrelated random variables with zero mean and constant finite variance. The three essential properties are:
1. **Zero Mean:** $E(\varepsilon_t) = 0$ for all $t$ — the process fluctuates around zero.
2. **Constant Variance:** $\text{Var}(\varepsilon_t) = \sigma^2 < \infty$ — the dispersion is uniform across time (homoscedasticity).
3. **Zero Autocorrelation:** $\text{Cov}(\varepsilon_t, \varepsilon_{t+k}) = 0$ for all $k \neq 0$ — there is no linear dependence between past and present values.

*(Full credit: 3 marks — 1 mark for definition + 1 mark for any two properties + 1 mark for the third property.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**Q2. What is the difference between Strict White Noise and Gaussian White Noise? Why is Gaussian White Noise particularly important in financial time series modeling? (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
- **Strict White Noise** requires only that the random variables be uncorrelated, have zero mean, and constant variance. They need not be independent or normally distributed.
- **Gaussian White Noise** is a stricter subclass where the innovations are additionally assumed to be **independent and identically distributed (i.i.d.) Normal** random variables with mean zero and variance $\sigma^2$, i.e., $\varepsilon_t \sim N(0, \sigma^2)$.
- **Importance in Finance:** Normality ensures that uncorrelatedness implies independence, which simplifies the likelihood function, enables closed-form Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE), and underpins the Black-Scholes option pricing framework and standard GARCH specifications. *(3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Module 1, Full-Marks Pattern]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** State and explain the formal definition of a White Noise process. Derive the Autocorrelation Function (ACF) of a white noise process and represent it using the Kronecker delta notation. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Consider a simulated white noise series $\varepsilon_t$ of length $T = 100$ generated from $N(0, 4)$. Compute the following: (i) The theoretical variance of the sample mean $\bar{\varepsilon}$; (ii) The theoretical standard error of $\bar{\varepsilon}$; (iii) Construct the 95% confidence interval for the true mean of the series. State your interpretation. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution to (a):**
- State the three conditions of white noise: $E(\varepsilon_t) = 0$, $\text{Var}(\varepsilon_t) = \sigma^2$, $\text{Cov}(\varepsilon_t, \varepsilon_s) = 0$ for $t \neq s$. *(1 mark)*
- Define the autocovariance function $\gamma(k) = E[(\varepsilon_t - \mu)(\varepsilon_{t+k} - \mu)]$ and substitute $\mu = 0$. *(1 mark)*
- Show $\gamma(0) = E[\varepsilon_t^2] = \sigma^2$ and $\gamma(k) = 0$ for $k \neq 0$. *(2 marks)*
- Normalize to obtain $\rho(k) = \gamma(k)/\gamma(0) = \delta_k$, where $\delta_k$ is the Kronecker delta. *(2 marks)*
- Conclude with a graphical statement: ACF spikes at 1 for lag 0 and zero elsewhere. *(1 mark)*

**Model Solution to (b):**
- **(i)** Variance of sample mean: $\text{Var}(\bar{\varepsilon}) = \sigma^2/T = 4/100 = 0.04$. *(2 marks)*
