- **(ii)** Standard error: $SE(\bar{\varepsilon}) = \sqrt{\text{Var}(\bar{\varepsilon})} = \sqrt{0.04} = 0.20$. *(2 marks)*
- **(iii)** 95% Confidence Interval: $\bar{\varepsilon} \pm 1.96 \times SE = \bar{\varepsilon} \pm 1.96 \times 0.20 = \bar{\varepsilon} \pm 0.392$. Since the true mean is zero, the interval is approximately $[-0.392, +0.392]$. *(2 marks)*
- **Interpretation:** If the computed sample mean lies within this interval, we fail to reject the null hypothesis that the series has zero mean, consistent with white noise behavior. *(1 mark)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain the role of White Noise as the fundamental innovation process in financial time series modeling. Describe how Ljung-Box and ARCH-LM tests are used to validate that a given series is white noise. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A financial analyst observes a series of daily log-returns of a stock. The computed sample statistics are: sample mean $\bar{r} = 0.0008$, sample standard deviation $s = 0.022$, sample size $T = 250$, and the Ljung-Box statistic at $m = 10$ lags is $Q(10) = 8.74$. The critical value of $\chi^2_{0.05, 10} = 18.307$. Test whether the series is white noise at the 5% significance level. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution to (a):**
- White noise serves as the **innovation (error) term** in AR, MA, ARMA, ARIMA, and GARCH models; it represents the unpredictable component. *(2 marks)*
- **Ljung-Box Test** $H_0$: Series is white noise (all autocorrelations up to lag $m$ are zero). Test statistic $Q(m) = T(T+2)\sum_{k=1}^{m}\hat{\rho}^2(k)/(T-k) \sim \chi^2(m)$. Reject $H_0$ if $Q(m) > \chi^2_{\alpha, m}$. *(2.5 marks)*
- **ARCH-LM Test** $H_0$: No ARCH effects (no conditional heteroscedasticity). Used to check the constant variance assumption. *(2.5 marks)*

**Model Solution to (b):**
- **Decision Rule for Ljung-Box:** Reject $H_0$ (series is not white noise) if $Q(10) > \chi^2_{0.05, 10}$. *(1 mark)*
- **Comparison:** $Q(10) = 8.74$ vs. $\chi^2_{0.05, 10} = 18.307$. Since $8.74 < 18.307$, we **fail to reject $H_0$**. *(2 marks)*
- **Mean Test (additional):** $t = \bar{r}/(s/\sqrt{T}) = 0.0008/(0.022/\sqrt{250}) = 0.0008/0.001391 = 0.575$. Since $\vert t \vert = 0.575 < 1.96$, we fail to reject $E(r_t) = 0$. *(2 marks)*
- **Conclusion:** Both tests support the null hypothesis. The return series is consistent with **white noise** behavior at the 5% significance level, suggesting no predictable structure — aligned with weak-form market efficiency. *(2 marks)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Alert:**
> 1. **Common Mistake 1 (–2 marks):** Confusing **autocorrelation** (zero for WN) with **autocovariance** (zero for WN, but not equal to 1). Examiners specifically test whether you know that $\rho(0) = 1$ even for WN.
> 2. **Common Mistake 2 (–1 mark):** Forgetting to state **independence** when discussing strict white noise. Zero autocorrelation is necessary but not sufficient for strict WN; independence must be stated explicitly.
> 3. **Common Mistake 3 (–1 mark):** Writing $\sigma$ and $\sigma^2$ interchangeably. Always distinguish: $\sigma^2$ is the variance, $\sigma$ is the standard deviation. Examiners mark this strictly.
> 4. **Common Mistake 4 (–1.5 marks):** For Part B numerical problems, students often forget the $\sqrt{T}$ in the standard error formula, leading to wildly incorrect confidence intervals. Memorize: $SE(\bar{x}) = s/\sqrt{T}$.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition (Z-C-Z):** White Noise = **Z**ero mean + **C**onstant variance + **Z**ero autocorrelation.
- **Notation:** $\varepsilon_t \sim \text{WN}(0, \sigma^2)$ for weak white noise; $\varepsilon_t \sim \text{i.i.d. } N(0, \sigma^2)$ for Gaussian white noise.
- **ACF Signature:** Spike of 1 at lag 0, zeros everywhere else — use the Kronecker delta $\delta_k$ to express compactly.
- **Variance of Sample Mean:** $\text{Var}(\bar{\varepsilon}) = \sigma^2/T$ — this is why larger samples yield more reliable mean estimates.
- **Strict vs. Gaussian:** Strict WN = uncorrelated; Gaussian WN = uncorrelated + Normal $\Rightarrow$ automatically independent.
- **Diagnostic Toolbox:** Ljung-Box (autocorrelation), t-test (mean), ARCH-LM (variance), Jarque-Bera (normality).
- **Role in ARMA-family:** White noise is the **innovation** $\varepsilon_t$ that drives all AR, MA, ARMA, and ARIMA processes. Validating that residuals are WN is the **terminal goodness-of-fit step**.
- **Real-World Use:** Models the unpredictable component in asset returns; the **Efficient Market Hypothesis** essentially posits that returns are (approximately) white noise.
- **Stochastic Calculus Link:** White noise is the discrete-time analogue of the **Wiener process increment** $dW_t$ in continuous-time finance (Black-Scholes, Geometric Brownian Motion).
- **Pitfall to Avoid:** White noise ≠ absence of any structure; it only rules out **linear autocorrelation**. Nonlinear patterns, GARCH effects, or chaos can still hide in a WN series.
- **Key Constant:** The variance $\sigma^2$ must be **finite** and **strictly positive** — otherwise the process is degenerate and not useful for modeling.
- **Exam Favorites:** Definition (3 marks), ACF derivation (7 marks), diagnostic testing interpretation (4 marks).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Autoregressive Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET742 - FINANCIAL TIME SERIES ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Autoregressive Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Autoregressive (AR) Model** of order $p$, denoted as **AR(p)**, is a stochastic time series model in which the current value of the series is expressed as a finite, linear aggregate of its own $p$ previous values (lagged observations) plus a white noise error term. It is one of the foundational parametric models in the **Box–Jenkins Methodology** for stationary time series analysis.

Mathematically, the AR(p) model is defined as:

$$Y_t = c + \phi_1 Y_{t-1} + \phi_2 Y_{t-2} + \dots + \phi_p Y_{t-p} + \varepsilon_t$$

where:
- $Y_t$ is the value of the time series at time $t$.
- $c$ is a constant (intercept / drift term).
- $\phi_1, \phi_2, \dots, \phi_p$ are the **autoregressive coefficients** (real-valued parameters).
- $\varepsilon_t$ is a **white noise** process, typically $\varepsilon_t \sim WN(0, \sigma_\varepsilon^2)$, meaning $E(\varepsilon_t)=0$, $Var(\varepsilon_t)=\sigma_\varepsilon^2$, and $Cov(\varepsilon_t, \varepsilon_s)=0$ for $t \neq s$.
- $p$ is a non-negative integer representing the **order of the model**.

When the mean of the series is zero (centered), the model is simplified to:

$$Y_t = \phi_1 Y_{t-1} + \phi_2 Y_{t-2} + \dots + \phi_p Y_{t-p} + \varepsilon_t$$

Using the **Backshift (Lag) Operator** $B$, where $B Y_t = Y_{t-1}$, the AR(p) process can be compactly written in operator form as:

$$\Phi(B) Y_t = c + \varepsilon_t \quad \text{where} \quad \Phi(B) = 1 - \phi_1 B - \phi_2 B^2 - \dots - \phi_p B^p$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** The AR model is the first building block of the **ARMA / ARIMA family**. The 2024 scheme places strong emphasis on deriving the **mean**, **variance**, **autocorrelation function (ACF)**, and **stationarity conditions** for the AR(1) and AR(2) cases, as these are the most frequently tested problems in University examinations.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are a student trying to predict **tomorrow's mood** ($Y_t$) based on the moods of the past few days. Your mood doesn't appear out of nowhere — it is heavily influenced by what happened recently. Suppose your mood follows a simple rule:

> *"My tomorrow's mood = 0.6 × today's mood + 0.2 × yesterday's mood + a small random surprise of the day."*

This is exactly what an **AR(2) model** does. The "random surprise" is the white noise $\varepsilon_t$ (an unexpected event — good news, bad news, a random compliment). The coefficients $\phi_1 = 0.6$ and $\phi_2 = 0.2$ are weights that tell us how much each past mood contributes.

**Financial Interpretation:** In financial time series, $Y_t$ could be the **log-return** of a stock, and the AR model captures the **serial dependence / momentum effect** — i.e., whether past returns carry predictive information about future returns.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Word "Auto-Regressive":** The prefix "Auto" means *self*. The model regresses $Y_t$ on **itself** (its own past), as opposed to a regression on external independent variables (which would be a regression model, not an autoregressive one).

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Parameters

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Range / Standard Value |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $p$ | Order of the AR process | Non-negative integer ($p \geq 1$) |
| $\phi_i$ | AR coefficients | Real numbers (must satisfy stationarity) |
| $\sigma_\varepsilon^2$ | White noise variance | Strictly positive ($\sigma_\varepsilon^2 > 0$) |
| $E(Y_t)$ | Unconditional mean of $Y_t$ | Depends on $c$ and the $\phi_i$'s |
| $\rho_k$ | Autocorrelation at lag $k$ | $\vert \rho_k \vert \leq 1$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The AR(1) Model — Foundational Case

The simplest AR model is the **AR(1)** process:

$$Y_t = c + \phi_1 Y_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t$$

#### Step-by-Step Logical Analysis

1. **Mean of AR(1):** Assuming a stationary process exists, take expectations on both sides:
   - $E(Y_t) = c + \phi_1 E(Y_{t-1}) + E(\varepsilon_t)$
   - By stationarity, $E(Y_t) = E(Y_{t-1}) = \mu$
   - So $\mu = c + \phi_1 \mu \implies \mu = \dfrac{c}{1 - \phi_1}$ (provided $\phi_1 \neq 1$).

2. **Variance of AR(1):** Subtract the mean and use $Var(\varepsilon_t) = \sigma_\varepsilon^2$:
   - $Var(Y_t) = \phi_1^2 Var(Y_{t-1}) + \sigma_\varepsilon^2$
   - By stationarity, $Var(Y_t) = Var(Y_{t-1}) = \gamma_0$
   - So $\gamma_0(1 - \phi_1^2) = \sigma_\varepsilon^2 \implies \gamma_0 = \dfrac{\sigma_\varepsilon^2}{1 - \phi_1^2}$.

3. **Autocorrelation Function (ACF) of AR(1):** The ACF decays **geometrically (exponentially)**:
   - $\rho_k = \phi_1^k$ for $k = 0, 1, 2, \dots$

4. **Stationarity Condition:** For the variance $\gamma_0$ to be **finite and positive**, we need:
   - $\vert \phi_1 \vert < 1$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why $\vert \phi_1 \vert < 1$?** If $\vert \phi_1 \vert \geq 1$, the variance $\gamma_0$ either becomes infinite (random walk / explosive case) or the process is non-stationary. This is the **causality / stationarity condition** for AR(1).

### 2.2 The AR(2) Model — Second-Order Case

The AR(2) model is the most commonly tested higher-order case:

$$Y_t = c + \phi_1 Y_{t-1} + \phi_2 Y_{t-2} + \varepsilon_t$$

**Yule–Walker Equations** for AR(2):

$$\rho_1 = \dfrac{\phi_1}{1 - \phi_2}$$

$$\rho_2 = \phi_1 \rho_1 + \phi_2 = \phi_2 + \dfrac{\phi_1^2}{1 - \phi_2}$$

For $k \geq 2$, the ACF follows the **homogeneous difference equation**:

$$\rho_k = \phi_1 \rho_{k-1} + \phi_2 \rho_{k-2}$$

**Stationarity Conditions for AR(2):** The roots of the characteristic polynomial $1 - \phi_1 z - \phi_2 z^2 = 0$ must lie **outside the unit circle**. In coefficient form:

- $\phi_1 + \phi_2 < 1$
- $\phi_2 - \phi_1 < 1$
- $\vert \phi_2 \vert < 1$

### 2.3 General AR(p) — The Yule–Walker System

For a general AR(p) model with zero mean, the **Yule–Walker equations** are:

$$\gamma_k = \phi_1 \gamma_{k-1} + \phi_2 \gamma_{k-2} + \dots + \phi_p \gamma_{k-p} \quad \text{for } k \geq 1$$

Dividing by $\gamma_0$:

$$\rho_k = \phi_1 \rho_{k-1} + \phi_2 \rho_{k-2} + \dots + \phi_p \rho_{k-p} \quad \text{for } k \geq 1$$

with initial conditions $\rho_0 = 1$ and $\rho_{-k} = \rho_k$.

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!NOTE]
> **Table Note:** The symbol `\vert` is used instead of $\vert$ to keep the markdown table rendering intact.

| Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Conditions / Remarks |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| AR(p) General Form | $Y_t = c + \sum_{i=1}^{p} \phi_i Y_{t-i} + \varepsilon_t$ | $\varepsilon_t \sim WN(0, \sigma_\varepsilon^2)$ |
| Backshift Form | $\Phi(B) Y_t = c + \varepsilon_t$ | $\Phi(B) = 1 - \phi_1 B - \dots - \phi_p B^p$ |
| AR(1) Mean | $\mu = \dfrac{c}{1 - \phi_1}$ | Requires $\vert \phi_1 \vert < 1$ |
| AR(1) Variance | $\gamma_0 = \dfrac{\sigma_\varepsilon^2}{1 - \phi_1^2}$ | Requires $\vert \phi_1 \vert < 1$ |
| AR(1) ACF | $\rho_k = \phi_1^{\,k}$ | Geometric decay |
| AR(2) Stationarity | $\phi_1 + \phi_2 < 1,\; \phi_2 - \phi_1 < 1,\; \vert \phi_2 \vert < 1$ | All roots of char. poly outside unit circle |
| Yule–Walker (General) | $\gamma_k = \sum_{i=1}^{p} \phi_i \gamma_{k-i} + \sigma_\varepsilon^2 \mathbf{1}_{\{k=0\}}$ | $k \geq 1$ |
| PACF of AR(p) | $\phi_{kk} = \phi_k$ for $k \leq p$, else $0$ | **Cuts off after lag $p$** |
| ACF of AR(p) | Tails off geometrically / sinusoidally | Does not cut off |
| AIC | $AIC = -2 \ln(L) + 2k$ | Lower AIC $\Rightarrow$ better model |
| BIC | $BIC = -2 \ln(L) + k \ln(n)$ | Penalizes complexity more than AIC |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering & Financial Utility

- **Stock Return Forecasting:** Predicting short-term equity returns based on lagged returns.
- **Volatility / Risk Modelling:** Foundation for ARCH/GARCH models (volatility clustering).
- **Signal Processing:** AR models are used in **speech coding** (LPC — Linear Predictive Coding) and **noise filtering**.
- **Econometrics:** Modelling GDP, inflation, and interest rate dynamics.
- **Machine Learning Predecessor:** AR models are the statistical ancestors of **RNNs (Recurrent Neural Networks)** — both rely on lagged self-regression.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Derivation — Mean of AR(1)

We start with the model:

$$Y_t = c + \phi_1 Y_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t$$

**Step 1: Take the expectation operator on both sides.**

$$E(Y_t) = E(c) + E(\phi_1 Y_{t-1}) + E(\varepsilon_t)$$

**Step 2: Use the linearity of expectation and the fact that $\phi_1$ is a constant.**

$$E(Y_t) = c + \phi_1 E(Y_{t-1}) + E(\varepsilon_t)$$

**Step 3: Substitute $E(\varepsilon_t) = 0$ (zero-mean white noise).**

$$E(Y_t) = c + \phi_1 E(Y_{t-1})$$

**Step 4: Apply the stationarity assumption. If the process is weakly stationary, the mean is constant over time.**

$$E(Y_t) = E(Y_{t-1}) = \mu$$

**Step 5: Substitute and solve the resulting algebraic equation.**

$$\mu = c + \phi_1 \mu \implies \mu - \phi_1 \mu = c \implies \mu(1 - \phi_1) = c \implies \mu = \frac{c}{1 - \phi_1}$$

**Conclusion:** The mean of a stationary AR(1) process is $\mu = \dfrac{c}{1 - \phi_1}$, valid only when $\vert \phi_1 \vert < 1$.

---

### 3.2 Detailed Derivation — Variance of AR(1)

To remove the intercept, define a **mean-centered** variable $Z_t = Y_t - \mu$. Then:

$$Z_t = \phi_1 Z_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t$$

**Step 1: Square both sides.**

$$Z_t^2 = \phi_1^2 Z_{t-1}^2 + 2 \phi_1 Z_{t-1} \varepsilon_t + \varepsilon_t^2$$

**Step 2: Take expectations.**

$$E(Z_t^2) = \phi_1^2 E(Z_{t-1}^2) + 2 \phi_1 E(Z_{t-1} \varepsilon_t) + E(\varepsilon_t^2)$$

**Step 3: Use three facts:**
- $E(Z_t^2) = E(Z_{t-1}^2) = \gamma_0$ (stationarity).
- $E(Z_{t-1} \varepsilon_t) = 0$ (white noise is uncorrelated with past observations).
- $E(\varepsilon_t^2) = \sigma_\varepsilon^2$.

**Step 4: Substitute and solve.**

$$\gamma_0 = \phi_1^2 \gamma_0 + \sigma_\varepsilon^2 \implies \gamma_0(1 - \phi_1^2) = \sigma_\varepsilon^2 \implies \gamma_0 = \frac{\sigma_\varepsilon^2}{1 - \phi_1^2}$$

**Conclusion:** The variance of a stationary AR(1) process is $\gamma_0 = \dfrac{\sigma_\varepsilon^2}{1 - \phi_1^2}$. Notice that if $\vert \phi_1 \vert \to 1$, the variance $\to \infty$ (non-stationary random walk).

---

### 3.3 Detailed Derivation — Autocorrelation Function of AR(1)

The **autocovariance at lag $k$** is defined as $\gamma_k = E[(Y_t - \mu)(Y_{t-k} - \mu)] = E(Z_t Z_{t-k})$.

**Step 1: Use the recursive form $Z_t = \phi_1 Z_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t$.**

$$\gamma_k = E(Z_t Z_{t-k}) = E[(\phi_1 Z_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t) Z_{t-k}]$$

**Step 2: Distribute.**

$$\gamma_k = \phi_1 E(Z_{t-1} Z_{t-k}) + E(\varepsilon_t Z_{t-k})$$

**Step 3: Recognize that $E(Z_{t-1} Z_{t-k}) = \gamma_{k-1}$ and $E(\varepsilon_t Z_{t-k}) = 0$.**

$$\gamma_k = \phi_1 \gamma_{k-1}$$

**Step 4: Solve the recursion (with base case $\gamma_0$ known).**

$$\gamma_k = \phi_1^k \gamma_0$$

**Step 5: Normalize to obtain the ACF.**

$$\rho_k = \frac{\gamma_k}{\gamma_0} = \phi_1^{\,k}$$

**Conclusion:** The ACF of an AR(1) process decays geometrically as $\rho_k = \phi_1^{\,k}$. The decay is **exponential** with rate determined by $\vert \phi_1 \vert$.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Side-by-Side Comparison of AR(1) and AR(2)

| Property | AR(1) | AR(2) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Model Equation | $Y_t = c + \phi_1 Y_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t$ | $Y_t = c + \phi_1 Y_{t-1} + \phi_2 Y_{t-2} + \varepsilon_t$ |
| Mean | $\mu = \dfrac{c}{1 - \phi_1}$ | $\mu = \dfrac{c}{1 - \phi_1 - \phi_2}$ |
| Variance | $\gamma_0 = \dfrac{\sigma_\varepsilon^2}{1 - \phi_1^2}$ | $\gamma_0 = \dfrac{(1 - \phi_2) \sigma_\varepsilon^2}{(1 + \phi_2)\big[(1 - \phi_2)^2 - \phi_1^2\big]}$ |
| ACF Form | Geometric decay: $\rho_k = \phi_1^{\,k}$ | Damped sinusoidal / mixed decay |
| Stationarity | $\vert \phi_1 \vert < 1$ | $\phi_1 + \phi_2 < 1,\; \phi_2 - \phi_1 < 1,\; \vert \phi_2 \vert < 1$ |
| PACF Behaviour | Cuts off after lag 1 | Cuts off after lag 2 |

---

### 3.5 Yule–Walker Estimation (Method of Moments)

Given observations $Y_1, Y_2, \dots, Y_n$, we replace the theoretical autocovariances $\gamma_k$ with their sample counterparts $\hat{\gamma}_k$:

$$\hat{\gamma}_k = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{t=k+1}^{n} (Y_t - \bar{Y})(Y_{t-k} - \bar{Y})$$

The Yule–Walker estimates $\hat{\phi}_1, \hat{\phi}_2, \dots, \hat{\phi}_p$ are obtained by solving the linear system:

$$\begin{aligned}
\hat{\gamma}_1 &= \hat{\phi}_1 \hat{\gamma}_0 + \hat{\phi}_2 \hat{\gamma}_1 + \dots + \hat{\phi}_p \hat{\gamma}_{p-1} \\
\hat{\gamma}_2 &= \hat{\phi}_1 \hat{\gamma}_1 + \hat{\phi}_2 \hat{\gamma}_0 + \dots + \hat{\phi}_p \hat{\gamma}_{p-2} \\
&\;\;\vdots \\
\hat{\gamma}_p &= \hat{\phi}_1 \hat{\gamma}_{p-1} + \hat{\phi}_2 \hat{\gamma}_{p-2} + \dots + \hat{\phi}_p \hat{\gamma}_0
\end{aligned}$$

In matrix form: $\hat{\boldsymbol{\Gamma}} \hat{\boldsymbol{\phi}} = \hat{\boldsymbol{\gamma}}$, which yields $\hat{\boldsymbol{\phi}} = \hat{\boldsymbol{\Gamma}}^{-1} \hat{\boldsymbol{\gamma}}$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an AR(p) Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input: Past Observations Yt-1, Yt-2, ..., Yt-p] --> B[Weighting by phi1, phi2, ..., phip]
    B --> C[Summation Node: c + sum]
    C --> D[Add White Noise epsilon_t]
    D --> E[Output: Current Observation Yt]
    E --> F[Shift / Delay Block B]
    F --> A
    G[Side: Mean mu = c / 1 minus phi1 ... phip] -.-> C
```

### 4.2 Workflow for AR Model Identification and Fitting

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start: Collect Stationary Time Series Data] --> S2[Step 1: Plot ACF and PACF]
    S2 --> S3{Identify Order p from PACF cutoff}
    S3 --> S4[Step 2: Fit ARp Model using Yule-Walker or OLS]
    S4 --> S5[Step 3: Check Stationarity Roots of Char. Poly]
    S5 --> S6{All roots outside unit circle?}
    S6 -->|No| S7[Refine Model: Re-estimate or Reduce Order]
    S7 --> S4
    S6 -->|Yes| S8[Step 4: Residual Diagnostics Ljung-Box Test]
    S8 --> S9{Residuals are White Noise?}
    S9 -->|No| S10[Re-evaluate Model Order or Consider ARMA]
    S10 --> S4
    S9 -->|Yes| S11[Step 5: Model Accepted for Forecasting]
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Diagram — Stationarity Triangle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    StationaryAR["STATIONARY AR Process"] --> A1[ACF Tails Off Geometrically]
    StationaryAR --> A2[PACF Cuts Off After Lag p]
    StationaryAR --> A3[Roots of Char. Poly Outside Unit Circle]
    NonStat["NON STATIONARY Case"] --> B1[Variance is Infinite]
    NonStat --> B2[Random Walk Behavior]
    NonStat --> B3[Unit Root Present]
    A1 --> Example["Example: phi1 equals 0.5 AR1 Decays Fast"]
    A2 --> Example
    A3 --> Example
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**  
*Define an Autoregressive (AR) model of order $p$. State the stationarity condition for an AR(1) process.*

**Model Answer:**

An **Autoregressive model of order $p$**, denoted **AR(p)**, is a linear time series model that expresses the current observation $Y_t$ as a linear combination of its own $p$ past values and a white noise error term:

$$Y_t = c + \phi_1 Y_{t-1} + \phi_2 Y_{t-2} + \dots + \phi_p Y_{t-p} + \varepsilon_t$$

where $\varepsilon_t \sim WN(0, \sigma_\varepsilon^2)$ and $\phi_1, \dots, \phi_p$ are real-valued autoregressive parameters.

**Stationarity Condition for AR(1):** For the AR(1) process $Y_t = c + \phi_1 Y_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t$, weak stationarity requires the autoregressive coefficient to satisfy $\vert \phi_1 \vert < 1$. Equivalently, the root of the characteristic polynomial $1 - \phi_1 z = 0$ must lie **outside the unit circle** (i.e., $z = 1/\phi_1$ with $\vert z \vert > 1$).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**  
*Distinguish between the ACF and PACF behaviour of an AR(p) process.*

**Model Answer:**

- **ACF (Autocorrelation Function):** For a stationary AR(p) process, the ACF **tails off** gradually, exhibiting a geometric or damped sinusoidal decay pattern. It does **not** cut off at any specific lag.
- **PACF (Partial Autocorrelation Function):** The PACF of an AR(p) process **cuts off sharply** after lag $p$. That is, $\phi_{kk} = \phi_k$ for $k \leq p$, and $\phi_{kk} = 0$ for $k > p$.

This contrasting behaviour is the **cornerstone of Box–Jenkins identification**: the PACF cutoff lag is used to determine the order $p$ of the AR model.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice Rule (KTU 2024 ESE):** Students must attempt **one** of the two questions. Both questions are designed to have sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — June 2023, Adapted]*

**(a)** Consider the AR(1) process $Y_t = 5 + 0.7 Y_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t$ where $\varepsilon_t \sim WN(0, 4)$. Compute:
1. The mean of the process.
2. The variance of the process.
3. The autocovariances $\gamma_0, \gamma_1, \gamma_2$.

**(7 Marks)**

**Solution:**

**1. Mean:** Using $\mu = \dfrac{c}{1 - \phi_1} = \dfrac{5}{1 - 0.7} = \dfrac{5}{0.3} = \dfrac{50}{3} \approx 16.667$.

**2. Variance:** Using $\gamma_0 = \dfrac{\sigma_\varepsilon^2}{1 - \phi_1^2} = \dfrac{4}{1 - 0.49} = \dfrac{4}{0.51} = \dfrac{400}{51} \approx 7.843$.

**3. Autocovariances:** Using the recursion $\gamma_k = \phi_1 \gamma_{k-1}$:
- $\gamma_0 = \dfrac{400}{51} \approx 7.843$.
- $\gamma_1 = \phi_1 \gamma_0 = 0.7 \times \dfrac{400}{51} = \dfrac{280}{51} \approx 5.490$.
- $\gamma_2 = \phi_1 \gamma_1 = 0.7 \times \dfrac{280}{51} = \dfrac{196}{51} \approx 3.843$.

---

**(b)** For an AR(2) process $Y_t = 2 + 0.5 Y_{t-1} - 0.3 Y_{t-2} + \varepsilon_t$:
1. Verify the stationarity conditions.
2. Compute the first three autocorrelations $\rho_1, \rho_2, \rho_3$ using the Yule–Walker equations.

**(7 Marks)**

**Solution:**

**1. Stationarity Conditions:** With $\phi_1 = 0.5$ and $\phi_2 = -0.3$:
- $\phi_1 + \phi_2 = 0.5 + (-0.3) = 0.2 < 1$ ✓
- $\phi_2 - \phi_1 = -0.3 - 0.5 = -0.8 < 1$ ✓
- $\vert \phi_2 \vert = 0.3 < 1$ ✓

All three conditions are satisfied; the process is **stationary**.

**2. Yule–Walker Computations:**

Using $\rho_1 = \dfrac{\phi_1}{1 - \phi_2} = \dfrac{0.5}{1 - (-0.3)} = \dfrac{0.5}{1.3} = \dfrac{5}{13} \approx 0.3846$.

Using $\rho_2 = \phi_1 \rho_1 + \phi_2 = (0.5)(0.3846) + (-0.3) = 0.1923 - 0.3 = -0.1077$.

For $k \geq 2$, $\rho_k = \phi_1 \rho_{k-1} + \phi_2 \rho_{k-2}$:
$\rho_3 = (0.5)(-0.1077) + (-0.3)(0.3846) = -0.0538 - 0.1154 = -0.1692$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Adapted]*

**(a)** Explain the concept of **Partial Autocorrelation Function (PACF)**. How is it useful in identifying the order of an AR model? Derive the PACF for an AR(1) process.

**(7 Marks)**

**Solution:**

The **PACF** measures the **direct** linear correlation between $Y_t$ and $Y_{t-k}$ after **removing the linear effects** of the intermediate observations $Y_{t-1}, Y_{t-2}, \dots, Y_{t-k+1}$.

**Identification of AR Order:** The PACF of an AR(p) process **cuts off sharply** after lag $p$. This is the primary diagnostic tool for selecting $p$ — the largest significant lag in the sample PACF plot indicates the AR order.

**Derivation of PACF for AR(1):** For AR(1), $Y_t = \phi_1 Y_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t$. Since $Y_t$ depends *only* on $Y_{t-1}$ (and no other lags):
- $\phi_{11} = \phi_1$ (the lag-1 PACF equals the AR(1) coefficient).
- $\phi_{kk} = 0$ for all $k \geq 2$ (cuts off after lag 1).

This is verified by noting that for $k \geq 2$, the partial correlation between $Y_t$ and $Y_{t-k}$ is zero because the linear influence is fully captured by the intermediate lag $Y_{t-1}$.

---

**(b)** Given observations from a stationary AR(2) process, the sample autocovariances are $\hat{\gamma}_0 = 5.0$, $\hat{\gamma}_1 = 3.5$, and $\hat{\gamma}_2 = 2.0$. Estimate the AR(2) coefficients $\hat{\phi}_1$ and $\hat{\phi}_2$ using the Yule–Walker method.

**(7 Marks)**

**Solution:**

The Yule–Walker equations for AR(2) (in terms of autocovariances) are:

$$\begin{aligned}
\hat{\gamma}_1 &= \hat{\phi}_1 \hat{\gamma}_0 + \hat{\phi}_2 \hat{\gamma}_1 \\
\hat{\gamma}_2 &= \hat{\phi}_1 \hat{\gamma}_1 + \hat{\phi}_2 \hat{\gamma}_0
\end{aligned}$$

**Substituting the values:**

$$\begin{aligned}
3.5 &= \hat{\phi}_1 (5.0) + \hat{\phi}_2 (3.5) \\
2.0 &= \hat{\phi}_1 (3.5) + \hat{\phi}_2 (5.0)
\end{aligned}$$

**Solving the system (multiply first equation by 5, second by 3.5):**

$$\begin{aligned}
17.5 &= 25.0 \hat{\phi}_1 + 17.5 \hat{\phi}_2 \\
7.0 &= 12.25 \hat{\phi}_1 + 17.5 \hat{\phi}_2
\end{aligned}$$

**Subtract the second from the first:**

$$10.5 = 12.75 \hat{\phi}_1 \implies \hat{\phi}_1 = \frac{10.5}{12.75} = \frac{14}{17} \approx 0.8235$$

**Substitute back into the first equation:**

$$3.5 = 0.8235 (5.0) + \hat{\phi}_2 (3.5) \implies 3.5 = 4.1176 + 3.5 \hat{\phi}_2$$
$$3.5 \hat{\phi}_2 = -0.6176 \implies \hat{\phi}_2 \approx -0.1765$$

**Result:** $\hat{\phi}_1 \approx 0.8235$, $\hat{\phi}_2 \approx -0.1765$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the stationarity check** before computing mean and variance. If $\vert \phi_1 \vert \geq 1$, the formulas **do not apply**. Examiners deduct **2 full marks** for this.
> 2. **Confusing ACF with PACF behaviour.** For an AR(p): **ACF tails off, PACF cuts off**. Mixing these up is a guaranteed loss of 1–2 marks in Part A.
> 3. **Sign errors in Yule–Walker recursions.** The recursion is $\rho_k = \phi_1 \rho_{k-1} + \phi_2 \rho_{k-2}$ — students often mistakenly write $\rho_k = \phi_1 \rho_{k-1} - \phi_2 \rho_{k-2}$ due to confusion with the model sign convention.
> 4. **Missing the Yule–Walker normal equation form** $\rho_1 = \dfrac{\phi_1}{1 - \phi_2}$ in 7-mark questions; full marks require using the correct closed-form.
> 5. **Forgetting to verify all three AR(2) stationarity conditions.** Only checking one or two is incomplete.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **AR(p) Form:** $Y_t = c + \sum_{i=1}^{p} \phi_i Y_{t-i} + \varepsilon_t$, with $\varepsilon_t \sim WN(0, \sigma_\varepsilon^2)$.
- **AR(1) Mean:** $\mu = \dfrac{c}{1 - \phi_1}$.
- **AR(1) Variance:** $\gamma_0 = \dfrac{\sigma_\varepsilon^2}{1 - \phi_1^2}$.
- **AR(1) ACF:** $\rho_k = \phi_1^{\,k}$ (geometric decay).
- **AR(1) Stationarity:** $\vert \phi_1 \vert < 1$.
- **AR(2) Stationarity:** $\phi_1 + \phi_2 < 1$, $\phi_2 - \phi_1 < 1$, $\vert \phi_2 \vert < 1$.
- **AR(2) ACF Recursion:** $\rho_k = \phi_1 \rho_{k-1} + \phi_2 \rho_{k-2}$ for $k \geq 2$.
- **Yule–Walker:** Fundamental recursive equations linking autocovariances to AR coefficients.
- **ACF of AR(p):** Tails off gradually (geometric or damped sinusoidal).
- **PACF of AR(p):** Cuts off sharply after lag $p$ — **this is the key identification tool**.
- **Estimation Methods:** Yule–Walker (method of moments), OLS, MLE (conditional least squares, exact MLE).
- **Order Selection:** AIC and BIC are used to balance goodness-of-fit vs. parsimony.
- **Operator Form:** $\Phi(B) Y_t = c + \varepsilon_t$ with $\Phi(B) = 1 - \phi_1 B - \dots - \phi_p B^p$.
- **Common Pitfall:** Always check **stationarity** before applying mean/variance formulas.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Moving Average Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET742 - FINANCIAL TIME SERIES ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Moving Average Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

A **Moving Average Model of order q**, denoted as **MA(q)**, is a linear time series model in which the current observation $X_t$ is expressed as a linear combination of the current white noise shock $\epsilon_t$ and a finite number $q$ of past white noise shocks $\epsilon_{t-1}, \epsilon_{t-2}, \dots, \epsilon_{t-q}$, together with a constant mean term $\mu$.

The general form of an **MA(q)** process is:

$$
X_t = \mu + \epsilon_t + \theta_1 \epsilon_{t-1} + \theta_2 \epsilon_{t-2} + \cdots + \theta_q \epsilon_{t-q}
$$

where:

- $X_t$ = value of the time series at time $t$ (e.g., daily stock return, exchange rate)
- $\mu$ = **constant mean** (level) of the process
- $\epsilon_t$ = white noise shock at time $t$, assumed to be **identically and independently distributed** with mean **0** and constant variance **$\sigma_{\epsilon}^2$**, i.e., $\epsilon_t \sim WN(0, \sigma_{\epsilon}^2)$
- $\theta_1, \theta_2, \dots, \theta_q$ = **moving average parameters** (real-valued coefficients) that govern the influence of past shocks on the present observation
- $q$ = order of the MA process (finite, non-negative integer)

The most elementary and widely examined special cases in the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus are:

$$
\text{MA(1):} \quad X_t = \mu + \epsilon_t + \theta_1 \epsilon_{t-1}
$$

$$
\text{MA(2):} \quad X_t = \mu + \epsilon_t + \theta_1 \epsilon_{t-1} + \theta_2 \epsilon_{t-2}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — MA(q) is ALWAYS Weakly Stationary.** Regardless of the values of $\theta_1, \dots, \theta_q$, any finite-order MA process is automatically (weakly) stationary because it is a finite linear combination of white noise terms with constant coefficients. This is a crucial contrast with the AR(p) model, where stationarity requires a parameter constraint.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **financial analyst tracking daily trading profits** of a retail trader. The trader's profit today ($X_t$) does **not** depend on yesterday's profit directly, but instead depends on **two things**:

1. **Today's market shock** ($\epsilon_t$): an unexpected event today (breaking news, sudden volatility).
2. **Yesterday's market shock** ($\theta_1 \epsilon_{t-1}$): an unexpected event yesterday that still "echoes" into today's outcome — perhaps because orders placed yesterday settle today.

So today's profit is essentially the **sum of today's surprise plus a delayed echo of yesterday's surprise**. The "echo" effect is captured by $\theta_1$. If $\theta_1 > 0$, the echo is in the same direction; if $\theta_1 < 0$, the echo is reversed (a kind of rebound).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Moving Average"?** Despite the name, it is **not** a simple average of past values of $X$. It is a *moving average of past shocks (errors)*. The terminology comes from the fact that the model can be rewritten as a linear filter applied to a moving sequence of white noise disturbances.

### 1.3 The White Noise Foundation

The entire MA framework rests on the **White Noise** process. Formally, $\{\epsilon_t\}$ is white noise if:

$$
E[\epsilon_t] = 0, \quad \text{Var}(\epsilon_t) = \sigma_{\epsilon}^2, \quad \text{and} \quad E[\epsilon_t \epsilon_s] = 0 \;\; \text{for} \;\; t \neq s
$$

Sometimes denoted compactly as $\epsilon_t \sim WN(0, \sigma_{\epsilon}^2)$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Strong White Noise (i.i.d.) vs. Weak White Noise:** KTU Module 1 typically uses the *weak* definition (mean zero, constant variance, zero autocorrelation). For derivations of ACF/ACVF, only the weak form is needed.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Breakdown of the MA(q) Process

Let us dissect the MA(q) model step-by-step to build intuition:

- **Step 1 — Random Shock Engine:** The process is driven entirely by an unobservable stream of white noise shocks $\{\epsilon_t\}$. These shocks are *innovations* — unpredictable new information arriving at each time step.

- **Step 2 — Finite Memory Filter:** Unlike AR models (which have infinite memory in theory), the MA model has **finite memory of exactly $q$ lags**. After $q$ periods, the effect of any shock completely vanishes. This is the defining structural property of MA processes.

- **Step 3 — The "Echo" Mechanism:** Each $\theta_k$ acts as a *gain* determining how strongly the shock $\epsilon_{t-k}$ continues to influence $X_t$. The further back the shock, the smaller (typically) its weighted contribution if the $\theta$'s decay.

- **Step 4 — Constant Mean Level:** The constant $\mu$ anchors the process around a stationary mean; without $\mu$, the mean would be zero.

- **Step 5 — Why MA(q) is Always Stationary:** Because $X_t$ is a *finite linear combination* of stationary (white noise) random variables with constant (time-invariant) coefficients, $E[X_t]$, $\text{Var}(X_t)$, and all autocovariances depend only on the lag $k$ and not on $t$. Hence MA(q) is **automatically weakly stationary** for any real values of $\theta_1, \dots, \theta_q$.

### 2.2 Mean of the MA(q) Process

Since $E[\epsilon_t] = 0$ for all $t$:

$$
E[X_t] = \mu + E[\epsilon_t] + \theta_1 E[\epsilon_{t-1}] + \cdots + \theta_q E[\epsilon_{t-q}] = \mu
$$

### 2.3 Variance of the MA(q) Process

Because the $\epsilon$'s are mutually uncorrelated (white noise), all cross-product terms vanish:

$$
\text{Var}(X_t) = \text{Var}(\epsilon_t) + \theta_1^2 \text{Var}(\epsilon_{t-1}) + \cdots + \theta_q^2 \text{Var}(\epsilon_{t-q}) = (1 + \theta_1^2 + \theta_2^2 + \cdots + \theta_q^2) \sigma_{\epsilon}^2
$$

### 2.4 Autocovariance Function (ACVF) — MA(1)

For the MA(1) process $X_t = \mu + \epsilon_t + \theta_1 \epsilon_{t-1}$:

- **Lag 0 (Variance):**

$$
\gamma(0) = \text{Var}(X_t) = (1 + \theta_1^2) \sigma_{\epsilon}^2
$$

- **Lag 1:**

$$
\gamma(1) = \text{Cov}(X_t, X_{t-1}) = E[(\epsilon_t + \theta_1 \epsilon_{t-1})(\epsilon_{t-1} + \theta_1 \epsilon_{t-2})] = \theta_1 \sigma_{\epsilon}^2
$$

- **Lag $k \geq 2$:** All cross-products involve at most one matching $\epsilon$ index; since $\epsilon$'s are uncorrelated across different time indices:

$$
\gamma(k) = 0 \quad \text{for} \quad k \geq 2
$$

### 2.5 Autocorrelation Function (ACF) — MA(1)

The ACF is the ACVF normalized by $\gamma(0)$:

$$
\rho(k) = \frac{\gamma(k)}{\gamma(0)}
$$

$$
\rho(0) = 1, \quad \rho(1) = \frac{\theta_1}{1 + \theta_1^2}, \quad \rho(k) = 0 \;\; \text{for} \;\; k \geq 2
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Truncation Property of MA(q) ACF:** The sample ACF of an MA(q) process *cuts off* (becomes exactly zero in theory) after lag $q$. This is the **diagnostic fingerprint** used in the Box-Jenkins identification stage to recognize an MA(q) process.

### 2.6 Invertibility — The "Second" Parameter Constraint

While MA(q) is *automatically* stationary, it is **not automatically invertible**. **Invertibility** is the property that allows the model to be rewritten as an infinite-order AR($\infty$) representation, expressing $\epsilon_t$ as a convergent infinite sum of past $X$'s. Without invertibility, the model has multiple mathematical representations pointing to the same ACF (a problem called *non-uniqueness*).

**MA(1) Invertibility Condition:** $|\theta_1| < 1$

**MA(2) Invertibility Conditions (from the characteristic equation):**

$$
\theta_2 + \theta_1 z + z^2 = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \text{roots must lie outside the unit circle}
$$

Equivalently:
- $\theta_2 + \theta_1 < 1$
- $\theta_2 - \theta_1 < 1$
- $\vert \theta_2 \vert < 1$

### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Quantity | MA(1): $X_t = \mu + \epsilon_t + \theta_1 \epsilon_{t-1}$ | MA(2): $X_t = \mu + \epsilon_t + \theta_1 \epsilon_{t-1} + \theta_2 \epsilon_{t-2}$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Mean $E[X_t]$ | $\mu$ | $\mu$ |
| Variance $\gamma(0)$ | $(1+\theta_1^2)\sigma_{\epsilon}^2$ | $(1+\theta_1^2+\theta_2^2)\sigma_{\epsilon}^2$ |
| $\gamma(1)$ | $\theta_1 \sigma_{\epsilon}^2$ | $(\theta_1 + \theta_1\theta_2)\sigma_{\epsilon}^2$ |
| $\gamma(2)$ | $0$ | $\theta_2 \sigma_{\epsilon}^2$ |
| $\gamma(k)$ for $k\geq 2$ / $k\geq 3$ | $0$ | $0$ for $k \geq 3$ |
| ACF $\rho(1)$ | $\dfrac{\theta_1}{1+\theta_1^2}$ | $\dfrac{\theta_1 + \theta_1\theta_2}{1+\theta_1^2+\theta_2^2}$ |
| ACF $\rho(2)$ | $0$ | $\dfrac{\theta_2}{1+\theta_1^2+\theta_2^2}$ |
| ACF $\rho(k)$ for large $k$ | $0$ for $k\geq 2$ | $0$ for $k\geq 3$ |
| Stationarity | Always | Always |
| Invertibility | $\vert \theta_1 \vert < 1$ | $\theta_2 + \theta_1 < 1$, $\theta_2 - \theta_1 < 1$, $\vert \theta_2 \vert < 1$ |
| ACF Behavior | Cuts off after lag 1 | Cuts off after lag 2 |
| PACF Behavior | Tails off (exponentially damped) | Tails off (may oscillate) |

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering / Financial Utility

- **Algorithmic Trading:** MA(q) models capture *short-memory momentum and reversal patterns* in high-frequency returns. After a market shock, the impact propagates for a few lags then dissipates — exactly the MA signature.
- **Risk Management (VaR Models):** The variance formula $\gamma(0) = (1+\sum \theta_i^2)\sigma^2$ is used to scale volatility estimates after shocks.
- **Signal Processing:** MA filters are the discrete-time analogue of *finite impulse response (FIR) filters* in electrical engineering, used in noise smoothing.
- **Demand Forecasting:** Manufacturing supply chains model demand shocks (promotions, supply disruptions) that decay within a small fixed window using MA(q).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Mean, Variance, and ACF of the MA(1) Process

Consider the canonical MA(1) process:

$$
X_t = \mu + \epsilon_t + \theta_1 \epsilon_{t-1}, \quad \epsilon_t \sim WN(0, \sigma_{\epsilon}^2)
$$

**Derivation of the Mean:**

Taking expectations on both sides and using linearity of expectation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E[X_t] &= E[\mu + \epsilon_t + \theta_1 \epsilon_{t-1}] \\
&= E[\mu] + E[\epsilon_t] + \theta_1 E[\epsilon_{t-1}] \\
&= \mu + 0 + \theta_1 \cdot 0 \\
&= \mu
\end{aligned}
$$

The mean is a constant $\mu$, independent of $t$. ✓

**Derivation of the Variance:**

Using $\text{Var}(a + bY + cZ) = b^2 \text{Var}(Y) + c^2 \text{Var}(Z) + 2bc \cdot \text{Cov}(Y,Z)$ when $a$ is constant and $Y, Z$ are uncorrelated:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Var}(X_t) &= \text{Var}(\mu + \epsilon_t + \theta_1 \epsilon_{t-1}) \\
&= \text{Var}(\epsilon_t) + \theta_1^2 \text{Var}(\epsilon_{t-1}) + 2\theta_1 \text{Cov}(\epsilon_t, \epsilon_{t-1}) \\
&= \sigma_{\epsilon}^2 + \theta_1^2 \sigma_{\epsilon}^2 + 2\theta_1 \cdot 0 \\
&= (1 + \theta_1^2) \sigma_{\epsilon}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

Since this depends only on the constant parameters and not on $t$, the variance is finite and time-invariant. ✓

**Derivation of the Autocovariance at Lag 1:**

By definition, $\gamma(1) = \text{Cov}(X_t, X_{t-1}) = E[(X_t - \mu)(X_{t-1} - \mu)]$.

First, compute $X_t - \mu = \epsilon_t + \theta_1 \epsilon_{t-1}$ and $X_{t-1} - \mu = \epsilon_{t-1} + \theta_1 \epsilon_{t-2}$.

Their product:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(X_t - \mu)(X_{t-1} - \mu) &= (\epsilon_t + \theta_1 \epsilon_{t-1})(\epsilon_{t-1} + \theta_1 \epsilon_{t-2}) \\
&= \epsilon_t \epsilon_{t-1} + \theta_1 \epsilon_t \epsilon_{t-2} + \theta_1 \epsilon_{t-1}^2 + \theta_1^2 \epsilon_{t-1} \epsilon_{t-2}
\end{aligned}
$$

Taking expectations (using $E[\epsilon_i \epsilon_j] = 0$ for $i \neq j$ and $E[\epsilon_i^2] = \sigma_{\epsilon}^2$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\gamma(1) &= E[\epsilon_t \epsilon_{t-1}] + \theta_1 E[\epsilon_t \epsilon_{t-2}] + \theta_1 E[\epsilon_{t-1}^2] + \theta_1^2 E[\epsilon_{t-1} \epsilon_{t-2}] \\
&= 0 + 0 + \theta_1 \sigma_{\epsilon}^2 + 0 \\
&= \theta_1 \sigma_{\epsilon}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Derivation of the Autocovariance at Lag 2:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(X_t - \mu)(X_{t-2} - \mu) &= (\epsilon_t + \theta_1 \epsilon_{t-1})(\epsilon_{t-2} + \theta_1 \epsilon_{t-3}) \\
&= \epsilon_t \epsilon_{t-2} + \theta_1 \epsilon_t \epsilon_{t-3} + \theta_1 \epsilon_{t-1} \epsilon_{t-2} + \theta_1^2 \epsilon_{t-1} \epsilon_{t-3}
\end{aligned}
$$

Every term involves a product of *different-time* $\epsilon$'s, which has expectation zero:

$$
\gamma(2) = 0 + 0 + 0 + 0 = 0
$$

**Derivation of the Autocorrelation Function:**

The ACF is $\rho(k) = \gamma(k)/\gamma(0)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho(1) &= \frac{\gamma(1)}{\gamma(0)} = \frac{\theta_1 \sigma_{\epsilon}^2}{(1+\theta_1^2)\sigma_{\epsilon}^2} = \frac{\theta_1}{1+\theta_1^2} \\
\rho(k) &= 0 \quad \text{for} \quad k \geq 2
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **truncation signature** of MA(1). ✓

### 3.2 Derivation: ACF of the MA(2) Process

For $X_t = \mu + \epsilon_t + \theta_1 \epsilon_{t-1} + \theta_2 \epsilon_{t-2}$:

**Variance:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Var}(X_t) &= \sigma_{\epsilon}^2 + \theta_1^2 \sigma_{\epsilon}^2 + \theta_2^2 \sigma_{\epsilon}^2 \\
&= (1 + \theta_1^2 + \theta_2^2)\sigma_{\epsilon}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Autocovariance at Lag 1:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(X_t - \mu)(X_{t-1} - \mu) &= (\epsilon_t + \theta_1 \epsilon_{t-1} + \theta_2 \epsilon_{t-2})(\epsilon_{t-1} + \theta_1 \epsilon_{t-2} + \theta_2 \epsilon_{t-3}) \\
&= \epsilon_t \epsilon_{t-1} + \theta_1 \epsilon_t \epsilon_{t-2} + \theta_2 \epsilon_t \epsilon_{t-3} \\
&\quad + \theta_1 \epsilon_{t-1}^2 + \theta_1^2 \epsilon_{t-1} \epsilon_{t-2} + \theta_1\theta_2 \epsilon_{t-1} \epsilon_{t-3} \\
&\quad + \theta_2 \epsilon_{t-1} \epsilon_{t-2} + \theta_1\theta_2 \epsilon_{t-2}^2 + \theta_2^2 \epsilon_{t-2}\epsilon_{t-3}
\end{aligned}
$$

Taking expectations — only squared terms survive:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\gamma(1) &= 0 + 0 + 0 + \theta_1 \sigma_{\epsilon}^2 + 0 + 0 + 0 + \theta_1 \theta_2 \sigma_{\epsilon}^2 + 0 \\
&= (\theta_1 + \theta_1 \theta_2)\sigma_{\epsilon}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Autocovariance at Lag 2:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(X_t - \mu)(X_{t-2} - \mu) &= (\epsilon_t + \theta_1 \epsilon_{t-1} + \theta_2 \epsilon_{t-2})(\epsilon_{t-2} + \theta_1 \epsilon_{t-3} + \theta_2 \epsilon_{t-4}) \\
&\Rightarrow \gamma(2) = \theta_2 \sigma_{\epsilon}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

(The only matching pair is $\theta_2 \epsilon_{t-2} \cdot \epsilon_{t-2}$.)

**Autocovariance at Lag $k \geq 3$:** All $\epsilon$ indices are disjoint, so $\gamma(k) = 0$.

**Final ACF Expressions for MA(2):**

$$
\rho(1) = \frac{\theta_1 + \theta_1 \theta_2}{1 + \theta_1^2 + \theta_2^2}, \quad \rho(2) = \frac{\theta_2}{1 + \theta_1^2 + \theta_2^2}, \quad \rho(k) = 0 \;\; \text{for} \;\; k \geq 3
$$

### 3.3 Derivation: Invertibility Condition for MA(1)

We seek to express $\epsilon_t$ as an infinite linear combination of past $X$'s:

$$
\epsilon_t = \sum_{j=0}^{\infty} \pi_j (X_{t-j} - \mu)
$$

For MA(1), the recursive inversion begins from $X_t - \mu = \epsilon_t + \theta_1 \epsilon_{t-1}$, hence:

$$
\epsilon_t = (X_t - \mu) - \theta_1 \epsilon_{t-1}
$$

Substituting $\epsilon_{t-1} = (X_{t-1} - \mu) - \theta_1 \epsilon_{t-2}$ recursively:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\epsilon_t &= (X_t - \mu) - \theta_1 \bigl[(X_{t-1} - \mu) - \theta_1 \epsilon_{t-2}\bigr] \\
&= (X_t - \mu) - \theta_1 (X_{t-1} - \mu) + \theta_1^2 \epsilon_{t-2} \\
&= (X_t - \mu) - \theta_1 (X_{t-1} - \mu) + \theta_1^2 (X_{t-2} - \mu) - \theta_1^3 \epsilon_{t-3} \\
&\;\;\vdots \\
&= \sum_{j=0}^{n} (-\theta_1)^j (X_{t-j} - \mu) + (-\theta_1)^{n+1} \epsilon_{t-n-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

For the infinite series $\sum_{j=0}^{\infty} (-\theta_1)^j$ to converge, we require:

$$
\vert \theta_1 \vert < 1
$$

This is the **invertibility condition**. ✓
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an MA(1) Process

The diagram below illustrates how a sequence of white noise shocks is filtered through the MA(1) coefficients to produce the observed time series $X_t$. Note the *finite memory window* — only the current shock and the immediately previous shock contribute to $X_t$.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeW["White Noise Source"] --> nodeEpsT["epsilon_t current shock"]
    nodeW --> nodeEpsTm1["epsilon_t-1 lag 1 shock"]
    nodeW --> nodeEpsTmq["epsilon_t-q lag q shock"]
    nodeEpsT --> nodeGainT["Gain 1"]
    nodeEpsTm1 --> nodeGain1["Gain theta_1"]
    nodeEpsTmq --> nodeGainQ["Gain theta_q"]
    nodeGainT --> nodeSum["Summation Block plus mu"]
    nodeGain1 --> nodeSum
    nodeGainQ --> nodeSum
    nodeSum --> nodeObs["Observed X_t output"]
    nodeEpsT -.->|fades after 1 step| nodeFade1[Memory Cutoff Lag 1]
    nodeEpsTm1 -.->|fades after 2 steps| nodeFade2[Memory Cutoff Lag 2]
    nodeEpsTmq -.->|fades after q steps| nodeFadeQ[Memory Cutoff Lag q]
```

### 4.2 Diagnostic Identification Flowchart for MA Models

This flowchart summarizes the Box-Jenkins identification procedure applied specifically to recognize MA(q) processes from sample data.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart["Observed Time Series Data"] --> nodeStation["Step 1 Test Stationarity ADF KPSS"]
    nodeStation -->|stationary| nodeACF["Step 2 Plot Sample ACF"]
    nodeStation -->|non stationary| nodeDiff["Apply Differencing"]
    nodeDiff --> nodeACF
    nodeACF --> nodeShape{"Step 3 ACF Cuts Off After Lag k"}
    nodeShape -->|Yes lag 1| nodeMA1["Conclude MA1 Model"]
    nodeShape -->|Yes lag 2| nodeMA2["Conclude MA2 Model"]
    nodeShape -->|Yes lag q| nodeMAq["Conclude MAq Model"]
    nodeShape -->|No tails off| nodeARorARMA["Consider AR or ARMA Model"]
    nodeMA1 --> nodePACF["Verify PACF Tails Off"]
    nodeMA2 --> nodePACF
    nodeMAq --> nodePACF
    nodePACF --> nodeInv{"Step 4 Check Invertibility"}
    nodeInv -->|theta within bounds| nodeEst["Step 5 Estimate Parameters MLE OLS"]
    nodeInv -->|violated| nodeFlip["Reflect Parameter Across Unit Circle"]
```

### 4.3 ACF vs PACF Behavior Schematic

The contrasting fingerprints of MA(q) and AR(p) processes are the most important diagnostic tool in Module 1.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph MAQ["MAq Model Fingerprint"]
        nodeMAQACF["ACF Cuts Off After Lag q"]
        nodeMAQPACF["PACF Tails Off Geometrically"]
    end
    subgraph ARP["ARp Model Fingerprint"]
        nodeARPACF["ACF Tails Off Geometrically"]
        nodeARPPACF["PACF Cuts Off After Lag p"]
    end
    nodeMAQACF --> nodeMAQTitle["Finite Memory of Shocks"]
    nodeMAQPACF --> nodeMAQTitle
    nodeARPACF --> nodeARPTitle["Infinite Memory of Past Values"]
    nodeARPPACF --> nodeARPTitle
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**Define the MA(q) model. State why every MA(q) process is weakly stationary.**

**Model Answer:**

A Moving Average model of order $q$, denoted MA(q), is a linear time series model that expresses the current observation $X_t$ as a linear combination of the current white noise shock and $q$ past shocks:

$$
X_t = \mu + \epsilon_t + \theta_1 \epsilon_{t-1} + \theta_2 \epsilon_{t-2} + \cdots + \theta_q \epsilon_{t-q}
$$

where $\epsilon_t \sim WN(0, \sigma_{\epsilon}^2)$.

Every MA(q) process is **weakly stationary** because:

- $E[X_t] = \mu$ (constant)
- $\text{Var}(X_t) = (1 + \theta_1^2 + \theta_2^2 + \cdots + \theta_q^2)\sigma_{\epsilon}^2$ (finite, time-invariant)
- $\gamma(k) = \text{Cov}(X_t, X_{t-k})$ depends only on the lag $k$ and not on $t$.

Since the model is a *finite* linear combination of uncorrelated white noise shocks with constant coefficients, all moment conditions for weak stationarity are automatically satisfied. **(3 Marks)**

---

#### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Explain the invertibility condition for an MA(1) process. Why is it important?**

**Model Answer:**

The **invertibility condition** for an MA(1) process $X_t = \mu + \epsilon_t + \theta_1 \epsilon_{t-1}$ requires:

$$
\vert \theta_1 \vert < 1
$$

This condition emerges from rewriting $\epsilon_t$ as an infinite past-weighted sum:

$$
\epsilon_t = \sum_{j=0}^{\infty} (-\theta_1)^j (X_{t-j} - \mu)
$$

The geometric series converges **if and only if** $\vert \theta_1 \vert < 1$.

**Importance:**

1. **Uniqueness:** It guarantees that the MA(1) representation of a given ACF is unique. Without invertibility, two different $\theta_1$ values (e.g., $\theta_1$ and $1/\theta_1$) yield the same ACF, creating ambiguity in interpretation.
2. **AR($\infty$) equivalence:** It allows the MA model to be expressed as an infinite-order AR process, useful for forecasting.
3. **Box-Jenkins convention:** KTU (and standard practice) mandates that fitted MA models must satisfy invertibility for unambiguous interpretation. **(3 Marks)**

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Forgetting to check invertibility after estimating MA parameters — losing **2 marks** in Part B derivations.
> - **Common Mistake #2:** Writing $\gamma(0) = \sigma_{\epsilon}^2$ for MA(1) instead of $(1+\theta_1^2)\sigma_{\epsilon}^2$ — a **2-mark deduction**.
> - **Common Mistake #3:** Claiming MA(q) ACF "tends to zero" instead of "cuts off exactly after lag $q$". Use precise language.
> - **Common Mistake #4:** Confusing MA with the simple moving average of past $X$ values — a fundamental conceptual error.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model]

**(a)** For the MA(1) process $X_t = \mu + \epsilon_t + \theta_1 \epsilon_{t-1}$ where $\epsilon_t \sim WN(0, \sigma_{\epsilon}^2)$, derive the **mean**, **variance**, and **autocovariance function** $\gamma(k)$ for $k = 0, 1, 2$. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** An MA(2) process is observed with parameters $\mu = 5$, $\theta_1 = 0.6$, $\theta_2 = 0.3$, and $\sigma_{\epsilon}^2 = 4$. Compute the **autocorrelation function** $\rho(1)$ and $\rho(2)$, and **verify invertibility**. **[7 Marks]**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation of moments of MA(1):**

**Mean:** Taking expectation on both sides:

$$
E[X_t] = \mu + E[\epsilon_t] + \theta_1 E[\epsilon_{t-1}] = \mu + 0 + 0 = \mu
$$

**Variance:** Expanding $\text{Var}(\epsilon_t + \theta_1 \epsilon_{t-1})$ — since $\epsilon_t$ and $\epsilon_{t-1}$ are uncorrelated, cross-term vanishes:

$$
\text{Var}(X_t) = \text{Var}(\epsilon_t) + \theta_1^2 \text{Var}(\epsilon_{t-1}) = \sigma_{\epsilon}^2 + \theta_1^2 \sigma_{\epsilon}^2 = (1 + \theta_1^2)\sigma_{\epsilon}^2
$$

**Autocovariance at Lag 1:** Compute $E[(X_t - \mu)(X_{t-1} - \mu)]$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(X_t - \mu)(X_{t-1} - \mu) &= (\epsilon_t + \theta_1 \epsilon_{t-1})(\epsilon_{t-1} + \theta_1 \epsilon_{t-2}) \\
&= \epsilon_t \epsilon_{t-1} + \theta_1 \epsilon_t \epsilon_{t-2} + \theta_1 \epsilon_{t-1}^2 + \theta_1^2 \epsilon_{t-1} \epsilon_{t-2}
\end{aligned}
$$

Taking expectations, only $\theta_1 \epsilon_{t-1}^2$ survives:

$$
\gamma(1) = \theta_1 \sigma_{\epsilon}^2
$$

**Autocovariance at Lag 2:**

$$
(X_t - \mu)(X_{t-2} - \mu) = (\epsilon_t + \theta_1 \epsilon_{t-1})(\epsilon_{t-2} + \theta_1 \epsilon_{t-3})
$$

All four product terms involve disjoint $\epsilon$ indices, so each has expectation zero:

$$
\gamma(2) = 0
$$

**Summary:** $\gamma(0) = (1+\theta_1^2)\sigma_{\epsilon}^2$, $\gamma(1) = \theta_1 \sigma_{\epsilon}^2$, $\gamma(2) = 0$. **[7 Marks]**

---

**(b) MA(2) Numerical Computation:**

Given: $\theta_1 = 0.6$, $\theta_2 = 0.3$, $\sigma_{\epsilon}^2 = 4$.

**Variance:**

$$
\gamma(0) = (1 + 0.6^2 + 0.3^2)(4) = (1 + 0.36 + 0.09)(4) = (1.45)(4) = 5.8
$$

**Autocovariance at Lag 1:**

$$
\gamma(1) = (\theta_1 + \theta_1 \theta_2)\sigma_{\epsilon}^2 = (0.6 + 0.6 \times 0.3)(4) = (0.6 + 0.18)(4) = (0.78)(4) = 3.12
$$

**Autocovariance at Lag 2:**

$$
\gamma(2) = \theta_2 \sigma_{\epsilon}^2 = 0.3 \times 4 = 1.2
$$

**Autocorrelations:**

$$
\rho(1) = \frac{\gamma(1)}{\gamma(0)} = \frac{3.12}{5.8} \approx 0.538
$$

$$
\rho(2) = \frac{\gamma(2)}{\gamma(0)} = \frac{1.2}{5.8} \approx 0.207
$$

**Invertibility Check:**

- Condition 1: $\theta_2 + \theta_1 = 0.3 + 0.6 = 0.9 < 1$ ✓
- Condition 2: $\theta_2 - \theta_1 = 0.3 - 0.6 = -0.3 < 1$ ✓
- Condition 3: $\vert \theta_2 \vert = 0.3 < 1$ ✓

All three invertibility conditions are satisfied. The MA(2) model is **invertible**. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]

**(a)** Compare MA(q) and AR(p) models with respect to their **autocorrelation function (ACF)** and **partial autocorrelation function (PACF)** behavior. Discuss why this contrast is essential for model identification in the Box-Jenkins methodology. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** For an MA(1) process $X_t = 10 + \epsilon_t + 0.8 \epsilon_{t-1}$, with $\epsilon_t \sim WN(0, 1)$:
  - (i) Compute $\rho(1)$.
  - (ii) State and verify the invertibility condition.
  - (iii) Determine the maximum and minimum possible values of $\rho(1)$ as $\theta_1$ varies over $\mathbb{R}$. **[7 Marks]**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Comparison of ACF and PACF for MA(q) vs AR(p):**

| Property | MA(q) Model | AR(p) Model |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| ACF Behavior | **Cuts off** (becomes exactly zero) after lag $q$ | **Tails off** gradually (geometric or sinusoidal decay) |
| PACF Behavior | **Tails off** gradually (exponentially damped) | **Cuts off** after lag $p$ |
| Memory Structure | Finite memory of past *shocks* | Infinite memory of past *values* |
| Stationarity | Automatic | Conditional (roots of AR char. poly outside unit circle) |
| Invertibility | Conditional (roots of MA char. poly outside unit circle) | Automatic |

**Why the contrast matters in Box-Jenkins identification:**

In the identification stage of Box-Jenkins methodology, the analyst computes the **sample ACF** and **sample PACF** from data. The diagnostic fingerprints above serve as *signatures*:

- If the sample ACF shows a sharp cutoff after lag $q$ and the PACF tails off → MA(q) is suggested.
- If the sample PACF shows a sharp cutoff after lag $p$ and the ACF tails off → AR(p) is suggested.

This pattern recognition is the cornerstone of model selection before parameter estimation. Without these distinguishing properties, MA and AR models could not be uniquely identified from data. **[7 Marks]**

---

**(b) Numerical MA(1) Analysis:**

Given: $X_t = 10 + \epsilon_t + 0.8 \epsilon_{t-1}$, $\sigma_{\epsilon}^2 = 1$.

**(i) Compute $\rho(1)$:**

Using the MA(1) formula:

$$
\rho(1) = \frac{\theta_1}{1 + \theta_1^2} = \frac{0.8}{1 + 0.64} = \frac{0.8}{1.64} \approx 0.4878
$$

**(ii) Invertibility:**

The invertibility condition for MA(1) is $\vert \theta_1 \vert < 1$.

Here $\theta_1 = 0.8$, so $\vert 0.8 \vert = 0.8 < 1$ ✓.

The model **is invertible**. ✓

**(iii) Maximum and Minimum of $\rho(1)$:**

We maximize the function $f(\theta_1) = \dfrac{\theta_1}{1 + \theta_1^2}$ over $\theta_1 \in \mathbb{R}$.

Differentiate with respect to $\theta_1$:

$$
\frac{df}{d\theta_1} = \frac{(1+\theta_1^2) - \theta_1(2\theta_1)}{(1+\theta_1^2)^2} = \frac{1 - \theta_1^2}{(1+\theta_1^2)^2}
$$

Setting the numerator to zero: $1 - \theta_1^2 = 0 \Rightarrow \theta_1 = \pm 1$.

By the second derivative test (or noting the sign change), $\theta_1 = +1$ gives a **maximum** and $\theta_1 = -1$ gives a **minimum**.

Evaluating at these points:

$$
f(1) = \frac{1}{1+1} = \frac{1}{2} = 0.5
$$

$$
f(-1) = \frac{-1}{1+1} = -\frac{1}{2} = -0.5
$$

**Maximum value of $\rho(1)$ is $0.5$ and minimum value is $-0.5$**, regardless of how large $\vert \theta_1 \vert$ becomes. This is a remarkable property: the autocorrelation of an MA(1) process is *bounded* between $-0.5$ and $+0.5$. **[7 Marks]**

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** MA(q) expresses $X_t$ as a linear combination of the *current* and *q past white noise shocks*, not past $X$ values.
- **Automatic Stationarity:** Every MA(q) is weakly stationary because it is a finite linear combination of stationary white noise shocks with constant coefficients. **No parameter constraint is needed.**
- **Variance Formula:** $\gamma(0) = (1 + \theta_1^2 + \theta_2^2 + \cdots + \theta_q^2)\sigma_{\epsilon}^2$.
- **ACF Truncation:** $\rho(k) = 0$ for $k > q$ — this is the **diagnostic fingerprint** of MA(q) processes.
- **PACF Tails Off:** Unlike ACF, the PACF decays gradually (often geometrically or with damped oscillations).
- **Invertibility:** For MA(1), $\vert \theta_1 \vert < 1$. For MA(2), three simultaneous conditions on $\theta_1, \theta_2$ must hold. Invertibility ensures unique representation.
- **Bounded Autocorrelation for MA(1):** $\rho(1) \in [-0.5, +0.5]$ for any real $\theta_1$. If you observe $\vert \hat{\rho}(1) \vert > 0.5$ in data, an MA(1) is **insufficient** — consider MA(2) or ARMA.
- **Identification vs AR:** ACF truncates for MA; PACF truncates for AR. This duality is the heart of Box-Jenkins identification.
- **Forecasting:** An MA(q) forecast uses only $q$ past residuals $\epsilon_{t-1}, \dots, \epsilon_{t-q}$ plus the expected future shocks (zero). Beyond $q$ steps ahead, the forecast collapses to the constant mean $\mu$.
- **White Noise Assumption:** $\epsilon_t \sim WN(0, \sigma_{\epsilon}^2)$ — zero mean, constant variance, *uncorrelated* across time. This is the foundation on which all MA(q) derivations rest.
- **Common Pitfall to Avoid:** Do not confuse *moving average model* with the *simple moving average smoother* (used in technical analysis). They are mathematically different concepts despite sharing the name.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: ARMA and ARIMA Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET742 - FINANCIAL TIME SERIES ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** ARMA and ARIMA Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**ARMA (AutoRegressive Moving Average)** and **ARIMA (AutoRegressive Integrated Moving Average)** are parametric linear time series models used to capture the stochastic dependency structure of a stationary (ARMA) or non-stationary (ARIMA) financial time series. The ARMA model combines two components: an **Autoregressive (AR)** part which regresses the current value on its past values, and a **Moving Average (MA)** part which models the current value as a function of past white noise error terms. The ARIMA model extends this by including an **Integration (I)** operator that applies differencing to convert a non-stationary series into a stationary one before fitting the ARMA structure.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PEIET742):** ARMA and ARIMA models form the foundational univariate parametric framework of **Box-Jenkins (1976)** methodology — the gold standard for stationary and trend-non-stationary financial series analysis.

### 1.2 The General ARMA(p, q) Model

An **ARMA(p, q)** process is defined as:

$$X_t = c + \phi_1 X_{t-1} + \phi_2 X_{t-2} + \dots + \phi_p X_{t-p} + \epsilon_t + \theta_1 \epsilon_{t-1} + \theta_2 \epsilon_{t-2} + \dots + \theta_q \epsilon_{t-q}$$

where $\epsilon_t \sim WN(0, \sigma^2)$ denotes **white noise**, $\phi_i$ are the **autoregressive coefficients**, $\theta_j$ are the **moving average coefficients**, $c$ is a constant term, and $p, q$ are the AR and MA orders respectively.

### 1.3 The General ARIMA(p, d, q) Model

$$(1-B)^d X_t = Y_t \sim \text{ARMA}(p, q)$$

where $B$ is the **backshift operator** defined by $B X_t = X_{t-1}$, and $d$ is the order of differencing required to achieve stationarity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction:** ARMA handles **stationary** series. If the series exhibits a unit root (non-stationary in mean or variance), we must first apply differencing $(1-B)^d$ and then fit ARMA — yielding ARIMA.

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Box — The "Memory and Surprises" Analogy**

Imagine you are tracking the daily closing price of a stock:
- The **AR (AutoRegressive)** part says: *"Tomorrow's price will partly depend on what it was yesterday and the day before — prices have memory."* The coefficient $\phi_1$ tells you how strongly yesterday influences today.
- The **MA (Moving Average)** part says: *"But there are also random shocks — surprises from yesterday, the day before, etc. — that still echo into today's price."* The coefficient $\theta_1$ tells you how strongly a past surprise still resonates.
- The **I (Integrated)** part handles trends. If prices drift upward like a random walk, we subtract yesterday from today (differencing) so the *changes* in price become predictable. Forecasting is then done on changes, and we add back (integrate) to recover prices.

So: **AR = memory of values, MA = memory of shocks, I = remove drift first.**

### 1.5 Standard Notation & Terminology

| Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|
| $X_t$ | Time series value at time $t$ |
| $\epsilon_t$ | White noise innovation (shock) at time $t$ |
| $B$ | Backshift operator: $B X_t = X_{t-1}$ |
| $\phi_i$ | AR coefficient at lag $i$ |
| $\theta_j$ | MA coefficient at lag $j$ |
| $p$ | Order of AR polynomial |
| $q$ | Order of MA polynomial |
| $d$ | Order of differencing |
| $\Phi(B)$ | AR polynomial: $1 - \phi_1 B - \phi_2 B^2 - \dots - \phi_p B^p$ |
| $\Theta(B)$ | MA polynomial: $1 + \theta_1 B + \theta_2 B^2 + \dots + \theta_q B^q$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Building Blocks — AR, MA, ARMA Decomposition

#### A. AR(p) Model — Pure Memory

$$X_t = c + \sum_{i=1}^{p} \phi_i X_{t-i} + \epsilon_t$$

In backshift form:

$$\Phi(B) X_t = c + \epsilon_t \quad \text{where} \quad \Phi(B) = 1 - \phi_1 B - \phi_2 B^2 - \dots - \phi_p B^p$$

> **Why AR works:** Today's value is a weighted average of its past. If $p=1$, then $X_t = c + \phi_1 X_{t-1} + \epsilon_t$ — a first-order Markov process.

#### B. MA(q) Model — Pure Shock Memory

$$X_t = c + \sum_{j=1}^{q} \theta_j \epsilon_{t-j} + \epsilon_t$$

In backshift form:

$$X_t = c + \Theta(B) \epsilon_t \quad \text{where} \quad \Theta(B) = 1 + \theta_1 B + \theta_2 B^2 + \dots + \theta_q B^q$$

> **Why MA works:** Today's value is today's shock plus echoes of past shocks. MA is always stationary.

#### C. ARMA(p, q) Model — Combined

$$\Phi(B) X_t = c + \Theta(B) \epsilon_t$$

### 2.2 Stationarity Condition

For ARMA to be **weakly (covariance) stationary**, all roots of the characteristic equation $\Phi(z) = 0$ must lie **outside the unit circle** in the complex plane.

For AR(1): $|\phi_1| < 1$

For AR(2): $|\phi_2| < 1$, $\phi_2 + \phi_1 < 1$, $\phi_1 - \phi_2 < 1$

### 2.3 Invertibility Condition

For ARMA to be **invertible** (expressible as infinite AR), all roots of $\Theta(z) = 0$ must lie **outside the unit circle**.

For MA(1): $|\theta_1| < 1$

### 2.4 Why ARIMA? — The Unit Root Problem

If a series has a unit root (e.g., $X_t = X_{t-1} + \epsilon_t$, a random walk), it is non-stationary. Differencing $d$ times removes the trend:

$$W_t = (1-B)^d X_t$$

Now $W_t$ is stationary and modeled as ARMA(p, q). Forecasts are made on $W_t$ and then **integrated** back to the original scale.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Condition | Expression | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| ARMA(p,q) general form | $\Phi(B) X_t = c + \Theta(B) \epsilon_t$ | Compact representation |
| AR polynomial | $\Phi(B) = 1 - \sum_{i=1}^{p} \phi_i B^i$ | Defines AR part |
| MA polynomial | $\Theta(B) = 1 + \sum_{j=1}^{q} \theta_j B^j$ | Defines MA part |
| Stationarity | All roots of $\Phi(z)=0$ outside unit circle | Ensures finite variance |
| Invertibility | All roots of $\Theta(z)=0$ outside unit circle | Ensures infinite AR representation |
| ARIMA backshift form | $\Phi(B)(1-B)^d X_t = \Theta(B) \epsilon_t$ | Handles non-stationarity |
| AR(1) variance | $\gamma_0 = \sigma^2 / (1 - \phi_1^2)$ | Long-run variance |
| AR(1) ACF | $\rho_k = \phi_1^k$ | Geometric decay |
| AR(2) ACF | $\rho_k = A \lambda_1^k + B \lambda_2^k$ | Damped sine/decay |
| MA(1) ACF | $\rho_1 = -\theta_1 / (1+\theta_1^2)$, $\rho_k = 0$ for $k \geq 2$ | Cuts off after lag $q$ |
| ARIMA differencing | $W_t = (1-B)^d X_t$ | Achieves stationarity |
| Yule-Walker | $\Gamma_p \phi = \gamma_p$ | Estimates AR coefficients |
| AIC | $-2 \ln(L) + 2k$ | Model selection |
| BIC | $-2 \ln(L) + k \ln(n)$ | Parsimonious selection |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ACF vs PACF Identification Rules (Board-Favorite Table):**

| Model | ACF | PACF |
|---|---|---|
| AR(p) | Tails off (geometric decay) | Cuts off after lag $p$ |
| MA(q) | Cuts off after lag $q$ | Tails off (geometric decay) |
| ARMA(p,q) | Tails off | Tails off |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Algorithmic Trading:** ARIMA-based strategies on FX, equity indices.
- **Risk Management:** Volatility and VaR forecasting via ARMA-GARCH hybrids.
- **Macroeconomic Forecasting:** GDP, inflation, interest rate projection.
- **Demand Forecasting:** Energy load, supply chain inventory.
- **Signal Processing:** Filtering noise in sensor arrays.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Deriving the Mean and Variance of AR(1)

**Setup:** $X_t = c + \phi_1 X_{t-1} + \epsilon_t$, with $\epsilon_t \sim WN(0, \sigma^2)$, and assume $|\phi_1| < 1$ (stationarity).

**Step 1: Take expectation of both sides.**

$$E[X_t] = E[c + \phi_1 X_{t-1} + \epsilon_t]$$

Since $c$ is constant, $\epsilon_t$ has mean zero, and stationarity implies $E[X_t] = E[X_{t-1}] = \mu$:

$$\mu = c + \phi_1 \mu + 0$$

**Step 2: Solve for the mean $\mu$.**

$$\mu - \phi_1 \mu = c \quad \Rightarrow \quad \mu (1 - \phi_1) = c$$

$$\mu = \frac{c}{1 - \phi_1}$$

**Step 3: Substitute the mean form back. Define $Y_t = X_t - \mu$ (mean-corrected series).**

$$X_t - \mu = \phi_1 (X_{t-1} - \mu) + \epsilon_t \quad \Rightarrow \quad Y_t = \phi_1 Y_{t-1} + \epsilon_t$$

**Step 4: Unroll the recursion repeatedly.**

$$Y_t = \epsilon_t + \phi_1 \epsilon_{t-1} + \phi_1^2 \epsilon_{t-2} + \phi_1^3 \epsilon_{t-3} + \dots$$

This is the **causal representation**: each $Y_t$ is an infinite weighted sum of past shocks.

**Step 5: Compute variance by squaring and using $\text{Var}(\epsilon_t) = \sigma^2$ and independence.**

$$\text{Var}(Y_t) = \sigma^2 (1 + \phi_1^2 + \phi_1^4 + \phi_1^6 + \dots)$$

**Step 6: Recognize the geometric series. The sum converges iff $|\phi_1| < 1$.**

$$\text{Var}(X_t) = \gamma_0 = \sigma^2 \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \phi_1^{2k} = \frac{\sigma^2}{1 - \phi_1^2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the rigorous proof that the AR(1) process is stationary only when $\vert \phi_1 \vert < 1$. Otherwise the variance diverges.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Autocorrelation Function (ACF) of AR(1)

**Setup:** From above, $Y_t = \phi_1 Y_{t-1} + \epsilon_t$. We want $\rho_k = \gamma_k / \gamma_0$.

**Step 1: Multiply both sides by $Y_{t-k}$ and take expectation.**

$$E[Y_t Y_{t-k}] = \phi_1 E[Y_{t-1} Y_{t-k}] + E[\epsilon_t Y_{t-k}]$$

**Step 2: For $k \geq 1$, $\epsilon_t$ is independent of $Y_{t-k}$ (causality), so the second term vanishes.**

$$\gamma_k = \phi_1 \gamma_{k-1} \quad \text{for } k \geq 1$$

**Step 3: Recursively substitute.**

$$\gamma_k = \phi_1^k \gamma_0$$

**Step 4: Divide by $\gamma_0$ to get the ACF.**

$$\rho_k = \phi_1^k \quad \text{for } k \geq 0$$

This is **geometric decay** — the hallmark ACF signature of an AR(1) process.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Deriving ACF of MA(1)

**Setup:** $X_t = \epsilon_t + \theta_1 \epsilon_{t-1}$.

**Step 1: Compute $\gamma_0 = E[X_t^2]$.**

$$\gamma_0 = E[(\epsilon_t + \theta_1 \epsilon_{t-1})^2] = \sigma^2 + \theta_1^2 \sigma^2 = \sigma^2 (1 + \theta_1^2)$$

**Step 2: Compute $\gamma_1 = E[X_t X_{t-1}]$.**

$$\gamma_1 = E[(\epsilon_t + \theta_1 \epsilon_{t-1})(\epsilon_{t-1} + \theta_1 \epsilon_{t-2})]$$

$$= E[\epsilon_t \epsilon_{t-1}] + \theta_1 E[\epsilon_{t-1}^2] + \theta_1 E[\epsilon_t \epsilon_{t-2}] + \theta_1^2 E[\epsilon_{t-1} \epsilon_{t-2}]$$

By white-noise independence, only the second term survives:

$$\gamma_1 = \theta_1 \sigma^2$$

**Step 3: Compute $\gamma_k$ for $k \geq 2$.**

No common shocks exist between $X_t$ and $X_{t-k}$ for $k \geq 2$, so:

$$\gamma_k = 0 \quad \text{for } k \geq 2$$

**Step 4: Form the ACF.**

$$\rho_1 = \frac{\gamma_1}{\gamma_0} = \frac{\theta_1}{1 + \theta_1^2}, \quad \rho_k = 0 \quad \text{for } k \geq 2$$

This is the **"cuts off after lag 1"** signature of MA(1) — a critical identification cue.

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Yule-Walker Equations for AR(p)

**Setup:** Multiply $X_t = \sum_{i=1}^{p} \phi_i X_{t-i} + \epsilon_t$ by $X_{t-k}$ and take expectation.

$$\gamma_k = \sum_{i=1}^{p} \phi_i \gamma_{k-i} \quad \text{for } k \geq 1$$

Divide by $\gamma_0$:

$$\rho_k = \sum_{i=1}^{p} \phi_i \rho_{k-i} \quad (k = 1, 2, \dots, p)$$

In matrix form:

$$\begin{pmatrix} \rho_1 \\ \rho_2 \\ \vdots \\ \rho_p \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & \rho_1 & \dots & \rho_{p-1} \\ \rho_1 & 1 & \dots & \rho_{p-2} \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ \rho_{p-1} & \rho_{p-2} & \dots & 1 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} \phi_1 \\ \phi_2 \\ \vdots \\ \phi_p \end{pmatrix}$$

Solving this Toeplitz system yields the AR(p) coefficients — the basis of the Yule-Walker estimation method.

### 3.5 Derivation 5 — Forecasting Equation for AR(1)

**Setup:** Given observations up to time $h$, the one-step-ahead forecast is:

$$\hat{X}_{h+1 \vert h} = E[X_{h+1} \vert X_h, X_{h-1}, \dots]$$

For AR(1):

$$\hat{X}_{h+1 \vert h} = c + \phi_1 X_h$$

Two-step-ahead:

$$\hat{X}_{h+2 \vert h} = c + \phi_1 \hat{X}_{h+1 \vert h} = c + \phi_1(c + \phi_1 X_h) = \frac{c}{1-\phi_1}(1 - \phi_1 + \phi_1^2) + \phi_1^2 X_h$$

As $h \to \infty$, the forecast converges to the unconditional mean $\mu = c/(1-\phi_1)$ — the **mean-reverting property** of stationary AR processes.

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Model Identification Quick Reference

| Step | Tool | What to Look For | Conclusion |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Plot series | Trend? Variance growth? | If yes → consider differencing |
| 2 | ADF / KPSS test | Unit root? | Decide $d$ |
| 3 | ACF plot | Cuts off or tails off? | Suggests MA or AR order |
| 4 | PACF plot | Cuts off or tails off? | Suggests AR or MA order |
| 5 | Fit candidates | AIC / BIC minimum | Choose best parsimonious model |
| 6 | Residual diagnostics | Ljung-Box on residuals | Check for white noise |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 ARMA Model Architecture — Block-Level Functional Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Observed Time Series Xt] --> B{Stationarity Test}
    B -- Yes --> C[Compute ACF]
    B -- No --> D[Apply Differencing d times]
    D --> E[Compute ACF of Wt]
    C --> F[Compute PACF]
    E --> F
    F --> G{Identify p and q}
    G --> H[Fit ARMA p q Model]
    H --> I[Estimate Parameters phi theta]
    I --> J{Diagnostic Check}
    J -- Pass --> K[Forecast Future Values]
    J -- Fail --> L[Re-specify Model]
    L --> G
```

### 4.2 ARIMA Process Workflow

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Raw Series Xt] --> B[Step 1: Identify d via ADF]
    B --> C[Step 2: Difference d times]
    C --> D[Step 3: Stationary Wt]
    D --> E[Step 4: Identify p via PACF]
    D --> F[Step 5: Identify q via ACF]
    E --> G[Step 6: Fit ARIMA p d q]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Step 7: Diagnostic Check]
    H --> I[Step 8: Forecast and Integrate Back]
```

### 4.3 Information Flow Inside an ARMA(p, q) Model

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Xt-1] --> M[phi1]
    B[Xt-2] --> N[phi2]
    C[Xt-p] --> O[phip]
    M --> SUM
    N --> SUM
    O --> SUM
    D[eps t-1] --> P[theta1]
    E[eps t-2] --> Q[theta2]
    F[eps t-q] --> R[thetaq]
    P --> SUM
    Q --> SUM
    R --> SUM
    SUM --> OUT[Xt]
    NOISE[eps t] --> SUM
```

### 4.4 Stationarity & Invertibility Region

```mermaid
graph TD
    R[Root z0 of Phi z equals 0] --> S{Location Test}
    S -- Outside Unit Circle --> STATIONARY[Stationary ARMA]
    S -- On Unit Circle --> NONSTAT[Non Stationary e g Random Walk]
    S -- Inside Unit Circle --> EXPLODE[Explosive Variance Diverges]
```

### 4.5 ACF and PACF Identification Pattern

```mermaid
graph TD
    ID[Identify Model Type] --> P{PACF Behavior}
    P -- Cuts off after lag p --> AR[AR p Model]
    P -- Tails off --> ACF{ACF Behavior}
    ACF -- Cuts off after lag q --> MA[MA q Model]
    ACF -- Tails off --> ARMA[ARMA p q Model]
```
