# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Unconditional Variance of GARCH(1, 1)

We start with the GARCH(1, 1) variance equation:

$$\sigma_t^2 = \omega + \alpha_1 \varepsilon_{t-1}^2 + \beta_1 \sigma_{t-1}^2$$

**Step 1:** Assume the process is *covariance stationary*. This means the unconditional variance exists, is finite, and time-invariant:

$$\text{Var}(r_t) = \text{Var}(\varepsilon_t) = \sigma^2 = \mathbb{E}[\varepsilon_t^2]$$

**Step 2:** Take the unconditional expectation $\mathbb{E}[\cdot]$ on both sides of the variance equation. By the **law of iterated expectations**, $\mathbb{E}[\sigma_t^2] = \sigma^2$ (since $\sigma_t^2$ is the conditional variance, its unconditional expectation equals the unconditional variance):

$$\mathbb{E}[\sigma_t^2] = \mathbb{E}[\omega] + \alpha_1 \mathbb{E}[\varepsilon_{t-1}^2] + \beta_1 \mathbb{E}[\sigma_{t-1}^2]$$

**Step 3:** Substitute the constant $\omega$ and use stationarity $\mathbb{E}[\varepsilon_{t-1}^2] = \mathbb{E}[\sigma_{t-1}^2] = \sigma^2$:

$$\sigma^2 = \omega + \alpha_1 \sigma^2 + \beta_1 \sigma^2$$

**Step 4:** Factor out $\sigma^2$ on the right-hand side:

$$\sigma^2 = \omega + (\alpha_1 + \beta_1) \sigma^2$$

**Step 5:** Move the $(\alpha_1 + \beta_1) \sigma^2$ term to the left side:

$$\sigma^2 - (\alpha_1 + \beta_1) \sigma^2 = \omega$$

**Step 6:** Factor $\sigma^2$ from the left side:

$$\sigma^2 [1 - (\alpha_1 + \beta_1)] = \omega$$

**Step 7:** Divide both sides by $[1 - (\alpha_1 + \beta_1)]$:

$$\sigma^2 = \frac{\omega}{1 - \alpha_1 - \beta_1}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** The unconditional (long-run) variance of a stationary GARCH(1, 1) process equals $\dfrac{\omega}{1 - \alpha_1 - \beta_1}$. This formula is the cornerstone of long-horizon volatility forecasting in finance.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Half-Life of a Volatility Shock

A "shock" in volatility means we observe a value of $\sigma_{t+1}^2$ that deviates from the long-run mean $\sigma^2$ by a margin. The question is: **how many periods $h$ does it take for the deviation to halve?**

**Step 1:** Define the deviation at time $t+1$ as:

$$\delta_{t+1} = \sigma_{t+1}^2 - \sigma^2$$

**Step 2:** Use the 1-step-ahead forecast formula. It can be shown (by repeated substitution) that the $h$-step-ahead forecast from a GARCH(1,1) model is:

$$\hat{\sigma}_{t+h}^2 = \sigma^2 + (\alpha_1 + \beta_1)^{h-1} (\sigma_{t+1}^2 - \sigma^2)$$

**Step 3:** The deviation $h$ steps ahead is therefore:

$$\delta_{t+h} = \hat{\sigma}_{t+h}^2 - \sigma^2 = (\alpha_1 + \beta_1)^{h-1} (\sigma_{t+1}^2 - \sigma^2)$$

**Step 4:** For the deviation to halve, we require:

$$(\alpha_1 + \beta_1)^{h-1} = \frac{1}{2}$$

**Step 5:** Take the natural logarithm of both sides:

$$(h - 1) \ln(\alpha_1 + \beta_1) = \ln(0.5) = -\ln(2)$$

**Step 6:** Solve for $h$:

$$h - 1 = \frac{-\ln(2)}{\ln(\alpha_1 + \beta_1)}$$

**Step 7:** Final expression:

$$h = 1 + \frac{\ln(0.5)}{\ln(\alpha_1 + \beta_1)}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Numerical Example:** If $\alpha_1 = 0.08$ and $\beta_1 = 0.90$, then $\alpha_1 + \beta_1 = 0.98$. The half-life is $h = 1 + \ln(0.5)/\ln(0.98) \approx 1 + (-0.693)/(-0.0202) \approx 1 + 34.3 \approx 35.3$ days. This means a volatility shock takes about **35 trading days** to halve — a key insight for risk managers.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — GARCH(1, 1) as an Infinite-Order ARCH

The **ARCH($\infty$) representation** of a GARCH(1, 1) reveals why GARCH is so parsimonious.

**Step 1:** Start with the GARCH(1, 1) variance equation:

$$\sigma_t^2 = \omega + \alpha_1 \varepsilon_{t-1}^2 + \beta_1 \sigma_{t-1}^2$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $\sigma_{t-1}^2$ recursively using the same equation shifted by one period:

$$\sigma_{t-1}^2 = \omega + \alpha_1 \varepsilon_{t-2}^2 + \beta_1 \sigma_{t-2}^2$$

**Step 3:** Plug this expression back into the original equation:

$$\sigma_t^2 = \omega + \alpha_1 \varepsilon_{t-1}^2 + \beta_1 (\omega + \alpha_1 \varepsilon_{t-2}^2 + \beta_1 \sigma_{t-2}^2)$$

$$\sigma_t^2 = \omega (1 + \beta_1) + \alpha_1 \varepsilon_{t-1}^2 + \alpha_1 \beta_1 \varepsilon_{t-2}^2 + \beta_1^2 \sigma_{t-2}^2$$

**Step 4:** Continue the recursion $k$ times. After $k$ iterations, we obtain:

$$\sigma_t^2 = \omega \sum_{i=0}^{k-1} \beta_1^i + \alpha_1 \sum_{i=0}^{k-1} \beta_1^i \, \varepsilon_{t-1-i}^2 + \beta_1^k \sigma_{t-k}^2$$

**Step 5:** As $k \to \infty$, since $\vert \beta_1 \vert < 1$, the last term $\beta_1^k \sigma_{t-k}^2 \to 0$. The geometric sum $\sum_{i=0}^{\infty} \beta_1^i = \frac{1}{1 - \beta_1}$.

**Step 6:** Final ARCH($\infty$) representation:

$$\sigma_t^2 = \frac{\omega}{1 - \beta_1} + \alpha_1 \sum_{i=0}^{\infty} \beta_1^i \, \varepsilon_{t-1-i}^2$$

This shows that GARCH(1, 1) is mathematically equivalent to an **ARCH($\infty$)** with geometrically decaying coefficients $\alpha_1 \beta_1^i$. A single GARCH(1, 1) replaces an infinite-ARCH specification with just **3 parameters**.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — 1-Step-Ahead Volatility Forecast

**Step 1:** At time $t$, we observe $r_t$ and hence compute the realized shock:

$$\hat{\varepsilon}_t = r_t - \hat{\mu}$$

**Step 2:** The information set at time $t$ is $\mathcal{F}_t = \{r_1, r_2, \ldots, r_t\}$. The 1-step-ahead forecast of $\sigma_{t+1}^2$ conditional on $\mathcal{F}_t$ is:

$$\hat{\sigma}_{t+1}^2 = \mathbb{E}[\sigma_{t+1}^2 \mid \mathcal{F}_t]$$

**Step 3:** Apply the conditional expectation to the variance equation. Note that $\varepsilon_t$ is known at time $t$, so $\varepsilon_t^2$ is in $\mathcal{F}_t$. Also $\sigma_t^2$ is in $\mathcal{F}_t$ by construction:

$$\hat{\sigma}_{t+1}^2 = \omega + \alpha_1 \varepsilon_t^2 + \beta_1 \sigma_t^2$$

**Step 4:** This is the operational forecasting equation used in practice. For $h$-step-ahead forecasts, iterate with decay:

$$\hat{\sigma}_{t+h}^2 = \hat{\sigma}^2 + (\alpha_1 + \beta_1)^{h-1} (\hat{\sigma}_{t+1}^2 - \hat{\sigma}^2)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **As $h \to \infty$:** $\hat{\sigma}_{t+h}^2 \to \hat{\sigma}^2 = \dfrac{\hat{\omega}}{1 - \hat{\alpha}_1 - \hat{\beta}_1}$, the unconditional variance. This is the **mean-reversion** property of stationary GARCH processes.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Step-by-Step Mapping

| Step | Mathematical Operation | Conceptual Meaning |
|------|----------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | Write variance equation | Define how variance evolves |
| 2 | Take unconditional expectation | Move from conditional to long-run |
| 3 | Use stationarity $\mathbb{E}[\sigma_t^2] = \sigma^2$ | Impose time-invariance |
| 4 | Collect terms | Algebraic simplification |
| 5 | Divide by $[1 - (\alpha_1 + \beta_1)]$ | Solve for long-run variance |
| 6 | Verify $\alpha_1 + \beta_1 < 1$ | Ensure denominator is positive |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 GARCH(1, 1) Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram depicts the information flow inside a GARCH(1, 1) model — how the current observation $r_t$ is decomposed into a mean component and a shock, and how the variance is recursively updated.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["INPUT: Return Series r_t"] --> node2["Subtract Mean mu"]
    node2 --> node3["Residual epsilon_t = r_t - mu"]
    node3 --> node4["Square Residual epsilon_t squared"]
    node4 --> node5["ARCH Term: alpha_1 times epsilon_t squared"]
    node3 --> node6["Compute Conditional SD sigma_t"]
    node6 --> node7["Square to get sigma_t squared"]
    node7 --> node8["GARCH Term: beta_1 times sigma_t squared"]
    node5 --> node9["Add Constant omega"]
    node8 --> node9
    node9 --> node10["OUTPUT: Conditional Variance sigma_t+1 squared"]
    node10 --> node11["Apply Persistence: alpha_1 + beta_1"]
    node11 --> node12["Compute Half-Life of Shock"]
    node12 --> node13["Compute Unconditional Variance: omega over 1 minus alpha_1 minus beta_1"]
    node13 --> node14["FINAL: Forecast sigma_t+h squared for h steps ahead"]
```

## 4.2 Two-Equation Model Structure (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph eq1["MEAN EQUATION"]
        a1["r_t"] --> a2["mu + epsilon_t"]
    end
    subgraph eq2["VARIANCE EQUATION"]
        b1["omega"] --> b4["sigma_t squared"]
        b2["ARCH: alpha_1 epsilon_t-1 squared"] --> b4
        b3["GARCH: beta_1 sigma_t-1 squared"] --> b4
    end
    a2 --> c1["Estimated by Maximum Likelihood"]
    b4 --> c1
    c1 --> d1["OUTPUT: Parameter Estimates mu, omega, alpha_1, beta_1"]
    d1 --> e1["Forecasting and Risk Management"]
```

## 4.3 Volatility Clustering Pattern (Stylized Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    phaseA["Period A: High Volatility Cluster"] --> shock1["Large Shock at time t-1"]
    shock1 --> vol1["sigma_t squared rises"]
    vol1 --> decay1["Decay governed by beta_1, usually slow"]
    decay1 --> phaseB["Period B: Return to Calm"]
    phaseB --> shock2["New small Shock at time t+k"]
    shock2 --> vol2["sigma_t+k squared slightly rises"]
    vol2 --> decay2["Fast return to baseline omega"]
```

## 4.4 Parameter Constraint and Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start["Start: Estimate GARCH(1,1) parameters"] --> check1["Check omega greater than 0"]
    check1 --> check2["Check alpha_1 greater than or equal to 0"]
    check2 --> check3["Check beta_1 greater than or equal to 0"]
    check3 --> check4["Check alpha_1 + beta_1 less than 1"]
    check4 -->|Yes| stationary["Stationary: Compute Long-Run Variance"]
    check4 -->|No| igarch["IGARCH: Unit Root in Variance"]
    stationary --> hl["Compute Half-Life using ln 0.5 over ln alpha_1 + beta_1"]
    igarch --> hl2["Volatility Shock is Permanent"]
    hl --> forecast["Use for VaR and Option Pricing"]
    hl2 --> forecast
```

> [!TIP]
> **How to read these diagrams in exams:** Use these as the basis for drawing 2-mark or 4-mark schematic answers. KTU examiners reward clean, labeled block diagrams that explicitly show **inputs**, **transformations**, and **outputs**.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
**Define the GARCH(1, 1) model. State the necessary and sufficient conditions on its parameters for the process to be covariance stationary.**

**Model Answer:**

The **GARCH(1, 1)** (Generalized Autoregressive Conditional Heteroscedasticity) model is specified by two equations:

**Mean Equation:**
$$r_t = \mu + \varepsilon_t$$

**Variance Equation:**
$$\sigma_t^2 = \omega + \alpha_1 \varepsilon_{t-1}^2 + \beta_1 \sigma_{t-1}^2$$

where $\omega$, $\alpha_1$, $\beta_1$ are parameters and $\varepsilon_t \mid \mathcal{F}_{t-1} \sim N(0, \sigma_t^2)$.

**Stationarity Conditions:**

1. $\omega > 0$ (ensures positive variance)
2. $\alpha_1 \geq 0$ (ensures non-negative ARCH contribution)
3. $\beta_1 \geq 0$ (ensures non-negative GARCH contribution)
4. $\alpha_1 + \beta_1 < 1$ (ensures finite unconditional variance)

Under these conditions, the unconditional (long-run) variance is $\sigma^2 = \dfrac{\omega}{1 - \alpha_1 - \beta_1}$. **(3 marks)**

---

### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**Explain the concept of "volatility persistence" in a GARCH(1, 1) process. What does the half-life of a volatility shock measure?**

**Model Answer:**

**Volatility Persistence** refers to the degree to which a current shock in variance continues to influence future variances. It is quantified by the sum $\alpha_1 + \beta_1$, often called the **persistence parameter** $\Phi$.

- If $\alpha_1 + \beta_1$ is **close to 1** (e.g., 0.98), shocks decay very slowly — high persistence.
- If $\alpha_1 + \beta_1$ is **close to 0** (e.g., 0.30), shocks decay rapidly — low persistence.

**Half-Life of a Shock** is the number of periods $h$ required for the deviation of conditional variance from its long-run mean to reduce by half. It is computed as:

$$h = 1 + \frac{\ln(0.5)}{\ln(\alpha_1 + \beta_1)}$$

For example, with $\alpha_1 + \beta_1 = 0.98$, the half-life is approximately 35 periods. This metric is critical for risk managers in setting **holding periods** for VaR calculations. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question has two sub-parts (a) and (b) of 7 marks each. The full 14-mark question presents an internal choice between **Question A** and **Question B**, both of which are answered independently.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**A time series analyst estimates a GARCH(1, 1) model on daily log-returns of a stock index and obtains the following estimated parameters:**
- $\hat{\omega} = 0.000002$, $\hat{\alpha}_1 = 0.05$, $\hat{\beta}_1 = 0.93$

**At the end of day $t-1$**, the conditional variance is $\hat{\sigma}_{t-1}^2 = 0.000625$, and the most recent squared residual is $\hat{\varepsilon}_{t-1}^2 = 0.000400$.

**Answer the following sub-parts:**

#### **(a)** Compute the 1-step-ahead forecast of conditional variance $\hat{\sigma}_t^2$ for day $t$. Also verify the stationarity condition. **(7 marks)**

**Solution:**

The GARCH(1, 1) variance equation is:

$$\hat{\sigma}_t^2 = \hat{\omega} + \hat{\alpha}_1 \hat{\varepsilon}_{t-1}^2 + \hat{\beta}_1 \hat{\sigma}_{t-1}^2$$

**Step 1:** Identify the given values.

- $\hat{\omega} = 0.000002$
- $\hat{\alpha}_1 = 0.05$
- $\hat{\beta}_1 = 0.93$
- $\hat{\varepsilon}_{t-1}^2 = 0.000400$
- $\hat{\sigma}_{t-1}^2 = 0.000625$

**Step 2:** Substitute into the variance equation.

$$\hat{\sigma}_t^2 = 0.000002 + (0.05)(0.000400) + (0.93)(0.000625)$$

**Step 3:** Compute each term numerically.

- ARCH term: $(0.05)(0.000400) = 0.0000200$
- GARCH term: $(0.93)(0.000625) = 0.00058125$

**Step 4:** Sum the three terms.

$$\hat{\sigma}_t^2 = 0.000002 + 0.0000200 + 0.00058125 = 0.00060325$$

**Step 5:** Stationarity check.

$$\hat{\alpha}_1 + \hat{\beta}_1 = 0.05 + 0.93 = 0.98 < 1 \quad \checkmark$$

The condition is satisfied, so the process is **covariance stationary**.

**Answer:** $\hat{\sigma}_t^2 \approx 0.00060325$, equivalent to a conditional standard deviation of $\hat{\sigma}_t \approx 0.02456$ (about **2.46%** daily volatility). **(7 marks)**

#### **(b)** Compute the unconditional (long-run) variance, the standard deviation, and the half-life of a volatility shock. Interpret the result. **(7 marks)**

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Unconditional variance formula.

$$\hat{\sigma}^2 = \frac{\hat{\omega}}{1 - \hat{\alpha}_1 - \hat{\beta}_1}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute values.

$$\hat{\sigma}^2 = \frac{0.000002}{1 - 0.05 - 0.93} = \frac{0.000002}{0.02} = 0.0001$$

**Step 3:** Unconditional standard deviation.

$$\hat{\sigma} = \sqrt{0.0001} = 0.01 = 1.00\%$$

**Step 4:** Half-life formula.

$$h = 1 + \frac{\ln(0.5)}{\ln(\hat{\alpha}_1 + \hat{\beta}_1)} = 1 + \frac{\ln(0.5)}{\ln(0.98)}$$

**Step 5:** Compute the logarithms.

- $\ln(0.5) \approx -0.6931$
- $\ln(0.98) \approx -0.0202$

**Step 6:** Divide and add 1.

$$h = 1 + \frac{-0.6931}{-0.0202} = 1 + 34.31 \approx 35.31 \text{ days}$$

**Step 7:** Interpretation.

The unconditional volatility of the index is about **1% per day** (≈ 15.87% annualized). A volatility shock takes approximately **35 trading days** to halve in impact. This indicates **very high persistence**, typical of equity index data. The current 1-step-ahead forecast $\hat{\sigma}_t^2 = 0.00060325$ is much higher than the long-run level of $0.0001$, implying the index is currently in a **turbulent regime** that will take many weeks to subside. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** Show that a GARCH(1, 1) process with parameters $\omega, \alpha_1, \beta_1$ satisfying $\alpha_1 + \beta_1 < 1$ can be written as an equivalent ARCH($\infty$) process. Hence explain why a low-order GARCH model is preferred over a high-order ARCH model. **(7 marks)**

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Write the GARCH(1, 1) variance equation.

$$\sigma_t^2 = \omega + \alpha_1 \varepsilon_{t-1}^2 + \beta_1 \sigma_{t-1}^2$$

**Step 2:** Lag the equation by 1 period.

$$\sigma_{t-1}^2 = \omega + \alpha_1 \varepsilon_{t-2}^2 + \beta_1 \sigma_{t-2}^2$$

**Step 3:** Substitute the lagged form into the original.

$$\sigma_t^2 = \omega + \alpha_1 \varepsilon_{t-1}^2 + \beta_1 (\omega + \alpha_1 \varepsilon_{t-2}^2 + \beta_1 \sigma_{t-2}^2)$$

**Step 4:** Expand.

$$\sigma_t^2 = \omega(1 + \beta_1) + \alpha_1 \varepsilon_{t-1}^2 + \alpha_1 \beta_1 \varepsilon_{t-2}^2 + \beta_1^2 \sigma_{t-2}^2$$

**Step 5:** Iterate $k$ times.

$$\sigma_t^2 = \omega \sum_{i=0}^{k-1} \beta_1^i + \alpha_1 \sum_{i=0}^{k-1} \beta_1^i \varepsilon_{t-1-i}^2 + \beta_1^k \sigma_{t-k}^2$$

**Step 6:** As $k \to \infty$, $\beta_1^k \sigma_{t-k}^2 \to 0$ (since $\vert \beta_1 \vert < 1$), and the geometric series $\sum_{i=0}^{\infty} \beta_1^i = \frac{1}{1 - \beta_1}$.

**Step 7:** Final ARCH($\infty$) representation.

$$\sigma_t^2 = \frac{\omega}{1 - \beta_1} + \alpha_1 \sum_{i=0}^{\infty} \beta_1^i \varepsilon_{t-1-i}^2$$

**Step 8:** Interpretation. The above is an ARCH($\infty$) with geometrically decaying weights $\alpha_1 \beta_1^i$. **Why GARCH is preferred:**

- An ARCH($\infty$) would require **infinitely many** parameters $\alpha_i$ to be estimated.
- A GARCH(1, 1) replaces this with only **3 parameters**: $\omega, \alpha_1, \beta_1$.
- This **parsimony** improves estimation efficiency, reduces standard errors, and avoids overfitting — a major advantage in finite financial samples. **(7 marks)**

#### **(b)** Consider a GARCH(1, 1) model with $\omega = 0.00001$, $\alpha_1 = 0.10$, $\beta_1 = 0.85$. If the current conditional variance is $\sigma_t^2 = 0.0004$ and the squared residual is $\varepsilon_t^2 = 0.0009$, compute the 5-step-ahead forecast $\hat{\sigma}_{t+5}^2$ and comment on whether the process is mean-reverting. **(7 marks)**

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Verify stationarity.

$$\alpha_1 + \beta_1 = 0.10 + 0.85 = 0.95 < 1 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 2:** Compute the unconditional (long-run) variance.

$$\hat{\sigma}^2 = \frac{\omega}{1 - \alpha_1 - \beta_1} = \frac{0.00001}{1 - 0.95} = \frac{0.00001}{0.05} = 0.0002$$

**Step 3:** Compute the 1-step-ahead forecast.

$$\hat{\sigma}_{t+1}^2 = \omega + \alpha_1 \varepsilon_t^2 + \beta_1 \sigma_t^2 = 0.00001 + (0.10)(0.0009) + (0.85)(0.0004)$$

$$\hat{\sigma}_{t+1}^2 = 0.00001 + 0.00009 + 0.00034 = 0.00044$$

**Step 4:** Apply the multi-step forecast formula.

$$\hat{\sigma}_{t+h}^2 = \hat{\sigma}^2 + (\alpha_1 + \beta_1)^{h-1}(\hat{\sigma}_{t+1}^2 - \hat{\sigma}^2)$$

**Step 5:** Substitute for $h = 5$.

$$\hat{\sigma}_{t+5}^2 = 0.0002 + (0.95)^{4}(0.00044 - 0.0002)$$

**Step 6:** Compute $(0.95)^4$.

$$(0.95)^2 = 0.9025, \quad (0.95)^4 = (0.9025)^2 = 0.81451$$

**Step 7:** Compute the deviation.

$$(0.95)^4 \times 0.00024 = 0.81451 \times 0.00024 \approx 0.0001955$$

**Step 8:** Add the long-run mean.

$$\hat{\sigma}_{t+5}^2 \approx 0.0002 + 0.0001955 = 0.0003955$$

**Step 9:** Comment. The 5-step-ahead forecast ($\approx 0.0003955$) is still well above the long-run level ($0.0002$), but it has decayed from the 1-step value of $0.00044$. Since $\alpha_1 + \beta_1 = 0.95 < 1$, the process is **strictly mean-reverting** and will continue to converge slowly to the long-run variance of $0.0002$ as $h \to \infty$. **(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the constant $\omega$ in the variance equation** — many students write only the ARCH and GARCH terms, losing **2 marks** immediately.
> 2. **Confusing GARCH(p, q) ordering** — note that $p$ is the GARCH term order and $q$ is the ARCH term order; reversing these is a frequent error.
> 3. **Using $\vert \beta_1 \vert < 1$ instead of $\alpha_1 + \beta_1 < 1$** — stationarity requires the *sum*, not just the GARCH coefficient.
> 4. **Skipping the unit check on the final answer** — always verify that $\hat{\sigma}_t^2$ is a variance (non-negative, expressed in squared-return units).
> 5. **Wrong half-life formula** — use $\ln(0.5) / \ln(\alpha_1 + \beta_1)$, NOT $\ln(2) / \ln(\alpha_1 + \beta_1)$. Both are mathematically correct (signs cancel), but the standard KTU textbook form is the former.
> 6. **Not interpreting results in financial context** — KTU awards **interpretation marks** (1–2 marks) for relating the math to volatility persistence, VaR, or market behavior.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **GARCH(p, q)** has $p$ GARCH lags and $q$ ARCH lags. The industry workhorse is **GARCH(1, 1)**.
- [x] The **variance equation** is $\sigma_t^2 = \omega + \sum \alpha_i \varepsilon_{t-i}^2 + \sum \beta_j \sigma_{t-j}^2$, with $\omega > 0$, $\alpha_i \geq 0$, $\beta_j \geq 0$.
- [x] **Stationarity** requires $\sum \alpha_i + \sum \beta_j < 1$.
- [x] **Unconditional variance**: $\sigma^2 = \dfrac{\omega}{1 - \alpha_1 - \beta_1}$ (for GARCH(1,1)).
- [x] **Persistence** is measured by $\Phi = \alpha_1 + \beta_1$; closer to 1 means longer memory.
- [x] **Half-life** of a shock: $h = 1 + \dfrac{\ln(0.5)}{\ln(\alpha_1 + \beta_1)}$.
- [x] GARCH(1, 1) is mathematically equivalent to an **ARCH($\infty$)** with geometrically decaying weights — hence its parsimony.
- [x] **1-step forecast**: $\hat{\sigma}_{t+1}^2 = \omega + \alpha_1 \varepsilon_t^2 + \beta_1 \sigma_t^2$.
- [x] **h-step forecast**: $\hat{\sigma}_{t+h}^2 = \hat{\sigma}^2 + (\alpha_1 + \beta_1)^{h-1} (\hat{\sigma}_{t+1}^2 - \hat{\sigma}^2)$.
- [x] GARCH(1, 1) is the **base of RiskMetrics**, used by central banks and major financial institutions.
- [x] Extensions include **EGARCH** (asymmetric), **GJR-GARCH** (leverage), **IGARCH** (unit root), and **FIGARCH** (long memory).
- [x] Estimation is by **Maximum Likelihood** assuming conditional normality (or Student-$t$ for fat tails).
- [x] Always state the **stationarity check**, **unconditional variance**, and **half-life** in KTU exam answers — these are the three "must-have" derivations.
- [x] Use `\mid` or `\vert` in LaTeX for conditional bars — never raw `|` inside markdown tables.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: GARCH-M Model
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET742 - FINANCIAL TIME SERIES ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** GARCH-M Model

# Module 2 — GARCH-M Model

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** This topic falls under *Module 2: Conditional Heteroscedasticity Models*, evolving directly from the standard GARCH(p,q) framework. Examiners typically test it as a 14-mark Part B question combining definition, specification, and estimation logic.

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

The **GARCH-M (GARCH-in-Mean)** model is a conditional heteroscedasticity model in which the **conditional variance** of the return series is allowed to enter the **conditional mean equation** as an explanatory variable. It captures the economic intuition that **risk affects expected return** — agents in financial markets demand higher returns when perceived risk is high.

Formally, a $\text{GARCH}(1,1)\text{-M}$ model is defined by the following two-equation system:

$$
\begin{aligned}
r_{t} &= \mu + \delta\,h_{t} + \varepsilon_{t}, \qquad \varepsilon_{t} \mid \mathcal{F}_{t-1} \sim \mathcal{N}(0,h_{t}) \\
h_{t} &= \omega + \alpha\,\varepsilon_{t-1}^{2} + \beta\,h_{t-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

where:
- $r_t$ is the asset return at time $t$,
- $h_t = \mathbb{E}\!\left[\varepsilon_t^{2} \mid \mathcal{F}_{t-1}\right]$ is the **conditional variance** given the information set $\mathcal{F}_{t-1}$,
- $\delta$ is the **risk-premium parameter** (the coefficient of conditional variance in the mean equation),
- $\omega > 0$, $\alpha \geq 0$, $\beta \geq 0$ are the standard GARCH parameters,
- The stationarity restriction $\alpha + \beta < 1$ ensures a finite unconditional variance.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "M"?** The "M" stands for **"in Mean"**, distinguishing it from ordinary GARCH where variance dynamics only appear in the second-moment (variance) equation. The GARCH-M model couples the **first moment** with the **second moment**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are a **street-food vendor** deciding what price to charge for your evening snack:

- On **calm, low-traffic evenings** (low risk), you know demand is steady → you charge a **modest price**.
- On **festival or stormy evenings** (high risk), footfall is unpredictable — demand may explode or collapse → you charge a **higher price** to compensate for the uncertainty.

The "price" is the **expected return** $r_t$, and the "uncertainty of footfall" is the **conditional variance** $h_t$. The vendor links the price (mean) directly to the uncertainty (variance). The parameter $\delta$ measures **how much extra price** the vendor charges per unit of perceived risk. **GARCH-M is precisely this linkage** for asset returns: returns embed a risk premium that responds to time-varying volatility.

> [!TIP]
> **One-line takeaway:** *GARCH-M says: "Risk doesn't just exist — it gets priced into the expected return."*

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why GARCH-M? — Motivation from Theory

The classical **Capital Asset Pricing Model (CAPM)** and **Arbitrage Pricing Theory (APT)** both posit that expected excess returns are positively related to risk. However, the basic GARCH model ignores this risk-return trade-off by treating $\mu$ as a constant. GARCH-M embeds the theoretical prediction of finance directly into the time-series structure.

The economic hypotheses embedded in GARCH-M are:

1. **H1 — Positive Risk Premium:** Investors require compensation for volatility → $\delta > 0$.
2. **H2 — Time-Varying Risk:** Volatility clusters (from GARCH dynamics) → $\alpha, \beta$ jointly meaningful.
3. **H3 — Asymmetric Pricing:** Sometimes $\sqrt{h_t}$ (not $h_t$) is used, implying a **volatility-risk premium** rather than a variance-risk premium.

### 2.2 Alternative Mean-Equation Specifications

KTU examiners often ask which functional form of risk enters the mean equation. The three popular choices are:

| Specification | Mean Equation | Economic Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| **Variance-in-Mean** | $r_t = \mu + \delta\, h_t + \varepsilon_t$ | Linear premium per unit variance |
| **Std-Deviation-in-Mean** | $r_t = \mu + \delta\, \sqrt{h_t} + \varepsilon_t$ | Linear premium per unit volatility (most common in practice) |
| **Log-Volatility-in-Mean** | $r_t = \mu + \delta\, \ln(h_t) + \varepsilon_t$ | Unit-free elasticity interpretation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU examiner pattern:** Whenever $\delta$ appears, **always explicitly state** whether it represents a *variance premium* or a *volatility premium*. Marks are awarded for this clarification.

### 2.3 Conditional and Unconditional Moments

**Conditional Mean:**
$$
\mathbb{E}\!\left[r_t \mid \mathcal{F}_{t-1}\right] = \mu + \delta\, h_t
$$

**Conditional Variance:**
$$
\mathbb{V}\!\left[r_t \mid \mathcal{F}_{t-1}\right] = h_t = \omega + \alpha\,\varepsilon_{t-1}^{2} + \beta\,h_{t-1}
$$

**Unconditional Variance (long-run, requires stationarity $\alpha+\beta<1$):**
$$
\mathbb{V}[r_t] = \sigma^{2} = \frac{\omega}{1 - \alpha - \beta}
$$

### 2.4 Stationarity & Parameter Constraints

For a well-defined GARCH-M(1,1):

| Parameter | Constraint | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| $\omega$ | $\omega > 0$ | Ensures $h_t > 0$ for all $t$ |
| $\alpha$ | $\alpha \geq 0$ | Past shocks cannot reduce variance |
| $\beta$ | $\beta \geq 0$ | Past variance persistence non-negative |
| $\alpha + \beta$ | $\alpha + \beta < 1$ | Covariance stationarity |
| $\delta$ | Sign unrestricted a priori | Theory predicts $\delta > 0$ for risky assets |

> [!WARNING]
> **Mark-loser alert:** Many students forget to impose $\alpha + \beta < 1$ when reporting estimates. KTU examiners deduct 1–2 marks for missing this constraint explicitly.

### 2.5 Estimation Method

Because $h_t$ depends on past residuals, GARCH-M is estimated by **Quasi-Maximum Likelihood (QML)** under the assumption that innovations are conditionally normal:

$$
\ell_T(\theta) = -\frac{T}{2}\ln(2\pi) - \frac{1}{2}\sum_{t=1}^{T}\!\left[\ln(h_t) + \frac{\varepsilon_t^{2}}{h_t}\right]
$$

where $\theta = (\mu, \delta, \omega, \alpha, \beta)'$. Maximisation is performed numerically (e.g., BHHH or Newton-Raphson). Standard errors are obtained from the inverse of the **Hessian** at the optimum.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Unconditional Variance

We derive $\sigma^2 = \dfrac{\omega}{1-\alpha-\beta}$ from the GARCH(1,1) recursion.

**Step 1.** Take unconditional expectations of both sides of $h_t = \omega + \alpha \varepsilon_{t-1}^2 + \beta h_{t-1}$.

$$
\mathbb{E}[h_t] = \omega + \alpha\,\mathbb{E}[\varepsilon_{t-1}^{2}] + \beta\,\mathbb{E}[h_{t-1}]
$$

**Step 2.** Under *covariance stationarity*, $\mathbb{E}[h_t] = \mathbb{E}[h_{t-1}] = \mathbb{E}[\varepsilon_{t-1}^{2}] = \sigma^{2}$ (a constant). Substitute:

$$
\sigma^{2} = \omega + \alpha\,\sigma^{2} + \beta\,\sigma^{2}
$$

**Step 3.** Rearrange to isolate $\sigma^{2}$:

$$
\sigma^{2} - (\alpha+\beta)\sigma^{2} = \omega
$$

$$
\sigma^{2}\,(1 - \alpha - \beta) = \omega
$$

**Step 4.** Solve:

$$
\sigma^{2} = \frac{\omega}{1 - \alpha - \beta}
$$

> **Logic check:** If $\alpha + \beta \to 1$, the denominator $\to 0$ and the unconditional variance **blows up** — this is the famous *IGARCH* (Integrated GARCH) borderline where volatility becomes non-stationary.

### 3.2 Persistence of Shocks (Half-Life Derivation)

A key quantity in KTU problems is the **persistence** of a shock to variance, measured by the half-life $\tau$ such that the impulse response halves after $\tau$ periods.

**Step 1.** The MA($\infty$) representation of $h_t$ in $\varepsilon_{t-i}^{2}$ is:

$$
h_t = \frac{\omega}{1-\alpha-\beta} + \sum_{i=1}^{\infty}(\alpha+\beta)^{i}\varepsilon_{t-i}^{2}
$$

**Step 2.** The coefficient on $\varepsilon_{t-i}^{2}$ decays geometrically at rate $(\alpha+\beta)^{i}$. A unit shock therefore carries weight $(\alpha+\beta)^{i}$ at horizon $i$.

**Step 3.** The half-life $\tau$ satisfies $(\alpha+\beta)^{\tau} = \tfrac{1}{2}$:

$$
\tau = \frac{\ln(0.5)}{\ln(\alpha+\beta)} = -\frac{\ln 2}{\ln(\alpha+\beta)}
$$

> **Numerical example:** If $\alpha+\beta = 0.95$, then $\tau = -\ln(2)/\ln(0.95) \approx 13.5$ periods. Shocks persist for roughly a year in monthly data — a textbook empirical result.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem.** Suppose estimates of a GARCH(1,1)-M model for an index return series (in % per day) are:

$$
\hat{\mu}=0.02,\quad \hat{\delta}=0.15,\quad \hat{\omega}=0.05,\quad \hat{\alpha}=0.08,\quad \hat{\beta}=0.89
$$

Compute (a) the unconditional variance, (b) the persistence half-life, and (c) interpret $\hat{\delta}$.

**(a) Unconditional variance**

$$
\sigma^{2} = \frac{0.05}{1 - 0.08 - 0.89} = \frac{0.05}{0.03} \approx 1.667
$$

So unconditional standard deviation is $\sqrt{1.667} \approx 1.291\%$ per day.

**(b) Half-life of shocks**

$$
\tau = -\frac{\ln 2}{\ln(0.97)} = -\frac{0.6931}{-0.0305} \approx 22.7 \text{ days}
$$

A volatility shock takes about **23 trading days (≈1 month)** to dissipate by half.

**(c) Interpretation of $\hat{\delta}=0.15$**

Each one-unit increase in conditional variance is associated with a **0.15-unit increase** in expected daily return. Since $\hat{\delta}>0$, the data support a **positive risk-return trade-off**, consistent with the financial intuition of a risk premium.

### 3.4 Hypotheses Test (Significance of $\delta$)

To test $H_0:\delta=0$ versus $H_1:\delta\neq 0$, construct the **Wald statistic**:

$$
W = \frac{\hat{\delta}^{2}}{\widehat{\text{Var}}(\hat{\delta})} \;\;\sim\;\; \chi^{2}(1) \quad \text{under } H_0
$$

Reject at the 5% level if $W > 3.841$. Failure to reject $H_0:\delta=0$ means **GARCH-M collapses to ordinary GARCH** for the dataset.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the GARCH-M Model

The diagram below shows how information $\mathcal{F}_{t-1}$ propagates through the **mean** and **variance** channels, with their **feedback coupling** through $\delta h_t$.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    F[tmin1["Information Set F sub t minus 1"]:::input]
    mu["Constant Drift mu"]:::param
    delta["Risk Premium Coefficient delta"]:::param
    epsPrev["Past Shock epsilon sub t minus 1"]:::input
    hPrev["Past Variance h sub t minus 1"]:::input
    omega["Constant omega"]:::param
    alpha["ARCH coefficient alpha"]:::param
    beta["GARCH coefficient beta"]:::param
    meanEq["Mean Equation: r sub t equals mu plus delta h sub t plus epsilon sub t"]:::mean
    varEq["Variance Equation: h sub t equals omega plus alpha epsilon squared sub t minus 1 plus beta h sub t minus 1"]:::var
    innov["Innovation epsilon sub t conditional on F sub t minus 1 is Normal zero h sub t"]:::innov
    output["Observed Return r sub t"]:::out
    condMean["Conditional Mean E r sub t given F sub t minus 1 equals mu plus delta h sub t"]:::cm
    condVar["Conditional Variance Var r sub t given F sub t minus 1 equals h sub t"]:::cv
    testH["Wald Test on delta: H0 delta equals 0 versus H1 delta not equals 0"]:::test
    uncondVar["Unconditional Variance sigma squared equals omega divided by 1 minus alpha minus beta"]:::uv
    halfLife["Half Life tau equals minus ln 2 divided by ln alpha plus beta"]:::hl

    F --> meanEq
    mu --> meanEq
    delta --> meanEq
    hPrev --> meanEq
    meanEq --> output
    innov --> output

    F --> varEq
    epsPrev --> varEq
    hPrev --> varEq
    omega --> varEq
    alpha --> varEq
    beta --> varEq
    varEq --> hPrev

    meanEq --> condMean
    varEq --> condVar
    output --> testH
    testH --> output
    varEq --> uncondVar
    varEq --> halfLife

    classDef input fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0,color:#0D47A1
    classDef param fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,color:#BF360C
    classDef mean fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#4A148C,color:#311B92
    classDef var fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#1B5E20
    classDef innov fill:#FCE4EC,stroke:#880E4F,color:#880E4F
    classDef out fill:#FFFDE7,stroke:#F57F17,color:#F57F17
    classDef cm fill:#EDE7F6,stroke:#311B92,color:#311B92
    classDef cv fill:#E0F2F1,stroke:#004D40,color:#004D40
    classDef test fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#B71C1C,color:#B71C1C
    classDef uv fill:#E1F5FE,stroke:#01579B,color:#01579B
    classDef hl fill:#F1F8E9,stroke:#33691E,color:#33691E
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram:** The two parallel channels (mean on the left, variance on the right) are *coupled* because $h_t$ from the variance equation re-enters the mean equation through $\delta$. This **bidirectional feedback** is the defining structural feature of GARCH-M.

### 4.2 Risk–Return Interaction — Conceptual Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Volatility Regime at t minus 1"]:::nodeA
    B["Conditional Variance h sub t"]:::nodeB
    C["Expected Excess Return E r sub t minus r sub f given F sub t minus 1"]:::nodeC
    D["Investor Utility Maximisation"]:::nodeD
    E["Empirical Estimation QML"]:::nodeE
    F["Test of delta greater than 0"]:::nodeF
    G["Risk Premium Confirmed or Rejected"]:::nodeG

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D --> E
    E --> F
    F --> G

    classDef nodeA fill:#E1F5FE,stroke:#01579B,color:#01579B
    classDef nodeB fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,color:#BF360C
    classDef nodeC fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#4A148C,color:#311B92
    classDef nodeD fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#1B5E20
    classDef nodeE fill:#FFFDE7,stroke:#F57F17,color:#F57F17
    classDef nodeF fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#B71C1C,color:#B71C1C
    classDef nodeG fill:#ECEFF1,stroke:#263238,color:#263238
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** *What do you mean by the GARCH-M model? Why is it also called the ARCH-M model in some textbooks?*

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Definition (2 marks):** The GARCH-M model is an extension of the standard GARCH framework in which the conditional variance $h_t$ is included as an explanatory variable in the conditional mean equation. The mean and variance equations are respectively:

$$
r_t = \mu + \delta h_t + \varepsilon_t, \qquad h_t = \omega + \alpha\varepsilon_{t-1}^2 + \beta h_{t-1}
$$

- **Naming (1 mark):** When the volatility dynamics are captured using an **ARCH(q)** rather than a GARCH(p,q) recursion, the resulting model is called **ARCH-M** (ARCH-in-Mean). It is the same logical structure — *risk in the mean* — so the two names refer to the same family, only differing in the lag structure used to model $h_t$.

---

**Q2.** *State the stationarity conditions for the parameters of a GARCH(1,1)-M model.*

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The parameters must satisfy:

1. $\omega > 0$ — guarantees strictly positive conditional variance for all $t$.
2. $\alpha \geq 0$ — past shocks cannot reduce $h_t$.
3. $\beta \geq 0$ — past variance must have non-negative impact.
4. $\alpha + \beta < 1$ — ensures covariance stationarity so that unconditional variance $\sigma^2 = \omega/(1-\alpha-\beta)$ is finite and positive.
5. $\delta$ is **free in sign** under pure time-series theory, but financial theory predicts $\delta > 0$ for risky assets (positive risk premium).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Major Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

#### QUESTION A — 14 Marks

**(a)** Derive the unconditional variance of a GARCH(1,1)-M process and discuss the role of the parameter $\delta$. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Consider a stock return series estimated to follow a GARCH(1,1)-M model with parameters $\mu = 0.03$, $\delta = 0.20$, $\omega = 0.04$, $\alpha = 0.07$, $\beta = 0.90$. Compute the unconditional variance, the half-life of volatility shocks, and test whether $\delta$ is significantly different from zero at the 5% level if its standard error is $0.08$. **[7 marks]**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

---

##### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Set up the system (1 mark):**

$$
r_t = \mu + \delta h_t + \varepsilon_t, \qquad h_t = \omega + \alpha \varepsilon_{t-1}^2 + \beta h_{t-1}
$$

with $\varepsilon_t \mid \mathcal{F}_{t-1} \sim \mathcal{N}(0, h_t)$.

**Step 2 — Unconditional moments of $r_t$ (1 mark):** Since $\delta h_t$ is deterministic conditional on $\mathcal{F}_{t-1}$, taking expectations:

$$
\mathbb{E}[r_t] = \mu + \delta\,\mathbb{E}[h_t]
$$

Under covariance stationarity, $\mathbb{E}[h_t] = \sigma^{2}$. So $\mathbb{E}[r_t] = \mu + \delta\,\sigma^2$.

**Step 3 — Take expectation of the variance equation (2 marks):**

$$
\sigma^{2} = \omega + \alpha\,\sigma^{2} + \beta\,\sigma^{2} \quad\Rightarrow\quad \sigma^{2} = \frac{\omega}{1 - \alpha - \beta}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation of $\delta$ (2 marks):** $\delta$ is the **risk-premium coefficient**. Each unit of conditional variance $h_t$ shifts the conditional mean of $r_t$ by $\delta$ units. The hypothesis $H_0: \delta = 0$ is equivalent to the statement that risk is **not priced** in this asset's return process. Empirical finance consistently finds $\delta > 0$, supporting the risk-return trade-off hypothesis.

**Step 5 — Caveat (1 mark):** For $\delta$ to be identified, the model must be estimable. Under Gaussian QML, $\delta$ is identified asymptotically, but finite-sample bias can be substantial — typically corrected via *bootstrap* or *bias-reduced ML*.

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Unconditional variance (2 marks):**

$$
\sigma^{2} = \frac{0.04}{1 - 0.07 - 0.90} = \frac{0.04}{0.03} \approx 1.3333
$$

So unconditional standard deviation $\sigma \approx 1.155\%$ per period.

**Step 2 — Persistence and half-life (2 marks):**

$$
\alpha + \beta = 0.07 + 0.90 = 0.97
$$

$$
\tau = -\frac{\ln 2}{\ln(0.97)} = -\frac{0.6931}{-0.03046} \approx 22.75 \text{ periods}
$$

**Step 3 — Wald test on $\delta$ (3 marks):**

$$
W = \frac{\hat{\delta}^{2}}{\widehat{\text{Var}}(\hat{\delta})} = \frac{(0.20)^{2}}{(0.08)^{2}} = \frac{0.04}{0.0064} = 6.25
$$

Compare with $\chi^{2}_{0.05,1} = 3.841$. Since $6.25 > 3.841$, **reject $H_0: \delta = 0$** at the 5% level. Conclusion: the conditional variance has a **statistically significant positive effect** on the conditional mean return — empirical support for the risk-return trade-off.

---

#### QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**(a)** Explain the rationale behind the GARCH-in-Mean model with reference to the Capital Asset Pricing Theory. Discuss how the mean equation can be specified using variance, standard deviation, or log-variance. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** The estimated GARCH(1,1)-M equation for a daily log-return series is:
$r_t = 0.05 + 0.18\,h_t + \varepsilon_t$ with $h_t = 0.02 + 0.06\,\varepsilon_{t-1}^{2} + 0.91\,h_{t-1}$.

Yesterday's return was $r_{t-1} = -1.8\%$ and $\hat{h}_{t-1} = 0.95$. Compute today's conditional variance $h_t$ and the conditional expected return. **[7 marks]**

**[KTU University Exam — Supplementary, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

---

##### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Theoretical rationale (3 marks):** CAPM states that expected excess return is a linear function of risk (beta). In a single-asset, time-varying-volatility setting, this generalises to: expected excess return depends on **conditional risk**. The GARCH-M model operationalises this by letting $h_t$ (or a function of it) enter the mean equation. The parameter $\delta$ captures the **market price of volatility risk**.

**Step 2 — Three alternative specifications (3 marks):**

- **Variance-in-Mean:** $r_t = \mu + \delta h_t + \varepsilon_t$ — premium proportional to variance.
- **Standard-Deviation-in-Mean:** $r_t = \mu + \delta \sqrt{h_t} + \varepsilon_t$ — premium proportional to volatility (most empirically common; units match returns).
- **Log-Variance-in-Mean:** $r_t = \mu + \delta \ln(h_t) + \varepsilon_t$ — semi-elasticity interpretation; $\delta$ gives percentage change in return per 1% change in variance.

**Step 3 — Practical recommendation (1 mark):** Empirical evidence (e.g., French, Schwert & Stambaugh 1987) favours $\sqrt{h_t}$ because it yields better-behaved likelihood surfaces and more stable estimates of $\delta$.

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Compute yesterday's shock (1 mark):**

$$
\varepsilon_{t-1} = r_{t-1} - \mu - \delta\,h_{t-1} = -1.8 - 0.05 - 0.18 \times 0.95
$$

$$
\varepsilon_{t-1} = -1.8 - 0.05 - 0.171 = -2.021
$$

**Step 2 — Square it (1 mark):**

$$
\varepsilon_{t-1}^{2} = (-2.021)^{2} = 4.0844
$$

**Step 3 — Plug into the variance equation (2 marks):**

$$
h_t = 0.02 + 0.06 \times 4.0844 + 0.91 \times 0.95
$$

$$
h_t = 0.02 + 0.2451 + 0.8645 = 1.1296
$$

**Step 4 — Conditional expected return (2 marks):**

$$
\mathbb{E}[r_t \mid \mathcal{F}_{t-1}] = \mu + \delta\,h_t = 0.05 + 0.18 \times 1.1296 = 0.05 + 0.2033 = 0.2533
$$

**Step 5 — Conclusion (1 mark):** Today's expected return is about **0.253%**, conditional on yesterday's negative shock of $-1.8\%$. The negative shock amplified today's conditional variance from $0.95$ to $1.1296$, and via the risk-premium channel, **lifted the expected return** — a textbook volatility-clustering-then-risk-pricing pattern.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $\alpha+\beta<1$ constraint** when computing unconditional variance (–1 mark).
> 2. **Confusing $\delta$ with the usual mean coefficient $\mu$** — $\delta$ multiplies $h_t$, not a regressor.
> 3. **Mis-computing $\varepsilon_{t-1}$:** Remember the residual is the *innovation*, not the observed return. Always subtract the conditional mean first.
> 4. **Sign-error in $\sqrt{\,}$:** When you use $\sqrt{h_t}$ specification, the sign of $\delta$ still reflects the *premium direction*, not volatility sign.
> 5. **Using critical value 1.96 instead of 3.841:** Wald test is $\chi^{2}(1)$, **not** a z-test.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-density rapid revision checklist for GARCH-M:**

- **GARCH-M = GARCH + Variance-in-Mean.** The defining feature is $h_t$ (or $\sqrt{h_t}$) appearing inside the mean equation.
- **Two equations:** Mean: $r_t = \mu + \delta h_t + \varepsilon_t$. Variance: $h_t = \omega + \alpha \varepsilon_{t-1}^{2} + \beta h_{t-1}$.
- **Five parameters:** $\mu, \delta, \omega, \alpha, \beta$. KTU problems often give you four and ask you to compute the fifth via restrictions.
- **Stationarity:** $\omega > 0$, $\alpha, \beta \geq 0$, $\alpha + \beta < 1$.
- **Unconditional variance:** $\sigma^{2} = \omega/(1-\alpha-\beta)$.
- **Half-life of shocks:** $\tau = -\ln(2)/\ln(\alpha+\beta)$.
- **Three mean-equation choices:** $h_t$, $\sqrt{h_t}$, $\ln(h_t)$ — know each one's economic reading.
- **Estimation:** Quasi-Maximum Likelihood under conditional normality; numerically maximised; standard errors from inverse Hessian.
- **Test of $\delta$:** Wald statistic $\delta^{2}/\widehat{\text{Var}}(\delta) \sim \chi^{2}(1)$.
- **Economic hypothesis:** Theory predicts $\delta > 0$ (positive risk premium) — this is the empirical signature of risk-return trade-off.
- **Interpretation rules:** When $\delta$ is significant and positive → volatility is priced; $\delta=0$ → model collapses to plain GARCH.
- **Common algebraic traps:** Use $\varepsilon_{t-1}^{2}$ (squared shock), not $r_{t-1}^{2}$; in the recursion, replace $h_{t-1}$ with *yesterday's computed* conditional variance, not the unconditional.
- **KTU exam weight:** This topic is **high-yield** — appears almost every semester as a 14-mark Part B question. Memorise the recursion and the variance formula.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: EGARCH Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET742 - FINANCIAL TIME SERIES ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** EGARCH Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

The **Exponential Generalized Autoregressive Conditional Heteroskedasticity (EGARCH)** model, introduced by **Daniel B. Nelson (1991)**, is a non-linear extension of the GARCH framework. Unlike the standard GARCH(p,q) model which assumes a symmetric response of conditional variance to positive and negative shocks of equal magnitude, the **EGARCH(p,q)** model explicitly captures the **asymmetric leverage effect** — the empirical observation that negative returns (bad news) tend to increase future volatility more than positive returns (good news) of the same absolute size.

Formally, for a return series $r_t$, the EGARCH model is expressed in two stages:

**Stage 1 — Mean Equation:**
$$r_t = \mu_t + \epsilon_t, \quad \epsilon_t = \sigma_t z_t, \quad z_t \sim \text{i.i.d.}(0,1)$$

**Stage 2 — Conditional Variance Equation (Log-form):**
$$\ln(\sigma_t^2) = \omega + \sum_{i=1}^{p} \alpha_i \vert z_{t-i}\vert + \sum_{j=1}^{q} \gamma_j z_{t-j} + \sum_{k=1}^{s} \beta_k \ln(\sigma_{t-k}^2)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — The 4 Core EGARCH Parameters**
> 1. **$\omega$** — constant/intercept term (no sign restriction required because of the log transformation).
> 2. **$\alpha_i$** — magnitude effect (always non-negative; controls overall shock sensitivity).
> 3. **$\gamma_j$** — **leverage/asymmetry coefficient** (sign determines asymmetric response).
> 4. **$\beta_k$** — persistence parameter (must satisfy stationarity: $\sum \beta_k < 1$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the logarithm?** The use of $\ln(\sigma_t^2)$ on the left-hand side **mathematically guarantees** that $\sigma_t^2 > 0$ for all $t$ regardless of the sign of the parameters. This elegantly eliminates the **non-negativity constraints** that plague standard GARCH models.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Seismograph" View of Markets

Imagine a highly sensitive **financial seismograph** strapped to a stock market index. The seismograph records every tremor (return shock) and its **directional intensity**.

- A **positive shock** (price rise / good news) is like a gentle nudge — the needle twitches but settles quickly.
- A **negative shock** (price fall / bad news) is like an earthquake — the needle swings wildly, and the **residual tremors (volatility) last much longer**.

The EGARCH model is the mathematical "calibration certificate" of this seismograph. The **$\alpha$ coefficient** is the seismograph's raw sensitivity, the **$\gamma$ coefficient** is the **asymmetric damping factor** (small for calm weather, large after earthquakes), and the **$\beta$ coefficient** is the "memory" of the device — how long the residual vibrations continue to ring through the chart.

> [!TIP]
> A useful **real-world parallel**: the 2008 Global Financial Crisis and the 2020 COVID-19 crash both demonstrate the **leverage effect** in action. The EGARCH framework was specifically designed to quantify precisely this "fear premium" embedded in volatility dynamics.

## 1.3 Historical Motivation — Why EGARCH Was Born

Standard GARCH(1,1) by Bollerslev (1986) was a major breakthrough but imposed two **unrealistic restrictions**:

1. **Symmetric volatility response** — it could not distinguish between a $+2\%$ shock and a $-2\%$ shock.
2. **Non-negativity parameter constraints** — every coefficient had to be carefully bounded, making estimation fragile.

Empirical research by **Black (1976)** and **Christie (1982)** on equity returns showed that **stock volatility tends to rise more when prices fall** than when they rise by the same amount — a phenomenon christened the **"leverage effect."** Nelson's EGARCH(1991) was the first model to embed this asymmetry into a **fully parametric, log-transformed, and tractable framework**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Anatomy of the EGARCH(1,1) Model

The most widely used specification in practice is the **EGARCH(1,1)**, which contains a single lag of each type. It is the workhorse of asymmetric volatility modeling in KTU examinations and central bank risk reports alike.

$$\boxed{\ln(\sigma_t^2) = \omega + \alpha \vert z_{t-1}\vert + \gamma z_{t-1} + \beta \ln(\sigma_{t-1}^2)}$$

where $z_{t-1} = \epsilon_{t-1}/\sigma_{t-1}$ is the **standardized innovation**.

### 2.1.1 Functional Role of Each Term

| Parameter | Symbol | Functional Role | Sign / Magnitude Convention |
|-----------|--------|----------------|------------------------------|
| Constant | $\omega$ | Long-run log-volatility baseline | No restriction; usually slightly negative |
| Magnitude Effect | $\alpha$ | Captures absolute size of shocks | $\alpha \geq 0$ (non-negative) |
| Leverage Effect | $\gamma$ | Captures asymmetry between +ve and −ve shocks | $\gamma < 0$ ⇒ negative shocks raise volatility more |
| Persistence | $\beta$ | Memory of past volatility | $\vert \beta \vert < 1$ for covariance stationarity |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stationarity Condition for EGARCH(1,1):**
> The conditional log-variance is covariance-stationary if and only if $\vert \beta \vert < 1$. Note that this is a **two-sided condition** (unlike GARCH which requires $\alpha + \beta < 1$), because $\beta$ alone governs the lag dynamics on the log-scale.

## 2.2 The News Impact Curve (NIC) — Heart of Asymmetric Modeling

The **News Impact Curve** plots $\sigma_t^2$ as a function of the lagged shock $z_{t-1}$, holding past volatility constant. It is the **single most important diagnostic** for asymmetric volatility models and is a **guaranteed KTU question**.

**Derivation of the NIC for EGARCH(1,1):**

For a given $z_{t-1} = z$ and fixed $\sigma_{t-1}^2 = \bar{\sigma}^2$:

$$\sigma_t^2(z) = \bar{\sigma}^{2\beta} \cdot \exp(\omega) \cdot \exp(\alpha \vert z \vert + \gamma z)$$

Decomposing the **exponential asymmetric term**:

$$\exp(\alpha \vert z \vert + \gamma z) = \begin{cases} \exp((\alpha + \gamma)z) & \text{if } z \geq 0 \text{ (good news)} \\ \exp((-\alpha + \gamma)z) = \exp((\alpha - \gamma)\vert z \vert) & \text{if } z < 0 \text{ (bad news)} \end{cases}$$

- For **good news** ($z > 0$): exponent coefficient is $\alpha + \gamma$
- For **bad news** ($z < 0$): exponent coefficient is $-(\alpha - \gamma) = \alpha - \gamma$

If $\gamma < 0$, then $\alpha - \gamma > \alpha + \gamma$, meaning **bad news generates a steeper rise in volatility** than good news — the empirical leverage effect.

## 2.3 Unconditional (Long-Run) Variance

The unconditional variance of $\epsilon_t$ under EGARCH(1,1) is obtained by taking expectations on both sides of the log-variance equation:

$$E[\ln(\sigma_t^2)] = \frac{\omega + \alpha E[\vert z_t \vert]}{1 - \beta}$$

For standardized innovations with $E[\vert z_t \vert] = \sqrt{2/\pi}$ (Gaussian case), the **long-run expected log-variance** is:

$$E[\ln(\sigma_t^2)] = \frac{\omega + \alpha \sqrt{2/\pi}}{1 - \beta}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The unconditional variance of $\epsilon_t$ itself does **not** have a clean closed-form under EGARCH due to the $\exp(\cdot)$ non-linearity. KTU examinations will normally only ask for the **expected log-variance**, which is a standard closed-form result.

## 2.4 The Half-Life of Volatility Shocks

A common KTU question is: *If a shock hits the market today, after how many days will its impact halve?* The **half-life** under EGARCH(1,1) is computed from the AR(1)-like recursion on log-variance:

$$\text{Half-Life} = \frac{\ln(0.5)}{\ln(\vert \beta \vert)} = -\frac{\ln(2)}{\ln(\vert \beta \vert)}$$

For example, if $\beta = 0.95$, then half-life $\approx -\ln(2)/\ln(0.95) \approx 13.5$ days. This is consistent with the well-known empirical finding that **volatility clustering has a half-life of roughly 2–4 weeks** in equity markets.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet — One-Page Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use |
|---|---------|----------------|
| 1 | $\ln(\sigma_t^2) = \omega + \alpha \vert z_{t-1}\vert + \gamma z_{t-1} + \beta \ln(\sigma_{t-1}^2)$ | EGARCH(1,1) core specification |
| 2 | $z_t = \epsilon_t / \sigma_t$ | Standardized innovation |
| 3 | $\sigma_t^2 = \exp(\omega + \alpha \vert z_{t-1}\vert + \gamma z_{t-1} + \beta \ln(\sigma_{t-1}^2))$ | Re-parameterized variance form |
| 4 | $\exp(\alpha \vert z \vert + \gamma z)$ | News Impact Curve kernel |
| 5 | $\gamma < 0$ | Leverage effect condition (asymmetry) |
| 6 | $\vert \beta \vert < 1$ | Stationarity condition |
| 7 | $E[\ln(\sigma_t^2)] = \dfrac{\omega + \alpha \sqrt{2/\pi}}{1 - \beta}$ | Unconditional expected log-variance |
| 8 | $\text{Half-Life} = -\ln(2) / \ln(\vert \beta \vert)$ | Shock decay duration |
| 9 | Persistence $= \vert \beta \vert$ | Volatility clustering strength |
| 10 | AIC / BIC | Model selection criterion (lower = better) |

## 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering & Finance

| Domain | Application of EGARCH |
|--------|----------------------|
| **Risk Management** | Computing asymmetric Value-at-Risk (VaR) for portfolios during crisis periods |
| **Derivative Pricing** | Forecasting local volatility surfaces for option Greeks |
| **Central Banking** | Modeling FX-volatility for monetary policy transmission analysis |
| **Algorithmic Trading** | Dynamic position-sizing using regime-switching volatility filters |
| **Insurance / Actuarial** | Pricing catastrophe bonds where downside-tail asymmetry is critical |
| **Energy Markets** | Modeling oil-price volatility where supply shocks (negative) dominate demand shocks |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The News Impact Curve in Explicit Form

**Problem:** Derive the News Impact Curve (NIC) for the EGARCH(1,1) model and show that the curve is asymmetric in $z_{t-1}$.

**Starting Equation:**

$$\ln(\sigma_t^2) = \omega + \alpha \vert z_{t-1}\vert + \gamma z_{t-1} + \beta \ln(\sigma_{t-1}^2)$$

**Step 1 — Exponentiate both sides:**

$$\sigma_t^2 = \exp\Bigl(\omega + \alpha \vert z_{t-1}\vert + \gamma z_{t-1} + \beta \ln(\sigma_{t-1}^2)\Bigr)$$

**Step 2 — Use the property $\exp(a + b) = \exp(a) \cdot \exp(b)$ repeatedly:**

$$\sigma_t^2 = \exp(\omega) \cdot \exp\bigl(\alpha \vert z_{t-1}\vert\bigr) \cdot \exp(\gamma z_{t-1}) \cdot \exp\bigl(\beta \ln(\sigma_{t-1}^2)\bigr)$$

**Step 3 — Simplify the last term using $\exp(\beta \ln x) = x^\beta$:**

$$\sigma_t^2 = \exp(\omega) \cdot \exp(\alpha \vert z_{t-1}\vert) \cdot \exp(\gamma z_{t-1}) \cdot (\sigma_{t-1}^2)^\beta$$

**Step 4 — Group the asymmetric exponential kernel:**

$$\sigma_t^2 = \underbrace{\exp(\omega) \cdot (\sigma_{t-1}^2)^\beta}_{\text{Symmetric baseline}} \cdot \underbrace{\exp(\alpha \vert z_{t-1}\vert + \gamma z_{t-1})}_{\text{Asymmetric news impact}}$$

**Step 5 — Hold $\sigma_{t-1}^2 = \bar{\sigma}^2$ constant to extract the pure NIC:**

$$\text{NIC}(z) = \exp(\omega) \cdot \bar{\sigma}^{2\beta} \cdot \exp(\alpha \vert z \vert + \gamma z)$$

**Step 6 — Split into the two regimes of $z$:**

$$\text{NIC}(z) = \begin{cases} K \cdot \exp\bigl((\alpha + \gamma)z\bigr), & z \geq 0 \\[4pt] K \cdot \exp\bigl((\gamma - \alpha)z\bigr) = K \cdot \exp\bigl((\alpha - \gamma)\vert z \vert\bigr), & z < 0 \end{cases}$$

where $K = \exp(\omega) \cdot \bar{\sigma}^{2\beta}$ is a positive scaling constant.

**Step 7 — Interpret the asymmetry:**

- For $z > 0$: slope parameter is $\alpha + \gamma$.
- For $z < 0$: slope parameter is $\alpha - \gamma$.

If $\gamma < 0$, then $\alpha - \gamma > \alpha + \gamma$, hence the curve is **steeper for negative shocks**. **This is the formal definition of the leverage effect.**

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Unconditional Expected Log-Variance

**Step 1 — Take expectations on both sides of the EGARCH(1,1) equation:**

$$E\bigl[\ln(\sigma_t^2)\bigr] = \omega + \alpha E\bigl[\vert z_{t-1}\vert\bigr] + \gamma E[z_{t-1}] + \beta E\bigl[\ln(\sigma_{t-1}^2)\bigr]$$

**Step 2 — Apply the standardization properties $E[z_{t-1}] = 0$ and (in stationarity) $E[\ln(\sigma_t^2)] = E[\ln(\sigma_{t-1}^2)]$:**

$$E\bigl[\ln(\sigma_t^2)\bigr] = \omega + \alpha E[\vert z \vert] + \beta E\bigl[\ln(\sigma_t^2)\bigr]$$

**Step 3 — Collect $E[\ln(\sigma_t^2)]$ on the left side:**

$$E\bigl[\ln(\sigma_t^2)\bigr] (1 - \beta) = \omega + \alpha E[\vert z \vert]$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the unconditional mean:**

$$E\bigl[\ln(\sigma_t^2)\bigr] = \frac{\omega + \alpha E[\vert z \vert]}{1 - \beta}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $E[\vert z \vert] = \sqrt{2/\pi}$ under Gaussian innovations:**

$$E\bigl[\ln(\sigma_t^2)\bigr] = \frac{\omega + \alpha \sqrt{2/\pi}}{1 - \beta}$$

> [!NOTE]
> For Student-$t$ innovations with $\nu$ degrees of freedom, $E[\vert z \vert] = \sqrt{\nu - 2}\,\Gamma\bigl(\tfrac{\nu - 1}{2}\bigr)\big/\bigl(\sqrt{\pi}\,\Gamma(\nu/2)\bigr)$ for $\nu > 2$. KTU exams typically restrict to the Gaussian case.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Half-Life of a Volatility Shock

**Step 1 — Consider a one-time shock to $\ln(\sigma_t^2)$ at $t = 0$. The recursive form is:**

$$\ln(\sigma_t^2) - E[\ln(\sigma^2)] = \beta^t \bigl(\ln(\sigma_0^2) - E[\ln(\sigma^2)]\bigr)$$

**Step 2 — The shock magnitude is halved when $\beta^t = 0.5$:**

$$\beta^t = 0.5$$

**Step 3 — Take natural logarithms of both sides:**

$$t \ln(\beta) = \ln(0.5) = -\ln(2)$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $t$ (the half-life):**

$$t = \frac{-\ln(2)}{\ln(\beta)} = \frac{\ln(2)}{-\ln(\beta)} = \frac{\ln(2)}{\ln(1/\beta)}$$

**Numerical Example:** If $\beta = 0.96$:

$$t = \frac{\ln(2)}{\ln(1/0.96)} = \frac{0.6931}{0.0408} \approx 17.0 \text{ days}$$

This is broadly consistent with empirical findings of 2–4 week half-lives for equity volatility.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** Given an EGARCH(1,1) specification with $\omega = -0.6$, $\alpha = 0.15$, $\gamma = -0.08$, $\beta = 0.92$, and $\sigma_{t-1}^2 = 0.0009$, compute the conditional variance $\sigma_t^2$ when the lagged shock is $z_{t-1} = -2$ (a "bad news" event).

**Step 1 — Compute the absolute value:** $\vert z_{t-1} \vert = 2$.

**Step 2 — Substitute into the log-variance equation:**

$$\ln(\sigma_t^2) = -0.6 + 0.15(2) + (-0.08)(-2) + 0.92 \ln(0.0009)$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate each term:**

- $\omega = -0.6$
- $\alpha \vert z \vert = 0.15 \times 2 = 0.30$
- $\gamma z = -0.08 \times (-2) = +0.16$
- $\ln(0.0009) = -7.013$
- $\beta \ln(\sigma_{t-1}^2) = 0.92 \times (-7.013) = -6.452$

**Step 4 — Sum all contributions:**

$$\ln(\sigma_t^2) = -0.6 + 0.30 + 0.16 - 6.452 = -6.592$$

**Step 5 — Exponentiate to recover $\sigma_t^2$:**

$$\sigma_t^2 = e^{-6.592} \approx 0.00138$$

**Step 6 — Compare with a "+2" shock (good news) for the same $\sigma_{t-1}^2$:**

$$\ln(\sigma_t^2)\vert_{z=+2} = -0.6 + 0.15(2) + (-0.08)(+2) + 0.92(-7.013) = -7.052$$

$$\sigma_t^2 \vert_{z=+2} = e^{-7.052} \approx 0.000875$$

**Step 7 — Quantify the asymmetry:**

$$\text{Asymmetry Ratio} = \frac{\sigma_t^2(z=-2)}{\sigma_t^2(z=+2)} = \frac{0.00138}{0.000875} \approx 1.577$$

> A **negative shock of magnitude 2 produced ~58% more variance** than a positive shock of equal magnitude — a textbook demonstration of the leverage effect in action.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 EGARCH(1,1) Information Flow Architecture

The following block diagram traces the **information-processing pipeline** within a single EGARCH(1,1) recursion, from raw return input to conditional variance output.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Return Series r_t] --> B[Residual Extraction]
    B --> C[Compute Innovation epsilon_t = r_t minus mu_t]
    C --> D[Compute Standardized Innovation z_t = epsilon_t divided by sigma_t]
    D --> E[Store z_t as z_{t-1} for next period]
    E --> F[Compute Magnitude Effect term alpha times absolute z_{t-1}]
    E --> G[Compute Leverage Effect term gamma times z_{t-1}]
    E --> H[Retrieve Lagged Log Variance ln sigma_{t-1}^2]
    H --> I[Multiply by Persistence parameter beta]
    F --> J[Aggregator Node]
    G --> J
    I --> J
    K[Constant omega] --> J
    J --> L[Sum the four log variance components]
    L --> M[Exponentiate to recover sigma_t squared]
    M --> N[Output Conditional Variance]
    N --> O[Feedback to next recursion step]
    O --> E
```

## 4.2 Comparison: GARCH vs. GJR-GARCH vs. EGARCH Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph GARCH_Block [GARCH 1 1 Model]
        A1[Shock epsilon_{t-1}] --> A2[Squared Shock term alpha epsilon_{t-1}^2]
        A3[Lagged variance sigma_{t-1}^2] --> A4[Persistence term beta sigma_{t-1}^2]
        A2 --> A5[Linear Sum in variance space]
        A4 --> A5
        A6[Constant omega] --> A5
    end

    subgraph GJR_Block [GJR GARCH 1 1 Model]
        B1[Shock epsilon_{t-1}] --> B2[Squared Shock alpha epsilon_{t-1}^2]
        B1 --> B3[Sign Indicator I_{neg} times gamma epsilon_{t-1}^2]
        B4[Lagged variance sigma_{t-1}^2] --> B5[Persistence beta sigma_{t-1}^2]
        B2 --> B6[Linear Sum in variance space]
        B3 --> B6
        B5 --> B6
        B7[Constant omega] --> B6
    end

    subgraph EGARCH_Block [EGARCH 1 1 Model]
        C1[Standardized Shock z_{t-1}] --> C2[Absolute magnitude term alpha absolute z_{t-1}]
        C1 --> C3[Leverage term gamma times z_{t-1}]
        C4[Lagged log variance ln sigma_{t-1}^2] --> C5[Persistence beta times ln sigma_{t-1}^2]
        C2 --> C6[Linear Sum in log variance space]
        C3 --> C6
        C5 --> C6
        C7[Constant omega] --> C6
        C6 --> C8[Exponentiate to recover sigma_t squared]
    end
```

## 4.3 Decision Flowchart — Choosing the Right Volatility Model

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Analyze Return Series for Volatility Clustering] --> Q1{Is volatility response symmetric?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| GARCH[Use Standard GARCH 1 1]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{Do negative shocks raise volatility more than positive?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| Q3{Need non-negativity guarantee?}
    Q3 -->|No| GJR[Use GJR GARCH with indicator function]
    Q3 -->|Yes| EGARCH[Use EGARCH 1 1 with log transform]
    Q2 -->|No but shocks are sign dependent| Q4{Other non-linear dynamics?}
    Q4 -->|Yes| APARCH[Use APARCH or Threshold GARCH]
    Q4 -->|No| EGARCH

    GARCH --> EST[Estimate via Maximum Likelihood]
    GJR --> EST
    EGARCH --> EST
    APARCH --> EST
    EST --> DIAG[Validate via News Impact Curve and Residual Diagnostics]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Architecture:** Each block represents a **computational stage**, and arrows denote **information flow** rather than physical wires. The EGARCH block's distinguishing feature is the **Exponentiate to recover sigma_t squared** stage, which is the only place where the log-to-level transformation occurs.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
> **Q1. Define the EGARCH(1,1) model. Why is the log transformation applied to the conditional variance equation?**

**Model Answer:**
The **EGARCH(1,1)** (Exponential GARCH) model proposed by Nelson (1991) is specified as:

$$\ln(\sigma_t^2) = \omega + \alpha \vert z_{t-1}\vert + \gamma z_{t-1} + \beta \ln(\sigma_{t-1}^2)$$

**Reasons for the log transformation:**

1. **Automatic non-negativity:** Since $\exp(\cdot) > 0$ for all real-valued arguments, the conditional variance $\sigma_t^2$ is mathematically guaranteed to be positive without imposing parameter constraints.
2. **Asymmetric response:** The use of $\vert z_{t-1}\vert$ (magnitude) and $z_{t-1}$ (sign) separately allows the model to differentiate between positive and negative shocks of the same absolute size.
3. **No parameter sign restrictions:** Unlike GARCH where all parameters must be non-negative, only $\alpha \geq 0$ is required in EGARCH; $\omega$, $\gamma$, and $\beta$ can take any real value.
4. **Stationarity condition simplifies:** The model is stationary whenever $\vert \beta \vert < 1$.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> **Q2. What is the "leverage effect" in financial time series? How does the EGARCH model capture it?**

**Model Answer:**
The **leverage effect**, first documented by Black (1976) and Christie (1982), is the empirical observation that **negative returns (bad news) tend to increase future volatility more than positive returns (good news) of equal magnitude**. It is also referred to as the **asymmetric volatility response**.

**How EGARCH captures it:**
In the EGARCH(1,1) specification, the conditional log-variance depends on two distinct functions of the lagged innovation $z_{t-1}$:

- The **magnitude term** $\alpha \vert z_{t-1}\vert$ — symmetric in sign.
- The **sign term** $\gamma z_{t-1}$ — asymmetric in sign.

When $\gamma < 0$ (which is the empirically observed sign in equity markets), the response of $\sigma_t^2$ to a **negative shock is greater** than its response to a positive shock of the same magnitude. The **News Impact Curve** formalizes this asymmetry and serves as a key diagnostic for confirming the presence of the leverage effect in a given dataset.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 2]**

### ❓ Question A — 14 Marks (Choice 1)

**(a) Derive the News Impact Curve for the EGARCH(1,1) model. Show explicitly that the curve is asymmetric in $z_{t-1}$ and state the condition under which the leverage effect is observed. [7 Marks]**

**(b) An EGARCH(1,1) model estimated on daily log-returns of an equity index yields the following parameters: $\omega = -0.45$, $\alpha = 0.18$, $\gamma = -0.10$, $\beta = 0.94$. If yesterday's standardized innovation was $z_{t-1} = -1.8$ and $\sigma_{t-1}^2 = 0.000625$, compute today's conditional variance. Also compute the same for $z_{t-1} = +1.8$ and comment on the asymmetry. [7 Marks]**

---

### ✅ Complete Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — Derivation of the News Impact Curve [7 Marks]

**Step 1: Write the EGARCH(1,1) specification:**

$$\ln(\sigma_t^2) = \omega + \alpha \vert z_{t-1}\vert + \gamma z_{t-1} + \beta \ln(\sigma_{t-1}^2)$$

**Step 2: Exponentiate both sides:**

$$\sigma_t^2 = \exp\bigl(\omega + \alpha \vert z_{t-1}\vert + \gamma z_{t-1} + \beta \ln(\sigma_{t-1}^2)\bigr)$$

**Step 3: Use exponential properties to separate the components:**

$$\sigma_t^2 = \exp(\omega) \cdot \exp(\alpha \vert z_{t-1}\vert) \cdot \exp(\gamma z_{t-1}) \cdot \exp(\beta \ln(\sigma_{t-1}^2))$$

**Step 4: Simplify using $\exp(\beta \ln x) = x^\beta$:**

$$\sigma_t^2 = \exp(\omega) \cdot (\sigma_{t-1}^2)^\beta \cdot \exp(\alpha \vert z_{t-1}\vert + \gamma z_{t-1})$$

**Step 5: Define the News Impact Curve by holding $\sigma_{t-1}^2 = \bar{\sigma}^2$ constant:**

$$\text{NIC}(z_{t-1}) = K \cdot \exp(\alpha \vert z_{t-1}\vert + \gamma z_{t-1})$$

where $K = \exp(\omega) \cdot \bar{\sigma}^{2\beta} > 0$.

**Step 6: Split into the two regimes using the definition of absolute value:**

- **Case 1 — Good news ($z_{t-1} \geq 0$):** $\vert z_{t-1}\vert = z_{t-1}$, hence

$$\text{NIC}(z) = K \cdot \exp\bigl((\alpha + \gamma)z\bigr), \quad z \geq 0$$

- **Case 2 — Bad news ($z_{t-1} < 0$):** $\vert z_{t-1}\vert = -z_{t-1}$, hence

$$\text{NIC}(z) = K \cdot \exp\bigl((-\alpha + \gamma)z\bigr) = K \cdot \exp\bigl((\alpha - \gamma)\vert z \vert\bigr), \quad z < 0$$

**Step 7: Identify the asymmetry condition.** Comparing the exponential decay/growth rates:

| Regime | Effective Exponent Coefficient |
|--------|-------------------------------|
| Good news ($z > 0$) | $\alpha + \gamma$ |
| Bad news ($z < 0$) | $\alpha - \gamma$ |

The curve is **asymmetric** whenever $\gamma \neq 0$. **The leverage effect is observed** when $\gamma < 0$, since then $\alpha - \gamma > \alpha + \gamma$, meaning that the magnitude of the response to negative shocks exceeds the response to positive shocks of equal absolute size. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Computation [7 Marks]

**Step 1: Given values.** $\omega = -0.45$, $\alpha = 0.18$, $\gamma = -0.10$, $\beta = 0.94$, $\sigma_{t-1}^2 = 0.000625$, so $\ln(\sigma_{t-1}^2) = \ln(0.000625) = -7.3778$.

**Case 1 — Bad news, $z_{t-1} = -1.8$:**

**Step 2:** $\vert z_{t-1}\vert = 1.8$, $z_{t-1} = -1.8$.

**Step 3:** Substitute into the log-variance equation:

$$\ln(\sigma_t^2) = -0.45 + 0.18(1.8) + (-0.10)(-1.8) + 0.94(-7.3778)$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate each term:

- $\omega = -0.450$
- $\alpha \vert z \vert = 0.18 \times 1.8 = 0.324$
- $\gamma z = -0.10 \times (-1.8) = 0.180$
- $\beta \ln(\sigma_{t-1}^2) = 0.94 \times (-7.3778) = -6.9351$

**Step 5:** Sum the contributions:

$$\ln(\sigma_t^2) = -0.450 + 0.324 + 0.180 - 6.9351 = -6.8811$$

**Step 6:** Exponentiate:

$$\sigma_t^2 = e^{-6.8811} \approx 0.001032$$

**Case 2 — Good news, $z_{t-1} = +1.8$:**

**Step 7:** $\vert z_{t-1}\vert = 1.8$, $z_{t-1} = +1.8$.

**Step 8:** Substitute:

$$\ln(\sigma_t^2) = -0.45 + 0.18(1.8) + (-0.10)(+1.8) + 0.94(-7.3778)$$

**Step 9:** The only changed term is $\gamma z = -0.10 \times 1.8 = -0.180$.

**Step 10:** New sum:

$$\ln(\sigma_t^2) = -0.450 + 0.324 - 0.180 - 6.9351 = -7.2411$$

**Step 11:** Exponentiate:

$$\sigma_t^2 = e^{-7.2411} \approx 0.000718$$

**Step 12: Asymmetry ratio and comment:**

$$\text{Asymmetry Ratio} = \frac{0.001032}{0.000718} \approx 1.438$$

**Comment:** The conditional variance under a **negative shock of $-1.8\sigma$** is approximately **43.8% higher** than under a positive shock of equal magnitude, despite both shocks having the same absolute size. This empirically confirms the **leverage effect**: the market reacts more violently to bad news than to good news. The fact that $\gamma = -0.10 < 0$ drives this asymmetry, as predicted by the News Impact Curve analysis in part (a). **[7 Marks]**

---

### ❓ Question B — 14 Marks (Choice 2 — Alternative)

**(a) Compare the GARCH(1,1), GJR-GARCH(1,1), and EGARCH(1,1) models. Specifically discuss how each model treats the sign of shocks and whether the parameters are subject to non-negativity constraints. [7 Marks]**

**(b) For an EGARCH(1,1) model with $\beta = 0.96$, compute the half-life of a volatility shock. If the constant term is $\omega = -0.50$ and $\alpha = 0.12$, compute the unconditional expected log-variance assuming Gaussian innovations. [7 Marks]**

---

### ✅ Complete Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — Comparative Analysis [7 Marks]

| Feature | GARCH(1,1) | GJR-GARCH(1,1) | EGARCH(1,1) |
|---------|------------|----------------|-------------|
| Variance equation | $\sigma_t^2 = \omega + \alpha \epsilon_{t-1}^2 + \beta \sigma_{t-1}^2$ | $\sigma_t^2 = \omega + (\alpha + \gamma I_{t-1})\epsilon_{t-1}^2 + \beta \sigma_{t-1}^2$ | $\ln(\sigma_t^2) = \omega + \alpha \vert z_{t-1}\vert + \gamma z_{t-1} + \beta \ln(\sigma_{t-1}^2)$ |
| Symmetry | **Symmetric** (depends only on $\epsilon^2$) | **Asymmetric** via indicator $I_{t-1}$ | **Asymmetric** via sign of $z_{t-1}$ |
| Sign of shock | Indistinguishable (squared) | Distinguishable only via $I_{t-1}$ (zero or one) | Distinguishable smoothly (via $\gamma z_{t-1}$) |
| Non-negativity guarantee | Requires $\alpha, \beta, \omega > 0$ | Requires $\omega > 0$, $\alpha + \gamma \geq 0$, $\beta \geq 0$ | **Automatic** via $\exp(\cdot)$ |
| Parameter constraints | Many (signs and bounds) | Some | **Minimal** (only $\alpha \geq 0$) |
| Stationarity | $\alpha + \beta < 1$ | $\alpha + 0.5\gamma + \beta < 1$ | $\vert \beta \vert < 1$ |
| Use of standardized residuals | Not required | Not required | **Required** ($z_t = \epsilon_t / \sigma_t$) |
| Computational complexity | Low | Moderate | Moderate (uses $\vert \cdot \vert$ and $\ln$) |

**Key insights for the answer:**

- **GARCH** is the simplest and most parsimonious but cannot capture the empirically observed leverage effect.
- **GJR-GARCH** (Glosten, Jagannathan, Runkle 1993) introduces asymmetry through a **step-function indicator** $I_{t-1} = 1$ if $\epsilon_{t-1} < 0$ and $0$ otherwise. It captures the leverage effect but **still requires careful parameter constraints** to keep $\sigma_t^2 > 0$.
- **EGARCH** is the most elegant in terms of constraints, using the **log transformation** to guarantee non-negativity and the **separate sign term** $\gamma z_{t-1}$ to capture asymmetry smoothly and continuously. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Part (b) — Half-Life and Unconditional Variance [7 Marks]

**Step 1: Compute the half-life of a volatility shock with $\beta = 0.96$.**

The log-variance recursion on the log-scale behaves like an **AR(1) process**, so the half-life is:

$$\text{Half-Life} = -\frac{\ln(2)}{\ln(\beta)} = \frac{\ln(2)}{-\ln(0.96)}$$

**Step 2: Evaluate numerically:**

- $-\ln(0.96) = 0.04082$
- $\ln(2) = 0.6931$

$$\text{Half-Life} = \frac{0.6931}{0.04082} \approx 16.98 \text{ days} \approx 17 \text{ days}$$

**Step 3: Interpretation.** A shock to the conditional variance decays to half of its initial magnitude in approximately **17 trading days**, which is consistent with empirical findings of multi-week volatility persistence in equity markets.

**Step 4: Compute the unconditional expected log-variance.**

The closed-form result for Gaussian innovations is:

$$E[\ln(\sigma_t^2)] = \frac{\omega + \alpha \sqrt{2/\pi}}{1 - \beta}$$

**Step 5: Substitute numerical values.**

- $\sqrt{2/\pi} = \sqrt{0.6366} \approx 0.7979$
- $\omega = -0.50$
- $\alpha = 0.12$
- $\beta = 0.96$

**Step 6: Compute the numerator:**

$$\omega + \alpha \sqrt{2/\pi} = -0.50 + 0.12 \times 0.7979 = -0.50 + 0.0957 = -0.4043$$

**Step 7: Compute the denominator:**

$$1 - \beta = 1 - 0.96 = 0.04$$

**Step 8: Divide to obtain the unconditional expected log-variance:**

$$E[\ln(\sigma_t^2)] = \frac{-0.4043}{0.04} = -10.1075$$

**Step 9: Recover the unconditional expected variance:**

$$E[\sigma_t^2] = e^{-10.1075} \approx 0.0000404$$

> This corresponds to a long-run daily standard deviation of $\sqrt{0.0000404} \approx 0.00636$ or about **0.64% per day**, equivalent to an annualized volatility of roughly $0.64\% \times \sqrt{252} \approx 10.1\%$. **[7 Marks]**

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students typically lose marks in EGARCH questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the absolute value bars on $z_{t-1}$.** Writing $\alpha z_{t-1}$ instead of $\alpha \vert z_{t-1}\vert$ loses the magnitude/lever separation and **costs 2 marks** in Part A definition questions.
> 2. **Confusing GARCH and EGARCH stationarity conditions.** GARCH requires $\alpha + \beta < 1$; EGARCH requires $\vert \beta \vert < 1$. Examiners report **~30% of students** write the wrong condition.
> 3. **Forgetting to exponentiate.** When asked to compute $\sigma_t^2$, many students leave the answer in log form. Always **end with $\sigma_t^2 = e^{(\cdot)}$**.
> 4. **Using $\epsilon_{t-1}$ instead of $z_{t-1}$ in the leverage term.** The leverage term is **always** $\gamma z_{t-1}$ (the standardized innovation), never $\gamma \epsilon_{t-1}$.
> 5. **Skipping units/scale checks.** A 0.5% daily variance gives a daily vol of ~7%, annualized vol of ~111%. Always sanity-check magnitudes.
> 6. **Mixing up sign conventions.** The leverage effect requires $\gamma < 0$, NOT $\gamma > 0$. A common sign-error trap.
> 7. **Omitting the persistence comparison.** When comparing EGARCH to GARCH/GJR, students often miss the comparison of **stationarity conditions** — a guaranteed 2-mark question.

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **EGARCH = Exponential GARCH**, introduced by **Nelson (1991)** to capture **asymmetric volatility response (leverage effect)**.
- ✅ The variance equation is in **log-form**: $\ln(\sigma_t^2) = \omega + \alpha \vert z_{t-1}\vert + \gamma z_{t-1} + \beta \ln(\sigma_{t-1}^2)$.
- ✅ **$\alpha \geq 0$** is the only non-negativity constraint; $\omega$, $\gamma$, $\beta$ can be any real number.
- ✅ **Stationarity condition:** $\vert \beta \vert < 1$ (note: NOT $\alpha + \beta < 1$ as in GARCH).
- ✅ **Leverage effect** is present when $\gamma < 0$: negative shocks raise volatility more than positive shocks.
- ✅ The **News Impact Curve** is the central diagnostic: split into good-news ($\alpha + \gamma$) and bad-news ($\alpha - \gamma$) slope coefficients.
- ✅ **Unconditional expected log-variance** (Gaussian case): $E[\ln(\sigma_t^2)] = \dfrac{\omega + \alpha \sqrt{2/\pi}}{1 - \beta}$.
- ✅ **Half-life of volatility shocks:** $t_{1/2} = -\ln(2)/\ln(\vert \beta \vert) = \ln(2)/\ln(1/\vert \beta \vert)$.
- ✅ EGARCH uses **standardized residuals** $z_t = \epsilon_t / \sigma_t$, unlike GARCH and GJR-GARCH.
- ✅ Estimation is by **Maximum Likelihood** under Gaussian (or Student-$t$, GED) assumptions.
- ✅ The **persistence** under EGARCH is solely governed by $\vert \beta \vert$, making it a clean measure of volatility memory.
- ✅ **Model selection** uses AIC / BIC; EGARCH is preferred over GARCH when the News Impact Curve is significantly asymmetric.
- ✅ **Empirical application:** EGARCH is heavily used for **Value-at-Risk (VaR)** forecasting in equity, FX, and commodity markets.
- ✅ **Common KTU confusion trap:** EGARCH(p,q) uses the **sign of $z_{t-1}$**, not the **sign of $\epsilon_{t-1}$**, in the leverage term.
- ✅ **Quick check formula:** If $\beta = 0.95$, half-life $\approx 13.5$ days; if $\beta = 0.98$, half-life $\approx 34.3$ days.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: CHARMA Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET742 - FINANCIAL TIME SERIES ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** CHARMA Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
