## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

The **CHARMA Model** (Conditional Heteroskedastic Autoregressive Moving Average) is a time series model that extends the classical **ARMA (Autoregressive Moving Average)** framework by allowing the **conditional variance** of the residuals to evolve over time, rather than remaining constant (homoskedastic). In essence, the CHARMA model simultaneously captures two key features of financial time series:

1. The **conditional mean** of the process is modeled by an ARMA structure.
2. The **conditional variance** (volatility) is modeled by a function of past squared innovations and/or past conditional variances.

Formally, a CHARMA$(p, q)(P, Q)$ model can be written as:

$$y_t = \mu_t + \epsilon_t$$

$$\mu_t = \phi_0 + \sum_{i=1}^{p} \phi_i y_{t-i} + \sum_{j=1}^{q} \theta_j \epsilon_{t-j}$$

$$\epsilon_t = \sigma_t z_t, \quad z_t \sim \text{i.i.d.}(0,1)$$

$$\sigma_t^2 = \alpha_0 + \sum_{i=1}^{P} \alpha_i \epsilon_{t-i}^2 + \sum_{j=1}^{Q} \beta_j \sigma_{t-j}^2$$

where $p, q$ are the ARMA orders for the mean, $P, Q$ are the orders for the variance equation, and $\{z_t\}$ is a sequence of independent and identically distributed random variables with zero mean and unit variance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:** CHARMA is positioned in Module 2 as a critical bridge model between classical ARMA and modern GARCH-family volatility models. Students are expected to derive its properties, stationarity conditions, and interpret volatility persistence.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are sailing a boat across a wavy ocean. The **average path** of the boat is your *conditional mean* (analogous to the ARMA part). However, some days the waves are calm (low variance), and other days the waves are violent storms (high variance). The **CHARMA model** is like having a sophisticated navigation system that:

- Predicts the *expected route* of the boat using past positions and past wave disturbances (the ARMA mean equation).
- Simultaneously predicts *how rough the waves will be* today based on how rough they were yesterday and how big the wave disturbances were recently (the conditional variance equation).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Financial returns tend to exhibit *volatility clustering* — large changes are followed by large changes, and small changes by small changes. CHARMA captures this "calm-then-storm-then-calm" pattern mathematically.

### Important Standard Metrics

- **Volatility Persistence:** Sum of coefficients in the variance equation ($\alpha + \beta$).
- **Stationarity Threshold:** $\alpha + \beta < 1$.
- **Unconditional Variance:** $\sigma^2 = \frac{\alpha_0}{1 - (\alpha_1 + \beta_1)}$.
- **Half-life of shocks:** $h = \frac{\ln(0.5)}{\ln(\alpha_1 + \beta_1)}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Bold Notation Convention:** Standard deviation is denoted by $\sigma_t$ in **bold form** in exam answers to denote its time-varying nature, distinguishing it from the constant $\sigma$ of a homoskedastic ARMA process.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Decomposition of the CHARMA Model

The CHARMA model fundamentally decomposes a financial time series $y_t$ into two coupled sub-processes:

**Step 1: The Conditional Mean Equation (ARMA Core)**

This equation captures the predictable, linear dependence in the data:
- **AR component** $\sum_{i=1}^{p} \phi_i y_{t-i}$: Captures momentum and mean-reversion in the series.
- **MA component** $\sum_{j=1}^{q} \theta_j \epsilon_{t-j}$: Captures the delayed effect of past shocks.
- **Intercept** $\phi_0$: Long-run equilibrium level of the process.

**Step 2: The Innovation Process**

The innovation $\epsilon_t$ is decomposed as:
- $\sigma_t$: The *time-varying standard deviation* (volatility).
- $z_t$: A standardized, unpredictable random shock (white noise).

**Step 3: The Conditional Variance Equation (GARCH Core)**

The variance equation captures the evolution of risk:
- $\alpha_0$: Baseline (long-run) variance floor.
- $\alpha_i \epsilon_{t-i}^2$: Reaction to past squared shocks (ARCH effect — news impact).
- $\beta_j \sigma_{t-j}^2$: Reaction to past volatility levels (GARCH effect — persistence of volatility).

### 2.2 Why CHARMA? — The "Why" Behind Each Step

| Component | Mathematical Purpose | Financial Interpretation |
|-----------|----------------------|--------------------------|
| ARMA mean | Removes predictable linear structure | Detrends the return series |
| $\epsilon_t$ | Captures unpredictable innovation | Daily unexpected return |
| $\sigma_t^2$ | Time-varying risk | Today's market volatility |
| $\alpha_i$ | Short-term shock sensitivity | Market reaction to news |
| $\beta_j$ | Long-term volatility memory | Volatility clustering effect |
| $z_t$ | Pure randomness | Idiosyncratic noise |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Condition / Note** |
|--------------|-------------|----------------------|
| Mean equation | $\mu_t = \phi_0 + \sum_{i=1}^{p} \phi_i y_{t-i} + \sum_{j=1}^{q} \theta_j \epsilon_{t-j}$ | ARMA$(p,q)$ form |
| Innovation | $\epsilon_t = \sigma_t z_t$ | $z_t \sim \text{i.i.d.}(0,1)$ |
| Variance equation | $\sigma_t^2 = \alpha_0 + \sum_{i=1}^{P} \alpha_i \epsilon_{t-i}^2 + \sum_{j=1}^{Q} \beta_j \sigma_{t-j}^2$ | ARCH-GARCH hybrid |
| Stationarity of variance | $\sum \alpha_i + \sum \beta_j < 1$ | Strict stationarity requirement |
| Non-negativity | $\alpha_0 > 0,\ \alpha_i \geq 0,\ \beta_j \geq 0$ | Ensures $\sigma_t^2 > 0$ |
| Unconditional variance | $\sigma^2 = \dfrac{\alpha_0}{1 - \left(\sum \alpha_i + \sum \beta_j\right)}$ | Long-run variance |
| Persistence | $\delta = \sum \alpha_i + \sum \beta_j$ | $\delta \to 1$ implies IGARCH |
| Half-life | $h = \dfrac{\ln(0.5)}{\ln(\delta)}$ | Time for shock to halve |
| Mean equation stationarity | Roots of $\phi(z) = 1 - \sum \phi_i z^i$ outside unit circle | ARMA stationarity |
| Invertibility | Roots of $\theta(z) = 1 + \sum \theta_j z^j$ outside unit circle | MA invertibility |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note for KTU Exams:** Always clearly label the *mean* equation and the *variance* equation separately. Examiners award partial credit for clearly distinguishing these two coupled structures.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering & Financial Utility

- **Risk Management:** Used by banks to compute Value-at-Risk (VaR) and Expected Shortfall.
- **Derivatives Pricing:** Option pricing models (e.g., Heston) embed CHARMA-like variance dynamics.
- **Algorithmic Trading:** Volatility forecasting informs position sizing and stop-loss placement.
- **Macroeconomic Forecasting:** Central banks use CHARMA models to forecast inflation volatility.
- **Energy Markets:** Electricity price volatility is famously modeled with ARCH-family models.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Unconditional Variance

We derive the long-run (unconditional) variance of a CHARMA$(0,0)(1,1)$ model (i.e., a GARCH$(1,1)$ combined with constant mean).

**Starting variance equation:**

$$\sigma_t^2 = \alpha_0 + \alpha_1 \epsilon_{t-1}^2 + \beta_1 \sigma_{t-1}^2$$

**Step 1:** Take the unconditional expectation on both sides. Denote $E[\cdot]$ by dropping subscripts. By the law of total expectation and stationarity, $E[\sigma_t^2] = E[\sigma_{t-1}^2] = \sigma^2$ and $E[\epsilon_{t-1}^2] = \sigma^2$.

$$\sigma^2 = \alpha_0 + \alpha_1 \sigma^2 + \beta_1 \sigma^2$$

**Step 2:** Rearrange to isolate $\sigma^2$:

$$\sigma^2 - \alpha_1 \sigma^2 - \beta_1 \sigma^2 = \alpha_0$$

**Step 3:** Factor out $\sigma^2$:

$$\sigma^2 \left(1 - \alpha_1 - \beta_1\right) = \alpha_0$$

**Step 4:** Solve for $\sigma^2$:

$$\boxed{\sigma^2 = \frac{\alpha_0}{1 - \alpha_1 - \beta_1}}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** This result is the cornerstone of KTU valuation. The unconditional variance exists (is finite) *if and only if* $\alpha_1 + \beta_1 < 1$, which is the **strict stationarity condition** for the variance process.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Half-Life of a Volatility Shock

We wish to determine how long it takes for a unit shock in $\epsilon_t^2$ to decay to half its impact on $\sigma_{t+h}^2$.

**Starting point:** Consider the impulse response of a GARCH$(1,1)$ model:

$$\sigma_{t+h}^2 = \alpha_0 (1 + (\alpha_1 + \beta_1) + \cdots + (\alpha_1 + \beta_1)^{h-1}) + (\alpha_1 + \beta_1)^h \sigma_t^2$$

**Step 1:** The impact of the initial shock $\sigma_t^2$ on $\sigma_{t+h}^2$ decays as $(\alpha_1 + \beta_1)^h$.

**Step 2:** Set the decay factor to $\frac{1}{2}$:

$$(\alpha_1 + \beta_1)^h = \frac{1}{2}$$

**Step 3:** Take natural logarithm on both sides:

$$h \ln(\alpha_1 + \beta_1) = \ln\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) = -\ln 2$$

**Step 4:** Solve for $h$:

$$\boxed{h = \frac{-\ln 2}{\ln(\alpha_1 + \beta_1)} = \frac{\ln(0.5)}{\ln(\alpha_1 + \beta_1)}}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** If $\alpha_1 + \beta_1 = 0.95$, then $h = \frac{\ln 0.5}{\ln 0.95} \approx 13.5$ days, meaning a shock's impact halves every ~13 trading days. This is empirically observed in equity markets.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of the Autocorrelation Function of Squared Returns

We derive the autocorrelation structure of $\epsilon_t^2$ under a GARCH$(1,1)$ process to show how CHARMA explains volatility clustering.

**Step 1:** The conditional expectation of $\epsilon_t^2$ given past information is:

$$E[\epsilon_t^2 \mid \mathcal{F}_{t-1}] = \sigma_t^2 = \alpha_0 + \alpha_1 \epsilon_{t-1}^2 + \beta_1 \sigma_{t-1}^2$$

**Step 2:** Multiply both sides by $\epsilon_{t-1}^2$ and take unconditional expectation:

$$E[\epsilon_t^2 \epsilon_{t-1}^2] = \alpha_0 E[\epsilon_{t-1}^2] + \alpha_1 E[\epsilon_{t-1}^4] + \beta_1 E[\epsilon_{t-1}^2 \sigma_{t-1}^2]$$

**Step 3:** Using $E[\epsilon_{t-1}^2 \sigma_{t-1}^2] = E[\sigma_{t-1}^2 E[\epsilon_{t-1}^2 \mid \mathcal{F}_{t-2}]] = E[\sigma_{t-1}^4]$ and standard moment results for a standardized distribution, the autocorrelation of squared returns at lag $k$ is:

$$\rho_k(\epsilon_t^2) \approx (\alpha_1 + \beta_1)^k$$

**Step 4:** This geometric decay is the **mathematical signature of volatility clustering**.

> **Conversion Logic:** The Ljung-Box test on squared returns is the standard diagnostic that justifies fitting a CHARMA model. If autocorrelations decay geometrically, the GARCH specification is empirically supported.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** Consider a CHARMA$(1,0)(1,1)$ model:
$$y_t = 0.05 + 0.6 y_{t-1} + \epsilon_t$$
$$\sigma_t^2 = 0.02 + 0.1 \epsilon_{t-1}^2 + 0.85 \sigma_{t-1}^2$$
Compute the one-step-ahead forecast of $y_{t+1}$ and $\sigma_{t+1}^2$ given $y_t = 0.5$, $\epsilon_t^2 = 0.04$, and $\sigma_t^2 = 0.05$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Forecast the mean:**
$$E[y_{t+1} \mid \mathcal{F}_t] = 0.05 + 0.6 \cdot y_t = 0.05 + 0.6(0.5) = 0.05 + 0.30 = 0.35$$

**Step 2 — Forecast the variance:**
$$E[\sigma_{t+1}^2 \mid \mathcal{F}_t] = 0.02 + 0.1 \cdot \epsilon_t^2 + 0.85 \cdot \sigma_t^2 = 0.02 + 0.1(0.04) + 0.85(0.05)$$
$$= 0.02 + 0.004 + 0.0425 = 0.0665$$

**Step 3 — Persistence check:**
$$\alpha_1 + \beta_1 = 0.1 + 0.85 = 0.95 < 1 \quad \checkmark$$
The model is strictly stationary and covariance-stationary.

**Step 4 — Unconditional variance:**
$$\sigma^2 = \frac{0.02}{1 - 0.95} = \frac{0.02}{0.05} = 0.4$$

**Step 5 — Half-life of shocks:**
$$h = \frac{\ln 0.5}{\ln 0.95} = \frac{-0.6931}{-0.0513} \approx 13.5 \text{ periods}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the CHARMA Model

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Input: Historical Returns y_t-1, y_t-2, ... y_t-p"] --> B["AR Component: Sum phi_i y_t-i"]
    C["Input: Past Errors epsilon_t-1, ... epsilon_t-q"] --> D["MA Component: Sum theta_j epsilon_t-j"]
    E["Constant: phi_0"] --> F["Mean Equation Aggregator"]
    B --> F
    D --> F
    F --> G["Compute mu_t = Conditional Mean"]
    G --> H["Compute epsilon_t = y_t minus mu_t"]
    H --> I["Square and Feed to Variance Engine"]
    I --> J["ARCH Term: Sum alpha_i epsilon_t-i squared"]
    K["Past Variances sigma_t-1, ... sigma_t-Q"] --> L["GARCH Term: Sum beta_j sigma_t-j squared"]
    M["Baseline alpha_0"] --> N["Variance Aggregator"]
    J --> N
    L --> N
    N --> O["Output: sigma_t squared = Conditional Variance"]
    O --> P["Volatility Forecast sigma_t+1"]
    H --> Q["Standardized Residual z_t = epsilon_t / sigma_t"]
    Q --> R["Diagnostic Check: Ljung-Box on z_t squared"]
```

### 4.2 Information Flow and Coupling Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Past Returns Y_t-1"] --> C["Mean Equation mu_t"]
    B["Past Errors E_t-1"] --> C
    C --> D["Current Innovation epsilon_t"]
    D --> E["Variance Equation sigma_t squared"]
    E --> F["Standardized Shock z_t"]
    F --> G["Next Period Innovation epsilon_t+1"]
    D --> H["Squared Shock E_t squared"]
    H --> E
    E --> I["Volatility Forecast for t+1"]
```

### 4.3 Volatility Clustering Behavior Over Time

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Time t: Calm Market"] --> B["Small sigma_t squared"]
    B --> C["Small epsilon_t"]
    C --> D["Small epsilon_t squared"]
    D --> E["Tomorrow's variance stays low"]
    E --> A
    A --> F["Alternative Path: Sudden News"]
    F --> G["Large epsilon_t squared"]
    G --> H["High variance tomorrow"]
    H --> I["Clustered High Volatility"]
    I --> J["Eventual Decay to Calm"]
    J --> A
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** The arrows show the recursive feedback loop. The squared innovation $\epsilon_{t-1}^2$ feeds into the variance equation, which scales the next innovation $\epsilon_t$. This feedback is the mathematical engine of volatility clustering.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define the CHARMA model. State the conditions for its strict stationarity.*

**Model Answer:**
The CHARMA (Conditional Heteroskedastic Autoregressive Moving Average) model is a time series specification that combines an ARMA structure for the conditional mean with a GARCH-type structure for the conditional variance. Mathematically, for a CHARMA$(p,q)(P,Q)$ process:

$$y_t = \mu_t + \epsilon_t,\quad \mu_t = \phi_0 + \sum_{i=1}^{p} \phi_i y_{t-i} + \sum_{j=1}^{q} \theta_j \epsilon_{t-j}$$

$$\epsilon_t = \sigma_t z_t,\quad \sigma_t^2 = \alpha_0 + \sum_{i=1}^{P} \alpha_i \epsilon_{t-i}^2 + \sum_{j=1}^{Q} \beta_j \sigma_{t-j}^2$$

**Strict Stationarity Conditions:**
1. All roots of the AR characteristic polynomial $\phi(z) = 1 - \sum \phi_i z^i$ lie strictly outside the unit circle.
2. All roots of the MA characteristic polynomial $\theta(z) = 1 + \sum \theta_j z^j$ lie strictly outside the unit circle.
3. The variance coefficients satisfy $\alpha_0 > 0$, $\alpha_i \geq 0$, $\beta_j \geq 0$, and $\sum_{i=1}^{P} \alpha_i + \sum_{j=1}^{Q} \beta_j < 1$.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Explain the concept of volatility persistence in a CHARMA model. Define the half-life of a volatility shock.*

**Model Answer:**
**Volatility persistence** measures the degree to which a current shock to the conditional variance continues to affect future variance forecasts. In a GARCH$(1,1)$-type CHARMA variance equation, persistence is quantified by the sum $\delta = \alpha_1 + \beta_1$. A value of $\delta$ close to 1 indicates high persistence — shocks decay slowly, leading to long-lasting volatility clusters.

The **half-life of a volatility shock** is the number of periods $h$ required for the impact of a one-time shock to the variance to decay to half its original magnitude. It is computed as:

$$h = \frac{\ln(0.5)}{\ln(\alpha_1 + \beta_1)}$$

For example, if $\delta = 0.95$, the half-life is approximately **13.5 periods**, indicating that volatility shocks in equity markets typically take about two trading weeks to halve in impact.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a)** *For a CHARMA$(1,0)(1,1)$ model, derive the expression for the unconditional variance. State all necessary stationarity conditions. **(7 marks)***

**(b)** *Given a GARCH$(1,1)$ variance equation $\sigma_t^2 = 0.05 + 0.08 \epsilon_{t-1}^2 + 0.90 \sigma_{t-1}^2$, compute the unconditional variance, persistence parameter, and the half-life of a volatility shock. Comment on the implications for financial risk management. **(7 marks)***

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation:**

We start with the variance equation of a CHARMA$(1,0)(1,1)$ model:

$$\sigma_t^2 = \alpha_0 + \alpha_1 \epsilon_{t-1}^2 + \beta_1 \sigma_{t-2}^2$$

Wait — correcting to a clean GARCH$(1,1)$ form for clarity:

$$\sigma_t^2 = \alpha_0 + \alpha_1 \epsilon_{t-1}^2 + \beta_1 \sigma_{t-1}^2$$

**Step 1:** Apply the unconditional expectation operator under stationarity. Since $\{y_t\}$ is covariance-stationary, $E[\sigma_t^2] = E[\sigma_{t-1}^2] = \sigma^2$ and $E[\epsilon_{t-1}^2] = \sigma^2$.

**Step 2:** Substituting:

$$\sigma^2 = \alpha_0 + \alpha_1 \sigma^2 + \beta_1 \sigma^2$$

**Step 3:** Rearranging:

$$\sigma^2 (1 - \alpha_1 - \beta_1) = \alpha_0$$

**Step 4:** Final result:

$$\sigma^2 = \frac{\alpha_0}{1 - \alpha_1 - \beta_1}$$

**Stationarity Conditions:**
- $\alpha_0 > 0$ ensures positivity of the long-run variance.
- $\alpha_1 \geq 0$ and $\beta_1 \geq 0$ prevent negative variance.
- $\alpha_1 + \beta_1 < 1$ guarantees finite, positive unconditional variance.
- The mean equation is stationary if the AR root $|1/\phi_1| > 1$, i.e., $\vert \phi_1 \vert < 1$.

**(b) Numerical Computation:**

Given: $\alpha_0 = 0.05$, $\alpha_1 = 0.08$, $\beta_1 = 0.90$.

**Step 1 — Unconditional variance:**

$$\sigma^2 = \frac{0.05}{1 - 0.08 - 0.90} = \frac{0.05}{0.02} = 2.5$$

So $\sigma \approx \sqrt{2.5} \approx 1.581$.

**Step 2 — Persistence:**

$$\delta = \alpha_1 + \beta_1 = 0.08 + 0.90 = 0.98$$

This is very close to 1, indicating **extremely high volatility persistence**.

**Step 3 — Half-life:**

$$h = \frac{\ln(0.5)}{\ln(0.98)} = \frac{-0.6931}{-0.0202} \approx 34.3 \text{ periods}$$

**Implications for Risk Management:**
- The long-run standard deviation of 1.581 implies substantial baseline risk.
- High persistence ($\delta = 0.98$) means shocks take ~34 periods to halve — VaR estimates must use long historical windows.
- The model is on the boundary of being an IGARCH (Integrated GARCH); risk managers should monitor for parameter instability.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** *Explain the concept of volatility clustering. Show mathematically how a CHARMA model generates this empirical regularity. **(7 marks)***

**(b)** *Consider the model $y_t = 0.02 + 0.7 y_{t-1} + \epsilon_t$, where $\epsilon_t = \sigma_t z_t$ and $\sigma_t^2 = 0.01 + 0.05 \epsilon_{t-1}^2 + 0.93 \sigma_{t-1}^2$. Compute the 1-step-ahead forecast of $y_{t+1}$ and $\sigma_{t+1}^2$ given $y_t = 0.10$, $\epsilon_t^2 = 0.02$, and $\sigma_t^2 = 0.03$. Verify the stationarity conditions. **(7 marks)***

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Volatility Clustering and CHARMA:**

**Volatility clustering** is the empirical observation in financial time series that large absolute returns tend to be followed by large absolute returns, and small absolute returns by small absolute returns. This phenomenon is observed in equity, FX, and commodity markets.

**Mathematical Derivation:**

Consider the GARCH$(1,1)$ variance equation:

$$\sigma_t^2 = \alpha_0 + \alpha_1 \epsilon_{t-1}^2 + \beta_1 \sigma_{t-1}^2$$

**Step 1:** If a large shock occurs at time $t-1$, then $\epsilon_{t-1}^2$ is large.

**Step 2:** This large $\epsilon_{t-1}^2$ increases $\sigma_t^2$ through the ARCH term $\alpha_1 \epsilon_{t-1}^2$.

**Step 3:** A high $\sigma_t^2$ scales up any realization of $\epsilon_t = \sigma_t z_t$, making large $\vert \epsilon_t \vert$ more likely.

**Step 4:** Consequently, $\epsilon_t^2$ tends to be large, which feeds back into $\sigma_{t+1}^2$ through the same mechanism.

**Step 5:** This positive feedback loop mathematically generates volatility clustering. The autocorrelation function of squared returns is:

$$\rho_k(\epsilon_t^2) \approx (\alpha_1 + \beta_1)^k$$

This geometric decay with positive coefficients for many lags is the statistical signature of clustering.

**(b) Numerical Forecasting:**

**Given:**
- Mean equation: $y_t = 0.02 + 0.7 y_{t-1} + \epsilon_t$
- Variance equation: $\sigma_t^2 = 0.01 + 0.05 \epsilon_{t-1}^2 + 0.93 \sigma_{t-1}^2$
- Current values: $y_t = 0.10$, $\epsilon_t^2 = 0.02$, $\sigma_t^2 = 0.03$

**Step 1 — Forecast the conditional mean:**

$$E[y_{t+1} \mid \mathcal{F}_t] = 0.02 + 0.7 \cdot y_t = 0.02 + 0.7(0.10) = 0.02 + 0.07 = 0.09$$

**Step 2 — Forecast the conditional variance:**

$$E[\sigma_{t+1}^2 \mid \mathcal{F}_t] = 0.01 + 0.05 \cdot \epsilon_t^2 + 0.93 \cdot \sigma_t^2$$
$$= 0.01 + 0.05(0.02) + 0.93(0.03)$$
$$= 0.01 + 0.001 + 0.0279 = 0.0389$$

**Step 3 — Stationarity checks:**

*Mean equation:* $\vert \phi_1 \vert = 0.7 < 1$ ✓ (stationary).

*Variance equation:*
- $\alpha_0 = 0.01 > 0$ ✓
- $\alpha_1 = 0.05 \geq 0$ ✓
- $\beta_1 = 0.93 \geq 0$ ✓
- $\alpha_1 + \beta_1 = 0.05 + 0.93 = 0.98 < 1$ ✓ (strictly stationary).

**Step 4 — Unconditional variance:**

$$\sigma^2 = \frac{0.01}{1 - 0.98} = \frac{0.01}{0.02} = 0.5$$

**Step 5 — Half-life:**

$$h = \frac{\ln(0.5)}{\ln(0.98)} = \frac{-0.6931}{-0.0202} \approx 34.3 \text{ periods}$$

The model is valid, stationary, and exhibits high volatility persistence.

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in CHARMA Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing mean stationarity with variance stationarity.** A CHARMA model has *two* stationarity requirements: (i) the ARMA mean must be stationary ($\vert \phi_i \vert < 1$), and (ii) the variance equation must satisfy $\sum \alpha_i + \sum \beta_j < 1$. Forgetting one loses 2-3 marks.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the conditional expectation notation.** Always write $E[y_{t+1} \mid \mathcal{F}_t]$ rather than $E[y_{t+1}]$. The conditioning on the sigma-algebra $\mathcal{F}_t$ is a hallmark of rigorous time series answers and earns full credit.
>
> 3. **Using $z_t$ incorrectly.** Students often write $z_t \sim N(0,1)$ — but the CHARMA model only requires $z_t$ to be i.i.d. with zero mean and unit variance. Common alternatives include Student-$t$ or GED (Generalized Error Distribution). Specifying normality when not assumed loses a mark.
>
> 4. **Negativity violations.** Writing a variance equation with $\alpha_1 = -0.05$ violates the non-negativity constraint. Examiners deduct heavily for this.
>
> 5. **Skipping the persistence interpretation.** In numerical problems, always end with a one-sentence *interpretation* of the parameters — e.g., "the high persistence of 0.98 indicates strong volatility clustering."
>
> 6. **Miscounting the half-life formula.** The half-life formula is $h = \ln(0.5)/\ln(\delta)$, NOT $\ln(2)/\ln(\delta)$. Both give the same magnitude but the sign convention matters. Use $\ln(0.5)$ which is negative, divided by $\ln(\delta)$ which is also negative (since $\delta < 1$), giving a positive $h$.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for CHARMA:**

- ⭐ **CHARMA = ARMA Mean + GARCH Variance** — always remember this dual structure.
- ⭐ The **mean equation** is ARMA$(p,q)$ with parameters $\phi_i, \theta_j$ and intercept $\phi_0$.
- ⭐ The **innovation** decomposes as $\epsilon_t = \sigma_t z_t$, where $z_t$ is i.i.d.$(0,1)$.
- ⭐ The **variance equation** is a GARCH$(P,Q)$ form with parameters $\alpha_0, \alpha_i, \beta_j$.
- ⭐ **Strict stationarity of variance** requires $\alpha_0 > 0$, $\alpha_i \geq 0$, $\beta_j \geq 0$, and $\sum \alpha_i + \sum \beta_j < 1$.
- ⭐ **Unconditional variance formula:** $\sigma^2 = \dfrac{\alpha_0}{1 - \sum \alpha_i - \sum \beta_j}$.
- ⭐ **Persistence** is $\delta = \sum \alpha_i + \sum \beta_j$. As $\delta \to 1$, the process becomes an IGARCH (Integrated GARCH).
- ⭐ **Half-life of shock:** $h = \dfrac{\ln(0.5)}{\ln(\delta)}$ — a unit shock's impact halves in $h$ periods.
- ⭐ **Volatility clustering** is generated by the ARCH feedback: large $\epsilon^2$ → high $\sigma^2$ → likely large $\epsilon$ → large $\epsilon^2$ → ... and so on.
- ⭐ **Autocorrelation of squared returns:** $\rho_k(\epsilon_t^2) \approx \delta^k$ — geometric decay is the empirical signature.
- ⭐ **Forecasting** is two-step: (1) forecast mean using ARMA structure, (2) forecast variance using GARCH structure with squared residuals and past variances.
- ⭐ **Diagnostic test:** Ljung-Box test on squared standardized residuals $z_t^2$ — should show no remaining autocorrelation if the model is well-specified.
- ⭐ **Practical importance:** VaR, option pricing, position sizing, and risk capital allocation all rely on CHARMA-style volatility forecasts.
- ⭐ **Exam tip:** Always separate the two equations with clear headings ("Mean Equation" and "Variance Equation") — examiners give partial credit generously when structure is clear.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Random Coefficient Autoregressive Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET742 - FINANCIAL TIME SERIES ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Random Coefficient Autoregressive Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RANDOM COEFFICIENT AUTOREGRESSIVE (RCA) MODEL — FORMAL DEFINITION**
>
> The **Random Coefficient Autoregressive model of order 1**, denoted **RCA(1)**, is a nonlinear stochastic process in which the autoregressive (AR) coefficient is itself treated as a **random variable** drawn independently at each time step. The canonical form, due to **Granger and Newbold (1986)** and formalised by **Aue, Horváth & Reeder (2011)**, is:
>
> $$X_t = \phi_t \, X_{t-1} + \epsilon_t, \quad t \in \mathbb{Z}$$
>
> where:
> - $\{\phi_t\}$ is a sequence of i.i.d. random variables with mean $E[\phi_t] = \phi$ and variance $\text{Var}(\phi_t) = \sigma_\phi^2$.
> - $\{\epsilon_t\}$ is a sequence of i.i.d. innovations with $E[\epsilon_t] = 0$ and variance $\sigma_\epsilon^2$.
> - $\{\phi_t\}$ and $\{\epsilon_t\}$ are mutually independent.

### 1.1.1 The Augmented Form

A more general version, useful in applied econometric modelling (especially when $X_t$ has a non-zero unconditional mean), is:

$$X_t - \mu = \phi_t \, (X_{t-1} - \mu) + \epsilon_t$$

where $\mu = E[X_t]$ is the long-run mean. For the standard RCA(1), we set $\mu = 0$.

## 1.2 Intuition: The "Unpredictable Spring" Analogy

Imagine a metal spring whose stiffness is not fixed but **randomly re-set every second** before the next oscillation is measured. Some seconds the spring is stiff (coefficient close to 1 → high persistence), some seconds it is loose (coefficient near 0 → quick mean-reversion), and occasionally it is even negative (oscillation/overshoot).

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Intuition**
> In a *standard* AR(1) model, the "memory" of yesterday's value is fixed — the system always remembers the past with the *same* strength. In the **RCA(1) model**, this memory strength itself flickers randomly from day to day. This produces a rich, realistic feature: **volatility clustering** — long calm periods (weak coefficients) followed by long stormy periods (strong coefficients), which is exactly what is observed in stock returns, FX rates, and commodity prices.

### 1.3 Why RCA(1) Matters in Finance

| Financial Phenomenon | Captured by RCA(1)? |
|---|---|
| Volatility clustering | ✅ Yes (via random $\phi_t$) |
| Heavy tails in returns | ✅ Yes (non-Gaussian marginals) |
| Non-linear dependence | ✅ Yes (coefficient depends on regime) |
| Mean reversion with stochastic speed | ✅ Yes |
| Pure linear AR dynamics | ❌ No (needs standard AR(1)) |

> [!TIP]
> **Key Highlight for KTU Exam:** The RCA(1) model is the **direct bridge between simple linear AR models and ARCH-type volatility models**. Understanding it unlocks half of Module 2.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Anatomy of the RCA(1) Process

We dissect the model layer by layer:

- **Step 1 — Random Coefficient Layer:** $\phi_t \sim \text{i.i.d.}(\phi, \sigma_\phi^2)$. This is the "engine" of nonlinearity. The coefficient is drawn afresh at every tick.
- **Step 2 — Memory Layer:** The product $\phi_t X_{t-1}$ propagates past shocks into the present, but with stochastic amplification or attenuation.
- **Step 3 — Innovation Layer:** $\epsilon_t \sim \text{WN}(0, \sigma_\epsilon^2)$ adds new randomness independent of past information.
- **Step 4 — Composition:** $X_t$ is therefore a *stochastic* linear combination of past information plus innovation.

## 2.2 Conditional vs. Unconditional Moments

### 2.2.1 Conditional Mean (Given $\mathcal{F}_{t-1}$)

$$E[X_t \mid \mathcal{F}_{t-1}] = E[\phi_t \mid \mathcal{F}_{t-1}] \, X_{t-1} + E[\epsilon_t \mid \mathcal{F}_{t-1}] = \phi \, X_{t-1}$$

This **mirrors the standard AR(1) conditional mean** — the average behaviour is linear.

### 2.2.2 Conditional Variance (Given $\mathcal{F}_{t-1}$)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Var}(X_t \mid \mathcal{F}_{t-1}) &= E[(X_t - \phi X_{t-1})^2 \mid \mathcal{F}_{t-1}] \\
&= E[\phi_t^2 \mid \mathcal{F}_{t-1}] \, X_{t-1}^2 + E[\epsilon_t^2 \mid \mathcal{F}_{t-1}] \\
&= (\phi^2 + \sigma_\phi^2) \, X_{t-1}^2 + \sigma_\epsilon^2
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **famous ARCH-like conditional variance formula**. Note that the variance **depends on the past squared value** — exactly the hallmark of an **ARCH(1)** structure when applied to $X_t^2$.

### 2.2.3 Unconditional (Long-Run) Variance

For stationarity, we require $\phi^2 + \sigma_\phi^2 < 1$. Then:

$$
\gamma_0 = \text{Var}(X_t) = \frac{\sigma_\epsilon^2}{1 - \phi^2 - \sigma_\phi^2}
$$

### 2.2.4 Autocovariance Function (ACVF)

$$
\gamma_k = \frac{(\phi^2 + \sigma_\phi^2) \cdot \phi^{k-1}}{1 - \phi^2 - \sigma_\phi^2} \cdot \sigma_\epsilon^2, \quad k \geq 1
$$

Dividing by $\gamma_0$ yields the autocorrelation function (ACF):

$$
\rho_k = \frac{\gamma_k}{\gamma_0} = (\phi^2 + \sigma_\phi^2) \cdot \phi^{k-1}, \quad k \geq 1
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The ACF has the same *exponential decay shape* as an AR(1) but multiplied by the factor $(\phi^2 + \sigma_\phi^2)$. This is one of the most testable results in the KTU syllabus.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — RCA(1) Master Table

> [!TIP]
> **Print this table. It covers ~70% of numerical questions on this topic.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Remarks |
|:---:|---|---|---|
| 1 | Model definition | $X_t = \phi_t X_{t-1} + \epsilon_t$ | $\phi_t \overset{\text{iid}}{\sim}(\phi, \sigma_\phi^2)$ |
| 2 | Conditional mean | $E[X_t \mid \mathcal{F}_{t-1}] = \phi X_{t-1}$ | Same shape as AR(1) |
| 3 | Conditional variance | $\text{Var}(X_t \mid \mathcal{F}_{t-1}) = (\phi^2 + \sigma_\phi^2) X_{t-1}^2 + \sigma_\epsilon^2$ | **ARCH(1)-like** |
| 4 | Unconditional mean | $E[X_t] = 0$ | Assumed centred |
| 5 | Stationarity condition | $\phi^2 + \sigma_\phi^2 < 1$ | Necessary & sufficient |
| 6 | Unconditional variance | $\gamma_0 = \sigma_\epsilon^2 / (1 - \phi^2 - \sigma_\phi^2)$ | Finite iff condition holds |
| 7 | Autocorrelation $\rho_1$ | $\rho_1 = \phi$ | **First-order ACF** |
| 8 | Autocorrelation $\rho_k$ | $\rho_k = (\phi^2 + \sigma_\phi^2) \phi^{k-1}$ | Geometric decay |
| 9 | Squared series AR rep. | $X_t^2 = (\phi^2 + \sigma_\phi^2) X_{t-1}^2 + \eta_t$ | AR(1)-type for $X_t^2$ |
| 10 | Equivalent ARCH(1) | $\sigma_t^2 = \alpha_0 + \alpha_1 X_{t-1}^2$ | with $\alpha_0 = \sigma_\epsilon^2$, $\alpha_1 = \phi^2 + \sigma_\phi^2$ |

**Notation conventions used above:**

- $\mathcal{F}_{t-1}$ = sigma-algebra generated by $\{X_s, \phi_s, \epsilon_s : s \leq t-1\}$.
- $\gamma_k = \text{Cov}(X_t, X_{t-k})$ (assuming stationarity).
- $\rho_k = \gamma_k / \gamma_0$.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering & Financial Utility

| Domain | Application of RCA(1) |
|---|---|
| **Equity risk modelling** | Captures stochastic persistence of returns during bull vs. bear regimes |
| **Macroeconomics** | Models GDP growth shocks whose propagation strength varies across policy regimes |
| **Signal processing** | Adaptive filters where the smoothing parameter drifts randomly |
| **Climate science** | Models temperature anomalies with stochastic auto-correlation |
| **Algorithmic trading** | Used in regime-switching volatility forecasting |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Unconditional Variance $\gamma_0$

**Goal:** Derive $\gamma_0 = \dfrac{\sigma_\epsilon^2}{1 - \phi^2 - \sigma_\phi^2}$ from first principles.

### Step 1 — Square the model equation

$$X_t^2 = (\phi_t X_{t-1} + \epsilon_t)^2 = \phi_t^2 X_{t-1}^2 + 2 \phi_t X_{t-1} \epsilon_t + \epsilon_t^2$$

### Step 2 — Take expectations, exploiting independence

By stationarity, $E[X_t^2] = E[X_{t-1}^2] = \gamma_0$. Also, $\phi_t$ is independent of $\mathcal{F}_{t-1}$, and $\epsilon_t$ is independent of everything except itself.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\gamma_0 &= E[\phi_t^2] \cdot E[X_{t-1}^2] + 2 E[\phi_t] E[X_{t-1}] E[\epsilon_t] + E[\epsilon_t^2] \\
&= (\phi^2 + \sigma_\phi^2) \gamma_0 + 0 + \sigma_\epsilon^2
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic note:** The cross-term vanishes because $E[\phi_t X_{t-1} \epsilon_t] = E[\phi_t] \cdot E[X_{t-1}] \cdot E[\epsilon_t]$ by mutual independence, and $E[\epsilon_t]=0$.

### Step 3 — Solve algebraically for $\gamma_0$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\gamma_0 - (\phi^2 + \sigma_\phi^2) \gamma_0 &= \sigma_\epsilon^2 \\
\gamma_0 \, [1 - \phi^2 - \sigma_\phi^2] &= \sigma_\epsilon^2 \\
\gamma_0 &= \frac{\sigma_\epsilon^2}{1 - \phi^2 - \sigma_\phi^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Stationarity condition

For $\gamma_0$ to be **finite and positive**, we require:

$$\phi^2 + \sigma_\phi^2 < 1$$

This is both **necessary and sufficient** for (second-order) strict stationarity.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Autocovariance Function $\gamma_k$ for $k \geq 1$

**Goal:** Derive $\gamma_k = \dfrac{\sigma_\epsilon^2 \, (\phi^2 + \sigma_\phi^2) \, \phi^{k-1}}{1 - \phi^2 - \sigma_\phi^2}$ for $k \geq 1$.

### Step 1 — Multiply the model equation by $X_{t-k}$ and take expectation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\gamma_k = E[X_t X_{t-k}] &= E[(\phi_t X_{t-1} + \epsilon_t) X_{t-k}] \\
&= E[\phi_t X_{t-1} X_{t-k}] + E[\epsilon_t X_{t-k}]
\end{aligned}
$$

The second term is **zero** since $\epsilon_t$ is independent of $\mathcal{F}_{t-k}$ for $k \geq 1$ and $E[\epsilon_t]=0$.

### Step 2 — Extract the conditional expectation for the first term

Using the tower property of expectation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E[\phi_t X_{t-1} X_{t-k}] &= E\big[ E[\phi_t X_{t-1} X_{t-k} \mid \mathcal{F}_{t-1}] \big] \\
&= E\big[ E[\phi_t \mid \mathcal{F}_{t-1}] \, X_{t-1} X_{t-k} \big] \\
&= E[\phi \, X_{t-1} X_{t-k}] \\
&= \phi \, \gamma_{k-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

So we obtain the **key recursion**:

$$\boxed{\;\gamma_k = \phi \, \gamma_{k-1}, \quad k \geq 2\;}$$

### Step 3 — Handle the base case $k = 1$ separately

$$
\begin{aligned}
\gamma_1 &= E[X_t X_{t-1}] = E[(\phi_t X_{t-1} + \epsilon_t) X_{t-1}] \\
&= E[\phi_t] E[X_{t-1}^2] + 0 \\
&= \phi \, \gamma_0
\end{aligned}
$$

(Note: $E[\epsilon_t X_{t-1}] = 0$ by independence.)

### Step 4 — Solve the recursion

The recursion $\gamma_k = \phi \gamma_{k-1}$ for $k \geq 2$ with $\gamma_1 = \phi \gamma_0$ gives:

$$\gamma_k = \phi^k \gamma_0 = \frac{\phi^k \, \sigma_\epsilon^2}{1 - \phi^2 - \sigma_\phi^2}, \quad k \geq 1$$

> Wait — let us reconcile with the formula table. There are **two equivalent representations** in the literature. The form $\gamma_k = \phi^k \gamma_0$ comes from this naive recursion. However, the formula in many texts (Aue et al., 2011) reads $\rho_k = (\phi^2 + \sigma_\phi^2) \phi^{k-1}$. These are the **same** under a slight re-parameterisation. Let us re-derive carefully:

### Step 5 — Re-derivation using $X_t^2$ recursion (alternative path)

From the squared model: $X_t^2 = (\phi^2 + \sigma_\phi^2) X_{t-1}^2 + \eta_t$, where $\eta_t$ is a martingale-difference sequence. Then:

$$E[X_t^2 X_{t-k}^2] = (\phi^2 + \sigma_\phi^2) E[X_{t-1}^2 X_{t-k}^2], \quad k \geq 1$$

This yields $\text{Corr}(X_t^2, X_{t-k}^2) = (\phi^2 + \sigma_\phi^2)^{k-1} (\phi^2 + \sigma_\phi^2)$ etc. — the **squared-ACF** decays at rate $(\phi^2 + \sigma_\phi^2)$, while the **level-ACF** decays at rate $\phi$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final consolidated answer for the KTU exam:**
> $$\rho_k = \phi^k \quad (\text{level ACF})$$
> $$\text{Corr}(X_t^2, X_{t-k}^2) = (\phi^2 + \sigma_\phi^2)^k \quad (\text{squared ACF})$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Example: Numerical Question (Typical KTU Style)

> **Problem:** Suppose $X_t = \phi_t X_{t-1} + \epsilon_t$, with $\phi = 0.6$, $\sigma_\phi^2 = 0.1$, and $\sigma_\epsilon^2 = 0.5$.
> Compute: (a) Stationarity condition. (b) Unconditional variance. (c) $\rho_1$ and $\rho_3$. (d) Conditional variance at $X_{t-1} = 2$.

### Solution

**(a) Stationarity check:**
$$\phi^2 + \sigma_\phi^2 = (0.6)^2 + 0.1 = 0.36 + 0.1 = 0.46 < 1 \quad \checkmark$$

**(b) Unconditional variance:**
$$\gamma_0 = \frac{\sigma_\epsilon^2}{1 - \phi^2 - \sigma_\phi^2} = \frac{0.5}{1 - 0.46} = \frac{0.5}{0.54} \approx 0.9259$$

**(c) Autocorrelations:**
$$\rho_1 = \phi = 0.6$$
$$\rho_3 = \phi^3 = (0.6)^3 = 0.216$$

**(d) Conditional variance at $X_{t-1} = 2$:**
$$\text{Var}(X_t \mid X_{t-1}=2) = (0.46)(2^2) + 0.5 = (0.46)(4) + 0.5 = 1.84 + 0.5 = 2.34$$

> [!TIP]
> **Always show substitution steps explicitly** — examiners award partial credit for the substitution even if arithmetic slips.

---

## 3.4 Analytic Proof of Equivalence Between RCA(1) and ARCH(1)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-yield theorem (favourite KTU long-answer topic):**
> *Every zero-mean RCA(1) process with Gaussian $\epsilon_t$ induces an ARCH(1) structure on its squares.*

**Proof sketch:**

1. From $X_t = \phi_t X_{t-1} + \epsilon_t$ we have:
$$X_t^2 = \phi_t^2 X_{t-1}^2 + 2 \phi_t X_{t-1} \epsilon_t + \epsilon_t^2$$

2. Take conditional expectation given $\mathcal{F}_{t-1}$:
$$E[X_t^2 \mid \mathcal{F}_{t-1}] = (\phi^2 + \sigma_\phi^2) X_{t-1}^2 + \sigma_\epsilon^2$$

3. Define $\sigma_t^2 = E[X_t^2 \mid \mathcal{F}_{t-1}]$. Then:
$$\sigma_t^2 = \sigma_\epsilon^2 + (\phi^2 + \sigma_\phi^2) X_{t-1}^2$$

4. This is **identical to an ARCH(1)** with constants $\alpha_0 = \sigma_\epsilon^2$ and $\alpha_1 = \phi^2 + \sigma_\phi^2$. $\blacksquare$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of the RCA(1) Process

The following block-level Mermaid diagram illustrates how randomness flows through the RCA(1) mechanism, from the underlying coefficient distribution to observable time-series output.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["RANDOM COEFFICIENT SOURCE phi_t"] --> C["STOCHASTIC MULTIPLIER phi_t * X_{t-1}"]
    B["INNOVATION SOURCE epsilon_t"] --> D["ADDITIVE NOISE epsilon_t"]
    C --> E["SUMMATION NODE: X_t = phi_t X_{t-1} + epsilon_t"]
    D --> E
    E --> F["OBSERVABLE SERIES X_t"]
    F --> G["SQUARED SERIES X_t^2"]
    G --> H["CONDITIONAL VARIANCE sigma_t^2 = alpha_0 + alpha_1 X_{t-1}^2"]
    H --> I["ARCH(1) EQUIVALENCE"]
    E --> J["STATIONARITY TEST phi^2 + sigma_phi^2 LESS THAN 1"]
    H --> J
    I --> K["VOLATILITY CLUSTERING OUTPUT"]
    J --> K
    K --> L["FINANCIAL RETURN FORECAST"]
```

## 4.2 Hierarchical Decomposition of RCA(1) Components

```mermaid
graph TD
    L1["RCA(1) MODEL: X_t = phi_t X_{t-1} + epsilon_t"] --> L2a["Layer A: Random Coefficient phi_t"]
    L1 --> L2b["Layer B: Lagged Memory X_{t-1}"]
    L1 --> L2c["Layer C: White Noise epsilon_t"]
    L2a --> L3a1["Mean = phi"]
    L2a --> L3a2["Variance = sigma_phi^2"]
    L2b --> L3b1["Persistence Strength"]
    L2c --> L3c1["Innovation Variance sigma_epsilon^2"]
    L3a1 --> L4["Conditional Mean: phi X_{t-1}"]
    L3a2 --> L4
    L3b1 --> L4
    L3c1 --> L4
    L3a2 --> L5["Conditional Variance: phi^2+sigma_phi^2 TIMES X_{t-1}^2 PLUS sigma_epsilon^2"]
    L3b1 --> L5
    L3c1 --> L5
    L4 --> L6["Unconditional ACF rho_k = phi^k"]
    L5 --> L7["Squared ACF rho_k^2 = phi^2+sigma_phi^2 RAISED TO k"]
```

## 4.3 Decision Flow for Stationarity and ARCH Equivalence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["Start: Given phi and sigma_phi^2"] --> S2{"Is phi^2 + sigma_phi^2 LESS THAN 1?"}
    S2 -- "Yes" --> S3["Process is STRICTLY STATIONARY"]
    S2 -- "No" --> S4["Process is NON-STATIONARY: variance explodes"]
    S3 --> S5["Compute gamma_0 using formula"]
    S5 --> S6["Compute rho_k = phi^k"]
    S3 --> S7["Check ARCH(1) equivalence: alpha_1 = phi^2 + sigma_phi^2 LESS THAN 1?"]
    S7 -- "Yes" --> S8["X_t^2 follows AR(1)-type process with valid ARCH(1) representation"]
    S7 -- "No" --> S9["Squared process is NON-STATIONARY: ARCH representation invalid"]
```

## 4.4 Schematic: From Linear AR(1) to RCA(1) to ARCH(1)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["AR(1): X_t = phi X_{t-1} + epsilon_t"] --> P2["Allow phi to vary: phi_t"]
    P2 --> P3["RCA(1): X_t = phi_t X_{t-1} + epsilon_t"]
    P3 --> P4["Square the model"]
    P4 --> P5["Take conditional expectation"]
    P5 --> P6["ARCH(1): sigma_t^2 = alpha_0 + alpha_1 X_{t-1}^2"]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Pattern]**

**Q:** Define the Random Coefficient Autoregressive Model of order 1, RCA(1). State the stationarity condition.

**Model Answer:**

> The RCA(1) model is defined as $X_t = \phi_t X_{t-1} + \epsilon_t$, where $\{\phi_t\}$ is a sequence of i.i.d. random variables with mean $\phi$ and variance $\sigma_\phi^2$, and $\{\epsilon_t\}$ is white noise with variance $\sigma_\epsilon^2$, independent of $\{\phi_t\}$.
>
> The **stationarity condition** is:
> $$\phi^2 + \sigma_\phi^2 < 1$$
>
> This ensures finite unconditional variance $\gamma_0 = \sigma_\epsilon^2 / (1 - \phi^2 - \sigma_\phi^2)$.

**[Marks allocation: 1 for definition, 1 for parameter roles, 1 for stationarity condition]**

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper Pattern]**

**Q:** Show that the conditional variance of an RCA(1) process has the ARCH(1) structure.

**Model Answer:**

> From $X_t = \phi_t X_{t-1} + \epsilon_t$, we compute:
> $$\text{Var}(X_t \mid \mathcal{F}_{t-1}) = E[(X_t - \phi X_{t-1})^2 \mid \mathcal{F}_{t-1}] = (\phi^2 + \sigma_\phi^2) X_{t-1}^2 + \sigma_\epsilon^2$$
>
> Setting $\sigma_t^2 = E[X_t^2 \mid \mathcal{F}_{t-1}]$, we get:
> $$\sigma_t^2 = \sigma_\epsilon^2 + (\phi^2 + \sigma_\phi^2) X_{t-1}^2$$
>
> This is **ARCH(1)** with $\alpha_0 = \sigma_\epsilon^2$ and $\alpha_1 = \phi^2 + \sigma_\phi^2$.

**[Marks allocation: 1 for conditional variance derivation, 1 for ARCH identification, 1 for parameter mapping]**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[Internal Choice Mechanism]:** Answer **EITHER** Question A **OR** Question B.

---

### 📘 Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Derive the unconditional mean and unconditional variance of an RCA(1) process $X_t = \phi_t X_{t-1} + \epsilon_t$. State and explain the stationarity condition.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Given $\phi = 0.5$, $\sigma_\phi^2 = 0.2$, and $\sigma_\epsilon^2 = 1.0$, compute: (i) the unconditional variance, (ii) the first three autocorrelations $\rho_1, \rho_2, \rho_3$, and (iii) the conditional variance when $X_{t-1} = 1.5$.

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1: Unconditional Mean**

$$E[X_t] = E[\phi_t X_{t-1}] + E[\epsilon_t] = E[\phi_t] E[X_{t-1}] + 0 = \phi \, E[X_{t-1}]$$

By stationarity, $E[X_t] = E[X_{t-1}] = \mu$, hence $\mu = \phi \mu$, giving $\mu = 0$ (assuming the process is mean-zero, or we set it to zero by centering).

**Step 2: Unconditional Variance — Squaring the Model**

$$X_t^2 = \phi_t^2 X_{t-1}^2 + 2 \phi_t X_{t-1} \epsilon_t + \epsilon_t^2$$

**Step 3: Take Expectations**

$$E[X_t^2] = E[\phi_t^2] E[X_{t-1}^2] + 2 E[\phi_t] E[X_{t-1}] E[\epsilon_t] + E[\epsilon_t^2]$$

Using $E[\phi_t^2] = \phi^2 + \sigma_\phi^2$, $E[\epsilon_t] = 0$, $E[X_t] = 0$:

$$\gamma_0 = (\phi^2 + \sigma_\phi^2) \gamma_0 + \sigma_\epsilon^2$$

**Step 4: Solve**

$$\gamma_0 (1 - \phi^2 - \sigma_\phi^2) = \sigma_\epsilon^2 \;\Rightarrow\; \gamma_0 = \frac{\sigma_\epsilon^2}{1 - \phi^2 - \sigma_\phi^2}$$

**Step 5: Stationarity Condition**

For $\gamma_0$ to be finite and positive, we must have $\phi^2 + \sigma_\phi^2 < 1$. This is the **necessary and sufficient condition for second-order strict stationarity**.

> If $\phi^2 + \sigma_\phi^2 \geq 1$, the variance either diverges ($\geq 1$) or is undefined ($= 1$), and the process becomes non-stationary.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:** $\phi = 0.5$, $\sigma_\phi^2 = 0.2$, $\sigma_\epsilon^2 = 1.0$.

**(i) Unconditional Variance:**

First check stationarity: $\phi^2 + \sigma_\phi^2 = 0.25 + 0.2 = 0.45 < 1$ ✓

$$\gamma_0 = \frac{1.0}{1 - 0.45} = \frac{1.0}{0.55} = \frac{20}{11} \approx 1.8182$$

**(ii) Autocorrelations (Level ACF):**

$$\rho_k = \phi^k = (0.5)^k$$

$$\rho_1 = 0.5, \quad \rho_2 = 0.25, \quad \rho_3 = 0.125$$

**(iii) Conditional Variance at $X_{t-1} = 1.5$:**

$$\text{Var}(X_t \mid X_{t-1}=1.5) = (\phi^2 + \sigma_\phi^2)(1.5)^2 + \sigma_\epsilon^2$$
$$= (0.45)(2.25) + 1.0 = 1.0125 + 1.0 = 2.0125$$

---

### 📗 Question B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Pattern]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Establish, with full derivation, that the squared series $\{X_t^2\}$ of an RCA(1) process follows an AR(1)-type representation, and use this to derive the autocorrelation function of $X_t^2$.

**(b)** [7 Marks] An analyst fits an RCA(1) model to a daily log-return series and obtains parameter estimates $\hat\phi = 0.4$, $\hat\sigma_\phi^2 = 0.3$, $\hat\sigma_\epsilon^2 = 0.8$. (i) Verify stationarity. (ii) Compute the equivalent ARCH(1) coefficients. (iii) Comment on whether the ARCH(1) representation is itself stationary, and forecast the conditional variance for tomorrow if today's squared return was $X_t^2 = 4$.

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1: Square the RCA(1) Model**

$$X_t^2 = \phi_t^2 X_{t-1}^2 + 2 \phi_t X_{t-1} \epsilon_t + \epsilon_t^2$$

**Step 2: Rearrange as AR(1)-type on $X_t^2$**

Let $Y_t = X_t^2$ and define $\eta_t = 2 \phi_t X_{t-1} \epsilon_t + \epsilon_t^2 - \sigma_\epsilon^2$. Then:

$$Y_t = (\phi^2 + \sigma_\phi^2) Y_{t-1} + \sigma_\epsilon^2 + \eta_t$$

**Step 3: Verify $\eta_t$ is a Martingale Difference Sequence**

- $E[\eta_t \mid \mathcal{F}_{t-1}] = 2 E[\phi_t] X_{t-1} E[\epsilon_t] + E[\epsilon_t^2] - \sigma_\epsilon^2 = 0$ ✓
- $E[\eta_t^2 \mid \mathcal{F}_{t-1}]$ is finite under stationarity ✓

**Step 4: ACF of $X_t^2$**

For $k \geq 1$:
$$E[X_t^2 X_{t-k}^2] = (\phi^2 + \sigma_\phi^2) E[X_{t-1}^2 X_{t-k}^2] + \sigma_\epsilon^2 E[X_{t-k}^2] + 0$$

Let $\delta_k = E[X_t^2 X_{t-k}^2]$. Then for $k \geq 1$:
$$\delta_k = (\phi^2 + \sigma_\phi^2) \delta_{k-1}$$

Combined with $\delta_0 = E[X_t^4]$, the **autocorrelation of squared series** is:

$$\text{Corr}(X_t^2, X_{t-k}^2) = (\phi^2 + \sigma_\phi^2)^k$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:** $\hat\phi = 0.4$, $\hat\sigma_\phi^2 = 0.3$, $\hat\sigma_\epsilon^2 = 0.8$.

**(i) Stationarity Check:**

$$\hat\phi^2 + \hat\sigma_\phi^2 = (0.4)^2 + 0.3 = 0.16 + 0.3 = 0.46 < 1 \quad \checkmark$$

The RCA(1) process is **strictly stationary** (second-order sense).

**(ii) Equivalent ARCH(1) Coefficients:**

From Section 2.4: $\alpha_0 = \sigma_\epsilon^2 = 0.8$ and $\alpha_1 = \phi^2 + \sigma_\phi^2 = 0.46$.

**ARCH(1) Model:** $\sigma_t^2 = 0.8 + 0.46 X_{t-1}^2$

**(iii) ARCH(1) Stationarity and Forecast:**

Stationarity of ARCH(1) requires $\alpha_1 < 1$. Here $\alpha_1 = 0.46 < 1$ ✓, so the ARCH representation is also stationary.

**Forecast for tomorrow's conditional variance given $X_t^2 = 4$:**

$$\hat\sigma_{t+1}^2 = \alpha_0 + \alpha_1 X_t^2 = 0.8 + 0.46 \times 4 = 0.8 + 1.84 = 2.64$$

**Comment:** The forecast conditional variance is $\hat\sigma_{t+1}^2 = 2.64$, indicating heightened volatility tomorrow (since $2.64 > \alpha_0 = 0.8$, the unconditional mean variance baseline). This illustrates the **volatility clustering** phenomenon: a large return today ($X_t^2 = 4$) drives up expected variance tomorrow.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students typically LOSE marks on RCA(1) questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to square $\phi$ in the conditional variance:** Writing $\text{Var}(X_t \mid \mathcal{F}_{t-1}) = \phi X_{t-1}^2 + \sigma_\epsilon^2$ instead of $(\phi^2 + \sigma_\phi^2) X_{t-1}^2 + \sigma_\epsilon^2$. The correct term involves $\phi^2 + \sigma_\phi^2$, **not** $\phi$.
>
> 2. **Confusing level ACF and squared ACF:** The level ACF decays as $\phi^k$, but the squared ACF decays as $(\phi^2 + \sigma_\phi^2)^k$. Students often write the wrong decay rate.
>
> 3. **Omitting the stationarity check:** Even in numerical problems, you must **verify** $\phi^2 + \sigma_\phi^2 < 1$ **before** computing $\gamma_0$. Otherwise, you lose 1–2 easy marks.
>
> 4. **Mixing up $\sigma_\phi^2$ and $\sigma_\epsilon^2$:** $\sigma_\phi^2$ is the variance of the random coefficient; $\sigma_\epsilon^2$ is the innovation variance. These play different roles in the formulas.
>
> 5. **Skipping independence justification:** In derivations, you must state that $\phi_t$ and $\epsilon_t$ are independent, and that $\epsilon_t$ is independent of $\mathcal{F}_{t-1}$, to justify zero cross-terms.
>
> 6. **Reporting $\rho_1 = \phi^2 + \sigma_\phi^2$:** The correct first-level autocorrelation is $\rho_1 = \phi$, **not** $\phi^2 + \sigma_\phi^2$. The latter is the coefficient in the ARCH(1) representation of $X_t^2$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!TIP]
> **Print this. Memorize. Win.**

- ✅ **Model:** $X_t = \phi_t X_{t-1} + \epsilon_t$ with $\phi_t \overset{\text{iid}}{\sim} (\phi, \sigma_\phi^2)$ and $\epsilon_t \sim \text{WN}(0, \sigma_\epsilon^2)$, all mutually independent.
- ✅ **Conditional Mean:** $E[X_t \mid \mathcal{F}_{t-1}] = \phi X_{t-1}$ — same shape as AR(1).
- ✅ **Conditional Variance:** $\text{Var}(X_t \mid \mathcal{F}_{t-1}) = (\phi^2 + \sigma_\phi^2) X_{t-1}^2 + \sigma_\epsilon^2$ — ARCH(1) form.
- ✅ **Unconditional Variance:** $\gamma_0 = \sigma_\epsilon^2 / (1 - \phi^2 - \sigma_\phi^2)$.
- ✅ **Stationarity:** $\phi^2 + \sigma_\phi^2 < 1$.
- ✅ **Level ACF:** $\rho_k = \phi^k$ (geometric decay).
- ✅ **Squared ACF:** $\text{Corr}(X_t^2, X_{t-k}^2) = (\phi^2 + \sigma_\phi^2)^k$.
- ✅ **ARCH(1) Equivalence:** $\alpha_0 = \sigma_\epsilon^2$, $\alpha_1 = \phi^2 + \sigma_\phi^2$.
- ✅ **Financial Interpretation:** Random $\phi_t$ ⇒ stochastic persistence ⇒ **volatility clustering**.
- ✅ **Bridge Concept:** RCA(1) is the link between linear AR(1) and ARCH(1) volatility models.
- ✅ **Assumptions Checklist:** (i) $\phi_t$ i.i.d., (ii) $\epsilon_t$ i.i.d., (iii) $\phi_t \perp \!\!\!\! \perp \epsilon_s$ for all $s, t$, (iv) zero mean.
- ✅ **Key Result to Memorise:** The squares $\{X_t^2\}$ of an RCA(1) process satisfy an AR(1)-type recursion with coefficient $\phi^2 + \sigma_\phi^2$.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET742 - FINANCIAL TIME SERIES ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 2

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Module 2 — Asymmetric Power ARCH (APARCH) Model

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **APARCH Definition (Ding, Granger & Engle, 1993):**
> The **Asymmetric Power ARCH (APARCH(p,q))** model is a generalized volatility framework that simultaneously captures **leverage (asymmetry)**, **power transformations of returns**, and **conditional heteroskedasticity**. The conditional variance is raised to an arbitrary power $\delta > 0$, and the lagged absolute innovations are allowed to enter asymmetrically through leverage parameters $\gamma_i$.

The mathematical form of the APARCH(p,q) model is:

$$\sigma_t^{\delta} = \omega + \sum_{i=1}^{q} \alpha_i \left( \vert \varepsilon_{t-i} \vert - \gamma_i \, \varepsilon_{t-i} \right)^{\delta} + \sum_{j=1}^{p} \beta_j \, \sigma_{t-j}^{\delta}$$

where the error term follows:

$$\varepsilon_t = \sigma_t \, z_t, \quad z_t \overset{iid}{\sim} (0, 1)$$

and the **key parameter set** is $\{ \omega, \alpha_i, \beta_j, \gamma_i, \delta \}$ with the usual **positivity constraints**: $\omega > 0$, $\alpha_i \ge 0$, $\beta_j \ge 0$, and $\delta > 0$.

---

## 1.2 Component Glossary

| Symbol | Meaning | Sign / Domain |
|---|---|---|
| $\sigma_t^2$ | Conditional variance at time $t$ | Positive |
| $\delta$ | Power (Box-Cox) exponent | $\delta > 0$ |
| $\gamma_i$ | Asymmetry (leverage) coefficient | $-1 < \gamma_i < 1$ |
| $\alpha_i$ | ARCH (shock) coefficient | $\alpha_i \ge 0$ |
| $\beta_j$ | GARCH (persistence) coefficient | $\beta_j \ge 0$ |
| $\omega$ | Drift (long-run variance base) | $\omega > 0$ |

---

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: "The Smart Thermostat of Financial Markets"**
> Imagine a thermostat that controls the *temperature of market fear*. Three dials govern it:
> 1. **Sensitivity dial ($\alpha_i$):** How loudly the thermostat reacts to a recent temperature spike.
> 2. **Memory dial ($\beta_j$):** How long the previous "fear setting" lingers in the system.
> 3. **Asymmetry dial ($\gamma_i$):** A clever knob — when bad news arrives (negative shocks), the thermostat overreacts and cranks the heat up **more** than when equally large good news arrives. The exponent $\delta$ is like a *curve-shaping knob* on the dial — it decides whether the system treats the *square*, the *absolute value*, or some intermediate power of the shock as the actual "signal."

In markets, this asymmetry is the famous **leverage effect** — stock volatility rises more on negative returns than on positive ones of the same magnitude.

---

## 1.4 Why Was APARCH Needed?

Before APARCH, no single parsimonious model could simultaneously:

1. Handle **arbitrary power transformations** of conditional standard deviation (hence unifying ARCH, GARCH, AGARCH, GJR-GARCH, TARCH under one roof).
2. Capture **asymmetric reaction** to signed shocks (leverage effect).
3. Maintain **parsimony** — it nests six famous GARCH-family models as special cases.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Pearl:** Whenever a question asks *"Name a model that nests GARCH, AGARCH, GJR-GARCH, TARCH, NARCH, and log-ARCH"* — the answer is **APARCH**. This is a high-yield one-liner.

---

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic of the APARCH Equation

Let us dissect the equation

$$\sigma_t^{\delta} = \omega + \sum_{i=1}^{q} \alpha_i \left( \vert \varepsilon_{t-i} \vert - \gamma_i \, \varepsilon_{t-i} \right)^{\delta} + \sum_{j=1}^{p} \beta_j \, \sigma_{t-j}^{\delta}$$

Step-by-step reasoning:

- **Step 1 — Baseline drift ($\omega$):** A positive constant ensuring $\sigma_t^{\delta}$ never collapses to zero. It represents the unconditional baseline of the powered variance.
- **Step 2 — Asymmetric shock propagation:** The crucial term $\left( \vert \varepsilon_{t-i} \vert - \gamma_i \, \varepsilon_{t-i} \right)$ performs a clever sign-aware scaling:
  - When $\varepsilon_{t-i} > 0$ (good news): the inside term becomes $(1 - \gamma_i)\varepsilon_{t-i}$.
  - When $\varepsilon_{t-i} < 0$ (bad news): the inside term becomes $(1 + \gamma_i)\vert\varepsilon_{t-i}\vert$.
  - Hence if $\gamma_i > 0$, **negative shocks** produce a **larger contribution** — this is the **leverage effect**.
- **Step 3 — Power transformation:** Raising the inside term to the power $\delta$ compresses or stretches the impact of small versus large shocks.
- **Step 4 — Persistence:** $\beta_j \sigma_{t-j}^{\delta}$ is the GARCH-like decay term that carries past powered variance into the present.

---

## 2.2 Important Special Cases (KTU High-Yield Table)

| Model | $\delta$ | $\gamma_i$ | Reduction of APARCH |
|---|---|---|---|
| **ARCH(q)** | $2$ | $0$ | $\sigma_t^2 = \omega + \sum \alpha_i \varepsilon_{t-i}^2$ |
| **GARCH(p,q)** | $2$ | $0$ | $\sigma_t^2 = \omega + \sum \alpha_i \varepsilon_{t-i}^2 + \sum \beta_j \sigma_{t-j}^2$ |
| **AGARCH (Absolute GARCH)** | $1$ | $0$ | $\sigma_t = \omega + \sum \alpha_i \vert \varepsilon_{t-i} \vert + \sum \beta_j \sigma_{t-j}$ |
| **GJR-GARCH / TARCH** | $2$ | $\gamma$ | $\sigma_t^2 = \omega + \sum (\alpha_i + \gamma_i I_{t-i}^{-}) \varepsilon_{t-i}^2 + \sum \beta_j \sigma_{t-j}^2$ |
| **NARCH (Nonlinear ARCH)** | $\delta$ | $0$ | $\sigma_t^{\delta} = \omega + \sum \alpha_i \vert \varepsilon_{t-i} \vert^{\delta}$ |
| **Log-ARCH** | $\delta \to 0$ | $0$ | $\log \sigma_t^2 = \omega + \sum \alpha_i \log \varepsilon_{t-i}^2$ |
| **Pure Power ARCH (q=0)** | $\delta$ | $\gamma_i$ | $\sigma_t^{\delta} = \omega + \sum \alpha_i \left( \vert \varepsilon_{t-i} \vert - \gamma_i \varepsilon_{t-i} \right)^{\delta}$ |

---

## 2.3 Stationarity & Covariance Stationarity Conditions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Covariance Stationarity of APARCH(p,q):**
> For the APARCH process to be covariance-stationary with finite unconditional variance, a necessary (and for many specifications sufficient) condition is:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{q} \alpha_i \, E\left[ \left( \vert \varepsilon_{t-i} \vert - \gamma_i \varepsilon_{t-i} \right)^{\delta} \right] + \sum_{j=1}^{p} \beta_j < 1$$

For the standard case with $z_t \sim N(0,1)$ and $\gamma_i = 0$:

$$E\left[ \vert \varepsilon_{t-i} \vert^{\delta} \right] = E\left[ \sigma_{t-i}^{\delta} \right] \cdot E\left[ \vert z_{t-i} \vert^{\delta} \right]$$

so that the condition reduces to a tractable inequality involving $E\vert z \vert^{\delta}$.

---

## 2.4 High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat-Sheet Table)

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the single most important compact reference for APARCH-based exam questions.

| Concept | Formula | Remark |
|---|---|---|
| APARCH(p,q) general form | $\sigma_t^{\delta} = \omega + \sum_{i=1}^{q} \alpha_i (\vert \varepsilon_{t-i} \vert - \gamma_i \varepsilon_{t-i})^{\delta} + \sum_{j=1}^{p} \beta_j \sigma_{t-j}^{\delta}$ | $\delta > 0$ |
| Innovation representation | $\varepsilon_t = \sigma_t z_t$, $z_t \overset{iid}{\sim}(0,1)$ | $E[z_t]=0$, $E[z_t^2]=1$ |
| Leverage / Asymmetry | $\gamma_i > 0 \Rightarrow$ negative shocks raise $\sigma_t$ more | $-1 < \gamma_i < 1$ |
| Positivity of $\sigma_t^{\delta}$ | $\omega > 0$, $\alpha_i \ge 0$, $\beta_j \ge 0$, $\delta > 0$ | Required for non-negative variance |
| Power exponent | $\delta = 1$ → AGARCH; $\delta = 2$ → GARCH/GJR; $\delta \to 0$ → log-GARCH | Box-Cox-type |
| Persistence (sum) | $\sum \alpha_i E[\cdots] + \sum \beta_j < 1$ | Stationarity |
| Asymmetric response ratio | $R = \dfrac{1+\gamma_i}{1-\gamma_i}$ | Ratio of bad-news to good-news contribution |
| Mean equation | $y_t = \mu + \varepsilon_t$ | Often AR/ARMA included |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering & Finance

- **Risk Management (VaR/ES):** Captures both heavy tails and asymmetry, improving tail-risk forecasts.
- **Derivative Pricing:** The asymmetric power formulation better fits the volatility smile/skew.
- **Algorithmic Trading:** More accurate volatility forecasts reduce transaction-cost-adjusted slippage.
- **Macro-Economic Forecasting:** Useful for inflation and exchange-rate volatility, which exhibit leverage effects.
- **High-Frequency Trading:** The power $\delta$ allows non-quadratic loss functions used in realised-variance estimators.

---

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Reduction of APARCH to GARCH

We start with the **APARCH(p,q)** model and set $\delta = 2$, $\gamma_i = 0$:

$$\sigma_t^{2} = \omega + \sum_{i=1}^{q} \alpha_i \left( \vert \varepsilon_{t-i} \vert - 0 \cdot \varepsilon_{t-i} \right)^{2} + \sum_{j=1}^{p} \beta_j \sigma_{t-j}^{2}$$

**Step A — Substituting $\gamma_i = 0$:**

$$\sigma_t^{2} = \omega + \sum_{i=1}^{q} \alpha_i \left( \vert \varepsilon_{t-i} \vert \right)^{2} + \sum_{j=1}^{p} \beta_j \sigma_{t-j}^{2}$$

**Step B — Apply the identity $\vert x \vert^{2} = x^{2}$:**

$$\sigma_t^{2} = \omega + \sum_{i=1}^{q} \alpha_i \varepsilon_{t-i}^{2} + \sum_{j=1}^{p} \beta_j \sigma_{t-j}^{2}$$

This is the canonical **GARCH(p,q)** equation. Hence, GARCH is a special case of APARCH with $\delta=2$ and $\gamma_i=0$. ✓

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Reduction of APARCH to AGARCH

Set $\delta = 1$, $\gamma_i = 0$:

$$\sigma_t^{1} = \omega + \sum_{i=1}^{q} \alpha_i \left( \vert \varepsilon_{t-i} \vert - 0 \cdot \varepsilon_{t-i} \right)^{1} + \sum_{j=1}^{p} \beta_j \sigma_{t-j}^{1}$$

Reducing:

$$\sigma_t = \omega + \sum_{i=1}^{q} \alpha_i \vert \varepsilon_{t-i} \vert + \sum_{j=1}^{p} \beta_j \sigma_{t-j}$$

This is the **AGARCH** (Absolute-Value GARCH) model of Taylor (1986) and Schwert (1990). ✓

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Reduction of APARCH to GJR-GARCH (Threshold GARCH)

Set $\delta = 2$ but keep $\gamma_i$ free:

$$\sigma_t^{2} = \omega + \sum_{i=1}^{q} \alpha_i \left( \vert \varepsilon_{t-i} \vert - \gamma_i \varepsilon_{t-i} \right)^{2} + \sum_{j=1}^{p} \beta_j \sigma_{t-j}^{2}$$

**Step A — Square the inner term.** Let $u_i = \vert \varepsilon_{t-i} \vert - \gamma_i \varepsilon_{t-i}$. Then

$$u_i^{2} = \vert \varepsilon_{t-i} \vert^{2} - 2\gamma_i \vert \varepsilon_{t-i} \vert \varepsilon_{t-i} + \gamma_i^{2} \varepsilon_{t-i}^{2}$$

**Step B — Note that $\vert \varepsilon_{t-i} \vert \varepsilon_{t-i} = \varepsilon_{t-i}^{2}$ if $\varepsilon_{t-i} \ge 0$ and $= -\varepsilon_{t-i}^{2}$ if $\varepsilon_{t-i} < 0$.** Equivalently, $\vert \varepsilon_{t-i} \vert \varepsilon_{t-i} = \varepsilon_{t-i}^{2} \cdot \mathrm{sgn}(\varepsilon_{t-i})$.

**Step C — Separate positive and negative shocks.** Define $I_{t-i}^{-} = 1$ if $\varepsilon_{t-i} < 0$ else $0$. Then

$$\vert \varepsilon_{t-i} \vert \varepsilon_{t-i} = \varepsilon_{t-i}^{2} (1 - 2 I_{t-i}^{-})$$

Substituting:

$$u_i^{2} = \varepsilon_{t-i}^{2} - 2\gamma_i \varepsilon_{t-i}^{2}(1 - 2 I_{t-i}^{-}) + \gamma_i^{2} \varepsilon_{t-i}^{2}$$

$$u_i^{2} = (1 - 2\gamma_i + \gamma_i^{2})\varepsilon_{t-i}^{2} + 4\gamma_i \varepsilon_{t-i}^{2} I_{t-i}^{-}$$

$$u_i^{2} = (1-\gamma_i)^{2} \varepsilon_{t-i}^{2} + 4\gamma_i \varepsilon_{t-i}^{2} I_{t-i}^{-}$$

**Step D — Plug into APARCH:**

$$\sigma_t^{2} = \omega + \sum_{i=1}^{q} \alpha_i (1-\gamma_i)^{2} \varepsilon_{t-i}^{2} + \sum_{i=1}^{q} 4\alpha_i \gamma_i I_{t-i}^{-} \varepsilon_{t-i}^{2} + \sum_{j=1}^{p} \beta_j \sigma_{t-j}^{2}$$

Let $\alpha_i' = \alpha_i (1-\gamma_i)^{2}$ and $\gamma_i' = 4\alpha_i \gamma_i$:

$$\sigma_t^{2} = \omega + \sum_{i=1}^{q} \alpha_i' \varepsilon_{t-i}^{2} + \sum_{i=1}^{q} \gamma_i' I_{t-i}^{-} \varepsilon_{t-i}^{2} + \sum_{j=1}^{p} \beta_j \sigma_{t-j}^{2}$$

This is the canonical **GJR-GARCH** formulation of Glosten, Jagannathan, and Runkle (1993). ✓

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Asymmetric Response Ratio

For a single lag ($i=1$):

- **Positive shock** $\varepsilon_{t-1} > 0$:

$$\text{Contribution} = \alpha_1 (1 - \gamma_1)^{\delta} \varepsilon_{t-1}^{\delta}$$

- **Negative shock** $\varepsilon_{t-1} < 0$:

$$\text{Contribution} = \alpha_1 (1 + \gamma_1)^{\delta} \vert \varepsilon_{t-1} \vert^{\delta}$$

**Ratio of bad-news to good-news contribution (same magnitude $|\varepsilon| = c$):**

$$R = \frac{\alpha_1 (1+\gamma_1)^{\delta} c^{\delta}}{\alpha_1 (1-\gamma_1)^{\delta} c^{\delta}} = \left( \frac{1+\gamma_1}{1-\gamma_1} \right)^{\delta}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Asymmetric Response Ratio:**
> 
> $$R = \left( \dfrac{1+\gamma_i}{1-\gamma_i} \right)^{\delta}$$
> 
> This compact formula is widely tested in KTU examinations. For $\gamma_i > 0$, we get $R > 1$, i.e., negative shocks raise volatility disproportionately.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Unconditional Variance of APARCH (Covariance Stationarity)

Assume $\gamma_i = 0$ for simplicity. The APARCH becomes:

$$\sigma_t^{\delta} = \omega + \sum_{i=1}^{q} \alpha_i \vert \varepsilon_{t-i} \vert^{\delta} + \sum_{j=1}^{p} \beta_j \sigma_{t-j}^{\delta}$$

Take expectations on both sides, using covariance stationarity so that $E[\sigma_t^{\delta}] = E[\sigma_{t-1}^{\delta}] = \cdots = \bar{\sigma}^{\delta}$:

$$\bar{\sigma}^{\delta} = \omega + \left( \sum_{i=1}^{q} \alpha_i \right) E[\vert \varepsilon_{t} \vert^{\delta}] + \left( \sum_{j=1}^{p} \beta_j \right) \bar{\sigma}^{\delta}$$

Since $\varepsilon_t = \sigma_t z_t$ and $\sigma_t$ is conditionally independent of $z_t$:

$$E[\vert \varepsilon_t \vert^{\delta}] = E[\sigma_t^{\delta}] \cdot E[\vert z_t \vert^{\delta}] = \bar{\sigma}^{\delta} \cdot E[\vert z_t \vert^{\delta}]$$

Define $m_{\delta} = E[\vert z_t \vert^{\delta}]$. Then:

$$\bar{\sigma}^{\delta} = \omega + m_{\delta} \left( \sum_{i=1}^{q} \alpha_i \right) \bar{\sigma}^{\delta} + \left( \sum_{j=1}^{p} \beta_j \right) \bar{\sigma}^{\delta}$$

$$\bar{\sigma}^{\delta} \left[ 1 - m_{\delta} \sum_{i=1}^{q} \alpha_i - \sum_{j=1}^{p} \beta_j \right] = \omega$$

$$\boxed{\bar{\sigma}^{\delta} = \dfrac{\omega}{1 - m_{\delta} \sum_{i=1}^{q} \alpha_i - \sum_{j=1}^{p} \beta_j}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> For **Gaussian** $z_t$, $m_{\delta} = E\vert z \vert^{\delta} = 2^{\delta/2} \Gamma\left(\dfrac{\delta+1}{2}\right) \big/ \sqrt{\pi}$. The stationarity condition is $m_{\delta} \sum \alpha_i + \sum \beta_j < 1$.

---

## 3.6 Derivation 6 — Quasi-Maximum Likelihood (QML) Estimation

Let $\theta = (\omega, \alpha_1, \ldots, \alpha_q, \beta_1, \ldots, \beta_p, \gamma_1, \ldots, \gamma_q, \delta)$ denote the parameter vector. The conditional log-likelihood under the **Gaussian** assumption is:

$$\ell_t(\theta) = -\frac{1}{2} \log(2\pi) - \frac{1}{2} \log \sigma_t^2 - \frac{\varepsilon_t^2}{2\sigma_t^2}$$

The full-sample log-likelihood:

$$\mathcal{L}(\theta) = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \ell_t(\theta) = -\frac{T}{2}\log(2\pi) - \frac{1}{2} \sum_{t=1}^{T} \log \sigma_t^2(\theta) - \frac{1}{2} \sum_{t=1}^{T} \frac{\varepsilon_t^2}{\sigma_t^2(\theta)}$$

The estimator is:

$$\hat{\theta} = \arg\max_{\theta \in \Theta} \mathcal{L}(\theta)$$

subject to the **constraints** $\omega > 0$, $\alpha_i \ge 0$, $\beta_j \ge 0$, $\delta > 0$, $-1 < \gamma_i < 1$.

**Score vector** $\partial \mathcal{L} / \partial \theta_i$ is computed by chain rule on the recursion. Standard errors are obtained from the inverse of the **Hessian** (numerically).

For non-Gaussian innovations, replace the Gaussian kernel with **Student-t** or **GED** densities (still QML consistent under correct conditional mean and variance).

---

## 3.7 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Reduction / Property | $\delta$ | $\gamma_i$ | Resulting Model | Test Weight |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $\delta=2$, $\gamma_i=0$ | $2$ | $0$ | GARCH | High |
| $\delta=1$, $\gamma_i=0$ | $1$ | $0$ | AGARCH | Medium |
| $\delta=2$, $\gamma_i \ne 0$ | $2$ | free | GJR / Threshold GARCH | High |
| $\delta$ free, $\gamma_i=0$ | $\delta$ | $0$ | NARCH | Medium |
| $\delta \to 0$, $\gamma_i=0$ | $0$ | $0$ | log-GARCH | Low |
| $p=0$, $\delta$ free, $\gamma_i$ free | $\delta$ | free | Pure Power ARCH | High |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of the APARCH Model

The following block diagram captures the flow of information from a raw return innovation to the conditional powered standard deviation used in forecasting.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Return y_t] --> B[Mean Equation: mu + epsilon_t]
    B --> C[Innovation epsilon_t]
    C --> D{Asymmetric Sign Detector}
    D -- Positive shock --> E1[Multiply by 1 - gamma]
    D -- Negative shock --> E2[Multiply by 1 + gamma]
    E1 --> F[Box-Cox Power delta]
    E2 --> F
    F --> G[Scale by alpha_i]
    G --> H[Sum over i from 1 to q]
    H --> I[Add Drift omega]
    I --> J[Add Past sigma^delta: sum beta_j sigma_{t-j}^delta]
    J --> K[Powered Conditional Std Dev sigma_t^delta]
    K --> L[Compute sigma_t = K power 1/delta]
    L --> M[Volatility Forecast Output]
    P[Stationarity Check] -.-> K
    P --> P1[m_delta sum alpha_i + sum beta_j less than 1]
    Q[QML / MLE] -.-> K
    Q --> Q1[Maximize Gaussian or t log-likelihood]
```

---

## 4.2 APARCH Family Nesting Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    APARCH[APARCH p q delta gamma]
    APARCH --> D2G0[GARCH: delta 2 gamma 0]
    APARCH --> D1G0[AGARCH: delta 1 gamma 0]
    APARCH --> D2Gfree[GJR GARCH: delta 2 gamma free]
    APARCH --> DNARCH[NARCH: delta free gamma 0]
    APARCH --> D0G0[Log GARCH: delta 0 gamma 0]
    APARCH --> TARCH[Threshold ARCH: asymmetric sign dummy]
    D2G0 --> GARCHq[GARCH q]
    D2G0 --> ARCHq[ARCH q when p 0]
    D2Gfree --> TGARCH[TGARCH by Zakoian]
    D1G0 --> ABSGARCH[Absolute GARCH Taylor Schwert]
    APARCH --> P0[Pure Power ARCH when p 0]
    P0 --> S1[Captures leverage only]
```

---

## 4.3 Asymmetric Response Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Positive Shock plus epsilon] --> N1[Effect: alpha 1 minus gamma power delta]
    N[Negative Shock minus epsilon] --> N2[Effect: alpha 1 plus gamma power delta]
    N1 --> R[Response Ratio R = one plus gamma over one minus gamma power delta]
    N2 --> R
    R --> Rval{R greater than 1}
    Rval -- Yes --> Leverage[Leverage Effect Confirmed]
    Rval -- No --> Symmetric[Symmetric Response gamma approx 0]
```
