# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] Define the APARCH(p,q) model. State the role of the power parameter $\delta$ and the leverage parameter $\gamma_i$.**

**Model Answer:**

> The **APARCH(p,q)** model (Ding, Granger & Engle, 1993) is given by
> 
> $$\sigma_t^{\delta} = \omega + \sum_{i=1}^{q} \alpha_i \left( \vert \varepsilon_{t-i} \vert - \gamma_i \varepsilon_{t-i} \right)^{\delta} + \sum_{j=1}^{p} \beta_j \, \sigma_{t-j}^{\delta}$$
> 
> where $\varepsilon_t = \sigma_t z_t$ and $z_t \overset{iid}{\sim}(0,1)$.
> 
> **Role of $\delta$ (power parameter, $\delta>0$):** Determines the transformation applied to the conditional standard deviation. Different values of $\delta$ nest different models — $\delta=2$ gives GARCH, $\delta=1$ gives AGARCH, and $\delta \to 0$ yields log-GARCH.
> 
> **Role of $\gamma_i$ (leverage parameter, $-1<\gamma_i<1$):** Controls the **asymmetric response** of volatility to positive versus negative shocks. If $\gamma_i > 0$, negative shocks raise $\sigma_t$ more than positive shocks of equal magnitude, capturing the **leverage effect**.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] List any four special cases of the APARCH model and the parameter values that yield them.**

**Model Answer:**

> The APARCH model nests several GARCH-family models:
> 1. **GARCH(p,q):** $\delta = 2$, $\gamma_i = 0$.
> 2. **AGARCH (Absolute GARCH):** $\delta = 1$, $\gamma_i = 0$.
> 3. **GJR-GARCH / Threshold GARCH:** $\delta = 2$, $\gamma_i \ne 0$.
> 4. **NARCH (Nonlinear ARCH):** $\gamma_i = 0$, $\delta$ arbitrary.
> 5. **Log-ARCH:** $\delta \to 0$, $\gamma_i = 0$.
> 6. **Pure Power ARCH:** $p = 0$, $\delta$ and $\gamma_i$ arbitrary.
> 
> This nesting property makes APARCH a powerful unifying framework for volatility modelling.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #1:** When asked to "show that APARCH reduces to GARCH/GJR/etc.", students often **set $\delta=2$ and $\gamma_i=0$ but forget to square the absolute value** or to expand the squared asymmetric term. Always expand the inner term explicitly, *do not* skip algebraic steps. A fully worked reduction typically carries **7 marks** by itself.
> 
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #2:** The covariance-stationarity condition for APARCH involves $E\vert z_t\vert^{\delta}$, **not simply** $\sum \alpha_i + \sum \beta_j < 1$. Writing the standard GARCH stationarity condition when $\delta \ne 2$ will attract a **partial-mark deduction**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the **asymmetric response ratio** $R$ of negative to positive shocks of equal magnitude in the APARCH(1,1) model. Explain the role of $\gamma_1$ in producing the leverage effect.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Show that the APARCH(p,q) model with $\delta = 2$ and asymmetric $\gamma_i$ reduces to the **GJR-GARCH** model of Glosten, Jagannathan, and Runkle (1993). Express the resulting parameters in terms of the original APARCH parameters.

---

#### **Solution to A(a):**

For APARCH(1,1), set $p=q=1$:

$$\sigma_t^{\delta} = \omega + \alpha_1 \left( \vert \varepsilon_{t-1} \vert - \gamma_1 \varepsilon_{t-1} \right)^{\delta} + \beta_1 \sigma_{t-1}^{\delta}$$

**Step 1 — Evaluate the inner asymmetric term for positive shock.** If $\varepsilon_{t-1} > 0$ with magnitude $c$:

$$\vert \varepsilon_{t-1} \vert - \gamma_1 \varepsilon_{t-1} = c - \gamma_1 c = c(1 - \gamma_1)$$

**Step 2 — Raise to the power $\delta$:**

$$\left( c(1 - \gamma_1) \right)^{\delta} = (1 - \gamma_1)^{\delta} c^{\delta}$$

**Step 3 — Multiply by $\alpha_1$:**

$$\text{Good-news contribution} = \alpha_1 (1 - \gamma_1)^{\delta} c^{\delta}$$

**Step 4 — Repeat for negative shock.** If $\varepsilon_{t-1} < 0$ with magnitude $c$, then $\vert \varepsilon_{t-1} \vert = c$ and $\varepsilon_{t-1} = -c$:

$$\vert \varepsilon_{t-1} \vert - \gamma_1 \varepsilon_{t-1} = c - \gamma_1 (-c) = c(1 + \gamma_1)$$

**Step 5 — Raise to $\delta$ and multiply by $\alpha_1$:**

$$\text{Bad-news contribution} = \alpha_1 (1 + \gamma_1)^{\delta} c^{\delta}$$

**Step 6 — Form the ratio $R$:**

$$R = \frac{\alpha_1 (1+\gamma_1)^{\delta} c^{\delta}}{\alpha_1 (1-\gamma_1)^{\delta} c^{\delta}} = \left( \frac{1+\gamma_1}{1-\gamma_1} \right)^{\delta}$$

**Interpretation:** If $\gamma_1 > 0$, then $1 + \gamma_1 > 1 - \gamma_1$, giving $R > 1$. This means a negative shock of magnitude $c$ contributes more to $\sigma_t^{\delta}$ than an equal positive shock — the **leverage effect**. The exponent $\delta$ amplifies the asymmetry: the further $\delta$ is from $1$, the stronger the leverage effect for a given $\gamma_1$. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Solution to A(b):**

Start with APARCH(p,q) and set $\delta = 2$:

$$\sigma_t^{2} = \omega + \sum_{i=1}^{q} \alpha_i \left( \vert \varepsilon_{t-i} \vert - \gamma_i \varepsilon_{t-i} \right)^{2} + \sum_{j=1}^{p} \beta_j \sigma_{t-j}^{2}$$

**Step 1 — Expand the inner square for a generic lag $i$:**

$$u_i^{2} = \left( \vert \varepsilon_{t-i} \vert - \gamma_i \varepsilon_{t-i} \right)^{2} = \vert \varepsilon_{t-i} \vert^{2} - 2\gamma_i \vert \varepsilon_{t-i} \vert \varepsilon_{t-i} + \gamma_i^{2} \varepsilon_{t-i}^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Note that $\vert \varepsilon \vert^2 = \varepsilon^2$.** Hence:

$$u_i^{2} = \varepsilon_{t-i}^{2} - 2\gamma_i \vert \varepsilon_{t-i} \vert \varepsilon_{t-i} + \gamma_i^{2} \varepsilon_{t-i}^{2}$$

**Step 3 — Express the cross-term using the indicator of negative shocks.** Let $I_{t-i}^{-} = \mathbf{1}\{\varepsilon_{t-i} < 0\}$. Then

$$\vert \varepsilon_{t-i} \vert \varepsilon_{t-i} = \varepsilon_{t-i}^{2} - 2 \varepsilon_{t-i}^{2} I_{t-i}^{-}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute:**

$$u_i^{2} = \varepsilon_{t-i}^{2} - 2\gamma_i \left( \varepsilon_{t-i}^{2} - 2 \varepsilon_{t-i}^{2} I_{t-i}^{-} \right) + \gamma_i^{2} \varepsilon_{t-i}^{2}$$

$$u_i^{2} = (1 - 2\gamma_i + \gamma_i^{2})\varepsilon_{t-i}^{2} + 4\gamma_i \varepsilon_{t-i}^{2} I_{t-i}^{-}$$

$$u_i^{2} = (1-\gamma_i)^{2} \varepsilon_{t-i}^{2} + 4\gamma_i \varepsilon_{t-i}^{2} I_{t-i}^{-}$$

**Step 5 — Plug back into the APARCH equation:**

$$\sigma_t^{2} = \omega + \sum_{i=1}^{q} \alpha_i (1-\gamma_i)^{2} \varepsilon_{t-i}^{2} + \sum_{i=1}^{q} 4\alpha_i \gamma_i I_{t-i}^{-} \varepsilon_{t-i}^{2} + \sum_{j=1}^{p} \beta_j \sigma_{t-j}^{2}$$

**Step 6 — Define new parameters:**

$$\alpha_i^{\text{GJR}} = \alpha_i (1 - \gamma_i)^{2}, \quad \gamma_i^{\text{GJR}} = 4 \alpha_i \gamma_i$$

**Step 7 — Write the GJR-GARCH form:**

$$\boxed{\sigma_t^{2} = \omega + \sum_{i=1}^{q} \alpha_i^{\text{GJR}} \varepsilon_{t-i}^{2} + \sum_{i=1}^{q} \gamma_i^{\text{GJR}} I_{t-i}^{-} \varepsilon_{t-i}^{2} + \sum_{j=1}^{p} \beta_j \sigma_{t-j}^{2}}$$

This is exactly the **GJR-GARCH** model. *(7 marks)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Adapted]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** For the APARCH(p,q) model with $\gamma_i = 0$, derive the **unconditional powered variance** $\bar{\sigma}^{\delta}$ in terms of $\omega$, $\alpha_i$, $\beta_j$, and the moment $m_\delta = E\vert z_t \vert^{\delta}$. State the covariance-stationarity condition.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss how the APARCH(1,1) model with $\delta = 1$ and $\gamma_1 = 0$ reduces to the AGARCH model, and explain why a constant $\sigma_t$ (deterministic volatility) would violate the model structure.

---

#### **Solution to B(a):**

**Step 1 — Write APARCH with $\gamma_i = 0$:**

$$\sigma_t^{\delta} = \omega + \sum_{i=1}^{q} \alpha_i \vert \varepsilon_{t-i} \vert^{\delta} + \sum_{j=1}^{p} \beta_j \sigma_{t-j}^{\delta}$$

**Step 2 — Take expectations on both sides assuming covariance stationarity:**

$$E[\sigma_t^{\delta}] = E[\sigma_{t-1}^{\delta}] = \cdots = \bar{\sigma}^{\delta}$$

$$\bar{\sigma}^{\delta} = \omega + \sum_{i=1}^{q} \alpha_i E[\vert \varepsilon_{t-i} \vert^{\delta}] + \sum_{j=1}^{p} \beta_j \bar{\sigma}^{\delta}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the law of iterated expectations.** Since $\varepsilon_t = \sigma_t z_t$ and $z_t$ is independent of $\sigma_t$:

$$E[\vert \varepsilon_t \vert^{\delta}] = E[\sigma_t^{\delta}] \cdot E[\vert z_t \vert^{\delta}] = \bar{\sigma}^{\delta} \cdot m_\delta$$

where $m_\delta = E\vert z_t \vert^{\delta}$.

**Step 4 — Substitute and solve:**

$$\bar{\sigma}^{\delta} = \omega + m_\delta \left( \sum_{i=1}^{q} \alpha_i \right) \bar{\sigma}^{\delta} + \left( \sum_{j=1}^{p} \beta_j \right) \bar{\sigma}^{\delta}$$

$$\bar{\sigma}^{\delta} \left[ 1 - m_\delta \sum_{i=1}^{q} \alpha_i - \sum_{j=1}^{p} \beta_j \right] = \omega$$

$$\boxed{\bar{\sigma}^{\delta} = \frac{\omega}{1 - m_\delta \sum_{i=1}^{q} \alpha_i - \sum_{j=1}^{p} \beta_j}}$$

**Step 5 — State the stationarity condition.** For $\bar{\sigma}^{\delta} > 0$ to be finite and positive, we require:

$$m_\delta \sum_{i=1}^{q} \alpha_i + \sum_{j=1}^{p} \beta_j < 1$$

For Gaussian $z_t$, $m_\delta = 2^{\delta/2} \Gamma((\delta+1)/2) / \sqrt{\pi}$. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Solution to B(b):**

**Step 1 — Set $\delta = 1$ and $\gamma_1 = 0$ in the APARCH(1,1) equation:**

$$\sigma_t^{1} = \omega + \alpha_1 \left( \vert \varepsilon_{t-1} \vert - 0 \cdot \varepsilon_{t-1} \right)^{1} + \beta_1 \sigma_{t-1}^{1}$$

**Step 2 — Reduce:**

$$\sigma_t = \omega + \alpha_1 \vert \varepsilon_{t-1} \vert + \beta_1 \sigma_{t-1}$$

**Step 3 — Recognise this as the AGARCH (Absolute GARCH) model** of Taylor (1986) and Schwert (1990). The conditional standard deviation $\sigma_t$ is a linear function of the lagged absolute innovation $\vert \varepsilon_{t-1} \vert$ and its own past value.

**Step 4 — Discuss why constant $\sigma_t$ violates the model.** Suppose $\sigma_t = c$ for all $t$. Then

$$c = \omega + \alpha_1 \vert \varepsilon_{t-1} \vert + \beta_1 c$$

$$\vert \varepsilon_{t-1} \vert = \frac{c (1 - \beta_1) - \omega}{\alpha_1}$$

The right-hand side is a constant $k$. This forces $\vert \varepsilon_t \vert = k$ for all $t$, which contradicts the assumption that innovations are i.i.d. (it would imply degenerate innovations with zero variance — and even then, the recursion would not hold consistently across all $t$). More fundamentally, a constant $\sigma_t$ contradicts the **heteroskedastic** nature of financial returns that APARCH is designed to capture. The model requires $\sigma_t$ to **evolve** as a function of past shocks and past volatility, generating volatility clustering. *(7 marks)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Asymmetric Power ARCH (APARCH)**

- **Master Equation:**
  $\sigma_t^{\delta} = \omega + \sum_{i=1}^{q} \alpha_i \left( \vert \varepsilon_{t-i} \vert - \gamma_i \varepsilon_{t-i} \right)^{\delta} + \sum_{j=1}^{p} \beta_j \sigma_{t-j}^{\delta}$
- **Innovation equation:** $\varepsilon_t = \sigma_t z_t$, with $z_t \overset{iid}{\sim}(0,1)$.
- **Five parameters** per lag: $\alpha_i, \beta_j, \gamma_i$ (with $\delta$ global). Recall: $\omega > 0$, $\alpha_i \ge 0$, $\beta_j \ge 0$, $\delta > 0$, $-1 < \gamma_i < 1$.
- **Power exponent $\delta$:** $\delta = 2$ → GARCH-type, $\delta = 1$ → AGARCH, $\delta \to 0$ → log-GARCH.
- **Leverage parameter $\gamma_i$:** positive $\gamma_i$ ⇒ negative shocks raise $\sigma_t$ disproportionately.
- **Asymmetric Response Ratio:** $R = \left( \dfrac{1+\gamma_i}{1-\gamma_i} \right)^{\delta}$. If $R > 1$, leverage effect is present.
- **Stationarity:** $m_\delta \sum \alpha_i + \sum \beta_j < 1$ where $m_\delta = E\vert z_t \vert^{\delta}$.
- **Unconditional powered variance:** $\bar{\sigma}^{\delta} = \dfrac{\omega}{1 - m_\delta \sum \alpha_i - \sum \beta_j}$.
- **Special cases (must memorise):**
  - $\delta=2, \gamma_i=0$ → **GARCH**
  - $\delta=1, \gamma_i=0$ → **AGARCH**
  - $\delta=2, \gamma_i \ne 0$ → **GJR-GARCH**
  - $\gamma_i=0, p=0$ → **NARCH**
  - $\delta \to 0, \gamma_i=0$ → **log-GARCH**
- **Estimation:** QML (Gaussian) or exact MLE under Student-t / GED; $\hat{\theta} = \arg\max \mathcal{L}(\theta)$.
- **Why it matters:** APARCH is the most general single-equation volatility model in the GARCH family and is widely used in risk management, derivative pricing, and VaR/ES computations.
- **Common exam slip-ups:** (i) Forgetting to expand $\left( \vert \varepsilon \vert - \gamma \varepsilon \right)^{2}$ when deriving GJR; (ii) Writing $\sum \alpha_i + \sum \beta_j < 1$ instead of $m_\delta \sum \alpha_i + \sum \beta_j < 1$; (iii) Treating $\gamma_i$ as unconstrained (must satisfy $-1 < \gamma_i < 1$).
- **Quick test:** If you see the words "leverage", "power", and "asymmetry" in the same model — the answer is **APARCH** (Ding–Granger–Engle, 1993).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Nonsymmetric GARCH Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET742 - FINANCIAL TIME SERIES ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Nonsymmetric GARCH Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Nonsymmetric GARCH Model (Asymmetric GARCH Family)**
> A class of Generalized Autoregressive Conditional Heteroscedasticity (GARCH) models in which the conditional variance equation explicitly distinguishes between the impact of **positive shocks** (good news) and **negative shocks** (bad news) on future volatility. Unlike the standard symmetric GARCH(p, q) model of Bollerslev (1986), the nonsymmetric family introduces a **leverage term** that allows past negative residuals to inflate conditional variance more strongly than past positive residuals of equal magnitude.

The principal members of this family studied in the KTU 2024 syllabus are:

1. **GJR-GARCH(p, q)** — Glosten, Jagannathan & Runkle (1993)
2. **TGARCH(p, q)** — Threshold GARCH, Zakoian (1994)
3. **EGARCH(p, q)** — Exponential GARCH, Nelson (1991)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Bad-News Pendulum"**
> Imagine a market investor standing on a tightrope. A piece of *good news* (e.g., an earnings beat) is a gentle push from the left — the rope wobbles, but settles quickly. A piece of *bad news* (e.g., a sudden policy shock) is a hard push from the right — the rope not only wobbles, but its swings grow larger and persist far longer. This asymmetry — where negative information destabilizes the system more than positive information of equal size — is the central premise of the Nonsymmetric GARCH family. The standard symmetric GARCH cannot capture this because it treats $\varepsilon_{t-1}^{2}$ identically regardless of sign. The nonsymmetric model injects an **indicator function** $I(\varepsilon_{t-1}<0)$ that activates an extra "fear multiplier" during downturns.

**Key Empirical Motivation:** Empirically, equity returns exhibit a strong **leverage effect** (Black, 1976): a sudden drop in stock price increases financial leverage (debt-to-equity ratio), which raises risk and hence volatility. This produces the characteristic negatively skewed conditional return distribution and the empirical fact that **$\text{Corr}(\varepsilon_t^2, \varepsilon_{t-1}) < 0$** while **$\text{Corr}(\vert \varepsilon_t \vert, \varepsilon_{t-1}) < 0$** — a sign that volatility itself depends asymmetrically on lagged signs, not just magnitudes.

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 Module 2 specifically emphasizes that the Asymmetric GARCH family is essential whenever the data exhibits **volatility asymmetry**, statistically testable via the **sign-bias test** of Engle & Ng (1993).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Justification — The "Why"

The standard **GARCH(1,1)** variance equation

$$\sigma_t^2 = \omega + \alpha \varepsilon_{t-1}^2 + \beta \sigma_{t-1}^2$$

is **symmetric** because it depends on $\varepsilon_{t-1}^2$ — the *square* of the shock — which discards the sign information. To reintroduce sign, we partition the innovation into two regimes:

$$
\varepsilon_{t-1}^2 = \underbrace{\varepsilon_{t-1}^2 \cdot I(\varepsilon_{t-1} \geq 0)}_{\text{good news}} + \underbrace{\varepsilon_{t-1}^2 \cdot I(\varepsilon_{t-1} < 0)}_{\text{bad news}}
$$

where $I(\cdot)$ is the Heaviside indicator function equal to 1 if the condition holds, 0 otherwise. The coefficient multiplying the bad-news component is allowed to be **larger** than the good-news coefficient, producing asymmetry.

### 2.2 The Three Principal Models

#### (A) GJR-GARCH(1,1) — Glosten, Jagannathan & Runkle (1993)

$$
\sigma_t^2 = \omega + \alpha \varepsilon_{t-1}^2 + \gamma \varepsilon_{t-1}^2 I(\varepsilon_{t-1} < 0) + \beta \sigma_{t-1}^2
$$

- **Interpretation:**
  - $\alpha$ → effect of good news (positive shocks).
  - $\alpha + \gamma$ → effect of bad news (negative shocks).
  - **Asymmetry condition:** $\gamma > 0$ ⇒ bad news increases volatility more than good news.
  - **Symmetry test:** $H_0: \gamma = 0$.

#### (B) TGARCH(1,1) — Zakoian (1994)

Uses conditional **standard deviation** instead of variance:

$$
\sigma_t = \omega + \alpha \vert \varepsilon_{t-1} \vert + \gamma \vert \varepsilon_{t-1} \vert I(\varepsilon_{t-1} < 0) + \beta \sigma_{t-1}
$$

- **Interpretation:** Identical economic structure to GJR but linear in $\vert \varepsilon_{t-1} \vert$.
- **Asymmetry condition:** $\gamma > 0$ for leverage effect.

#### (C) EGARCH(1,1) — Nelson (1991)

$$
\ln(\sigma_t^2) = \omega + \alpha \frac{\vert \varepsilon_{t-1} \vert}{\sigma_{t-1}} + \gamma \frac{\varepsilon_{t-1}}{\sigma_{t-1}} + \beta \ln(\sigma_{t-1}^2)
$$

- **Interpretation:**
  - The log-form guarantees $\sigma_t^2 > 0$ automatically (no positivity constraints on parameters).
  - $\gamma$ measures **asymmetry** (size effect of sign).
  - $\gamma < 0$ ⇒ negative shocks increase volatility more.
  - $\alpha$ captures **magnitude effect** (GARCH-style persistence of absolute shocks).
  - $\beta$ captures **volatility clustering** persistence.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the single most important reference for the ESE. Memorize the formulas, stationarity conditions, and sign conventions.

| **Element** | **GJR-GARCH(1,1)** | **TGARCH(1,1)** | **EGARCH(1,1)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Variance Eq.** | $\sigma_t^2 = \omega + \alpha \varepsilon_{t-1}^2 + \gamma \varepsilon_{t-1}^2 I(\varepsilon_{t-1}<0) + \beta \sigma_{t-1}^2$ | $\sigma_t = \omega + \alpha \vert \varepsilon_{t-1} \vert + \gamma \vert \varepsilon_{t-1} \vert I(\varepsilon_{t-1}<0) + \beta \sigma_{t-1}$ | $\ln(\sigma_t^2) = \omega + \alpha \frac{\vert \varepsilon_{t-1} \vert}{\sigma_{t-1}} + \gamma \frac{\varepsilon_{t-1}}{\sigma_{t-1}} + \beta \ln(\sigma_{t-1}^2)$ |
| **News Type** | Quadratic in $\varepsilon$ | Linear in $\vert \varepsilon \vert$ | Log-form, scaled standardized residual |
| **Persistence** | $\alpha + \beta + \gamma/2$ | $\alpha + \beta + \gamma/2$ | $\vert \beta \vert$ |
| **Stationarity** | $\alpha + \beta + \gamma/2 < 1$ | $\alpha + \beta + \gamma/2 < 1$ | $\vert \beta \vert < 1$ |
| **Positivity** | $\omega > 0$, $\alpha \geq 0$, $\beta \geq 0$, $\alpha + \gamma \geq 0$ | $\omega > 0$, $\alpha \geq 0$, $\beta \geq 0$ | $\sigma_t^2 > 0$ automatic |
| **Asymmetry Param.** | $\gamma$ (sign of leverage) | $\gamma$ (sign of leverage) | $\gamma$ (negative = leverage) |
| **Unconditional Var.** | $\frac{\omega}{1-\alpha-\beta-\gamma/2}$ | $\left(\frac{\omega}{1-\alpha-\beta-\gamma/2}\right)^2$ | $\frac{\omega}{1-\beta}$ (for $\ln \sigma^2$) |
| **Half-Life** | $\frac{\ln(0.5)}{\ln(\alpha+\beta+\gamma/2)}$ | $\frac{\ln(0.5)}{\ln(\alpha+\beta+\gamma/2)}$ | $\frac{\ln(0.5)}{\ln \vert \beta \vert}$ |
| **Returns Std. Error** | $\hat{\sigma}_t$ from recursion | $\hat{\sigma}_t$ from recursion | $\exp(\hat{\omega}/2(1-\beta))$ for stationarity |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering / Financial Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Where Nonsymmetric GARCH is Used Professionally:**
> 1. **Risk Management** — computing asymmetric Value-at-Risk (VaR) and Expected Shortfall (ES) during crisis periods.
> 2. **Option Pricing** — feeding asymmetric conditional variance into stochastic volatility option models (e.g., Heston, Bates).
> 3. **Derivative Hedging** — dynamic delta-hedging ratios depend on whether the underlying is in a "good-news" or "bad-news" regime.
> 4. **Algorithmic Trading** — volatility breakout strategies exploit the asymmetric persistence of shocks.
> 5. **Macro-Prudential Regulation** — central banks (e.g., RBI, Federal Reserve) use GJR-type models to forecast tail risk in equity markets during recession periods.

### 2.5 Logical Flow of Model Construction (Step-by-Step Logic)

1. **Step 1 — Specify Mean Equation:** Fit an ARMA(p, q) or constant-mean to the return series $r_t$ to obtain residuals $\hat{\varepsilon}_t$.
2. **Step 2 — Test for ARCH Effects:** Apply the **Engle (1982) Lagrange Multiplier (LM) test** on $\hat{\varepsilon}_t^2$. Rejecting $H_0$ of no ARCH justifies GARCH-family modeling.
3. **Step 3 — Specify Asymmetric Variance Equation:** Choose GJR, TGARCH, or EGARCH based on data properties (e.g., EGARCH preferred when negative parameter constraints are binding).
4. **Step 4 — Estimate Parameters via MLE:** Maximize the Gaussian quasi-log-likelihood:

$$
\ell(\theta) = -\frac{T}{2} \ln(2\pi) - \frac{1}{2} \sum_{t=1}^{T} \left[ \ln \sigma_t^2 + \frac{\varepsilon_t^2}{\sigma_t^2} \right]
$$

5. **Step 5 — Diagnostic Checking:** Examine standardized residuals $\hat{z}_t = \hat{\varepsilon}_t / \hat{\sigma}_t$ and $\hat{z}_t^2$ for remaining ARCH effects via Ljung-Box Q-statistics.
6. **Step 6 — Test for Asymmetry:** Conduct the **Engle-Ng (1993) sign-bias test** to verify the asymmetric specification is justified.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the GJR-GARCH(1,1) Stationarity Constraint

**Starting Point:** The GJR-GARCH(1,1) variance equation:

$$
\sigma_t^2 = \omega + \alpha \varepsilon_{t-1}^2 + \gamma \varepsilon_{t-1}^2 I(\varepsilon_{t-1} < 0) + \beta \sigma_{t-1}^2
$$

**Step 1 — Take the Unconditional Expectation**

Assume the process is strictly stationary with $E[\sigma_t^2] = E[\sigma_{t-1}^2] = \bar{\sigma}^2$ and $E[\varepsilon_t^2] = \bar{\sigma}^2$ (the unconditional second moment equals the long-run variance). Also assume symmetry of the innovation distribution so that $E[I(\varepsilon_{t-1} < 0)] = 1/2$.

Taking expectations of both sides:

$$
E[\sigma_t^2] = \omega + \alpha E[\varepsilon_{t-1}^2] + \gamma E[\varepsilon_{t-1}^2 I(\varepsilon_{t-1} < 0)] + \beta E[\sigma_{t-1}^2]
$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate the Cross-Moment**

Under the symmetry assumption:

$$
E[\varepsilon_{t-1}^2 I(\varepsilon_{t-1} < 0)] = \frac{1}{2} E[\varepsilon_{t-1}^2] = \frac{1}{2} \bar{\sigma}^2
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute and Solve for the Long-Run Variance**

$$
\bar{\sigma}^2 = \omega + \alpha \bar{\sigma}^2 + \gamma \cdot \frac{1}{2} \bar{\sigma}^2 + \beta \bar{\sigma}^2
$$

$$
\bar{\sigma}^2 - \alpha \bar{\sigma}^2 - \frac{\gamma}{2} \bar{\sigma}^2 - \beta \bar{\sigma}^2 = \omega
$$

$$
\bar{\sigma}^2 \left( 1 - \alpha - \beta - \frac{\gamma}{2} \right) = \omega
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the Long-Run Variance**

$$
\boxed{\bar{\sigma}^2 = \frac{\omega}{1 - \alpha - \beta - \frac{\gamma}{2}}}
$$

**Step 5 — Derive the Stationarity Condition**

For $\bar{\sigma}^2$ to be a finite, positive, real number, the denominator must be strictly positive:

$$
1 - \alpha - \beta - \frac{\gamma}{2} > 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \boxed{\alpha + \beta + \frac{\gamma}{2} < 1}
$$

This is the **covariance stationarity condition** for GJR-GARCH(1,1). Note that when $\gamma = 0$, this collapses to the familiar GARCH(1,1) condition $\alpha + \beta < 1$.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Half-Life of a Shock

A common KTU question asks for the "persistence" or "half-life" of a volatility shock. We derive it for GJR-GARCH(1,1).

**Starting Point:** Let $\Phi = \alpha + \beta + \gamma/2$ be the persistence parameter. After a unit shock, the variance recursion behaves approximately as $\sigma_{t+h}^2 - \bar{\sigma}^2 \approx \Phi^h (\sigma_t^2 - \bar{\sigma}^2)$.

**Step 1 — Set the Decay Fraction to 0.5**

We want $h$ such that the shock has decayed to half of its initial magnitude:

$$
\Phi^h = \frac{1}{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Take the Natural Logarithm**

$$
h \ln \Phi = \ln(0.5) = -\ln 2
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $h$**

$$
\boxed{h = \frac{\ln(0.5)}{\ln(\alpha + \beta + \gamma/2)} = -\frac{\ln 2}{\ln(\alpha + \beta + \gamma/2)}}
$$

This gives the **half-life in trading days**. For example, with $\alpha = 0.05$, $\beta = 0.90$, $\gamma = 0.06$, we get $\Phi = 0.98$ and $h \approx 34.3$ days.

### 3.3 Derivation of the Maximum Likelihood Estimator (MLE) — Gaussian QML

Assume the conditional distribution of $\varepsilon_t$ given the past is **Gaussian with mean 0 and variance $\sigma_t^2$**:

$$
\varepsilon_t \mid \mathcal{F}_{t-1} \sim N(0, \sigma_t^2)
$$

**Step 1 — Write the Conditional Density**

$$
f(\varepsilon_t \mid \mathcal{F}_{t-1}) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi \sigma_t^2}} \exp\left( -\frac{\varepsilon_t^2}{2\sigma_t^2} \right)
$$

**Step 2 — Form the Log-Likelihood Function**

The full log-likelihood for a sample of size $T$:

$$
\ell(\theta) = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \log f(\varepsilon_t \mid \mathcal{F}_{t-1})
$$

$$
\ell(\theta) = -\frac{T}{2} \ln(2\pi) - \frac{1}{2} \sum_{t=1}^{T} \ln \sigma_t^2 - \frac{1}{2} \sum_{t=1}^{T} \frac{\varepsilon_t^2}{\sigma_t^2}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the GJR-GARCH Variance Recursion**

For each $t$, $\sigma_t^2 = \omega + \alpha \varepsilon_{t-1}^2 + \gamma \varepsilon_{t-1}^2 I(\varepsilon_{t-1}<0) + \beta \sigma_{t-1}^2$.

**Step 4 — Numerical Maximization via Quasi-Newton (BFGS)**

The MLE does not have a closed-form solution; we maximize numerically. The score vector for parameter $\theta_k$:

$$
\frac{\partial \ell}{\partial \theta_k} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \frac{1}{2\sigma_t^2} \frac{\partial \sigma_t^2}{\partial \theta_k} \left( \frac{\varepsilon_t^2}{\sigma_t^2} - 1 \right)
$$

**Step 5 — Bollerslev-Wooldridge (1992) Robust Standard Errors**

Because Gaussian QML may be misspecified (innovations often have fat tails), use the **Bollerslev-Wooldridge robust covariance matrix**:

$$
\widehat{\text{Var}}(\hat{\theta}) = H^{-1} \left( \sum_{t=1}^{T} g_t g_t' \right) H^{-1}
$$

where $H = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \frac{\partial^2 \ell_t}{\partial \theta \partial \theta'}$ is the Hessian and $g_t = \frac{\partial \ell_t}{\partial \theta}$ is the per-observation score.

### 3.4 Derivation of the Leverage Function for EGARCH

For EGARCH(1,1):

$$
\ln(\sigma_t^2) = \omega + \alpha \frac{\vert \varepsilon_{t-1} \vert}{\sigma_{t-1}} + \gamma \frac{\varepsilon_{t-1}}{\sigma_{t-1}} + \beta \ln(\sigma_{t-1}^2)
$$

**Step 1 — Define the Standardized Residual**

$$
z_{t-1} = \frac{\varepsilon_{t-1}}{\sigma_{t-1}}
$$

**Step 2 — Rewrite the Equation**

$$
\ln(\sigma_t^2) = \omega + \alpha \vert z_{t-1} \vert + \gamma z_{t-1} + \beta \ln(\sigma_{t-1}^2)
$$

**Step 3 — Identify the Two Components**

The term $\alpha \vert z_{t-1} \vert$ is the **magnitude effect** (symmetric, depends on $\vert z_{t-1} \vert$). The term $\gamma z_{t-1}$ is the **sign effect** (asymmetric, depends on sign of $z_{t-1}$).

**Step 4 — Derive the Conditional Log-Variance Response**

For a unit-magnitude shock, $z_{t-1} = +1$ (good news):

$$
\Delta \ln(\sigma_t^2)_{+} = \alpha(1) + \gamma(1) = \alpha + \gamma
$$

For a unit-magnitude shock, $z_{t-1} = -1$ (bad news):

$$
\Delta \ln(\sigma_t^2)_{-} = \alpha(1) + \gamma(-1) = \alpha - \gamma
$$

**Step 5 — Define the Asymmetry Ratio**

$$
\text{Asymmetry Ratio} = \frac{\alpha - \gamma}{\alpha + \gamma}
$$

If $\gamma < 0$ (leverage effect present), then $\alpha - \gamma > \alpha + \gamma$, so the bad-news response exceeds the good-news response by a factor of $\frac{\alpha - \gamma}{\alpha + \gamma}$. The leverage effect is empirically confirmed when this ratio is significantly greater than 1.

### 3.5 Summary of Analytical Transitions

| **From** | **To** | **Key Operation** |
|---|---|---|
| Symmetric GARCH | GJR-GARCH | Add $\gamma \varepsilon_{t-1}^2 I(\varepsilon_{t-1}<0)$ |
| Variance form | TGARCH | Take $\sqrt{\cdot}$ and replace $\varepsilon^2$ with $\vert \varepsilon \vert$ |
| Variance form | EGARCH | Apply $\ln(\cdot)$ and standardize residuals |
| Unconditional var. | Stationarity | Denominator positivity: $1 - \alpha - \beta - \gamma/2 > 0$ |
| Stationarity | Half-life | $h = \ln(0.5) / \ln(\Phi)$ |
| Likelihood | Score equation | Differentiate $\ell(\theta)$ wrt $\theta_k$ |
| MLE Covariance | Robust SE | Bollerslev-Wooldridge sandwich formula |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Asymmetric GARCH Modeling Pipeline

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the complete analytical workflow from raw return data to validated asymmetric GARCH model. It treats the modeling process as a **flow graph of information transformations** — a safer alternative to physical drawings that may not render properly.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Return Series r_t] --> B[ARMA Mean Equation Fit]
    B --> C[Residual Series epsilon_t]
    C --> D{Engle LM Test for ARCH}
    D -- Reject H0 --> E[Justify GARCH Family]
    D -- Fail to Reject --> F[Stop: No Volatility Clustering]
    E --> G[Asymmetry Detection via Engle-Ng Sign-Bias Test]
    G -- Significant --> H{Choose Model Type}
    G -- Not Significant --> I[Use Symmetric GARCH 1,1]
    H --> J[GJR-GARCH 1,1]
    H --> K[TGARCH 1,1]
    H --> L[EGARCH 1,1]
    J --> M[Gaussian QML Estimation]
    K --> M
    L --> M
    M --> N[Parameter Estimates omega, alpha, beta, gamma]
    N --> O[Diagnostic Check on Standardized Residuals]
    O -- Residuals Clean --> P[Validated Asymmetric GARCH Model]
    O -- Residuals Inadequate --> Q[Refine: Increase p, q or Use Student-t Distribution]
    P --> R[Volatility Forecasting and Risk Management]
```

### 4.2 Information Flow: Shock Decomposition Mechanism

This diagram shows how a single shock $\varepsilon_{t-1}$ is decomposed by the asymmetric GARCH model into its directional contributions to the next-period conditional variance $\sigma_t^2$.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S[Shock epsilon at t-1] --> SQ[Square: epsilon squared]
    SQ --> SPLIT{Sign Test}
    SPLIT -- Positive --> G[Good News Branch]
    SPLIT -- Negative --> B[Bad News Branch]
    G --> GM[Multiply by alpha]
    B --> BM1[Multiply by alpha]
    B --> BM2[Multiply by gamma then add to BM1]
    GM --> ADD[Sum: omega + GM + BM1 + BM2 + beta sigma at t-1 squared]
    BM1 --> ADD
    BM2 --> ADD
    P[Past Variance sigma at t-1 squared] --> PB[Multiply by beta]
    PB --> ADD
    ADD --> OUT[New Conditional Variance sigma at t squared]
```

### 4.3 Stationarity Region in the (α, β, γ) Parameter Space

The following conceptual diagram maps the **stationarity region** for GJR-GARCH(1,1). This is critical for KTU 14-mark derivations.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    O[Origin: alpha = 0, beta = 0, gamma = 0] --> A1[Increasing alpha]
    A1 --> PLANE{Constraint Surface}
    PLANE -- alpha + beta + gamma/2 < 1 --> INSIDE[Stationary Region: All shocks decay exponentially]
    PLANE -- alpha + beta + gamma/2 = 1 --> BOUND[Unit Root Boundary: IGARCH Behavior, infinite persistence]
    PLANE -- alpha + beta + gamma/2 > 1 --> OUTSIDE[Explosive Region: Variance diverges to infinity]
    INSIDE --> N1[Persistence Phi between 0 and 1]
    N1 --> HL[Half-Life h = ln 0.5 over ln Phi]
    HL --> APPL[Apply to: Volatility Forecasting, VaR Computation]
```

### 4.4 Diagnostic Cascade for Asymmetric GARCH Models

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    EST[Estimated Model: omega, alpha, beta, gamma] --> Z[Compute Standardized Residuals z_t]
    Z --> Z1[Ljung-Box Test on z_t: Should be White Noise]
    Z --> Z2[Ljung-Box Test on z_t squared: Should be White Noise]
    Z1 -- Reject --> FAIL1[Model Inadequate: Add ARMA terms]
    Z2 -- Reject --> FAIL2[Model Inadequate: Increase GARCH Order]
    Z1 -- Pass --> NB[Engle-Ng Sign-Bias Test]
    Z2 -- Pass --> NB
    NB -- gamma Significant --> OK[Asymmetric Component Justified]
    NB -- gamma Insignificant --> SYM[Refit as Symmetric GARCH]
    OK --> ARCHLM[ARCH-LM Test on z_t squared]
    ARCHLM -- Pass --> VAL[Validated Model Ready for Forecasting]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **What is meant by the "leverage effect" in financial time series, and how does the GJR-GARCH model capture it?**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> The **leverage effect** refers to the empirical finding that **negative returns (bad news) tend to increase future volatility more than positive returns (good news) of the same magnitude**. It was first documented by Black (1976), who observed a strong negative correlation between equity returns and changes in return volatility.

The **GJR-GARCH(1,1)** model of Glosten, Jagannathan & Runkle (1993) captures this by augmenting the standard GARCH variance equation with an indicator-function term:

$$
\sigma_t^2 = \omega + \alpha \varepsilon_{t-1}^2 + \gamma \varepsilon_{t-1}^2 \, I(\varepsilon_{t-1} < 0) + \beta \sigma_{t-1}^2
$$

where $I(\varepsilon_{t-1} < 0)$ is the indicator function equal to 1 if the lagged residual is negative, and 0 otherwise.

- The effect of **good news** ($\varepsilon_{t-1} \geq 0$) on $\sigma_t^2$ is governed by $\alpha$.
- The effect of **bad news** ($\varepsilon_{t-1} < 0$) on $\sigma_t^2$ is governed by $\alpha + \gamma$.
- A **positive and statistically significant $\gamma$** is the empirical signature of the leverage effect, i.e., bad news increases volatility more than good news.

> **Word Count Guideline:** ~110 words. *(3 marks)*

---

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **State the GARCH mean equation, GJR-GARCH(1,1) variance equation, and the stationarity condition. Explain the role of the indicator function $I(\varepsilon_{t-1} < 0)$.**

**Model Answer:**

**Mean Equation:**

$$
r_t = \mu + \varepsilon_t, \qquad \varepsilon_t = \sigma_t z_t, \qquad z_t \overset{iid}{\sim} N(0,1)
$$

**GJR-GARCH(1,1) Variance Equation:**

$$
\sigma_t^2 = \omega + \alpha \varepsilon_{t-1}^2 + \gamma \varepsilon_{t-1}^2 \, I(\varepsilon_{t-1} < 0) + \beta \sigma_{t-1}^2
$$

**Stationarity Condition:**

$$
\alpha + \beta + \frac{\gamma}{2} < 1
$$

**Role of the Indicator Function $I(\varepsilon_{t-1} < 0)$:**

- It is a Heaviside step function taking value **1** when $\varepsilon_{t-1} < 0$ (bad news) and **0** otherwise.
- It **selects** the bad-news component of the squared shock, which is then multiplied by the additional asymmetry parameter $\gamma$.
- It enables the variance equation to **respond differently** to positive and negative shocks, breaking the symmetry of the standard GARCH(1,1).
- When $\gamma = 0$, the indicator term vanishes, and the model reduces to the symmetric GARCH(1,1) of Bollerslev (1986).

> **Word Count Guideline:** ~120 words. *(3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — GJR-GARCH Modeling and Half-Life Derivation

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 2]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Define the GJR-GARCH(1,1) model. Clearly state the mean equation, variance equation, parameter constraints, and stationarity condition. Derive the **covariance stationarity condition** from first principles by taking the unconditional expectation of the variance equation under a symmetric innovation distribution.

**(b)** [7 Marks] For a fitted GJR-GARCH(1,1) model with $\omega = 0.00002$, $\alpha = 0.06$, $\beta = 0.88$, $\gamma = 0.10$:
   (i) Verify whether the model satisfies the stationarity condition.
   (ii) Compute the **unconditional long-run variance** $\bar{\sigma}^2$.
   (iii) Compute the **half-life of a volatility shock** in trading days.
   (iv) Comment on whether the leverage effect is present.

---

#### **Complete Model Solution**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Mean Equation**

$$
r_t = \mu + \varepsilon_t, \quad \varepsilon_t = \sigma_t z_t, \quad z_t \overset{iid}{\sim} N(0,1)
$$

**Step 2 — Variance Equation**

$$
\sigma_t^2 = \omega + \alpha \varepsilon_{t-1}^2 + \gamma \varepsilon_{t-1}^2 \, I(\varepsilon_{t-1} < 0) + \beta \sigma_{t-1}^2
$$

**Step 3 — Parameter Constraints**

- $\omega > 0$ (ensures positive variance).
- $\alpha \geq 0$ (positive reaction to good news).
- $\beta \geq 0$ (volatility persistence).
- $\alpha + \gamma \geq 0$ (positive reaction to bad news).

**Step 4 — Derivation of Stationarity Condition**

Take the unconditional expectation of both sides:

$$
E[\sigma_t^2] = \omega + \alpha E[\varepsilon_{t-1}^2] + \gamma E[\varepsilon_{t-1}^2 \, I(\varepsilon_{t-1} < 0)] + \beta E[\sigma_{t-1}^2]
$$

By strict stationarity, $E[\sigma_t^2] = E[\sigma_{t-1}^2] = \bar{\sigma}^2$ and $E[\varepsilon_{t-1}^2] = \bar{\sigma}^2$. Under symmetry of the innovation distribution, $E[\varepsilon_{t-1}^2 I(\varepsilon_{t-1}<0)] = \tfrac{1}{2} \bar{\sigma}^2$. Substituting:

$$
\bar{\sigma}^2 = \omega + \alpha \bar{\sigma}^2 + \frac{\gamma}{2} \bar{\sigma}^2 + \beta \bar{\sigma}^2
$$

$$
\bar{\sigma}^2 \left( 1 - \alpha - \beta - \frac{\gamma}{2} \right) = \omega
$$

$$
\bar{\sigma}^2 = \frac{\omega}{1 - \alpha - \beta - \gamma/2}
$$

For finite positive $\bar{\sigma}^2$, the denominator must be positive:

$$
\boxed{\alpha + \beta + \frac{\gamma}{2} < 1}
$$

This is the **covariance stationarity condition** of GJR-GARCH(1,1).

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Stationarity Check**

Compute the persistence parameter:

$$
\Phi = \alpha + \beta + \frac{\gamma}{2} = 0.06 + 0.88 + \frac{0.10}{2} = 0.06 + 0.88 + 0.05 = 0.99
$$

Since $\Phi = 0.99 < 1$, the **stationarity condition is satisfied** (marginally).

**Step 2 — Unconditional Long-Run Variance**

$$
\bar{\sigma}^2 = \frac{\omega}{1 - \Phi} = \frac{0.00002}{1 - 0.99} = \frac{0.00002}{0.01} = 0.002
$$

Equivalently, $\bar{\sigma} = \sqrt{0.002} \approx 0.0447$, or roughly **4.47% per period** as the long-run standard deviation.

**Step 3 — Half-Life of a Volatility Shock**

$$
h = \frac{\ln(0.5)}{\ln(\Phi)} = \frac{-0.6931}{\ln(0.99)} = \frac{-0.6931}{-0.01005} \approx 68.97 \text{ days}
$$

Thus, a volatility shock takes approximately **69 trading days** (about 3.3 months) to decay to half its original magnitude. This is consistent with the empirical stylized fact that equity volatility is highly persistent.

**Step 4 — Leverage Effect Assessment**

- Good-news coefficient: $\alpha = 0.06$.
- Bad-news coefficient: $\alpha + \gamma = 0.06 + 0.10 = 0.16$.
- Ratio: $\frac{\alpha + \gamma}{\alpha} = \frac{0.16}{0.06} \approx 2.67$.

A **negative shock increases conditional variance by 2.67 times more** than a positive shock of equal magnitude. **Leverage effect is strongly present.**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — EGARCH Derivation and News Impact Curve

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Define the EGARCH(1,1) model. Show that it **guarantees positive conditional variance regardless of parameter signs**, and explain why this is an advantage over GJR-GARCH. Derive the **leverage function** showing how the conditional log-variance responds to good versus bad news.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Compute the **one-step-ahead conditional variance forecast** $\hat{\sigma}_{T+1}^2$ for an EGARCH(1,1) model with $\omega = -0.10$, $\alpha = 0.20$, $\beta = 0.95$, $\gamma = -0.08$, given the following: at time $T$, the standardized residual $z_T = \hat{\varepsilon}_T / \hat{\sigma}_T = -1.5$ and the previous conditional variance $\hat{\sigma}_T^2 = 0.0025$. Verify the **stationarity** of the process.

---

#### **Complete Model Solution**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — EGARCH(1,1) Variance Equation**

$$
\ln(\sigma_t^2) = \omega + \alpha \frac{\vert \varepsilon_{t-1} \vert}{\sigma_{t-1}} + \gamma \frac{\varepsilon_{t-1}}{\sigma_{t-1}} + \beta \ln(\sigma_{t-1}^2)
$$

**Step 2 — Proof of Automatic Positivity**

Exponentiate both sides:

$$
\sigma_t^2 = \exp\!\left[ \omega + \alpha \frac{\vert \varepsilon_{t-1} \vert}{\sigma_{t-1}} + \gamma \frac{\varepsilon_{t-1}}{\sigma_{t-1}} + \beta \ln(\sigma_{t-1}^2) \right]
$$

Since the **exponential function always yields a strictly positive value** for any finite real argument, $\sigma_t^2 > 0$ is **mathematically guaranteed** without imposing sign restrictions on $\alpha$, $\beta$, or $\gamma$.

**Step 3 — Advantage over GJR-GARCH**

In GJR-GARCH, the positivity constraints $\omega > 0$, $\alpha \geq 0$, $\beta \geq 0$, $\alpha + \gamma \geq 0$ create a **restrictive feasible region** in parameter space. EGARCH eliminates these, making estimation more numerically stable and avoiding boundary issues during optimization.

**Step 4 — Derivation of the Leverage Function**

Let $z_{t-1} = \varepsilon_{t-1}/\sigma_{t-1}$. Then:

$$
\ln(\sigma_t^2) = \omega + \alpha \vert z_{t-1} \vert + \gamma z_{t-1} + \beta \ln(\sigma_{t-1}^2)
$$

For a unit-magnitude shock:

- **Good news** ($z_{t-1} = +1$): $\Delta \ln \sigma_t^2 \big|_{z=+1} = \alpha + \gamma$.
- **Bad news** ($z_{t-1} = -1$): $\Delta \ln \sigma_t^2 \big|_{z=-1} = \alpha - \gamma$.

**Leverage function:**

$$
\boxed{\text{News Impact Asymmetry} = (\alpha - \gamma) - (\alpha + \gamma) = -2\gamma}
$$

If $\gamma < 0$ (leverage effect), then the bad-news response exceeds the good-news response by $-2\gamma$ in log-variance units.

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Stationarity Check**

For EGARCH, the stationarity condition is:

$$
\vert \beta \vert < 1
$$

Given $\beta = 0.95$, we have $\vert 0.95 \vert = 0.95 < 1$. **The process is covariance stationary.**

**Step 2 — Compute the One-Step-Ahead Forecast**

Substitute into the EGARCH(1,1) equation:

$$
\ln(\hat{\sigma}_{T+1}^2) = \omega + \alpha \vert z_T \vert + \gamma z_T + \beta \ln(\hat{\sigma}_T^2)
$$

Plug in the values: $\omega = -0.10$, $\alpha = 0.20$, $\beta = 0.95$, $\gamma = -0.08$, $z_T = -1.5$, $\hat{\sigma}_T^2 = 0.0025$.

**Step 3 — Evaluate Each Term**

- $\alpha \vert z_T \vert = 0.20 \times 1.5 = 0.30$
- $\gamma z_T = (-0.08) \times (-1.5) = +0.12$
- $\ln(\hat{\sigma}_T^2) = \ln(0.0025) = -5.9915$

**Step 4 — Assemble and Exponentiate**

$$
\ln(\hat{\sigma}_{T+1}^2) = -0.10 + 0.30 + 0.12 + 0.95 \times (-5.9915)
$$

$$
\ln(\hat{\sigma}_{T+1}^2) = -0.10 + 0.30 + 0.12 - 5.6919 = -5.3719
$$

$$
\hat{\sigma}_{T+1}^2 = \exp(-5.3719) \approx 0.00464
$$

**Step 5 — Final Answer**

$$
\boxed{\hat{\sigma}_{T+1}^2 \approx 0.00464 \quad \text{or} \quad \hat{\sigma}_{T+1} \approx 6.81\% \text{ per period}}
$$

Compared to the previous $\hat{\sigma}_T = 5.0\%$, this represents a **36% increase in variance forecast** — consistent with the leverage effect, since the standardized residual is negative.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks (KTU Valuation Patterns):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the 1/2 factor in stationarity condition:** Many students write $\alpha + \beta + \gamma < 1$ instead of the correct $\alpha + \beta + \gamma/2 < 1$. This costs **2 marks** in the 7-mark derivation. The 1/2 arises from the symmetry assumption $E[I(\varepsilon_{t-1}<0)] = 1/2$.
>
> 2. **Mixing up TGARCH with GJR-GARCH:** TGARCH uses **conditional standard deviation** $\sigma_t$ and **absolute value** $\vert \varepsilon_{t-1} \vert$, not the squared terms. Examiners will deduct marks for this confusion.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the sign flip in EGARCH:** In EGARCH, the **leverage condition is $\gamma < 0$** (not $\gamma > 0$ as in GJR). Writing "$\gamma > 0$ indicates leverage" in an EGARCH question is a guaranteed **1-mark loss**.
>
> 4. **Not using Bollerslev-Wooldridge robust standard errors:** If the question asks for inference on the asymmetry parameter, the answer must explicitly mention that robust SEs are used because the Gaussian QML may be misspecified under fat-tailed returns.
>
> 5. **Skipping the diagnostic step:** Every 14-mark answer must include a brief **diagnostic check** (Ljung-Box on $z_t$ and $z_t^2$) for full marks.
>
> 6. **Forgetting to exponentiate in EGARCH:** When asked to compute $\sigma_{T+1}^2$ from $\ln(\sigma_{T+1}^2)$, students often stop at the log form. The **exponential transformation is mandatory** for a complete answer.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Nonsymmetric GARCH**

- [ ] **GJR-GARCH(1,1):** $\sigma_t^2 = \omega + \alpha \varepsilon_{t-1}^2 + \gamma \varepsilon_{t-1}^2 I(\varepsilon_{t-1}<0) + \beta \sigma_{t-1}^2$. Leverage ⇒ $\gamma > 0$.
- [ ] **TGARCH(1,1):** Uses $\sigma_t$ and $\vert \varepsilon_{t-1} \vert$ (not squared). Leverage ⇒ $\gamma > 0$.
- [ ] **EGARCH(1,1):** $\ln(\sigma_t^2) = \omega + \alpha \vert z_{t-1} \vert + \gamma z_{t-1} + \beta \ln(\sigma_{t-1}^2)$. **Automatic positivity.** Leverage ⇒ $\gamma < 0$.
- [ ] **Stationarity Condition (GJR/TGARCH):** $\alpha + \beta + \gamma/2 < 1$.
- [ ] **Stationarity Condition (EGARCH):** $\vert \beta \vert < 1$.
- [ ] **Unconditional Variance (GJR):** $\bar{\sigma}^2 = \omega / (1 - \alpha - \beta - \gamma/2)$.
- [ ] **Half-Life Formula:** $h = \ln(0.5) / \ln(\Phi)$ where $\Phi$ is the persistence parameter.
- [ ] **News Impact Asymmetry in EGARCH:** $-2\gamma$ in log-variance units.
- [ ] **Estimation:** Gaussian Quasi-MLE with Bollerslev-Wooldridge robust SEs.
- [ ] **Diagnostics:** Ljung-Box on $z_t$ and $z_t^2$; Engle-Ng sign-bias test for asymmetry.
- [ ] **Leverage Effect Origin:** Black (1976) — falling stock price increases financial leverage (debt/equity), raising risk and volatility.
- [ ] **Indicator Function:** $I(\varepsilon_{t-1} < 0) = 1$ if $\varepsilon_{t-1} < 0$, else 0. Acts as a **regime switch** between good-news and bad-news coefficients.
- [ ] **Order of Multiplication in EGARCH:** News terms ($\alpha \vert z_{t-1} \vert + \gamma z_{t-1}$) enter **linearly and additively** in log-variance space.
- [ ] **Empirical Stylized Facts Addressed:** Negative skewness, leverage effect, volatility clustering, fat tails.

> **Final Tip for the KTU ESE:** Always begin your 14-mark answer with the **mean equation**, then the **variance equation**, then **constraints**, then **derivation of stationarity**, then **numerical application**. This mirrors the official marking scheme and ensures no marking point is left uncovered.

---

topics.title: Stochastic Volatility Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET742 - FINANCIAL TIME SERIES ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Stochastic Volatility Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stochastic Volatility (SV) Model** is a class of financial time series models in which the variance (volatility) of the asset returns is itself modeled as a latent stochastic process driven by its own random innovation term, in contrast to deterministic volatility specifications (such as GARCH) where today's variance is a deterministic function of past squared returns.

The canonical **Basic SV Model (SV(1))** is formally specified as the two-equation system:

$$
y_t = \sigma_t \, \varepsilon_t, \quad \varepsilon_t \overset{iid}{\sim} N(0, 1)
$$

$$
\ln(\sigma_t^2) = \mu + \phi \ln(\sigma_{t-1}^2) + \eta_t, \quad \eta_t \overset{iid}{\sim} N(0, \sigma_\eta^2)
$$

where:
- $y_t$ is the observed asset return (or demeaned log return) at time $t$.
- $\sigma_t > 0$ is the time-varying, **unobserved** (latent) volatility.
- $\ln(\sigma_t^2)$ is the **log-volatility** (log-variance) process, which follows a Gaussian AR(1) (autoregressive order 1) process.
- $\mu$ is the level parameter (drift of log-volatility).
- $\phi$ is the persistence parameter with the stationarity condition $\vert \phi \vert < 1$.
- $\sigma_\eta^2$ is the variance of the log-volatility innovation.
- The two innovations $(\varepsilon_t, \eta_t)$ are assumed to be **mutually independent** (this is relaxed in leverage SV models).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** In GARCH, $\sigma_t^2$ is a *function* of past **observed** squared returns. In SV, $\sigma_t^2$ is an *unobserved random variable*. This single conceptual shift dramatically changes the inference paradigm from classical MLE to latent-state methods (filtering, MCMC, Expectation-Maximization).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition — "The Weather of Financial Markets"**
> 
> Imagine a financial analyst trying to estimate the *daily wind speed* on a stock market. The analyst can only see the resulting dust clouds (returns), but cannot directly see the wind. GARCH says: "Tomorrow's wind is just a weighted average of today's and yesterday's dust cloud intensity" — a deterministic recurrence. SV says: "Tomorrow's wind is a random perturbation around a *smoothly mean-reverting* long-term climate, AND we can never perfectly measure it." 
> 
> This captures a fundamental empirical truth: volatility "regime" shifts (calm → storm → calm) feel genuinely random and not fully explainable by past returns alone.

**Real-world analogy for log-volatility $\ln(\sigma_t^2)$:** A weather thermometer where temperature (volatility) drifts around a long-run average (governed by $\mu$), with a "thermal inertia" of $\phi$, and random "weather shocks" $\eta_t$.

## 1.3 Why SV Models? (Motivation from Stylized Facts)

| Stylized Fact of Returns | SV Model Property |
|---|---|
| Volatility clustering | Captured by persistence $\phi$ close to 1 |
| Heavy-tailed return distribution | Inherited from stochastic $\sigma_t$ even with Gaussian $\varepsilon_t$ (marginal kurtosis $>3$) |
| Leverage effect (asymmetric correlation between $y_t$ and $\sigma_{t+1}^2$) | Addressed by **Leverage SV (SV-L)** models with non-zero correlation $\rho$ between $\varepsilon_t$ and $\eta_t$ |
| Slow mean-reversion of volatility | $\phi$ close to 1 reflects this empirical regularity |
| Volatility of volatility ("vol-of-vol") | Explicitly captured by $\sigma_\eta$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The module's emphasis is on understanding *why* the volatility process is treated as a *latent stochastic state* (unobservable), and how this leads to estimation challenges distinct from GARCH.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of the Basic SV(1) Model

The model has **two structural equations** and **four core parameters** $(\mu, \phi, \sigma_\eta^2)$ plus any mean parameters for $y_t$.

### 2.1.1 The Observation (Measurement) Equation
$$
y_t \mid \sigma_t^2 \;\sim\; N\!\left(0,\; \sigma_t^2\right)
$$

This says: conditional on the current latent volatility, the return is zero-mean Gaussian with that volatility.

### 2.1.2 The State (Transition) Equation
$$
\ln(\sigma_t^2) \mid \ln(\sigma_{t-1}^2) \;\sim\; N\!\left(\mu + \phi \ln(\sigma_{t-1}^2),\; \sigma_\eta^2\right)
$$

The state equation is **linear in log-variance** (the critical modeling choice). Taking logs ensures:
1. $\sigma_t^2 > 0$ automatically.
2. Innovations are approximately multiplicative on $\sigma_t^2$.
3. The state process is well-defined as a Gaussian AR(1).

### 2.1.3 The State Vector and Stationarity

Define the latent state $h_t \equiv \ln(\sigma_t^2)$. Then:
$$
h_t = \mu + \phi \, h_{t-1} + \eta_t
$$

**Stationarity condition:** $\vert \phi \vert < 1$. When this holds, the process has a **causal (weakly stationary) Gaussian AR(1)** representation with:

$$
h_t = \mu(1-\phi) \sum_{j=0}^{\infty} \phi^j + \sum_{j=0}^{\infty} \phi^j \, \eta_{t-j}
$$

**Unconditional mean and variance:**

$$
E[h_t] = \frac{\mu}{1-\phi}
$$

$$
\text{Var}(h_t) = \frac{\sigma_\eta^2}{1-\phi^2}
$$

## 2.2 Marginal Distribution of Returns (Why SV Generates Heavy Tails)

Even though $\varepsilon_t \sim N(0,1)$, the **unconditional** distribution of $y_t$ is not Gaussian. Conditional on $\sigma_t^2$, $y_t^2 / \sigma_t^2 \sim \chi_1^2$. Mixing over the log-normal distribution of $\sigma_t^2$ yields a marginal with:

$$
\text{Kurtosis}(y_t) = 3 \exp\!\left(\frac{\sigma_\eta^2}{1-\phi^2}\right) > 3
$$

This automatically reproduces the **leptokurtic (heavy-tailed)** nature of financial returns without needing a Student-$t$ innovation.

## 2.3 Extension: Leverage SV Model (SV-L)

To capture the empirical observation that negative returns increase future volatility more than positive returns (the **leverage effect**), we allow correlation between the two innovations:

$$
\begin{pmatrix} \varepsilon_t \\ \eta_t \end{pmatrix} \;\sim\; N\!\left(\mathbf{0},\; \begin{pmatrix} 1 & \rho \\ \rho & 1 \end{pmatrix}\right)
$$

The typical empirical value is $\rho < 0$ (around $-0.3$ to $-0.7$ for equity indices).

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam-Critical Formula Reference**

| # | Concept | Formula / Statement | Exam Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Observation eq. | $y_t = \sigma_t \varepsilon_t$, $\varepsilon_t \sim N(0,1)$ | Model specification |
| 2 | Log-vol AR(1) | $h_t = \mu + \phi h_{t-1} + \eta_t$, $\eta_t \sim N(0,\sigma_\eta^2)$ | State equation |
| 3 | Stationarity | $\vert \phi \vert < 1$ | Validity check |
| 4 | Unconditional mean of $h_t$ | $E[h_t] = \mu / (1-\phi)$ | Long-run log-vol |
| 5 | Unconditional variance of $h_t$ | $\text{Var}(h_t) = \sigma_\eta^2 / (1-\phi^2)$ | Vol-of-vol |
| 6 | Persistence of $h_t$ | $\text{Corr}(h_t, h_{t-1}) = \phi$ | Interpretation |
| 7 | Excess kurtosis of $y_t$ | $3\!\left[\exp\!\left(\frac{\sigma_\eta^2}{1-\phi^2}\right) - 1\right]$ | Heavy-tail proof |
| 8 | Leverage correlation | $\text{Corr}(\varepsilon_t, \eta_t) = \rho$, typically $\rho \in (-0.7, -0.3)$ | SV-L extension |
| 9 | KSC (1998) transformed obs. | $y_t^* = \ln(y_t^2) = h_t + \ln(\varepsilon_t^2) = h_t + z_t$ | Estimation trick |
| 10 | Mixture approx. of $z_t$ | $z_t \approx 0.086\, y^*_1 + 0.453\, y^*_2 + 0.461\, y^*_3$ | Likelihood-based estimation |
| 11 | QML log-likelihood (per $t$) | $\ell_t = -\tfrac{1}{2}\!\left[\ln(2\pi) + \ln(\sigma_t^2) + y_t^2/\sigma_t^2\right]$ | Filtered MLE |
| 12 | Kalman filter update | $h_{t\mid t} = h_{t\mid t-1} + K_t (y_t^2 - E[y_t^2 \mid h_{t\mid t-1}])$ | Linearized filtering |
| 13 | Multivariate SV (factorial) | $\ln(\sigma_{i,t}^2)$ evolves with **common factor** $h_{0,t}$ + idiosyncratic $h_{i,t}$ | Portfolio VaR |
| 14 | Long-run variance | $\bar{\sigma}^2 = \exp\!\left(\frac{\mu}{1-\phi} + \frac{\sigma_\eta^2}{2(1-\phi^2)}\right)$ | Unconditional level |

> [!WARNING]
> **Table Syntax Rule:** Note that the table uses `\vert` (LaTeX) for absolute value, **not** the bare pipe character `|`, to avoid breaking the markdown table column delimiters.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Risk Management (VaR/ES):** Producing forward-looking volatility forecasts for Value-at-Risk computations.
- **Options Pricing:** Local-volatility and stochastic-vol inputs for derivatives pricing (Heston model is the continuous-time cousin).
- **Asset Allocation:** Volatility-weighted portfolio rebalancing.
- **Regulatory Capital:** Basel framework relies on robust volatility estimates.
- **High-Frequency Trading:** State-space formulation permits online filtering of intraday volatility.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Unconditional Mean of the Log-Volatility Process

We want $E[h_t]$ where $h_t = \mu + \phi h_{t-1} + \eta_t$, $\vert\phi\vert < 1$.

**Step 1 — Take expectations of both sides:**
$$
E[h_t] = E[\mu] + E[\phi h_{t-1}] + E[\eta_t]
$$

**Step 2 — Apply linearity and use $E[\eta_t] = 0$:**
$$
E[h_t] = \mu + \phi \, E[h_{t-1}]
$$

**Step 3 — At stationarity, $E[h_t] = E[h_{t-1}] \equiv m$ (long-run mean):**
$$
m = \mu + \phi \, m
$$

**Step 4 — Solve algebraically:**
$$
m - \phi m = \mu
$$

$$
m(1-\phi) = \mu
$$

$$
\boxed{\,m = E[h_t] = \frac{\mu}{1-\phi}\,}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Unconditional Variance of $h_t$

**Step 1 — Take the variance of the state equation:**
$$
\text{Var}(h_t) = \text{Var}(\mu + \phi h_{t-1} + \eta_t) = \phi^2 \text{Var}(h_{t-1}) + \sigma_\eta^2
$$
(using independence of $\eta_t$ from $h_{t-1}$ and constant $\mu$).

**Step 2 — Stationarity: $\text{Var}(h_t) = \text{Var}(h_{t-1}) \equiv v$:**
$$
v = \phi^2 v + \sigma_\eta^2
$$

**Step 3 — Solve:**
$$
v(1-\phi^2) = \sigma_\eta^2
$$

$$
\boxed{\,v = \text{Var}(h_t) = \frac{\sigma_\eta^2}{1-\phi^2}\,}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Excess Kurtosis of $y_t$

We want to show that even with $\varepsilon_t \sim N(0,1)$, the marginal distribution of $y_t = \sigma_t \varepsilon_t$ has excess kurtosis.

**Step 1 — Conditional fourth moment:** Given $\sigma_t^2$, $y_t \mid \sigma_t^2 \sim N(0, \sigma_t^2)$, so:
$$
E[y_t^4 \mid \sigma_t^2] = 3(\sigma_t^2)^2
$$
(For Gaussian, the 4th moment is $3 \times$ variance squared.)

**Step 2 — Unconditional fourth moment by law of total expectation:**
$$
E[y_t^4] = E\!\left[E[y_t^4 \mid \sigma_t^2]\right] = 3 \, E[(\sigma_t^2)^2]
$$

**Step 3 — Use $\sigma_t^2 = \exp(h_t)$ and the log-normal moment formula.** If $h_t \sim N(m, v)$ (with $m, v$ the unconditional moments above), then:
$$
E\!\left[\exp(2h_t)\right] = \exp\!\left(2m + 2v\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Compute unconditional variance of $y_t$:**
$$
\text{Var}(y_t) = E[\sigma_t^2] = E[\exp(h_t)] = \exp\!\left(m + \tfrac{v}{2}\right) = \exp\!\left(\frac{\mu}{1-\phi} + \frac{\sigma_\eta^2}{2(1-\phi^2)}\right)
$$

**Step 5 — Excess kurtosis formula:** $\gamma_2 = \dfrac{E[y_t^4]}{(\text{Var}(y_t))^2} - 3$:
$$
\gamma_2 = \frac{3 \exp(2m + 2v)}{\exp(2m + v)} - 3 = 3\exp(v) - 3
$$

$$
\boxed{\,\gamma_2 = 3\!\left[\exp\!\left(\frac{\sigma_\eta^2}{1-\phi^2}\right) - 1\right] > 0\,}
$$

**Interpretation:** Whenever $\sigma_\eta^2 > 0$ (i.e., volatility is *truly* stochastic), $y_t$ exhibits positive excess kurtosis — heavy tails — **without needing non-Gaussian $\varepsilon_t$**.

## 3.4 Derivation: Kim–Shephard–Chib (KSC) Mixture Approximation

The challenge in SV estimation is that the likelihood involves an intractable integral over the latent $h_t$. KSC (1998) proposed a transformation that converts the problem into a linear Gaussian state-space form.

**Step 1 — Square and log the observations:**
$$
y_t^2 = \sigma_t^2 \varepsilon_t^2 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \ln(y_t^2) = h_t + \ln(\varepsilon_t^2)
$$

**Step 2 — Define the transformed state-space form:**
$$
y_t^* \equiv \ln(y_t^2) = h_t + z_t
$$
where $z_t = \ln(\varepsilon_t^2)$. The state process is the standard AR(1):
$$
h_t = \mu + \phi h_{t-1} + \eta_t
$$

**Step 3 — Problem:** $z_t$ is the log of a $\chi_1^2$, which is **not** Gaussian — its distribution is $\ln(\chi^2_1) \equiv \log\chi^2(0, 2)$ with mean $-1.2704$ and variance $\pi^2/2$.

**Step 4 — KSC's key trick — Gaussian mixture approximation of $z_t$:** They approximate the density of $z_t$ by a 7-component (or 10-component) Gaussian mixture:
$$
f(z_t) \approx \sum_{j=1}^{J} q_j \, N(z_t \mid m_j - 1.2704,\; v_j^2)
$$
with weights $q_j$, means $m_j$, variances $v_j^2$ tabulated in their paper.

**Step 5 — Augmented state-space form:** Introduce a discrete indicator $s_t \in \{1, \dots, J\}$ such that, conditional on $s_t = j$:
$$
z_t \mid s_t = j \;\sim\; N(m_j - 1.2704,\; v_j^2)
$$

This makes the system **conditionally Gaussian**, allowing application of the **Kalman filter** and **EM algorithm** to estimate the parameters.

**Step 6 — The "three-component" simplified version (commonly used in exams):**
$$
z_t \approx 0.086 \, y_{1,t}^* + 0.453 \, y_{2,t}^* + 0.461 \, y_{3,t}^*
$$
where each $y_{j,t}^*$ is a Gaussian with the appropriate pre-tabulated mean and variance. This yields a linear Gaussian state-space that is amenable to standard Kalman filter likelihood evaluation.

## 3.5 Quasi-Maximum Likelihood (QML) Estimation via Particle Filter

When the KSC approximation is not preferred, one can use a **particle filter** to approximate:
$$
L(\theta) = \prod_{t=1}^{T} p(y_t \mid y_{1:t-1};\theta) = \prod_{t=1}^{T} \int p(y_t \mid h_t) \, p(h_t \mid y_{1:t-1}) \, dh_t
$$

**Step 1 — Generate particles $h_t^{(i)} \sim q(\cdot)$ (proposal), $i = 1, \dots, N$.**

**Step 2 — Compute importance weights:**
$$
w_t^{(i)} \propto w_{t-1}^{(i)} \cdot \frac{p(y_t \mid h_t^{(i)}) \, p(h_t^{(i)} \mid h_{t-1}^{(i)})}{q(h_t^{(i)} \mid \cdot)}
$$

**Step 3 — Resample if effective sample size $N_{\text{eff}} = 1/\sum (w_t^{(i)})^2$ is too small.**

**Step 4 — Approximate the predictive density:**
$$
\hat{p}(y_t \mid y_{1:t-1}) \approx \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} w_t^{(i)} p(y_t \mid h_t^{(i)})
$$

**Step 5 — Maximize $\hat{L}(\theta) = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \ln \hat{p}(y_t \mid y_{1:t-1})$ numerically** (e.g., via BHHH or numerical gradient).

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** For KTU 2024 Scheme derivations, you will most often be asked to (i) write down the two SV equations, (ii) derive the unconditional mean/variance of $h_t$, and (iii) demonstrate the heavy-tail property via the excess-kurtosis formula. The KSC approximation and particle filter are typically **explanation** items, not step-derivations.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an SV Estimation Pipeline

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the data flow and computational components used in fitting an SV model to a return series.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[Raw Return Series y_t] --> C1[Sample Statistics and Stylized Fact Checks]
    C1 --> C2{SV vs GARCH Selection}
    C2 -- Choose SV --> A1[Specify Observation Equation]
    C2 -- Choose GARCH --> A2[GARCH Family Specification]
    A1 --> S1[State Equation h_t equals mu plus phi h_t-1 plus eta_t]
    S1 --> E1{Choose Estimation Method}
    E1 -- M1 --> M1[KSC Mixture Approximation via Kalman Filter]
    E1 -- M2 --> M2[Quasi Maximum Likelihood with Particle Filter]
    E1 -- M3 --> M3[Bayesian MCMC with Gibbs Sampling]
    M1 --> V1[Parameter Estimates mu hat, phi hat, sigma eta hat]
    M2 --> V1
    M3 --> V1
    V1 --> D1[Diagnostics: Residual Analysis, Volatility Forecasts, VaR Backtest]
    D1 --> O1[Final Output: Volatility Trajectory and Forecasts]
    A2 --> O2[Comparison Baseline Model]
    O2 --> D1
```

## 4.2 Conceptual Schematic: The Two-Layer Stochastic Process

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph StateLayer["Latent Layer — Log Volatility Process"]
        H1["h_{t-1}"] --AR1 phi-- H2["h_t"]
        H2 --AR1 phi-- H3["h_{t+1}"]
        H1 -. eta_{t-1} .- H2
        H2 -. eta_t .- H3
    end
    subgraph ObsLayer["Observed Layer — Returns"]
        Y1["y_{t-1}"]:::obs
        Y2["y_t"]:::obs
        Y3["y_{t+1}"]:::obs
    end
    H1 -- exp half -- Y1
    H2 -- exp half -- Y2
    H3 -- exp half -- Y3
    Y1 -. epsilon_{t-1} .- Y2
    Y2 -. epsilon_t .- Y3
    classDef obs fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#c33,color:#000
```

**Reading guide:**
- The **Latent Layer** (top) shows the AR(1) recurrence in log-volatility, driven by innovations $\eta_t$ and persistence $\phi$.
- The **Observed Layer** (bottom) shows the noisy, multiplicative realization through $y_t = \exp(h_t/2)\varepsilon_t$.
- The arrows crossing between layers are the **measurement links** from latent state to observation, while arrows within each layer are the **transition links**.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Definition of Stochastic Volatility Model
> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]**

**Q:** Define the Stochastic Volatility (SV) model. Write down its two structural equations and state the stationarity condition.

**Model Answer:**

The **Stochastic Volatility (SV) model** treats the variance of asset returns as an unobserved random variable that follows its own stochastic process, unlike GARCH where volatility is a deterministic function of past squared returns.

The two structural equations of the basic **SV(1) model** are:

**Observation equation:**
$$
y_t = \sigma_t \, \varepsilon_t, \quad \varepsilon_t \overset{iid}{\sim} N(0, 1)
$$

**State (log-volatility) equation:**
$$
\ln(\sigma_t^2) = \mu + \phi \ln(\sigma_{t-1}^2) + \eta_t, \quad \eta_t \overset{iid}{\sim} N(0, \sigma_\eta^2)
$$

**Stationarity condition:** $\vert \phi \vert < 1$, which ensures that the AR(1) process for $h_t \equiv \ln(\sigma_t^2)$ is covariance stationary with finite unconditional mean $\mu/(1-\phi)$ and variance $\sigma_\eta^2/(1-\phi^2)$. **(3 marks — full credit for equations + stationarity condition.)**

---

### Question 2: SV vs GARCH Comparison
> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]**

**Q:** Highlight three key differences between the SV model and the GARCH(1,1) model.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | GARCH(1,1) | SV(1) |
|---|---|---|
| **Volatility specification** | Deterministic function of past squared returns | Latent stochastic process (unobserved) |
| **Source of randomness** | Only one source ($\varepsilon_t$) | Two independent sources ($\varepsilon_t, \eta_t$) |
| **Estimation** | Closed-form (approximate) MLE | Requires latent-state methods (KSC, particle filter, MCMC) |
| **Marginal kurtosis** | Requires Student-$t$ innovations to get heavy tails | Naturally heavy-tailed even with Gaussian $\varepsilon_t$ |
| **Leverage effect** | Captured via EGARCH/GJR-GARCH asymmetric term | Captured via correlation $\rho$ between $\varepsilon_t$ and $\eta_t$ |

**(3 marks — any three points with crisp explanation accepted.)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Specification, Unconditional Moments, Heavy-Tail Property

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] State the basic stochastic volatility model. Derive the unconditional mean and variance of the log-volatility process $h_t \equiv \ln(\sigma_t^2)$.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Show that the marginal distribution of returns $y_t$ in an SV model exhibits excess kurtosis even when the observation innovation $\varepsilon_t$ is standard normal.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) [7 Marks]**

The basic SV(1) model is given by the two equations:
$$
y_t = \sigma_t \varepsilon_t, \quad \varepsilon_t \sim N(0,1) \qquad \text{(Observation eq.)}
$$
$$
h_t = \mu + \phi h_{t-1} + \eta_t, \quad \eta_t \sim N(0, \sigma_\eta^2) \qquad \text{(State eq.)}
$$

with $\vert\phi\vert<1$ and $\varepsilon_t \perp \eta_t$.

**Unconditional mean of $h_t$:** Take expectations of the state equation:
$$
E[h_t] = \mu + \phi E[h_{t-1}]
$$

At stationarity, $E[h_t] = E[h_{t-1}] = m$. Solving:
$$
m(1-\phi) = \mu \;\Longrightarrow\; \boxed{\,m = \dfrac{\mu}{1-\phi}\,}
$$

**Unconditional variance of $h_t$:** Take variance of state equation (using independence of $h_{t-1}$ and $\eta_t$):
$$
\text{Var}(h_t) = \phi^2 \text{Var}(h_{t-1}) + \sigma_\eta^2
$$

At stationarity, $v = \phi^2 v + \sigma_\eta^2$, giving:
$$
\boxed{\,v = \text{Var}(h_t) = \dfrac{\sigma_\eta^2}{1-\phi^2}\,}
$$

**(b) [7 Marks]**

Given $h_t$, the conditional distribution is $y_t \mid h_t \sim N(0, e^{h_t})$, so:
$$
E[y_t^2 \mid h_t] = e^{h_t}, \qquad E[y_t^4 \mid h_t] = 3 e^{2 h_t}
$$

By the **law of total expectation**:
$$
E[y_t^4] = E\!\left[E[y_t^4 \mid h_t]\right] = 3\, E[e^{2h_t}]
$$

Since $h_t \sim N(m, v)$, the log-normal moment gives:
$$
E[e^{2h_t}] = e^{2m + 2v}
$$

The unconditional variance of $y_t$ is:
$$
\text{Var}(y_t) = E[e^{h_t}] = e^{m + v/2}
$$

Excess kurtosis:
$$
\gamma_2 = \frac{E[y_t^4]}{(\text{Var}(y_t))^2} - 3 = \frac{3 e^{2m+2v}}{e^{2m+v}} - 3 = 3 e^{v} - 3
$$

$$
\boxed{\,\gamma_2 = 3\!\left[\exp\!\left(\dfrac{\sigma_\eta^2}{1-\phi^2}\right) - 1\right] > 0 \text{ for } \sigma_\eta^2 > 0\,}
$$

Since $\sigma_\eta^2 > 0$ (volatility is *truly* stochastic), $\gamma_2 > 0$, establishing that returns are **leptokurtic** even with Gaussian $\varepsilon_t$. This is the "free" heavy-tail result of SV models. **$\blacksquare$**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Estimation Methods (KSC and Particle Filter)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the **Kim–Shephard–Chib (KSC) transformation** used in SV estimation. Write down the resulting state-space form.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Outline the **Quasi-Maximum Likelihood** estimation procedure for SV models using a **particle filter**. List three sources of approximation error.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) [7 Marks]**

The KSC (1998) transformation converts the nonlinear SV observation equation into an approximately linear Gaussian state-space form, enabling Kalman filter–based estimation.

**Step 1 — Square and log:**
$$
y_t^2 = \sigma_t^2 \varepsilon_t^2 \;\Longrightarrow\; \ln(y_t^2) = \ln(\sigma_t^2) + \ln(\varepsilon_t^2) = h_t + z_t
$$

**Step 2 — Define transformed observations:** $y_t^* = \ln(y_t^2)$.

**Step 3 — State equation unchanged:**
$$
h_t = \mu + \phi h_{t-1} + \eta_t
$$

**Step 4 — Approximate the log-$\chi^2_1$ distribution of $z_t$ by a Gaussian mixture:**
$$
f(z_t) \approx \sum_{j=1}^{J} q_j \, N(z_t \mid m_j - 1.2704,\; v_j^2)
$$

where the weights $q_j$, means $m_j$, and variances $v_j^2$ are pre-tabulated (e.g., a 7-component or 10-component mixture).

**Step 5 — Indicator-augmented state-space form:** Let $s_t \in \{1, \dots, J\}$ be a discrete mixture indicator. Then conditional on $s_t = j$, the observation noise $z_t$ is Gaussian, so the **Kalman filter applies**:

$$
y_t^* = h_t + z_t, \quad z_t \mid s_t = j \sim N(m_j - 1.2704, v_j^2)
$$

$$
h_t = \mu + \phi h_{t-1} + \eta_t, \quad \eta_t \sim N(0, \sigma_\eta^2)
$$

**Step 6 — EM algorithm** alternates between:
- **E-step:** Run Kalman + smoothing filters conditional on $\theta$ to compute sufficient statistics for $h_t$ and indicator probabilities $P(s_t = j \mid y_{1:T}^*)$.
- **M-step:** Maximize the conditional expected complete-data log-likelihood w.r.t. $(\mu, \phi, \sigma_\eta^2)$.

**(b) [7 Marks]**

In the QML approach, we maximize the **prediction-error decomposition** of the log-likelihood:
$$
\ell(\theta) = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \ln p(y_t \mid y_{1:t-1};\theta)
$$
where $p(y_t \mid y_{1:t-1}) = \int p(y_t \mid h_t)\, p(h_t \mid y_{1:t-1})\, dh_t$ is the **one-step predictive density** of the latent state.

**Particle Filter Procedure:**

1. **Initialize:** Draw $h_0^{(i)} \sim p(h_0)$ for $i = 1, \dots, N$ (particles), and $w_0^{(i)} = 1/N$.

2. **For each $t = 1, \dots, T$:**
   - **Resample:** if effective sample size $N_{\text{eff}} = (\sum (w_{t-1}^{(i)})^2)^{-1} < N/2$, resample particles proportionally to weights.
   - **Propagate:** Draw $h_t^{(i)} \sim p(h_t \mid h_{t-1}^{(i)})$ from the state transition (Gaussian AR(1) transition).
   - **Weight update:** Compute
     $$
     w_t^{(i)} \propto w_{t-1}^{(i)} \cdot p(y_t \mid h_t^{(i)}) = w_{t-1}^{(i)} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi \sigma_t^{2(i)}}}\exp\!\left(-\frac{y_t^2}{2\sigma_t^{2(i)}}\right)
     $$
     and normalize $\sum_i w_t^{(i)} = 1$.
   - **Predictive density estimate:** $\hat{p}(y_t \mid y_{1:t-1}) \approx \frac{1}{N}\sum_i w_t^{(i)}$.

3. **Optimization:** Maximize $\hat{\ell}(\theta) = \sum_t \ln \hat{p}(y_t \mid y_{1:t-1};\theta)$ using numerical optimization (e.g., BHHH).

**Three sources of approximation error:**
1. **Monte Carlo sampling error** — finite particle count $N$ produces noisy likelihood estimates.
2. **Importance sampling variance** — the proposal distribution $q(h_t \mid h_{t-1}^{(i)}, y_t)$ may be a poor match to the true posterior.
3. **Numerical optimization error** — non-convex likelihood surface can trap the optimizer in local maxima (especially for the persistence parameter $\phi$ near 1).

**$\blacksquare$**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in SV Model Questions:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the log transform:** The state equation is on $\ln(\sigma_t^2)$, **not** on $\sigma_t^2$ directly. Writing $h_t = \mu + \phi h_{t-1} + \eta_t$ with the wrong definition (e.g., on $\sigma_t$ instead of $\ln \sigma_t^2$) costs the full derivation marks.
> 
> 2. **Mixing up stationarity conditions:** For GARCH, stationarity is $\alpha_1 + \beta_1 < 1$. For SV(1), it is $\vert \phi \vert < 1$. Do not interchange them.
> 
> 3. **Skipping the "why" in heavy-tail derivation:** Marks are awarded not just for the formula $\gamma_2 = 3\exp(\sigma_\eta^2/(1-\phi^2)) - 3$ but for the *log-normal moment manipulation*. Always show the **law of total expectation** explicitly.
> 
> 4. **Pipes in tables:** When writing $\vert \phi \vert < 1$ inside a markdown table, use `\vert` not `|`, or the table will break and the examiner will see garbled text.
> 
> 5. **Independence assumption:** In the basic SV(1), $\varepsilon_t \perp \eta_t$. If the question specifies "leverage SV," you **must** introduce $\rho \neq 0$ between the two innovations. Do not default to the basic model when leverage is requested.
> 
> 6. **Units and positivity:** Always state that $\sigma_t^2 > 0$ by construction due to the exponential link, and emphasize this as a key advantage over naive GARCH parameter restrictions.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Stochastic Volatility Models**

- ☐ **Two equations:** Observation $y_t = \sigma_t \varepsilon_t$ + State $h_t = \mu + \phi h_{t-1} + \eta_t$ (Gaussian AR(1) on log-volatility).
- ☐ **Latent state:** $h_t$ is **unobserved** — this is the central difference from GARCH.
- ☐ **Stationarity:** $\vert \phi \vert < 1$ → unconditional mean $\mu/(1-\phi)$, variance $\sigma_\eta^2/(1-\phi^2)$.
- ☐ **Persistence:** $\phi$ near $1$ implies highly persistent (slow mean-reverting) volatility.
- ☐ **Vol-of-vol:** $\sigma_\eta$ quantifies "volatility of volatility" — must be strictly positive.
- ☐ **Heavy tails are free:** Excess kurtosis $\gamma_2 = 3[\exp(\sigma_\eta^2/(1-\phi^2)) - 1] > 0$ even with Gaussian $\varepsilon_t$.
- ☐ **Leverage SV (SV-L):** Introduce $\rho = \text{Corr}(\varepsilon_t, \eta_t) < 0$ for asymmetric volatility response.
- ☐ **Marginal of $\sigma_t^2$ is log-normal** (because $h_t$ is Gaussian AR(1)).
- ☐ **KSC (1998) trick:** Transform via $\ln(y_t^2) = h_t + \ln(\varepsilon_t^2)$, approximate the log-$\chi^2$ noise with a Gaussian mixture → Kalman filter applicable.
- ☐ **Particle filter:** General-purpose simulation-based method for QML when likelihood is intractable.
- ☐ **Bayesian MCMC:** Fully Bayesian alternative using Gibbs sampling on the augmented state space.
- ☐ **Long-run volatility:** $\bar{\sigma}^2 = \exp\!\left(\dfrac{\mu}{1-\phi} + \dfrac{\sigma_\eta^2}{2(1-\phi^2)}\right)$.
- ☐ **Independence assumption (basic SV):** $\varepsilon_t \perp \eta_t$ — relax this for leverage.
- ☐ **Estimation challenges:** (i) latent state, (ii) intractable likelihood, (iii) near-unit-root $\phi$, (iv) identification of $\mu$ vs. $\sigma_\eta$ (scale identification).
- ☐ **Continuous-time analogue:** The Heston model $dS/S = \mu\,dt + \sigma_t\, dW_1$, $d\sigma_t^2 = \kappa(\theta - \sigma_t^2)dt + \xi \sigma_t\, dW_2$.

> **End of Module 2 — Stochastic Volatility Model Notes**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Long-Memory Stochastic Volatility Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET742 - FINANCIAL TIME SERIES ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Long-Memory Stochastic Volatility Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Long-Memory Stochastic Volatility (LMSV) Model** is a continuous-time financial time series model in which the instantaneous variance (squared volatility) of asset returns is driven by an unobserved state process whose **autocorrelation function decays hyperbolically** rather than exponentially. This is in sharp contrast to the classical Heston (1993) stochastic volatility model, where log-volatility follows an Ornstein–Uhlenbeck (OU) process and the ACF decays geometrically (exponentially).

Mathematically, in the canonical formulation of Comte and Renault (1998), the discrete-time log-squared-volatility process $\{h_t\}$ is expressed as:

$$h_t = \mu + \sum_{j=0}^{\infty} \psi_j \, \varepsilon_{t-j}, \qquad \varepsilon_t \sim \text{i.i.d.}(0, \sigma_\varepsilon^2)$$

where the impulse response weights $\psi_j$ are **non-summable**, i.e., $\sum_{j=0}^{\infty} \vert \psi_j \vert = \infty$, but the squared weights are summable. This is the defining property of a long-memory (or *long-range dependent*) process. The fractional differencing parameter $d$ lies strictly in the interval $\mathbf{(0, 1/2)}$.

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**
> The Long-Memory Stochastic Volatility model unifies two empirical stylized facts of financial returns:
> 1. **Volatility clustering** (ARCH effects)
> 2. **Hyperbolic decay** of the volatility autocorrelation function at long horizons
> Standard GARCH and Heston SV models can only capture (1) — the LMSV framework is required for (2).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are observing the **daily average temperature** of a city over many years.

- In a **short-memory model** (like a standard GARCH), today's volatility "forgets" yesterday's volatility very quickly — the influence of a shock decays like a half-life of a few days. Think of a thermometer placed indoors that quickly returns to room temperature when the window is closed.
- In a **long-memory stochastic volatility model**, the influence of shocks persists for **months or even years**. After a financial crisis (e.g., 2008), volatility remains elevated for an unusually long period, slowly returning to normal — much like the way a large oceanic anomaly (El Niño) influences global weather patterns for 12–18 months.

> [!NOTE] **Plain-English Summary**
> A Long-Memory SV model says: "Once the financial market gets scared, it stays scared for a *very long time* — and the rate at which it forgets is much slower than what an exponential decay would predict."

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Parameters

The following **bolded** metrics define and characterize an LMSV process:

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Range | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Fractional Differencing Parameter** | $d$ | $0 < d < 1/2$ | Controls the degree of long-range dependence |
| **Hurst Exponent** | $H$ | $1/2 < H < 1$ | Equivalent to $d$ via $H = d + 1/2$ for fractional noise |
| **Volatility Persistence** | — | Hyperbolic | ACF $\rho(k) \sim c \, k^{2d-1}$ as $k \to \infty$ |
| **Spectral Density Singularity** | $f(\lambda)$ | $f(\lambda) \sim \lambda^{-2d}$ | Diverges at zero frequency |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Operational Logic — How a LMSV Model Works

The Long-Memory Stochastic Volatility model operates on **two coupled layers**:

### Layer 1 — The Observed Return Equation
The log-return $r_t$ of an asset follows a conditionally Gaussian process whose variance is driven by an unobservable latent log-volatility $h_t$:

$$r_t = \sigma_t \, \varepsilon_t = \exp\!\left(\tfrac{1}{2} h_t\right) \varepsilon_t, \qquad \varepsilon_t \sim \text{i.i.d. } \mathcal{N}(0,1)$$

### Layer 2 — The Latent Volatility Dynamics
The log-variance $h_t$ evolves as a **fractionally integrated (ARFIMA-type) process**:

$$(1-L)^d \, \Phi(L) \, h_t = \Theta(L) \, \eta_t, \qquad \eta_t \sim \text{i.i.d.}(0, \sigma_\eta^2)$$

where:
