## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper, Module 3]**

**Q1.** Define a **Self-Exciting Threshold Autoregressive (SETAR)** model. State the role of the **threshold delay parameter** $d$.

**Model Answer:**
A SETAR model is a piecewise-linear AR model in which the **threshold variable is a delayed value of the series itself**, $Z_t = Y_{t-d}$, where $d \ge 1$ is the **threshold delay parameter**. The series is divided into regimes based on whether $Y_{t-d}$ lies below or above the threshold $\gamma$. Each regime follows its own AR($p_j$) process. The delay $d$ controls how far back in time the "trigger" for a regime change is taken and is typically selected by minimizing AIC over $d \in \{1, 2, \dots, D_{\max}\}$.

**Q2.** State **two differences** between a linear AR($p$) model and a two-regime TAR model.

**Model Answer:**
1. **Linearity vs. Non-linearity:** A linear AR($p$) assumes a single AR polynomial for all time $t$, whereas a TAR model switches between two or more AR polynomials based on the value of the threshold variable.
2. **Symmetric Response vs. Asymmetric Response:** An AR($p$) treats positive and negative shocks identically (linear response), whereas a TAR model allows the autoregressive coefficients — and hence the conditional mean dynamics — to differ across regimes, producing an **asymmetric** response.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper, Module 3]*

**(a)** Derive the **conditional least squares (CLS)** estimators of the regime-specific AR coefficients in a two-regime TAR model. Clearly state the partitioning of the sample and the form of the normal equations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **procedure for selecting the threshold $\gamma$ and delay $d$** using the Akaike Information Criterion. Mention how the candidate grid for $\gamma$ is typically constructed. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** *Solution already detailed in Section 3.1.* The student should write the partitioned indices $\mathcal{I}_1, \mathcal{I}_2$, define the design matrix $X^{(k)}$, write $\text{RSS}^{(k)}$, differentiate, and arrive at the normal equation and CLS estimator:

$$
\hat{\phi}^{(k)} = \left( X^{(k)\top} X^{(k)} \right)^{-1} X^{(k)\top} \mathbf{y}^{(k)}
$$

Plus the variance estimator $\hat{\sigma}_k^2$.

**(b)** For a fixed $d$, the candidate thresholds are constructed from the **empirical quantiles** of $Y_{t-d}$:

$$
\gamma \in \left\{ \hat{F}^{-1}_{Y_{t-d}}(\pi) : \pi \in \{0.15, 0.20, \dots, 0.85\} \right\}
$$

Endpoints are avoided to ensure each regime has enough observations. For each candidate $\gamma$, fit the TAR model via CLS, compute

$$
\text{AIC}(\gamma) = N \ln\!\left( \frac{\text{RSS}(\gamma)}{N} \right) + 2(p_1 + p_2 + 3)
$$

and pick $\gamma^* = \arg\min \text{AIC}(\gamma)$. The procedure is repeated for $d = 1, 2, \dots, D_{\max}$, and the global minimizer gives $(d^*, \gamma^*)$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper, Module 3, Alternative Set]*

**(a)** Explain the **Tsay (1989) F-test** for threshold non-linearity. State the null hypothesis, the test statistic, and its asymptotic distribution. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A two-regime SETAR(2,2; 1) model is fitted to a financial returns series with threshold $\gamma = 0$. The lower-regime fit gives $\hat{\phi}^{(1)} = (0.05, \, 0.85, \, 0.10)^\top$ and the upper-regime fit gives $\hat{\phi}^{(2)} = (-0.02, \, 0.30, \, 0.55)^\top$. **Interpret** both regimes economically. What does this imply about asymmetry in the data? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** *See Section 3.3 for derivation.* Key points:
- $H_0$: Linear AR($p$) is adequate (parameters constant across regimes).
- $H_1$: TAR model with different parameters across regimes.
- Test statistic: $F_T = \dfrac{(\text{SSR}_0 - \text{SSR}_1)/q}{\text{SSR}_1/(n - n_p)}$.
- Asymptotic distribution: $F(q, n - n_p)$.
- Rejection indicates threshold non-linearity.

**(b)** **Lower regime** ($Y_{t-1} \le 0$, "down" days):
$$
Y_t = 0.05 + 0.85\,Y_{t-1} + 0.10\,Y_{t-2} + \varepsilon_t^{(1)}
$$
Coefficients are close to 1 → **high persistence**; shocks die out slowly. The series is "sticky" in the lower regime, consistent with empirical findings that **negative returns cluster** longer than positive ones.

**Upper regime** ($Y_{t-1} > 0$, "up" days):
$$
Y_t = -0.02 + 0.30\,Y_{t-1} + 0.55\,Y_{t-2} + \varepsilon_t^{(2)}
$$
Coefficients sum to $\approx 0.85$ → **faster mean reversion**; positive shocks dissipate more quickly.

**Economic interpretation:** The data exhibit **asymmetric dynamics**. The lower regime is highly persistent (long memory of past losses), while the upper regime mean-reverts faster — consistent with empirical "leverage effect" observed in equity returns.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in TAR Questions (KTU Board Pattern):**
> 1. **Forgetting to specify the threshold convention** — students lose 1 mark if they write the model without stating whether the inequality is $\le$ or $<$ at the threshold.
> 2. **Mixing up $Z_t$ with $Y_t$** — the threshold variable can be a *delayed* value $Y_{t-d}$, NOT $Y_t$ itself.
> 3. **Treating $d$ as an integer greater than 0** — $d=0$ is invalid because the threshold cannot depend on a contemporaneous (yet-to-be-observed) value in real-time forecasting.
> 4. **Forgetting the $+1$ for the intercept** in each regime when counting free parameters — KTU examiners explicitly deduct half a mark for off-by-one errors in parameter counts.
> 5. **Quoting the wrong asymptotic distribution** of the Tsay test as $\chi^2$ instead of $F$ — this is a frequently observed mistake.
> 6. **Failing to justify the quantile-based grid for $\gamma$** — students must explain why endpoints (0% and 100% quantiles) are excluded (to prevent empty regimes).

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **TAR = Piecewise-linear AR** with the switch governed by a **threshold variable** $Z_t$ relative to a threshold $\gamma$.
- **SETAR** uses $Z_t = Y_{t-d}$ where $d \ge 1$.
- A **two-regime TAR** is fully specified by $(p_1, p_2, d, \gamma)$ and the regime coefficients/variances.
- **CLS estimation** is done regime-by-regime: $\hat{\phi}^{(k)} = (X^{(k)\top}X^{(k)})^{-1}X^{(k)\top}\mathbf{y}^{(k)}$.
- **Threshold selection** uses AIC over a quantile-based grid: $\gamma \in \hat{F}_{Y_{t-d}}^{-1}([0.15, 0.85])$.
- **Delay selection** uses AIC over $d \in \{1, \dots, D_{\max}\}$.
- **Stationarity condition:** spectral radius $\rho(\Phi^{(k)}) < 1$ in every regime.
- **Tsay F-test** checks linearity vs. threshold non-linearity: $F_T \sim F(q, n-n_p)$.
- **Hansen LM test** uses a supremum statistic and does not require $\gamma$ to be estimated.
- TAR models capture **asymmetric dynamics** — a key feature of financial returns, FX rates, and macro variables.
- Always state **threshold convention** ($\le$ vs. $<$) before writing the piecewise model.
- **Number of regimes $L$** is typically 2; higher $L$ adds complexity and overfitting risk.
- The **AIC penalty** is $2(p_1 + p_2 + 3)$ — count carefully: intercept + AR coefficients per regime + threshold + delay (when estimated).
- **Real-world applications:** equity returns, FX, energy load, industrial control, business cycles.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Markov Switching Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET742 - FINANCIAL TIME SERIES ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Markov Switching Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Markov Switching Model (MSM)** is a regime-switching time series model in which the parameters of the data-generating process are governed by an unobservable (latent) discrete-state variable that follows a **first-order Markov chain**. Introduced formally by **James D. Hamilton (1989)** in his seminal Econometrica paper, the MSM allows a single stochastic process to exhibit *fundamentally different statistical behavior* (different mean, variance, autocorrelation structure) across unobserved economic regimes such as expansions, recessions, bull markets, and bear markets.

Formally, for an observed scalar time series $\{y_t\}_{t=1}^T$, the MSM is defined as:

$$y_t = \mu_{S_t} + \phi_1 y_{t-1} + \phi_2 y_{t-2} + \cdots + \phi_p y_{t-p} + \varepsilon_t$$

where:
- $S_t \in \{1, 2, \ldots, M\}$ is the **unobserved regime state** at time $t$
- $\mu_{S_t}$ is the **regime-dependent intercept** (state-conditional mean shift)
- $\varepsilon_t \mid S_t = j \;\sim\; \mathcal{N}(0,\sigma^2_j)$ is the **regime-dependent innovation**
- The transition mechanism is governed by the **Markov transition matrix** $\mathbf{P} = [p_{ij}]$ where $p_{ij} = P(S_t = j \mid S_{t-1} = i)$ with $\sum_{j=1}^M p_{ij} = 1$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Markov Switching Models belong to the family of **non-linear time series models** because the conditional mean and variance of $y_t$ depend on the *entire history* of the latent state process, not just on a finite vector of past observations of $y_t$. This non-linearity distinguishes MSM from ARMA/GARCH models.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Weather-Traffic Story

Imagine you are a data scientist observing the **number of vehicles entering a city every hour**. The same road behaves *wildly differently* on weekdays versus weekends. If you naïvely fit a single linear AR(1) model to the pooled data, you will get a poor fit because the model is forced to find a "compromise" between two clearly different regimes.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Hidden Regime Analogy**
>
> - **Regime 1 (Weekday, $S_t = 1$):** High mean traffic ($\mu_1 = 1200$ cars/hr), high variance ($\sigma_1^2 = 400$). The road is "active."
> - **Regime 2 (Weekend, $S_t = 2$):** Low mean traffic ($\mu_2 = 300$ cars/hr), low variance ($\sigma_2^2 = 80$). The road is "quiet."
> - The Markov property says: *the probability of being in a regime tomorrow depends only on whether today was a weekday or weekend, not on the day before yesterday.* This is realistic!

A Markov Switching Model is exactly this: it **assumes the regime is hidden**, **infers it probabilistically** from the observed traffic counts, and lets the mean/variance change accordingly. The transition matrix $P$ learns, e.g., that the probability of switching from weekday to weekend is high, and the probability of staying on a weekend is also high.

## 1.3 Why Markov Switching Matters in Financial Engineering

| Financial Phenomenon | Why MSM Captures It |
|---|---|
| **Business cycles** (expansion vs. recession) | GDP growth has a bimodal distribution; MSM fits this naturally |
| **Bull vs. bear markets** | Returns have different means and volatilities in up vs. down markets |
| **Sudden volatility spikes** | High-volatility "panic" regime vs. low-volatility "calm" regime |
| **Regime persistence** | Once a recession starts, it tends to persist — Markov chains capture this |
| **Asymmetric risk** | Conditional variance differs by regime, enabling risk-aware forecasting |

## 1.4 Key Terminology Standardized for KTU

- **Regime / State** $\mathbf{S_t}$: The unobservable categorical variable at time $t$.
- **Transition Probability** $\mathbf{p_{ij}}$: $P(S_t = j \mid S_{t-1} = i)$.
- **Filtered Probability** $\boldsymbol{\xi_{t \mid t}}$: $P(S_t = j \mid Y_t)$ — knowing data up to time $t$.
- **Predicted Probability** $\boldsymbol{\xi_{t \mid t-1}}$: $P(S_t = j \mid Y_{t-1})$ — knowing data up to time $t-1$.
- **Smoothed Probability** $\boldsymbol{\xi_{t \mid T}}$: $P(S_t = j \mid Y_T)$ — using the *entire* sample.
- **Ergodic (Unconditional) Probability** $\boldsymbol{\pi^*}$: Long-run fraction of time spent in each regime.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two-Stage Hierarchy of Markov Switching Models

A complete MSM is a **hierarchical two-stage system**:

### Stage 1 — The Hidden Markov Chain (State Equation)

$$P(S_t = j \mid S_{t-1} = i, S_{t-2}, \ldots, S_1) = P(S_t = j \mid S_{t-1} = i) = p_{ij}$$

This is the **Markov property**: the future depends on the past only through the present. The transition matrix for a 2-state MSM is:

$$\mathbf{P} = \begin{bmatrix} p_{11} & p_{12} \\ p_{21} & p_{22} \end{bmatrix}, \quad p_{i1} + p_{i2} = 1, \quad 0 \le p_{ij} \le 1$$

For an $M$-state model, $\mathbf{P}$ is $M \times M$ with each row summing to 1.

### Stage 2 — The State-Conditional Observation Equation

Given the state $S_t = j$, the observable $y_t$ is drawn from a state-specific density:

$$f(y_t \mid S_t = j,\; Y_{t-1}) = f(y_t \mid \theta_j)$$

The **complete parameter set** is $\theta = (\boldsymbol{\phi}, \mu_1, \ldots, \mu_M, \sigma_1^2, \ldots, \sigma_M^2, \mathbf{P})$.

## 2.2 Why AR Coefficients Are Often Held Constant

In the **canonical Hamilton (1989) model**, $\phi_1, \ldots, \phi_p$ are regime-invariant and only $\mu_j$ and $\sigma_j^2$ switch. This is a deliberate modeling choice because:
- Estimating regime-dependent AR coefficients requires more data per regime.
- Most economic intuition is captured by allowing **mean and variance** to shift.
- The intercept shift $\mu_j$ is the "regime premium."

> [!NOTE]
> **Generalization (Mark for Advanced Study):** A **Markov Switching AR (MS-AR)** allows $\phi_{k,j}$ to be regime-specific, but this is computationally expensive and rarely required for KTU-level questions.

## 2.3 The KTU Formula Sheet — Markov Switching Models

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains every formula you will need for a KTU End Semester Examination on this topic. Memorize it; reproduce it confidently in the answer sheet.

| # | Concept | Formula | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Markov property | $P(S_t \mid S_{t-1}, S_{t-2}, \ldots) = P(S_t \mid S_{t-1})$ | Future depends only on present |
| 2 | Transition probability | $p_{ij} = P(S_t = j \mid S_{t-1} = i)$ | Probability of moving $i \to j$ |
| 3 | Row-stochasticity | $\sum_{j=1}^M p_{ij} = 1$ | Each row of $\mathbf{P}$ sums to 1 |
| 4 | Hamilton (1989) Model | $y_t = \mu_{S_t} + \sum_{k=1}^p \phi_k y_{t-k} + \varepsilon_t,\ \varepsilon_t \mid S_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\sigma_{S_t}^2)$ | Canonical MSM with mean + variance switching |
| 5 | Predicted regime prob. | $\xi_{t \mid t-1}(j) = P(S_t = j \mid Y_{t-1}) = \sum_{i=1}^M \xi_{t-1 \mid t-1}(i)\, p_{ij}$ | Hamilton filter — prediction step |
| 6 | Conditional density | $f(y_t \mid S_t = j, Y_{t-1}) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma_j^2}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(y_t - \mu_j - \phi'(Y_{t-1}))^2}{2\sigma_j^2}\right)$ | Likelihood contribution of regime $j$ |
| 7 | Filtered regime prob. | $\xi_{t \mid t}(j) = \dfrac{f(y_t \mid S_t=j, Y_{t-1})\,\xi_{t \mid t-1}(j)}{\sum_{k=1}^M f(y_t \mid S_t=k, Y_{t-1})\,\xi_{t \mid t-1}(k)}$ | Hamilton filter — updating step (Bayes) |
| 8 | Joint prediction | $\eta_{t \mid t-1}(i,j) = P(S_t=j, S_{t-1}=i \mid Y_{t-1}) = \xi_{t-1 \mid t-1}(i)\, p_{ij}$ | Used in Kim smoother |
| 9 | Log-likelihood | $\ell(\theta) = \sum_{t=1}^T \log\!\left(\sum_{j=1}^M f(y_t \mid S_t=j, Y_{t-1})\,\xi_{t \mid t-1}(j)\right)$ | Numerical maximum-likelihood objective |
| 10 | Ergodic probabilities | $\boldsymbol{\pi^*} \mathbf{P} = \boldsymbol{\pi^*},\ \sum_j \pi^*_j = 1$ | Solve $(\mathbf{P}' - \mathbf{I} + \mathbf{11}')\boldsymbol{\pi^*} = \mathbf{1}$ |
| 11 | Expected duration of regime $i$ | $E[D_i] = \dfrac{1}{1 - p_{ii}}$ | Average number of periods spent in state $i$ |
| 12 | State-conditional mean | $E[y_t \mid S_t = j] = \mu_j + \sum_k \phi_k E[y_{t-k} \mid S_t = j]$ | Used in interpretation |
| 13 | State-conditional variance | $\text{Var}(y_t \mid S_t = j) = \sigma_j^2$ | Variance shifts by regime |
| 14 | Two-step-ahead forecast | $E[y_{t+1} \mid Y_t] = \sum_{j=1}^M \xi_{t+1 \mid t}(j) \cdot E[y_{t+1} \mid S_{t+1}=j, Y_t]$ | Uses predicted regime probabilities |

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE FOR ANSWER SHEETS:** When writing the filtered probability formula, never use the vertical bar inside the denominator (e.g., avoid `|Y_{t-1}` typesetting) because it will confuse the reader. Always write it out as "$\sum_{k=1}^M f(y_t \mid S_t=k, Y_{t-1})\,\xi_{t \mid t-1}(k)$".

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering / Economics Utility

- **Central banks** (e.g., the Reserve Bank of India, US Federal Reserve) use MSMs to detect *regime changes in inflation* and decide on monetary policy.
- **Hedge funds** use MSM-based volatility forecasts to size positions in bull/bear regimes.
- **Risk managers** compute **regime-conditional Value-at-Risk (VaR)**: VaR in a high-volatility regime is materially higher than in a low-volatility regime, even for the same portfolio.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Hamilton Filter (1989)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Hamilton filter is the engine that makes MSM estimation tractable. It recursively computes the **filtered regime probability** $\xi_{t \mid t}(j) = P(S_t = j \mid Y_t)$ using only the data up to time $t$. This is the discrete-state analog of the Kalman filter.

### Step 0 — Notation

- $Y_t = (y_1, y_2, \ldots, y_t)$: the data observed up to time $t$.
- $S_t \in \{1, 2, \ldots, M\}$: the unobserved regime.
- $\xi_{t \mid t}(j) \equiv P(S_t = j \mid Y_t)$: **filtered probability** of regime $j$ at time $t$.
- $\xi_{t \mid t-1}(j) \equiv P(S_t = j \mid Y_{t-1})$: **predicted probability** (before seeing $y_t$).

We assume the **parameters** $\theta = (\boldsymbol{\phi}, \{\mu_j\}, \{\sigma_j^2\}, \mathbf{P})$ are known for now; estimation (MLE) is tackled in §3.3.

### Step 1 — The Law of Total Probability (Prediction Step)

We want $\xi_{t \mid t-1}(j) = P(S_t = j \mid Y_{t-1})$. Condition on $S_{t-1} = i$ and sum over $i$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\xi_{t \mid t-1}(j) &= P(S_t = j \mid Y_{t-1}) \\
&= \sum_{i=1}^M P(S_t = j, S_{t-1} = i \mid Y_{t-1}) \quad \text{(law of total probability)} \\
&= \sum_{i=1}^M P(S_t = j \mid S_{t-1} = i, Y_{t-1}) \cdot P(S_{t-1} = i \mid Y_{t-1}) \quad \text{(chain rule of probability)} \\
&= \sum_{i=1}^M P(S_t = j \mid S_{t-1} = i) \cdot \xi_{t-1 \mid t-1}(i) \quad \text{(Markov property: } S_t \perp Y_{t-1} \mid S_{t-1} \text{)} \\
&= \sum_{i=1}^M p_{ij} \cdot \xi_{t-1 \mid t-1}(i)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** To predict being in regime $j$ *before* seeing $y_t$, average the previous filtered probabilities weighted by the transition probabilities $p_{ij}$. This is **Markov propagation**.

### Step 2 — Bayes' Rule (Updating Step)

Now we *observe* $y_t$. Use Bayes' theorem to update:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\xi_{t \mid t}(j) &= P(S_t = j \mid Y_t) = P(S_t = j \mid Y_{t-1}, y_t) \\
&= \frac{P(y_t \mid S_t = j, Y_{t-1}) \cdot P(S_t = j \mid Y_{t-1})}{P(y_t \mid Y_{t-1})} \quad \text{(Bayes' rule)} \\
&= \frac{f(y_t \mid S_t = j, Y_{t-1}) \cdot \xi_{t \mid t-1}(j)}{\sum_{k=1}^M P(y_t \mid S_t = k, Y_{t-1}) \cdot P(S_t = k \mid Y_{t-1})} \quad \text{(denominator: total probability)} \\
&= \frac{f(y_t \mid S_t = j, Y_{t-1}) \cdot \xi_{t \mid t-1}(j)}{\sum_{k=1}^M f(y_t \mid S_t = k, Y_{t-1}) \cdot \xi_{t \mid t-1}(k)}
\end{aligned}
$$

The denominator is a **normalizing constant** ensuring $\sum_{j=1}^M \xi_{t \mid t}(j) = 1$.

### Step 3 — The Gaussian State-Conditional Density

In the canonical Hamilton model, $y_t \mid (S_t = j, Y_{t-1}) \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_j + \phi_1 y_{t-1} + \cdots + \phi_p y_{t-p},\; \sigma_j^2)$. Let $m_{t-1} = \phi_1 y_{t-1} + \cdots + \phi_p y_{t-p}$. Then:

$$f(y_t \mid S_t = j, Y_{t-1}) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi \sigma_j^2}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(y_t - \mu_j - m_{t-1})^2}{2\sigma_j^2}\right)$$

### Step 4 — Initial Conditions (Filter Bootstrap)

The filter is recursive in $t$, so we need $\xi_{1 \mid 0}(j) = P(S_1 = j) = \pi_j^0$. A common choice is the **ergodic distribution** $\pi_j^*$ (the long-run stationary distribution of the Markov chain), obtained by solving:

$$\boldsymbol{\pi^*} \mathbf{P} = \boldsymbol{\pi^*}, \quad \sum_{j=1}^M \pi_j^* = 1$$

Equivalently, $(\mathbf{P}' - \mathbf{I} + \mathbf{11}')\boldsymbol{\pi^*} = \mathbf{1}$.

### Step 5 — The Complete Hamilton Filter Algorithm (Summary)

> [!NOTE]
> **Algorithm: Hamilton Filter for a 2-State Markov Switching Model**
>
> **Inputs:** Data $\{y_t\}_{t=1}^T$, parameters $\theta = (\phi_1,\ldots,\phi_p, \mu_1, \mu_2, \sigma_1^2, \sigma_2^2, p_{11}, p_{22})$.
>
> **Initialize:** Set $\xi_{1 \mid 0}(1) = \pi_1^*$, $\xi_{1 \mid 0}(2) = \pi_2^*$.
>
> **For** $t = 1, 2, \ldots, T$:
>
> 1. Compute conditional densities $f_j \equiv f(y_t \mid S_t = j, Y_{t-1})$ for $j = 1, 2$.
> 2. Numerator: $n_j = f_j \cdot \xi_{t \mid t-1}(j)$.
> 3. Denominator: $D = n_1 + n_2$.
> 4. Update: $\xi_{t \mid t}(j) = n_j / D$.
> 5. Predict: $\xi_{t+1 \mid t}(1) = \xi_{t \mid t}(1) p_{11} + \xi_{t \mid t}(2) p_{21}$.
> 6. Predict: $\xi_{t+1 \mid t}(2) = \xi_{t \mid t}(1) p_{12} + \xi_{t \mid t}(2) p_{22}$.
> 7. Accumulate log-likelihood: $\ell \mathrel{+}= \log(D)$.
>
> **Output:** Filtered probabilities $\{\xi_{t \mid t}\}_{t=1}^T$ and log-likelihood $\ell(\theta)$.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Expected Duration of a Regime

The **expected duration** (sojourn time) in regime $i$ is a quantity examiners love to ask.

$$
\begin{aligned}
E[D_i] &= \sum_{d=1}^\infty d \cdot P(D_i = d) \\
&= \sum_{d=1}^\infty d \cdot (p_{ii})^{d-1} (1 - p_{ii}) \quad \text{(geometric distribution: } d-1 \text{ stays + 1 exit)} \\
&= (1 - p_{ii}) \sum_{d=1}^\infty d \cdot (p_{ii})^{d-1} \\
&= (1 - p_{ii}) \cdot \frac{1}{(1 - p_{ii})^2} \quad \text{(using } \sum_{d=1}^\infty d x^{d-1} = \frac{1}{(1-x)^2} \text{ for } \vert x \vert < 1 \text{)} \\
&= \frac{1}{1 - p_{ii}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Example:** If $p_{11} = 0.95$ (probability of staying in recession next quarter), then $E[D_1] = 1 / (1 - 0.95) = 20$ quarters $\approx$ 5 years. This is consistent with empirical recession durations.

## 3.3 Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE) of MSM Parameters

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU examiners often ask "How are MSM parameters estimated?" — the answer is MLE with the Hamilton filter providing the likelihood.**

The full-sample log-likelihood is:

$$\ell(\theta) = \sum_{t=1}^T \log f(y_t \mid Y_{t-1}; \theta)$$

where

$$f(y_t \mid Y_{t-1}; \theta) = \sum_{j=1}^M f(y_t \mid S_t = j, Y_{t-1}; \theta)\;\xi_{t \mid t-1}(j;\theta)$$

The MLE estimate is:

$$\hat{\theta} = \arg\max_{\theta} \ell(\theta)$$

subject to the constraints $0 \le p_{ij} \le 1$ and $\sum_j p_{ij} = 1$. Numerical optimization is performed via **Newton-Raphson**, **BFGS**, or **EM algorithm**.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — A 2-State MSM

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example (KTU Examination Style)**

**Problem:** Consider a 2-state Markov switching model for a financial return series $y_t$. The state-conditional means are $\mu_1 = 0.5\%$ (bull market) and $\mu_2 = -1.0\%$ (bear market). The transition probabilities are $p_{11} = 0.9$, $p_{12} = 0.1$, $p_{21} = 0.2$, $p_{22} = 0.8$. Assume $p_{21} = 1 - p_{22} = 0.2$ and $p_{12} = 1 - p_{11} = 0.1$. At time $t-1$, the filtered probabilities are $\xi_{t-1 \mid t-1}(1) = 0.7$ and $\xi_{t-1 \mid t-1}(2) = 0.3$. Compute the predicted and updated regime probabilities for time $t$, assuming the new observation $y_t$ has state-conditional densities $f_1 = 0.4$ and $f_2 = 0.1$ (normalized).

**Step 1: Prediction Step**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\xi_{t \mid t-1}(1) &= \xi_{t-1 \mid t-1}(1)\, p_{11} + \xi_{t-1 \mid t-1}(2)\, p_{21} \\
&= (0.7)(0.9) + (0.3)(0.2) \\
&= 0.63 + 0.06 = 0.69 \\
\xi_{t \mid t-1}(2) &= \xi_{t-1 \mid t-1}(1)\, p_{12} + \xi_{t-1 \mid t-1}(2)\, p_{22} \\
&= (0.7)(0.1) + (0.3)(0.8) \\
&= 0.07 + 0.24 = 0.31
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification:** $0.69 + 0.31 = 1.00$ ✓

**Step 2: Updating Step (Bayes)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D &= f_1 \cdot \xi_{t \mid t-1}(1) + f_2 \cdot \xi_{t \mid t-1}(2) \\
&= (0.4)(0.69) + (0.1)(0.31) \\
&= 0.276 + 0.031 = 0.307 \\
\xi_{t \mid t}(1) &= \frac{(0.4)(0.69)}{0.307} = \frac{0.276}{0.307} \approx 0.899 \\
\xi_{t \mid t}(2) &= \frac{(0.1)(0.31)}{0.307} = \frac{0.031}{0.307} \approx 0.101
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** The high likelihood $f_1 = 0.4$ for regime 1 (bull market) confirms the model is now **89.9% confident** we are in the bull regime, up from the predicted 69%.

**Step 3: Expected Duration of Each Regime**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E[D_1] &= \frac{1}{1 - p_{11}} = \frac{1}{1 - 0.9} = 10 \text{ periods} \\
E[D_2] &= \frac{1}{1 - p_{22}} = \frac{1}{1 - 0.8} = 5 \text{ periods}
\end{aligned}
$$

The bull regime (state 1) is more persistent than the bear regime (state 2), which aligns with empirical financial market data.

## 3.5 Comparison Matrix: MSM vs. Other Volatility Models

| Feature | ARMA | GARCH | Markov Switching |
|---|---|---|---|
| Captures time-varying mean | No (static) | No (assumes zero mean) | **Yes** ($\mu_{S_t}$) |
| Captures time-varying variance | No | **Yes** ($\sigma_t^2$ smooth) | **Yes** ($\sigma_{S_t}^2$ jumps) |
| Non-linearity | No | Mild (quadratic) | **Strong** (regime-based) |
| Captures persistence | Via AR/MA roots | Via $\alpha + \beta$ | **Yes** via $p_{ii}$ |
| Captures "regime" concept | No | No | **Yes** (key strength) |
| Estimation | OLS / MLE | MLE | **MLE + Hamilton filter** |
| Forecasting | Linear | Conditional variance | **Regime-weighted forecast** |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The MSM Two-Layer Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph HIDDEN["LAYER 1 - HIDDEN MARKOV CHAIN (Unobserved)"]
        S_prev["State S_{t-1}"]
        S_curr["State S_t"]
        S_next["State S_{t+1}"]
        P_matrix["Transition Matrix P = p_{ij}"]
    end

    subgraph OBSERVED["LAYER 2 - OBSERVED DATA GENERATION"]
        mu_switch["State-Conditional Mean μ_{S_t}"]
        sigma_switch["State-Conditional Variance σ²_{S_t}"]
        y_observed["Observed Series y_t"]
    end

    subgraph FILTER["LAYER 3 - HAMILTON FILTER (Inference Engine)"]
        predict["Prediction Step ξ_{t|t-1}"]
        bayes["Bayes Update ξ_{t|t}"]
        likelihood["Log-Likelihood ℓ(θ)"]
        mle["MLE θ-hat"]
    end

    S_prev -->|"Markov transition p_{ij}"| S_curr
    S_curr -->|"Markov transition p_{ij}"| S_next
    P_matrix -.->|"governs"| S_curr

    S_curr -->|"selects"| mu_switch
    S_curr -->|"selects"| sigma_switch
    mu_switch --> y_observed
    sigma_switch --> y_observed

    y_observed -->|"feeds into"| bayes
    S_prev -->|"feeds into"| predict
    predict --> bayes
    bayes --> likelihood
    likelihood --> mle
    mle -.->|"estimated parameters"| P_matrix
    mle -.->|"estimated parameters"| mu_switch
    mle -.->|"estimated parameters"| sigma_switch
```

## 4.2 The Recursive Hamilton Filter Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Initialize ξ_{1|0} = π*"] --> B["t = 1"]
    B --> C["Predict ξ_{t|t-1} using P matrix"]
    C --> D["Compute f(y_t | S_t = j, Y_{t-1})"]
    D --> E["Bayes Update ξ_{t|t}"]
    E --> F["Accumulate log-likelihood"]
    F --> G{"t = T?"}
    G -->|"No"| H["Increment t -> t+1"]
    H --> C
    G -->|"Yes"| I["Output: Filtered probs + ℓ-hat"]
```

## 4.3 Two-Regime State Diagram (Geometric Intuition)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Regime1
    Regime1 --> Regime2: p_{12} = 0.10
    Regime2 --> Regime1: p_{21} = 0.20
    Regime1 --> Regime1: p_{11} = 0.90
    Regime2 --> Regime2: p_{22} = 0.80
    Regime1: REGIME 1 - BULL MARKET
    Regime1: Mean = 0.5 percent
    Regime1: Variance = sigma_1 squared
    Regime2: REGIME 2 - BEAR MARKET
    Regime2: Mean = -1.0 percent
    Regime2: Variance = sigma_2 squared
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Self-loops ($p_{11}, p_{22}$) represent **regime persistence**. Cross-arrows ($p_{12}, p_{21}$) represent **regime transitions**. The expected duration in regime 1 is $1/(1-p_{11})$; in regime 2 it is $1/(1-p_{22})$.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question]

**Q1.** Define a **Markov Switching Model (MSM)**. State the key assumption regarding the unobserved state variable $S_t$ in an MSM. How does this assumption differ from a standard ARMA model?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A Markov Switching Model is a regime-switching time series model in which the parameters of the data-generating process depend on an unobserved discrete state $S_t \in \{1, 2, \ldots, M\}$ (M1).

The key assumption is that the state variable $S_t$ follows a **first-order Markov chain**: $P(S_t = j \mid S_{t-1}, S_{t-2}, \ldots) = P(S_t = j \mid S_{t-1}) = p_{ij}$ (M1).

This differs from a standard ARMA model, which is **linear** with a constant mean and constant variance, and assumes a *single* regime. MSM allows the mean $\mu_{S_t}$ and variance $\sigma_{S_t}^2$ to switch across regimes, making it a **non-linear** specification (M1).

---

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]

**Q2.** Explain the meaning of the **transition probability** $p_{ij}$ in a 2-state Markov switching model. If $p_{11} = 0.95$ and $p_{22} = 0.90$, compute the **expected duration** of each regime.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
$p_{ij}$ denotes the probability of being in state $j$ at time $t$ given the system was in state $i$ at time $t-1$: $p_{ij} = P(S_t = j \mid S_{t-1} = i)$ (M1).

The expected duration (sojourn time) of regime $i$ is derived from the geometric distribution:

$$E[D_i] = \frac{1}{1 - p_{ii}}$$

(M1 for formula)

Applying this:
- $E[D_1] = 1/(1 - 0.95) = 1/0.05 = \mathbf{20 \text{ periods}}$ (M0.5)
- $E[D_2] = 1/(1 - 0.90) = 1/0.10 = \mathbf{10 \text{ periods}}$ (M0.5)

Regime 1 is more persistent than regime 2.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** For each Part B question, KTU provides an internal choice between two 14-mark questions on related but distinct sub-topics. Both must be attempted OR the student may choose. Here we provide two complete alternatives.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Model]

**Q.A.** Consider a 2-state Markov Switching Model for monthly log-returns $y_t$ of an equity index:

$$y_t = \mu_{S_t} + \phi_1 y_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t, \quad \varepsilon_t \mid S_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_{S_t}^2)$$

where $S_t \in \{1, 2\}$ represents "bull" and "bear" regimes. The estimated parameters are:

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| $\mu_1$ (bull mean) | +0.008 |
| $\mu_2$ (bear mean) | -0.015 |
| $\sigma_1^2$ (bull variance) | 0.0004 |
| $\sigma_2^2$ (bear variance) | 0.0025 |
| $\phi_1$ | 0.20 |
| $p_{11}$ | 0.92 |
| $p_{22}$ | 0.85 |

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Compute the **expected duration** of the bull and bear regimes. Comment on the practical interpretation of the persistence parameters.

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** At time $t-1$, the filtered probabilities are $\xi_{t-1 \mid t-1}(1) = 0.80$ and $\xi_{t-1 \mid t-1}(2) = 0.20$. A new observation $y_t = +0.012$ arrives. Compute the **predicted** and **filtered** regime probabilities. (Use the formula $f_j = \exp(-(y_t - \mu_j - \phi_1 y_{t-1})^2 / (2\sigma_j^2))/\sqrt{2\pi\sigma_j^2}$ and assume $y_{t-1} = 0.005$.)

---

**MODEL SOLUTION FOR QUESTION A**

**Part (a) Solution:**

The expected duration of regime $i$ is given by $E[D_i] = 1/(1 - p_{ii})$.

For the bull regime (state 1):
$$E[D_1] = \frac{1}{1 - 0.92} = \frac{1}{0.08} = 12.5 \text{ months} \approx 1.04 \text{ years} \quad \text{(M2)}$$

For the bear regime (state 2):
$$E[D_2] = \frac{1}{1 - 0.85} = \frac{1}{0.15} \approx 6.67 \text{ months} \quad \text{(M2)}$$

**Practical interpretation (M3):**
- The bull regime is **highly persistent** (≈ 1 year on average), meaning once a bull market begins, it tends to continue for about a year. This reflects the empirical observation that bull markets are longer and steadier than bear markets.
- The bear regime is shorter (≈ 6.7 months) but more intense, with a larger negative mean ($-1.5\%$) and higher volatility ($\sigma_2^2 = 0.0025$ vs. $\sigma_1^2 = 0.0004$).
- High $p_{11}$ and $p_{22}$ (close to 1) imply that regime changes are *infrequent*, which is a stylized fact of financial markets.

**Part (b) Solution:**

**Step 1: Prediction Step.** Compute the predicted probabilities:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\xi_{t \mid t-1}(1) &= \xi_{t-1 \mid t-1}(1) p_{11} + \xi_{t-1 \mid t-1}(2) p_{21} \\
&= (0.80)(0.92) + (0.20)(1 - 0.85) \\
&= 0.736 + 0.030 = 0.766 \quad \text{(M1)} \\
\xi_{t \mid t-1}(2) &= 1 - 0.766 = 0.234 \quad \text{(M0.5)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Conditional Densities.** First compute $\hat{y}_t = \phi_1 y_{t-1} = (0.20)(0.005) = 0.001$. Then for each regime:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Residual}_1 &= y_t - \mu_1 - \hat{y}_t = 0.012 - 0.008 - 0.001 = 0.003 \\
\text{Residual}_2 &= y_t - \mu_2 - \hat{y}_t = 0.012 - (-0.015) - 0.001 = 0.026
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_1 &= \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi (0.0004)}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(0.003)^2}{2(0.0004)}\right) \\
&= \frac{1}{\sqrt{0.002513}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{9 \times 10^{-6}}{0.0008}\right) \\
&= 19.95 \times \exp(-0.01125) \\
&= 19.95 \times 0.9888 \approx 19.73 \quad \text{(M1.5)}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_2 &= \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi (0.0025)}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(0.026)^2}{2(0.0025)}\right) \\
&= \frac{1}{\sqrt{0.01571}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{6.76 \times 10^{-4}}{0.005}\right) \\
&= 7.98 \times \exp(-0.1352) \\
&= 7.98 \times 0.8735 \approx 6.97 \quad \text{(M1.5)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Bayes Update.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D &= f_1 \cdot \xi_{t \mid t-1}(1) + f_2 \cdot \xi_{t \mid t-1}(2) \\
&= (19.73)(0.766) + (6.97)(0.234) \\
&= 15.11 + 1.63 = 16.74 \quad \text{(M1)} \\
\xi_{t \mid t}(1) &= \frac{19.73 \times 0.766}{16.74} = \frac{15.11}{16.74} \approx \mathbf{0.903} \quad \text{(M1)} \\
\xi_{t \mid t}(2) &= \frac{6.97 \times 0.234}{16.74} = \frac{1.63}{16.74} \approx \mathbf{0.097} \quad \text{(M0.5)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation (M0.5):** The strong positive return (1.2%) sharply increases our belief in the bull regime from 76.6% (predicted) to **90.3% (filtered)**. The Hamilton filter correctly identifies the new observation as bull-consistent.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted]

**Q.B.** (a) [7 Marks] Derive the **Hamilton filter** recursion for a general $M$-state Markov Switching Model. Clearly state the prediction and updating steps with mathematical justification using the **Markov property** and **Bayes' rule**.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Explain, with formulas, the concept of **ergodic (unconditional) probabilities** $\pi_j^*$ of the hidden Markov chain. For a 2-state MSM with transition matrix:

$$\mathbf{P} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.9 & 0.1 \\ 0.2 & 0.8 \end{bmatrix}$$

compute the ergodic probabilities and the long-run fraction of time spent in state 1.

---

**MODEL SOLUTION FOR QUESTION B**

**Part (a) Solution:**

Define:
- $Y_t = (y_1, \ldots, y_t)$ — observed data up to time $t$
- $\xi_{t \mid t}(j) = P(S_t = j \mid Y_t)$ — filtered probability
- $\xi_{t \mid t-1}(j) = P(S_t = j \mid Y_{t-1})$ — predicted probability
- $f(y_t \mid S_t = j, Y_{t-1})$ — state-conditional density of the observation

**Step 1: Prediction (using the Markov property and law of total probability).** The Markov property states that $P(S_t = j \mid S_{t-1} = i, Y_{t-1}) = P(S_t = j \mid S_{t-1} = i) = p_{ij}$. Conditioning on $S_{t-1} = i$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\xi_{t \mid t-1}(j) &= P(S_t = j \mid Y_{t-1}) \\
&= \sum_{i=1}^M P(S_t = j, S_{t-1} = i \mid Y_{t-1}) \\
&= \sum_{i=1}^M P(S_t = j \mid S_{t-1} = i) P(S_{t-1} = i \mid Y_{t-1}) \\
&= \sum_{i=1}^M p_{ij}\, \xi_{t-1 \mid t-1}(i) \quad \text{(M3)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Updating (Bayes' rule).** Upon observing $y_t$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\xi_{t \mid t}(j) &= P(S_t = j \mid Y_{t-1}, y_t) \\
&= \frac{P(y_t \mid S_t = j, Y_{t-1}) P(S_t = j \mid Y_{t-1})}{P(y_t \mid Y_{t-1})} \\
&= \frac{f(y_t \mid S_t = j, Y_{t-1})\, \xi_{t \mid t-1}(j)}{\sum_{k=1}^M f(y_t \mid S_t = k, Y_{t-1})\, \xi_{t \mid t-1}(k)} \quad \text{(M4)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Initialization (M1 extra):** Set $\xi_{1 \mid 0}(j) = \pi_j^0$, often taken as the ergodic distribution $\pi_j^*$.

The recursion is now complete. The full log-likelihood can be accumulated at each step $t$ as $\ell_t = \log \sum_{k=1}^M f(y_t \mid S_t = k, Y_{t-1})\, \xi_{t \mid t-1}(k)$, and the total log-likelihood is $\ell(\theta) = \sum_{t=1}^T \ell_t$.

**Part (b) Solution:**

**Ergodic Probabilities — Conceptual Explanation (M1):** The ergodic (stationary, long-run) probability $\pi_j^*$ of the hidden Markov chain represents the **unconditional fraction of time** the chain spends in state $j$ as $T \to \infty$, regardless of the starting state. It is the invariant (stationary) distribution of the Markov chain.

**Computation (M1):** The ergodic probabilities satisfy the **stationarity equation**:

$$\pi_j^* = \sum_{i=1}^M \pi_i^* p_{ij}, \quad j = 1, \ldots, M, \quad \text{with } \sum_{j=1}^M \pi_j^* = 1$$

In matrix form: $\boldsymbol{\pi^*} \mathbf{P} = \boldsymbol{\pi^*}$.

**Numerical Solution for the Given Matrix (M4):**

The system of equations is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\pi_1^* &= 0.9 \pi_1^* + 0.2 \pi_2^* \\
\pi_2^* &= 0.1 \pi_1^* + 0.8 \pi_2^* \\
\pi_1^* + \pi_2^* &= 1
\end{aligned}
$$

From the first equation:
$$0.1 \pi_1^* = 0.2 \pi_2^* \implies \pi_1^* = 2 \pi_2^*$$

Combined with $\pi_1^* + \pi_2^* = 1$:
$$2 \pi_2^* + \pi_2^* = 1 \implies 3 \pi_2^* = 1 \implies \pi_2^* = 1/3$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\pi_1^* = 2/3 \approx 66.67\%, \quad \pi_2^* = 1/3 \approx 33.33\%}
$$

**Interpretation (M1):** In the long run, the system spends approximately **66.67%** of the time in state 1 and **33.33%** of the time in state 2. This is consistent with the fact that $p_{11} > p_{22}$, i.e., state 1 is more persistent.

**Alternative Derivation via Expected Durations (M1):** The long-run fraction of time in state 1 is:

$$\pi_1^* = \frac{E[D_1]}{E[D_1] + E[D_2]} = \frac{1/(1-p_{11})}{1/(1-p_{11}) + 1/(1-p_{22})} = \frac{1/0.1}{1/0.1 + 1/0.2} = \frac{10}{10 + 5} = \frac{10}{15} = 2/3 \;\checkmark$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Confusing $p_{ij}$ notation:** Some students write $p_{ij}$ as $P(S_t = j \mid S_{t-1} = i)$ but then mistakenly compute $\xi_{t \mid t-1}(1) = \xi_{t-1 \mid t-1}(1) p_{11} + \xi_{t-1 \mid t-1}(2) p_{12}$. The **correct** formula uses $p_{i1}$ (column 1) summed over previous states. Always verify that rows of $P$ sum to 1, not columns.
> 2. **Forgetting to normalize:** The filtered probability $\xi_{t \mid t}(j)$ **must** be normalized (sum to 1). Students often skip the denominator and lose 2 marks.
> 3. **Mixing up expected duration formula:** The expected duration of regime $i$ is $1/(1 - p_{ii})$, **not** $1/p_{ii}$ or $1/(1 - p_{ij})$ with $i \neq j$.
> 4. **Ergodic probability sign error:** In solving $\boldsymbol{\pi}^* \mathbf{P} = \boldsymbol{\pi}^*$, students sometimes transpose and solve the wrong system. The ergodic distribution is the **left eigenvector** of $\mathbf{P}$ with eigenvalue 1.
> 5. **Skipping the Markov property justification:** When deriving the Hamilton filter, *always* explicitly state "by the Markov property, $P(S_t = j \mid S_{t-1}, Y_{t-1}) = p_{ij}$." This is worth 1 mark by itself.
> 6. **Numerical normalization in conditional density:** When using the Gaussian density, students often write $2\pi\sigma_j$ instead of $2\pi\sigma_j^2$ in the denominator's square root, or forget the square on $\sigma_j$ in the exponent. Read the formula **carefully**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Print this checklist on the morning of your exam for a 5-minute rapid revision.**

### **Core Concepts**
- [x] A Markov Switching Model has an **unobserved state** $S_t$ following a **first-order Markov chain**.
- [x] The **canonical Hamilton (1989) model** allows $\mu_{S_t}$ (mean) and $\sigma_{S_t}^2$ (variance) to switch across regimes; AR coefficients are typically fixed.
- [x] The transition matrix $\mathbf{P}$ has entries $p_{ij} = P(S_t = j \mid S_{t-1} = i)$, with each row summing to 1.

### **The Hamilton Filter — Must Memorize**
- [x] **Prediction:** $\xi_{t \mid t-1}(j) = \sum_{i=1}^M \xi_{t-1 \mid t-1}(i)\, p_{ij}$
- [x] **Update (Bayes):** $\xi_{t \mid t}(j) = \dfrac{f(y_t \mid S_t = j, Y_{t-1})\,\xi_{t \mid t-1}(j)}{\sum_k f(y_t \mid S_t = k, Y_{t-1})\,\xi_{t \mid t-1}(k)}$

### **Key Derived Quantities**
- [x] **Expected duration of regime $i$:** $E[D_i] = 1 / (1 - p_{ii})$ (geometric sojourn time).
- [x] **Ergodic probabilities:** Solve $\boldsymbol{\pi}^* \mathbf{P} = \boldsymbol{\pi}^*$, $\sum_j \pi_j^* = 1$.
- [x] **Long-run fraction in state 1:** $\pi_1^* = E[D_1] / (E[D_1] + E[D_2])$.

### **Estimation**
- [x] MSM parameters are estimated by **MLE**, with the **Hamilton filter** providing the likelihood.
- [x] Log-likelihood: $\ell(\theta) = \sum_{t=1}^T \log \sum_{j=1}^M f(y_t \mid S_t = j, Y_{t-1})\, \xi_{t \mid t-1}(j)$.

### **Interpretive Insights**
- [x] High $p_{ii}$ $\Leftrightarrow$ **regime persistence** (the regime lasts long).
- [x] Higher $\sigma_j^2$ $\Leftrightarrow$ **turbulent regime** (recession, crisis).
- [x] $\mu_1 > \mu_2$ $\Leftrightarrow$ **bull vs. bear market** distinction.
- [x] MSM is **non-linear** because the conditional mean depends on the *entire* history of latent states, not just lagged $y_t$.

### **Pitfalls to Avoid (Examiners Love These Traps)**
- [x] Do **not** confuse row and column of $\mathbf{P}$: rows of $P$ sum to 1.
- [x] Do **not** forget the **normalization** in the Bayes update.
- [x] Do **not** transpose the matrix when computing ergodic probabilities.
- [x] Do **not** confuse expected duration $1/(1-p_{ii})$ with $1/p_{ii}$.
- [x] Always state the **Markov property** explicitly when deriving the Hamilton filter.

### **One-Line Takeaway**
> A Markov Switching Model is a **regime-aware** time series model where an unobservable Markov chain drives regime changes in the mean and variance, estimated via the **Hamilton filter** and MLE.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module 3

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3 — Smooth Transition Autoregressive (STAR) Models

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Smooth Transition Autoregressive (STAR) Model** is a class of **nonlinear time series models** in which the dynamics of a series switch smoothly between two (or more) regimes according to the value of a *transition variable*, controlled by a continuous, differentiable *transition function*.

The general two-regime STAR model proposed by **Chan and Tong (1986)** and formalized by **Tong (1990)** is expressed as:

$$
y_t = \phi_{0}^{\prime} \mathbf{x}_t + \phi_{1}^{\prime} \mathbf{x}_t \, G(z_{t-d};\,\gamma,\,c) + \varepsilon_t
$$

where:
- $y_t$ is the dependent (response) series,
- $\mathbf{x}_t = (1,\, y_{t-1},\, y_{t-2},\, \ldots,\, y_{t-p})^{\prime}$ is the regressor vector,
- $G(\cdot)$ is the **transition function** taking values in $[0,\,1]$,
- $z_{t-d}$ is the **transition variable** (often a lagged value of $y_t$ or another exogenous variable),
- $d$ is the **delay parameter** (a non-negative integer),
- $\gamma \geq 0$ is the **smoothness (or slope) parameter**,
- $c$ is the **threshold (location) parameter**,
- $\varepsilon_t$ is white noise with $E(\varepsilon_t)=0$ and $\mathrm{Var}(\varepsilon_t)=\sigma^2$,
- $\phi_0$ and $\phi_1$ are parameter vectors.

> [!NOTE]
> When $\gamma \to \infty$, $G(\cdot) \to \mathbb{1}_{\{z_{t-d} \geq c\}}$, and the STAR model collapses into the **Self-Exciting Threshold Autoregressive (SETAR)** model of Tong (1978). When $\gamma = 0$, the model reduces to a **linear AR(p)** specification.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Intuition for First-Time Learners

Imagine a car cruising on a highway. As the road climbs a hill, the driver **smoothly** presses the accelerator deeper — there is no sudden "on/off" jump in fuel delivery; the response is **gradual and continuous**.

In financial markets, an asset return series behaves similarly:

- In **calm regimes** (low volatility), returns follow one dynamic (e.g., a mean-reverting gentle drift).
- In **turbulent regimes** (crashes, shocks), returns follow a different dynamic (large swings, asymmetric responses).
- The **shift** between these two regimes is **not instantaneous** — it builds up as some *indicator* (transition variable) — such as the previous day's return magnitude or a volatility proxy — crosses a critical threshold.

The STAR model captures precisely this **gradual regime-switching behavior**, making it ideal for modeling **nonlinearities**, **asymmetries**, and **regime-dependent dynamics** common in financial time series such as equity returns, exchange rates, interest rates, and commodity prices.

### 1.3 Key Physical / Statistical Constants

- **Smoothness parameter $\gamma$**: controls the steepness of the transition. Typical empirical range: $\gamma \in [0.1,\, 100]$.
- **Threshold $c$**: usually estimated within the empirical range of the transition variable.
- **Delay parameter $d$**: integer in $\{0, 1, \ldots, p\}$ selected by information criterion.
- **Order $p$**: AR lag order.

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Breakdown — Why and How

The STAR model can be viewed as a **regime-weighted mixture** of two AR(p) processes:

$$
y_t = \underbrace{\phi_{0}^{\prime} \mathbf{x}_t}_{\text{Lower regime AR}} + \underbrace{\phi_{1}^{\prime} \mathbf{x}_t \, G(\cdot)}_{\text{Transition-weighted AR}} + \varepsilon_t
$$

**Step 1 — Lower regime baseline.** When $G(z_{t-d};\gamma,c) \approx 0$, the model collapses to the linear AR(p): $y_t \approx \phi_0^{\prime}\mathbf{x}_t + \varepsilon_t$. This corresponds to the case when the transition variable lies far below the threshold $c$.

**Step 2 — Upper regime activation.** When $G(z_{t-d};\gamma,c) \approx 1$, the model behaves as $y_t \approx (\phi_0+\phi_1)^{\prime}\mathbf{x}_t + \varepsilon_t$, giving the second AR(p) regime.

**Step 3 — Smooth blending.** For intermediate values of the transition variable, the two AR processes are **continuously mixed** with weights $1-G$ and $G$ respectively. This guarantees:

$$
G: \mathbb{R} \to [0,\,1], \quad G \text{ continuous and monotonically non-decreasing}
$$

**Step 4 — Why this matters in finance.** Asset returns exhibit asymmetric reactions — good news and bad news do not impact volatility identically (the *leverage effect*). STAR models capture this asymmetry naturally without resorting to GARCH-type exponential smoothing.

### 2.2 The Two Canonical Transition Functions

#### (a) Logistic STAR (LSTAR)

$$
G(z_{t-d};\,\gamma,\,c) = \left[1 + \exp\!\left(-\gamma(z_{t-d}-c)\right)\right]^{-1}, \quad \gamma > 0
$$

- $G$ is bounded between **0** and **1**.
- $G = 0.5$ when $z_{t-d} = c$.
- As $\gamma \to \infty$: sharp threshold (SETAR limit).
- Best suited when **asymmetric** (signed) transitions occur — e.g., large positive vs. negative returns.

#### (b) Exponential STAR (ESTAR)

$$
G(z_{t-d};\,\gamma,\,c) = 1 - \exp\!\left(-\gamma(z_{t-d}-c)^2\right), \quad \gamma > 0
$$

- $G$ is bounded between **0** and **1**.
- $G = 0$ when $z_{t-d} = c$.
- **Symmetric** about $c$ — best for modeling **magnitude-based** nonlinearity (volatility clustering).
- As $\gamma \to \infty$: again collapses to a symmetric SETAR.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Element | Symbol | Definition / Expression | Range / Domain |
|---|---|---|---|
| Observation | $y_t$ | Dependent time-series variable | $\mathbb{R}$ |
| Regressor vector | $\mathbf{x}_t$ | $(1, y_{t-1}, \ldots, y_{t-p})^{\prime}$ | $\mathbb{R}^{p+1}$ |
| Transition variable | $z_{t-d}$ | Lagged $y$ or exogenous series | $\mathbb{R}$ |
| Delay parameter | $d$ | Non-negative integer | $d \in \{0,1,\ldots,p\}$ |
| Threshold | $c$ | Threshold (location) parameter | $\mathbb{R}$ |
| Smoothness | $\gamma$ | Slope / steepness | $\gamma > 0$ |
| LSTAR transition | $G_L$ | $(1+e^{-\gamma(z-c)})^{-1}$ | $[0,1]$ |
| ESTAR transition | $G_E$ | $1-e^{-\gamma(z-c)^2}$ | $[0,1]$ |
| White-noise error | $\varepsilon_t$ | $W.N.(0,\sigma^2)$ | $\mathbb{R}$ |
| Linearity test stat. | $F$ | Taylor-expansion auxiliary regression | $\mathbb{R}^+$ |
| Conditional mean LSTAR | $\mu_t$ | $\phi_0^{\prime}\mathbf{x}_t+\phi_1^{\prime}\mathbf{x}_t\,G_L$ | $\mathbb{R}$ |
| Conditional mean ESTAR | $\mu_t$ | $\phi_0^{\prime}\mathbf{x}_t+\phi_1^{\prime}\mathbf{x}_t\,G_E$ | $\mathbb{R}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> The **default transition variable** in univariate STAR modelling is $z_t = y_t$ (or $y_{t-d}$), which yields a *self-exciting* STAR. When $z_t$ is exogenous (e.g., a macroeconomic indicator), the model becomes a *functional-Coefficient AR (FAR)* model.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering & Financial Utility

- **Risk Management**: Value-at-Risk (VaR) estimation under asymmetric, regime-switching volatility.
- **Algorithmic Trading**: Detection of *breakpoints* in market microstructure.
- **Macroeconomic Forecasting**: Smooth business-cycle transitions (recession $\leftrightarrow$ expansion).
- **Energy Markets**: STAR models capture *mean-reversion asymmetry* in crude oil and electricity prices.
- **Central Banking**: Modeling the **nonlinear Taylor rule** where policy rate reactions differ across inflation regimes.

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Reformulation of the STAR Model

The two-regime STAR model can be rewritten by **expanding the inner product**:

$$
y_t = (\phi_0 + \phi_1 G_t)^{\prime}\mathbf{x}_t + \varepsilon_t
$$

where $G_t \equiv G(z_{t-d};\gamma,c)$.

**Equivalent AR-coefficient form:**

$$
y_t = \sum_{j=1}^{p}\bigl[\phi_{0,j} + \phi_{1,j}\,G_t\bigr]\, y_{t-j} + \bigl[\phi_{0,0}+\phi_{1,0}\,G_t\bigr] + \varepsilon_t
$$

Each AR coefficient becomes a **smooth function** of the transition variable:

$$
\alpha_j(z_{t-d}) = \phi_{0,j} + \phi_{1,j}\,G(z_{t-d};\gamma,c), \quad j=0,1,\ldots,p
$$

This shows the model belongs to the broader **Functional-Coefficient Autoregressive (FAR)** family.

### 3.2 Limiting Behavior of the Transition Function

#### (a) LSTAR — Lower Regime Limit ($z_{t-d} \to -\infty$)

$$
\begin{aligned}
G_L(z_{t-d}) &= \bigl[1+\exp(-\gamma(z_{t-d}-c))\bigr]^{-1} \\[4pt]
\lim_{z_{t-d} \to -\infty} G_L(z_{t-d}) &= \lim_{z_{t-d} \to -\infty}\bigl[1+e^{+\infty}\bigr]^{-1} = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

#### (b) LSTAR — Upper Regime Limit ($z_{t-d} \to +\infty$)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lim_{z_{t-d} \to +\infty} G_L(z_{t-d}) &= \lim_{z_{t-d} \to +\infty}\bigl[1+e^{-\infty}\bigr]^{-1} = \frac{1}{1+0} = 1
\end{aligned}
$$

#### (c) ESTAR — Symmetry Check

$$
\begin{aligned}
G_E(z_{t-d}) &= 1-\exp\!\bigl(-\gamma(z_{t-d}-c)^2\bigr) \\
G_E(c+\delta) &= 1-e^{-\gamma\delta^2} = G_E(c-\delta)
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence ESTAR is **symmetric about $c$** — meaning the model treats *equal-magnitude departures* from the threshold identically regardless of sign.

### 3.3 Linearity Testing — Luukkonen–Saikkonen–Terasvirta (LST) Approach

Under the null hypothesis $H_0: \gamma = 0$, the model is linear. The trick is to **Taylor-expand $G$ around $\gamma = 0$** and replace the STAR with an **auxiliary regression**.

**Step 1 — Logistic Taylor expansion around $\gamma=0$:**

Using $G(z_{t-d};\gamma,c) = \bigl[1+e^{-\gamma(z_{t-d}-c)}\bigr]^{-1}$ and the first-order Taylor approximation $e^{-\gamma(z-c)} \approx 1-\gamma(z-c)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
G &\approx \bigl[1+1-\gamma(z_{t-d}-c)\bigr]^{-1} = \bigl[2-\gamma(z_{t-d}-c)\bigr]^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator through, and using a **third-order Taylor expansion** (Terasvirta 1994), one obtains the auxiliary regression:

$$
y_t = \beta_0 + \sum_{j=1}^{p}\beta_j y_{t-j} + \sum_{j=1}^{p}\beta_{p+j}\,y_{t-j}\,z_{t-d} + \sum_{j=1}^{p}\beta_{2p+j}\,y_{t-j}\,z_{t-d}^2 + \sum_{j=1}^{p}\beta_{3p+j}\,y_{t-j}\,z_{t-d}^3 + u_t
$$

**Step 2 — F-test for linearity.**

Test

$$
H_0: \beta_{p+1} = \beta_{p+2} = \ldots = \beta_{4p} = 0 \quad \text{vs.} \quad H_1: \text{at least one} \neq 0
$$

using the standard F-statistic:

$$
F = \frac{(\mathrm{RSS}_0 - \mathrm{RSS}_1)/q}{\mathrm{RSS}_1/(n-k)}
$$

where:
- $\mathrm{RSS}_0$ = residual sum of squares under $H_0$ (linear AR),
- $\mathrm{RSS}_1$ = residual sum of squares under $H_1$ (auxiliary regression),
- $q$ = number of restrictions,
- $k$ = number of parameters under $H_1$,
- $n$ = sample size.

Under $H_0$, $F \sim F(q,\,n-k)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This **LST test** is one of the **highest-frequency KTU Module 3 questions** — expect either an MCQ-style conceptual query or a full derivation.

### 3.4 Parameter Estimation — Nonlinear Least Squares (NLS)

Because the transition function is nonlinear in $(\gamma, c)$, the STAR parameters are estimated via **NLS**:

$$
\hat{\theta} = \arg\min_{\theta}\sum_{t=1}^{T}\bigl[y_t - \phi_0^{\prime}\mathbf{x}_t - \phi_1^{\prime}\mathbf{x}_t\,G(z_{t-d};\gamma,c)\bigr]^2
$$

where $\theta = (\phi_0^{\prime}, \phi_1^{\prime}, \gamma, c, \sigma^2)^{\prime}$.

**Procedure:**
1. Specify candidate $p$ and $d$ via **AIC/BIC**.
2. Choose initial $c^{(0)}$ as the empirical median/quantile of $z_{t-d}$.
3. Use a **grid search** over $\gamma \in [\gamma_{\min}, \gamma_{\max}]$.
4. At each $(\gamma^{(0)}, c^{(0)})$, condition on $G_t$ and estimate $\phi_0, \phi_1$ by **OLS**.
5. Refine via **Gauss–Newton** or **Levenberg–Marquardt** algorithms.

### 3.5 Residual Diagnostics

Once $\hat{\theta}$ is obtained, check:

- **Autocorrelation:** Ljung–Box $Q(m)$ test on residuals $\hat{\varepsilon}_t$.
- **Heteroskedasticity:** Engle's ARCH-LM test.
- **Functional form:** Ramsey RESET test.

If residuals still exhibit dependence, extend to **ST-GARCH** or **ST-VAR** architectures.

---

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of STAR Modelling

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Input Time Series Yt]
    nodeB[Step 1: Stationarity Test ADF or KPSS]
    nodeC[Step 2: Select AR Order p by AIC or BIC]
    nodeD[Step 3: Choose Delay d and Transition Variable z]
    nodeE[Step 4: Linearity Test LST Auxiliary Regression F Test]
    nodeF[Step 5: Decide Transition Type LSTAR or ESTAR]
    nodeG[Step 6: NLS Estimation of Phi0 Phi1 Gamma C Sigma2]
    nodeH[Step 7: Residual Diagnostics Ljung Box and ARCH LM]
    nodeI[Step 8: Forecasting and Interpretation]
    nodeJ[Reject H0 Nonlinearity Confirmed]
    nodeK[Fail to Reject Remain Linear AR]
    nodeL[Final STAR Model Yt Conditional Mean Function]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeJ
    nodeE --> nodeK
    nodeJ --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH
    nodeH --> nodeI
    nodeG --> nodeL
    nodeI --> nodeL
```

### 4.2 Regime-Weighted Mixture Interpretation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Lower Regime AR when G equals 0]
    nodeB[Transition Blending Region G between 0 and 1]
    nodeC[Upper Regime AR when G equals 1]
    nodeD[Transition Variable z sub t minus d]
    nodeE[Smooth Transition Function G]
    nodeF[Final STAR Output Yt]

    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeA
    nodeE --> nodeB
    nodeE --> nodeC
    nodeA --> nodeF
    nodeB --> nodeF
    nodeC --> nodeF
```

### 4.3 LSTAR vs ESTAR Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Identify Nature of Nonlinearity]
    nodeB[Asymmetric Sign Dependent]
    nodeC[Symmetric Magnitude Dependent]
    nodeD[Use LSTAR Logistic Transition]
    nodeE[Use ESTAR Exponential Transition]
    nodeF[Final Model Selection]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeA --> nodeC
    nodeB --> nodeD
    nodeC --> nodeE
    nodeD --> nodeF
    nodeE --> nodeF
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Decision Rule (KTU Quick Reference):**
> - *Good-news vs bad-news asymmetry* → **LSTAR**.
> - *Volatility / clustering effects* → **ESTAR**.

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Define the Smooth Transition Autoregressive (STAR) model. Highlight the role of the transition function.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The STAR model is a **nonlinear extension of the linear AR(p)** process in which the autoregressive coefficients evolve smoothly between two regimes according to a transition variable. It is written as:

$$
y_t = \phi_0^{\prime}\mathbf{x}_t + \phi_1^{\prime}\mathbf{x}_t\,G(z_{t-d};\gamma,c) + \varepsilon_t
$$

The transition function $G: \mathbb{R} \to [0,1]$ acts as a **continuous weighting mechanism** that smoothly blends the two AR regimes. When $G \to 0$, the series follows the lower regime; when $G \to 1$, it follows the upper regime; intermediate values produce smooth interpolation — hence the term *smooth* transition. **(3 Marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Distinguish between LSTAR and ESTAR models with suitable transition functions.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Feature | LSTAR | ESTAR |
|---|---|---|
| Transition function | $G = (1+e^{-\gamma(z-c)})^{-1}$ | $G = 1-e^{-\gamma(z-c)^2}$ |
| Symmetry | Asymmetric | Symmetric about $c$ |
| Best for | Sign-dependent nonlinearities | Magnitude-dependent nonlinearities |
| Limit at $z=c$ | $G = 0.5$ | $G = 0$ |

The LSTAR captures **directional** (bullish/bearish) asymmetries while ESTAR captures **size-based** (volatility) effects. **(3 Marks)**

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT** confuse $d$ (delay) with $p$ (AR order) — they are distinct integers.
> 2. **Do NOT** treat $\gamma$ as freely signed — it must be **non-negative**.
> 3. Students frequently **omit the Taylor-expansion justification** for the linearity test; the KTU examiner specifically deducts marks if the auxiliary regression is not derived.
> 4. Always report the **transition variable used** — generic answers lose 2 marks.
> 5. In numerical questions, **show all NLS steps**; an unexplained answer script will be penalized.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]*

**(a)** Derive the general form of a two-regime Smooth Transition Autoregressive (STAR) model and explain the role of the parameters $\gamma$, $c$, and $d$. Discuss the limiting behavior of the transition function as $\gamma \to 0$ and $\gamma \to \infty$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Consider the time series $y_t$ generated by the model:

$$
y_t = 0.3 + 0.5\,y_{t-1} + \bigl[0.6 - 0.4\,y_{t-1}\bigr]\,G(y_{t-1};\,\gamma,\,0) + \varepsilon_t
$$

where $G(z;\gamma,0) = (1+e^{-\gamma z})^{-1}$ and $\varepsilon_t \sim N(0,\,0.25)$. Find the conditional mean $E(y_t \mid y_{t-1})$ when $y_{t-1} = 1$ and $\gamma = 5$. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Solution A(a) — Model Derivation (7 Marks)**

The two-regime STAR model combines two AR(p) specifications through a smooth transition function:

$$
y_t = \phi_0^{\prime}\mathbf{x}_t + \phi_1^{\prime}\mathbf{x}_t\,G(z_{t-d};\gamma,c) + \varepsilon_t, \qquad \varepsilon_t \sim W.N.(0,\sigma^2)
$$

with $\mathbf{x}_t = (1, y_{t-1}, \ldots, y_{t-p})^{\prime}$.

**Parameter roles:**
- $\gamma > 0$: **smoothness/slope** parameter controlling the steepness of the transition. Higher $\gamma$ → faster (sharper) regime-switch.
- $c \in \mathbb{R}$: **threshold** parameter — the value of the transition variable at which $G = 0.5$ (LSTAR) or $G = 0$ (ESTAR).
- $d \in \{0,1,\ldots,p\}$: **delay** parameter specifying which lag of $z$ drives the transition.

**Limiting cases:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lim_{\gamma \to 0} G(z_{t-d};\gamma,c) &= 0.5 \quad \text{(constant)} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; y_t = (\phi_0 + 0.5\phi_1)^{\prime}\mathbf{x}_t + \varepsilon_t \;\;\text{(linear AR)} \\[4pt]
\lim_{\gamma \to \infty} G(z_{t-d};\gamma,c) &= \mathbb{1}_{\{z_{t-d} \geq c\}} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; y_t = \begin{cases}\phi_0^{\prime}\mathbf{x}_t + \varepsilon_t, & z_{t-d} < c \\ (\phi_0+\phi_1)^{\prime}\mathbf{x}_t + \varepsilon_t, & z_{t-d} \geq c\end{cases} \;\;\text{(SETAR)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(7 Marks — full derivation and limit analysis)**

---

**Solution A(b) — Numerical Computation (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — Identify parameters:**
- $\phi_0 = (0.3,\, 0.5)^{\prime}$, $\phi_1 = (0.6,\, -0.4)^{\prime}$
- $\mathbf{x}_t = (1,\, y_{t-1})^{\prime} = (1,\,1)^{\prime}$
- $z_{t-1} = y_{t-1} = 1$, $c = 0$, $\gamma = 5$

**Step 2 — Compute the LSTAR transition function:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
G(y_{t-1};\,\gamma,\,0) &= \bigl[1 + e^{-\gamma(y_{t-1}-0)}\bigr]^{-1} \\[4pt]
&= \bigl[1 + e^{-5 \cdot 1}\bigr]^{-1} = \bigl[1 + e^{-5}\bigr]^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

Using $e^{-5} \approx 0.006738$:

$$
G \approx \frac{1}{1.006738} \approx 0.99329
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the lower-regime contribution:**

$$
\phi_0^{\prime}\mathbf{x}_t = 0.3 \cdot 1 + 0.5 \cdot 1 = 0.8
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the transition-weighted contribution:**

$$
\phi_1^{\prime}\mathbf{x}_t = 0.6 \cdot 1 + (-0.4)\cdot 1 = 0.2
$$

$$
\phi_1^{\prime}\mathbf{x}_t \cdot G = 0.2 \times 0.99329 \approx 0.19866
$$

**Step 5 — Conditional mean:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E(y_t \mid y_{t-1}=1) &= 0.8 + 0.19866 = 0.99866 \approx 0.999
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation:**

Since $y_{t-1}=1$ is *above* the threshold $c=0$ with high $\gamma = 5$, the series is **deep inside the upper regime**. Hence the conditional mean approaches the upper-regime value:

$$
E(y_t) \to (\phi_0+\phi_1)^{\prime}\mathbf{x}_t = (0.3+0.6) + (0.5-0.4) = 0.9 + 0.1 = 1.0
$$

The slight deviation ($0.999$) from $1.0$ reflects that $G$ has not quite reached $1$ (because $e^{-5}$ is small but non-zero). **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]*

**(a)** Explain the Luukkonen–Saikkonen–Terasvirta (LST) linearity test for STAR models. State the auxiliary regression and the test statistic used. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For the same time series in Question A(b), suppose an investigator fits an auxiliary regression under $H_0$ (linear AR) and obtains $\mathrm{RSS}_0 = 240$, while under the LST-augmented regression she obtains $\mathrm{RSS}_1 = 180$ with $q = 4$ restrictions, $k = 6$ parameters under $H_1$, and $n = 200$ observations. Test the linearity null at the 5% significance level. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Solution B(a) — LST Linearity Test (7 Marks)**

The LST test is conducted by **Taylor-expanding** the transition function $G(z_{t-d};\gamma,c)$ around $\gamma = 0$ and replacing the nonlinear STAR with an **auxiliary linear regression** involving powers of the transition variable.

For an LSTAR transition function, the **third-order Taylor expansion** around $\gamma = 0$ yields the auxiliary regression:

$$
y_t = \beta_0 + \sum_{j=1}^{p}\beta_j y_{t-j} + \sum_{j=1}^{p}\beta_{p+j}\,y_{t-j}\,z_{t-d} + \sum_{j=1}^{p}\beta_{2p+j}\,y_{t-j}\,z_{t-d}^2 + \sum_{j=1}^{p}\beta_{3p+j}\,y_{t-j}\,z_{t-d}^3 + u_t
$$

**Hypotheses:**

$$
H_0: \beta_{p+1}=\beta_{p+2}=\ldots=\beta_{4p}=0 \quad \text{(linearity)}
$$

$$
H_1: \text{at least one } \beta_{p+j},\,\beta_{2p+j},\,\beta_{3p+j} \neq 0
$$

**Test Statistic (F-form):**

$$
F = \frac{(\mathrm{RSS}_0 - \mathrm{RSS}_1)/q}{\mathrm{RSS}_1/(n-k)} \;\;\sim\;\; F(q,\,n-k) \text{ under } H_0
$$

where $q$ is the number of restrictions (here $q = 3p$ in the full third-order form), $k$ is the number of estimated parameters under $H_1$, and $n$ is the sample size.

**Decision Rule:** Reject $H_0$ if $F > F_{\alpha}(q,\,n-k)$.

For ESTAR, an analogous procedure uses an **even-power Taylor expansion** (typically up to $z_{t-d}^4$) because of the symmetric exponential form.

**(7 Marks — full marks allocated to the auxiliary regression derivation, hypotheses, and F-statistic expression)**

---

**Solution B(b) — Numerical F-Test (7 Marks)**

**Given:**
- $\mathrm{RSS}_0 = 240$ (linear AR residual SS),
- $\mathrm{RSS}_1 = 180$ (LST-augmented residual SS),
- $q = 4$ restrictions,
- $k = 6$ parameters under $H_1$,
- $n = 200$ observations.

**Step 1 — Compute numerator:**

$$
\frac{\mathrm{RSS}_0 - \mathrm{RSS}_1}{q} = \frac{240 - 180}{4} = \frac{60}{4} = 15
$$

**Step 2 — Compute denominator:**

$$
\frac{\mathrm{RSS}_1}{n - k} = \frac{180}{200 - 6} = \frac{180}{194} \approx 0.9278
$$

**Step 3 — F-statistic:**

$$
F = \frac{15}{0.9278} \approx 16.166
$$

**Step 4 — Critical value:**

At $\alpha = 0.05$, with $(q, n-k) = (4, 194)$ degrees of freedom, the critical value is:

$$
F_{0.05}(4,\,194) \approx 2.42
$$

**Step 5 — Decision:**

Since $F_{\text{calc}} \approx 16.17 \gg F_{\text{crit}} \approx 2.42$, we **reject $H_0$** at the 5% significance level.

**Conclusion:** The data exhibit **significant nonlinear behavior** consistent with a STAR specification. The linear AR model is rejected in favor of the smooth-transition alternative.

**Step 6 — p-value (optional, for full credit):**

The corresponding $p$-value is essentially zero ($p < 0.0001$), confirming overwhelming evidence against linearity.

**(7 Marks — full marks for explicit step-by-step computation, critical value, and conclusion)**

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 3 — STAR Models)**

- [x] **STAR model structure:** $y_t = \phi_0^{\prime}\mathbf{x}_t + \phi_1^{\prime}\mathbf{x}_t\,G(z_{t-d};\gamma,c) + \varepsilon_t$.
- [x] **Transition function $G$** must map $\mathbb{R} \to [0,1]$ continuously.
- [x] **LSTAR:** asymmetric, $G = (1+e^{-\gamma(z-c)})^{-1}$.
- [x] **ESTAR:** symmetric, $G = 1-e^{-\gamma(z-c)^2}$.
- [x] **$\gamma$ is the smoothness parameter** ($\gamma \geq 0$); larger $\gamma$ → sharper transition.
- [x] **$c$ is the threshold**; $G_L(c) = 0.5$, $G_E(c) = 0$.
- [x] **$d$ is the delay** parameter selecting which lag of $z$ drives the transition.
- [x] **Limiting cases:** $\gamma \to 0$ → linear AR; $\gamma \to \infty$ → SETAR.
- [x] **LST linearity test:** Taylor-expand $G$, fit auxiliary regression with powers of $z$, apply F-test.
- [x] **Estimation method:** Nonlinear Least Squares (NLS).
- [x] **Common transition variable:** $z_t = y_t$ (self-exciting); alternatively an exogenous regressor.
- [x] **Model selection:** AIC/BIC for $(p,d)$; reset/ARCH-LM for residual diagnostics.
- [x] **Default regime count:** two regimes (lower/upper); multiple-regime extensions exist (M-STAR).
- [x] **Forecasting:** use the estimated conditional mean $\hat{\mu}_t = \hat{\phi}_0^{\prime}\mathbf{x}_t + \hat{\phi}_1^{\prime}\mathbf{x}_t\,G(z_{t-d};\hat{\gamma},\hat{c})$.
- [x] **Default framework:** Univariate, conditional-mean modeling; variance extensions require ST-GARCH.

---

*End of Module 3 — Smooth Transition Autoregressive Models*

topics.title: Univariate Nonparametric Models - Kernel Smoothing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET742 - FINANCIAL TIME SERIES ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Univariate Nonparametric Models - Kernel Smoothing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Univariate Nonparametric Models – Kernel Smoothing** is a flexible, data-driven estimation technique used to recover the unknown regression function $m(x) = E(Y \mid X = x)$ in the nonparametric regression model $Y_i = m(x_i) + \varepsilon_i$ WITHOUT assuming any rigid parametric form (such as linear or polynomial) for the underlying relationship.

**Kernel smoothing** is the cornerstone of nonparametric regression. The estimate at any point $x$ is obtained as a **locally weighted average** of nearby observed responses $\{Y_i\}$, where the weights are dictated by a **kernel function** $K(\cdot)$ and a smoothing parameter called the **bandwidth** $h > 0$.

Formally, the **Nadaraya–Watson (NW) kernel regression estimator** at point $x$ is:

$$\hat{m}_h(x) = \frac{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} K\!\left(\frac{x - x_i}{h}\right) Y_i}{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} K\!\left(\frac{x - x_i}{h}\right)}$$

where:
- $K(\cdot)$ is the **kernel function** (a symmetric, non-negative, integrable function).
- $h > 0$ is the **bandwidth** (smoothing parameter) controlling the width of the local neighborhood.
- $x_i$ are the observed predictor values.
- $Y_i$ are the observed responses.
- $n$ is the sample size.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy: "The Spotlit Stage"**
> Imagine a theatrical stage where an actor (the unknown function $m(x)$) is performing in a dark room. The audience can only see through circular spotlights of variable size. Each spotlight reveals only the part of the stage near its centre, blurring the rest. The bandwidth $h$ is the **diameter of the spotlight**: a *small* $h$ = tiny, sharp spotlight (you see the actor's exact pose but the picture is noisy/jittery). A *large* $h$ = wide, soft spotlight (you see a smooth blur of the stage but lose local detail). The **kernel $K(\cdot)$** is the *shape* of the spotlight (round, triangular, Gaussian). Kernel smoothing shines a moving spotlight on the data and reports a locally weighted average.

In financial time series terms, kernel smoothing is used to extract the *smooth trend* of asset returns, volatility, or risk–return profiles **without imposing** a pre-specified ARMA/GARCH parametric structure.

## 1.3 Key Terminology (Syllabus Highlights)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Vocabulary for KTU Board Exams:**
> - **Kernel function $K(\cdot)$**: A symmetric probability density with $\int K(u) \, du = 1$.
> - **Bandwidth $h$**: Controls the bias–variance trade-off. Optimal $h = O(n^{-1/5})$ for second-order kernels.
> - **Order of the kernel $p$**: The order of the polynomial that the kernel reproduces exactly. A kernel of order $p$ has the first $p$ moments vanish (except the 0th moment). **Second-order kernels** ($p=2$) are the most common.
> - **Bias–Variance Trade-off**: As $h \downarrow 0$, bias $\to 0$ but variance $\to \infty$.
> - **Curse of Dimensionality**: Kernel smoothing degrades quickly when $d$ (number of predictors) grows, because data become sparse.
> - **Boundary Bias**: Near the edges of the data, fewer points fall inside the bandwidth window, causing asymmetric bias.

## 1.4 The Nonparametric Regression Setup

The standard univariate nonparametric regression model assumed in Module 3 is:

$$Y_i = m(x_i) + \varepsilon_i, \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, n$$

with assumptions:
1. $E(\varepsilon_i \mid x_i) = 0$ (mean-zero errors).
2. $\text{Var}(\varepsilon_i \mid x_i) = \sigma^2$ (homoscedastic variance).
3. $\varepsilon_i$ are i.i.d. (or at least uncorrelated).
4. The design points $x_i$ are either **fixed** (fixed design) or **random** (random design, i.i.d. from a density $f_X$).
5. $m(\cdot)$ is assumed to be **smooth** (twice continuously differentiable: $m \in C^2$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Pearl:** The KTU 2024 scheme stresses that nonparametric models are essential when **misspecification of the parametric form** would lead to erroneous inference — a common problem when modelling financial returns whose true DGP (data-generating process) is unknown.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic of the Nadaraya–Watson Estimator

The estimator is constructed in **5 systematic steps**:

1. **Step 1 – Choose a centre of interest** $x$ at which we want to estimate $m(x)$.
2. **Step 2 – Define a scaled kernel** $K_h(u) = \dfrac{1}{h} K\!\left(\dfrac{u}{h}\right)$. The factor $1/h$ ensures the scaled kernel still integrates to **1**.
3. **Step 3 – Compute the weight** $w_i(x) = K_h(x - x_i)$ assigned to each observation $(x_i, Y_i)$. Observations close to $x$ receive **large weights**; faraway observations receive **negligible weights**.
4. **Step 4 – Normalise the weights** to sum to one:
   $$\tilde{w}_i(x) = \frac{K_h(x - x_i)}{\displaystyle\sum_{j=1}^{n} K_h(x - x_j)}$$
5. **Step 5 – Form the weighted average**:
   $$\hat{m}_h(x) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \tilde{w}_i(x) \, Y_i$$
   This is exactly the NW estimator formula in Section 1.1.

> [!NOTE]
> **The 'Why' Behind Each Step:** The local weighted average mimics a Taylor expansion of $m(\cdot)$ around $x$ in a small neighbourhood. By giving more weight to nearby points, the estimator captures the local behaviour of $m(\cdot)$ adaptively. The bandwidth $h$ defines what "nearby" means.

## 2.2 Properties of a Valid Kernel

A function $K: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ qualifies as a **second-order kernel** if:

1. $K(u) \geq 0$ for all $u$ (non-negativity).
2. $\displaystyle\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} K(u) \, du = 1$ (normalisation).
3. $K(-u) = K(u)$ (symmetry).
4. $\displaystyle\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} u \, K(u) \, du = 0$ (zero first moment).
5. $0 < \displaystyle\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} u^2 K(u) \, du = \mu_2(K) < \infty$ (finite, non-zero second moment — the *roughness*).
6. $\displaystyle\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} K(u)^2 \, du = R(K) < \infty$ (square-integrability).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Higher-order kernels** have additional zero moments $\int u^k K(u) du = 0$ for $k = 1, 2, \ldots, p-1$, where $p$ is the order. They reduce bias at the cost of higher variance.

## 2.3 Common Kernel Functions (KTU Cheat Sheet)

| Kernel Name | Functional Form $K(u)$ | $\mu_2(K)$ | $R(K) = \int K^2$ | Optimal Efficiency | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Gaussian** | $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp(-u^2/2)$ | $1$ | $\dfrac{1}{2\sqrt{\pi}}$ | $\approx 95.5\%$ of Epanechnikov | Smooth, infinitely differentiable; default in `R ksmooth()`. |
| **Epanechnikov** | $\dfrac{3}{4}(1 - u^2)\mathbf{1}_{\{\vert u \vert \leq 1\}}$ | $\dfrac{1}{5}$ | $\dfrac{3}{5}$ | **100% (optimal in MSE)** | Minimises AMISE among 2nd-order kernels. |
| **Uniform (Rectangular)** | $\dfrac{1}{2}\mathbf{1}_{\{\vert u \vert \leq 1\}}$ | $\dfrac{1}{3}$ | $\dfrac{1}{2}$ | $\approx 92.9\%$ | Simplest; produces ragged estimates. |
| **Triangular** | $(1 - \vert u \vert)\mathbf{1}_{\{\vert u \vert \leq 1\}}$ | $\dfrac{1}{6}$ | $\dfrac{2}{3}$ | $\approx 98.6\%$ | Linear decay in weight. |
| **Biweight (Quartic)** | $\dfrac{15}{16}(1 - u^2)^2 \mathbf{1}_{\{\vert u \vert \leq 1\}}$ | $\dfrac{1}{7}$ | $\dfrac{5}{7}$ | $\approx 99.4\%$ | Smooth compact support. |
| **Triweight** | $\dfrac{35}{32}(1 - u^2)^3 \mathbf{1}_{\{\vert u \vert \leq 1\}}$ | $\dfrac{1}{9}$ | $\dfrac{35}{32}$ | $\approx 99.8\%$ | Higher-order smoothness. |

> [!NOTE]
> The Epanechnikov kernel is **AMISE-optimal** among second-order kernels, but the Gaussian kernel is preferred in practice due to its smoothness and differentiability.

## 2.4 Bandwidth Selection — The Heart of Kernel Smoothing

The **bandwidth $h$** is the single most important user choice. There are two broad classes:

### A) Global (Fixed) Bandwidth
- Same $h$ used for every evaluation point $x$.
- Selected by minimising a criterion such as **Cross-Validation (CV)** or **Generalised Cross-Validation (GCV)**.

**Leave-One-Out Cross-Validation (LOOCV) score:**

$$CV(h) = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( Y_i - \hat{m}_h^{(-i)}(x_i) \right)^2$$

where $\hat{m}_h^{(-i)}(x_i)$ is the NW estimate at $x_i$ computed **without** the $i$-th observation.

The optimal $h^* = \arg\min_h CV(h)$.

### B) Local (Variable) Bandwidth — Nearest-Neighbour (NN) Bandwidth
- $h(x) = $ the distance from $x$ to the $k$-th nearest neighbour among the $x_i$'s.
- Adapts to the **local density** of the data.

### C) Plug-in Bandwidth (Reference: Silverman's Rule of Thumb)

For Gaussian reference:
$$h_{opt} = \left( \frac{4 \hat{\sigma}^5}{3n} \right)^{1/5} \approx 1.06 \hat{\sigma} n^{-1/5}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bias–Variance Trade-off Insight:** For NW estimator with second-order kernel and fixed equispaced design:
> $$\text{Bias}(\hat{m}_h(x)) \;\approx\; \frac{h^2}{2} \mu_2(K) \, m''(x)$$
> $$\text{Var}(\hat{m}_h(x)) \;\approx\; \frac{\sigma^2 R(K)}{n h f_X(x)}$$
> So **small $h$** $\Rightarrow$ low bias, high variance (underfit the smooth shape, overfit noise). **Large $h$** $\Rightarrow$ high bias, low variance (overfit a flat line). The optimal $h$ balances them at $h^* \propto n^{-1/5}$.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Conditions / Units |
|---|---|---|
| NW Estimator | $\hat{m}_h(x) = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} K_h(x-x_i) Y_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} K_h(x-x_i)}$ | $K_h(u) = \dfrac{1}{h}K(u/h)$ |
| Scaled Kernel | $K_h(u) = \dfrac{1}{h}K\!\left(\dfrac{u}{h}\right)$ | Preserves $\int K_h(u)du = 1$ |
| Bias (order 2) | $\text{Bias} \approx \dfrac{h^2}{2}\mu_2(K) m''(x)$ | $m \in C^2$, $h \to 0$ |
| Variance | $\text{Var} \approx \dfrac{\sigma^2 R(K)}{n h f_X(x)}$ | $nh \to \infty$ |
| MISE | $\text{MISE}(h) = \int E[(\hat{m}_h - m)^2] dx$ | Integral of MSE |
| AMISE | $\dfrac{h^4}{4}\mu_2(K)^2 \int (m'')^2 dx + \dfrac{\sigma^2 R(K)}{n h} \int \dfrac{dx}{f_X(x)}$ | Asymptotic expansion |
| Optimal $h$ (AMISE) | $h_{opt} = \left( \dfrac{\sigma^2 R(K) \int dx/f_X}{\mu_2(K)^2 \int (m'')^2 dx} \right)^{1/5} n^{-1/5}$ | $h_{opt} \propto n^{-1/5}$ |
| Convergence Rate | $\Vert \hat{m}_h - m \Vert^2 = O_p(n^{-4/5})$ | Slower than parametric $O_p(n^{-1})$ |
| Local Polynomial Estimator | $\min_{\beta} \sum K_h(x-x_i) (Y_i - X_i^\top \beta)^2$ | Reduces boundary bias |
| Cross-Validation | $CV(h) = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum (Y_i - \hat{m}_h^{(-i)}(x_i))^2$ | Pick $h^* = \arg\min_h CV(h)$ |

## 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering / Finance

- **Volatility Surface Estimation**: Smoothing implied volatility surfaces in options markets (S&P 500, Nifty 50 option chains).
- **Yield Curve Construction**: Estimating the term structure of interest rates from sparse bond price data.
- **Risk-Return Profiles**: Estimating $E(R \mid \text{risk factor})$ nonparametrically in portfolio construction.
- **Technical Indicators**: LOESS-smoothed moving averages used in algorithmic trading.
- **Credit Scoring**: Recovering the relationship between default probability and macroeconomic covariates.
- **Density Estimation**: Kernel density estimation (KDE) of asset-return distributions (fat tails, skewness).
