# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Nadaraya–Watson Estimator (Local Constant Least Squares)

**Starting point:** We want to estimate $m(x)$ locally around a point $x$ by solving a **locally weighted least-squares problem**:

$$\min_{\alpha \in \mathbb{R}} \sum_{i=1}^{n} K_h(x - x_i) \left( Y_i - \alpha \right)^2$$

This fits a *local constant* (i.e. $m(x_i) \approx \alpha$ near $x$).

**Step 1:** Expand the objective function:
$$\mathcal{L}(\alpha) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} K_h(x - x_i) \left( Y_i^2 - 2\alpha Y_i + \alpha^2 \right)$$

**Step 2:** Differentiate w.r.t. $\alpha$ and set equal to zero:
$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \alpha} = -2 \sum_{i=1}^{n} K_h(x - x_i) Y_i + 2\alpha \sum_{i=1}^{n} K_h(x - x_i) = 0$$

**Step 3:** Solve for $\hat{\alpha} = \hat{m}_h(x)$:
$$\hat{m}_h(x) = \frac{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} K_h(x - x_i) \, Y_i}{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} K_h(x - x_i)}$$

This is the **Nadaraya–Watson estimator**. The derivation is complete.

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The denominator $\sum K_h(x-x_i)$ acts as a **local sample size** — it normalises the estimator to be unbiased (in mean) at locations with varying data density.

## 3.2 Bias Derivation (Asymptotic, Random Design)

Assume $x_i$ are i.i.d. with density $f_X(\cdot)$, and the kernel $K$ is of order 2. Then:

$$\text{Bias}(\hat{m}_h(x)) = E[\hat{m}_h(x) \mid X] - m(x)$$

**Step 1:** Compute the conditional expectation of $\hat{m}_h(x)$:
$$E[\hat{m}_h(x) \mid X] = \frac{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} K_h(x - x_i) \, m(x_i)}{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} K_h(x - x_i)}$$

This is the **ratio of two Riemann–Stieltjes sums** in the limit.

**Step 2:** Replace the discrete sum by its continuous approximation. For large $n$ and small $h$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{i=1}^{n} K_h(x - x_i) \, m(x_i) &\approx n \int K_h(x - u) \, m(u) \, f_X(u) \, du \\
&= n \int \frac{1}{h}K\!\left(\frac{x-u}{h}\right) m(u) f_X(u) \, du
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute $t = (u - x)/h$, so $u = x + ht$ and $du = h \, dt$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum K_h(x - x_i) m(x_i) &\approx n \int K(t) \, m(x+ht) \, f_X(x+ht) \, dt
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Apply a 2nd-order Taylor expansion of $m(x+ht) f_X(x+ht)$ around $t=0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
m(x+ht)f_X(x+ht) &\approx m(x)f_X(x) + ht \cdot \left[m(x)f_X(x)\right]' + \frac{h^2 t^2}{2}\left[m(x)f_X(x)\right]'' + \ldots
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Integrate term-by-term against $K(t)$, using $\int K(t) dt = 1$, $\int t K(t) dt = 0$, and $\int t^2 K(t) dt = \mu_2(K)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int K(t) m(x+ht) f_X(x+ht) \, dt &\approx m(x) f_X(x) + 0 + \frac{h^2 \mu_2(K)}{2} \left[m(x)f_X(x)\right]'' + O(h^3)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6:** Apply the same logic to the denominator $\sum K_h(x-x_i) \approx n f_X(x) + O(h^2)$.

**Step 7:** Take the ratio:
$$E[\hat{m}_h(x) \mid X] \approx \frac{m(x) f_X(x) + \frac{h^2 \mu_2(K)}{2} [mf_X]''(x)}{f_X(x)} + O(h^3)$$

**Step 8:** Simplify using the quotient rule $[mf_X]''/f_X = m'' + 2m'f_X'/f_X + m f_X''/f_X$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E[\hat{m}_h(x) \mid X] &\approx m(x) + \frac{h^2 \mu_2(K)}{2} m''(x) + O(h^2) \\
\text{Bias}(\hat{m}_h(x)) &\approx \frac{h^2 \mu_2(K)}{2} m''(x)
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Result:** Bias is $O(h^2)$ and proportional to the **local curvature** $m''(x)$. Where $m$ is very curved (e.g. near volatility smiles), bias is large unless $h$ shrinks.

## 3.3 Variance Derivation

**Step 1:** Conditional variance of a weighted average:
$$\text{Var}(\hat{m}_h(x) \mid X) = \frac{\sum K_h(x-x_i)^2 \sigma^2}{\left(\sum K_h(x-x_i)\right)^2}$$

**Step 2:** Approximate numerator and denominator by integrals:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum K_h(x-x_i)^2 &\approx n \int \frac{1}{h^2} K\!\left(\frac{x-u}{h}\right)^2 f_X(u) \, du = \frac{n}{h} \int K(t)^2 f_X(x) \, dt + O(1) \\
&= \frac{n f_X(x) R(K)}{h}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left(\sum K_h(x-x_i)\right)^2 &\approx \left( n f_X(x) \right)^2 = n^2 f_X(x)^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Take the ratio:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Var}(\hat{m}_h(x) \mid X) &\approx \frac{\sigma^2 \cdot \dfrac{n f_X(x) R(K)}{h}}{n^2 f_X(x)^2} = \frac{\sigma^2 R(K)}{n h f_X(x)}
\end{aligned}
$$

So:

$$\boxed{\text{Var}(\hat{m}_h(x)) \approx \frac{\sigma^2 R(K)}{n h f_X(x)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Variance grows when (i) the bandwidth is small (few effective points), (ii) the data density $f_X(x)$ is low (sparse region), and (iii) $\sigma^2$ is large (noisy data). It shrinks as $1/(nh)$ — the **effective sample size** is $n h f_X(x)$.

## 3.4 Derivation of Optimal Bandwidth via AMISE Minimisation

**Step 1:** Integrate the pointwise MSE $E[(\hat{m}_h(x) - m(x))^2]$ over the support to get MISE:

$$\text{MISE}(h) = \int \text{Bias}^2 \, dx + \int \text{Var} \, dx$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the asymptotic expansions:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int \text{Bias}^2 dx &\approx \frac{h^4 \mu_2(K)^2}{4} \int (m''(x))^2 \, dx \\
\int \text{Var} \, dx &\approx \frac{\sigma^2 R(K)}{nh} \int \frac{dx}{f_X(x)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Add them to get **Asymptotic MISE (AMISE):**

$$\text{AMISE}(h) = \underbrace{\frac{h^4 \mu_2(K)^2}{4} \int (m''(x))^2 \, dx}_{\text{squared-bias term}} + \underbrace{\frac{\sigma^2 R(K)}{nh} \int \frac{dx}{f_X(x)}}_{\text{variance term}}$$

**Step 4:** Differentiate AMISE w.r.t. $h$ and set to zero:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{d \text{AMISE}}{dh} &= h^3 \mu_2(K)^2 \int (m'')^2 dx - \frac{\sigma^2 R(K)}{n h^2} \int \frac{dx}{f_X} = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Solve for $h_{opt}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
h^5 \mu_2(K)^2 \int (m'')^2 dx &= \frac{\sigma^2 R(K)}{n} \int \frac{dx}{f_X}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
h_{opt}^5 &= \frac{\sigma^2 R(K) \int dx / f_X}{n \mu_2(K)^2 \int (m'')^2 dx}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{h_{opt} = \left( \frac{\sigma^2 R(K)}{n \mu_2(K)^2} \cdot \frac{\int dx / f_X(x)}{\int (m''(x))^2 dx} \right)^{1/5}}
$$

**Step 6:** Substitute back to get the **optimal AMISE**:

$$
\boxed{\text{AMISE}_{opt} = \frac{5}{4} \left( \frac{\sigma^2 R(K) \int dx/f_X}{\mu_2(K)^2 \int (m'')^2 dx} \right)^{4/5} n^{-4/5}}
$$

This shows the **nonparametric convergence rate** $n^{-4/5}$, slower than the parametric rate $n^{-1}$.

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison: Kernels at a Glance

| Kernel | $\mu_2(K)$ | $R(K)$ | $\mu_2(K)^2 R(K)$ (smaller = better) | AMISE Ratio (vs Epanechnikov) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Epanechnikov | $1/5$ | $3/5$ | $0.0400$ | $1.000$ (best) |
| Biweight | $1/7$ | $5/7$ | $0.0417$ | $1.043$ |
| Triweight | $1/9$ | $35/32$ | $0.0432$ | $1.080$ |
| Triangular | $1/6$ | $2/3$ | $0.0556$ | $1.389$ |
| Gaussian | $1$ | $1/(2\sqrt{\pi})$ | $0.2821$ | $7.05$ |
| Uniform | $1/3$ | $1/2$ | $0.1667$ | $4.17$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **The Epanechnikov kernel is AMISE-optimal**, but its 4% lead over the biweight is rarely meaningful in practice. The Gaussian remains popular for its analytical convenience and differentiability.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define the Nadaraya–Watson kernel regression estimator and explain the role of the bandwidth parameter. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Nadaraya–Watson (NW) estimator** is a nonparametric estimator of the regression function $m(x) = E(Y \mid X = x)$ in the model $Y_i = m(x_i) + \varepsilon_i$, given by:

$$\hat{m}_h(x) = \frac{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} K_h(x - x_i) Y_i}{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} K_h(x - x_i)}, \quad \text{where} \quad K_h(u) = \frac{1}{h}K\!\left(\frac{u}{h}\right)$$

**Role of bandwidth $h$:**
- $h$ is the smoothing parameter that controls the **width of the local neighbourhood** used at each evaluation point.
- **Small $h$** $\Rightarrow$ narrow window, few points in the local average $\Rightarrow$ low bias but **high variance** (wiggly estimate).
- **Large $h$** $\Rightarrow$ wide window, many points $\Rightarrow$ high bias (oversmoothed) but **low variance**.
- The optimal $h \propto n^{-1/5}$ balances the two and is selected by **cross-validation** or **plug-in** methods.

### Question 2: List any four common kernel functions used in nonparametric regression. State the optimal bandwidth rate $h_{opt}$ under the AMISE criterion. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Four common kernel functions:

1. **Gaussian kernel**: $K(u) = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-u^2/2}$, support $\mathbb{R}$.
2. **Epanechnikov kernel**: $K(u) = \dfrac{3}{4}(1 - u^2) \mathbf{1}_{\{\vert u \vert \leq 1\}}$, support $[-1, 1]$.
3. **Uniform (rectangular) kernel**: $K(u) = \dfrac{1}{2} \mathbf{1}_{\{\vert u \vert \leq 1\}}$, support $[-1, 1]$.
4. **Biweight (quartic) kernel**: $K(u) = \dfrac{15}{16}(1 - u^2)^2 \mathbf{1}_{\{\vert u \vert \leq 1\}}$, support $[-1, 1]$.

The **optimal AMISE bandwidth** is:

$$h_{opt} = \left( \frac{\sigma^2 R(K)}{n \mu_2(K)^2} \cdot \frac{\int dx / f_X(x)}{\int (m''(x))^2 dx} \right)^{1/5} \;\propto\; n^{-1/5}$$

The corresponding **AMISE** is $O(n^{-4/5})$, the celebrated nonparametric convergence rate.

---

## 5.2 PART B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** **[7 Marks]** Derive the asymptotic bias and variance of the Nadaraya–Watson estimator for a second-order kernel under random design. State clearly the assumptions used. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted]`

**(b)** **[7 Marks]** Using the bias and variance expressions derived in (a), obtain the asymptotically optimal bandwidth $h_{opt}$ that minimises the AMISE. Hence compute the resulting optimal AMISE. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]`

#### Model Solution to Part (a) — 7 marks

**Assumptions:**
- Model: $Y_i = m(x_i) + \varepsilon_i$ with $E(\varepsilon_i \mid x_i) = 0$, $\text{Var}(\varepsilon_i \mid x_i) = \sigma^2$.
- $x_i$ are i.i.d. from density $f_X(\cdot)$ on a compact support.
- $m \in C^2$ (twice continuously differentiable).
- $K$ is a **second-order kernel**: $\int K = 1$, $\int uK(u)du = 0$, $\int u^2 K(u)du = \mu_2(K) > 0$, $R(K) = \int K^2 < \infty$.
- $h \to 0$ and $nh \to \infty$ as $n \to \infty$.

**Bias derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E[\hat{m}_h(x) \mid X] &= \frac{\sum K_h(x-x_i) m(x_i)}{\sum K_h(x-x_i)} \\
&\approx \frac{\int K_h(x-u) m(u) f_X(u) du}{\int K_h(x-u) f_X(u) du} \quad \text{(Riemann sum)} \\
&= \frac{\int K(t) m(x+ht) f_X(x+ht) dt}{\int K(t) f_X(x+ht) dt}
\end{aligned}
$$

Taylor-expand $m(x+ht)f_X(x+ht)$ and $f_X(x+ht)$ around $h=0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Numerator} &\approx m(x)f_X(x) + \frac{h^2 \mu_2(K)}{2} [m f_X]''(x) \\
\text{Denominator} &\approx f_X(x) + \frac{h^2 \mu_2(K)}{2} f_X''(x) \approx f_X(x) \quad \text{(to leading order)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:
$$\text{Bias}(\hat{m}_h(x)) = E[\hat{m}_h(x) \mid X] - m(x) \approx \frac{h^2 \mu_2(K)}{2} m''(x)$$

**Variance derivation:**

$$\text{Var}(\hat{m}_h(x) \mid X) = \frac{\sigma^2 \sum K_h(x-x_i)^2}{\left(\sum K_h(x-x_i)\right)^2} \approx \frac{\sigma^2 R(K)}{n h f_X(x)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark distribution:** Assumptions 2 marks, Bias derivation 3 marks, Variance derivation 2 marks.

#### Model Solution to Part (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1:** The AMISE is the integral of the asymptotic pointwise MSE:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{AMISE}(h) &= \int \left[\text{Bias}^2 + \text{Var}\right] dx \\
&= \frac{h^4 \mu_2(K)^2}{4} \int (m''(x))^2 dx + \frac{\sigma^2 R(K)}{nh} \int \frac{dx}{f_X(x)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Differentiate w.r.t. $h$ and set to zero:

$$\frac{d \text{AMISE}}{dh} = h^3 \mu_2(K)^2 \int (m'')^2 dx - \frac{\sigma^2 R(K)}{n h^2} \int \frac{dx}{f_X} = 0$$

**Step 3:** Solve:

$$
h_{opt}^5 = \frac{\sigma^2 R(K) \int dx / f_X}{n \mu_2(K)^2 \int (m'')^2 dx} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad h_{opt} = \left( \frac{\sigma^2 R(K) \int dx/f_X}{n \mu_2(K)^2 \int (m'')^2 dx} \right)^{1/5}
$$

**Step 4:** Substitute back into AMISE:

$$
\text{AMISE}_{opt} = \frac{5}{4} \left( \frac{\sigma^2 R(K) \int dx/f_X}{\mu_2(K)^2 \int (m'')^2 dx} \right)^{4/5} n^{-4/5}
$$

This proves the nonparametric convergence rate of $\boldsymbol{n^{-4/5}}$, which is slower than the parametric rate $n^{-1}$ (a quantitative expression of the curse of dimensionality in 1-D).

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark distribution:** AMISE expansion 2 marks, differentiation 2 marks, $h_{opt}$ result 1 mark, optimal AMISE 2 marks.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** **[7 Marks]** Explain the bias–variance trade-off in kernel smoothing with a clear mathematical demonstration. Show that for a second-order kernel:

$$\text{Bias}(\hat{m}_h(x)) = O(h^2), \qquad \text{Var}(\hat{m}_h(x)) = O\!\left(\frac{1}{nh}\right).$$

What is the optimal rate at which $h$ should decrease with $n$? `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Adapted]`

**(b)** **[7 Marks]** Compare the following three kernels — Gaussian, Epanechnikov, and Uniform — in terms of: (i) support, (ii) $\mu_2(K)$, (iii) $R(K)$, (iv) AMISE efficiency. State and prove the property of the Epanechnikov kernel that makes it AMISE-optimal. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Adapted]`

#### Model Solution to Part (a) — 7 marks

The **bias–variance trade-off** is the central compromise in kernel smoothing:
- **Bias** measures how far the *expected* estimate is from the truth. It is determined by the *smoothing* (bandwidth).
- **Variance** measures the *random fluctuation* of the estimate around its mean. It is determined by the *effective sample size* inside the bandwidth window.

**Mathematical demonstration:**

For a second-order kernel $K$ with $m \in C^2$, expanding the conditional expectation yields (from Section 3.2):

$$\text{Bias}(\hat{m}_h(x)) = E[\hat{m}_h(x) \mid X] - m(x) \approx \frac{h^2 \mu_2(K)}{2} m''(x) = O(h^2)$$

This is $O(h^2)$ because $\mu_2(K)$ and $m''(x)$ are constants (independent of $h$).

For the variance (from Section 3.3):

$$\text{Var}(\hat{m}_h(x)) = \frac{\sigma^2 \sum K_h(x-x_i)^2}{[\sum K_h(x-x_i)]^2} \approx \frac{\sigma^2 R(K)}{n h f_X(x)} = O\!\left(\frac{1}{nh}\right)$$

This is $O(1/(nh))$ because $R(K)$ and $f_X(x)$ are constants.

**Optimal rate:** Minimise the asymptotic MSE $\text{Bias}^2 + \text{Var}$:

$$\text{MSE}(h) \approx \underbrace{C_1 h^4}_{\text{increases with } h} + \underbrace{\frac{C_2}{nh}}_{\text{decreases with } h}$$

Differentiating and setting to zero:

$$4 C_1 h^3 = \frac{C_2}{n h^2} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad h^5 = \frac{C_2}{4 C_1 n} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \boxed{h_{opt} \propto n^{-1/5}}$$

The resulting convergence rate of the MSE is:

$$\text{MSE}_{opt} = O(n^{-4/5})$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark distribution:** Bias formula 1.5 marks, Variance formula 1.5 marks, optimal rate derivation 2 marks, final $O(n^{-4/5})$ rate 1 mark, conceptual explanation 1 mark.

#### Model Solution to Part (b) — 7 marks

**Comparison Table:**

| Kernel | Support | $\mu_2(K)$ | $R(K) = \int K^2$ | AMISE Efficiency |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Gaussian | $\mathbb{R}$ | $1$ | $1/(2\sqrt{\pi}) \approx 0.2821$ | $\approx 95.5\%$ |
| Epanechnikov | $\vert u \vert \leq 1$ | $1/5 = 0.2$ | $3/5 = 0.6$ | $\mathbf{100\%}$ (best) |
| Uniform | $\vert u \vert \leq 1$ | $1/3 \approx 0.3333$ | $1/2 = 0.5$ | $\approx 92.9\%$ |

**Proof that Epanechnikov is AMISE-optimal:**

The AMISE is:

$$\text{AMISE}(h) = \frac{h^4}{4}\mu_2(K)^2 \int (m'')^2 dx + \frac{\sigma^2 R(K)}{nh} \int \frac{dx}{f_X}$$

For fixed $h$ and $n$, the **AMISE ratio** between any kernel $K$ and the Epanechnikov kernel $K^*$ is:

$$\frac{\text{AMISE}(K)}{\text{AMISE}(K^*)} = \frac{\mu_2(K)^2 R(K)}{\mu_2(K^*)^2 R(K^*)}$$

This ratio is the **AMISE efficiency**. Minimising $\mu_2(K)^2 R(K)$ over all 2nd-order kernels gives the Epanechnikov kernel. The proof uses the Cauchy–Schwarz inequality:

$$\mu_2(K^*) = \int u^2 K^*(u) du, \qquad R(K^*) = \int K^*(u)^2 du$$

Setting up the calculus-of-variations problem: minimise $\int K(u)^2 du$ subject to $\int K(u) du = 1$ and $\int u^2 K(u) du = \mu_2$. The Lagrangian is:

$$\mathcal{L} = \int K(u)^2 du - \lambda_0 \left( \int K(u) du - 1 \right) - \lambda_2 \left( \int u^2 K(u) du - \mu_2 \right)$$

The Euler–Lagrange equation $\partial \mathcal{L}/\partial K = 0$ yields $2K(u) = \lambda_0 + \lambda_2 u^2$, hence:

$$K^*(u) = \frac{\lambda_0}{2} + \frac{\lambda_2}{2} u^2$$

Imposing the constraints yields:

$$K^*(u) = \frac{3}{4}(1 - u^2), \quad \vert u \vert \leq 1$$

which is the **Epanechnikov kernel**. It is unique up to scaling, confirming it is the AMISE-optimal second-order kernel.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark distribution:** Comparison table 2 marks, AMISE ratio formula 1 mark, Lagrangian setup 2 marks, derivation of Epanechnikov 2 marks.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor $1/h$** in the scaled kernel $K_h(u) = (1/h)K(u/h)$. Students often write $K((x-x_i)/h)$ without the $1/h$ — this is INCORRECT.
> 2. **Confusing MISE with MSE**: MISE is the *integrated* (over $x$) MSE. AMISE is the *asymptotic* MISE.
> 3. **Wrong convergence rate**: Parametric regression has rate $n^{-1}$, but nonparametric kernel regression has rate $\boldsymbol{n^{-4/5}}$. This is a very common error.
> 4. **Forgetting to state assumptions**: The bias formula $h^2 \mu_2(K) m''(x)/2$ is only valid under (i) $m \in C^2$, (ii) second-order kernel, (iii) $h \to 0$, $nh \to \infty$.
> 5. **Confusing bandwidth with sample size**: $h$ is a *length-scale* in the predictor space (same units as $x$), not a count.
> 6. **Stating $h = 0$ as optimal**: It is NOT. $h = 0$ gives zero bias but **infinite** variance (interpolates the data).
> 7. **Missing the $f_X(x)$ in the denominator of the variance**: The variance diverges at sparse regions of the data.
> 8. **Writing $\vert x \vert$ in markdown tables** — KTU graders will mark it correct ONLY if rendered properly. Use $\vert x \vert$ or $\lvert x \rvert$ in $\LaTeX$.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Summary — Print This Before the Exam!**
> - [x] **Kernel Smoothing** = data-driven local averaging to estimate $m(x) = E(Y \mid X = x)$ nonparametrically.
> - [x] **Nadaraya–Watson estimator**: $\hat{m}_h(x) = \dfrac{\sum K_h(x-x_i) Y_i}{\sum K_h(x-x_i)}$.
> - [x] **Scaled kernel**: $K_h(u) = \dfrac{1}{h}K(u/h)$ preserves unit area.
> - [x] **Bandwidth $h$** controls the bias–variance trade-off; optimal $h \propto n^{-1/5}$.
> - [x] **Bias** = $\dfrac{h^2 \mu_2(K)}{2} m''(x) = O(h^2)$.
> - [x] **Variance** = $\dfrac{\sigma^2 R(K)}{n h f_X(x)} = O(1/(nh))$.
> - [x] **AMISE** = $\dfrac{h^4 \mu_2(K)^2}{4} \int (m'')^2 dx + \dfrac{\sigma^2 R(K)}{nh} \int \dfrac{dx}{f_X}$.
> - [x] **Optimal AMISE** = $O(n^{-4/5})$ — slower than parametric $O(n^{-1})$.
> - [x] **Epanechnikov kernel** $K(u) = \dfrac{3}{4}(1 - u^2) \mathbf{1}_{\{\vert u \vert \leq 1\}}$ is AMISE-optimal among 2nd-order kernels.
> - [x] **Gaussian** is most popular in practice; $\mu_2(K) = 1$, $R(K) = 1/(2\sqrt{\pi})$.
> - [x] **Cross-validation (CV)** is the standard data-driven bandwidth selector.
> - [x] **Local polynomial estimators** (e.g., local linear) fix the **boundary bias** problem of the NW estimator.
> - [x] **Curse of dimensionality**: kernel smoothing becomes impractical for $d \geq 5$ predictors.
> - [x] **Standardisation of $x$** before applying the kernel is good practice — it ensures $h$ is dimensionless and comparable across covariates.
> - [x] In **finance**, kernel smoothing is used for **yield-curve fitting**, **implied-volatility surfaces**, **risk–return profiles**, and **trend extraction** from noisy price data.
> - [x] **Assumptions for NW theory**: $m \in C^2$, 2nd-order kernel, random or fixed design, $h \to 0$, $nh \to \infty$.

> [!WARNING]
> **One-Line Exam Punchline:** "The Nadaraya–Watson estimator is a locally weighted average, where the weights come from a scaled kernel of bandwidth $h$; it has bias $O(h^2)$, variance $O(1/(nh))$, and the AMISE-optimal bandwidth is $h_{opt} \propto n^{-1/5}$, giving a nonparametric convergence rate of $n^{-4/5}$."
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Local Conditional Mean
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET742 - FINANCIAL TIME SERIES ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Local Conditional Mean

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In financial time series analysis, the **Local Conditional Mean** refers to the time-dependent expected value of a stochastic process $r_t$ (typically an asset return) given the information set (filtration) $\mathcal{F}_{t-1}$ available up to the immediate past. Mathematically, it is expressed as:

$$\mu_t = E(r_t \mid \mathcal{F}_{t-1})$$

Unlike the **unconditional mean** $E(r_t)$, which is a fixed constant assuming the series is stationary, the local conditional mean $\mu_t$ is allowed to vary with time as a function of past observations. The fundamental decomposition of a financial return series is:

$$r_t = \mu_t + a_t$$

where $a_t$ denotes the innovation, shock, or residual term at time $t$, satisfying:
- $E(a_t \mid \mathcal{F}_{t-1}) = 0$ (zero conditional mean of shocks)
- $\text{Var}(a_t \mid \mathcal{F}_{t-1}) = \sigma_a^2$ (conditional homoscedasticity — Volatility modeling, which is a separate concern covered later)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** Local conditional mean models include the **Autoregressive (AR)**, **Moving Average (MA)**, and combined **Autoregressive Moving Average (ARMA)** families. They form the cornerstone of linear time-domain modeling, before the introduction of **ARCH/GARCH** models (which handle conditional *variance* — a different focus).

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

Imagine you are a sailor navigating a boat. The **unconditional mean** would be like a fixed compass direction you set once at the start of the journey — it never changes, no matter what happens around you. The **local conditional mean**, on the other hand, is like a smart GPS system that **continuously recomputes your heading based on the waves, wind, and current conditions you just experienced**. 

In the same way, the local conditional mean of a stock return depends on *what the returns were doing yesterday, last week, and last month*. If returns were rising steadily, the model predicts a slightly higher mean for tomorrow; if they crashed, it predicts a reversion or continuation accordingly. The past "weather" of the market conditions the future "direction."

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Local"?** The term emphasizes that the mean is *local in time* — it is computed at each instant $t$ using a sliding window of recent history, rather than being a global, time-invariant statistic.

## 1.3 Stationarity — The Foundation of Local Mean Modeling

For any conditional mean model to be statistically meaningful and its parameters to be consistently estimable, the underlying series must be **(weakly) stationary**. A series $\{r_t\}$ is weakly stationary if:

1. $E(r_t) = \mu$ (constant finite mean)
2. $\text{Var}(r_t) = \sigma^2 < \infty$ (constant finite variance)
3. $\text{Cov}(r_t, r_{t-k}) = \gamma_k$ (autocovariance depends only on lag $k$, not on $t$)

| Property | Unconditional | Local Conditional |
|----------|---------------|-------------------|
| Mean | $\mu$ (constant) | $\mu_t$ (time-varying) |
| Variance | $\sigma^2$ (constant) | $\sigma_t^2$ (potentially time-varying) |
| Autocovariance | $\gamma_k$ (lag-dependent only) | Depends on $\mathcal{F}_{t-1}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric:** The conditional mean models discussed in this module operate strictly under the **stationarity** assumption. Non-stationary series require **ARIMA** (with differencing) or **cointegration** techniques.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Autocorrelation Function (ACF) and Partial Autocorrelation Function (PACF)

### 2.1.1 Autocorrelation Function (ACF)

The ACF measures the linear dependence between $r_t$ and its lagged value $r_{t-k}$:

$$\rho_k = \frac{\gamma_k}{\gamma_0} = \frac{\text{Cov}(r_t, r_{t-k})}{\text{Var}(r_t)}$$

The sample ACF estimator is:

$$\hat{\rho}_k = \frac{\sum_{t=k+1}^{T}(r_t - \bar{r})(r_{t-k} - \bar{r})}{\sum_{t=1}^{T}(r_t - \bar{r})^2}$$

### 2.1.2 Partial Autocorrelation Function (PACF)

The PACF at lag $k$ measures the correlation between $r_t$ and $r_{t-k}$ **after removing the linear effects of the intermediate lags** $r_{t-1}, r_{t-2}, \ldots, r_{t-k+1}$. It is computed as the last coefficient of an AR($k$) model fitted via the Yule-Walker equations.

## 2.2 Autoregressive (AR) Model

An **AR($p$)** model expresses the current return as a linear combination of $p$ past returns plus a white-noise shock:

$$r_t = \phi_0 + \phi_1 r_{t-1} + \phi_2 r_{t-2} + \cdots + \phi_p r_{t-p} + a_t$$

In mean-adjusted form (often used to drop the intercept):

$$r_t = \phi_1 r_{t-1} + \phi_2 r_{t-2} + \cdots + \phi_p r_{t-p} + a_t$$

**Stationarity Condition:** All roots of the characteristic polynomial $\Phi(z) = 1 - \phi_1 z - \phi_2 z^2 - \cdots - \phi_p z^p$ must lie **outside the unit circle** in the complex plane (i.e., moduli > 1).

## 2.3 Moving Average (MA) Model

An **MA($q$)** model expresses $r_t$ as a linear combination of the current shock and $q$ past shocks:

$$r_t = c_0 + a_t - \theta_1 a_{t-1} - \theta_2 a_{t-2} - \cdots - \theta_q a_{t-q}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Invertibility Condition:** All roots of the MA polynomial $\Theta(z) = 1 - \theta_1 z - \theta_2 z^2 - \cdots - \theta_q z^q$ must lie outside the unit circle. This ensures a unique AR($\infty$) representation.

## 2.4 Autoregressive Moving Average (ARMA) Model

The **ARMA($p$, $q$)** model combines both:

$$r_t = \phi_0 + \sum_{i=1}^{p}\phi_i r_{t-i} + a_t - \sum_{j=1}^{q}\theta_j a_{t-j}$$

Using the backshift operator $B$ (where $B r_t = r_{t-1}$):

$$\Phi(B) r_t = \phi_0 + \Theta(B) a_t$$

where:
- $\Phi(B) = 1 - \phi_1 B - \phi_2 B^2 - \cdots - \phi_p B^p$
- $\Theta(B) = 1 - \theta_1 B - \theta_2 B^2 - \cdots - \theta_q B^q$

## 2.5 ARIMA Model for Non-Stationary Series

If the series is non-stationary but becomes stationary after $d$ differencing operations, the **ARIMA($p, d, q$)** model applies:

$$\Phi(B)(1-B)^d r_t = \phi_0 + \Theta(B) a_t$$

## 2.6 Model Identification — The Box-Jenkins Approach

| Model | ACF Pattern | PACF Pattern |
|-------|-------------|--------------|
| **AR($p$)** | Tails off (exponential decay or damped sinusoid) | Cuts off after lag $p$ |
| **MA($q$)** | Cuts off after lag $q$ | Tails off (exponential decay or damped sinusoid) |
| **ARMA($p,q$)** | Tails off | Tails off |
| **White Noise** | All lags ≈ 0 (within confidence bands) | All lags ≈ 0 |

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Conditions / Units |
|---------|---------|-------------------|
| Conditional Mean Decomposition | $r_t = \mu_t + a_t$ | $E(a_t \mid \mathcal{F}_{t-1}) = 0$ |
| ACF | $\rho_k = \gamma_k / \gamma_0$ | $\vert \rho_k \vert \leq 1$, dimensionless |
| AR($p$) | $r_t = \sum_{i=1}^{p}\phi_i r_{t-i} + a_t$ | $\Phi(z) = 0 \Rightarrow \vert z \vert > 1$ |
| MA($q$) | $r_t = a_t - \sum_{j=1}^{q}\theta_j a_{t-j}$ | $\Theta(z) = 0 \Rightarrow \vert z \vert > 1$ |
| ARMA($p,q$) | $\Phi(B)r_t = \Theta(B) a_t$ | Both polynomials invertible |
| ARIMA($p,d,q$) | $\Phi(B)(1-B)^d r_t = \Theta(B) a_t$ | $d \in \mathbb{Z}_{\geq 0}$ |
| Yule-Walker (for AR) | $\boldsymbol{\rho} = \mathbf{R} \boldsymbol{\phi}$ | $\mathbf{R}$ is Toeplitz ACF matrix |
| AIC | $\text{AIC} = -2 \ln(L) + 2k$ | $k$ = number of parameters |
| BIC | $\text{BIC} = -2 \ln(L) + k \ln(T)$ | $T$ = sample size |
| Ljung-Box Statistic | $Q(m) = T(T+2)\sum_{k=1}^{m}\hat{\rho}_k^2 / (T-k)$ | $\sim \chi^2(m-p-q)$ under $H_0$ |

## 2.8 Parameter Estimation Methods

1. **Method of Moments / Yule-Walker** — solves linear equations using sample ACF. Simple, but inefficient.
2. **Least Squares (OLS)** — minimizes $\sum a_t^2$; equivalent to MLE under Gaussian assumption for AR models.
3. **Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE)** — exact under Gaussian innovations; standard for ARMA.
4. **Innovations Algorithm** — recursive MLE for MA($q$) and ARMA models.

## 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility

In quantitative finance and risk engineering, local conditional mean models are used for:
- **Forecasting** of asset returns and exchange rates
- **Signal extraction** in econometric control systems
- **Risk modeling** (VaR backtesting under conditional mean assumptions)
- **Algorithmic trading strategy design** based on momentum/reversion signals
- **Economic policy analysis** (e.g., forecasting inflation, GDP growth)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Yule-Walker Equations for AR($p$)

**Objective:** Derive the recursive system that links the AR coefficients to the theoretical ACF.

**Step 1 — Start with the AR($p$) model** (assume zero mean for simplicity):

$$r_t = \phi_1 r_{t-1} + \phi_2 r_{t-2} + \cdots + \phi_p r_{t-p} + a_t$$

**Step 2 — Multiply both sides by $r_{t-k}$ and take expectations** (for $k \geq 1$):

$$E(r_t r_{t-k}) = \phi_1 E(r_{t-1} r_{t-k}) + \phi_2 E(r_{t-2} r_{t-k}) + \cdots + \phi_p E(r_{t-p} r_{t-k}) + E(a_t r_{t-k})$$

**Step 3 — Apply properties of autocovariance and white noise:**

Since $a_t$ is uncorrelated with $r_{t-k}$ for $k \geq 1$, $E(a_t r_{t-k}) = 0$. The remaining terms are autocovariances:

$$\gamma_k = \phi_1 \gamma_{k-1} + \phi_2 \gamma_{k-2} + \cdots + \phi_p \gamma_{k-p}$$

**Step 4 — Divide by $\gamma_0$ to obtain the ACF recursion:**

$$\rho_k = \phi_1 \rho_{k-1} + \phi_2 \rho_{k-2} + \cdots + \phi_p \rho_{k-p}, \quad k \geq 1$$

**Step 5 — Write as a linear system (Yule-Walker form):**

For $k = 1, 2, \ldots, p$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\rho_1 &= \phi_1 + \phi_2 \rho_1 + \phi_3 \rho_2 + \cdots + \phi_p \rho_{p-1} \\
\rho_2 &= \phi_1 \rho_1 + \phi_2 + \phi_3 \rho_1 + \cdots + \phi_p \rho_{p-2} \\
&\;\;\vdots \\
\rho_p &= \phi_1 \rho_{p-1} + \phi_2 \rho_{p-2} + \cdots + \phi_p
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Express in matrix form:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix} \rho_1 \\ \rho_2 \\ \vdots \\ \rho_p \end{bmatrix} =
\begin{bmatrix} 
1 & \rho_1 & \rho_2 & \cdots & \rho_{p-1} \\
\rho_1 & 1 & \rho_1 & \cdots & \rho_{p-2} \\
\vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\
\rho_{p-1} & \rho_{p-2} & \rho_{p-3} & \cdots & 1
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} \phi_1 \\ \phi_2 \\ \vdots \\ \phi_p \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The matrix on the right is a **Toeplitz matrix** (constant along diagonals), which is symmetric positive definite under stationarity, guaranteeing a unique solution.

## 3.2 Derivation: Variance of an AR(1) Process

**Model:** $r_t = \phi_1 r_{t-1} + a_t$, with $a_t \sim WN(0, \sigma_a^2)$.

**Step 1 — Compute the variance of $r_t$:**

Since the process is stationary, $\text{Var}(r_t) = \text{Var}(r_{t-1}) = \sigma_r^2$.

$$E(r_t^2) = E[(\phi_1 r_{t-1} + a_t)^2]$$

**Step 2 — Expand the square:**

$$E(r_t^2) = \phi_1^2 E(r_{t-1}^2) + 2\phi_1 E(r_{t-1} a_t) + E(a_t^2)$$

**Step 3 — Apply independence** ($r_{t-1}$ and $a_t$ are independent):

$$\sigma_r^2 = \phi_1^2 \sigma_r^2 + \sigma_a^2$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $\sigma_r^2$:**

$$\sigma_r^2(1 - \phi_1^2) = \sigma_a^2$$

$$\sigma_r^2 = \frac{\sigma_a^2}{1 - \phi_1^2}$$

**Validity condition:** $\vert \phi_1 \vert < 1$ (so that the variance is finite and positive).

## 3.3 Worked Example: AR(1) Identification from Sample ACF

Suppose a financial return series has sample ACF values: $\hat{\rho}_1 = 0.7$, $\hat{\rho}_2 = 0.49$, $\hat{\rho}_3 = 0.343$, etc.

**Observation:** The ACF decays geometrically as $\hat{\rho}_k \approx 0.7^k$.

**Conclusion:** This is consistent with an **AR(1) model** with $\phi_1 \approx 0.7$. The PACF should cut off sharply after lag 1.

## 3.4 Worked Example: Fitting MA(1) to Sample Data

**Model:** $r_t = a_t - \theta_1 a_{t-1}$.

**Step 1 — Compute theoretical ACF of MA(1):**

By definition, $\gamma_0 = (1 + \theta_1^2)\sigma_a^2$ and $\gamma_1 = -\theta_1 \sigma_a^2$. Therefore:

$$\rho_1 = \frac{\gamma_1}{\gamma_0} = \frac{-\theta_1}{1 + \theta_1^2}$$

**Step 2 — Use sample $\hat{\rho}_1$ to solve for $\theta_1$:**

$$\hat{\theta}_1 = \frac{-1 \pm \sqrt{1 - 4\hat{\rho}_1^2}}{2\hat{\rho}_1}$$

**Step 3 — Select the invertible solution** (the one with $\vert \theta_1 \vert < 1$).

**Step 4 — Check**: $\hat{\rho}_k = 0$ for $k \geq 2$ (cuts off after lag 1).

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — ARIMA Forecasting Equation

For an ARIMA($1,1,0$) model: $(1-\phi_1 B)(1-B) r_t = a_t$.

**Step 1 — Expand the operator:**

$$(1 - \phi_1 B)(1 - B) = 1 - (1+\phi_1)B + \phi_1 B^2$$

**Step 2 — Write the model explicitly:**

$$r_t = (1+\phi_1) r_{t-1} - \phi_1 r_{t-2} + a_t$$

**Step 3 — One-step-ahead forecast:**

$$\hat{r}_{T+1} = E(r_{T+1} \mid \mathcal{F}_T) = (1+\phi_1) r_T - \phi_1 r_{T-1}$$

This is a **random walk with drift** extension when $\phi_1 = 0$.

## 3.6 Ljung-Box Test — Diagnostic for Residual White Noise

**Hypotheses:**
- $H_0$: The residuals $\{a_t\}$ are white noise (uncorrelated).
- $H_1$: At least one autocorrelation is non-zero.

**Test Statistic:**

$$Q(m) = T(T+2) \sum_{k=1}^{m} \frac{\hat{\rho}_k^2}{T-k} \sim \chi^2_{m-p-q}$$

**Decision Rule:** Reject $H_0$ at significance level $\alpha$ if $Q(m) > \chi^2_{m-p-q, 1-\alpha}$. A non-significant $Q$ confirms the model has adequately captured the linear dependence.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Conditional Mean Modeling Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Return Series rt] --> B{Stationarity Test}
    B -->|ADF / KPSS| C[Stationary?]
    C -->|No| D[Apply Differencing d times]
    D --> E[Stationary Series]
    C -->|Yes| E
    E --> F[Compute Sample ACF and PACF]
    F --> G[Identify p and q via Box-Jenkins Table]
    G --> H[Fit ARMA p q Model]
    H --> I[Estimate Parameters via MLE or OLS]
    I --> J[Residual Diagnostics]
    J --> K{Ljung-Box Test}
    K -->|Fail to Reject H0| L[Model Adequate]
    K -->|Reject H0| M[Re-specify Model]
    M --> G
    L --> N[Forecasting and Inference]
    N --> O[Final Conditional Mean Model]
```

## 4.2 Decision Flowchart: Model Selection via ACF/PACF

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Examine ACF and PACF] --> B{ACF cuts off?}
    B -->|Yes at lag q| C{ACF cuts off?}
    C -->|No, PACF tails off| D[MA q Model]
    C -->|Yes at lag p| E[White Noise]
    B -->|No, tails off| F{PACF cuts off?}
    F -->|Yes at lag p| G[AR p Model]
    F -->|No, tails off| H[ARMA p q Model]
    D --> I[Estimate and Validate]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    E --> I
    I --> J[Use AIC/BIC for Order Selection]
    J --> K[Final Model]
```

## 4.3 Schematic: Information Flow in a Conditional Mean Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Past Observations r t-1, r t-2, ...] --> B[Conditional Mean Estimator mu t]
    C[Past Shocks a t-1, a t-2, ...] --> B
    D[Current Shock a t] --> E[Observed Return r t]
    B --> E
    E --> F[Residual = r t - mu t]
    F --> G{Is Residual White Noise?}
    G -->|Yes| H[Model Validated]
    G -->|No| I[Re-fit with New Orders]
```

## 4.4 Hierarchy of Local Conditional Mean Models

```mermaid
graph TD
    LCM[Local Conditional Mean Models] --> AR[AR p]
    LCM --> MA[MA q]
    LCM --> ARMA[ARMA p q]
    LCM --> ARIMA[ARIMA p d q]
    LCM --> SARIMA[SARIMA Seasonal]
    AR --> AR1[AR 1 - Simple Memory]
    AR --> ARp[AR p - Multiple Lags]
    MA --> MA1[MA 1 - One Lag Shock]
    MA --> MAq[MA q - Multiple Shock Lags]
    ARMA --> ARMA11[ARMA 1 1 - Compact Form]
    ARMA --> ARMApq[ARMA p q - General]
    ARIMA --> ARIMA011[ARIMA 0 1 1 - Random Walk]
    ARIMA --> ARIMA111[ARIMA 1 1 1 - Differenced ARMA]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]** Define the **local conditional mean** of a financial return series. How does it differ from the unconditional mean?

**Model Answer:**

The **local conditional mean** of a return series $r_t$ is the time-varying expected value conditioned on the past information set $\mathcal{F}_{t-1}$:
$$\mu_t = E(r_t \mid \mathcal{F}_{t-1})$$

The **unconditional mean** $E(r_t) = \mu$ is a constant (provided the series is stationary), whereas the local conditional mean $\mu_t$ is allowed to change with time as a function of past observations. The unconditional mean is essentially the *long-run average*, while the local conditional mean is the *adaptive, short-run expected value* used for forecasting.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** State the **stationarity conditions** for an AR($p$) model. Why are they important?

**Model Answer:**

An AR($p$) model $r_t = \phi_1 r_{t-1} + \cdots + \phi_p r_{t-p} + a_t$ is stationary if **all roots of the characteristic polynomial** $\Phi(z) = 1 - \phi_1 z - \phi_2 z^2 - \cdots - \phi_p z^p$ lie **outside the unit circle** (i.e., moduli > 1).

**Importance:**
1. Ensures the mean, variance, and autocovariances are finite and time-invariant.
2. Guarantees a unique solution to the Yule-Walker equations.
3. Ensures shocks decay over time (no explosive behavior).
4. Validates the use of standard estimation and inference procedures.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Derive the **Yule-Walker equations** for an AR(2) model. Express them in matrix form.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Given the sample autocorrelations $\hat{\rho}_1 = 0.6$ and $\hat{\rho}_2 = 0.4$ for a stationary return series, identify the appropriate ARMA model and estimate its parameters.

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

For an AR(2) model: $r_t = \phi_1 r_{t-1} + \phi_2 r_{t-2} + a_t$.

**Step 1 — Multiply by $r_{t-1}$ and take expectation:**

$$E(r_t r_{t-1}) = \phi_1 E(r_{t-1}^2) + \phi_2 E(r_{t-2} r_{t-1}) + E(a_t r_{t-1})$$

Since $a_t$ is white noise, $E(a_t r_{t-1}) = 0$:

$$\gamma_1 = \phi_1 \gamma_0 + \phi_2 \gamma_1$$

Dividing by $\gamma_0$:

$$\rho_1 = \phi_1 + \phi_2 \rho_1 \quad \text{...(i)}$$

**Step 2 — Multiply by $r_{t-2}$ and take expectation:**

$$\gamma_2 = \phi_1 \gamma_1 + \phi_2 \gamma_0 + 0$$

Dividing by $\gamma_0$:

$$\rho_2 = \phi_1 \rho_1 + \phi_2 \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

**Step 3 — Write in matrix form:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix} \rho_1 \\ \rho_2 \end{bmatrix} =
\begin{bmatrix} 1 & \rho_1 \\ \rho_1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} \phi_1 \\ \phi_2 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Solve by inversion:**

$$\phi_1 = \frac{\rho_1(1 - \rho_2)}{1 - \rho_1^2}, \quad \phi_2 = \frac{\rho_2 - \rho_1^2}{1 - \rho_1^2}$$

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Use Yule-Walker formulas with $\hat{\rho}_1 = 0.6$, $\hat{\rho}_2 = 0.4$:**

$$\hat{\phi}_1 = \frac{0.6(1 - 0.4)}{1 - 0.6^2} = \frac{0.6 \times 0.6}{1 - 0.36} = \frac{0.36}{0.64} = 0.5625$$

$$\hat{\phi}_2 = \frac{0.4 - 0.6^2}{1 - 0.6^2} = \frac{0.4 - 0.36}{0.64} = \frac{0.04}{0.64} = 0.0625$$

**Step 2 — Fitted model:**

$$r_t = 0.5625\, r_{t-1} + 0.0625\, r_{t-2} + a_t$$

**Step 3 — Verify stationarity:** The characteristic polynomial $1 - 0.5625z - 0.0625z^2 = 0$ has roots whose moduli exceed 1 (check via discriminant / numerical evaluation). Both $\hat{\phi}_1$ and $\hat{\phi}_2$ are small in magnitude, satisfying $|\hat{\phi}_1| + |\hat{\phi}_2| = 0.625 < 1$ — a sufficient condition for AR(2) stationarity.

**Step 4 — Conclusion:** The data is well-modeled by an **AR(2)** process with the parameters above.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the **Box-Jenkins methodology** for identifying the orders $p$ and $q$ of an ARMA model. Use a clear decision table.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A fitted AR(1) model $r_t = 0.8\, r_{t-1} + a_t$ yields residual sample ACF values $\hat{\rho}_1 = 0.05$, $\hat{\rho}_2 = -0.03$, $\hat{\rho}_3 = 0.02$ for $T = 250$ observations. Perform a **Ljung-Box test** at $m = 5$ lags to check model adequacy at $\alpha = 0.05$. The critical value $\chi^2_{4, 0.95} = 9.488$.

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

The **Box-Jenkins methodology** is a three-stage iterative procedure:

**Stage 1 — Identification:**
1. Plot the series; apply stationarity tests (ADF, KPSS).
2. If non-stationary, difference $d$ times.
3. Compute sample ACF and PACF up to lag 20–30.
4. Use the **decision table** to identify candidate $p$ and $q$:

| ACF Behavior | PACF Behavior | Suggested Model |
|--------------|---------------|-----------------|
| Cuts off after lag $q$ | Tails off | MA($q$) |
| Tails off | Cuts off after lag $p$ | AR($p$) |
| Tails off | Tails off | ARMA($p,q$) |
| All within confidence bands | All within confidence bands | White Noise |

5. Use **AIC** and **BIC** to compare candidate models.

**Stage 2 — Estimation:** Estimate parameters using MLE, OLS, or Yule-Walker.

**Stage 3 — Diagnostic Checking:** Apply Ljung-Box test on residuals; ensure they resemble white noise. If not, return to Stage 1.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — State hypotheses:**
- $H_0$: Residuals are white noise (model is adequate).
- $H_1$: Residuals exhibit autocorrelation (model is inadequate).

**Step 2 — Compute $Q(5)$ with $p = 1$, so degrees of freedom $= m - p = 4$:**

$$Q(5) = T(T+2) \sum_{k=1}^{5} \frac{\hat{\rho}_k^2}{T - k}$$

Assume remaining lags beyond lag 3 are negligible ($\hat{\rho}_4 \approx \hat{\rho}_5 \approx 0$):

$$Q(5) \approx 250 \times 252 \left[ \frac{0.05^2}{249} + \frac{(-0.03)^2}{248} + \frac{0.02^2}{247} + 0 + 0 \right]$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate:**

$$\frac{0.0025}{249} + \frac{0.0009}{248} + \frac{0.0004}{247} \approx 0.0000100 + 0.0000036 + 0.0000016 \approx 0.0000152$$

$$Q(5) \approx 63000 \times 0.0000152 \approx 0.96$$

**Step 4 — Decision:** Since $Q(5) = 0.96 < 9.488 = \chi^2_{4, 0.95}$, **fail to reject $H_0$**.

**Step 5 — Conclusion:** The residuals are consistent with white noise. The **AR(1) model is adequate** at the 5% significance level.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing ACF and PACF patterns**: For AR models, students often write "ACF cuts off" — it is the **PACF that cuts off** for AR, and vice versa for MA. This is a guaranteed **2-mark deduction**.
> 2. **Forgetting the stationarity check**: When estimating ARMA parameters, you must verify (or at least mention) that the stationarity and invertibility conditions are satisfied.
> 3. **Degrees of freedom in Ljung-Box**: The degrees of freedom is $m - p - q$, not just $m$. Many students write $\chi^2_m$ instead of $\chi^2_{m-p-q}$.
> 4. **Unit of test statistic**: Always quote both the test statistic value and the critical value with comparison.
> 5. **Box-Jenkins logic**: Many students skip the "diagnostic checking" stage and jump straight to forecasting — this loses method marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**

- ✅ **Conditional Mean** = $E(r_t \mid \mathcal{F}_{t-1})$; varies with time, unlike unconditional mean.
- ✅ **Stationarity** is a prerequisite — checked via ADF, KPSS, or visual ACF decay.
- ✅ **AR($p$)**: PACF cuts off at lag $p$; ACF tails off.
- ✅ **MA($q$)**: ACF cuts off at lag $q$; PACF tails off.
- ✅ **ARMA($p,q$)**: both ACF and PACF tail off.
- ✅ **ARIMA($p,d,q$)**: difference $d$ times to achieve stationarity, then apply ARMA.
- ✅ **Yule-Walker** equations: $\boldsymbol{\rho} = \mathbf{R}\boldsymbol{\phi}$ for AR models.
- ✅ **Stationarity Condition** for AR($p$): all roots of $\Phi(z)$ outside unit circle.
- ✅ **Invertibility Condition** for MA($q$): all roots of $\Theta(z)$ outside unit circle.
- ✅ **Parameter Estimation**: MLE is gold standard; OLS for AR; Yule-Walker for quick estimates.
- ✅ **Model Selection**: AIC, BIC — lower is better; BIC penalizes complexity more.
- ✅ **Ljung-Box Test**: $Q(m) \sim \chi^2_{m-p-q}$ — used on residuals, not raw data.
- ✅ **Backshift operator** $B$: $B r_t = r_{t-1}$, $B^k r_t = r_{t-k}$.
- ✅ **Forecast formula** for AR(1): $\hat{r}_{T+1} = \phi_1 r_T$.
- ✅ **Mean zero vs. with constant**: Always check whether to include $\phi_0$ (intercept) in the model.
- ✅ **White noise assumption**: $a_t \sim WN(0, \sigma_a^2)$ — zero mean, constant variance, no autocorrelation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Local Polynomial Fitting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET742 - FINANCIAL TIME SERIES ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Local Polynomial Fitting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Local Polynomial Fitting (LPF)** is a nonparametric regression technique in which a polynomial of low degree (typically degree 0, 1, or 2) is fitted *locally* — that is, only within a small neighborhood surrounding each target point — using weighted least squares. The fitted polynomial is then evaluated **only at the center of that neighborhood** to produce the smooth estimate. Repeating this procedure at every observation yields a smooth estimate $\hat{m}(x)$ of the underlying regression function $m(x) = E(Y \mid X = x)$ without imposing a global functional form.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for *Financial Time Series Analysis* (PEIET742, Module 3), Local Polynomial Fitting sits at the heart of nonparametric smoothing. It generalizes the **Nadaraya–Watson estimator** (a degree-0 local constant fit) by allowing the local model to capture **local curvature**, thereby reducing the well-known **boundary bias** that plagues kernel regression near the edges of the data support.

Mathematically, for a target point $x_0$, we solve:

$$\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}(x_0) \;=\; \arg\min_{\boldsymbol{\beta}} \sum_{i=1}^{n}\Big[\, Y_i - \sum_{j=0}^{p}\beta_j (X_i - x_0)^j \,\Big]^2 \, K_h(X_i - x_0)$$

where $K_h(\cdot)$ is a kernel function with bandwidth $h$, $p$ is the polynomial degree, and $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}(x_0)$ are the locally estimated coefficients. The smooth estimate is then:

$$\hat{m}(x_0) \;=\; \hat{\beta}_0(x_0) \;=\; \mathbf{e}_1^{\top}\, (\mathbf{X}_0^{\top}\mathbf{W}_0\mathbf{X}_0)^{-1}\mathbf{X}_0^{\top}\mathbf{W}_0\,\mathbf{Y}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Highlight:** For degree $p=0$, LPF reduces to the **Nadaraya–Watson kernel estimator** (KTU frequently asks this reduction as a 3-mark question). For degree $p=1$, it becomes **local linear regression**, which is the practical default in financial applications due to its superior bias properties and automatic boundary correction.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "Sculpting with a Soft Pencil"

Imagine you are an artist drawing the trend of a stock price from a noisy chart. Instead of trying to draw **one giant straight line** (global linear regression) or **one giant curve** (global polynomial) through every point, you use a **short, flexible ruler** that you press down on a small section of the chart at a time.

- You slide this "ruler" along the x-axis (time).
- In each small window, you let the ruler **bend slightly** into a mini-polynomial that hugs the local trend.
- You record **only the value at the center** of the window.
- When you move to the next point, you repeat the process.

The **window size** is the *bandwidth* $h$ — narrow windows capture fine detail but are noisy; wide windows are smooth but blur the local trend. The **degree of the ruler's bend** is the *polynomial order* $p$ — a flat ruler (degree 0) only models a horizontal average, a tilted ruler (degree 1) models a local slope, and a curved ruler (degree 2) models local acceleration.

This is precisely what Local Polynomial Fitting does: it is **curve estimation by sliding-window mini-regressions**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical/Terminological Constants & Standards**
> - **Standard bandwidth range** for daily financial returns: $h \approx n^{-1/5}$ (Silverman's rule) for $p=1$.
> - **Common kernel**: Gaussian kernel $K(u) = \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}e^{-u^2/2}$, Epanechnikov kernel $K(u) = \tfrac{3}{4}(1-u^2)\mathbf{1}_{\{|u|\le 1\}}$ (optimal in MSE sense).
> - **Typical polynomial degrees used in practice**: $p = 0, 1, 2$; rarely above 2 due to variance explosion.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Logic — How Local Polynomial Fitting Works

Local Polynomial Fitting is implemented as a sequence of structured steps. Understanding each step is essential for the KTU ESE and is a frequent part-(a) of 14-mark questions.

**Step 1 — Choose the target point.** Pick a point $x_0$ at which we want to estimate the regression function $m(x_0)$.

**Step 2 — Define the local neighborhood.** Select a bandwidth $h > 0$. Only observations with $X_i$ within roughly $h$ of $x_0$ will receive non-negligible weight. The *effective neighborhood* is the interval $[x_0 - h,\; x_0 + h]$ for symmetric kernels.

**Step 3 — Construct the local design matrix.** For each candidate observation $(X_i, Y_i)$, form the row of a local design matrix that holds a polynomial basis evaluated at the *centered* covariate:

$$\mathbf{X}_i(x_0) = \big[\,1,\;(X_i - x_0),\;(X_i - x_0)^2,\;\ldots,\;(X_i - x_0)^p\,\big]$$

**Step 4 — Assign weights via the kernel.** Compute a weight for each observation based on its distance from $x_0$:

$$w_i(x_0) \;=\; K\!\left(\frac{X_i - x_0}{h}\right) \;\geq\; 0$$

Points near $x_0$ get large weights; far-away points get weights approaching zero. The Epanechnikov, Gaussian, triangular, uniform, and biweight kernels are all common choices.

**Step 5 — Solve the local weighted least-squares problem.** This yields the coefficient vector $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}(x_0)$. The estimate of the smooth function is the *intercept* of this local polynomial:

$$\hat{m}(x_0) = \hat{\beta}_0(x_0)$$

**Step 6 — Slide to the next $x_0$ and repeat.** For a finite grid of $N_g$ target points, the entire procedure is repeated $N_g$ times — making LPF computationally heavier than global regression but highly flexible.

## 2.2 Why "Local" Instead of "Global"?

Global polynomial regression fits a single function $\hat{m}(x) = \sum_{j=0}^{p}\hat{\beta}_j x^j$ using **all** data. This forces the same shape across regions with very different behavior — disastrous for financial data where volatility regimes and trends shift.

Local polynomial fitting relaxes this rigidity: the coefficients $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}(x_0)$ are *functions of $x_0$* themselves. The model admits the locally-varying truth $m(x)$ while preserving the analytic convenience of linear regression *within each neighborhood*.

## 2.3 The Bias–Variance Tradeoff — Heart of the KTU Module

Three competing forces govern the choice of $h$ and $p$:

| Quantity | Behavior as $h \uparrow$ | Behavior as $h \downarrow$ |
|---|---|---|
| **Bias** | Increases (window too wide → wrong model averaged in) | Decreases |
| **Variance** | Decreases (more data averaged) | Increases (noisy estimates) |
| **Smoothness** | Over-smoothed | Under-smoothed (wiggly) |

For polynomial degree $p$, the **asymptotic bias** is:

$$\mathrm{Bias}\big(\hat{m}^{(p)}(x)\big) \;\approx\; \frac{h^{p+1}}{(p+1)!}\, m^{(p+1)}(x)\, \mu_{p+1}\big(K\big) \;+\; \text{(design bias)}$$

where $\mu_j(K) = \int u^j K(u)\,du$ is the $j$-th moment of the kernel. The **asymptotic variance** is:

$$\mathrm{Var}\big(\hat{m}^{(p)}(x)\big) \;\approx\; \frac{\sigma^2(x)}{f(x)\, n\, h}\, \nu_0(K) \, \big[\,\mathbf{S}^{-1}\big]_{11}$$

where $\mathbf{S}$ is the matrix of kernel-weighted moments, $f(x)$ is the marginal density of $X$, and $\nu_0(K) = \int K(u)^2 du$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Bias Property (KTU Favorite):** For odd $p$, the design bias term **vanishes**, which is why $p = 1$ (local linear) is preferred over $p = 0$ (local constant) at the boundaries of the data.

## 2.4 The Optimal Bandwidth

The theoretically optimal bandwidth that minimizes the asymptotic Mean Integrated Squared Error (AMISE) is:

$$h_{\text{opt}} \;=\; \left[\frac{\sigma^2\, \nu_0(K)\, (p+1)!^2}{2n\, \mu_{p+1}^2(K)\, \int \big(m^{(p+1)}(x)\big)^2 w(x)\,dx}\right]^{1/(2p+3)}$$

Two practical data-driven selectors:

- **Rule-of-thumb (Silverman):** $h \approx 1.06\,\hat{\sigma}\, n^{-1/5}$ (for $p=1$, Gaussian kernel).
- **Cross-validation:** $\mathrm{CV}(h) = \tfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\big(Y_i - \hat{m}_{-i}(X_i)\big)^2$, picking $h$ that minimizes CV.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet — Local Polynomial Fitting

| **Element** | **Formula / Definition** | **Remarks** |
|---|---|---|
| Local polynomial estimator | $\hat{m}(x_0) = \mathbf{e}_1^{\top}(\mathbf{X}_0^{\top}\mathbf{W}_0\mathbf{X}_0)^{-1}\mathbf{X}_0^{\top}\mathbf{W}_0 \mathbf{Y}$ | $\mathbf{e}_1 = (1,0,\ldots,0)^{\top}$ |
| Local weighted regression | $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}(x_0) = \arg\min_{\boldsymbol{\beta}} \sum_i \big[Y_i - \sum_j \beta_j(X_i-x_0)^j\big]^2 K_h(X_i-x_0)$ | Definition |
| Kernel weight | $w_i(x_0) = K\big((X_i - x_0)/h\big)$ | $K \geq 0$, integrates to 1 |
| Epanechnikov kernel | $K(u) = \tfrac{3}{4}(1-u^2)\mathbf{1}_{\{\vert u \vert \leq 1\}}$ | MSE-optimal |
| Gaussian kernel | $K(u) = (2\pi)^{-1/2} e^{-u^2/2}$ | Smooth, infinite support |
| Uniform (rectangular) | $K(u) = \tfrac{1}{2}\mathbf{1}_{\{\vert u \vert \leq 1\}}$ | Discontinuous derivative |
| Asymptotic bias | $\mathrm{Bias} \approx \tfrac{h^{p+1}}{(p+1)!}\, m^{(p+1)}(x)\, \mu_{p+1}(K)$ | Interior, $p$ odd ⇒ design bias $\to 0$ |
| Asymptotic variance | $\mathrm{Var} \approx \tfrac{\sigma^2(x)}{f(x)\,n\,h}\, \nu_0(K)\,[\mathbf{S}^{-1}]_{11}$ | Inversely proportional to $nh$ |
| Optimal bandwidth (AMISE) | $h_{\text{opt}} \propto n^{-1/(2p+3)}$ | Order, $p=0$: $n^{-1/5}$; $p=1$: $n^{-1/5}$ (numerator); $p=2$: $n^{-1/7}$ |
| Cross-validation criterion | $\mathrm{CV}(h) = \tfrac{1}{n}\sum_i (Y_i - \hat{m}_{-i,h}(X_i))^2$ | Data-driven selection |
| Effective degrees of freedom | $\mathrm{df}(h) = \mathrm{tr}(\mathbf{S})$ where $\mathbf{S} = \mathbf{L}(\mathbf{X}^{\top}\mathbf{W}\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^{\top}\mathbf{W}$ | Used in GCV |
| Reduction to Nadaraya–Watson | $\hat{m}(x_0) = \tfrac{\sum_i Y_i K_h(X_i-x_0)}{\sum_i K_h(X_i-x_0)}$ | Happens when $p = 0$ |

## 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering & Finance

- **Trend estimation in equity indices** (S&P 500, NIFTY 50) — extracting the slow-moving drift $m(t)$ from noisy daily returns.
- **Yield-curve smoothing** — fitting a local quadratic to the term structure of interest rates.
- **Volatility surface interpolation** — local polynomial fitting across strike-price and maturity dimensions in option pricing.
- **Implied volatility smile/dew smile** — nonparametric regression of Black–Scholes implied vol on moneyness.
- **Risk metric estimation** — smooth estimation of conditional Value-at-Risk (VaR) and Expected Shortfall as functions of macro covariates.
- **Econometric policy analysis** — estimating the state-dependent effect of monetary policy on output (Aizenman–Hutchison–Noy type regressions).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Closed-Form Local Polynomial Estimator

**Statement.** For a fixed target point $x_0$, polynomial degree $p$, and bandwidth $h$, the solution to the locally-weighted least-squares problem admits a closed-form matrix expression.

**Step 1 — Set up the loss.** We wish to find a vector of coefficients $\boldsymbol{\beta} = (\beta_0,\beta_1,\ldots,\beta_p)^{\top} \in \mathbb{R}^{p+1}$ that minimizes:

$$L(\boldsymbol{\beta}) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \Big[\,Y_i - \beta_0 - \beta_1(X_i - x_0) - \beta_2(X_i - x_0)^2 - \cdots - \beta_p(X_i - x_0)^p\,\Big]^2 \, K_h(X_i - x_0)$$

**Step 2 — Write in matrix form.** Define:

$$\mathbf{Y} = \begin{pmatrix} Y_1 \\ Y_2 \\ \vdots \\ Y_n \end{pmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{X}_0 = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & (X_1-x_0) & \cdots & (X_1-x_0)^p \\ 1 & (X_2-x_0) & \cdots & (X_2-x_0)^p \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ 1 & (X_n-x_0) & \cdots & (X_n-x_0)^p \end{pmatrix}$$

$$\mathbf{W}_0 = \mathrm{diag}\big(K_h(X_1-x_0),\;K_h(X_2-x_0),\;\ldots,\;K_h(X_n-x_0)\big)$$

So $L(\boldsymbol{\beta}) = (\mathbf{Y} - \mathbf{X}_0\boldsymbol{\beta})^{\top}\mathbf{W}_0(\mathbf{Y} - \mathbf{X}_0\boldsymbol{\beta})$.

**Step 3 — Differentiate and set gradient to zero.** The gradient is:

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial \boldsymbol{\beta}} = -2\,\mathbf{X}_0^{\top}\mathbf{W}_0(\mathbf{Y} - \mathbf{X}_0\boldsymbol{\beta}) = 0$$

Solving:

$$\mathbf{X}_0^{\top}\mathbf{W}_0\,\mathbf{X}_0\,\boldsymbol{\beta} = \mathbf{X}_0^{\top}\mathbf{W}_0\,\mathbf{Y}$$

**Step 4 — Invert and read off the estimator.** As long as $\mathbf{X}_0^{\top}\mathbf{W}_0\mathbf{X}_0$ is nonsingular (which holds when $n_h \geq p+1$ points fall in the local window), we obtain:

$$\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}(x_0) = (\mathbf{X}_0^{\top}\mathbf{W}_0\mathbf{X}_0)^{-1}\mathbf{X}_0^{\top}\mathbf{W}_0\,\mathbf{Y}$$

**Step 5 — Extract the intercept.** Since $\hat{m}(x_0) = \hat{\beta}_0(x_0) = \mathbf{e}_1^{\top}\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}(x_0)$, we get the desired estimator:

$$\boxed{\;\hat{m}(x_0) = \mathbf{e}_1^{\top}\,(\mathbf{X}_0^{\top}\mathbf{W}_0\mathbf{X}_0)^{-1}\,\mathbf{X}_0^{\top}\mathbf{W}_0\,\mathbf{Y}\;}$$

The vector $\mathbf{e}_1 = (1,0,\ldots,0)^{\top} \in \mathbb{R}^{p+1}$ simply picks the intercept.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Reduction to Nadaraya–Watson When $p = 0$

**Step 1 — Set $p = 0$.** Then $\mathbf{X}_0$ collapses to a column of ones, i.e., $\mathbf{X}_0 = \mathbf{1}_n$.

**Step 2 — Substitute.** $\mathbf{X}_0^{\top}\mathbf{W}_0\mathbf{X}_0 = \mathbf{1}_n^{\top}\mathbf{W}_0\mathbf{1}_n = \sum_{i=1}^{n} K_h(X_i - x_0)$. Similarly, $\mathbf{X}_0^{\top}\mathbf{W}_0\mathbf{Y} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} K_h(X_i - x_0)\,Y_i$.

**Step 3 — Apply estimator.**

$$\hat{m}(x_0) = \big[\mathbf{e}_1^{\top}(\sum_i K_h(X_i-x_0))^{-1}\big]\sum_{i=1}^{n} K_h(X_i-x_0)\,Y_i = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} Y_i\,K_h(X_i-x_0)}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} K_h(X_i-x_0)}$$

This is precisely the **Nadaraya–Watson kernel regression estimator**, confirming the KTU statement that "local constant fitting equals kernel regression."

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Asymptotic Bias & Variance

Assume the true model is $Y_i = m(X_i) + \varepsilon_i$ with $E(\varepsilon_i \mid X_i) = 0$ and $\mathrm{Var}(\varepsilon_i \mid X_i) = \sigma^2(X_i)$. Taylor-expand $m(X_i)$ about $x_0$:

$$m(X_i) = \sum_{j=0}^{p} \frac{m^{(j)}(x_0)}{j!}(X_i - x_0)^j + \frac{m^{(p+1)}(x_0)}{(p+1)!}(X_i-x_0)^{p+1} + o\!\left((X_i-x_0)^{p+1}\right)$$

Plugging this into the locally-weighted score and taking expectations, the bias arises from the truncation after order $p$:

$$\mathrm{Bias}\big(\hat{m}^{(p)}(x_0)\big) = \frac{m^{(p+1)}(x_0)}{(p+1)!}\,h^{p+1}\,\mu_{p+1}(K) + \frac{m^{(p+1)}(x_0)\,h\,f'(x_0)\,[\mathbf{S}^{-1}]_{1,\cdot}\,\mathbf{t}_{p+1}}{(p+1)!\,f(x_0)} + \cdots$$

The second term is the **design bias**. For **odd $p$**, the symmetry of the kernel forces $\mathbf{t}_{p+1} = 0$, eliminating the design bias. This is the cornerstone justification for the local linear fit ($p = 1$) being the **default recommendation** in financial applications.

For the variance, recognizing $\hat{m}(x_0) = \sum_i \ell_i(x_0)\,Y_i$ where $\ell_i$ are the smoother weights, conditioning on the $X_i$:

$$\mathrm{Var}\big(\hat{m}(x_0)\big) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \ell_i(x_0)^2 \,\sigma^2(X_i) \;\approx\; \frac{\sigma^2(x_0)}{f(x_0)\,n\,h}\,\nu_0(K)\,[\mathbf{S}^{-1}]_{11}$$

This shows variance falls as $\tfrac{1}{nh}$ while squared bias grows as $h^{2(p+1)}$ — yielding the AMISE-optimal rate $h \propto n^{-1/(2p+3)}$.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Local Linear Fit at One Point)

**Setup.** Suppose $n = 5$, $X = (1, 2, 3, 4, 5)$, $Y = (2.1, 3.9, 6.2, 7.8, 10.1)$, target $x_0 = 3$, bandwidth $h = 2$, Gaussian kernel, $p = 1$.

**Step 1 — Compute distances and kernel weights.**

$$\begin{aligned}
u_i &= \frac{X_i - x_0}{h} = \frac{X_i - 3}{2} \\
u_1 &= -1, \quad u_2 = -0.5, \quad u_3 = 0, \quad u_4 = 0.5, \quad u_5 = 1
\end{aligned}$$

$$w_i = K(u_i) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}\,e^{-u_i^2/2} \approx \big(0.2420,\;0.3521,\;0.3989,\;0.3521,\;0.2420\big)$$

**Step 2 — Construct $\mathbf{X}_0$.**

$$\mathbf{X}_0 = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & -2 \\ 1 & -1 \\ 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \end{pmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{W}_0 = \mathrm{diag}(0.2420,\;0.3521,\;0.3989,\;0.3521,\;0.2420)$$

**Step 3 — Compute $\mathbf{X}_0^{\top}\mathbf{W}_0\mathbf{X}_0$.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{X}_0^{\top}\mathbf{W}_0\mathbf{X}_0 &= \begin{pmatrix} \sum w_i & \sum w_i u_i h \\ \sum w_i u_i h & \sum w_i (u_i h)^2 \end{pmatrix} \\
&= \begin{pmatrix} 1.5871 & -0.2202 \\ -0.2202 & 1.0000 \end{pmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Compute $\mathbf{X}_0^{\top}\mathbf{W}_0\mathbf{Y}$.**

$$\mathbf{X}_0^{\top}\mathbf{W}_0\mathbf{Y} = \begin{pmatrix} \sum w_i Y_i \\ \sum w_i Y_i (X_i - x_0) \end{pmatrix} \approx \begin{pmatrix} 9.275 \\ 5.612 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 5 — Solve.**

$$\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}(3) = \begin{pmatrix} 1.5871 & -0.2202 \\ -0.2202 & 1.0000 \end{pmatrix}^{-1} \begin{pmatrix} 9.275 \\ 5.612 \end{pmatrix} \approx \begin{pmatrix} 6.04 \\ 6.94 \end{pmatrix}$$

Thus $\hat{m}(3) = \hat{\beta}_0(3) \approx 6.04$, the smooth estimate at $x_0 = 3$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity Check:** A simple least-squares line through all five points gives slope $\approx 1.98$ and intercept $\approx 0.06$, predicting $Y(3) \approx 6.00$. The local linear estimate $\hat{m}(3) \approx 6.04$ is essentially identical here, confirming consistency in the dense-data regime.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Local Polynomial Fitting Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Raw financial time series Xi Yi] --> stepB[Choose bandwidth h and polynomial degree p]
    stepB --> stepC[Pick target point x0]
    stepC --> stepD[Compute centered distances ui = Xi minus x0 divided by h]
    stepD --> stepE[Evaluate kernel weights wi = K of ui]
    stepE --> stepF[Construct local design matrix X0 and weight matrix W0]
    stepF --> stepG[Solve weighted LS beta hat = X0t W0 X0 inverse X0t W0 Y]
    stepG --> stepH[Read off smooth estimate m hat x0 equals beta hat sub 0]
    stepH --> stepI{More target points?}
    stepI -- Yes --> stepC
    stepI -- No --> stepJ[Assemble complete smooth curve m hat x]
    stepJ --> stepK[Validate via cross-validation or AMISE]
    stepK --> stepL[Apply to volatility, trend, VaR estimation]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of LPF

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Input Layer]
        dataA[Time series observations Xi Yi]
        paramsA[Hyperparameters: bandwidth h, degree p, kernel K]
    end

    subgraph KERNEL[Kernel Weighting Engine]
        distA[Distance computation module]
        wgtA[Kernel evaluation module]
    end

    subgraph LS[Local Least Squares Solver]
        desA[Local design matrix builder]
        solvA[Matrix inversion and WLS solver]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT[Output Layer]
        coefA[Coefficient vector beta hat x0]
        smtA[Smoothed estimate m hat x0]
    end

    subgraph VALIDATE[Validation & Tuning]
        cvA[Cross-validation bandwidth selector]
        amiseA[AMISE-optimal h selector]
    end

    dataA --> distA
    paramsA --> distA
    distA --> wgtA
    wgtA --> desA
    desA --> solvA
    solvA --> coefA
    coefA --> smtA
    smtA --> cvA
    smtA --> amiseA
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Bias–Variance Tradeoff Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    axisA[Bandwidth h axis] --> leftA[Small h]
    axisA --> rightA[Large h]
    leftA --> lowBiasA[Low bias]
    leftA --> highVarA[High variance]
    leftA --> wigglyA[Wiggly, undersmoothed curve]
    rightA --> highBiasA[High bias]
    rightA --> lowVarA[Low variance]
    rightA --> flatA[Over-smoothed, biased curve]
    lowBiasA --> mida[MISE minimum]
    highBiasA --> mida
    highVarA --> mida
    lowVarA --> mida
    mida --> optBandA[Optimal h star]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define local polynomial fitting and state how it generalizes the Nadaraya–Watson estimator.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Local polynomial fitting is a nonparametric regression technique in which a polynomial of degree $p$ is fitted *locally* at each target point $x_0$ via weighted least squares, with weights supplied by a kernel function $K_h(\cdot)$ of bandwidth $h$. Formally:

$$\hat{m}(x_0) = \mathbf{e}_1^{\top}\big(\mathbf{X}_0^{\top}\mathbf{W}_0\mathbf{X}_0\big)^{-1}\mathbf{X}_0^{\top}\mathbf{W}_0\,\mathbf{Y}$$

It generalizes the Nadaraya–Watson estimator in the special case $p = 0$. When $p = 0$, the local design matrix $\mathbf{X}_0$ collapses to a column of ones, and the closed-form reduces exactly to:

$$\hat{m}(x_0) = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} Y_i K_h(X_i - x_0)}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} K_h(X_i - x_0)}$$

which is the Nadaraya–Watson kernel regression estimator. Choosing $p = 1$ or $p = 2$ further corrects boundary bias.

*(Examiner's note: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for the $p=0$ reduction.)*

---

### Question A2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State the asymptotic bias and variance of a local polynomial estimator of degree $p$ at an interior point.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

For an interior point $x$ of the support, the asymptotic bias and variance are:

$$\mathrm{Bias}\big(\hat{m}^{(p)}(x)\big) \;\approx\; \frac{h^{p+1}}{(p+1)!}\, m^{(p+1)}(x)\, \mu_{p+1}(K)$$

$$\mathrm{Var}\big(\hat{m}^{(p)}(x)\big) \;\approx\; \frac{\sigma^2(x)}{f(x)\,n\,h}\,\nu_0(K)\,[\mathbf{S}^{-1}]_{11}$$

The bias is dominated by the $(p+1)$-th derivative of $m$ times $h^{p+1}$; the variance falls as $1/(nh)$. For odd polynomial degree $p$, the additional design-bias term vanishes, which is why $p = 1$ is the default choice.

*(Examiner's note: 2 marks for the two formulas, 1 mark for the odd-$p$ remark.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question B-A. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` — Question A (14 Marks)

**(a) [7 marks]** Derive the closed-form expression for the local polynomial estimator $\hat{m}(x_0)$ at a target point $x_0$, clearly defining the local design matrix $\mathbf{X}_0$ and the diagonal weight matrix $\mathbf{W}_0$. Show how the intercept $\hat{\beta}_0(x_0)$ becomes the smooth estimate.

**(b) [7 marks]** Using the Epanechnikov kernel $K(u) = \tfrac{3}{4}(1-u^2)\mathbf{1}_{\{|u|\le 1\}}$ and the data $(X_i, Y_i)$ for $i=1,\ldots,5$ with $X = (1, 2, 3, 4, 5)$ and $Y = (2.1, 3.9, 6.2, 7.8, 10.1)$, compute the local linear estimate $\hat{m}(3)$ using bandwidth $h = 1.5$. Discuss the role of bandwidth choice.

---

**Model Solution (Question B-A):**

**(a) Full Derivation:**

Start with the locally-weighted loss function:

$$L(\boldsymbol{\beta}) = \sum_{i=1}^{n}\Big[\,Y_i - \sum_{j=0}^{p}\beta_j (X_i-x_0)^j\Big]^2 K_h(X_i - x_0)$$

Define the local design matrix (each row is a polynomial basis centered at $x_0$):

$$\mathbf{X}_0 = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & (X_1-x_0) & (X_1-x_0)^2 & \cdots & (X_1-x_0)^p \\ \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ 1 & (X_n-x_0) & (X_n-x_0)^2 & \cdots & (X_n-x_0)^p \end{pmatrix}$$

Define the diagonal weight matrix:

$$\mathbf{W}_0 = \mathrm{diag}\big(K_h(X_1-x_0),\, K_h(X_2-x_0),\, \ldots,\, K_h(X_n-x_0)\big)$$

Then $L(\boldsymbol{\beta}) = (\mathbf{Y}-\mathbf{X}_0\boldsymbol{\beta})^{\top}\mathbf{W}_0(\mathbf{Y}-\mathbf{X}_0\boldsymbol{\beta})$.

Differentiating with respect to $\boldsymbol{\beta}$ and equating to zero:

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial\boldsymbol{\beta}} = -2\mathbf{X}_0^{\top}\mathbf{W}_0(\mathbf{Y}-\mathbf{X}_0\boldsymbol{\beta}) = 0$$

This yields the normal equation:

$$\mathbf{X}_0^{\top}\mathbf{W}_0\mathbf{X}_0\,\boldsymbol{\beta} = \mathbf{X}_0^{\top}\mathbf{W}_0\,\mathbf{Y}$$

Provided $\mathbf{X}_0^{\top}\mathbf{W}_0\mathbf{X}_0$ is invertible (which requires at least $p+1$ observations in the support of the kernel), we obtain:

$$\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}(x_0) = (\mathbf{X}_0^{\top}\mathbf{W}_0\mathbf{X}_0)^{-1}\mathbf{X}_0^{\top}\mathbf{W}_0\,\mathbf{Y}$$

The smooth estimate is the intercept:

$$\hat{m}(x_0) = \hat{\beta}_0(x_0) = \mathbf{e}_1^{\top}(\mathbf{X}_0^{\top}\mathbf{W}_0\mathbf{X}_0)^{-1}\mathbf{X}_0^{\top}\mathbf{W}_0\,\mathbf{Y}$$

with $\mathbf{e}_1 = (1, 0, \ldots, 0)^{\top} \in \mathbb{R}^{p+1}$. $\blacksquare$

**(b) Numerical Computation:**

For $h = 1.5$, $x_0 = 3$, Epanechnikov kernel:

**Step 1.** Compute centered, scaled distances $u_i = (X_i - 3)/1.5$:

$$u_1 = -\tfrac{4}{3}, \quad u_2 = -\tfrac{2}{3}, \quad u_3 = 0, \quad u_4 = \tfrac{2}{3}, \quad u_5 = \tfrac{4}{3}$$

Since $|u_1| = 1.33 > 1$ and $|u_5| = 1.33 > 1$, the Epanechnikov kernel assigns **zero** weight to observations $i = 1$ and $i = 5$. So effectively $n_h = 3$.

**Step 2.** Compute weights for the three interior points:

$$w_2 = K(-\tfrac{2}{3}) = \tfrac{3}{4}\big(1 - \tfrac{4}{9}\big) = \tfrac{3}{4}\cdot\tfrac{5}{9} = \tfrac{5}{12} \approx 0.4167$$

$$w_3 = K(0) = \tfrac{3}{4}(1 - 0) = 0.7500$$

$$w_4 = K(\tfrac{2}{3}) = \tfrac{5}{12} \approx 0.4167$$

**Step 3.** Form the local design matrix (only $i = 2, 3, 4$ contribute):

$$\mathbf{X}_0 = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 1 \end{pmatrix}, \qquad \mathbf{W}_0 = \mathrm{diag}(0.4167,\; 0.7500,\; 0.4167)$$

**Step 4.** Compute $\mathbf{X}_0^{\top}\mathbf{W}_0\mathbf{X}_0$:

$$\mathbf{X}_0^{\top}\mathbf{W}_0 = \begin{pmatrix} 0.4167 & 0.7500 & 0.4167 \\ -0.4167 & 0 & 0.4167 \end{pmatrix}$$

$$\mathbf{X}_0^{\top}\mathbf{W}_0\mathbf{X}_0 = \begin{pmatrix} 1.5834 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.8334 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 5.** Compute $\mathbf{X}_0^{\top}\mathbf{W}_0\mathbf{Y}$ using only $Y_2 = 3.9$, $Y_3 = 6.2$, $Y_4 = 7.8$:

$$\mathbf{X}_0^{\top}\mathbf{W}_0\mathbf{Y} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.4167(3.9) + 0.7500(6.2) + 0.4167(7.8) \\ -0.4167(3.9) + 0 + 0.4167(7.8) \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 8.2751 \\ 1.6250 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 6.** Solve:

$$\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}(3) = \begin{pmatrix} 1.5834 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.8334 \end{pmatrix}^{-1} \begin{pmatrix} 8.2751 \\ 1.6250 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 5.226 \\ 1.950 \end{pmatrix}$$

Therefore $\hat{m}(3) = \hat{\beta}_0(3) \approx \mathbf{5.23}$, with local slope $\hat{\beta}_1(3) \approx 1.95$.

**Discussion of Bandwidth:** The bandwidth $h = 1.5$ kept only three observations in the support, leading to a small but possibly undersmoothed estimate. Increasing $h$ would bring more points into the local regression and reduce variance at the cost of increased bias (bias–variance tradeoff). Cross-validation would typically select an optimal $h \approx 1.0$–$2.0$ for this dataset. The Epanechnikov kernel's compact support acts as an automatic data-trimming device.

---

### Question B-B. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` — Question B (14 Marks) — *Internal Choice Alternative*

**(a) [7 marks]** State and prove the asymptotic bias and variance of the local polynomial estimator of degree $p$. Show explicitly why local linear fitting ($p = 1$) is preferred over local constant fitting ($p = 0$) near the boundaries of the data.

**(b) [7 marks]** Describe the cross-validation bandwidth selection procedure for local polynomial regression. Derive the AMISE-optimal bandwidth order $h_{\mathrm{opt}} \propto n^{-1/(2p+3)}$ from the asymptotic expressions.

---

**Model Solution (Question B-B):**

**(a) Asymptotic Bias and Variance:**

Under the model $Y = m(X) + \varepsilon$ with $E(\varepsilon \mid X) = 0$ and $\mathrm{Var}(\varepsilon \mid X) = \sigma^2(X)$, Taylor expand $m(X_i)$ around $x_0$:

$$m(X_i) = \sum_{j=0}^{p}\frac{m^{(j)}(x_0)}{j!}(X_i - x_0)^j + \frac{m^{(p+1)}(x_0)}{(p+1)!}(X_i - x_0)^{p+1} + o\!\left((X_i-x_0)^{p+1}\right)$$

Write the local polynomial estimator as $\hat{m}(x_0) = \mathbf{e}_1^{\top}\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}(x_0)$. Taking expectations:

$$E\big[\hat{m}(x_0)\big] = \mathbf{e}_1^{\top}(\mathbf{X}_0^{\top}\mathbf{W}_0\mathbf{X}_0)^{-1}\mathbf{X}_0^{\top}\mathbf{W}_0\,\mathbf{m} \approx m(x_0) + \frac{h^{p+1}}{(p+1)!}\,m^{(p+1)}(x_0)\,B_p(x_0)$$

where $B_p(x_0)$ contains the kernel moments and design correction. For *interior* points with symmetric kernel, $B_p(x_0) \to \mu_{p+1}(K)$.

The variance follows from recognizing $\hat{m}(x_0) = \sum_i \ell_i(x_0)\,Y_i$ and applying conditional variance:

$$\mathrm{Var}\big(\hat{m}(x_0)\big) = \sum_i \ell_i(x_0)^2\,\sigma^2(X_i) \approx \frac{\sigma^2(x_0)\,\nu_0(K)\,[\mathbf{S}^{-1}]_{11}}{f(x_0)\,n\,h}$$

**Boundary Advantage of $p = 1$:** Near the boundary of the support, the *design matrix* $\mathbf{S} = \mathbf{X}_0^{\top}\mathbf{W}_0\mathbf{X}_0 / (nf(x_0)\,h)$ becomes asymmetric. For $p = 0$, the constant fit cannot adapt to the asymmetry and a large **design bias** proportional to $h\,m'(x_0)\,f'(x_0)/f(x_0)$ appears — this dominates over the interior bias. For $p = 1$, the local slope $\hat{\beta}_1$ absorbs the asymmetry of the design, and the leading boundary bias vanishes identically. Hence **local linear fitting is automatically boundary-corrected** — a major practical advantage in financial time series where data at the start/end of the sample is inherently "boundary-like." $\blacksquare$

**(b) Cross-Validation and AMISE-Optimal Bandwidth:**

**Cross-Validation Procedure:**
Define the leave-one-out prediction:

$$\hat{m}_{-i}(X_i) = \mathbf{e}_1^{\top}\big(\mathbf{X}_{-i}^{\top}\mathbf{W}_{-i}\mathbf{X}_{-i}\big)^{-1}\mathbf{X}_{-i}^{\top}\mathbf{W}_{-i}\,\mathbf{Y}_{-i}$$

The cross-validation criterion is:

$$\mathrm{CV}(h) = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\big(Y_i - \hat{m}_{-i}(X_i)\big)^2$$

The optimal bandwidth is $\hat{h}_{\mathrm{CV}} = \arg\min_h \mathrm{CV}(h)$, computed on a grid or via optimization.

**AMISE Derivation:** The asymptotic mean integrated squared error is:

$$\mathrm{AMISE}(h) \approx \frac{h^{2(p+1)}}{(p+1)!^2}\,\mu_{p+1}^2(K)\int\big(m^{(p+1)}(x)\big)^2 w(x)\,dx \;+\; \frac{\sigma^2\,\nu_0(K)\,R(K)}{n\,h}$$

where $R(K)$ depends on the kernel and polynomial degree. Differentiating $\mathrm{AMISE}$ with respect to $h$ and equating to zero:

$$2(p+1)\,\frac{h^{2p+1}}{(p+1)!^2}\,\mu_{p+1}^2(K)\,\mathcal{M} \;=\; \frac{\sigma^2\,\nu_0(K)\,R(K)}{n\,h^2}$$

Solving:

$$h^{2p+3} \;=\; \frac{(p+1)!^2\,\sigma^2\,\nu_0(K)\,R(K)}{2(p+1)\,n\,\mu_{p+1}^2(K)\,\mathcal{M}}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\;h_{\mathrm{opt}} \;=\; C(K, p)\,\Big(\tfrac{1}{n}\Big)^{1/(2p+3)}\;}$$

with $C(K, p)$ a constant depending on the kernel and polynomial degree. For $p = 0$ and $p = 1$, this gives $h \propto n^{-1/5}$; for $p = 2$, $h \propto n^{-1/7}$. $\blacksquare$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting the centering:** Students often write the design matrix using $X_i$ instead of $(X_i - x_0)$. This is a 2-mark penalty because it destroys the interpretability of the coefficients as local intercept/slope.
> 2. **Mixing up bias and variance rates:** The bias falls as $h^{p+1}$, not $h^p$. The variance falls as $1/(nh)$, not $1/n$.
> 3. **Neglecting the design bias term:** Failing to mention that design bias vanishes for odd $p$ costs the boundary-comparison mark in 14-mark questions.
> 4. **Wrong AMISE exponent:** Forgetting the leading coefficient of $h^{2(p+1)}$ when deriving $h_{\mathrm{opt}}$ — both $p+1$ and $p$ exponents appear; don't confuse them.
> 5. **Kernel normalization:** When using Gaussian or Epanechnikov kernels in numerical work, ensure correct scaling factors ($\tfrac{3}{4}$ for Epanechnikov, $(2\pi)^{-1/2}$ for Gaussian).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** Local Polynomial Fitting estimates $m(x_0)$ by fitting a polynomial of degree $p$ in a window of width $h$ centered at $x_0$, weighted by a kernel.
- **Estimator formula:** $\hat{m}(x_0) = \mathbf{e}_1^{\top}(\mathbf{X}_0^{\top}\mathbf{W}_0\mathbf{X}_0)^{-1}\mathbf{X}_0^{\top}\mathbf{W}_0\mathbf{Y}$.
- **Reduction:** $p = 0 \Rightarrow$ Nadaraya–Watson; $p = 1 \Rightarrow$ local linear (default); $p = 2 \Rightarrow$ local quadratic.
- **Bias:** $\mathrm{Bias} \approx \tfrac{h^{p+1}}{(p+1)!} m^{(p+1)}(x_0)\mu_{p+1}(K)$ (interior).
- **Variance:** $\mathrm{Var} \approx \tfrac{\sigma^2(x_0)}{f(x_0)\,n\,h}\nu_0(K)[\mathbf{S}^{-1}]_{11}$.
- **Optimal bandwidth order:** $h_{\mathrm{opt}} \propto n^{-1/(2p+3)}$.
- **Odd-$p$ boundary advantage:** Design bias vanishes for $p = 1, 3, \ldots$ — boundary-corrected by construction.
- **Kernels:** Epanechnikov (MSE-optimal), Gaussian (smooth, infinite support), Uniform (simple but discontinuous derivative).
- **Bandwidth selectors:** Rule-of-thumb (Silverman $h \approx 1.06\hat{\sigma}n^{-1/5}$ for $p=1$ Gaussian), Cross-Validation, Generalized Cross-Validation.
- **Financial applications:** trend extraction, yield-curve smoothing, volatility-surface interpolation, conditional VaR/ES estimation, state-dependent policy effects.
- **Tradeoff:** Small $h \Rightarrow$ low bias, high variance (wiggly); Large $h \Rightarrow$ high bias, low variance (over-smoothed).
- **Common errors to avoid:** incorrect centering, swapping bias/variance exponents, omitting design-bias discussion, ignoring boundary effects.

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEIET742 - FINANCIAL TIME SERIES ANALYSIS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Splines # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) A **Spline** is a *piecewise-defined special function* constructed from polynomials of degree $d$, joined together at predefined points called **knots**, such that the overall function exhibits a prescribed degree of smoothness (continuity of the function and its derivatives up to order $d-1$) across the entire domain. In **Financial Time Series Analysis**, splines are nonparametric regression tools used to extract smooth nonlinear trends, interpolate missing observations, and estimate the term structure of interest rates from sparse, noisy, or irregularly spaced financial data. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – PEIET742):** Splines belong to the family of **nonparametric and semi-parametric smoothing techniques**. They are indispensable when the underlying data-generating process is too complex to be captured by rigid linear or polynomial regression yet must preserve local structural features (e.g., yield curve kinks, volatility regime changes). ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Flexible Ruler" Intuition Imagine you have a set of irregularly placed financial data points — say, the daily closing prices of a stock, or the yields of zero-coupon bonds at various maturities. If you connect each consecutive pair of points with a **straight line**, you get a jagged, angular polyline that looks like a child’s connect-the-dots drawing. This is piecewise **linear** interpolation: technically a spline of degree 1, but visually unappealing and statistically inefficient. Now, instead of a rigid ruler, imagine placing a **thin, flexible wooden spline** (the very tool draftsmen and shipbuilders have used for centuries) over the same pegs (knots).
