- [x] **Nadaraya–Watson estimator** — $\hat{m}_j(x) = \sum_t K_h(x - X_{j,t}) r_{j,t} \,/\, \sum_t K_h(x - X_{j,t})$; bandwidth $h$ controls bias-variance trade-off.
- [x] **ACE algorithm** — Maximizes $\text{Corr}^2[\theta(Y), \sum \phi_j(X_j)]$; the $\phi_j$ at convergence are the optimal $m_j$ under Gaussian-like assumptions.
- [x] **GAMs** — Extend linear models by replacing $\beta_j X_j$ with $m_j(X_j)$, combined with a link function $g$ from the exponential family.
- [x] **Curse of dimensionality is avoided** because each $m_j$ is one-dimensional, even when $k$ is large.
- [x] **Financial applications** — Nonlinear CAPM, yield curve fitting, implied-volatility surfaces, VaR/ES estimation, credit scoring.
- [x] **Key exam skill** — Be able to perform **at least one iteration of backfitting** by hand on a small dataset; examiners often test this directly.
- [x] **Common confusion** — Additive $\neq$ Linear; Additive $\neq$ Independent. Predictors may be correlated, but $m_j$ functions are still fitted one at a time.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Index Model and Sliced Inverse Regression
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET742 - FINANCIAL TIME SERIES ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Index Model and Sliced Inverse Regression

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 Module 3 — Index Model and Sliced Inverse Regression

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Course Outcome Mapping (CO):**
> This topic primarily addresses **CO3** — *Apply dimension-reduction and market-regression models to financial time-series data.* The Index Model links portfolio construction to a single market factor, while Sliced Inverse Regression (SIR) performs supervised dimension reduction in high-dimensional regression settings.

---

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The Single Index Model (SIM)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (Sharpe, 1963):** The *Single Index Model* is a one-factor asset-pricing regression that decomposes the excess return of a security into a systematic (market-driven) component and an idiosyncratic (firm-specific) residual:
> $$R_{i,t} \;=\; \alpha_i \;+\; \beta_i\, R_{M,t} \;+\; \varepsilon_{i,t}$$
> where $R_{i,t} = r_{i,t} - r_{f,t}$ is the excess return of asset $i$, $R_{M,t} = r_{M,t} - r_{f,t}$ is the excess return of the market index, $\beta_i$ measures *systematic risk*, and $\varepsilon_{i,t}$ is the firm-specific shock.

**Intuitive Analogy — "The Thermostat and Weather" 🌍:**
Imagine the temperature inside your house ($R_{i,t}$) is influenced by two things: the outside weather ($R_{M,t}$) and your own heater settings ($\varepsilon_{i,t}$). The **Index Model** says that whatever happens to your indoor temperature can be *mostly* explained by a single driving force — the outside weather — scaled by a sensitivity factor (your house's insulation, $\beta_i$). The "extra" deviation is random noise (someone opening a window). This is exactly how the market index drives a stock's return: most co-movement is explained by the market, and a small idiosyncratic residue is asset-specific news.

**Key terms in bold:**

- **$\beta_i$ (Beta):** Sensitivity of asset $i$ to the market; unit-less scalar.
- **$\alpha_i$ (Alpha):** Abnormal expected return not explained by the market; measured in percentage return.
- **$\sigma^2(\varepsilon_i)$:** Residual variance — the *unsystematic risk*.
- **$R^2_i = \dfrac{\beta_i^2\, \sigma_M^2}{\beta_i^2\, \sigma_M^2 + \sigma^2(\varepsilon_i)}$:** Proportion of return variance explained by the market.

---

### 1.2 Sliced Inverse Regression (SIR)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (Li, 1991):** *Sliced Inverse Regression* is a **nonparametric, supervised dimension-reduction** technique. It searches for directions $\boldsymbol{\beta}_1, \dots, \boldsymbol{\beta}_k \in \mathbb{R}^p$ such that the conditional expectation of the response $Y$ given the predictor vector $\mathbf{X}\in\mathbb{R}^p$ depends on $\mathbf{X}$ only through a small set of linear projections:
> $$\mathbb{E}[Y \mid \mathbf{X}] \;=\; g\!\left(\boldsymbol{\beta}_1^{\!\top}\mathbf{X},\, \boldsymbol{\beta}_2^{\!\top}\mathbf{X},\, \dots,\, \boldsymbol{\beta}_k^{\!\top}\mathbf{X}\right)$$
> for some unknown link function $g$. The *effective dimension reduction (e.d.r.)* subspace is $S = \mathrm{span}(\boldsymbol{\beta}_1,\dots,\boldsymbol{\beta}_k)$.

**Intuitive Analogy — "Shadow on a Wall" 🕯️:**
Imagine a 3-D sculpture ($\mathbf{X}$) casting a shadow on a 2-D wall. The shadow is a *linear projection* that preserves the information needed to predict the light-source angle ($Y$). SIR automatically finds the **best wall orientation** so that the shadow alone is enough to recover the relationship between $Y$ and the sculpture. Even though $\mathbf{X}$ has $p$ dimensions, only $k \ll p$ linear combinations of $\mathbf{X}$ carry the regression information — SIR finds them.

**The Three Key Building Blocks:**

1. **Standardization:** $\mathbf{Z} = \widehat{\boldsymbol{\Sigma}}^{-1/2}(\mathbf{X} - \bar{\mathbf{X}})$ removes scale and correlation.
2. **Slicing:** $Y$ is discretized into $H$ non-overlapping slices $S_1, S_2, \dots, S_H$.
3. **Moment Decomposition:** $\mathrm{Cov}(\mathbb{E}[\mathbf{Z}\mid Y]) = \boldsymbol{\Gamma}\,\mathrm{diag}(\lambda_1,\dots,\lambda_d)\,\boldsymbol{\Gamma}^{\top}$ — eigenvalues reveal the effective dimension $k$.

> [!TIP]
> **Why "Inverse"?** Classical regression models $\mathbb{E}[Y\mid \mathbf{X}]$ directly. SIR *inverts* this: it studies the inverse regression curve $\mathbb{E}[\mathbf{X}\mid Y]$ and recovers the directions that govern the forward regression — a numerically far easier task because slicing on $Y$ is one-dimensional.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

---

## 2.1 Single Index Model — Structural Decomposition

### 2.1.1 Assumptions (Sharpe's SIM)

Let $R_{i,t}$ and $R_{M,t}$ denote excess returns at time $t$. The SIM is grounded on three core probabilistic assumptions:

1. **Zero-Mean Residual (Mean Independence):**
$$\mathbb{E}[\varepsilon_{i,t}\mid R_{M,t}] \;=\; 0 \quad \forall\, t$$
2. **Homoscedastic Idiosyncratic Variance:**
$$\mathrm{Var}(\varepsilon_{i,t}\mid R_{M,t}) \;=\; \sigma^2(\varepsilon_i) \quad \text{(constant in } t\text{)}$$
3. **Cross-Sectional Independence of Residuals:**
$$\mathrm{Cov}(\varepsilon_{i,t},\, \varepsilon_{j,t}) \;=\; 0, \quad i \neq j$$

### 2.1.2 Variance Decomposition (The "Why" behind $\beta$)

> [!NOTE]
> **Variance Additivity:** Because $\varepsilon_i$ and $R_M$ are uncorrelated, total risk *adds up*:
> $$\mathrm{Var}(R_i) \;=\; \beta_i^{\,2}\,\sigma_M^{\,2} \;+\; \sigma^2(\varepsilon_i)$$
> The first term is **systematic risk** (diversifiable away only if the *index* itself is hedged); the second is **unsystematic risk** (eliminated by diversification).

### 2.1.3 Covariance Between Two Assets

A direct consequence of assumption (3) is the **two-asset covariance formula**:
$$\mathrm{Cov}(R_i,\, R_j) \;=\; \beta_i\,\beta_j\,\sigma_M^{\,2}$$

This linear structure is what makes the SIM computationally tractable: **only $N$ betas and $N$ residual variances** are needed to specify the full $N \times N$ covariance matrix (versus $N(N+1)/2$ parameters in a fully general model).

### 2.1.4 The OLS Estimation Procedure

Given $T$ paired observations $\{(R_{i,t},\,R_{M,t})\}_{t=1}^{T}$:

**Step 1 — Estimate $\beta_i$ via OLS:**
$$\widehat{\beta}_i \;=\; \dfrac{\sum_{t=1}^{T}(R_{M,t}-\bar R_M)(R_{i,t}-\bar R_i)}{\sum_{t=1}^{T}(R_{M,t}-\bar R_M)^2} \;=\; \dfrac{\widehat{\mathrm{Cov}}(R_i,R_M)}{\widehat{\mathrm{Var}}(R_M)}$$

**Step 2 — Estimate $\alpha_i$:**
$$\widehat{\alpha}_i \;=\; \bar R_i \;-\; \widehat{\beta}_i\,\bar R_M$$

**Step 3 — Estimate Residual Variance:**
$$\widehat{\sigma}^2(\varepsilon_i) \;=\; \dfrac{1}{T-2}\sum_{t=1}^{T}\bigl(R_{i,t}-\widehat{\alpha}_i-\widehat{\beta}_i R_{M,t}\bigr)^2$$

**Step 4 — Standard Errors (for hypothesis testing):**
$$\mathrm{SE}(\widehat{\beta}_i) \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{\widehat{\sigma}^2(\varepsilon_i)}{\sum_{t}(R_{M,t}-\bar R_M)^2}}, \qquad \mathrm{SE}(\widehat{\alpha}_i) \;=\; \sqrt{\widehat{\sigma}^2(\varepsilon_i)\!\left[\dfrac{1}{T}+\dfrac{\bar R_M^{\,2}}{\sum_t(R_{M,t}-\bar R_M)^2}\right]}$$

---

## 2.2 Sliced Inverse Regression — Algorithmic Logic

### 2.2.1 The Theoretical Foundation

The link between $\mathbb{E}[Y\mid \mathbf{X}]$ and $\mathbb{E}[\mathbf{X}\mid Y]$ is established through the **inverse regression curve**:
$$\mathbb{E}[\mathbf{X}\mid Y] \;=\; \boldsymbol{\mu} + \boldsymbol{\Sigma}\,\mathbf{P}\,\mathbf{g}(Y)$$
where $\mathbf{P}$ is an *orthogonal* $p\times k$ matrix whose columns span the e.d.r. subspace $S$, and $\mathbf{g}:\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R}^k$ is a smooth vector function.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Li (1991) — Linearity Condition:** If $\mathbb{E}[\mathbf{X}\mid \boldsymbol{\eta}^{\!\top}\mathbf{X}]$ is linear in $\boldsymbol{\eta}^{\!\top}\mathbf{X}$ for every $\boldsymbol{\eta}\in S$, then SIR recovers the e.d.r. subspace asymptotically. This holds when $\mathbf{X}$ is elliptically symmetric (e.g., multivariate normal).

### 2.2.2 The Standardized SIR Algorithm

**Step 0 — Pre-processing (Whitening):**
$$\mathbf{Z}_t \;=\; \widehat{\boldsymbol{\Sigma}}^{-1/2}(\mathbf{X}_t - \bar{\mathbf{X}}), \quad t=1,\dots,n$$
where $\widehat{\boldsymbol{\Sigma}} = \tfrac{1}{n}\sum_t (\mathbf{X}_t-\bar{\mathbf{X}})(\mathbf{X}_t-\bar{\mathbf{X}})^{\!\top}$.

**Step 1 — Slice the Response:**
Order the $\{Y_t\}$ and partition into $H$ slices $S_1,\dots,S_H$, each containing $n_h$ observations (typical $H\in[5,10]$).

**Step 2 — Compute Slice Means:**
$$\bar{\mathbf{Z}}_h \;=\; \dfrac{1}{n_h}\sum_{t:\,Y_t\in S_h}\mathbf{Z}_t, \qquad \bar{\mathbf{Z}} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n}\mathbf{Z}_t \;=\; \mathbf{0}$$

**Step 3 — Between-Slice Covariance Matrix:**
$$\widehat{\mathbf{M}} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{h=1}^{H} n_h\,(\bar{\mathbf{Z}}_h-\bar{\mathbf{Z}})(\bar{\mathbf{Z}}_h-\bar{\mathbf{Z}})^{\!\top} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{h=1}^{H} n_h\,\bar{\mathbf{Z}}_h\,\bar{\mathbf{Z}}_h^{\!\top}$$

**Step 4 — Spectral Decomposition (Whitened Space):**
$$\widehat{\mathbf{M}} \,=\, \sum_{j=1}^{p} \widehat{\lambda}_j\,\widehat{\boldsymbol{\eta}}_j\,\widehat{\boldsymbol{\eta}}_j^{\!\top}, \qquad \widehat{\lambda}_1 \ge \widehat{\lambda}_2 \ge \cdots \ge \widehat{\lambda}_p$$

**Step 5 — Back-transform to Original Coordinates:**
$$\widehat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}_j \;=\; \widehat{\boldsymbol{\Sigma}}^{-1/2}\,\widehat{\boldsymbol{\eta}}_j, \qquad j=1,2,\dots,k$$

**Step 6 — Choose Dimension $k$:** Use the cumulative-variance criterion:
$$\dfrac{\sum_{j=1}^{k}\widehat{\lambda}_j}{\sum_{j=1}^{p}\widehat{\lambda}_j} \;\ge\; 0.80 \quad (\text{or any threshold } \tau)$$

---

## 2.3 📊 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Examination Cheat-Code)

### Table 2.A — Index Model Core Quantities

| **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Symbol Meaning** |
|---|---|---|
| Index Model | $R_{i,t} = \alpha_i + \beta_i R_{M,t} + \varepsilon_{i,t}$ | Excess return decomposition |
| OLS Beta Estimator | $\widehat{\beta}_i = \widehat{\mathrm{Cov}}(R_i,R_M) \,/\, \widehat{\mathrm{Var}}(R_M)$ | Slope coefficient |
| OLS Alpha Estimator | $\widehat{\alpha}_i = \bar R_i - \widehat{\beta}_i \bar R_M$ | Intercept |
| Residual Variance | $\widehat{\sigma}^2(\varepsilon_i) = \tfrac{1}{T-2}\sum_t \hat\varepsilon_{i,t}^2$ | Unsystematic risk |
| Total Variance | $\mathrm{Var}(R_i) = \beta_i^2\sigma_M^2 + \sigma^2(\varepsilon_i)$ | Systematic + Idiosyncratic |
| Inter-Asset Covariance | $\mathrm{Cov}(R_i,R_j) = \beta_i\beta_j\sigma_M^2$ | Used in Markowitz |
| Coefficient of Determination | $R_i^2 = \beta_i^2\sigma_M^2 \,/\, \mathrm{Var}(R_i)$ | Goodness-of-fit |
| Test Statistic for $\beta_i$ | $t_{\beta} = \widehat{\beta}_i \,/\, \mathrm{SE}(\widehat{\beta}_i)$ | $H_0: \beta_i = 0$ |

### Table 2.B — Sliced Inverse Regression Core Quantities

| **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Symbol Meaning** |
|---|---|---|
| Standardized Predictor | $\mathbf{Z} = \widehat{\boldsymbol{\Sigma}}^{-1/2}(\mathbf{X}-\bar{\mathbf{X}})$ | Whitening transformation |
| E.D.R. Condition | $\mathbb{E}[Y\mid\mathbf{X}] = g(\boldsymbol{\beta}_1^{\!\top}\mathbf{X},\dots,\boldsymbol{\beta}_k^{\!\top}\mathbf{X})$ | Dimension reduction goal |
| Slice Mean | $\bar{\mathbf{Z}}_h = \tfrac{1}{n_h}\sum_{t\in S_h}\mathbf{Z}_t$ | Inverse regression point |
| SIR Moment Matrix | $\widehat{\mathbf{M}} = \tfrac{1}{n}\sum_h n_h \bar{\mathbf{Z}}_h \bar{\mathbf{Z}}_h^{\!\top}$ | Between-slice covariance |
| Eigen-Equation | $\widehat{\mathbf{M}}\,\widehat{\boldsymbol{\eta}} = \widehat{\lambda}\,\widehat{\boldsymbol{\eta}}$ | Extract SIR directions |
| Back-transformed Direction | $\widehat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}_j = \widehat{\boldsymbol{\Sigma}}^{-1/2}\widehat{\boldsymbol{\eta}}_j$ | Original-scale e.d.r. vector |
| Cumulative Variance Ratio | $\mathrm{CVR}(k) = \sum_{j=1}^{k}\widehat{\lambda}_j \,/\, \sum_{j=1}^{p}\widehat{\lambda}_j$ | Choose $k$ via threshold $\tau$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Units & Boundary Conditions:**
> - $\beta_i$ is **dimensionless** (a ratio of covariances), typically $\beta_i \in [-0.5,\, 2.5]$ for equity returns.
> - $R_i^2 \in [0,1]$ always; an $R^2$ above **0.50** indicates strong market dependence.
> - $\widehat{\lambda}_j \in [0,1]$ and $\sum_j \widehat{\lambda}_j = \mathrm{tr}(\widehat{\mathbf{M}}) \le 1$.
> - For $H$ slices, at most $\min(H-1,\, p)$ non-zero eigenvalues can exist.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering & Financial Utility

| **Method** | **Domain Application** |
|---|---|
| Single Index Model | Portfolio construction (Sharpe, 1963), CAPM estimation, beta-hedging, ETF replication, risk decomposition in Basel III frameworks |
| Sliced Inverse Regression | High-dimensional financial econometrics (Chen & Yin 2005), credit-scoring, yield-curve dimension reduction, asset-pricing factor discovery, mortgage-default prediction, macroeconomic nowcasting with $p\gg n$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Industry Connection:** Global asset managers (BlackRock, Vanguard) use Index-Model beta estimates to construct trillions of dollars in index-tracking portfolios. SIR-style methods are embedded in the **model risk** frameworks of central banks (BIS) and in **credit-card fraud** systems where $p$ (features) $\gg n$ (transactions flagged).

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following derivations are **board-exam ready**. Every algebraic transition is written explicitly with annotation. KTU examiners award **partial marks at every valid intermediate step**, so showing the work is non-negotiable.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — OLS Estimators for the Index Model

### Setting

We are given pairs $(R_{i,t},\, R_{M,t})$ for $t=1,\dots,T$. Define deviations from the mean:
$$\tilde R_{i,t} \;=\; R_{i,t} - \bar R_i, \qquad \tilde R_{M,t} \;=\; R_{M,t} - \bar R_M$$

### Step 1 — Set up the OLS Minimization

The OLS criterion is to choose $(\alpha_i,\beta_i)$ to minimize:
$$S(\alpha_i,\beta_i) \;=\; \sum_{t=1}^{T}\bigl(R_{i,t} - \alpha_i - \beta_i R_{M,t}\bigr)^2$$

### Step 2 — First-Order Conditions

Take partial derivatives and set to zero:
$$\dfrac{\partial S}{\partial \alpha_i} \;=\; -2\sum_{t=1}^{T}\bigl(R_{i,t}-\alpha_i-\beta_i R_{M,t}\bigr) \;=\; 0$$
$$\dfrac{\partial S}{\partial \beta_i} \;=\; -2\sum_{t=1}^{T}R_{M,t}\bigl(R_{i,t}-\alpha_i-\beta_i R_{M,t}\bigr) \;=\; 0$$

### Step 3 — Solve for $\alpha_i$

From the first FOC:
$$\sum_{t}R_{i,t} \;=\; T\alpha_i + \beta_i\sum_{t}R_{M,t}$$
Dividing by $T$:
$$\bar R_i \;=\; \alpha_i + \beta_i \bar R_M \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \boxed{\;\widehat{\alpha}_i \;=\; \bar R_i - \widehat{\beta}_i\bar R_M\;}$$

### Step 4 — Substitute $\alpha_i$ into the second FOC

Use the deviation form. Substituting $\widehat{\alpha}_i$ into the second equation and writing $R_{i,t}-\alpha_i-\beta_i R_{M,t} = \tilde R_{i,t} - \beta_i \tilde R_{M,t}$:
$$\sum_{t}R_{M,t}\bigl(\tilde R_{i,t}-\beta_i \tilde R_{M,t}\bigr) \;=\; 0$$
$$\sum_{t}\bigl(\bar R_M + \tilde R_{M,t}\bigr)\bigl(\tilde R_{i,t}-\beta_i \tilde R_{M,t}\bigr) \;=\; 0$$
Expanding, the cross-term $\bar R_M\sum_t \tilde R_{i,t} = 0$ (since $\sum_t \tilde R_{i,t}=0$), giving:
$$\bar R_M \cdot 0 \;-\; \beta_i\bar R_M\sum_t \tilde R_{M,t} \;+\; \sum_t \tilde R_{M,t}\tilde R_{i,t} \;-\; \beta_i\sum_t \tilde R_{M,t}^2 \;=\; 0$$
The middle term also vanishes because $\sum_t \tilde R_{M,t}=0$. So:
$$\sum_{t=1}^{T}\tilde R_{M,t}\,\tilde R_{i,t} \;-\; \beta_i\sum_{t=1}^{T}\tilde R_{M,t}^2 \;=\; 0$$

### Step 5 — Final OLS Solution

$$\widehat{\beta}_i \;=\; \dfrac{\sum_{t}\tilde R_{M,t}\tilde R_{i,t}}{\sum_{t}\tilde R_{M,t}^2} \;=\; \dfrac{\widehat{\mathrm{Cov}}(R_i,R_M)}{\widehat{\mathrm{Var}}(R_M)}$$

### Step 6 — Unbiasedness of the Residual Variance Estimator

Let $\hat\varepsilon_{i,t} = R_{i,t} - \widehat{\alpha}_i - \widehat{\beta}_i R_{M,t}$. With $T-2$ degrees of freedom (we lost 1 each for $\alpha_i$ and $\beta_i$):
$$\mathbb{E}\!\left[\sum_{t}\hat\varepsilon_{i,t}^{\,2}\right] \;=\; (T-2)\,\sigma^2(\varepsilon_i)$$
Hence:
$$\widehat{\sigma}^2(\varepsilon_i) \;=\; \dfrac{1}{T-2}\sum_{t=1}^{T}\hat\varepsilon_{i,t}^{\,2} \quad\text{is unbiased.}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Variance of $\widehat{\beta}_i$:** Under classical OLS assumptions, conditional on $\{R_{M,t}\}$:
> $$\mathrm{Var}(\widehat{\beta}_i\mid \{R_{M,t}\}) \;=\; \dfrac{\sigma^2(\varepsilon_i)}{\sum_{t=1}^{T}(R_{M,t}-\bar R_M)^2}$$
> This is the **Gauss–Markov theorem** in action: OLS is the Best Linear Unbiased Estimator (BLUE).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — The SIR Moment Matrix & Eigen-Decomposition

### Step 1 — Conditional Mean of the Whitened Predictor

Standardize so that $\mathbf{Z}=\widehat{\boldsymbol{\Sigma}}^{-1/2}(\mathbf{X}-\bar{\mathbf{X}})$ with $\mathrm{Cov}(\mathbf{Z}) = \mathbf{I}_p$. Apply the **inverse regression curve** identity:
$$\mathbb{E}[\mathbf{Z}\mid Y=y] \;=\; \boldsymbol{\Gamma}\,\mathbf{g}(y)$$
where $\boldsymbol{\Gamma}$ is $p\times k$ with orthonormal columns spanning the e.d.r. subspace, and $\mathbf{g}(y)\in\mathbb{R}^k$.

### Step 2 — Slice-Approximated Conditional Means

For discrete slices $S_h$ ($h=1,\dots,H$) of $Y$:
$$\bar{\mathbf{Z}}_h \;\approx\; \mathbb{E}[\mathbf{Z}\mid Y\in S_h] \;=\; \boldsymbol{\Gamma}\,\bar{\mathbf{g}}_h$$
where $\bar{\mathbf{g}}_h$ is the average of $\mathbf{g}(Y)$ over the $h$-th slice.

### Step 3 — Construct the Empirical $\widehat{\mathbf{M}}$

The within-slice pooled sample mean is $\bar{\mathbf{Z}} = \tfrac{1}{n}\sum_t \mathbf{Z}_t = \mathbf{0}$ (by construction of the deviation form). Therefore:
$$\widehat{\mathbf{M}} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{h=1}^{H}n_h\,\bar{\mathbf{Z}}_h\,\bar{\mathbf{Z}}_h^{\!\top} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{h=1}^{H}n_h\,\boldsymbol{\Gamma}\bar{\mathbf{g}}_h\bar{\mathbf{g}}_h^{\!\top}\boldsymbol{\Gamma}^{\!\top}$$

### Step 4 — Identify a Key Decomposition

Define the $k\times k$ matrix:
$$\mathbf{A} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{h=1}^{H}n_h\,\bar{\mathbf{g}}_h\,\bar{\mathbf{g}}_h^{\!\top} \;=\; \mathbb{E}\!\left[\bar{\mathbf{g}}(Y)\bar{\mathbf{g}}(Y)^{\!\top}\right] \;\approx\; \mathrm{Cov}(\mathbf{g}(Y))$$
This is a symmetric positive semi-definite matrix. Since $\boldsymbol{\Gamma}$ has orthonormal columns, the rank of $\widehat{\mathbf{M}}$ equals the rank of $\mathbf{A}$, which is at most $k$. The eigendecomposition of $\mathbf{A}$ is:
$$\mathbf{A} \;=\; \mathbf{V}\,\mathrm{diag}(\lambda_1,\dots,\lambda_k)\,\mathbf{V}^{\!\top}$$

### Step 5 — Full Eigen-Decomposition of $\widehat{\mathbf{M}}$

Substituting back, the non-zero eigenpairs of $\widehat{\mathbf{M}}$ are:
$$\widehat{\mathbf{M}}\,\underbrace{(\boldsymbol{\Gamma}\mathbf{V})}_{\text{eigenvector matrix}} \;=\; (\boldsymbol{\Gamma}\mathbf{V})\,\mathrm{diag}(\lambda_1,\dots,\lambda_k)$$

Thus, the eigenvectors $\widehat{\boldsymbol{\eta}}_j$ of $\widehat{\mathbf{M}}$ are exactly the columns of $\boldsymbol{\Gamma}\mathbf{V}$ (in the whitened space), and the eigenvalues $\widehat{\lambda}_j$ match the variance of $\mathbf{g}(Y)$ along each e.d.r. direction.

### Step 6 — Back to Original Predictor Space

The SIR directions in the original $\mathbf{X}$-space are:
$$\widehat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}_j \;=\; \widehat{\boldsymbol{\Sigma}}^{-1/2}\,\widehat{\boldsymbol{\eta}}_j, \qquad j=1,2,\dots,k$$
with $\widehat{\boldsymbol{\eta}}_j^{\!\top}\widehat{\boldsymbol{\eta}}_{j'}=\delta_{jj'}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The SIR recipe boils down to one linear-algebra fact:**
> - $\widehat{\mathbf{M}}$ is a $p\times p$ matrix,
> - we compute its top $k$ eigenvectors,
> - we whiten-transform back to the original scale.
> The choice of $k$ is the only "art" — a cumulative-variance rule $\sum_{j\le k}\widehat{\lambda}_j \ge 0.80\sum_j\widehat{\lambda}_j$ is standard.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — SIR on a Toy 2-D Dataset

**Problem.** Suppose $p=2$, $n=100$, and we have standardized the predictors so that $\widehat{\boldsymbol{\Sigma}}=\mathbf{I}_2$. After slicing $Y$ into $H=3$ slices, we observe the slice means:
$$\bar{\mathbf{Z}}_1 = (0.6,\, 0.0)^{\!\top},\quad \bar{\mathbf{Z}}_2 = (0.0,\, 0.0)^{\!\top},\quad \bar{\mathbf{Z}}_3 = (-0.6,\, 0.0)^{\!\top}$$
with $n_1=n_2=n_3=33$ (ignoring rounding).

**Step 1 — Compute $\widehat{\mathbf{M}}$:**
$$\widehat{\mathbf{M}} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{100}\bigl[33(0.6)^2 + 33(0)^2 + 33(-0.6)^2\bigr]\begin{pmatrix}1\\0\end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix}1&0\end{pmatrix} \;=\; \dfrac{33\cdot 0.72}{100}\begin{pmatrix}1&0\\0&0\end{pmatrix}$$
$$\widehat{\mathbf{M}} \;=\; 0.2376\,\begin{pmatrix}1&0\\0&0\end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Eigendecomposition:**
$$\widehat{\lambda}_1 = 0.2376,\quad \widehat{\boldsymbol{\eta}}_1 = (1,\,0)^{\!\top}, \qquad \widehat{\lambda}_2 = 0,\quad \widehat{\boldsymbol{\eta}}_2 = (0,\,1)^{\!\top}$$

**Step 3 — Select $k$ and interpret:**
The first eigenvalue captures **100%** of the variability $\Rightarrow k=1$. The SIR direction is along $X_1$ — the first predictor alone suffices to explain $Y$. The second direction $X_2$ carries **no regression information** and can be dropped.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Takeaway:** When slice means line up along a single axis (here, the $X_1$ axis), the data is *effectively one-dimensional for predicting $Y$*. This is the geometric signature of an e.d.r. structure with $k=1$.

## 4.1 Architecture of the Single Index Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    n1[Input: Asset Excess Returns R_i_t]
    n2[Input: Market Excess Returns R_M_t]

    n6[Step 4: OLS Intercept Formula]
    n7[Step 5: Compute Residuals e_i_t]
    n8[Step 6: Estimate sigma squared e]
    n9[Step 7: Test H0 beta equals 0]
    n10[Output: alpha, beta, R squared]

    n1 --> n3
    n2 --> n3
    n3 --> n4
    n4 --> n5
    n5 --> n6
    n5 --> n7
    n4 --> n7
    n7 --> n8
    n5 --> n9
    n6 --> n10
    n5 --> n10
    n8 --> n10
```

**Reading the diagram:** The pipeline takes two parallel inputs (asset and market excess-return series), centers them, runs the OLS slope/intercept formulas, recovers residuals, estimates the idiosyncratic variance, and finally outputs the parameter triplet $(\widehat{\alpha}_i,\widehat{\beta}_i,\widehat{\sigma}^2(\varepsilon_i))$ plus the $R^2$ and the $t$-statistic for $\beta$.

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the SIR Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    n1[Input: Predictor Matrix X n by p]
    n2[Input: Response Vector Y n by 1]
    nA[Block A: Centering X minus X bar]
    nB[Block B: Whitening Z equals Sigma power minus 1 half times X minus X bar]
    nC[Block C: Order Y and Slice into H Bins]
    nD[Block D: Compute Slice Means Z bar h for h 1 to H]
    nE[Block E: Build Moment Matrix M hat equals 1 over n sum h n h Z bar h Z bar h transpose]
    nF[Block F: Spectral Decomposition M hat equals Gamma Lambda Gamma transpose]
    nG[Block G: Select top k eigenvectors by eigenvalue magnitude]
    nH[Block H: Back Transform beta hat j equals Sigma power minus 1 half eta hat j]
    nI[Output: EDR subspace spanned by beta hat 1 to beta hat k]

    n1 --> nA
    nA --> nB
    n2 --> nC
    nB --> nD
    nC --> nD
    nD --> nE
    nE --> nF
    nF --> nG
    nG --> nH
    nB --> nH
    nH --> nI
```

**Reading the diagram:** The SIR algorithm has two *parallel* data streams — the predictor matrix $\mathbf{X}$ (top) and the response $Y$ (bottom) — that meet at Block D where the inverse-regression slice means are computed. The pipeline then performs a single eigen-decomposition (Block F), selects the top $k$ eigenvectors (Block G), and back-transforms to the original predictor space (Block H) to deliver the e.d.r. basis vectors.

---

## 4.3 Conceptual Bridge — Index Model as 1-D SIR

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    n1[Index Model: R_i equals alpha plus beta R_M plus epsilon]
    n2[SIR with 1 Slice: Single Direction]
    n3[Linear Projection of X onto Beta]
    n4[Conditional Mean Structure]

    n1 --> n3
    n2 --> n3
    n3 --> n4
```

**Reading the diagram:** A Single Index Model is conceptually a **1-dimensional SIR** in which the response $Y$ is regressed on a single linear projection $\boldsymbol{\beta}^{\!\top}\mathbf{X}$. The unifying idea: *both methods seek a low-dimensional linear subspace of the predictors that captures the entire regression information.*

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution Compliance:** All questions below follow the KTU 2024 ESE pattern — Part A short-answers (3 marks each, no choice) and Part B long-answer (14 marks, with internal choice between Question A and Question B). Sub-parts split the 14 marks as **7 + 7**, the standard KTU module-end convention.

---

## 📝 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`** State the assumptions of the Single Index Model and explain the role of $\beta_i$ as a measure of systematic risk.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Single Index Model $R_{i,t} = \alpha_i + \beta_i R_{M,t} + \varepsilon_{i,t}$ rests on three assumptions:

1. $\mathbb{E}[\varepsilon_{i,t}\mid R_{M,t}]=0$ — the residual has zero mean conditional on the market.
2. $\mathrm{Var}(\varepsilon_{i,t}\mid R_{M,t}) = \sigma^2(\varepsilon_i)$ — constant (homoscedastic) residual variance.
3. $\mathrm{Cov}(\varepsilon_{i,t}, \varepsilon_{j,t}) = 0$ for $i\neq j$ — cross-sectional independence.

**Role of $\beta_i$ (the systematic-risk measure):** $\beta_i = \mathrm{Cov}(R_i,R_M)/\mathrm{Var}(R_M)$ quantifies the **sensitivity** of asset $i$'s return to a unit change in the market return. A $\beta_i > 1$ indicates *aggressive* (amplified) market response, $\beta_i < 1$ indicates *defensive* behaviour, and $\beta_i = 1$ tracks the market. Because all assets share exposure $\beta_i R_{M,t}$ to the same market factor, $\beta_i$ is the channel through which market-wide shocks propagate — it is therefore the *systematic (non-diversifiable)* component of risk. [3 marks]

---

### **Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`** What is Sliced Inverse Regression? State the *effective dimension reduction* condition it seeks to satisfy.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

*Sliced Inverse Regression (SIR)*, introduced by **Ker-Chau Li (1991)**, is a nonparametric, supervised dimension-reduction method for high-dimensional regression. It searches for an orthogonal $p\times k$ basis matrix $\boldsymbol{\beta} = (\boldsymbol{\beta}_1,\dots,\boldsymbol{\beta}_k)$ such that the conditional expectation of the scalar response $Y$ given the $p$-dimensional predictor $\mathbf{X}$ depends on $\mathbf{X}$ only through $k$ linear projections:

$$\mathbb{E}[Y\mid \mathbf{X}] \;=\; g\!\left(\boldsymbol{\beta}_1^{\!\top}\mathbf{X},\, \boldsymbol{\beta}_2^{\!\top}\mathbf{X},\, \dots,\, \boldsymbol{\beta}_k^{\!\top}\mathbf{X}\right)$$

This is the **effective dimension reduction (e.d.r.) condition**, and the subspace $S = \mathrm{span}(\boldsymbol{\beta}_1,\dots,\boldsymbol{\beta}_k)$ is called the *e.d.r. subspace* of dimension $k$ (with $k \ll p$). SIR estimates $S$ by slicing $Y$ into $H$ bins, computing the between-slice covariance of the standardized predictors, and extracting the top $k$ eigenvectors of that matrix. [3 marks]

---

## 📝 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the "$-2$" in residual variance** — Many students use $\tfrac{1}{T}$ instead of $\tfrac{1}{T-2}$, losing 1 mark for the unbiasedness derivation.
> 2. **Confusing the directions of SIR** — Students often write $\widehat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = \widehat{\boldsymbol{\Sigma}}^{1/2}\widehat{\boldsymbol{\eta}}$ instead of the correct $\widehat{\boldsymbol{\Sigma}}^{-1/2}$. The minus-half exponent is *mandatory* because we initially whitened with $\widehat{\boldsymbol{\Sigma}}^{+1/2}$ and must invert it.
> 3. **Skipping the linearity condition** — Li's theorem requires the Linearity Condition (elliptical symmetry). Quoting SIR without it costs a full mark in the theoretical question.
> 4. **Not showing the eigen-decomposition explicitly** — Always write the eigen-equation $\widehat{\mathbf{M}}\widehat{\boldsymbol{\eta}} = \widehat{\lambda}\widehat{\boldsymbol{\eta}}$ and solve it on the board.

---

### ✏️ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** *Derive the OLS estimators* $\widehat{\alpha}_i$ and $\widehat{\beta}_i$ of the Single Index Model $R_{i,t} = \alpha_i + \beta_i R_{M,t} + \varepsilon_{i,t}$, starting from the residual sum-of-squares objective. State the Gauss–Markov theorem and discuss the unbiasedness of $\widehat{\sigma}^2(\varepsilon_i)$. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** *Explain the Sliced Inverse Regression algorithm* step by step, with the assumption of elliptical symmetry of $\mathbf{X}$. Show explicitly how the eigenvalues $\widehat{\lambda}_j$ of the moment matrix $\widehat{\mathbf{M}}$ identify the e.d.r. directions. **[7 marks]**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Question A

**(a) OLS Derivation [7 marks]:**

Define $S(\alpha,\beta) = \sum_{t=1}^{T}(R_{i,t}-\alpha_i-\beta_i R_{M,t})^2$.

*Step 1 — FOCs:*
$$\dfrac{\partial S}{\partial \alpha_i} = -2\sum_t(R_{i,t}-\alpha_i-\beta_i R_{M,t}) = 0 \tag{1}$$
$$\dfrac{\partial S}{\partial \beta_i} = -2\sum_t R_{M,t}(R_{i,t}-\alpha_i-\beta_i R_{M,t}) = 0 \tag{2}$$

*Step 2 — Solve (1):* Dividing by $2T$, we get $\bar R_i = \alpha_i + \beta_i \bar R_M$, hence:
$$\widehat{\alpha}_i = \bar R_i - \widehat{\beta}_i \bar R_M \tag{3}$$

*Step 3 — Substitute into (2) using deviations:* With $\tilde R_{i,t} = R_{i,t}-\bar R_i$, $\tilde R_{M,t} = R_{M,t}-\bar R_M$:
$$\sum_t R_{M,t}(\tilde R_{i,t} - \beta_i \tilde R_{M,t}) = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \sum_t \bar R_M(\tilde R_{i,t}-\beta_i\tilde R_{M,t}) + \sum_t \tilde R_{M,t}(\tilde R_{i,t}-\beta_i\tilde R_{M,t}) = 0$$

Both $\sum_t \tilde R_{i,t}=0$ and $\sum_t \tilde R_{M,t}=0$ eliminate the first group, leaving:
$$\sum_t \tilde R_{M,t}\tilde R_{i,t} = \beta_i \sum_t \tilde R_{M,t}^2 \;\Rightarrow\; \boxed{\widehat{\beta}_i = \dfrac{\sum_t \tilde R_{M,t}\tilde R_{i,t}}{\sum_t \tilde R_{M,t}^2}}$$

*Step 4 — Residual variance:* Let $\hat\varepsilon_{i,t} = \tilde R_{i,t} - \widehat{\beta}_i \tilde R_{M,t}$. The OLS identities $\sum_t \hat\varepsilon_{i,t}=0$ and $\sum_t R_{M,t}\hat\varepsilon_{i,t}=0$ imply that two degrees of freedom are lost, hence:
$$\widehat{\sigma}^2(\varepsilon_i) = \dfrac{1}{T-2}\sum_{t=1}^T \hat\varepsilon_{i,t}^{\,2}$$
is **unbiased**: $\mathbb{E}[\widehat{\sigma}^2(\varepsilon_i)] = \sigma^2(\varepsilon_i)$.

*Step 5 — Gauss–Markov Theorem:* Under the SIM assumptions (zero mean, homoscedasticity, no cross-correlation of residuals), $\widehat{\beta}_i$ is the **Best Linear Unbiased Estimator** — it has the minimum variance among all linear unbiased estimators:
$$\mathrm{Var}(\widehat{\beta}_i) = \dfrac{\sigma^2(\varepsilon_i)}{\sum_t \tilde R_{M,t}^2} \tag{4}$$

> **[Award 1 mark for the FOCs, 1 mark for the $\alpha$ solution, 2 marks for the $\beta$ derivation, 1 mark for residual variance, 1 mark for unbiasedness, 1 mark for the Gauss–Markov statement.]**

---

**(b) SIR Algorithm & Eigen-Decomposition [7 marks]:**

*Step 1 — Standardize:* $\mathbf{Z} = \widehat{\boldsymbol{\Sigma}}^{-1/2}(\mathbf{X}-\bar{\mathbf{X}})$ so that $\mathrm{Cov}(\mathbf{Z}) = \mathbf{I}_p$.

*Step 2 — Linearity Condition (Li 1991):* We require that for every $\boldsymbol{\eta}\in\mathbb{R}^p$, the conditional mean $\mathbb{E}[\mathbf{X}\mid \boldsymbol{\eta}^{\!\top}\mathbf{X}]$ is linear in $\boldsymbol{\eta}^{\!\top}\mathbf{X}$. This holds when $\mathbf{X}$ is **elliptically symmetric** (multivariate normal being the canonical case).

*Step 3 — Inverse regression curve:* Under the linearity condition, $\mathbb{E}[\mathbf{Z}\mid Y=y] = \boldsymbol{\Gamma}\,\mathbf{g}(y)$ for some orthonormal $p\times k$ matrix $\boldsymbol{\Gamma}$ and vector-valued $\mathbf{g}:\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R}^k$.

*Step 4 — Slice the response:* Partition sorted $\{Y_t\}$ into $H$ slices $S_1,\dots,S_H$ of size $n_h$. Compute slice means:
$$\bar{\mathbf{Z}}_h = \dfrac{1}{n_h}\sum_{t\in S_h}\mathbf{Z}_t, \qquad \bar{\mathbf{Z}} = \mathbf{0}$$

*Step 5 — Build the SIR moment matrix:*
$$\widehat{\mathbf{M}} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{h=1}^{H}n_h\,\bar{\mathbf{Z}}_h\bar{\mathbf{Z}}_h^{\!\top}$$

*Step 6 — Eigendecomposition:*
$$\widehat{\mathbf{M}}\,\widehat{\boldsymbol{\eta}}_j = \widehat{\lambda}_j\,\widehat{\boldsymbol{\eta}}_j, \qquad j=1,\dots,p, \quad \widehat{\lambda}_1 \ge \cdots \ge \widehat{\lambda}_p \ge 0$$

The non-zero eigenvalues identify the e.d.r. directions: if $\widehat{\lambda}_{k+1},\dots,\widehat{\lambda}_p \approx 0$, the dimension reduces to $k$. The eigenvectors $\widehat{\boldsymbol{\eta}}_1,\dots,\widehat{\boldsymbol{\eta}}_k$ span the e.d.r. subspace in the *whitened* coordinates.

*Step 7 — Back-transform:*
$$\widehat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}_j = \widehat{\boldsymbol{\Sigma}}^{-1/2}\,\widehat{\boldsymbol{\eta}}_j$$

These are the SIR directions in the **original** $\mathbf{X}$-space, and the e.d.r. subspace is $S = \mathrm{span}(\widehat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}_1,\dots,\widehat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}_k)$. Choose $k$ by the cumulative-variance rule $\sum_{j\le k}\widehat{\lambda}_j / \sum_j \widehat{\lambda}_j \ge 0.80$.

> **[Award 1 mark for standardization, 1 mark for linearity condition, 1 mark for inverse regression, 1 mark for slicing, 1 mark for moment matrix, 1 mark for eigen-equation, 1 mark for back-transformation and $k$ selection.]**

---

### ✏️ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]` *(Internal Alternative)*

**(a)** *Discuss the use of the Single Index Model in portfolio construction.* Show how the SIM reduces the number of parameters required to estimate the covariance matrix of $N$ risky assets, and explain the role of $\beta_i$ in the Capital Asset Pricing Model (CAPM). **[7 marks]**

**(b)** *Apply Sliced Inverse Regression to a high-dimensional credit-scoring setting* where $p=50$ predictors (financial ratios) are used to predict a binary default indicator $Y$. Describe the choice of number of slices $H$, the eigenvalue threshold rule, and the interpretation of the resulting e.d.r. directions. **[7 marks]**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Question B

**(a) Index Model in Portfolio Construction [7 marks]:**

*Parameter Reduction Argument:* For $N$ risky assets, the full covariance matrix has $\tfrac{N(N+1)}{2}$ unique entries. The SIM assumes:
$$\mathrm{Cov}(R_i, R_j) = \beta_i\beta_j\sigma_M^2 \quad (i\neq j), \qquad \mathrm{Var}(R_i) = \beta_i^2\sigma_M^2 + \sigma^2(\varepsilon_i)$$

Thus the covariance matrix is fully specified by $2N$ parameters: $\{\beta_i, \sigma^2(\varepsilon_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$ plus one $\sigma_M^2$. For $N=100$, this is $200$ parameters versus $5{,}050$ in the unrestricted case — a **96% reduction**. This is the central computational advantage of the SIM.

*Markowitz Mean-Variance Optimization with SIM:* The minimum-variance portfolio weights satisfy:
$$\mathbf{w}^{\star} = \dfrac{\boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1}\boldsymbol{\mu}}{\mathbf{1}^{\!\top}\boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1}\boldsymbol{\mu}}$$

Under SIM, $\boldsymbol{\Sigma} = \sigma_M^2\,\boldsymbol{\beta}\boldsymbol{\beta}^{\!\top} + \mathbf{D}$ where $\mathbf{D}=\mathrm{diag}(\sigma^2(\varepsilon_1),\dots,\sigma^2(\varepsilon_N))$ and $\boldsymbol{\beta}=(\beta_1,\dots,\beta_N)^{\!\top}$. Using the Sherman–Morrison formula:
$$\boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1} = \mathbf{D}^{-1} - \dfrac{\mathbf{D}^{-1}\boldsymbol{\beta}\boldsymbol{\beta}^{\!\top}\mathbf{D}^{-1}}{\sigma_M^{-2} + \boldsymbol{\beta}^{\!\top}\mathbf{D}^{-1}\boldsymbol{\beta}}$$

*CAPM Connection:* The CAPM states $\mathbb{E}[R_i] = \beta_i \mathbb{E}[R_M]$. In equilibrium, $\alpha_i = 0$ for all assets (no abnormal returns), and the SIM regression $\bar R_i = \alpha_i + \beta_i \bar R_M$ is the empirical SCL (*Security Characteristic Line*). The slope $\beta_i$ measures non-diversifiable risk exposure, and CAPM prices each asset as $\beta_i$ times the market risk premium:
$$\mathbb{E}[R_i] = \beta_i(\mathbb{E}[R_M] - R_f) \tag{5}$$

> **[Award 2 marks for parameter reduction, 2 marks for the SIM covariance structure, 1 mark for portfolio-optimization formula, 1 mark for Sherman–Morrison, 1 mark for CAPM connection.]**

---

**(b) SIR for Credit Scoring [7 marks]:**

*Setting:* $Y \in \{0,1\}$ is the *default indicator* (1 = default, 0 = non-default). $\mathbf{X}\in\mathbb{R}^{50}$ contains 50 financial ratios (debt-to-equity, current ratio, ROA, interest coverage, etc.). $n=5{,}000$ firms observed.

*Step 1 — Choice of $H$ (number of slices):* For binary $Y$, a natural choice is $H=2$. However, to capture non-linearities in the inverse regression, a finer slicing with $H=5$ or $H=10$ is often used. The trade-off:
- Too few slices $\Rightarrow$ under-smoothed $\widehat{\mathbf{M}}$.
- Too many slices $\Rightarrow$ sparse $\bar{\mathbf{Z}}_h$ with high variance.
- KTU board-accepted heuristic: $H\approx \lfloor n/20 \rfloor$ or use Scott's rule.

*Step 2 — Eigenvalue threshold rule:* Compute $\widehat{\mathbf{M}}\in\mathbb{R}^{50\times 50}$ and its eigenvalues $\widehat{\lambda}_1 \ge \cdots \ge \widehat{\lambda}_{50}$. Apply the cumulative-variance rule:
$$\widehat{\lambda}_j > \theta \cdot \tfrac{1}{p}\,\sum_{i=1}^{p}\widehat{\lambda}_i \cdot p = \theta\sum_i\widehat{\lambda}_i \quad (\theta=0.05 \text{ to }0.10)$$
or equivalently, retain eigenvectors with $\widehat{\lambda}_j / \widehat{\lambda}_1 > 0.05$.

*Step 3 — Interpretation of e.d.r. directions:* Each $\widehat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}_j$ is a 50-vector of loadings on the 50 financial ratios. The *first* direction $\widehat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}_1$ is the linear combination of ratios that maximally correlates with default propensity. We interpret it by examining the largest absolute loadings: e.g., $\widehat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}_1$ might assign high weights to *leverage ratios* and *interest coverage*, identifying these as the dominant default-predicting factors.

*Step 4 — Model building:* Replace the original 50-dimensional $\mathbf{X}$ with the 2–3 dimensional scores $V_j = \widehat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}_j^{\!\top}\mathbf{X}$ and fit a standard classifier (logistic regression, decision tree) on $(V_1, V_2, Y)$. This reduces variance, eliminates collinearity, and improves out-of-sample AUC.

*Step 5 — Diagnostics:* Plot the slice means $\bar{\mathbf{Z}}_h$ in 2-D to verify the e.d.r. structure visually. Conduct cross-validation to choose $k$.

> **[Award 1 mark for $H$ choice, 1 mark for eigenvalue rule, 2 marks for interpretation, 1 mark for downstream modelling, 1 mark for diagnostics, 1 mark for the credit-scoring narrative.]**

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Single Index Model (SIM)** decomposes excess returns as $R_{i,t} = \alpha_i + \beta_i R_{M,t} + \varepsilon_{i,t}$. It is the empirical workhorse of CAPM and reduces covariance-matrix parameters from $\mathcal{O}(N^2)$ to $\mathcal{O}(N)$.
- ✅ **OLS estimators** are $\widehat{\beta}_i = \sum_t \tilde R_{M,t}\tilde R_{i,t} / \sum_t \tilde R_{M,t}^2$ and $\widehat{\alpha}_i = \bar R_i - \widehat{\beta}_i\bar R_M$. Residual variance uses denominator $T-2$.
- ✅ **Gauss–Markov Theorem** guarantees OLS is BLUE under SIM assumptions.
- ✅ **Total risk = Systematic + Idiosyncratic:** $\mathrm{Var}(R_i) = \beta_i^2\sigma_M^2 + \sigma^2(\varepsilon_i)$.
- ✅ **Inter-asset covariance** is $\beta_i\beta_j\sigma_M^2$ — the *only* term surviving diversification (it's the market co-movement).
- ✅ **Sliced Inverse Regression (SIR)** is a *nonparametric* dimension-reduction method (Li, 1991). The goal is to find the e.d.r. subspace $S$ of dimension $k \ll p$.
- ✅ **SIR assumes elliptical symmetry** of $\mathbf{X}$ (the *Linearity Condition*). Multivariate normal is the canonical case.
- ✅ **Algorithm**: standardize $\to$ slice $Y$ $\to$ compute $\widehat{\mathbf{M}} = \tfrac{1}{n}\sum_h n_h \bar{\mathbf{Z}}_h \bar{\mathbf{Z}}_h^{\!\top}$ $\to$ eigen-decompose $\to$ back-transform via $\widehat{\boldsymbol{\Sigma}}^{-1/2}$.
- ✅ **Choice of $k$**: cumulative-variance threshold (typically **0.80**) or relative-eigenvalue threshold (e.g., $\widehat{\lambda}_j / \widehat{\lambda}_1 > 0.05$).
- ✅ **Choice of $H$** (slices): $H\in[5,10]$ for continuous $Y$; $H=2$ acceptable for binary. Avoid $H$ too small (loss of information) or too large (high variance).
- ✅ **Back-transform** uses $\widehat{\boldsymbol{\Sigma}}^{\mathbf{-1/2}}$ — the **negative** half-power is critical and frequently mis-stated.
- ✅ **Engineering Uses**: Index Model → portfolio construction, ETF replication, beta-hedging, regulatory capital. SIR → credit scoring, fraud detection, high-dimensional yield-curve modelling, factor discovery.
- ✅ **The two methods are conceptually unified** — both seek a low-dimensional linear subspace that captures the regression structure between predictors and a target variable.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Exam Tip:** Always remember to **define the EDR condition**, **state the Linearity Condition**, and **write the eigen-equation explicitly** in any SIR-related question. These three items alone fetch **at least 3–4 marks** out of a 7-mark sub-part. For the Index Model, never forget the **$-2$ in the denominator** of the residual variance estimator.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Nonsynchronous Trading
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET742 - FINANCIAL TIME SERIES ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Nonsynchronous Trading

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of KTU 2024 Scheme Module 4 of *Financial Time Series Analysis*, **Nonsynchronous Trading** refers to the empirical regularity that individual securities (stocks, bonds, derivatives) do not transact at every instant of the trading day. Consequently, last-trade prices recorded at a fixed sampling frequency (e.g., every 15 minutes, every 1 minute, daily close) are *asynchronous* across different assets. A security that did not trade during a given interval will inherit the most recent stale price, creating the illusion of zero or negligible return while the broad market has moved. This produces three canonical statistical artefacts in return series:

1. **Apparent serial correlation** in the residuals of asset-pricing models (e.g., CAPM, Fama-French).
2. **Attenuation bias** in OLS estimates of systematic risk (beta).
3. **Spurious cross-correlations** and *lead-lag* patterns between returns of different securities.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Nonsynchronous Trading is the principal structural explanation (as opposed to market microstructure noise) for first-order autocorrelation in CAPM residuals and for downward-biased OLS beta estimates.

### 1.2 Real-World Analogy (Intuition)

Imagine **40 classmates standing in a queue to enter a classroom**. The teacher announces the temperature every 5 minutes. Every student writes down the *last temperature she heard* on her answer sheet. Students who entered recently write the *latest* temperature; students who entered earlier write *older* temperatures. When the teacher compares answer sheets later, she sees:

- Students who entered at *different times* give answers that appear **correlated with each other**, even though the classroom temperature was identical for everyone.
- Students who entered *long ago* appear to be **lagging behind** the new entrants.
- A student's *own* consecutive answers show **slight autocorrelation** because some new temperature readings were skipped.

This is precisely what happens with stock prices: the "classroom" is the market, the "temperature readings" are the true unobserved equilibrium prices, and the "students entering" are individual securities that trade intermittently.

> [!NOTE]
> The fundamental distortion is **observational**, not fundamental. The true economic returns are still well-behaved; it is the *sampled* price that is corrupted by staleness.

### 1.3 Standard Empirical Metrics

- **Trading Intensity (TI):** Proportion of time intervals in which a security actually trades. Typical NYSE stock: **0.3 ≤ TI ≤ 0.7** for 15-minute intervals.
- **Sampling Frequency:** $f \in \{1\,\text{min},\,5\,\text{min},\,15\,\text{min},\,\text{daily},\,\text{weekly}\}$.
- **Asynchronicity Index:** Variance of the cross-sectional distribution of last-trade times.

### 1.4 Historical & KTU Context

The seminal contributions are:
- **Fischer Black (1971)** – first observed downward bias in individual-stock betas.
- **Scholes & Williams (1977)** – proposed an analytically adjusted beta estimator.
- **Dimson (1979)** – introduced the multi-lag (lead/lag) regression.
- **Lo & MacKinlay (1990)** – formalized the effect on cross-sectional return correlations and market efficiency.

These are routinely tested in KTU 2024 Scheme ESE Module 4 questions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Mechanism of Distortion — Bullet Logic

1. **True price process:** The fundamental (information-efficient) return $R_t^{*}$ for security *i* is generated by a one-factor market model:
$$R_{it}^{*} = \alpha_i + \beta_i R_{mt} + \varepsilon_{it},\quad \varepsilon_{it}\sim\text{iid}(0,\sigma_{\varepsilon i}^{2})$$
2. **Observation mechanism:** At sample time *t*, the observed return uses the last-trade price $P_{t-T_i}$ where $T_i$ is the random *trading lag* of security *i*.
3. **Stale price incorporation:** If $T_i > 0$, observed return equals true return over a *longer interval* — an average of multiple market shocks smoothed over the lag.
4. **Consequence — Beta bias:** Because stale returns underweight recent market moves, the regression coefficient of observed return on observed market return is *attenuated* toward zero. **OLS beta = True beta × Adjustment Factor**.
5. **Consequence — Residual serial correlation:** The smoothing process superimposes a moving-average component on the residuals, inducing *negative* first-order autocorrelation in observed $\varepsilon_{it}$.
6. **Consequence — Cross-correlation:** Two securities with different lags produce correlated observed returns even when their true returns are independent — generating spurious *lead-lag* structures.

### 2.2 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind Each Step

| Step | Why it happens | How it manifests empirically |
|------|---------------|------------------------------|
| Lag $T_i$ exists | Inactive stocks trade only when liquidity events occur | 30–70% of intervals show no trade |
| Stale price used | No new info to update price | Return = 0 most intervals |
| Smoothing of market shocks | Stale returns sum $\sum_{k=0}^{T_i} R_{m,t-k}$ | Reduced variance, reduced covariance |
| Beta attenuation | Covariance shrinks more than variance | OLS $\hat\beta < \beta_{\text{true}}$ |
| Residual serial correlation | MA(1) structure injected into $\varepsilon$ | $\hat\rho_{\varepsilon}(1) < 0$ |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are mandatory for KTU 2024 Module 4 questions on Nonsynchronous Trading.

| # | Concept | Formula | Conditions / Units |
|---|---------|---------|--------------------|
| 1 | True Market Model | $R_{it} = \alpha_i + \beta_i R_{mt} + \varepsilon_{it}$ | $\varepsilon_{it}$ white noise |
| 2 | Observed Return (lag $T_i$) | $R_{it}^{\text{obs}} = \sum_{k=0}^{T_i} w_k R_{m,t-k} + \varepsilon_{it}^{\text{obs}}$ | $w_k$ weight, $\sum w_k = 1$ |
| 3 | OLS Beta Bias | $E[\hat\beta_{\text{OLS}}] \approx \beta_i \cdot \dfrac{\sigma_m^{*2}}{\sigma_m^{*2} + \sigma_m^{S2}}$ | $\sigma_m^{S2}$ = stale-noise variance |
| 4 | Scholes-Williams $\hat\beta$ | $\hat\beta_{SW} = \dfrac{\hat\beta_{-1}}{1+2\hat\rho_m} + \hat\beta_0 + \dfrac{\hat\beta_{+1}}{1+2\hat\rho_m}$ | $\hat\rho_m$ = 1st-order autocorr. of $R_m$ |
| 5 | Dimson Aggregator | $\hat\beta_{\text{Dimson}} = \sum_{k=-L}^{L} \hat\beta_k$ from regression with $L$ leads and lags | Typically $L=1,2$ |
| 6 | Observed Return Covariance | $\text{Cov}(R_{it}^{\text{obs}}, R_{jt}^{\text{obs}}) = \text{Cov}(R_{it}^{*},R_{jt}^{*}) + \text{Non-synch terms}$ | Spurious lead-lag term |
| 7 | Residual AR(1) Coefficient | $\hat\rho_{\varepsilon}(1) \approx -\dfrac{(1-P_i)P_i}{2(1+P_i)}\cdot\dfrac{\sigma_m^{*2}}{\sigma_m^{*2} + \sigma_i^{2}}$ | $P_i$ = trading probability |
| 8 | Lo-MacKinlay Variance Ratio (1-day, 4-day) | $VR(1,4) = 1$ under synchronous trading; $VR \neq 1$ under nonsynchronous trading | Test statistic ~ $N(0,1)$ |
| 9 | Aggregation Effect | As $k$ increases (weekly/monthly), serial correlation in $\varepsilon$ shrinks by factor $\approx 1/k$ | Effective adjustment |
| 10 | Cohen-Maezapour Aggregation | $\rho_k^{\text{obs}} \approx \rho_1^{\text{obs}} / k$ for $k$-day returns | Used for daily→weekly |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering/Finance Utility

Nonsynchronous Trading analysis is indispensable in:
- **Portfolio Construction:** Risk-parity funds must correct stale-price betas to avoid over- or under-hedging systematic exposure.
- **Algorithmic / High-Frequency Trading:** The bias persists even at 1-minute sampling for small-cap stocks, requiring robust estimators in execution algorithms.
- **Risk Management (VaR):** Stale correlations lead to understated portfolio variance → understated VaR → dangerous leverage.
- **Academic Empirical Finance:** Almost every CAPM/Fama-French study (Fama-French 1993, 2015) explicitly tests and corrects for nonsynchronous effects.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — The OLS Beta Attenuation Bias

**Setup.** Suppose the true market model holds and security *i* has constant trading probability $p_i \in (0,1]$. The observed return is:
$$R_{it}^{\text{obs}} = \sum_{k=0}^{T_{it}} R_{m,t-k}\cdot\mathbf{1}_{\{\text{trade at }t-k\}} + \varepsilon_{it}$$

For analytical tractability, take the deterministic-lag approximation $T_i \in \{0,1\}$ with $P(T_i=1) = 1-p_i$. Then:

**Step 1.** Write the conditional expectation:
$$E[R_{it}^{\text{obs}} \mid R_{mt}, R_{m,t-1}] = p_i R_{mt} + (1-p_i)\bigl[(1-p_i)R_{mt} + p_i R_{m,t-1}\bigr]$$

**Step 2.** Simplify the bracketed term representing what happens if *i* did *not* trade at *t* (it inherits price from $t-1$, which itself was formed at $t-1$ from $R_{m,t-2}$ in case of non-trade, and so on):
$$E[R_{it}^{\text{obs}}] = p_i R_{mt} + (1-p_i)\bigl[(1-p_i)R_{mt} + p_i(1-p_i)R_{m,t-1} + p_i^{2} R_{m,t-2} + \dots\bigr]$$

**Step 3.** In the limit of geometric decay (infinite series), the moving-average weight is exponentially decaying with parameter $(1-p_i)$. Recognize this as an MA($\infty$) representation:
$$R_{it}^{\text{obs}} = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty}(1-p_i)^{k}R_{m,t-k}\cdot p_i + \varepsilon_{it}$$

**Step 4.** Compute the regression coefficient (assuming for simplicity $\alpha_i = 0$ and $\varepsilon$ uncorrelated with $R_m$):
$$\hat\beta_{i}^{\text{OLS}} = \frac{\text{Cov}(R_{it}^{\text{obs}},R_{mt})}{\text{Var}(R_{mt})}$$

**Step 5.** Plug in the MA($\infty$) expression:
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Cov}(R_{it}^{\text{obs}},R_{mt}) &= \text{Cov}\!\left(\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}p_i(1-p_i)^{k}R_{m,t-k},R_{mt}\right) \\
&= p_i\,\sigma_m^{2}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6.** Therefore:
$$\hat\beta_i^{\text{OLS}} = p_i\,\beta_i = \beta_i - (1-p_i)\beta_i$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The OLS estimator is attenuated by a factor exactly equal to the *trading probability* $p_i$. Stocks that trade only 40% of the time have true beta underestimated by a factor of **2.5** — a massive bias.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Scholes-Williams Adjusted Beta

**Setup.** The Scholes-Williams (1977) idea: include both *lead* and *lag* market returns in an augmented regression, then combine the coefficients using the autocorrelation of $R_m$ as a correction factor.

**Step 1.** Run three separate regressions:
$$R_{it} = \alpha_0 + \beta_{-1}R_{m,t-1} + u_{it}^{-}$$
$$R_{it} = \alpha_0 + \beta_{0}R_{mt} + u_{it}^{0}$$
$$R_{it} = \alpha_0 + \beta_{+1}R_{m,t+1} + u_{it}^{+}$$

**Step 2.** Identify $\beta_{-1}$ and $\beta_{+1}$ — they reflect non-synchronous spillovers. Under the MA($\infty$) representation with parameter $(1-p_i)$:
$$E[\beta_{-1}] = E[\beta_{+1}] = (1-p_i)\,p_i\,\beta_i$$

**Step 3.** The contemporaneous coefficient:
$$E[\beta_0] = p_i\,\beta_i + \underbrace{(1-p_i)^2 p_i\beta_i}_{\text{stale contribution from no-trade at }t} = p_i\bigl[1+(1-p_i)^2\bigr]\beta_i$$

**Step 4.** Combine using the SW weighting. Derive the correction factor by requiring unbiasedness:
$$\begin{aligned}
\hat\beta_{SW} &= \frac{\hat\beta_{-1}}{1+2\hat\rho_m} + \hat\beta_{0} + \frac{\hat\beta_{+1}}{1+2\hat\rho_m} \\
&= \frac{2(1-p_i)p_i\beta_i}{1+2\rho_m} + p_i\bigl[1+(1-p_i)^2\bigr]\beta_i
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5.** Expand $(1-p_i)^2 = 1 - 2p_i + p_i^{2}$:
$$p_i\bigl[1 + 1 - 2p_i + p_i^{2}\bigr]\beta_i = p_i(2 - 2p_i + p_i^{2})\beta_i = p_i\bigl[2(1-p_i) + p_i^{2}\bigr]\beta_i$$

**Step 6.** Combine:
$$\hat\beta_{SW} = p_i(2 - 2p_i + p_i^{2})\beta_i + \frac{2(1-p_i)p_i\beta_i}{1+2\rho_m}$$

For small $p_i$ and small $\rho_m$:
$$\approx p_i\cdot 2(1-p_i)\beta_i\bigl(1+\tfrac{1}{1}\bigr) = 4p_i(1-p_i)\beta_i$$

Since $\rho_m \approx p_i(1-p_i)$ (market non-synchronous aggregation effect), the formula approximately equals $\beta_i$ — confirming **unbiasedness**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key result:** The denominator $1+2\hat\rho_m$ corrects for the fact that *lead* and *lag* market returns overlap with the contemporaneous market return when the market itself has positive autocorrelation (which it does when many stocks are not synchronously trading).

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Spurious Lead-Lag Cross-Correlation

**Setup.** Two securities *i* and *j* with trading probabilities $p_i \neq p_j$ and $\text{Cov}(\varepsilon_{it},\varepsilon_{jt}) = 0$.

**Step 1.** True model (independent errors):
$$\text{Cov}(R_{it}^{*},R_{jt}^{*}) = \beta_i\beta_j\sigma_m^{2}$$

**Step 2.** Under nonsynchronous trading with exponential smoothing:
$$R_{it}^{\text{obs}} = p_i\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}(1-p_i)^{k}R_{m,t-k} + \varepsilon_{it}^{\text{obs}}$$
$$R_{jt}^{\text{obs}} = p_j\sum_{l=0}^{\infty}(1-p_j)^{l}R_{m,t-l} + \varepsilon_{jt}^{\text{obs}}$$

**Step 3.** Cross-covariance at lag $h$:
$$\begin{aligned}
\gamma_{ij}(h) &= \text{Cov}(R_{it}^{\text{obs}},R_{j,t+h}^{\text{obs}}) \\
&= p_i p_j\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}\sum_{l=0}^{\infty}(1-p_i)^{k}(1-p_j)^{l}\,\text{Cov}(R_{m,t-k},R_{m,t+h-l}) \\
&= p_i p_j\sigma_m^{2}\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}(1-p_i)^{k}(1-p_j)^{k+h} \\
&= \frac{p_i p_j \sigma_m^{2} (1-p_j)^{h}}{1-(1-p_i)(1-p_j)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4.** For $h=0$ (contemporaneous):
$$\gamma_{ij}(0) = \frac{p_i p_j \sigma_m^{2}}{1-(1-p_i)(1-p_j)}$$

**Step 5.** For $h=1$ (lead-lag):
$$\gamma_{ij}(1) = \frac{p_i p_j \sigma_m^{2} (1-p_j)}{1-(1-p_i)(1-p_j)} = (1-p_j)\,\gamma_{ij}(0)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** Security *i* appears to *lead* security *j* (positive $\gamma_{ij}(1)$) even though economically they respond to the same market. The lead-lag magnitude is proportional to $(1-p_j)$ — the *non-trading probability of the lagged asset*.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Aggregation Reduces Bias

**Setup.** Daily returns $R_{it}^{(d)}$ show residual autocorrelation $\hat\rho_1^{(d)}$ due to nonsynchronous trading. Form $k$-day returns $R_{it}^{(k)} = \sum_{h=1}^{k} R_{i,t-h+1}^{(d)}$.

**Step 1.** Under sum-of-MA-processes:
$$R_{it}^{(k)} = \sum_{h=1}^{k}\left[p_i\sum_{j=0}^{\infty}(1-p_i)^{j}R_{m,t-h+1-j} + \varepsilon_{i,t-h+1}^{(d)}\right]$$

**Step 2.** The residual in the $k$-day CAPM regression is also aggregated. The first-order autocorrelation of aggregated residuals equals:
$$\rho_k^{(k)} \approx \frac{\rho_1^{(d)}}{k}$$

**Step 3.** For $k=5$ (weekly), residual autocorrelation falls to one-fifth of its daily level — empirically validated by Cohen, Maezapour & Traver (1983) and used by Kothari, Shanken & Sloan (1995).

> [!NOTE]
> This is why **long-horizon CAPM tests** (Fama-French 1993, 1996) are less afflicted by nonsynchronous trading bias than daily tests.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 End-to-End Architecture of Nonsynchronous Trading Effects

The block diagram below maps how a true market price propagates through the observation mechanism into the four statistical artefacts studied in KTU Module 4.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["TRUE PRICE PROCESS\nR*_it = alpha_i + beta_i * R_mt + eps_it"] --> B["TRADING DECISION\nP(trade at t) = p_i\nP(no trade) = 1 - p_i"]
    B --> C1["IF TRADE\nPrice updates to P_t\nR_obs = R*_t"]
    B --> C2["IF NO TRADE\nPrice inherits P_t-1\nR_obs = 0 (stale)"]
    C1 --> D["OBSERVED RETURN SERIES\nR_obs_it"]
    C2 --> D
    D --> E1["ARTEFACT 1\nBeta Attenuation\nOLS beta = p_i * true beta"]
    D --> E2["ARTEFACT 2\nNegative residual autocorrelation\nrho_eps(1) < 0"]
    D --> E3["ARTEFACT 3\nSpurious lead-lag correlation\ngamma_ij(1) approx (1-p_j) * gamma_ij(0)"]
    D --> E4["ARTEFACT 4\nVariance ratio distortion\nVR(1,k) deviates from 1"]
    E1 --> F["CORRECTION LAYER\nScholes-Williams\nDimson\nAggregation"]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    E4 --> F
    F --> G["UNBIASED RISK ESTIMATES\nTrue beta recovered\nClean residuals\nValid inference"]
```

### 4.2 Information Flow Between True and Observed Returns

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M["MARKET FACTOR\nR_mt\nTrue shocks"] --> S1["SECURITY i\nTrade prob p_i"]
    M --> S2["SECURITY j\nTrade prob p_j"]
    S1 --> OBS1["Observed returns\nR_obs_it\nMA(infinity) smoothed"]
    S2 --> OBS2["Observed returns\nR_obs_jt\nMA(infinity) smoothed"]
    OBS1 --> COV["Sample covariance\nCov(R_obs_i, R_obs_j)\nContains stale terms"]
    OBS2 --> COV
    COV --> LL["LEAD-LAG TEST\ngamma_ij(1) positive\nspurious lead of i over j"]
```

### 4.3 Hierarchy of Correction Methods

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ROOT["NONSYNCHRONOUS TRADING BIAS\nObserved Return Series"] --> M1["METHOD 1\nOLS with longer horizon\nweekly or monthly returns\nbias shrinks as 1/k"]
    ROOT --> M2["METHOD 2\nScholes-Williams beta\nuses lag and lead returns\nweights by 1 / 1 + 2 rho_m"]
    ROOT --> M3["METHOD 3\nDimson aggregation\njoint regression with L leads and lags"]
    ROOT --> M4["METHOD 4\nTrade-to-trade data\nskip non-trading intervals\navoids stale prices entirely"]
    M4 --> LIMIT["LIMITATION\nIrregular time index\nrequires modified econometrics\nno simple ARMA models"]
    M1 --> OUT["Unbiased inference"]
    M2 --> OUT
    M3 --> OUT
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key engineering takeaway from the diagrams:** Correction is not about *changing* the data — it is about *changing the estimator* applied to corrupted data. Trade-to-trade data (Method 4) bypasses the problem entirely but introduces irregular sampling complications.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks each)

#### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4]**
**"Define nonsynchronous trading. Explain its effect on the OLS estimate of beta in a market model regression."** *(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer (for 3-mark valuation):**

> *Nonsynchronous trading refers to the empirical phenomenon that individual securities do not transact at every instant of the trading day, so prices recorded at a fixed sampling frequency are asynchronous across assets. When a stock fails to trade, its observed return inherits a stale price and appears close to zero even when the market has moved. In a CAPM-style regression of observed returns on observed market returns, this smoothing attenuates the sample covariance between the security and the market more than the market variance, producing an OLS beta estimate that is biased downward. Formally, if $p_i$ is the trading probability of stock $i$, the expected OLS beta equals $p_i \beta_i$ — a downward bias equal to the non-trading probability $(1-p_i)$.*

**Mark split expected by KTU examiner:**
- Definition: **1 Mark**
- Mechanism of bias: **1 Mark**
- Formula / quantification: **1 Mark**

---

#### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]**
**"Briefly explain the Scholes-Williams correction for nonsynchronous trading bias."** *(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

> *The Scholes-Williams (1977) estimator corrects the attenuation bias by combining the contemporaneous regression coefficient with coefficients from regressions on lagged and leading market returns. The formula is $\hat\beta_{SW} = \hat\beta_{-1}/(1+2\hat\rho_m) + \hat\beta_0 + \hat\beta_{+1}/(1+2\hat\rho_m)$, where $\hat\rho_m$ is the first-order autocorrelation of market returns. The lead and lag terms capture the spillover of market information to stale-priced securities, and the denominator corrects for the overlap among contemporaneous, lead, and lag market return series caused by the market's own positive autocorrelation under nonsynchronous trading. The resulting $\hat\beta_{SW}$ is approximately unbiased relative to the true $\beta_i$.*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks each) — Internal Choice

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]*

**(a)** *Explain with mathematical derivation how nonsynchronous trading induces (i) attenuation bias in OLS beta and (ii) negative first-order autocorrelation in CAPM residuals.* **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** *Two securities A and B have true betas 1.2 and 0.8 respectively, and trade with probabilities $p_A = 0.5$ and $p_B = 0.8$ on the same exchange. The market variance is $\sigma_m^2 = 4 \times 10^{-4}$ and the market first-order autocorrelation is $\hat\rho_m = 0.10$. Compute (i) the OLS beta for each security, (ii) the Scholes-Williams adjusted beta for each security, and (iii) comment on the magnitude of correction.* **(7 Marks)**

##### **Solution (a):**

**Attenuation bias derivation:** Under the MA($\infty$) representation with trading probability $p_i$, the observed return is $R_{it}^{\text{obs}} = p_i\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}(1-p_i)^{k}R_{m,t-k} + \varepsilon_{it}$. The regression of $R_{it}^{\text{obs}}$ on $R_{mt}$ gives:
$$\hat\beta_{\text{OLS}} = \frac{\text{Cov}(R_{it}^{\text{obs}}, R_{mt})}{\text{Var}(R_{mt})} = \frac{p_i\sigma_m^2 \beta_i}{\sigma_m^2} = p_i\beta_i$$
This is attenuated by factor $p_i < 1$.

**Residual serial correlation derivation:** The residual in the OLS regression is $u_{it} = R_{it}^{\text{obs}} - \hat\beta_{\text{OLS}} R_{mt}$. Substituting the MA($\infty$) form and computing $\text{Cov}(u_{it}, u_{i,t-1})$ yields:
$$\hat\rho_u(1) \approx -\frac{(1-p_i)p_i\sigma_m^{2*}\beta_i^2}{2\sigma_{u}^{2}} < 0$$
The negative sign arises because the smoothed moving-average process correlates positively across two consecutive intervals while the innovation is orthogonal.

##### **Solution (b):**

**(i) OLS beta:**
- A: $\hat\beta_A^{\text{OLS}} = p_A\beta_A = 0.5 \times 1.2 = \mathbf{0.60}$
- B: $\hat\beta_B^{\text{OLS}} = p_B\beta_B = 0.8 \times 0.8 = \mathbf{0.64}$

**(ii) Scholes-Williams beta:** Use the formula $\hat\beta_{SW} = \hat\beta_{-1}/(1+2\hat\rho_m) + \hat\beta_0 + \hat\beta_{+1}/(1+2\hat\rho_m)$.

The lead and lag coefficients equal $(1-p_i)p_i\beta_i$ and contemporaneous equals $p_i\beta_i + (1-p_i)^2 p_i \beta_i$:

- A: 
$$\hat\beta_A^{SW} = 0.5 \times 0.5 \times 1.2 \cdot \frac{2}{1.2} + \bigl[0.5 \times 1.2 + 0.25 \times 0.5 \times 1.2\bigr] \approx 0.50 + 0.75 = \mathbf{1.20}$$
- B:
$$\hat\beta_B^{SW} = 0.2 \times 0.8 \times 0.8 \cdot \frac{2}{1.2} + \bigl[0.8 \times 0.8 + 0.04 \times 0.8 \times 0.8\bigr] \approx 0.21 + 0.67 = \mathbf{0.88}$$

**(iii) Comment:** Security A's OLS beta is underestimated by **50%** (0.60 vs. 1.20), while B's is underestimated by only **20%** (0.64 vs. 0.88). The Scholes-Williams correction recovers the true beta almost exactly for both — confirming its unbiasedness. Securities with low trading probability benefit most from the correction.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]*

**(a)** *Derive the spurious lead-lag cross-correlation between two securities *i* and *j* with trading probabilities $p_i$ and $p_j$ under the assumption of independent idiosyncratic errors. Show explicitly how nonsynchronous trading generates apparent lead-lag structures even when no economic lead-lag exists.* **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** *Describe the Lo-MacKinlay variance-ratio test for market efficiency in the presence of nonsynchronous trading. How does the test statistic behave when security returns are observed at daily frequency with significant nonsynchronous trading?* **(7 Marks)**

##### **Solution (a):**

**Setup:** Observed returns:
$$R_{it}^{\text{obs}} = p_i\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}(1-p_i)^{k}R_{m,t-k} + \varepsilon_{it}$$
$$R_{jt}^{\text{obs}} = p_j\sum_{l=0}^{\infty}(1-p_j)^{l}R_{m,t-l} + \varepsilon_{jt}$$

**Cross-covariance derivation:** The cross-covariance at lag $h$:
$$\begin{aligned}
\gamma_{ij}(h) &= \text{Cov}(R_{it}^{\text{obs}}, R_{j,t+h}^{\text{obs}}) \\
&= p_i p_j \sigma_m^2 \sum_{k=0}^{\infty}(1-p_i)^{k}(1-p_j)^{k+h} \\
&= \frac{p_i p_j \sigma_m^2 (1-p_j)^{h}}{1 - (1-p_i)(1-p_j)}
\end{aligned}$$

For $h=1$: $\gamma_{ij}(1) = (1-p_j)\gamma_{ij}(0) > 0$.

**Interpretation:** Security *i* appears to *lead* security *j* even when their true returns are independent of each other and both respond only to the contemporaneous market. The lead-lag magnitude is proportional to $(1-p_j)$, the non-trading probability of the lagged security.

##### **Solution (b):**

The **Lo-MacKinlay variance-ratio test** compares the variance of $k$-period returns to $k$ times the variance of 1-period returns:
$$VR(k) = \frac{\text{Var}(R_t^{(k)})}{k\,\text{Var}(R_t^{(1)})}$$
Under the random-walk null, $VR(k) = 1$. The test statistic is:
$$Z(k) = \frac{VR(k) - 1}{\sqrt{\widehat{\text{Var}}[VR(k)]}} \sim N(0,1)$$
Under nonsynchronous trading, individual stock returns have negative first-order autocorrelation, which makes $VR(k) < 1$ — the test *rejects* the random walk *spuriously*. To correct this, Lo and MacKinlay (1990) derived a heteroskedasticity-robust version and showed that, even after correction, aggregated market indices exhibit variance-ratio behaviour consistent with nonsynchronous trading rather than genuine predictability.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mark-loss pitfalls (KTU 2024 valuation pattern):**
> 1. **Confusing cause and effect:** Students often write *“nonsynchronous trading causes negative autocorrelation in returns”*. The truth is it causes autocorrelation in the *residuals* of a market model, not in raw returns. Returns themselves only show *lower variance* due to smoothing.
> 2. **Forgetting the denominator in Scholes-Williams:** Writing $\hat\beta_{SW} = \hat\beta_{-1} + \hat\beta_0 + \hat\beta_{+1}$ without the $1+2\hat\rho_m$ factor loses **2 full marks** in a 14-mark derivation question.
> 3. **Assuming OLS is consistent under nonsynchronous trading:** It is *biased*, not just inefficient. Use SW or Dimson.
> 4. **Mixing up trade-to-trade data with fixed-interval data:** Trade-to-trade data solves the bias but is irregular — applying standard OLS/ARMA gives wrong standard errors.
> 5. **Forgetting the aggregation factor:** Saying “weekly returns eliminate the bias” is wrong; the bias falls by factor $1/k$, not to zero.
> 6. **Stating the Lo-MacKinlay test is for serial correlation only:** It is a *variance-ratio* test and is sensitive to both autocorrelation and heteroskedasticity.
> 7. **Not specifying $p_i$ explicitly in derivations:** Examiners expect a defined trading-probability parameter; otherwise the derivation is incomplete.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Nonsynchronous Trading**

- **Definition:** Securities trade intermittently; observed prices at fixed intervals are stale when no trade occurred.
- **Three statistical artefacts:** (i) Downward OLS beta bias, (ii) Negative residual autocorrelation, (iii) Spurious lead-lag cross-correlations.
- **OLS Beta bias formula:** $E[\hat\beta_i^{\text{OLS}}] = p_i \beta_i$, where $p_i$ is the trading probability.
- **Scholes-Williams estimator:** Uses contemporaneous + lead + lag market returns, weighted by $1/(1+2\hat\rho_m)$.
- **Dimson estimator:** Joint OLS regression on multiple leads and lags of the market index.
- **Aggregation effect:** Bias shrinks as $\approx 1/k$ when forming $k$-day returns; weekly returns are ~5× less biased than daily.
- **Trade-to-trade data:** Eliminates staleness but introduces irregular sampling — requires modified econometric methods.
- **Lo-MacKinlay test:** Variance-ratio diagnostic that *spuriously rejects* random walk under nonsynchronous trading.
- **Cohen-Maezapour rule:** $\rho_k^{(k)} \approx \rho_1^{(1)} / k$.
- **Lead-lag formula:** $\gamma_{ij}(1) = (1-p_j)\,\gamma_{ij}(0) > 0$ — apparent lead of *i* over *j*.
- **Practical fix list (in order of strength):**
   1. Use weekly or monthly returns (Cheapest).
   2. Apply Scholes-Williams or Dimson beta.
   3. Switch to trade-to-trade data.
   4. Use robust standard errors and HAC covariance estimators.
- **Historical anchors:** Black (1971), Scholes-Williams (1977), Dimson (1979), Cohen-Maezapour (1983), Lo-MacKinlay (1990).
- **Expected KTU 2024 question weight:** One 14-mark derivation question + one 3-mark definitional question per exam cycle; pairs naturally with **Volatility Modeling** questions in Module 3.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bid-Ask Spread of Trading Prices
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET742 - FINANCIAL TIME SERIES ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Bid-Ask Spread of Trading Prices

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is the Bid-Ask Spread of Trading Prices?

The **Bid-Ask Spread (BAS)** of trading prices is a fundamental microstructural measure in financial econometrics that quantifies the difference between the highest price a buyer is willing to pay for an asset (the **bid price**) and the lowest price a seller is willing to accept (the **ask price**, also called the offer price). Formally, for a tradable security at time $t$:

$$
\text{BAS}_t = A_t - B_t
$$

where:
- $A_t$ = Ask price at time $t$ (seller's quoted price)
- $B_t$ = Bid price at time $t$ (buyer's quoted price)

The **midpoint (mid-quote)** of the spread is computed as:

$$
M_t = \frac{A_t + B_t}{2}
$$

The **relative (percentage) bid-ask spread** is given by:

$$
\text{BAS}^{\%}_t = \frac{A_t - B_t}{M_t} \times 100\%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** The bid-ask spread is a transaction-cost proxy in financial time series. It arises because of **order processing costs**, **inventory holding costs**, and **information asymmetry (adverse selection costs)** in dealer markets. It is the most fundamental variable studied in the field of *market microstructure*.

> [!NOTE]  
> **Standard Metric Convention:** Bid-ask spread is expressed in three standard ways: (1) **Quoted Spread** ($A - B$), (2) **Effective Spread** ($2 \cdot \text{sign} \cdot (P - M)$), and (3) **Realized Spread** ($2 \cdot \text{sign} \cdot (P - M_{t+n})$). All three are quoted in **basis points (bps)** where $1 \text{ bps} = 0.01\%$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Real-World Intuition

Imagine you are at a **flea market** trying to buy a vintage watch from a dealer. You, the buyer, will quote a low price ("I'll pay ₹500"). The dealer (seller) will quote a high price ("I want ₹700"). The difference (₹200) is the **bid-ask spread**.

**Why does this spread exist?**
- The dealer incurs costs for setting up the stall, verifying the watch, holding inventory, and bearing the risk that the watch may not sell.
- Some buyers know more about the watch's true value than others (e.g., a professional collector). The dealer must protect against being "cheated" by an informed buyer — this is **adverse selection**.
- The dealer must also be compensated for the **risk of holding the watch** without a guaranteed buyer (inventory risk).

The bid-ask spread in financial markets works the **exact same way**. Market makers (dealers) stand ready to buy and sell continuously, and they earn the spread as compensation for providing **liquidity**.

| Actor in Market | Analogy Role | Price Quoted |
|---|---|---|
| Investor wanting to SELL | Customer selling watch | Receives **Bid Price** $B_t$ |
| Investor wanting to BUY | Customer buying watch | Pays **Ask Price** $A_t$ |
| Market Maker / Dealer | Flea market stall owner | Earns the **Spread** $A_t - B_t$ |

---

## 1.3 Why the Bid-Ask Spread Matters in Time Series Analysis

In KTU 2024 syllabus, the bid-ask spread is treated as a **distortion source** in observed transaction prices. Even if the *true* (efficient) price of an asset is constant at ₹100, the *observed* prices will bounce between ₹99.50 (bid) and ₹100.50 (ask), creating **artificial volatility** in price series.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **The "Bid-Ask Bounce" Effect:** Because trades alternate between hitting the bid and the ask, observed prices exhibit a **negative first-order autocorrelation** purely due to microstructure noise — *not* because of any fundamental market inefficiency. This must be modeled or removed for valid econometric inference.

This is critical in the KTU framework because the classical time series models (ARMA, GARCH, VAR) assume that the observed price reflects the *efficient* price. When bid-ask bounce is ignored, the estimated autocorrelation structure and volatility become **biased**.

---

## 1.4 The Three Component Costs of the Spread

Modern market microstructure theory (Glosten & Harris, Stoll) decomposes the observed spread into three economic costs:

| Component | Symbol | Economic Meaning | Typical Share |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Order Processing Cost** | $\alpha$ | Fixed costs of executing a trade (clearing, settlement, computer systems) | ~50% |
| **Inventory Holding Cost** | $\beta$ | Compensation for risk of holding positions overnight or during volatility | ~25% |
| **Adverse Selection Cost** | $\gamma$ | Loss to informed traders (e.g., insider traders) | ~25% |

Mathematically:

$$
\text{BAS} = \alpha + \beta + \gamma
$$

Each component has different time-series dynamics:
- $\alpha$ is approximately **constant** (deterministic).
- $\beta$ is **mean-reverting** and scales with volatility.
- $\gamma$ is **information-driven** and tends to spike before major news.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Decomposition of the Observed Price

In KTU's formal treatment, the **observed transaction price** $P_t$ is modeled as a combination of the **true (efficient) price** $P^*_t$ and a microstructure noise term $\varepsilon_t$:

$$
P_t = P^*_t + \varepsilon_t
$$

The noise term $\varepsilon_t$ depends on the **trade direction** $Q_t$, where:

$$
Q_t = \begin{cases} +1, & \text{if trade is buyer-initiated (hits ask)} \\ -1, & \text{if trade is seller-initiated (hits bid)} \end{cases}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\varepsilon_t = \frac{S_t}{2} \cdot Q_t
$$

where $S_t$ is the (potentially time-varying) **half-spread** at time $t$. Combining these:

$$
P_t = P^*_t + \frac{S_t}{2} \cdot Q_t
$$

This is the foundational **Hasbrouck (1993) framework** that KTU 2024 syllabus adopts as the benchmark.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU Board Examination Note:** When you write the observed price equation in exams, you **must explicitly define** $P^*_t$, $Q_t$, and $S_t$ separately. Examiners consistently deduct 1–2 marks for undefined symbols.

---

## 2.2 Three Primary Measures of the Bid-Ask Spread

### Measure 1: Quoted Spread
The most direct measure, computed directly from the limit order book:

$$
\text{Quoted Spread}_t = A_t - B_t
$$

- **Pros:** Easy to compute, observable in real-time.
- **Cons:** Measures *depth at best quotes*, not actual transaction costs.

### Measure 2: Effective (Transaction) Spread
Measures the *actual* cost paid by an aggressive trader relative to the mid-quote at trade time:

$$
\text{Effective Spread}_t = 2 \cdot Q_t \cdot (P_t - M_t)
$$

Since $Q_t \in \{-1, +1\}$, this is always **non-negative**. It captures the **realized** cost of liquidity.

### Measure 3: Realized Spread
Measures the market maker's *actual revenue* evaluated some periods later, capturing permanent price impact:

$$
\text{Realized Spread}_{t,t+n} = 2 \cdot Q_t \cdot \left(P_t - M_{t+n}\right)
$$

where $M_{t+n}$ is the mid-quote $n$ periods after the trade.

> [!NOTE]  
> **Interpretation Triangle:**  
> - **Effective Spread** = Total cost to trader.  
> - **Realized Spread** = Revenue to market maker.  
> - **Effective − Realized = Adverse Selection Component** (the loss due to informed trading).

---

## 2.3 The Roll (1984) Model — The Most Tested Model in KTU

**Robert Roll's 1984 model** is the single most important bid-ask model for KTU 2024 examinations. It provides a **closed-form estimator** of the bid-ask spread using only observed transaction prices.

### Model Assumptions:
1. The efficient price $P^*_t$ follows a **random walk**: $P^*_t = P^*_{t-1} + u_t$, where $u_t \sim \text{WN}(0, \sigma_u^2)$.
2. Trades occur with **equal probability** at the bid and ask.
3. The spread $S$ is **constant** over time.
4. Trade direction $Q_t$ is **independent** of past price changes.

### Key Result — Roll's Covariance Estimator:

$$
S = 2\sqrt{-\text{Cov}(\Delta P_t, \Delta P_{t-1})}
$$

Equivalently, the first-order serial covariance of price changes is:

$$
\gamma_1 = \text{Cov}(\Delta P_t, \Delta P_{t-1}) = -\frac{S^2}{4}
$$

Sample estimator:

$$
\hat{S} = 2\sqrt{-\hat{\gamma}_1}, \quad \text{where } \hat{\gamma}_1 = \frac{1}{T-1}\sum_{t=2}^{T}(\Delta P_t - \bar{\Delta P})(\Delta P_{t-1} - \bar{\Delta P})
$$

> [!WARNING]  
> **Pitfall:** The formula only yields a valid (real) spread if $\hat{\gamma}_1 < 0$. If sample covariance is positive, Roll's estimator fails — this happens in low-spread, high-volatility environments.

---

## 2.4 The Glosten-Harris (1988) Extended Model

When the spread varies with time, KTU syllabus adopts the **Glosten-Harris decomposition**:

$$
\Delta P_t = \lambda_t \cdot Q_t + \Delta P^*_t
$$

where:
- $\Delta P^*_t$ = change in efficient price (often modeled as GARCH process).
- $\lambda_t$ = variable half-spread, modeled as:

$$
\lambda_t = \psi_0 + \psi_1 \cdot Q_{t-1} + \psi_2 \cdot \vert \Delta P_{t-1}\vert
$$
