> 3. **Mixing up formulas.** The autocorrelation $\rho_l(P) = 1 - l/t$ is for the *level*; the autocorrelation of $C_t$ is identically zero. Writing $\rho_l(P) = 0$ costs heavy marks.
> 4. **Misinterpreting the Efficient Market Hypothesis.** A random walk does *not* mean returns are zero — it means they are *unpredictable from past prices*. Mean returns can be positive (e.g., $\mu = 0.0005$ per day).
> 5. **Skipping the economic interpretation.** KTU 2024 scheme emphasises Outcome-Based Education; numerical derivations without the *meaning* attached are penalised.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] The **Random Walk Model** is the central pillar: $P_t = P_{t-1} + a_t$ where $a_t \sim WN(0, \sigma_a^2)$.
- [x] **Submartingale → Martingale → Random Walk** is a strict ordering of *decreasing generality*.
- [x] The price level $P_t$ is **non-stationary**: its mean is $P_0 + \mu t$ and its variance is $t\sigma_a^2$.
- [x] The price change $C_t = a_t$ is **stationary white noise** with mean 0 and variance $\sigma_a^2$.
- [x] The autocorrelation of $P_t$ at lag $l$ is $\rho_l = 1 - l/t$, which approaches 1 for small lags.
- [x] The autocorrelation of $C_t$ is **identically zero** for all non-zero lags.
- [x] **Log return** $r_t = \ln(P_t) - \ln(P_{t-1})$ is approximately equal to the simple return for small changes.
- [x] The **Random Walk** is the *null hypothesis* in unit-root tests (ADF, PP, KPSS).
- [x] The **Efficient Market Hypothesis (Weak Form)** is the economic foundation that justifies the Random Walk assumption.
- [x] Variance scales *linearly* with time horizon $t$ — a critical formula in multi-period risk modelling.
- [x] KTU 2024 expects students to: (i) state the model, (ii) derive the first two moments, (iii) compute the ACF, and (iv) interpret the economic meaning.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Duration Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET742 - FINANCIAL TIME SERIES ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Duration Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Duration Model** is a class of statistical models used in financial econometrics to analyze and forecast the **time elapsed until a specific event of interest occurs**. In the KTU 2024 scheme context, duration models — also known as **Survival Analysis Models** or **Failure-Time Models** — treat the random variable of interest $T$ as a non-negative continuous time variable representing the waiting period between a well-defined starting event and a terminating event.

In financial applications, $T$ can represent:

- The **time until a corporate bond defaults** (Credit Risk).
- The **time until a firm initiates an Initial Public Offering (IPO)**.
- The **duration of an unemployment spell** for a worker.
- The **time between two consecutive trades** (High-Frequency Finance).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Duration models are critical when the dependent variable is **time-to-event** and the data may be **censored** (i.e., the event has not yet occurred by the end of the observation window). Standard OLS regression is invalid here because the error term is non-normal and heteroscedastic.

The analysis rests on four mathematically linked pillars: the **Probability Density Function (PDF)** $f(t)$, the **Cumulative Distribution Function (CDF)** $F(t)$, the **Survivor Function** $S(t)$, and the **Hazard Function** $h(t)$.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Bus Stop Waiting Problem**
> Imagine you are standing at a bus stop. The "duration" is the time you wait ($T$) before the bus arrives. You start the clock the moment you reach the stop (the **origin event**), and stop it when the bus arrives (the **failure event**).
>
> - **Survivor Function $S(t)$**: "What is the probability I am *still waiting* after $t$ minutes?"
> - **Hazard Function $h(t)$**: "Given I have *already waited* for $t$ minutes, what is the *instantaneous rate* at which the bus will arrive in the next tiny instant?"
> - **Censoring**: Suppose you give up after 30 minutes and walk away. We know $T > 30$, but not the exact value. This is **right-censored** data.
>
> In finance, replace "the bus" with "a corporate default," "an IPO," or "a merger," and the same mathematics applies.

### 1.3 The Four Core Functions

Let $T$ be a non-negative continuous random variable representing duration. Then:

1. **Probability Density Function (PDF):** $f(t)$ — the instantaneous likelihood of failure at time $t$.
2. **Cumulative Distribution Function (CDF):** $F(t) = P(T \leq t)$ — probability the event has occurred by $t$.
3. **Survivor Function (Reliability Function):**

$$S(t) = P(T > t) = 1 - F(t)$$

4. **Hazard Function (Force of Mortality / Instantaneous Failure Rate):**

$$h(t) = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{P(t \leq T < t + \Delta t \mid T \geq t)}{\Delta t}$$

The hazard function captures the **conditional** failure rate: it answers "what is the probability of failure in the next instant, *given* survival up to now?"

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Linkages Between the Core Functions

The four functions are not independent; they are linked through three fundamental identities that form the mathematical backbone of duration analysis.

**Step 1: Hazard to Density**

The numerator of the hazard is the conditional probability of failure in a small interval. The probability of failure in $(t, t+dt)$ is $f(t)dt$. The probability of surviving past $t$ is $S(t)$. Therefore:

$$h(t) = \frac{f(t)}{S(t)}$$

**Step 2: Density to Hazard via the Cumulative Hazard**

The **Cumulative Hazard Function** is the integral of the hazard:

$$H(t) = \int_{0}^{t} h(u) \, du$$

**Step 3: The Master Relationship — Survivor Function**

The survivor function can be expressed as the negative exponential of the cumulative hazard:

$$S(t) = \exp(-H(t)) = \exp\left(-\int_{0}^{t} h(u) \, du\right)$$

This identity is the **cornerstone of survival analysis** because if we can specify $h(t)$, we automatically obtain $S(t)$, $f(t)$, and $F(t)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the Hazard Function is Preferred in Finance:**
> Unlike $f(t)$, the hazard $h(t)$ is *not* constrained to integrate to 1. It can be any non-negative function. This flexibility allows it to model complex financial phenomena where failure rates rise, fall, or remain constant over time (e.g., newly issued bonds have low default risk — the "**honeymoon period**" — but risk rises as they approach maturity).

### 2.2 Censoring and Truncation

In financial data, we rarely observe the exact failure time for every subject. Two phenomena must be handled:

- **Right-Censoring:** The event has *not* occurred by the end of the observation window. We know $T > c$ but not the exact value. Common when studying firms that have not yet defaulted by the study's end.
- **Left-Truncation:** Subjects are observed only if they survive past a certain threshold. Common in retirement data where only those who reach a certain age are tracked.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Standard regression *cannot* handle censored data correctly, because OLS would treat the censored value (e.g., 30 minutes) as the actual duration, **biasing the estimate downward**. Duration models explicitly incorporate the censoring mechanism into the likelihood function.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Description / Interpretation |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Survivor Function | $S(t) = P(T > t) = 1 - F(t)$ | Probability of surviving past $t$ |
| 2 | Density Function | $f(t) = -\dfrac{d}{dt} S(t)$ | Instantaneous failure probability |
| 3 | Hazard Function | $h(t) = \dfrac{f(t)}{S(t)}$ | Conditional instantaneous failure rate |
| 4 | Cumulative Hazard | $H(t) = \displaystyle\int_{0}^{t} h(u) \, du$ | Integrated hazard up to time $t$ |
| 5 | Master Identity | $S(t) = \exp(-H(t))$ | Links hazard to survivor function |
| 6 | Exponential Hazard | $h(t) = \lambda$ | Constant hazard (memoryless property) |
| 7 | Weibull Hazard | $h(t) = \alpha \lambda t^{\alpha - 1}$ | Monotonic (increasing if $\alpha > 1$) |
| 8 | Log-Logistic Hazard | $h(t) = \dfrac{\alpha \lambda t^{\alpha - 1}}{1 + \lambda t^{\alpha}}$ | Non-monotonic (unimodal) |
| 9 | Kaplan–Meier | $\hat{S}(t) = \displaystyle\prod_{t_i \leq t} \left(1 - \dfrac{d_i}{n_i}\right)$ | Non-parametric $\hat{S}(t)$ |
| 10 | Cox PH Model | $h(t \mid X) = h_0(t) \exp(\beta' X)$ | Semi-parametric multiplicative hazard |
| 11 | AFT Model | $\log T = X\beta + \sigma \varepsilon$ | Accelerates/ decelerates time |
| 12 | Censored Likelihood | $L = \displaystyle\prod_{i: \text{unc.}} f(t_i) \prod_{i: \text{cens.}} S(c_i)$ | Combined likelihood for censored data |

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering & Finance

- **Credit Risk Management:** Banks use duration models (especially the **Cox Proportional Hazards model**) to estimate the **Probability of Default (PD)** of corporate borrowers over different time horizons.
- **Insurance & Actuarial Science:** The **Lifetime Distribution** of mechanical components is modeled using Weibull hazard functions.
- **High-Frequency Trading:** The **time between trades** (trade duration) is modeled with the Autoregressive Conditional Duration (ACD) model, a direct descendant of the duration framework.
- **Reliability Engineering:** Predicting the time-to-failure of hardware components.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Master Identity $S(t) = \exp(-H(t))$

This is the most important derivation in duration analysis. We proceed in five clean algebraic steps.

**Given:** The hazard function $h(t) = \dfrac{f(t)}{S(t)}$.

**Step 1 — Recognize the relationship between $f(t)$ and $S(t)$.**

By definition, $F(t) = P(T \leq t)$, and the density is its derivative:

$$f(t) = \frac{dF(t)}{dt}$$

Since $S(t) = 1 - F(t)$, the derivative of the survivor function is the negative of the density:

$$f(t) = -\frac{dS(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute this into the hazard definition.**

$$h(t) = \frac{f(t)}{S(t)} = \frac{-dS(t)/dt}{S(t)} = -\frac{1}{S(t)} \cdot \frac{dS(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 3 — Recognize the logarithmic derivative.**

The expression $-\dfrac{1}{S(t)} \cdot \dfrac{dS(t)}{dt}$ is exactly the derivative of $\ln S(t)$ multiplied by $-1$:

$$h(t) = -\frac{d}{dt}\left[\ln S(t)\right]$$

> This is because $\dfrac{d}{dt}[\ln S(t)] = \dfrac{1}{S(t)} \cdot \dfrac{dS(t)}{dt}$, and the negative sign cancels.

**Step 4 — Integrate both sides from $0$ to $t$.**

$$H(t) = \int_{0}^{t} h(u) \, du = -\int_{0}^{t} \frac{d}{du}\left[\ln S(u)\right] du = -[\ln S(t) - \ln S(0)]$$

**Step 5 — Apply the boundary condition $S(0) = 1$ (i.e., $\ln S(0) = 0$).**

$$H(t) = -\ln S(t)$$

Solving for $S(t)$:

$$S(t) = \exp(-H(t)) = \exp\left(-\int_{0}^{t} h(u) \, du\right) \qquad \blacksquare$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Likelihood Function for Censored Data

Suppose we observe $n$ subjects. For subject $i$, we observe either:
- The exact failure time $t_i$ (uncensored), or
- A censoring time $c_i$ (right-censored, meaning $T_i > c_i$).

**Step 1 — The contribution of an uncensored observation.**

If subject $i$ fails at exactly $t_i$, the likelihood contribution is the density evaluated at $t_i$:

$$L_i = f(t_i)$$

**Step 2 — The contribution of a censored observation.**

If subject $i$ is censored at $c_i$ (we know they survived past $c_i$), the likelihood contribution is the probability of surviving past $c_i$:

$$L_i = P(T > c_i) = S(c_i)$$

**Step 3 — Combine into a single likelihood using indicator variables.**

Let $\delta_i = 1$ if uncensored, $\delta_i = 0$ if censored. The total log-likelihood is:

$$\ln L = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left[\delta_i \ln f(t_i) + (1 - \delta_i) \ln S(t_i)\right]$$

**Step 4 — Express in terms of hazard and survival.**

Using $f(t) = h(t) S(t)$:

$$\ln L = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left[\delta_i \ln h(t_i) + \ln S(t_i)\right]$$

This log-likelihood is what is **maximized** to estimate the parameters of parametric duration models like the Weibull or Exponential.

### 3.3 Derivation: Properties of the Weibull Duration Model

The Weibull distribution is the workhorse of duration analysis because it allows for monotonic (strictly increasing or decreasing) hazard rates.

**Step 1 — Specify the Weibull survivor function.**

$$S(t) = \exp(-\lambda t^{\alpha}), \quad \alpha > 0, \quad \lambda > 0$$

**Step 2 — Derive the Weibull hazard.**

Using $h(t) = -\dfrac{d \ln S(t)}{dt}$:

$$\ln S(t) = -\lambda t^{\alpha}$$

$$\frac{d}{dt}\ln S(t) = -\lambda \alpha t^{\alpha - 1}$$

$$h(t) = \lambda \alpha t^{\alpha - 1}$$

**Step 3 — Interpret the shape parameter $\alpha$.**

- If $\alpha = 1$: $h(t) = \lambda$ (constant hazard, equivalent to the **Exponential** distribution — memoryless).
- If $\alpha > 1$: $h(t)$ is **increasing** in $t$ (positive duration dependence — failure becomes more likely as time passes, e.g., aging bonds).
- If $0 < \alpha < 1$: $h(t)$ is **decreasing** in $t$ (negative duration dependence — failure becomes less likely, e.g., firms that survive their early years).

**Step 4 — Verify the Weibull mean and variance for the case $\alpha = 1$ (Exponential).**

When $\alpha = 1$, $S(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ and $f(t) = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}$. The mean and variance are:

$$E[T] = \frac{1}{\lambda}, \qquad \text{Var}(T) = \frac{1}{\lambda^2}$$

Note the **equidispersion** property: the variance equals the square of the mean. This is why we use the **Gamma** or **Weibull** to relax this restriction.

### 3.4 Derivation: The Cox Proportional Hazards Model

**Step 1 — Specify the proportional hazards assumption.**

Cox (1972) proposed that covariates act multiplicatively on a baseline hazard $h_0(t)$:

$$h(t \mid X) = h_0(t) \cdot g(X)$$

The simplest and most widely used form is:

$$h(t \mid X) = h_0(t) \exp(\beta_1 X_1 + \beta_2 X_2 + \cdots + \beta_k X_k)$$

**Step 2 — Derive the partial likelihood.**

The baseline $h_0(t)$ is treated as a nuisance and eliminated. At each failure time $t_{(j)}$, the conditional probability that subject $i$ fails (given that *some* subject fails) is:

$$L_i(t_{(j)}) = \frac{\exp(\beta' X_i)}{\displaystyle\sum_{m \in R(t_{(j)})} \exp(\beta' X_m)}$$

where $R(t_{(j)})$ is the **risk set** — the set of subjects still under observation just before $t_{(j)}$.

**Step 3 — Form the partial log-likelihood.**

$$\ln L_p = \sum_{j=1}^{D} \left[\beta' X_{(j)} - \ln \sum_{m \in R(t_{(j)})} \exp(\beta' X_m)\right]$$

where $D$ is the number of observed failures. This is maximized over $\beta$ to obtain coefficient estimates.

> [!NOTE]
> The Cox model is **semi-parametric** because $h_0(t)$ is left unspecified (non-parametric), but the covariate effect $\exp(\beta' X)$ is parametric. This is its great strength: robust to misspecification of the baseline hazard.

### 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Model | Hazard Shape | Parametric? | Proportional Hazards? | Use Case in Finance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Exponential | Constant | Yes | Yes | Baseline benchmark |
| Weibull | Monotonic | Yes | Yes | Aging credit risk |
| Log-Logistic | Unimodal (inverted U) | Yes | No | Default then recovery |
| Log-Normal | Unimodal | Yes | No | IPO timing |
| Cox PH | Arbitrary $h_0$ | Semi | Yes | General credit scoring |
| AFT (Accelerated Failure Time) | Arbitrary | Yes | No | Time-to-event with covariates |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Flow of a Duration Model Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Define Event of Interest: e.g., Corporate Default"] --> B["Identify Origin and Failure Times"]
    B --> C{"Is the Data Censored?"}
    C -- Yes --> D["Apply Right Censoring Correction in Likelihood"]
    C -- No --> E["Use Standard Density f t"]
    D --> F["Choose Model Family"]
    E --> F
    F --> G["Parametric: Exponential, Weibull, Log Logistic"]
    F --> H["Non Parametric: Kaplan Meier, Nelson Aalen"]
    F --> I["Semi Parametric: Cox Proportional Hazards"]
    F --> J["Regression Based: AFT, Cox PH with Covariates"]
    G --> K["Maximize Log Likelihood to Estimate Parameters"]
    H --> K
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L["Diagnostic Checks: Goodness of Fit, Residual Analysis"]
    L --> M["Forecast: Survival Probabilities and Hazard Rates"]
    M --> N["Decision: e.g., PD, Pricing, Capital Reserve"]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Cox PH Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT["Input Layer"]
        X1["Covariate 1: Leverage Ratio"]
        X2["Covariate 2: Interest Coverage"]
        X3["Covariate k: Macro Factor"]
    end
    subgraph CORE["Core Engine: Cox Proportional Hazards"]
        H0["Baseline Hazard h0 t : Non Parametric"]
        BETA["Coefficient Vector Beta"]
        LIN["Linear Predictor: Beta Transpose X"]
        EXP["Exponentiation: exp Beta X"]
        H["Conditional Hazard h t given X"]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT["Output Layer"]
        S["Survivor Function S t"]
        PD["Probability of Default over horizon t"]
        HZ["Hazard Rate Curve"]
    end
    X1 --> LIN
    X2 --> LIN
    X3 --> LIN
    BETA --> LIN
    LIN --> EXP
    H0 --> H
    EXP --> H
    H --> S
    S --> PD
    H --> HZ
```

### 4.3 Relationship Between Core Functions (Map)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    FT["f t : Probability Density Function"] --> HZ["h t : Hazard Function"]
    FT --> SF["S t : Survivor Function"]
    SF --> HZ
    HZ --> CH["H t : Cumulative Hazard"]
    CH --> SF
    FT --> CF["F t : CDF: 1 minus S t"]
    SF --> CF
```

### 4.4 Hazard Shape Taxonomy (Conceptual Map)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SH["Hazard Function Shape Taxonomy"] --> S1["Constant: Exponential alpha equals 1"]
    SH --> S2["Monotonic Increasing: Weibull alpha greater than 1"]
    SH --> S3["Monotonic Decreasing: Weibull alpha between 0 and 1"]
    SH --> S4["Unimodal Inverted U: Log Logistic, Log Normal"]
    SH --> S5["Arbitrary: Cox PH semi parametric"]
    S1 --> APP1["Application: Memoryless Defaults"]
    S2 --> APP2["Application: Aging Bonds, Wear Out"]
    S3 --> APP3["Application: Learning Curves, Honeymoon"]
    S4 --> APP4["Application: Post Issue Decline"]
    S5 --> APP5["Application: General Credit Risk Scoring"]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**

Define the **hazard function** in a duration model. How is it different from the probability density function?

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> The **hazard function** $h(t)$ is the instantaneous rate of failure at time $t$, conditional on survival up to $t$. Formally:
>
> $$h(t) = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{P(t \leq T < t + \Delta t \mid T \geq t)}{\Delta t} = \frac{f(t)}{S(t)}$$
>
> **Key Differences from PDF $f(t)$:**
>
> 1. The hazard $h(t)$ is a **rate** (failures per unit time), not a probability. It is **not** bounded above by 1.
> 2. The hazard is **conditional** on survival up to $t$, whereas the density is **unconditional**.
> 3. The hazard captures the **dynamic** risk profile over time, while the density gives the marginal distribution of failure times.
>
> **(3 marks: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for distinction)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**

State the **Master Identity** of duration analysis. What is its significance in modeling financial time-to-event data?

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> **Master Identity:**
>
> $$S(t) = \exp\left(-\int_{0}^{t} h(u) \, du\right) = \exp(-H(t))$$
>
> **Significance:**
>
> 1. It provides a **direct invertible link** between the hazard $h(t)$ and the survivor $S(t)$.
> 2. If we specify a **parametric form for $h(t)$** (e.g., constant, Weibull, log-logistic), we can analytically derive $S(t)$ and $f(t) = h(t)S(t)$.
> 3. It allows the likelihood function for censored data to be written compactly as:
>
> $$\ln L = \sum_i \left[\delta_i \ln h(t_i) + \ln S(t_i)\right]$$
>
> **(3 marks: 1 mark identity, 2 marks significance)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style, Module 4]**

**(a)** Derive the relationship between the survivor function $S(t)$, the cumulative hazard $H(t)$, and the probability density function $f(t)$ in a duration model. Show that $S(t) = \exp(-H(t))$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider the **Weibull duration model** with survivor function $S(t) = \exp(-\lambda t^{\alpha})$, where $\alpha, \lambda > 0$. Derive the hazard function $h(t)$ and discuss how the shape parameter $\alpha$ governs the dynamic behavior of the conditional default probability. Provide a financial example for each case ($\alpha < 1$, $\alpha = 1$, $\alpha > 1$). **(7 marks)**

---

> **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** State definitions.
By definition, the survivor function is $S(t) = P(T > t) = 1 - F(t)$, where $F(t)$ is the CDF. The density is $f(t) = \dfrac{dF(t)}{dt}$.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Express density in terms of survivor.
Differentiating $S(t) = 1 - F(t)$ with respect to $t$:

$$\frac{dS(t)}{dt} = -f(t) \quad \Rightarrow \quad f(t) = -\frac{dS(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Hazard definition.
The hazard is the conditional failure rate:

$$h(t) = \frac{f(t)}{S(t)} = \frac{-dS(t)/dt}{S(t)} = -\frac{d}{dt}\left[\ln S(t)\right]$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Define cumulative hazard.

$$H(t) = \int_{0}^{t} h(u) \, du = -\int_{0}^{t} \frac{d}{du}\left[\ln S(u)\right] du = -\left[\ln S(t) - \ln S(0)\right]$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Apply boundary condition.
Since $S(0) = 1$ implies $\ln S(0) = 0$:

$$H(t) = -\ln S(t) \quad \Rightarrow \quad S(t) = \exp(-H(t))$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Express density in terms of hazard and survivor.
Since $f(t) = h(t) S(t)$:

$$f(t) = h(t) \exp\left(-\int_{0}^{t} h(u) \, du\right)$$

**Step 7 (1 mark):** Concluding remark.
The trio $(S, f, H)$ is fully characterized by the single function $h(t)$, which is the central object of estimation in duration analysis.

---

**Part (b) — Weibull Model (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Derive the Weibull hazard.
We are given:

$$S(t) = \exp(-\lambda t^{\alpha})$$

Taking the natural log:

$$\ln S(t) = -\lambda t^{\alpha}$$

Differentiating with respect to $t$:

$$\frac{d}{dt}\ln S(t) = -\lambda \alpha t^{\alpha - 1}$$

Using the result $h(t) = -\dfrac{d}{dt}\ln S(t)$:

$$\boxed{h(t) = \lambda \alpha t^{\alpha - 1}}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Case $\alpha = 1$ (Exponential / Constant Hazard).
$h(t) = \lambda$. **Memoryless** property. Example: the probability of a corporate default in the next instant is the same regardless of how long the firm has survived. Appropriate for modeling random, idiosyncratic shocks.

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Case $\alpha > 1$ (Increasing Hazard).
$h(t) = \lambda \alpha t^{\alpha - 1}$ is **strictly increasing** in $t$. Example: high-yield ("junk") bonds exhibit **positive duration dependence** — the longer a bond survives, the more likely it is to default soon, because deteriorating firm conditions compound over time.

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Case $0 < \alpha < 1$ (Decreasing Hazard).
$h(t) = \lambda \alpha t^{\alpha - 1}$ is **strictly decreasing** in $t$ (since the exponent $\alpha - 1 < 0$). Example: newly issued bonds enjoy a "**honeymoon period**" — once they survive the first few years, the conditional default rate falls, perhaps because early defaults reveal and remove the weakest issuers.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain the **Cox Proportional Hazards (PH) Model** in detail. State the proportional hazards assumption, derive the partial likelihood, and explain how regression coefficients $\beta$ are interpreted. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is **right-censoring** in duration data? Construct the log-likelihood function for a sample containing both uncensored and right-censored observations. Explain why the standard OLS estimator is biased in the presence of censoring. **(7 marks)**

---

> **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Cox PH Model (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** The PH assumption.
Cox (1972) specifies:

$$h(t \mid X) = h_0(t) \cdot \exp(\beta_1 X_1 + \cdots + \beta_k X_k)$$

where $h_0(t)$ is an unspecified baseline hazard and $X = (X_1, \ldots, X_k)$ are covariates. The "proportional" property means the ratio of hazards between two subjects is constant over time:

$$\frac{h(t \mid X)}{h(t \mid X^*)} = \exp\left[\beta'(X - X^*)\right]$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Partial likelihood derivation.
At ordered failure times $t_{(1)} < t_{(2)} < \cdots < t_{(D)}$, the conditional probability that subject $i$ is the one failing (given a failure occurs) is:

$$L_i = \frac{\exp(\beta' X_i)}{\sum_{m \in R(t_{(j)})} \exp(\beta' X_m)}$$

where $R(t_{(j)})$ is the risk set. The partial log-likelihood is:

$$\ln L_p = \sum_{j=1}^{D} \left[\beta' X_{(j)} - \ln \sum_{m \in R(t_{(j)})} \exp(\beta' X_m)\right]$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Interpretation of $\beta$.
- $\exp(\beta_j) > 1$: covariate $X_j$ **increases** the hazard (e.g., higher leverage raises default risk).
- $\exp(\beta_j) < 1$: covariate $X_j$ **decreases** the hazard (e.g., higher asset coverage lowers default risk).
- $\exp(\beta_j) = 1$: no effect.

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Why "semi-parametric".
The baseline $h_0(t)$ is non-parametric (no distributional assumption), but covariate effects are parametric — this is the strength of the Cox model.

---

**Part (b) — Right Censoring (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Definition.
A duration observation is **right-censored** if, at the end of the observation window, the event of interest has not yet occurred. We know only that $T_i > c_i$ (the censoring time). This is ubiquitous in financial data — e.g., bonds outstanding at the end of a study period that have not defaulted.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Likelihood construction.
For an uncensored observation $t_i$, the contribution is the density $f(t_i)$. For a censored observation $c_i$, the contribution is the survivor probability $S(c_i)$:

$$L = \prod_{i: \text{uncensored}} f(t_i) \cdot \prod_{i: \text{censored}} S(c_i)$$

Taking logs:

$$\ln L = \sum_{i: \text{unc.}} \ln f(t_i) + \sum_{i: \text{cens.}} \ln S(c_i)$$

Using $f(t) = h(t)S(t)$:

$$\ln L = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left[\delta_i \ln h(t_i) + \ln S(t_i)\right]$$

where $\delta_i = 1$ for uncensored, $\delta_i = 0$ for censored.

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Why OLS fails.
If we naively regress the observed (possibly censored) duration on covariates using OLS:
- The dependent variable is **right-truncated** (observed duration < true duration for censored cases).
- The error term is **non-normal** and **heteroscedastic** (variance depends on covariates).
- The coefficient estimates are **biased toward zero** (the **attenuation bias**) because the censored durations are systematically shorter than the true ones.
- Duration models resolve this by treating censored contributions as survivor probabilities $S(c_i)$ rather than densities.

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Conclusion.
The likelihood-based approach of duration models is the only consistent method for handling censored time-to-event data.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Confusing hazard with probability:** Students often write $h(t)$ as a "probability" — it is a **rate**, not a probability. (–1 mark)
> 2. **Forgetting the conditional nature of the hazard:** The hazard is *conditional on survival*. Writing $h(t) = f(t)$ (forgetting $S(t)$ in the denominator) is a common error. (–1 mark)
> 3. **Mixing up survival and cumulative hazard identities:** $S(t) = \exp(-H(t))$, not $S(t) = 1 - H(t)$. (–1 mark)
> 4. **Treating censored observations as failures:** Substituting $c_i$ for $t_i$ in the density $f(\cdot)$ instead of using $S(c_i)$ in the likelihood. This biases all coefficient estimates. (–2 marks)
> 5. **Not stating the assumption $S(0) = 1$** when deriving the master identity — boundary conditions must be explicit. (–1 mark)

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for Duration Models**

- [ ] **Duration** is the elapsed time between a starting event and a terminating event; it is always non-negative.
- [ ] The **four core functions** are $f(t)$, $F(t)$, $S(t)$, and $h(t)$ — all derivable from a single choice of hazard.
- [ ] **Master Identity:** $S(t) = \exp(-H(t))$ where $H(t) = \int_0^t h(u)du$.
- [ ] **Hazard Formula:** $h(t) = f(t)/S(t)$ — this is a *rate*, not a probability.
- [ ] **Exponential** model: $h(t) = \lambda$ (constant, memoryless).
- [ ] **Weibull** model: $h(t) = \lambda \alpha t^{\alpha - 1}$ — monotone hazard; $\alpha > 1$ increasing, $\alpha < 1$ decreasing.
- [ ] **Log-Logistic** model: non-monotone (unimodal) hazard — useful when failure rate rises then falls.
- [ ] **Right-censoring** is the norm in financial data and must be handled via the censored log-likelihood.
- [ ] **OLS is biased** under censoring — always use maximum likelihood with $S(c_i)$ for censored cases.
- [ ] **Cox Proportional Hazards (PH)** model: $h(t \mid X) = h_0(t)\exp(\beta'X)$ — semi-parametric, no assumption on $h_0(t)$.
- [ ] **PH assumption:** hazard ratio between subjects is **constant over time**; verify with Schoenfeld residuals.
- [ ] **Accelerated Failure Time (AFT) model:** $\log T = X\beta + \sigma\varepsilon$ — covariates accelerate/decelerate the time clock.
- [ ] **Kaplan–Meier estimator:** $\hat{S}(t) = \prod_{t_i \le t} (1 - d_i/n_i)$ — non-parametric, step function.
- [ ] **Nelson–Aalen estimator:** $\hat{H}(t) = \sum_{t_i \le t} d_i/n_i$ — non-parametric cumulative hazard.
- [ ] **Interpretation of $\beta$ in Cox:** $\exp(\beta_j) = $ **hazard ratio** for a one-unit change in $X_j$.
- [ ] **Financial Applications:** credit scoring, bond default modeling, IPO timing, unemployment duration, trade durations (ACD models).
- [ ] **Equidispersion of Exponential:** $E[T] = 1/\lambda$, $\text{Var}(T) = 1/\lambda^2$ — relaxes with Weibull/Gamma.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Value at Risk (VaR)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET742 - FINANCIAL TIME SERIES ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Value at Risk (VaR)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Value at Risk (VaR)** is a statistically rigorous, single-scalar quantitative measure that estimates the maximum potential financial loss a portfolio, asset, or trading position is expected to suffer over a specified holding (or horizon) period, under normal market conditions, at a predetermined confidence level.

In KTU 2024 Scheme formal notation, VaR is defined as the negative quantile of the loss distribution:

$$\text{VaR}_{\alpha}(X) = -\inf\{x \in \mathbb{R} : F_X(x) > \alpha\}$$

where:
- $X$ represents the random variable denoting the Profit and Loss (P&L) of the portfolio over the holding period.
- $F_X(x)$ is the cumulative distribution function (CDF) of $X$.
- $\alpha \in (0, 1)$ is the chosen **confidence level** (typically 95% or 99%).
- $\inf$ denotes the infimum (greatest lower bound) of the set.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Memorize Verbatim):**
> *Value at Risk (VaR) is the maximum loss that a portfolio will not exceed with a given probability $(1-\alpha)$ over a specified time horizon under normal market conditions.*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

Imagine you are a **merchant sailor** preparing for a voyage across a stormy sea. Before departing, you want to know: *"What is the worst-case wave height I should brace for, so that I am safe 95 out of 100 voyages?"*

- The **holding period** $\Delta t$ = the duration of your voyage (e.g., 1 day, 10 days).
- The **confidence level** $\alpha$ = the percentage of voyages where you want to be safe (e.g., 95%).
- The **VaR** = the worst wave height (i.e., maximum loss) that you will NOT exceed with that probability.

So if your 1-day VaR at the **99% confidence level is ₹10 lakh**, it means:
> *There is only a 1% probability that you will lose MORE than ₹10 lakh tomorrow. Conversely, 99 times out of 100, your loss will be at or below ₹10 lakh.*

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuition:** VaR does **NOT** tell you the loss when things go catastrophically wrong — it tells you the threshold you are safe within, *most of the time*. This subtle distinction is heavily tested in KTU exams.

### 1.3 Standardized Metrics (KTU Syllabus Highlights)

- **Standard Confidence Levels:** **95%** and **99%** (industry standard; Basel III mandates 99%).
- **Standard Holding Periods:** **1 day** (trading desks), **10 days** (Basel regulatory standard).
- **Risk Horizon:** Can be daily, weekly, or monthly.
- **Units:** Always expressed in the **domestic currency** (₹, $, €, etc.) or as a **percentage of portfolio value**.

### 1.4 Classification of VaR by Methodology

| Method | Type | Assumption |
|---|---|---|
| **Historical VaR** | Non-parametric | Past repeats itself |
| **Variance-Covariance VaR** | Parametric | Returns are normally distributed |
| **Monte Carlo VaR** | Simulation-based | Large sample approximates reality |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Foundational Logical Steps in VaR Computation

The VaR computation proceeds via a structured five-stage pipeline:

1. **Portfolio Identification:** Specify the assets, weights, and the notional value $V_0$ of the portfolio under analysis.
2. **Return Distribution Selection:** Decide whether returns are Gaussian (parametric), empirical (historical), or simulated (Monte Carlo).
3. **Confidence Level Fixing:** Choose $\alpha$ — typically **95%** (academic) or **99%** (Basel III regulatory).
4. **Quantile Extraction:** Locate the lower-tail quantile of the loss distribution.
5. **Scaling & Reporting:** Convert to monetary loss and adjust for the horizon using square-root-of-time scaling.

### 2.2 The Parametric (Variance-Covariance) Method — Deep Theory

Assume the portfolio return $R \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$. The portfolio value at the end of the horizon is:

$$V = V_0 (1 + R)$$

The loss $L$ is defined as:

$$L = V_0 - V = -V_0 \cdot R$$

Since $R \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$, then $L \sim \mathcal{N}(-V_0 \mu, V_0^2 \sigma^2)$.

The VaR at confidence level $\alpha$ becomes:

$$\text{VaR}_{\alpha} = V_0 \mu - z_{\alpha} \cdot V_0 \sigma = V_0 (\mu - z_{\alpha} \sigma)$$

where $z_{\alpha}$ is the standard normal critical value (e.g., $z_{0.95} = 1.645$, $z_{0.99} = 2.326$).

### 2.3 The Historical Simulation Method — Deep Theory

1. Collect $N$ historical returns $r_1, r_2, \ldots, r_N$.
2. Sort them in **ascending order** (worst losses first).
3. Identify the empirical quantile at the $(1-\alpha)$ position.
4. Report VaR as the negated empirical quantile:

$$\text{VaR}_{\alpha}^{\text{Hist}} = -r_{(\lfloor N(1-\alpha) \rfloor + 1)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Historical VaR?** It makes **NO distributional assumption**. It captures fat tails, skewness, and empirical anomalies — making it a favourite for KTU numerical problems.

### 2.4 The Monte Carlo Simulation Method — Deep Theory

1. Specify the stochastic process (e.g., Geometric Brownian Motion for equities).
2. Generate $M$ (typically 10,000+) simulated paths of returns over horizon $\Delta t$.
3. Compute the simulated P&L for each path.
4. Sort the simulated losses and extract the $(1-\alpha)$ quantile.

For a GBM-based equity:

$$S_{t+\Delta t} = S_t \cdot \exp\left[\left(\mu - \tfrac{1}{2}\sigma^2\right)\Delta t + \sigma \sqrt{\Delta t} \cdot Z\right], \quad Z \sim \mathcal{N}(0,1)$$

### 2.5 Square-Root-of-Time Scaling

When converting a 1-day VaR to a $T$-day VaR under i.i.d. normal assumptions:

$$\text{VaR}_{T} = \text{VaR}_{1} \cdot \sqrt{T}$$

> [!WARNING]
> This scaling **assumes i.i.d. returns and normality**. It is INVALID during periods of volatility clustering — a classic KTU trick question.

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula | Expression | Used For |
|---|---|---|
| VaR Definition (Quantile) | $\text{VaR}_{\alpha} = -\inf\{x : F_X(x) > \alpha\}$ | General mathematical definition |
| Parametric VaR (Normal) | $\text{VaR}_{\alpha} = -(\mu - z_{\alpha}\sigma) V_0$ | Single-asset parametric VaR |
| Portfolio Parametric VaR | $\text{VaR}_{\alpha} = z_{\alpha} \sqrt{\mathbf{w}^\top \Sigma \mathbf{w}}$ | Multi-asset portfolio |
| Historical VaR | $\text{VaR}_{\alpha} = -r_{(\lceil N(1-\alpha) \rceil)}$ | Empirical method |
| Time Scaling | $\text{VaR}_{T} = \text{VaR}_{1}\sqrt{T}$ | Horizon conversion |
| Component VaR | $\text{CVaR}_i = w_i \cdot \beta_i \cdot \text{VaR}_{p}$ | Marginal contribution |
| Marginal VaR | $\Delta \text{VaR}_p / \Delta w_i$ | Sensitivity analysis |
| Conditional VaR (CVaR) | $\text{CVaR}_{\alpha} = \mathbb{E}[L \mid L > \text{VaR}_{\alpha}]$ | Expected Shortfall |
| Backtesting (Kupiec) | $LR = -2 \ln\left[\frac{(1-p)^n}{(1-\hat{p})^{n-x} \hat{p}^x}\right]$ | Model validation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Utility:** VaR is the **cornerstone of modern risk management**. It is mandated by the Basel II/III accords for banks, used by the Reserve Bank of India (RBI) for treasury operations, and is the daily reporting metric on trading floors of Goldman Sachs, JP Morgan, and HSBC.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Derivation: Parametric VaR for a Single Asset

**Given:**
- Current portfolio value: $V_0 = \text{₹}10,00,000$
- Daily mean return: $\mu = 0.0005$ (0.05%)
- Daily standard deviation: $\sigma = 0.015$ (1.5%)
- Confidence level: $\alpha = 99\%$

**Step 1:** State the assumption. We assume returns follow a normal distribution $R \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$.

**Step 2:** Express the loss random variable. The dollar loss over one day is:

$$L = V_0 - V_0(1+R) = -V_0 R$$

**Step 3:** Identify the distribution of $L$. Since $R$ is normal, $L$ is also normal with mean $-V_0 \mu$ and standard deviation $V_0 \sigma$.

**Step 4:** Apply the VaR quantile definition. We seek the value $\ell$ such that:

$$P(L \leq \ell) = 1 - \alpha = 0.01$$

**Step 5:** Standardize the loss variable:

$$Z = \frac{L - (-V_0 \mu)}{V_0 \sigma} = \frac{L + V_0 \mu}{V_0 \sigma}$$

**Step 6:** Use the inverse standard normal CDF. From the standard normal table, $z_{0.01} = -2.326$. Therefore:

$$\frac{\ell + V_0 \mu}{V_0 \sigma} = -2.326$$

**Step 7:** Solve algebraically for $\ell$:

$$\ell + V_0 \mu = -2.326 \cdot V_0 \sigma$$

$$\ell = -V_0 \mu - 2.326 \cdot V_0 \sigma$$

$$\ell = -V_0(\mu + 2.326 \sigma)$$

**Step 8:** Substitute numerical values:

$$\ell = -10,00,000 \cdot (0.0005 + 2.326 \cdot 0.015)$$

$$\ell = -10,00,000 \cdot (0.0005 + 0.03489)$$

$$\ell = -10,00,000 \cdot 0.03539$$

$$\ell = -\text{₹}35,390$$

**Step 9:** Report VaR as the positive magnitude of loss:

$$\boxed{\text{VaR}_{99\%} = \text{₹}35,390}$$

### 3.2 Detailed Derivation: Portfolio VaR with Two Assets

**Given:**
- Asset A: weight $w_A = 0.6$, $\sigma_A = 0.02$
- Asset B: weight $w_B = 0.4$, $\sigma_B = 0.03$
- Correlation: $\rho_{AB} = 0.4$
- Portfolio value: $V_0 = \text{₹}50,00,000$
- Confidence: $\alpha = 95\%$, so $z_{0.95} = 1.645$

**Step 1:** Compute the portfolio variance using the bilinear form:

$$\sigma_p^2 = w_A^2 \sigma_A^2 + w_B^2 \sigma_B^2 + 2 w_A w_B \rho_{AB} \sigma_A \sigma_B$$

**Step 2:** Plug in values:

$$\sigma_p^2 = (0.6)^2 (0.02)^2 + (0.4)^2 (0.03)^2 + 2(0.6)(0.4)(0.4)(0.02)(0.03)$$

$$\sigma_p^2 = 0.36 \times 0.0004 + 0.16 \times 0.0009 + 0.192 \times 0.0006$$

$$\sigma_p^2 = 0.000144 + 0.000144 + 0.0001152$$

$$\sigma_p^2 = 0.0004032$$

**Step 3:** Take the square root to get portfolio standard deviation:

$$\sigma_p = \sqrt{0.0004032} = 0.02008$$

**Step 4:** Apply the portfolio VaR formula:

$$\text{VaR}_{95\%} = z_{0.95} \cdot \sigma_p \cdot V_0$$

$$\text{VaR}_{95\%} = 1.645 \times 0.02008 \times 50,00,000$$

$$\boxed{\text{VaR}_{95\%} = \text{₹}1,65,158}$$

### 3.3 Detailed Derivation: Time Scaling Application

**Given:** 1-day VaR = ₹1,65,158, scaling to a 10-day horizon.

**Step 1:** Apply the square-root-of-time rule:

$$\text{VaR}_{10} = \text{VaR}_{1} \times \sqrt{10}$$

$$\text{VaR}_{10} = 1,65,158 \times 3.1623$$

$$\boxed{\text{VaR}_{10} = \text{₹}5,22,427}$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of VaR Methods

| Property | Historical | Parametric (V-C) | Monte Carlo |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Distributional Assumption** | None | Normality | Flexible |
| **Tail Behaviour Capture** | Empirical | Symmetric | Configurable |
| **Computational Cost** | Low | Very Low | Very High |
| **Fat-Tail Handling** | Yes | No (unless extended) | Yes (if specified) |
| **Data Requirement** | Large historical sample | Mean & covariance only | Simulated paths |
| **Use Case** | Backtesting | Daily reporting | Exotic derivatives |
| **Common Error Risk** | Ghosting effect | Underestimates extremes | Spec error |

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example: Historical Simulation VaR

**Given:** The 10 most recent daily returns (in %) of a stock: $\{-2.8, -1.5, -0.7, +0.3, +1.1, -3.2, +0.8, -0.2, +2.0, -1.1\}$. We need 95% Historical VaR.

**Step 1:** Sort in ascending order:

$$\{-3.2, -2.8, -1.5, -1.1, -0.7, -0.2, +0.3, +0.8, +1.1, +2.0\}$$

**Step 2:** Identify the position. With $N = 10$ and $\alpha = 95\%$, we need the $N(1-\alpha) = 10 \times 0.05 = 0.5 \rightarrow$ use the 1st element (the most extreme loss).

**Step 3:** The 1st ordered return is $-3.2\%$.

**Step 4:** VaR is the negated value:

$$\text{VaR}_{95\%}^{\text{Hist}} = -(-3.2\%) = 3.2\% \text{ of portfolio}$$

**Step 5:** For a ₹5,00,000 portfolio:

$$\boxed{\text{VaR}_{95\%}^{\text{Hist}} = 0.032 \times 5,00,000 = \text{₹}16,000}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of VaR Computation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Input Portfolio Data] --> nodeB[Select VaR Methodology]
    nodeB --> nodeC{Method Choice}
    nodeC --> nodeD[Historical Simulation]
    nodeC --> nodeE[Variance Covariance Parametric]
    nodeC --> nodeF[Monte Carlo Simulation]
    nodeD --> nodeG[Sort Historical Returns]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Extract Empirical Quantile]
    nodeE --> nodeI[Estimate Mean and Sigma]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Apply Z Score Formula]
    nodeF --> nodeK[Generate Simulated Returns]
    nodeK --> nodeL[Sort Simulated Losses]
    nodeH --> nodeM[VaR Output]
    nodeJ --> nodeM
    nodeL --> nodeM
    nodeM --> nodeN[Time Scaling Adjustment]
    nodeN --> nodeO[Final VaR Report]
```

### 4.2 Process Flow: Backtesting VaR with Kupiec Test

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeP[Observed VaR Series] --> nodeQ[Count Exceptions]
    nodeQ --> nodeR[Expected Exception Rate]
    nodeR --> nodeS[Compute Likelihood Ratio]
    nodeS --> nodeT{Compare with Critical Chi Square}
    nodeT --> nodeU[Model Accepted]
    nodeT --> nodeV[Model Rejected]
    nodeV --> nodeW[Recalibrate or Upgrade Model]
```

### 4.3 Component VaR Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeX[Portfolio VaR] --> nodeY[Asset 1 Component VaR]
    nodeX --> nodeZ[Asset 2 Component VaR]
    nodeX --> nodeAA[Asset N Component VaR]
    nodeY --> nodeBB[Weight times Beta times Portfolio VaR]
    nodeZ --> nodeBB
    nodeAA --> nodeBB
    nodeBB --> nodeCC[Diversification Benefit]
```

### 4.4 Schematic: VaR Concept on Loss Distribution Curve

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeDD[Loss Distribution X] --> nodeEE[Tail Region]
    nodeEE --> nodeFF[Quantile at alpha equals 0.05]
    nodeFF --> nodeGG[VaR equals 0]
    nodeGG --> nodeHH[Loss NOT exceeded 95 percent of time]
    nodeHH --> nodeII[95 percent Confidence Safe Zone]
```

### 4.5 Decision Tree: Which VaR Method to Apply?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeJJ{Is normality valid?}
    nodeJJ --> nodeKK[Yes] --> nodeLL[Use Variance Covariance]
    nodeJJ --> nodeMM[No] --> nodeNN{Distribution known analytically?}
    nodeNN --> nodeOO[Yes] --> nodePP[Use Analytical VaR with Cornish Fisher]
    nodeNN --> nodeQQ[No] --> nodeRR{Is data available?}
    nodeRR --> nodeSS[Yes and large] --> nodeTT[Use Historical Simulation]
    nodeRR --> nodeUU[No or paths needed] --> nodeVV[Use Monte Carlo Simulation]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Define Value at Risk (VaR). State its significance in financial risk management.

**Model Answer:**
Value at Risk (VaR) is a statistical measure that quantifies the maximum expected loss of a portfolio over a specified holding period at a given confidence level, under normal market conditions. Mathematically, it is the negative of the $\alpha$-quantile of the loss distribution:

$$\text{VaR}_{\alpha} = -\inf\{x \in \mathbb{R} : F_X(x) > \alpha\}$$

**Significance:**
- It provides a **single-number summary** of portfolio risk.
- It enables **comparison** across different portfolios and asset classes.
- It is the **regulatory benchmark** mandated by the Basel III framework.
- It supports **capital allocation** and **risk budgeting** decisions.

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024] Differentiate between Historical Simulation VaR and Parametric VaR.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Historical VaR | Parametric VaR |
|---|---|---|
| Distribution | Non-parametric | Assumes normality |
| Data Required | Large historical sample | Mean and standard deviation |
| Fat Tails | Captured automatically | Underestimated |
| Computation | Simple sorting | Closed-form formula |
| Limitation | Ghost effect | Misses empirical anomalies |

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Choose Either A or B)

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** A portfolio worth ₹25,00,000 has a daily mean return of 0.08% and a daily standard deviation of 1.8%. Compute the 1-day 99% Value at Risk using the parametric method.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the backtesting procedure of VaR using the Kupiec test.

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Step 1:** Identify parameters.
- $V_0 = 25,00,000$
- $\mu = 0.0008$
- $\sigma = 0.018$
- $z_{0.99} = 2.326$

**Step 2:** Apply the parametric formula:

$$\text{VaR}_{99\%} = -V_0 (\mu - z_{0.99} \sigma)$$

**Step 3:** Compute the inner bracket:

$$\mu - z_{0.99} \sigma = 0.0008 - (2.326)(0.018) = 0.0008 - 0.041868 = -0.041068$$

**Step 4:** Multiply by portfolio value:

$$\text{VaR}_{99\%} = -25,00,000 \times (-0.041068) = \text{₹}1,02,670$$

$$\boxed{\text{VaR}_{99\%} = \text{₹}1,02,670}$$

**Model Solution for (b):**

The **Kupiec Proportion of Failures (POF) test** validates whether the observed exception rate matches the expected rate.

**Step 1:** Define variables.
- $N$ = total number of VaR observations
- $x$ = number of exceptions (days where actual loss > VaR)
- $p = 1 - \alpha$ = expected exception probability

**Step 2:** Construct the likelihood ratio statistic:

$$LR_{POF} = -2 \ln\left[\frac{(1-p)^{N-x} p^x}{\left(1 - \tfrac{x}{N}\right)^{N-x} \left(\tfrac{x}{N}\right)^x}\right]$$

**Step 3:** Decision rule:
- $LR_{POF} \sim \chi^2_{(1)}$ asymptotically.
- Reject the model if $LR_{POF} > \chi^2_{(1, 0.95)} = 3.841$.

**Step 4:** Interpretation:
- If rejected, the model **underestimates or overestimates** tail risk.
- Recalibrate volatility or switch to a heavier-tailed distribution.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** A portfolio contains two assets. Asset X has weight 0.7, standard deviation 2.5%, and Asset Y has weight 0.3, standard deviation 4.0%. The correlation between them is 0.3. Compute the 95% 1-day VaR for a portfolio valued at ₹40,00,000.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the Monte Carlo simulation method for computing VaR.

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Step 1:** Compute portfolio variance:

$$\sigma_p^2 = w_X^2 \sigma_X^2 + w_Y^2 \sigma_Y^2 + 2 w_X w_Y \rho \sigma_X \sigma_Y$$

**Step 2:** Substitute:

$$\sigma_p^2 = (0.7)^2(0.025)^2 + (0.3)^2(0.04)^2 + 2(0.7)(0.3)(0.3)(0.025)(0.04)$$

$$\sigma_p^2 = 0.00030625 + 0.000144 + 0.000126$$

$$\sigma_p^2 = 0.00057625$$

**Step 3:** Standard deviation:

$$\sigma_p = \sqrt{0.00057625} = 0.02400$$

**Step 4:** Apply VaR formula with $z_{0.95} = 1.645$:

$$\text{VaR}_{95\%} = 1.645 \times 0.02400 \times 40,00,000$$

$$\boxed{\text{VaR}_{95\%} = \text{₹}1,57,920}$$

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Monte Carlo Simulation VaR — Step-by-Step Procedure:**

1. **Specify the stochastic process** for asset returns, e.g., Geometric Brownian Motion:

$$S_{t+\Delta t} = S_t \exp\left[\left(\mu - \tfrac{1}{2}\sigma^2\right)\Delta t + \sigma \sqrt{\Delta t} Z\right]$$

2. **Generate $M$ random paths** where $Z \sim \mathcal{N}(0,1)$. Typical $M = 10,000$ or more.

3. **Compute the terminal portfolio value** for each simulated path:

$$V_j^{(s)} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i S_{i,j}^{(s)}, \quad j = 1, 2, \ldots, M$$

4. **Calculate the simulated P&L**:

$$\text{P\&L}_j = V_j^{(s)} - V_0$$

5. **Sort the simulated P&L** in ascending order.

6. **Extract the $(1-\alpha)$ quantile** to obtain the VaR:

$$\text{VaR}_{\alpha}^{MC} = -\text{P\&L}_{(\lceil M(1-\alpha) \rceil)}$$

7. **Diagrammatic representation:**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Input Parameters mu sigma S0] --> nodeB[Generate Random Z Standard Normal]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Simulate Asset Price Path]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Compute Portfolio Value]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Compute P and L]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Repeat 10000 times]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Sort P and L]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Extract Quantile]
    nodeH --> nodeI[VaR Output]
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> - Many students forget to **NEGATE the quantile** when reporting VaR as a positive loss. Marks are deducted immediately.
> - Confusing **confidence level** with **failure probability** is a frequent error. At 99% confidence, the failure rate is **1%**, not 99%.
> - Students often skip the **square-root-of-time scaling** derivation step. Board examiners specifically check this step for partial credit.
> - In Monte Carlo problems, failing to mention the **number of simulations** ($M$) loses methodology marks.
> - Writing $|x|$ in markdown tables will break the table syntax — always use $\vert x \vert$ in exam answer scripts too.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: Value at Risk**

- **Definition:** VaR is the maximum loss at confidence level $\alpha$ over horizon $T$ — expressed as a **positive number** denoting loss.
- **Three Methods:** Historical (non-parametric), Variance-Covariance (parametric/normal), Monte Carlo (simulation).
- **Parametric Formula:** $\text{VaR}_{\alpha} = V_0 (\mu - z_{\alpha} \sigma)$ for a single asset; $\text{VaR}_{\alpha} = z_{\alpha} \sigma_p V_0$ for a portfolio.
- **Portfolio Std Dev:** Use bilinear form $\sigma_p^2 = \mathbf{w}^\top \Sigma \mathbf{w}$ — **never** compute simple weighted average.
- **Standard Z-values:** $z_{0.95} = 1.645$, $z_{0.99} = 2.326$, $z_{0.975} = 1.96$.
- **Time Scaling:** $\text{VaR}_{T} = \text{VaR}_{1}\sqrt{T}$ — only valid under **i.i.d. normality**.
- **Historical VaR Procedure:** Sort returns ascending → take the **worst** $(1-\alpha)\%$ → negate.
- **Backtesting:** Kupiec POF test statistic $LR_{POF} \sim \chi^2_{(1)}$; reject if $> 3.841$.
- **CVaR (Expected Shortfall):** Average loss beyond VaR — addresses the **tail blindness** of VaR.
- **Limitations:** VaR is **NOT coherent** (fails sub-additivity); it does not capture loss magnitude beyond the quantile.
- **Regulatory Standard:** Basel III mandates **99% confidence, 10-day horizon** for market risk capital.
- **Common Trap:** VaR is always reported as a **positive** number even though it is derived from a quantile of the **negative** loss distribution.
- **Exam Tip:** Always state assumptions (normality, i.i.d., zero drift) before applying the parametric formula — examiners award marks for this declaration.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Risk Metrics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET742 - FINANCIAL TIME SERIES ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Risk Metrics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 What are Risk Metrics?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
> **Risk Metrics** are quantitative statistical measures used to estimate, monitor, and control the downside uncertainty associated with holding a portfolio or asset over a specified time horizon at a given confidence level. They convert volatile price/return processes into a single number (or curve) that decision-makers, regulators, and traders can act upon.

In the language of the **Basel Committee on Banking Supervision (BCBS)** and modern portfolio theory, a risk metric is a functional mapping from a stochastic return process $\{R_t\}_{t=1}^{T}$ to a real-valued scalar or function:

$$\text{RiskMetric} : \{R_t\}_{t=1}^{T} \;\longrightarrow\; \mathbb{R} \;\;\text{or}\;\; [0,1]$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The Weather Forecast:**
> Imagine you are planning a wedding outdoors. The meteorologist does not just say "it may rain"; they quantify risk with statements like *"there is a 95% probability of less than 10 mm of rain."* Similarly, **Risk Metrics do not say "I might lose money"** — they say *"with 99% confidence, my maximum loss tomorrow will not exceed ₹4.5 lakhs."* That single number is the Value-at-Risk (VaR). The Risk Metrics toolkit is the meteorologist's full dashboard: probability of rain (probability of loss), expected rainfall given rain (expected shortfall), and the worst drought in the past 10 years (maximum drawdown).

## 1.2 Classification of Risk Metrics

| Family | Examples | What it Measures |
|---|---|---|
| **Quantile-based (Coherent-ish)** | **VaR**, **CVaR / ES** | Threshold losses at a confidence level |
| **Volatility-based** | **Standard Deviation**, **GARCH-$\sigma_t$**, **EWMA $\lambda$** | Dispersion of returns |
| **Drawdown-based** | **MDD**, **Calmar Ratio** | Peak-to-trough decline |
| **Return-to-Risk Ratios** | **Sharpe**, **Sortino**, **Treynor**, **Information Ratio** | Reward per unit of risk |
| **Systematic Risk** | **Beta ($\beta$)**, **Tracking Error** | Exposure to market / benchmark |
| **Tail / Distress** | **Skewness**, **Kurtosis**, **Expected Shortfall**, **Stress VaR** | Behaviour in extreme scenarios |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module Focus:**
> The syllabus explicitly anchors Module 4 on **Value-at-Risk (VaR)** and **Expected Shortfall (ES)** along with **drawdown measures, volatility models (EWMA, GARCH), and performance ratios (Sharpe, Sortino, Beta, Tracking Error)**. All exam questions will revolve around this closed set.

## 1.3 Why Risk Metrics Matter in Engineering & Finance

1. **Capital Adequacy:** Banks (Basel III) must hold capital $\geq$ **VaR$_{99\%, 10\text{-day}}$**.
2. **Algorithmic Trading:** Risk parity funds allocate capital *inversely* to **$\sigma_t$** estimated via **GARCH**.
3. **Corporate Hedging:** Treasurers measure **Maximum Drawdown (MDD)** to set stop-loss policies.
4. **Performance Attribution:** Hedge funds advertise **Sharpe Ratios > 1** as a quality seal.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic — "V-VED-SB-SD":**
> **V**aR, **V**olatility → **E**xpected **S**hortfall, **D**rawdown → **S**harpe, **B**eta → **S**ortino, **D**ownside deviation.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Value-at-Risk (VaR)

### 2.1.1 Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition — Value-at-Risk:**
> For a portfolio with random loss $L$ over horizon $h$, and confidence level $\alpha \in (0,1)$, the **VaR** is the smallest number $v$ such that the probability of loss exceeding $v$ is at most $(1-\alpha)$:
> $$\text{VaR}_{\alpha}(L) \;=\; \inf\{v \in \mathbb{R} : \Pr(L \le v) \ge \alpha\} \;=\; -F^{-1}_L(1-\alpha)$$
> Equivalently, for the **profit-and-loss (P&L)** random variable $X = -L$:
> $$\text{VaR}_{\alpha}(X) \;=\; -q_{1-\alpha}(X) \;=\; -\inf\{x : F_X(x) \ge 1-\alpha\}$$
> where $q_{p}(\cdot)$ is the $p$-th quantile function.

### 2.1.2 Three Computational Engines for VaR

#### (A) Historical Simulation VaR
Sort empirical returns $\{r_1, r_2, \dots, r_T\}$ in ascending order, then pick the empirical $\alpha$-quantile:

$$\widehat{\text{VaR}}_{\alpha}^{\text{HS}} \;=\; -\,\hat{q}_{1-\alpha}(\{r_t\}_{t=1}^{T})$$

- **Why:** No distributional assumption; non-parametric.
- **How:** Sort losses, take the $(1-\alpha)$-percentile.
- **Drawback:** Slow reaction to new volatility regimes.

#### (B) Variance-Covariance (Parametric / Gaussian) VaR
Assume $r_t \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$. Then:

$$\widehat{\text{VaR}}_{\alpha}^{\text{VC}} \;=\; -\bigl(\hat{\mu} - z_{1-\alpha}\,\hat{\sigma}\bigr)$$

where $z_{1-\alpha}$ is the standard normal quantile. For $\alpha = 0.99$, $z_{0.99} \approx 2.326$; for $\alpha = 0.975$, $z_{0.025}^{-1} = 1.96$.

#### (C) Monte Carlo VaR
Simulate $M$ paths of $r_{t+h}$ under a calibrated model (e.g., GARCH or GBM), compute terminal P&L $L_i$, then:

$$\widehat{\text{VaR}}_{\alpha}^{\text{MC}} \;=\; -\,\hat{q}_{1-\alpha}(\{L_i\}_{i=1}^{M})$$

### 2.1.3 VaR Backtesting — Kupiec Test

To verify if the realised "exception" (loss > VaR) frequency matches $\hat{p} = 1-\alpha$, the **Kupiec likelihood-ratio statistic** is:

$$\text{LR}_{\text{Kupiec}} \;=\; -2\ln\!\Bigl[(1-p)^{T-N}\,p^{N}\Bigr] \;+\; 2\ln\!\Bigl[\bigl(1-\tfrac{N}{T}\bigr)^{T-N}\bigl(\tfrac{N}{T}\bigr)^{N}\Bigr]$$

where $T$ = number of observations and $N$ = number of VaR breaches. Under $H_0$ (model is correct), $\text{LR}_{\text{Kupiec}} \sim \chi^2_1$.

---

## 2.2 Expected Shortfall (ES) / Conditional VaR (CVaR)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition — Expected Shortfall:**
> $$\text{ES}_{\alpha}(X) \;=\; \mathbb{E}\!\left[\,X \,\big\vert\, X \le -\text{VaR}_{\alpha}(X)\,\right] \;=\; -\dfrac{1}{1-\alpha}\int_{0}^{1-\alpha} q_u(X)\,du$$
> For a continuous distribution $F$ with density $f$:
> $$\text{ES}_{\alpha}(X) \;=\; -\dfrac{1}{1-\alpha}\int_{-\infty}^{-F^{-1}_X(1-\alpha)} x\,f_X(x)\,dx$$

For the Gaussian case with $\mu = 0$, the closed form is:

$$\text{ES}_{\alpha}^{\mathcal{N}} \;=\; \sigma\,\dfrac{\varphi(z_{1-\alpha})}{1-\alpha}$$

where $\varphi(\cdot)$ is the standard normal PDF.

> [!NOTE]
> **Coherence:** Unlike VaR, **ES is a coherent risk measure** (Artzner et al., 1999) — it satisfies *sub-additivity, monotonicity, translation invariance, and positive homogeneity.* This is why **Basel 3.5 (FRTB)** replaces VaR with **ES at 97.5%** for market risk capital.

---

## 2.3 Volatility-Based Risk Metrics

### 2.3.1 Realised Volatility (Sample Standard Deviation)

$$\hat{\sigma}_T \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{T-1}\sum_{t=1}^{T}(r_t - \bar{r})^2}$$

### 2.3.2 Exponentially Weighted Moving Average (EWMA) — RiskMetrics™

$$\hat{\sigma}_t^2 \;=\; \lambda\,\hat{\sigma}_{t-1}^2 \;+\; (1-\lambda)\,r_{t-1}^2, \qquad \lambda \in [0.94, 0.99]$$

- $\lambda = 0.94$ for daily; $\lambda = 0.97$ for monthly (JP Morgan RiskMetrics default).

### 2.3.3 GARCH(1,1) Conditional Volatility

$$\sigma_t^2 \;=\; \omega \;+\; \alpha\,r_{t-1}^2 \;+\; \beta\,\sigma_{t-1}^2$$

with persistence $\alpha + \beta < 1$ and unconditional variance $\sigma^2 = \omega / (1 - \alpha - \beta)$.

The **1-step-ahead VaR under GARCH** is then:

$$\text{VaR}_{\alpha, t+1}^{\text{GARCH}} \;=\; -\mu_t \;+\; z_{1-\alpha}\,\hat{\sigma}_{t+1}$$

---

## 2.4 Drawdown-Based Metrics

### 2.4.1 Drawdown at Time $t$

$$DD_t \;=\; \dfrac{P_t - M_t}{M_t}, \qquad M_t = \max_{0 \le s \le t} P_s$$

### 2.4.2 Maximum Drawdown (MDD)

$$\text{MDD}_T \;=\; \min_{0 \le t \le T} DD_t \;=\; \min_{0 \le t \le T}\;\dfrac{P_t - M_t}{M_t}$$

### 2.4.3 Calmar Ratio

$$\text{Calmar} \;=\; \dfrac{\bar{R}_T}{\vert\text{MDD}_T\vert}$$

---

## 2.5 Return-to-Risk Performance Ratios

### 2.5.1 Sharpe Ratio

$$\text{Sharpe} \;=\; \dfrac{\bar{R}_p - R_f}{\hat{\sigma}_p}$$

- Excess return per unit of *total* volatility.

### 2.5.2 Sortino Ratio

$$\text{Sortino} \;=\; \dfrac{\bar{R}_p - R_f}{\hat{\sigma}_d}, \qquad \hat{\sigma}_d = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{T}\sum_{t=1}^{T}\min(r_t - R_f,\,0)^2}$$

- Excess return per unit of *downside* deviation — penalises only harmful volatility.

### 2.5.3 Treynor Ratio

$$\text{Treynor} \;=\; \dfrac{\bar{R}_p - R_f}{\hat{\beta}_p}$$

- Excess return per unit of *systematic* (market) risk.

---

## 2.6 Systematic Risk — Beta ($\beta$) and Tracking Error

### 2.6.1 Beta from CAPM / Market Model

$$r_{p,t} - r_{f,t} \;=\; \alpha_p \;+\; \beta_p\,(r_{m,t} - r_{f,t}) \;+\; \varepsilon_t$$

$$\hat{\beta}_p \;=\; \dfrac{\text{Cov}(r_p, r_m)}{\text{Var}(r_m)} \;=\; \dfrac{\sum_{t=1}^{T}(r_{p,t}-\bar{r}_p)(r_{m,t}-\bar{r}_m)}{\sum_{t=1}^{T}(r_{m,t}-\bar{r}_m)^2}$$

### 2.6.2 Tracking Error

$$\text{TE} \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{T-1}\sum_{t=1}^{T}(r_{p,t} - r_{b,t} - \bar{d})^2}$$

where $d_t = r_{p,t} - r_{b,t}$ is the active return and $\bar{d}$ its mean.

### 2.6.3 Information Ratio

$$\text{IR} \;=\; \dfrac{\bar{d}}{\text{TE}}$$

---

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Metric | Formula | Key Inputs | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Historical VaR** | $-\hat{q}_{1-\alpha}(\{r_t\})$ | Sorted returns | Currency |
| 2 | **Parametric VaR** | $-(\mu - z_{1-\alpha}\sigma)$ | $\mu, \sigma, z$ | Currency |
| 3 | **Monte Carlo VaR** | $-\hat{q}_{1-\alpha}(\{L_i\})$ | Simulated P&L | Currency |
| 4 | **Expected Shortfall (Normal)** | $\sigma\,\varphi(z_{1-\alpha})/(1-\alpha)$ | $\sigma, z, \alpha$ | Currency |
| 5 | **Sample Volatility** | $\sqrt{\tfrac{1}{T-1}\sum(r_t-\bar{r})^2}$ | Returns | % or decimal |
| 6 | **EWMA Volatility** | $\sigma_t^2=\lambda\sigma_{t-1}^2+(1-\lambda)r_{t-1}^2$ | $r_t, \lambda$ | % or decimal |
| 7 | **GARCH(1,1)** | $\sigma_t^2=\omega+\alpha r_{t-1}^2+\beta\sigma_{t-1}^2$ | $r_t, \omega, \alpha, \beta$ | squared returns |
| 8 | **Max Drawdown** | $\min_t (P_t - M_t)/M_t$ | Price series | Decimal (negative) |
| 9 | **Sharpe Ratio** | $(\bar{R}_p - R_f)/\hat{\sigma}_p$ | Returns, $R_f$ | Dimensionless |
| 10 | **Sortino Ratio** | $(\bar{R}_p - R_f)/\hat{\sigma}_d$ | Returns, MAR | Dimensionless |
| 11 | **Treynor Ratio** | $(\bar{R}_p - R_f)/\hat{\beta}_p$ | Returns, $\beta$ | Dimensionless |
| 12 | **Beta** | $\text{Cov}(r_p, r_m)/\text{Var}(r_m)$ | Returns | Dimensionless |
| 13 | **Tracking Error** | $\text{std}(r_p - r_b)$ | Active returns | % |
| 14 | **Information Ratio** | $\bar{d}/\text{TE}$ | Active returns | Dimensionless |
| 15 | **Kupiec LR** | $-2\ln[(1-p)^{T-N}p^N]+2\ln[\cdot]$ | Breaches $N$, $T$ | $\chi^2_1$ |
| 16 | **Calmar Ratio** | $\bar{R}_T/\vert\text{MDD}_T\vert$ | Returns, MDD | Dimensionless |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall (KTU Valuation):** When stating VaR, **always specify confidence $\alpha$ and horizon $h$** (e.g., *1-day 99% VaR = ₹4.5L*). A bare number without these qualifiers is treated as a *non-answer* and may fetch 0 marks.

---

## 2.8 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

- **Quantitative Finance:** Algorithmic desks compute **GARCH-VaR** every minute for limit monitoring.
- **Banking Regulation:** Basel III uses **99% 10-day VaR** and (post-FRTB) **97.5% ES** for capital.
- **Insurance:** Re-insurers measure **MDD** on catastrophe-bond portfolios.
- **Energy Trading:** Power-grid risk desks use **Monte Carlo VaR** on spark-spread portfolios.
- **Supply-Chain Engineering:** Inventory risk measured using **maximum-drawdown** analogues.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Parametric VaR under Normality

**Given:** $r_t \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$ independently over horizon $h$.

**Step 1 — Standardise:**
Let $Z = \dfrac{r - \mu}{\sigma}$. Then $Z \sim \mathcal{N}(0,1)$.

**Step 2 — Define VaR via Quantile:**
By definition, $\Pr(r \le -\text{VaR}_{\alpha}) = 1-\alpha$.

**Step 3 — Transform to Standard Normal:**
$$\Pr\!\left(\dfrac{r - \mu}{\sigma} \le \dfrac{-\text{VaR}_\alpha - \mu}{\sigma}\right) = 1-\alpha$$

**Step 4 — Use $\Phi(z_{1-\alpha}) = 1-\alpha$:**
$$\dfrac{-\text{VaR}_\alpha - \mu}{\sigma} = z_{1-\alpha}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for VaR:**
$$\text{VaR}_\alpha \;=\; -\mu \;-\; z_{1-\alpha}\,\sigma$$

Equivalently, expressed in terms of loss $L = -r$:

$$\boxed{\;\text{VaR}_\alpha(L) \;=\; \mu \;-\; z_{1-\alpha}\,\sigma\;}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign Convention:** If returns $r$ are P&L (gain positive), then "loss" is $-r$, and $\text{VaR}_\alpha = -(z_{1-\alpha}\sigma - \mu) = \mu - z_{1-\alpha}\sigma$. If you are using **losses** $L$ directly, $\text{VaR}_\alpha(L) = \mu_L + z_{1-\alpha}\sigma_L$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Expected Shortfall for the Normal Distribution

**Given:** $X \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$ (WLOG zero-mean for clarity).

**Step 1 — Write ES as a Tail Integral:**
$$\text{ES}_\alpha \;=\; -\dfrac{1}{1-\alpha}\int_{-\infty}^{-z_{1-\alpha}\sigma} \dfrac{x}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}}\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{x^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)dx$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $u = x/\sigma$, $du = dx/\sigma$:**
$$\text{ES}_\alpha \;=\; -\dfrac{\sigma}{1-\alpha}\int_{-\infty}^{-z_{1-\alpha}} u\,\varphi(u)\,du$$

**Step 3 — Integrate by Parts (or use known identity):**
Recall that for the standard normal:
$$\int_{-\infty}^{-a} u\,\varphi(u)\,du \;=\; -\varphi(a)$$

This follows because $\dfrac{d}{du}\varphi(u) = -u\,\varphi(u)$, hence:
$$\int_{-\infty}^{-a} u\,\varphi(u)\,du \;=\; -\int_{-\infty}^{-a}\dfrac{d}{du}\varphi(u)\,du \;=\; -[\varphi(-a) - 0] \;=\; -\varphi(a)$$

**Step 4 — Plug back:**
$$\text{ES}_\alpha \;=\; -\dfrac{\sigma}{1-\alpha}\,(-\varphi(z_{1-\alpha})) \;=\; \dfrac{\sigma\,\varphi(z_{1-\alpha})}{1-\alpha}$$

$$\boxed{\;\text{ES}_\alpha \;=\; \sigma\,\dfrac{\varphi(z_{1-\alpha})}{1-\alpha}\;}$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Parametric VaR

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical Worked Example:**
> A portfolio has daily mean return $\hat{\mu} = 0.0005$, daily standard deviation $\hat{\sigma} = 0.012$ (i.e., 1.2%). Find the 1-day 99% VaR of a ₹1,00,00,000 (1 crore) position.

**Step 1 — Identify $z_{0.99}$:**
From standard normal table, $z_{0.99} \approx 2.3263$.

**Step 2 — Compute VaR in Return Units:**
$$\text{VaR}_{0.99} \;=\; -\bigl(\hat{\mu} - z_{0.99}\hat{\sigma}\bigr) \;=\; -\bigl(0.0005 - 2.3263 \times 0.012\bigr)$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the Bracket:**
$$2.3263 \times 0.012 \;=\; 0.0279156$$
$$0.0005 - 0.0279156 \;=\; -0.0274156$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Negative Sign:**
$$\text{VaR}_{0.99} \;=\; 0.0274156$$

**Step 5 — Multiply by Portfolio Value:**
$$\text{VaR}_{0.99}^{\text{₹}} \;=\; 0.0274156 \times 1{,}00{,}00{,}000 \;=\; \text{₹}\,2{,}74{,}156$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** With 99% confidence, the portfolio will *not* lose more than **₹2,74,156** over the next trading day.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Expected Shortfall

Using the same data: $\sigma = 0.012$, $\alpha = 0.99$.

**Step 1 — Compute $\varphi(z_{0.99})$:**
$$\varphi(2.3263) \;=\; \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{2.3263^2}{2}\right) \;=\; 0.39894 \times \exp(-2.7058) \;=\; 0.39894 \times 0.06681$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate:**
$$\varphi(2.3263) \;\approx\; 0.02665$$

**Step 3 — Compute ES:**
$$\text{ES}_{0.99} \;=\; 0.012 \times \dfrac{0.02665}{0.01} \;=\; 0.012 \times 2.665 \;=\; 0.03198$$

**Step 4 — Rupee Value:**
$$\text{ES}_{0.99}^{\text{₹}} \;=\; 0.03198 \times 1\text{ Cr} \;=\; \text{₹}\,3{,}19{,}800$$

> [!TIP]
> **Sanity Check:** ES $\approx 1.166 \times$ VaR here. This ratio $\text{ES}/\text{VaR} = \varphi(z_{1-\alpha})/[(1-\alpha)z_{1-\alpha}]$ grows as $\alpha \to 1$ — tails get fatter than VaR suggests.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — Sharpe, Sortino, Beta

Suppose a fund has monthly excess returns $\{0.02, -0.01, 0.03, -0.02, 0.04, 0.01\}$ over 6 months; the market excess returns over the same period are $\{0.015, -0.005, 0.025, -0.015, 0.030, 0.005\}$.

**Step 1 — Means:**
$$\bar{r}_p = (0.02 - 0.01 + 0.03 - 0.02 + 0.04 + 0.01)/6 = 0.07/6 = 0.01167$$
$$\bar{r}_m = (0.015 - 0.005 + 0.025 - 0.015 + 0.030 + 0.005)/6 = 0.055/6 = 0.00917$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\hat{\sigma}_p$:**
Deviations: $0.00833, -0.02167, 0.01833, -0.03167, 0.02833, -0.00167$.
Squared: $6.94\text{e-}5, 4.70\text{e-}4, 3.36\text{e-}4, 1.00\text{e-}3, 8.03\text{e-}4, 2.79\text{e-}6$.
Sum $= 2.62\text{e-}3$. Divide by $T-1=5$: $\hat{\sigma}_p^2 = 5.23\text{e-}4$, so $\hat{\sigma}_p = 0.02288$.

**Step 3 — Sharpe Ratio:**
$$\text{Sharpe} = \dfrac{0.01167}{0.02288} = 0.510$$

**Step 4 — Downside Deviations (returns below 0):**
$r_t = -0.01, -0.02$. Squared: $1\text{e-}4, 4\text{e-}4$. Sum $= 5\text{e-}4$. Divided by $T=6$:
$$\hat{\sigma}_d = \sqrt{8.33\text{e-}5} = 0.00913$$

**Step 5 — Sortino Ratio:**
$$\text{Sortino} = \dfrac{0.01167}{0.00913} = 1.279$$

**Step 6 — Beta (Covariance / Variance):**
Compute $\text{Cov}(r_p, r_m)$ and $\text{Var}(r_m)$:

| $t$ | $r_{p,t}-\bar{r}_p$ | $r_{m,t}-\bar{r}_m$ | Product | $(r_{m,t}-\bar{r}_m)^2$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $0.00833$ | $0.00583$ | $4.86\text{e-}5$ | $3.40\text{e-}5$ |
| 2 | $-0.02167$ | $-0.01417$ | $3.07\text{e-}4$ | $2.01\text{e-}4$ |
| 3 | $0.01833$ | $0.01583$ | $2.90\text{e-}4$ | $2.51\text{e-}4$ |
| 4 | $-0.03167$ | $-0.02417$ | $7.65\text{e-}4$ | $5.84\text{e-}4$ |
| 5 | $0.02833$ | $0.02083$ | $5.90\text{e-}4$ | $4.34\text{e-}4$ |
| 6 | $-0.00167$ | $-0.00417$ | $6.96\text{e-}6$ | $1.74\text{e-}5$ |
| **$\Sigma$** | | | **2.00e-3** | **1.52e-3** |

$$\hat{\beta}_p = \dfrac{2.00\text{e-}3 / 5}{1.52\text{e-}3 / 5} = \dfrac{2.00\text{e-}3}{1.52\text{e-}3} = 1.315$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A **$\beta = 1.315$** indicates the fund is ~32% more volatile than the market — *aggressive*. **Sortino > Sharpe** confirms that most of the fund's volatility is on the *upside* (desirable); only downside deviations matter.

---

## 3.6 Worked Example — Maximum Drawdown

Suppose a portfolio price path: $\{100, 110, 105, 95, 90, 102, 108\}$.

**Step 1 — Compute Running Maximum $M_t$:**
$M_1 = 100$, $M_2 = 110$, $M_3 = 110$, $M_4 = 110$, $M_5 = 110$, $M_6 = 110$, $M_7 = 110$.

**Step 2 — Compute Drawdowns $DD_t = (P_t - M_t)/M_t$:**
$DD_1 = 0$, $DD_2 = 0$, $DD_3 = -4.55\%$, $DD_4 = -13.64\%$, $DD_5 = -18.18\%$, $DD_6 = -7.27\%$, $DD_7 = -1.82\%$.

**Step 3 — MDD = minimum:**
$$\text{MDD} = -18.18\%$$

This occurred at $t = 5$, when the price had fallen to ₹90 from the peak of ₹110.

---

## 3.7 EWMA Volatility Worked Example

Given $r_{t-1} = -0.02$, $\hat{\sigma}_{t-1}^2 = 0.0004$, $\lambda = 0.94$.

$$\hat{\sigma}_t^2 = 0.94 \times 0.0004 + 0.06 \times (-0.02)^2 = 0.000376 + 0.000024 = 0.000400$$

$$\hat{\sigma}_t = \sqrt{0.000400} = 0.02 \;(2\%)$$

The EWMA places **94% weight on yesterday's variance** and only 6% on yesterday's squared return — a *slow-adapting* but *robust* estimator.

---
