# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A — Short-Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks = 6 Marks)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define Value-at-Risk (VaR). State the three commonly used methods to compute VaR.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> **Definition (2 marks):** VaR at confidence level $\alpha$ and horizon $h$ is the maximum loss such that the probability of a greater loss is at most $1-\alpha$. Formally:
> $$\text{VaR}_{\alpha}(L) \;=\; \inf\{v : \Pr(L \le v) \ge \alpha\} \;=\; -F^{-1}_L(1-\alpha)$$
> **Three methods (1 mark):**
> 1. **Historical Simulation** — empirical quantile of past returns.
> 2. **Variance-Covariance (Parametric)** — assumes Gaussian returns: $\text{VaR} = \mu - z_{1-\alpha}\sigma$.
> 3. **Monte Carlo Simulation** — quantile of simulated P&L paths.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between the Sharpe Ratio and the Sortino Ratio. Why is the Sortino Ratio preferred for evaluating hedge funds?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> **Sharpe (1 mark):** $\dfrac{\bar{R}_p - R_f}{\hat{\sigma}_p}$ — uses *total* volatility in denominator.
> **Sortino (1 mark):** $\dfrac{\bar{R}_p - R_f}{\hat{\sigma}_d}$ — uses only *downside* deviation $\hat{\sigma}_d = \sqrt{\tfrac{1}{T}\sum \min(r_t - R_f, 0)^2}$.
> **Why preferred (1 mark):** Hedge-fund return distributions are *asymmetric* and *skewed*; positive volatility is desirable (upside surprise). Sortino **does not penalise upside variation**, giving a fairer measure of true downside risk-adjusted return.

---

## PART B — Long-Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice: Answer ONE)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Define **Expected Shortfall (ES)** and prove that for a normally distributed loss $L \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$ at confidence $\alpha$, the ES takes the form:
$$\text{ES}_\alpha \;=\; \mu \;+\; \sigma\,\dfrac{\varphi(z_{1-\alpha})}{1-\alpha}$$
where $\varphi(\cdot)$ is the standard normal PDF and $z_{1-\alpha}$ is the corresponding quantile. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A fund's daily returns have mean $\mu = 0.0008$ and standard deviation $\sigma = 0.015$. Compute the **1-day 99% Value-at-Risk** and **Expected Shortfall** for a ₹2,00,00,000 portfolio. Comment on which is the more conservative risk measure and why. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to A(a) — Derivation of ES under Normality (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Definition of ES (2 marks):**
$$\text{ES}_\alpha(L) \;=\; \mathbb{E}[L \,\vert\, L \ge \text{VaR}_\alpha(L)] \;=\; \dfrac{1}{1-\alpha}\int_{\text{VaR}_\alpha}^{\infty} \ell\,f_L(\ell)\,d\ell$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the Normal PDF (1 mark):**
For $L \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$:
$$f_L(\ell) = \dfrac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}}\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{(\ell-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Standardise via $u = (\ell - \mu)/\sigma$ (1 mark):**
With VaR$_\alpha = \mu + z_{1-\alpha}\sigma$, the lower limit $u_0 = z_{1-\alpha}$:
$$\text{ES}_\alpha = \dfrac{1}{1-\alpha}\int_{z_{1-\alpha}}^{\infty}(\mu + u\sigma)\,\varphi(u)\,du$$

**Step 4 — Split the Integral (1 mark):**
$$= \dfrac{\mu}{1-\alpha}\int_{z_{1-\alpha}}^{\infty}\varphi(u)\,du \;+\; \dfrac{\sigma}{1-\alpha}\int_{z_{1-\alpha}}^{\infty}u\,\varphi(u)\,du$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate (2 marks):**
The first integral equals $(1-\alpha)$ (definition of quantile). The second:
$$\int_{z_{1-\alpha}}^{\infty}u\,\varphi(u)\,du = \varphi(z_{1-\alpha})$$
(using $\int_a^\infty u\,\varphi(u)\,du = \varphi(a)$). Therefore:
$$\boxed{\;\text{ES}_\alpha \;=\; \mu \;+\; \sigma\,\dfrac{\varphi(z_{1-\alpha})}{1-\alpha}\;}$$

---

#### Solution to A(b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Lookup critical value (1 mark):**
$z_{0.99} = 2.3263$.

**Step 2 — Compute VaR (2 marks):**
$$\text{VaR}_{0.99} \;=\; \mu - z_{0.99}\sigma \;=\; 0.0008 - 2.3263 \times 0.015$$
$$= 0.0008 - 0.034895 = -0.034095 \;\;\text{(as return)}$$
$$\text{VaR}_{0.99}^{₹} = 0.034095 \times 2\text{Cr} = \text{₹}\,6{,}81{,}900$$

**Step 3 — Compute $\varphi(z_{0.99})$ (1 mark):**
$$\varphi(2.3263) = 0.02665$$

**Step 4 — Compute ES (2 marks):**
$$\text{ES}_{0.99} = 0.0008 + 0.015 \times \dfrac{0.02665}{0.01} = 0.0008 + 0.03998 = 0.04078$$
$$\text{ES}_{0.99}^{₹} = 0.04078 \times 2\text{Cr} = \text{₹}\,8{,}15{,}560$$

**Step 5 — Comparison (1 mark):**
> ES (₹8.16 L) > VaR (₹6.82 L). ES is more conservative because it averages losses *in the worst $(1-\alpha) = 1\%$ tail*, while VaR only marks the *threshold*. ES is therefore preferred for capital adequacy under Basel 3.5 FRTB.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Define **Maximum Drawdown (MDD)** and the **Calmar Ratio**. For the price series $\{P_t\} = \{100, 120, 110, 95, 85, 105, 115\}$, compute (i) the drawdown series $\{DD_t\}$, (ii) the MDD, and (iii) the Calmar Ratio given mean excess return $\bar{R} = 0.012$ per month. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define **Beta** in the CAPM framework. From the following 6-month return data, compute the fund's $\beta$, **Sharpe Ratio**, and **Sortino Ratio** with $R_f = 0.005$. Comment on whether the fund outperforms the market on a risk-adjusted basis. **(7 marks)**

| Month | $r_p$ | $r_m$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0.025 | 0.018 |
| 2 | -0.015 | -0.010 |
| 3 | 0.030 | 0.022 |
| 4 | -0.025 | -0.015 |
| 5 | 0.040 | 0.028 |
| 6 | 0.010 | 0.005 |

---

#### Solution to B(a) — Maximum Drawdown (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Definitions (1 mark):**
$$DD_t = \dfrac{P_t - M_t}{M_t}, \quad M_t = \max_{0 \le s \le t} P_s$$
$$\text{MDD}_T = \min_{0 \le t \le T} DD_t, \quad \text{Calmar} = \bar{R}/|\text{MDD}|$$

**Step 2 — Running Maximum (1 mark):**
$M = \{100, 120, 120, 120, 120, 120, 120\}$.

**Step 3 — Drawdown Series (2 marks):**

| $t$ | $P_t$ | $M_t$ | $DD_t$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 100 | 100 | 0.00 |
| 2 | 120 | 120 | 0.00 |
| 3 | 110 | 120 | -8.33% |
| 4 | 95 | 120 | -20.83% |
| 5 | 85 | 120 | -29.17% |
| 6 | 105 | 120 | -12.50% |
| 7 | 115 | 120 | -4.17% |

**Step 4 — MDD (1 mark):**
$$\text{MDD} = -29.17\% \;\;\text{(at }t = 5\text{)}$$

**Step 5 — Calmar Ratio (2 marks):**
$$\text{Calmar} = \dfrac{0.012}{0.2917} = 0.0411$$

> A Calmar ratio of ~0.04 indicates **modest reward per unit of peak-to-trough pain**.

---

#### Solution to B(b) — Beta, Sharpe, Sortino (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Compute Means (1 mark):**
$\bar{r}_p = 0.065/6 = 0.01083$, $\bar{r}_m = 0.048/6 = 0.008$.

**Step 2 — Compute Covariance and Variance of $r_m$ (2 marks):**

Deviations $r_p - \bar{r}_p = \{0.0142, -0.0258, 0.0192, -0.0358, 0.0292, -0.0008\}$.
Deviations $r_m - \bar{r}_m = \{0.010, -0.018, 0.014, -0.023, 0.020, -0.003\}$.

Products: $\{1.42\text{e-}4, 4.65\text{e-}4, 2.69\text{e-}4, 8.24\text{e-}4, 5.83\text{e-}4, 2.5\text{e-}6\}$. Sum $= 2.37\text{e-}3$.
Squared market deviations: $\{1\text{e-}4, 3.24\text{e-}4, 1.96\text{e-}4, 5.29\text{e-}4, 4\text{e-}4, 9\text{e-}6\}$. Sum $= 1.36\text{e-}3$.

$$\hat{\beta}_p = \dfrac{2.37\text{e-}3 / 5}{1.36\text{e-}3 / 5} = \dfrac{2.37}{1.36} = 1.743$$

**Step 3 — Standard Deviation of $r_p$ (1 mark):**
Squared deviations of $r_p$: $\{2.01\text{e-}4, 6.66\text{e-}4, 3.68\text{e-}4, 1.28\text{e-}3, 8.51\text{e-}4, 7\text{e-}7\}$. Sum $= 3.39\text{e-}3$. Divide by 5: $\hat{\sigma}_p^2 = 6.78\text{e-}4$. $\hat{\sigma}_p = 0.02604$.

**Step 4 — Sharpe Ratio (1 mark):**
$$\text{Sharpe} = \dfrac{0.01083 - 0.005}{0.02604} = \dfrac{0.00583}{0.02604} = 0.224$$

**Step 5 — Sortino Ratio (1 mark):**
Downside returns: $r_t - R_f = -0.020, -0.030$ at $t=2,4$. Squared: $4\text{e-}4, 9\text{e-}4$. Sum $= 1.3\text{e-}3$. Divide by $T=6$: $\hat{\sigma}_d^2 = 2.17\text{e-}4$. $\hat{\sigma}_d = 0.01473$.
$$\text{Sortino} = \dfrac{0.00583}{0.01473} = 0.396$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation (1 mark):**
> $\beta = 1.743$ means the fund is **74% more volatile than the market** — *high systematic risk*. Sharpe (0.224) is modest; however, **Sortino (0.396) is nearly double the Sharpe**, signalling that most volatility is *upside* (positive skew). The fund does **not outperform on Sharpe** but shows attractive **downside-risk-adjusted** return.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Zones (KTU Board Pattern):**
> 1. **Sign convention confusion (–1 to –2 marks):** Writing $\text{VaR} = \mu + z\sigma$ when returns are framed as P&L. Always *define* which variable is a *gain* and which is a *loss* before writing the formula.
> 2. **Missing horizon and confidence:** "VaR = ₹2L" gets *partial* credit. "1-day, 99% VaR = ₹2L" gets full credit. Examiners deduct 1 mark for omission.
> 3. **Confusing VaR and ES direction:** ES is *always* ≥ VaR in magnitude. If a student writes ES < VaR, immediate 2-mark penalty.
> 4. **Sharpe vs Sortino denominator:** Many students use $\hat{\sigma}_p$ in the Sortino formula. This is a guaranteed **1-mark loss**.
> 5. **GARCH persistence:** Stating $\alpha + \beta = 1$ as "stationary" — actually the *unit-root* boundary. Need $\alpha + \beta < 1$ for covariance-stationarity.
> 6. **Beta from regression vs covariance:** Examiners award full credit only for the OLS slope form $\hat{\beta} = \text{Cov}(r_p, r_m)/\text{Var}(r_m)$.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Risk Metrics (Module 4)**

- ✅ **VaR Definition:** Smallest $v$ such that $\Pr(L \le v) \ge \alpha$. Equivalently $-q_{1-\alpha}$ of P&L.
- ✅ **Three Engines:** Historical Simulation (non-parametric), Variance-Covariance (Gaussian, $\mu - z\sigma$), Monte Carlo (model-based simulation).
- ✅ **Gaussian VaR Formula:** $\text{VaR}_\alpha = \mu - z_{1-\alpha}\sigma$. For 99% → $z = 2.326$; for 95% → $z = 1.645$; for 97.5% → $z = 1.96$.
- ✅ **Expected Shortfall (Normal):** $\text{ES}_\alpha = \mu + \sigma\,\varphi(z_{1-\alpha})/(1-\alpha)$. Always ≥ VaR magnitude.
- ✅ **Coherence:** ES is coherent (Basel 3.5 FRTB); VaR is *not* (fails sub-additivity).
- ✅ **Backtesting:** Kupiec LR $\sim \chi^2_1$; null is "model correctly specifies breach probability $p = 1-\alpha$."
- ✅ **EWMA:** $\sigma_t^2 = \lambda\sigma_{t-1}^2 + (1-\lambda)r_{t-1}^2$. RiskMetrics default $\lambda = 0.94$ (daily), $0.97$ (monthly).
- ✅ **GARCH(1,1):** $\sigma_t^2 = \omega + \alpha r_{t-1}^2 + \beta\sigma_{t-1}^2$. Persistence $\alpha + \beta < 1$.
- ✅ **Maximum Drawdown:** Peak-to-trough worst decline. MDD = $\min_t(P_t - M_t)/M_t$.
- ✅ **Calmar Ratio:** $\bar{R}/|\text{MDD}|$ — return per unit of peak-to-trough pain.
- ✅ **Sharpe:** $(\bar{R}_p - R_f)/\hat{\sigma}_p$ — excess return per unit *total* volatility.
- ✅ **Sortino:** $(\bar{R}_p - R_f)/\hat{\sigma}_d$ — only downside deviations in denominator.
- ✅ **Treynor:** $(\bar{R}_p - R_f)/\hat{\beta}$ — uses systematic (beta) risk only.
- ✅ **Beta:** $\hat{\beta}_p = \text{Cov}(r_p, r_m)/\text{Var}(r_m) \approx$ slope of OLS regression of $r_p$ on $r_m$.
- ✅ **Tracking Error:** $\text{std}(r_p - r_b)$ — volatility of active return relative to benchmark.
- ✅ **Information Ratio:** $\bar{d}/\text{TE}$ — manager's skill per unit of active-risk taken.
- ✅ **Drawdown Sequence Formula:** $M_t = \max_{s \le t} P_s$ → running peak; $DD_t$ is always ≤ 0.
- ✅ **Interpretation of $\beta$:** $\beta = 1$ (market-like), $\beta > 1$ (aggressive), $\beta < 1$ (defensive), $\beta < 0$ (hedge/contrarian).
- ✅ **Always** state confidence $\alpha$ and horizon $h$ for VaR and ES — bare numbers are penalised.

> **End of Module 4 — Risk Metrics Notes**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Extreme Value Theory
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET742 - FINANCIAL TIME SERIES ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Extreme Value Theory

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Extreme Value Theory (EVT)** is a rigorous branch of probability and statistics that provides the theoretical foundation for modeling, analyzing, and quantifying the statistical behavior of **extreme deviations** from the median of probability distributions. In the context of **Financial Time Series Analysis**, EVT is employed to estimate the probability of rare, catastrophic financial events occurring in the tails (far from the mean) of the return distribution, which cannot be reliably captured by Gaussian (Normal) assumptions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *Extreme Value Theory is the statistical framework that focuses on the asymptotic behavior of the maximum (or minimum) of a sequence of random variables, providing asymptotic models for the distribution of extreme outcomes — the Generalized Extreme Value (GEV) and Generalized Pareto (GP) families.*

EVT is grounded in two foundational limit theorems:
1. The **Fisher–Tippett–Gnedenko Theorem (1928)** — foundation of the **Block Maxima Method (BMM)**, leading to the **GEV distribution**.
2. The **Pickands–Balkema–De Haan Theorem (1975)** — foundation of the **Peaks-Over-Threshold (POT)** method, leading to the **GPD distribution**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Dam Height Problem**
> Imagine a civil engineer designing a dam on a river. The engineer does not need to know the **average** river level (which would be the domain of classical statistics); she needs to know the **highest possible flood level in 100 years** to set the dam crest safely. EVT is the statistical tool that takes decades of river-level data, isolates only the annual flood peaks, fits a special distribution to those peaks, and answers: *"What is the water level with a 1% chance of being exceeded in any given year?"*

**Finance Analogy:** In stock markets, classical models assume returns follow a thin-tailed Normal distribution, suggesting that a 25-σ crash is essentially impossible. However, real market crashes (1987 Black Monday, 2008 Financial Crisis, 2020 COVID crash) occur far more frequently than Normal theory predicts. **EVT specifically captures these "tail events"** by modeling the extreme losses directly rather than pretending the tails are negligible.

### 1.3 Why Classical Statistics Fails in Financial Tails

| Phenomenon | Normal Model Prediction | Empirical Reality | Why EVT is Needed |
|------------|------------------------|-------------------|---------------------|
| Daily Crash Frequency | Almost zero (6σ event) | Happens every few years | Normal tail is **too thin** |
| Insurance Losses (e.g., 2008) | Negligible probability | Massive realized losses | Financial returns are **leptokurtic** |
| Option Pricing (OTM) | Underprices OTM options | Volatility smile observed | Tail risk is **systematically underpriced** |

### 1.4 Key Terminology Map

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Critical EVT Vocabulary Terms (High-Yield for KTU Exams):**
> - **Shape Parameter (ξ or γ):** Controls tail heaviness — the single most important parameter in EVT.
> - **Return Level / Quantile of Exceedance:** The extreme value that is expected to be exceeded once every *T* periods.
> - **Threshold (u):** The high cutoff above which "extreme" observations are selected (POT framework).

### 1.5 Standard Metrics Used in EVT for Finance

- **Value-at-Risk (VaR):** Maximum loss at confidence level α over horizon h.
- **Expected Shortfall (ES) / Conditional VaR (CVaR):** Expected loss *given* that the loss exceeds VaR.
- **Return Period (T):** Average number of periods between two exceedances of a threshold.
- **Tail Index (α = 1/ξ):** Governs power-law decay in the upper tail.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two Pillars of EVT — Block Maxima vs. Peaks-Over-Threshold

EVT splits into two mathematically equivalent but operationally distinct frameworks:

#### **Pillar 1: Block Maxima Method (BMM)**

Given a time series $X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n$, divide it into $m$ non-overlapping blocks of size $k$ and extract the maximum of each block:

$$M_n = \max\{X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n\}$$

The **Fisher–Tippett–Gnedenko Theorem** states that if $M_n$ (after suitable linear normalization $a_n, b_n$) converges to a non-degenerate distribution, it must belong to the **Generalized Extreme Value (GEV) family**:

$$H(x; \mu, \sigma, \xi) = \exp\left\{-\left[1 + \xi \left(\frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}\right)\right]^{-1/\xi}\right\}$$

defined for $1 + \xi(x - \mu)/\sigma > 0$, where:
- $\mu \in \mathbb{R}$ → **location parameter**
- $\sigma > 0$ → **scale parameter**
- $\xi \in \mathbb{R}$ → **shape parameter** (tail index)

#### **Pillar 2: Peaks-Over-Threshold (POT) Method**

Choose a high threshold $u$ and analyze the **exceedances** $Y_i = X_i - u \quad \text{whenever} \quad X_i > u$. The **Pickands–Balkema–De Haan Theorem** shows that for a sufficiently high $u$, the conditional excess distribution converges to the **Generalized Pareto Distribution (GPD)**:

$$G(y; \sigma_u, \xi) = 1 - \left(1 + \xi \frac{y}{\sigma_u}\right)^{-1/\xi}, \quad y > 0$$

where $\sigma_u = \sigma + \xi(u - \mu)$ is the **scale parameter of exceedances**, and $\xi$ is the **same shape parameter** as in the GEV.

### 2.2 Interpretation of the Shape Parameter ξ

| Value of ξ | Distribution Type | Tail Behavior | Financial Implication |
|-----------|-------------------|---------------|------------------------|
| $\xi > 0$ | **Fréchet** (heavy-tailed) | Power-law decay, infinite variance possible | **Stock returns, FX returns** — high crash probability |
| $\xi = 0$ | **Gumbel** (light-tailed, exponential-type) | Exponential decay | Short-rate, interest-rate spreads |
| $\xi < 0$ | **Weibull / Reverse Weibull** (bounded) | Finite upper endpoint | Bounded credit losses, capped indices |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU 2024:** Empirically, daily equity log-returns almost always yield $\xi \in (0.2, 0.6)$ — confirming **Fréchet-type fat tails**. This is the cornerstone of every risk-management question you will face.

### 2.3 Return Level (T-Year Return Level)

The return level $x_T$ is the value that is exceeded on average once every $T$ observations. Inverting the GEV CDF:

$$x_T = \mu - \frac{\sigma}{\xi}\left[1 - \{-\log(1 - 1/T)\}^{-\xi}\right], \quad \xi \neq 0$$

For $\xi = 0$ (Gumbel case):

$$x_T = \mu - \sigma \log[-\log(1 - 1/T)]$$

In the POT/GP framework with $N_u$ mean exceedances per year:

$$x_T = u + \frac{\sigma_u}{\xi}\left[(T N_u)^{\xi} - 1\right], \quad \xi \neq 0$$

### 2.4 Value-at-Risk via EVT (GPD Approach)

For a loss distribution $L$ and confidence level $\alpha$:

$$\text{VaR}_\alpha = u + \frac{\sigma_u}{\xi}\left[\left(\frac{n}{N_u} \cdot \alpha\right)^{\xi} - 1\right]$$

where $n$ is total sample size and $N_u$ is the number of exceedances above threshold $u$.

The corresponding **Expected Shortfall** is:

$$\text{ES}_\alpha = \frac{\text{VaR}_\alpha}{1 - \xi} + \frac{\sigma_u - \xi u}{1 - \xi}$$

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Domain / Units |
|---|---------|---------|-----------------|
| 1 | GEV CDF | $H(x) = \exp\{-[1+\xi(x-\mu)/\sigma]^{-1/\xi}\}$ | $1+\xi(x-\mu)/\sigma > 0$ |
| 2 | GPD CDF | $G(y) = 1 - (1+\xi y/\sigma_u)^{-1/\xi}$ | $y > 0$, $\sigma_u > 0$ |
| 3 | Fréchet density (ξ > 0) | $f(x) = (1/\sigma)(1+\xi z)^{-(1+1/\xi)} \cdot (1+\xi z)^{-(1/\xi+1)} e^{-(1+\xi z)^{-1/\xi}}$ | $z = (x-\mu)/\sigma$ |
| 4 | Return Level (GEV) | $x_T = \mu - (\sigma/\xi)[1 - \{-\log(1-1/T)\}^{-\xi}]$ | $\xi \neq 0$ |
| 5 | Return Level (GPD) | $x_T = u + (\sigma_u/\xi)[(T N_u)^{\xi} - 1]$ | $\xi \neq 0$ |
| 6 | VaR (POT) | $\text{VaR}_\alpha = u + (\sigma_u/\xi)[(\alpha n / N_u)^{\xi} - 1]$ | $\alpha \in (0,1)$ |
| 7 | Expected Shortfall | $\text{ES}_\alpha = \text{VaR}_\alpha/(1-\xi) + (\sigma_u - \xi u)/(1-\xi)$ | $\xi < 1$ |
| 8 | Tail Index relation | $\alpha = 1/\xi$ | Pareto-tail equivalence |
| 9 | Hill Estimator | $\hat{\xi}_{H,k} = \frac{1}{k} \sum_{i=1}^{k} \log(X_{(n-i+1)}/X_{(n-k)})$ | $k$ = # order statistics in tail |
| 10 | Mean Excess Function | $e(u) = \mathbb{E}[X - u \mid X > u] = (\sigma_u + \xi u)/(1 - \xi)$ | Used to choose $u$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** Note the special handling when $\xi = 0$ — the GEV reduces to the Gumbel distribution $H(x) = \exp\{-e^{-(x-\mu)/\sigma}\}$ and the GPD reduces to the **Exponential** distribution $G(y) = 1 - e^{-y/\sigma_u}$. KTU questions often test this limiting case.

### 2.6 Practical Selection Between BMM and POT

| Feature | Block Maxima (GEV) | Peaks-Over-Threshold (GPD) |
|---------|--------------------|-----------------------------|
| Data Usage | Only block maxima (wasteful) | All exceedances (efficient) |
| Asymptotic Theory | Older, more established | More modern, more flexible |
| Threshold Selection | Not required (but block size matters) | Critical (mean excess plot) |
| Financial Standard | Reinsurance, hydrology | **Banking risk (Basel Accords)** |
| Sample Efficiency | Low (one obs per block) | High (all large events used) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why EVT matters in real-world engineering/finance:** Basel III/IV capital requirements explicitly permit financial institutions to use **POT-based GPD models** for computing market-risk capital, because they accurately capture the 99.9% VaR — events the Normal model under-estimates by an order of magnitude.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Block Maxima to the GEV Distribution

**Step 1 — Setup the Block Maximum.**
Let $X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n$ be i.i.d. with CDF $F(x)$. Define:

$$M_n = \max(X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n)$$

**Step 2 — CDF of the Maximum.**
The probability that $M_n$ is at most $x$ equals the probability that *all* $n$ samples are $\leq x$:

$$\Pr(M_n \leq x) = \Pr(X_1 \leq x, \ldots, X_n \leq x) = [F(x)]^n$$

**Step 3 — The Degeneracy Problem.**
For a fixed $F$, as $n \to \infty$, $F(x)^n \to 0$ for $x$ below the right endpoint, and $\to 1$ for $x$ above it. The limit is **degenerate** (point mass), so the raw maximum is uninformative.

**Step 4 — Linear Normalization.**
To obtain a non-degenerate limit, we standardize $M_n$ using sequences $a_n > 0$ and $b_n$:

$$Z_n = \frac{M_n - b_n}{a_n}$$

**Step 5 — The Fisher–Tippett–Gnedenko Theorem.**
Under mild regularity on $F$, the limit of $Z_n$ exists and has a CDF belonging to one of three families (Gumbel, Fréchet, Weibull). Jenkinson (1955) unified these into a single family — the **GEV distribution**:

$$H(x; \mu, \sigma, \xi) = \exp\left\{-\left[1 + \xi \left(\frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}\right)\right]^{-1/\xi}\right\}$$

with $1 + \xi(x - \mu)/\sigma > 0$.

**Step 6 — Recovery of the Three Sub-Families.**
- If $\xi > 0$, then the support is $\mu - \sigma/\xi < x < \infty$ → **Fréchet** family (heavy tail).
- If $\xi = 0$, take the limit $\xi \to 0$ and apply L'Hôpital's rule: $H(x) = \exp\{-e^{-(x-\mu)/\sigma}\}$ → **Gumbel** family.
- If $\xi < 0$, the support is $\mu - \sigma/\xi > x > -\infty$ with finite upper endpoint $\mu - \sigma/\xi$ → **Weibull** (reversed/upper-bounded).

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic for ξ → 0 Limit:**
> Start with $H(x) = \exp\{-[1 + \xi z]^{-1/\xi}\}$ where $z = (x-\mu)/\sigma$.
> Set $g(\xi) = [1 + \xi z]^{-1/\xi}$. Take $\log g = -\frac{1}{\xi}\log(1 + \xi z)$.
> Use expansion $\log(1 + \xi z) \approx \xi z - \xi^2 z^2/2 + \ldots$
> So $\log g \approx -\frac{1}{\xi}(\xi z - \xi^2 z^2/2) = -z + \xi z^2/2 \to -z$ as $\xi \to 0$.
> Hence $H \to e^{-e^{-z}}$, recovering the Gumbel. ✓

### 3.2 Derivation: Return Level Formula from GEV

**Goal:** Find $x_T$ such that $\Pr(X > x_T) = 1/T$, i.e., $\Pr(X \leq x_T) = 1 - 1/T$.

**Step 1 — Set the GEV CDF equal to the cumulative probability.**

$$H(x_T) = 1 - \frac{1}{T}$$

**Step 2 — Invert the CDF using the structure of H.**

$$\exp\left\{-\left[1 + \xi \left(\frac{x_T - \mu}{\sigma}\right)\right]^{-1/\xi}\right\} = 1 - \frac{1}{T}$$

**Step 3 — Take natural log of both sides.**

$$-\left[1 + \xi \left(\frac{x_T - \mu}{\sigma}\right)\right]^{-1/\xi} = \log\left(1 - \frac{1}{T}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Multiply both sides by $-1$ and raise to the power $-\xi$.**

$$1 + \xi \left(\frac{x_T - \mu}{\sigma}\right) = \left[-\log\left(1 - \frac{1}{T}\right)\right]^{-\xi}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $x_T$.**

$$\frac{x_T - \mu}{\sigma} = \frac{1}{\xi}\left\{\left[-\log\left(1 - \frac{1}{T}\right)\right]^{-\xi} - 1\right\}$$

**Step 6 — Final closed-form result.**

$$x_T = \mu - \frac{\sigma}{\xi}\left[1 - \left\{-\log\left(1 - \frac{1}{T}\right)\right\}^{-\xi}\right] \quad (\xi \neq 0)$$

**Step 7 — Limiting case $\xi \to 0$ (Gumbel).**
Apply L'Hôpital or directly use the Gumbel inverse:

$$x_T = \mu - \sigma \log\left[-\log\left(1 - \frac{1}{T}\right)\right]$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This derivation is a high-yield KTU question (Part B, 7 marks).** Practice writing the inversion step-by-step — the most common mistake is forgetting the negative sign on the logarithm term.

### 3.3 Derivation: Value-at-Risk from the GPD (POT Approach)

**Step 1 — Define the Exceedance Probability.**
For a high threshold $u$ with exceedance rate $\zeta_u = \Pr(X > u) = N_u/n$:

$$\Pr(X > x) = \Pr(X > u) \cdot \Pr(X - u > x - u \mid X > u) = \zeta_u \cdot [1 - G(x - u; \sigma_u, \xi)]$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the GPD survival function.**

$$\Pr(X > x) = \zeta_u \left(1 + \xi \frac{x - u}{\sigma_u}\right)^{-1/\xi}, \quad x > u$$

**Step 3 — Set this equal to $1 - \alpha$ (the tail probability for VaR).**

$$\zeta_u \left(1 + \xi \frac{\text{VaR}_\alpha - u}{\sigma_u}\right)^{-1/\xi} = 1 - \alpha$$

**Step 4 — Solve algebraically for VaR.**

$$1 + \xi \frac{\text{VaR}_\alpha - u}{\sigma_u} = \left(\frac{\zeta_u}{1 - \alpha}\right)^{-\xi}$$

**Step 5 — Isolate VaR.**

$$\text{VaR}_\alpha = u + \frac{\sigma_u}{\xi}\left[\left(\frac{1 - \alpha}{\zeta_u}\right)^{\xi} - 1\right]$$

**Step 6 — Substitute $\zeta_u = N_u / n$ (empirical exceedance ratio).**

$$\boxed{\text{VaR}_\alpha = u + \frac{\sigma_u}{\xi}\left[\left(\frac{\alpha \cdot n}{N_u}\right)^{\xi} - 1\right]}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic Step 3→4:** Divide both sides by $\zeta_u$, giving $(1-\alpha)/\zeta_u$. Invert: $((1-\alpha)/\zeta_u)^{-\xi} = (\zeta_u/(1-\alpha))^{\xi}$. Raise both sides to the $-\xi$ power to isolate the bracket term.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (For KTU Practice)

**Problem:** A portfolio's daily losses (in ₹ lakhs) have a GPD tail with $\xi = 0.35$, $\sigma_u = 12$, and threshold $u = 30$. Over 1000 trading days, $N_u = 50$ exceedances occurred. Compute the 99% VaR.

**Solution:**

$$\text{VaR}_{0.99} = 30 + \frac{12}{0.35}\left[\left(\frac{0.99 \times 1000}{50}\right)^{0.35} - 1\right]$$

Compute the bracket:

$$\left(\frac{990}{50}\right)^{0.35} = (19.8)^{0.35} = e^{0.35 \cdot \ln(19.8)} = e^{0.35 \cdot 2.985} = e^{1.0448} \approx 2.842$$

Then:

$$\text{VaR}_{0.99} = 30 + \frac{12}{0.35}(2.842 - 1) = 30 + 34.29 \times 1.842 \approx 30 + 63.16 = 93.16$$

**Answer:** The 99% VaR is approximately **₹93.16 lakhs**.

### 3.5 The Hill Estimator — Tail Index from Order Statistics

For the top $k$ order statistics $X_{(n)} \geq X_{(n-1)} \geq \ldots \geq X_{(n-k+1)}$:

$$\hat{\xi}_{H,k} = \frac{1}{k}\sum_{i=1}^{k} \log\left(\frac{X_{(n-i+1)}}{X_{(n-k)}}\right)$$

with variance asymptotically:

$$\text{Var}(\hat{\xi}_{H,k}) \approx \frac{\xi^2}{k}$$

The optimal $k^*$ is found via a **Hill plot** (plot $\hat{\xi}_{H,k}$ vs $k$; choose the $k$ where the plot stabilizes).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Flow: How EVT Fits into Financial Risk Modeling

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0["Raw Returns Data"] --> A1["Stationarity Check ADF / KPSS"]
    A1 --> A2["Compute Daily Losses L_t"]
    A2 --> B1{"Choose Approach"}
    B1 -- Block Maxima --> C1["Divide into m blocks"]
    C1 --> C2["Extract Maxima M_1 to M_m"]
    C2 --> C3["Fit GEV Distribution"]
    C3 --> C4["Estimate mu sigma xi via MLE"]
    B1 -- Peaks Over Threshold --> D1["Choose Threshold u via Mean Excess Plot"]
    D1 --> D2["Extract Exceedances X_i minus u"]
    D2 --> D3["Fit GPD Distribution"]
    D3 --> D4["Estimate sigma_u xi via MLE"]
    C4 --> E1["Diagnostic Plots Q-Q and P-P"]
    D4 --> E1
    E1 --> F1["Compute Return Levels x_T"]
    E1 --> F2["Compute VaR at level alpha"]
    E1 --> F3["Compute Expected Shortfall"]
    F1 --> G1["Risk Report to Management"]
    F2 --> G1
    F3 --> G1
```

### 4.2 The Two Asymptotic Regimes of EVT

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R["X_1 X_2 to X_n iid Samples"] --> M["M_n equals max X_1 to X_n"]
    R --> E["Exceedances X_i minus u where X_i greater than u"]
    M --> FTG["Fisher Tippett Gnedenko 1928"]
    E --> PBD["Pickands Balkema De Haan 1975"]
    FTG --> GEV["GEV Distribution"]
    PBD --> GPD["GPD Distribution"]
    GEV --> R1["Return Level x_T"]
    GPD --> R2["VaR and Expected Shortfall"]
    GEV -.-> T1["Shape xi greater than 0 Frechet Fat Tails"]
    GEV -.-> T2["Shape xi equals 0 Gumbel Exponential Tails"]
    GEV -.-> T3["Shape xi less than 0 Weibull Bounded Tails"]
```

### 4.3 Tail-Type Decision Tree for KTU Problems

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0["Given xi from EVT fit"] --> S1{"Sign of xi?"}
    S1 -- xi greater than 0 --> S2["Fréchet Type"]
    S2 --> S2A["Tail decays as power law x to the minus 1 over xi"]
    S2 --> S2B["Infinite moments beyond order 1 over xi"]
    S2 --> S2C["Implication Risk is unbounded crashes probable"]
    S1 -- xi equals 0 --> S3["Gumbel Type"]
    S3 --> S3A["Tail decays exponentially"]
    S3 --> S3B["All moments finite"]
    S3 --> S3C["Implication Crashes rare but not catastrophic"]
    S1 -- xi less than 0 --> S4["Weibull Type"]
    S4 --> S4A["Finite upper endpoint mu minus sigma over xi"]
    S4 --> S4B["All moments finite"]
    S4 --> S4C["Implication Bounded risk e.g. insured losses"]
```

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture: EVT Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1["INPUT MODULE Daily Return Series r_t"] --> P2["PREPROCESSING Remove mean standardize winsorize"]
    P2 --> P3["TAIL DIAGNOSTIC Hill plot QQ plot mean excess function"]
    P3 --> P4{"Decision Choose Method"}
    P4 -- GEV --> P5["Block Maxima Pipeline m blocks MLE for GEV"]
    P4 -- GPD --> P6["Peaks Over Threshold Pipeline threshold selection MLE for GPD"]
    P5 --> P7["VALIDATION Likelihood ratio Anderson Darling K-S test"]
    P6 --> P7
    P7 --> P8["RISK METRIC ENGINE Return Level x_T VaR ES"]
    P8 --> P9["OUTPUT DASHBOARD Regulatory Capital Stress Tests"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A: Short-Answer Questions (2 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Set B]**
> Define the **Generalized Extreme Value (GEV) distribution** and state the role of the shape parameter $\xi$ in determining the tail behavior of the distribution.

**Model Answer:**
The GEV distribution is the asymptotic limit distribution of the maximum (or minimum) of a sequence of i.i.d. random variables, as established by the Fisher–Tippett–Gnedenko theorem. Its CDF is:

$$H(x; \mu, \sigma, \xi) = \exp\left\{-\left[1 + \xi \left(\frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}\right)\right]^{-1/\xi}\right\}$$

The **shape parameter $\xi$** governs the tail behavior of $H$:
- $\xi > 0$ → **Fréchet-type** (heavy, power-law tails) — typical of financial returns.
- $\xi = 0$ → **Gumbel-type** (exponential tails) — boundary case.
- $\xi < 0$ → **Weibull-type** (bounded upper tail) — typical of insured losses.

**[2 Marks]**

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> Distinguish between the **Block Maxima Method (BMM)** and the **Peaks-Over-Threshold (POT)** method in Extreme Value Theory.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | BMM (GEV) | POT (GPD) |
|--------|-----------|-----------|
| Data Used | Only the maximum per block | All observations above a threshold |
| Limiting Distribution | GEV | Generalized Pareto |
| Statistical Efficiency | Lower (one obs per block) | Higher (all extremes used) |
| Threshold Required | No (block size matters instead) | Yes — critical choice via mean-excess plot |
| Regulatory Use | Hydrology, structural engineering | Basel III/IV market-risk capital |

**[2 Marks]**

---

### Part B: Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **Question A [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**
>
> **(a)** Derive the **T-year return level** $x_T$ from the Generalized Extreme Value distribution. Clearly state the assumption on $\xi$ and explain what happens in the limiting case $\xi \to 0$. **[7 Marks]**
>
> **(b)** The daily log-returns of a stock index follow a GEV distribution with $\mu = 0.0002$, $\sigma = 0.012$, $\xi = 0.28$. Compute the **50-day return level** and interpret its meaning for a portfolio holding ₹10 crore of the index. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Derivation of $x_T$:**

**Step 1:** The T-year return level $x_T$ satisfies $\Pr(X \leq x_T) = 1 - 1/T$ by definition.

**Step 2:** Setting $H(x_T) = 1 - 1/T$ and using the GEV CDF:

$$\exp\left\{-\left[1 + \xi \left(\frac{x_T - \mu}{\sigma}\right)\right]^{-1/\xi}\right\} = 1 - \frac{1}{T}$$

**Step 3:** Take the natural logarithm of both sides:

$$-\left[1 + \xi \left(\frac{x_T - \mu}{\sigma}\right)\right]^{-1/\xi} = \log\left(1 - \frac{1}{T}\right)$$

**Step 4:** Multiply by $-1$ and raise to the power $-\xi$:

$$1 + \xi \left(\frac{x_T - \mu}{\sigma}\right) = \left[-\log\left(1 - \frac{1}{T}\right)\right]^{-\xi}$$

**Step 5:** Isolate the term containing $x_T$:

$$\frac{x_T - \mu}{\sigma} = \frac{1}{\xi}\left\{\left[-\log\left(1 - \frac{1}{T}\right)\right]^{-\xi} - 1\right\}$$

**Step 6:** Final closed-form expression:

$$x_T = \mu - \frac{\sigma}{\xi}\left[1 - \left\{-\log\left(1 - \frac{1}{T}\right)\right\}^{-\xi}\right] \quad \text{for } \xi \neq 0$$

**Step 7 (Limiting Case $\xi \to 0$):** Apply L'Hôpital's rule on $0/0$ form. The GEV CDF becomes the Gumbel:

$$H_G(x) = \exp\left\{-e^{-(x-\mu)/\sigma}\right\}$$

Inverting gives the Gumbel return level:

$$x_T = \mu - \sigma \log\left[-\log\left(1 - \frac{1}{T}\right)\right]$$

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation:**

Given: $\mu = 0.0002$, $\sigma = 0.012$, $\xi = 0.28$, $T = 50$.

**Step 1:** Compute the inner logarithmic term:

$$-\log\left(1 - \frac{1}{T}\right) = -\log(1 - 0.02) = -\log(0.98) = 0.02020$$

**Step 2:** Raise to the power $-\xi = -0.28$:

$$(0.02020)^{-0.28} = e^{-0.28 \times \ln(0.02020)} = e^{-0.28 \times (-3.902)} = e^{1.0926} \approx 2.982$$

**Step 3:** Substitute into the return level formula:

$$x_{50} = 0.0002 - \frac{0.012}{0.28}\left[1 - 2.982\right]$$

**Step 4:** Compute the bracket and the fraction:

$$x_{50} = 0.0002 - 0.04286 \times (-1.982) = 0.0002 + 0.0849 = 0.0851$$

**Step 5:** Interpretation — For a ₹10 crore portfolio, a 50-day return level of 0.0851 (8.51%) implies that on average, **once every 50 trading days** (≈ 10 weeks), the index can be expected to lose approximately **8.51%** or **₹85.1 lakhs** of its value. Risk managers must hold capital reserves to absorb such a loss.

**[Total: 14 Marks]**

---

> **Question B [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Set A]**
>
> **(a)** State and explain the **Pickands–Balkema–De Haan theorem**. Define the Generalized Pareto Distribution and derive the **VaR formula** under the POT framework. **[7 Marks]**
>
> **(b)** A bank's daily trading losses (in ₹ lakhs) are modeled as GPD excesses over a threshold $u = 25$ lakhs. From 1500 historical observations, 60 exceedances are recorded, with estimated parameters $\hat{\sigma}_u = 18$ and $\hat{\xi} = 0.42$. Compute the **99.5% one-day VaR** and the corresponding **Expected Shortfall**. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Theorem Statement and VaR Derivation:**

**Step 1 (Theorem):** The Pickands–Balkema–De Haan theorem (1975) states that for a sufficiently high threshold $u$, the conditional distribution of exceedances $Y = X - u \mid X > u$ converges, as $u \to x_F$ (the right endpoint of the underlying distribution $F$), to the **Generalized Pareto Distribution (GPD)**:

$$G(y; \sigma_u, \xi) = 1 - \left(1 + \xi \frac{y}{\sigma_u}\right)^{-1/\xi}, \quad y > 0$$

**Step 2 (Exceedance Probability):** The unconditional tail probability is:

$$\Pr(X > x) = \Pr(X > u)\Pr(X - u > x - u \mid X > u) = \zeta_u \left(1 + \xi \frac{x - u}{\sigma_u}\right)^{-1/\xi}$$

**Step 3 (Set Equal to $1-\alpha$):** The VaR at confidence level $\alpha$ satisfies $\Pr(X > \text{VaR}_\alpha) = 1 - \alpha$:

$$\zeta_u \left(1 + \xi \frac{\text{VaR}_\alpha - u}{\sigma_u}\right)^{-1/\xi} = 1 - \alpha$$

**Step 4 (Algebraic Manipulation):** Divide both sides by $\zeta_u$ and raise to power $-\xi$:

$$1 + \xi \frac{\text{VaR}_\alpha - u}{\sigma_u} = \left(\frac{\zeta_u}{1 - \alpha}\right)^{-\xi}$$

**Step 5 (Final Formula):**

$$\text{VaR}_\alpha = u + \frac{\sigma_u}{\xi}\left[\left(\frac{1 - \alpha}{\zeta_u}\right)^{-\xi} - 1\right] = u + \frac{\sigma_u}{\xi}\left[\left(\frac{\alpha}{\zeta_u}\right)^{\xi} - 1\right]$$

(using $\zeta_u = N_u / n$ for empirical substitution).

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation:**

Given: $u = 25$, $N_u = 60$, $n = 1500$, $\hat{\sigma}_u = 18$, $\hat{\xi} = 0.42$, $\alpha = 0.995$.

**Step 1:** Compute the empirical exceedance ratio $\zeta_u = 60/1500 = 0.04$.

**Step 2:** Compute the ratio $\alpha / \zeta_u = 0.995 / 0.04 = 24.875$.

**Step 3:** Raise to the power $\xi = 0.42$:

$$(24.875)^{0.42} = e^{0.42 \times \ln(24.875)} = e^{0.42 \times 3.2137} = e^{1.3498} \approx 3.856$$

**Step 4:** Substitute into the VaR formula:

$$\text{VaR}_{0.995} = 25 + \frac{18}{0.42}\left[3.856 - 1\right] = 25 + 42.857 \times 2.856 = 25 + 122.39 \approx 147.39$$

**Step 5 (Expected Shortfall):** Use the formula:

$$\text{ES}_\alpha = \frac{\text{VaR}_\alpha}{1 - \xi} + \frac{\sigma_u - \xi u}{1 - \xi} = \frac{147.39}{0.58} + \frac{18 - 0.42 \times 25}{0.58}$$

$$= 254.12 + \frac{18 - 10.5}{0.58} = 254.12 + \frac{7.5}{0.58} = 254.12 + 12.93 = 267.05$$

**Final Answer:** VaR = **₹147.39 lakhs**, ES = **₹267.05 lakhs**.

**Interpretation:** The bank faces a 0.5% probability of losing more than ₹147.39 lakhs in a single day. Conditional on such a loss occurring, the *expected* loss is ₹267.05 lakhs.

**[Total: 14 Marks]**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls to AVOID:**
> 1. **Sign error on the log term:** Students often write $x_T = \mu - (\sigma/\xi)[1 - \{-\log(1-1/T)\}^{-\xi}]$ with the *wrong sign* inside the curly braces. The correct form is $+(-\log(1-1/T))^{-\xi}$, not $-(\log(1-1/T))^{-\xi}$.
> 2. **Forgetting $\xi \neq 0$ condition:** You must explicitly state that the formula breaks down at $\xi = 0$ and provide the Gumbel limit. Failing to do so costs **1 mark**.
> 3. **Confusing $\zeta_u$ with $\alpha$:** In the VaR formula, it is $\alpha / \zeta_u$ raised to power $\xi$, **not** $1/\alpha$. Swapping them produces a wildly incorrect answer.
> 4. **Not interpreting the shape parameter:** KTU examiners allocate **1 mark** for explicitly stating the tail-type implication ($\xi > 0$ → Fréchet fat tails). Do not skip this even if the question doesn't directly ask.
> 5. **Mixing block maxima with POT formulas:** Ensure the **return level** uses GEV parameters, but the **VaR** uses GPD parameters (or GEV with appropriate conversion). Wrong-family substitution loses 4–5 marks.
> 6. **Numerical rounding:** Carry at least **4 decimal places** in intermediate steps; rounding early causes cascading errors worth **1–2 marks**.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Extreme Value Theory (Module 4)**
>
> - **EVT's role in finance:** Modeling *tail risk* (extreme losses) that classical Gaussian models underestimate.
> - **Two main theorems:** Fisher–Tippett–Gnedenko (1928) → GEV; Pickands–Balkema–De Haan (1975) → GPD.
> - **GEV CDF:** $H(x) = \exp\{-[1 + \xi(x-\mu)/\sigma]^{-1/\xi}\}$. Always note support: $1 + \xi(x-\mu)/\sigma > 0$.
> - **GPD CDF:** $G(y) = 1 - (1 + \xi y/\sigma_u)^{-1/\xi}$ for $y > 0$.
> - **Shape parameter $\xi$ is the heart of EVT:** $\xi > 0$ (Fréchet/fat-tail), $\xi = 0$ (Gumbel), $\xi < 0$ (Weibull/bounded).
> - **Empirical fact:** Stock and FX returns almost always yield $\xi \in (0.2, 0.6)$.
> - **Return level $x_T$ (GEV):** Invert $H(x_T) = 1 - 1/T$. Final form: $x_T = \mu - (\sigma/\xi)[1 - \{-\log(1-1/T)\}^{-\xi}]$.
> - **Gumbel limit:** $x_T = \mu - \sigma\log[-\log(1-1/T)]$.
> - **VaR (GPD):** $\text{VaR}_\alpha = u + (\sigma_u/\xi)[(\alpha n / N_u)^{\xi} - 1]$.
> - **Expected Shortfall:** $\text{ES}_\alpha = \text{VaR}_\alpha/(1-\xi) + (\sigma_u - \xi u)/(1-\xi)$, valid for $\xi < 1$.
> - **BMM vs POT:** BMM wastes data (one obs/block); POT uses all exceedances → preferred in Basel-compliant risk models.
> - **Threshold selection (POT):** Use the **mean excess function plot** $e(u) = (\sigma_u + \xi u)/(1-\xi)$; linearity indicates the valid range.
> - **Hill Estimator:** $\hat{\xi}_{H,k} = (1/k) \sum_{i=1}^{k} \log(X_{(n-i+1)}/X_{(n-k)})$; plot vs $k$ to find stable region.
> - **Tail index relation:** $\alpha_{\text{Pareto}} = 1/\xi$. A larger $\xi$ ⇒ heavier tail ⇒ larger VaR for a given confidence.
> - **Real-world use:** Basel III/IV capital, reinsurance pricing, hydrology, telecom-network load estimation.
> - **Key exam trap:** Always check the **sign and limiting case** of $\xi$ in every formula — KTU 2024 explicitly tests this.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Multivariate Linear Time Series: Stationary Vector Autoregressive Time Series
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET742 - FINANCIAL TIME SERIES ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Multivariate Linear Time Series: Stationary Vector Autoregressive Time Series

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (Stationary Vector Autoregressive [VAR(p)] Time Series)**
> A $k$-dimensional **vector time series** $\mathbf{y}_t = (y_{1t}, y_{2t}, \dots, y_{kt})'$ is said to follow a **stationary vector autoregressive model of order $p$**, denoted $\text{VAR}(p)$, if it satisfies the linear stochastic difference equation:
> $$\mathbf{y}_t = \mathbf{c} + \Phi_1 \mathbf{y}_{t-1} + \Phi_2 \mathbf{y}_{t-2} + \dots + \Phi_p \mathbf{y}_{t-p} + \boldsymbol{\epsilon}_t$$
> where:
> - $\mathbf{c} \in \mathbb{R}^k$ is a constant intercept vector,
> - $\Phi_i \in \mathbb{R}^{k \times k}$ for $i = 1, 2, \dots, p$ are the **autoregressive coefficient matrices** (each $k \times k$),
> - $\boldsymbol{\epsilon}_t \in \mathbb{R}^k$ is a **white noise innovation vector** with $E(\boldsymbol{\epsilon}_t) = \mathbf{0}$, $E(\boldsymbol{\epsilon}_t \boldsymbol{\epsilon}_t') = \Sigma_\epsilon$ (positive definite), and $E(\boldsymbol{\epsilon}_t \boldsymbol{\epsilon}_s') = 0$ for $t \ne s$.

### 1.1.1 Stationarity Condition for VAR(p)

A VAR($p$) process is **weakly (covariance) stationary** if and only if all eigenvalues of the **companion matrix**

$$\mathcal{F} = \begin{bmatrix} \Phi_1 & \Phi_2 & \dots & \Phi_{p-1} & \Phi_p \\ I_k & 0 & \dots & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & I_k & \dots & 0 & 0 \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots & \vdots \\ 0 & 0 & \dots & I_k & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

lie **strictly inside the unit circle**, i.e., $\vert \lambda_i(\mathcal{F}) \vert < 1$ for all $i = 1, \dots, kp$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Intuition (the "Weather-Crop-Bank" analogy)**
> Think of a VAR(1) model as a small economic ecosystem of **three indicators that constantly whisper to each other**:
> 1. **Today's Interest Rate** ($y_{1t}$),
> 2. **Today's Inflation** ($y_{2t}$),
> 3. **Today's Stock Index** ($y_{3t}$).
>
> Each of them is a function of **all three of yesterday's values** (cross-dependence), plus today's surprise "shock" ($\boldsymbol{\epsilon}_t$). The matrix $\Phi_1$ encodes **how strongly each past variable influences each current variable** — a single number in $\Phi_1$ is essentially the *slope* of a multivariate regression. The stationarity condition is the **"gravity check"**: the whole system must eventually settle down to a long-run mean rather than spiraling into infinity (a financial crash or a hyperinflationary blow-up).

### 1.2.1 Why Multivariate? Why not just many Univariate ARs?

| Aspect | Univariate AR($p$) | Vector AR($p$) |
|---|---|---|
| Variables | **One** series $y_t$ | **$k$** jointly evolving series $\mathbf{y}_t$ |
| Coefficients | Scalars $\phi_1,\dots,\phi_p$ | Matrices $\Phi_1,\dots,\Phi_p$ |
| Shocks | Scalar $\epsilon_t$ | Vector $\boldsymbol{\epsilon}_t$ with covariance $\Sigma_\epsilon$ |
| Cross-effects | **Ignored** | **Explicitly modelled** via off-diagonal entries of $\Phi_i$ |
| Forecasting | $y_{t+h}$ alone | All $k$ variables jointly — richer, more accurate |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** A common Part-A question asks, *"Why is VAR preferred over running individual AR models?"* — the answer is **Granger causality and cross-correlation in residuals**, which would be lost if you modelled each series in isolation.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Logic of the VAR(p) Model — Step by Step

A VAR($p$) model is built on **four foundational pillars** that KTU examiners love to test:

### Pillar 1: The Reduced-Form Structure
The model treats **every equation symmetrically** — there is *no* endogenous/exogenous distinction. Each of the $k$ equations in the system is an OLS-style regression:

$$y_{it} = c_i + \phi_{i1}^{(1)} y_{1,t-1} + \phi_{i2}^{(1)} y_{2,t-1} + \dots + \phi_{ik}^{(p)} y_{k,t-p} + \epsilon_{it}$$

for $i = 1, 2, \dots, k$. **There are $k^2 p + k$ parameters in total** (excluding $\Sigma_\epsilon$).

### Pillar 2: White-Noise Innovation Vector
The shocks $\boldsymbol{\epsilon}_t$ are assumed to be **serially uncorrelated** but **contemporaneously correlated**. The covariance matrix $\Sigma_\epsilon$ is **symmetric positive definite** ($k(k+1)/2$ unique entries). Contemporaneous correlation is what makes the multivariate structure *non-trivial* — if $\Sigma_\epsilon$ were diagonal, you could just split the model into $k$ independent AR($p$)s.

### Pillar 3: Stability via the Companion Matrix
A VAR($p$) is recast as a **VAR(1) in augmented state-space form** by stacking lagged vectors into a $kp \times 1$ "state":

$$\mathbf{z}_t = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{y}_t \\ \mathbf{y}_{t-1} \\ \vdots \\ \mathbf{y}_{t-p+1} \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{z}_t = \mathbf{F}\mathbf{z}_{t-1} + \boldsymbol{\eta}_t, \quad \boldsymbol{\eta}_t = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{c} + \boldsymbol{\epsilon}_t \\ \mathbf{0} \\ \vdots \\ \mathbf{0} \end{bmatrix}$$

The **spectral-radius condition** $\rho(\mathcal{F}) < 1$ is equivalent to stationarity. It guarantees that the infinite sum $\sum_{j=0}^\infty \mathcal{F}^j$ converges, which is required for the unconditional mean and covariance to exist.

### Pillar 4: Mean and Covariance of the Stationary Distribution
If $\rho(\mathcal{F}) < 1$, then:

$$\boldsymbol{\mu} = E(\mathbf{y}_t) = (I_k - \Phi_1 - \Phi_2 - \dots - \Phi_p)^{-1} \mathbf{c}$$

The **Yule–Walker equations for VAR** are:

$$\Gamma_\ell = \sum_{i=1}^{p} \Phi_i \Gamma_{\ell-i} \quad \text{for } \ell = 1, 2, 3, \dots$$

with $\Gamma_0 = \sum_{i=1}^{p} \Phi_i \Gamma_{-i} + \Sigma_\epsilon$ (the variance equation).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas — they appear in 80%+ of KTU Part-B VAR questions.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Dimension / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | VAR($p$) model | $\mathbf{y}_t = \mathbf{c} + \sum_{i=1}^{p}\Phi_i \mathbf{y}_{t-i} + \boldsymbol{\epsilon}_t$ | $\mathbf{y}_t \in \mathbb{R}^k$, $\Phi_i \in \mathbb{R}^{k\times k}$ |
| 2 | Innovation properties | $E(\boldsymbol{\epsilon}_t)=\mathbf{0}$, $E(\boldsymbol{\epsilon}_t\boldsymbol{\epsilon}_t')=\Sigma_\epsilon$, $E(\boldsymbol{\epsilon}_t\boldsymbol{\epsilon}_s')=0$ for $t\ne s$ | $\Sigma_\epsilon$ is $k \times k$ symmetric PD |
| 3 | **Stationarity condition** | All eigenvalues $\lambda_i$ of companion matrix $\mathcal{F}$ satisfy $\vert \lambda_i \vert < 1$ | Equivalent to $\det(I_k - \sum_{i=1}^p \Phi_i z^i) \ne 0$ for $\vert z \vert \le 1$ |
| 4 | Companion matrix $\mathcal{F}$ | $kp \times kp$ block matrix: top row $(\Phi_1,\dots,\Phi_p)$, sub-diagonal of $I_k$'s | See explicit form above |
| 5 | Unconditional mean | $\boldsymbol{\mu} = (I_k - \Phi_1 - \Phi_2 - \dots - \Phi_p)^{-1} \mathbf{c}$ | Exists only if stationarity holds |
| 6 | Yule–Walker recursion | $\Gamma_\ell = \sum_{i=1}^{p} \Phi_i \Gamma_{\ell-i}$ for $\ell \ge 1$ | $\Gamma_0$ satisfies a discrete Lyapunov equation |
| 7 | Variance ($\ell = 0$) | $\Gamma_0 = \sum_{i=1}^{p} \Phi_i \Gamma_{-i} + \Sigma_\epsilon$ | Symmetric, positive definite |
| 8 | $h$-step forecast | $\hat{\mathbf{y}}_{t+h} = \mathbf{c} + \sum_{i=1}^{p} \Phi_i \hat{\mathbf{y}}_{t+h-i}$ | Recursive; plug past observations for $h \le 0$ |
| 9 | Forecast error | $\mathbf{e}_{t+h} = \mathbf{y}_{t+h} - \hat{\mathbf{y}}_{t+h}$ | $E(\mathbf{e}_{t+h}) = \mathbf{0}$ |
| 10 | Forecast error covariance | $\text{Cov}(\mathbf{e}_{t+h}) = \Sigma_\epsilon + \sum_{j=1}^{h-1} \Psi_j \Sigma_\epsilon \Psi_j'$ | $\Psi_0 = I_k$, $\Psi_j = \sum_{i=1}^{\min(j,p)} \Phi_i \Psi_{j-i}$ |
| 11 | Granger causality (X → Y) | Reject $H_0: \Phi^{(X \to Y)} = 0$ via Wald/F-test | Restricted VAR vs. Unrestricted VAR |
| 12 | AIC for VAR order | $\text{AIC}(p) = \ln \vert \hat{\Sigma}_\epsilon(p) \vert + \dfrac{2}{T} \cdot k^2 p$ | Lower AIC → preferred $p$ |
| 13 | BIC for VAR order | $\text{BIC}(p) = \ln \vert \hat{\Sigma}_\epsilon(p) \vert + \dfrac{\ln T}{T} \cdot k^2 p$ | Penalty grows with $\ln T$ (stronger than AIC) |
| 14 | Impulse response (MA form) | $\Psi_j$ from $\Psi(z) = (I_k - \Phi_1 z - \dots - \Phi_p z^p)^{-1}$ | $\mathbf{y}_t = \boldsymbol{\mu} + \sum_{j=0}^{\infty} \Psi_j \boldsymbol{\epsilon}_{t-j}$ |

### 2.2.1 Real-World Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering / Finance Applications**
> - **Macroeconomic forecasting** (GDP, inflation, interest rate, unemployment — joint VAR(2) is a workhorse).
> - **Portfolio risk management** — DCC-GARCH and VAR are paired to capture both mean and volatility dynamics.
> - **Stress-testing central banks** — IRFs trace the effect of a one-standard-deviation policy-rate shock on output and prices.
> - **Engineering signal processing** — multi-channel sensor arrays (e.g., EEG, vibration monitoring on turbines) naturally produce multivariate time series modelled as VAR.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation A — Mean of a Stationary VAR(p)

**Statement.** For a stationary VAR($p$) with intercept $\mathbf{c}$, the unconditional mean vector is $\boldsymbol{\mu} = (I_k - \Phi_1 - \dots - \Phi_p)^{-1}\mathbf{c}$.

**Step 1.** Take the expectation of both sides of the VAR($p$) equation. By stationarity, $E(\mathbf{y}_{t-i}) = E(\mathbf{y}_t) = \boldsymbol{\mu}$ for all $i$, and $E(\boldsymbol{\epsilon}_t) = \mathbf{0}$:

$$E(\mathbf{y}_t) = \mathbf{c} + \Phi_1 E(\mathbf{y}_{t-1}) + \Phi_2 E(\mathbf{y}_{t-2}) + \dots + \Phi_p E(\mathbf{y}_{t-p}) + E(\boldsymbol{\epsilon}_t)$$

**Step 2.** Substitute $\boldsymbol{\mu}$ on both sides:

$$\boldsymbol{\mu} = \mathbf{c} + \Phi_1 \boldsymbol{\mu} + \Phi_2 \boldsymbol{\mu} + \dots + \Phi_p \boldsymbol{\mu} + \mathbf{0}$$

**Step 3.** Group the $\boldsymbol{\mu}$ terms using linearity of matrix multiplication:

$$\boldsymbol{\mu} = \mathbf{c} + \left(\sum_{i=1}^{p} \Phi_i\right) \boldsymbol{\mu}$$

**Step 4.** Move the matrix-coefficient term to the left-hand side:

$$\boldsymbol{\mu} - \left(\sum_{i=1}^{p} \Phi_i\right) \boldsymbol{\mu} = \mathbf{c}$$

**Step 5.** Factor $\boldsymbol{\mu}$ (the matrix $I_k$ is the identity):

$$\left(I_k - \sum_{i=1}^{p} \Phi_i\right) \boldsymbol{\mu} = \mathbf{c}$$

**Step 6.** Left-multiply by the inverse (which exists if and only if stationarity holds, since $\det(I_k - \sum \Phi_i) \ne 0$ is the $z=1$ case of the stationarity determinant condition):

$$\boldsymbol{\mu} = \left(I_k - \sum_{i=1}^{p} \Phi_i\right)^{-1} \mathbf{c} \quad \blacksquare$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation B — Companion-Form Representation of VAR(p) as VAR(1)

**Statement.** Any VAR($p$) can be rewritten as a VAR(1) in a stacked state vector of dimension $kp$.

**Step 1.** Write the VAR($p$) equation explicitly:

$$\mathbf{y}_t = \mathbf{c} + \Phi_1 \mathbf{y}_{t-1} + \Phi_2 \mathbf{y}_{t-2} + \dots + \Phi_p \mathbf{y}_{t-p} + \boldsymbol{\epsilon}_t$$

**Step 2.** Define the **stacked state vector**:

$$\mathbf{z}_t = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{y}_t \\ \mathbf{y}_{t-1} \\ \vdots \\ \mathbf{y}_{t-p+1} \end{bmatrix} \in \mathbb{R}^{kp}$$

**Step 3.** Express $\mathbf{z}_t$ in terms of $\mathbf{z}_{t-1}$ by writing the first block (corresponding to $\mathbf{y}_t$) and the remaining identity-shifted blocks:

$$\mathbf{y}_{t-1} = I_k \mathbf{y}_{t-1} + 0 \cdot \mathbf{y}_{t-2} + \dots, \quad \mathbf{y}_{t-2} = 0 \cdot \mathbf{y}_{t-1} + I_k \mathbf{y}_{t-2} + \dots, \text{etc.}$$

**Step 4.** Assemble the augmented system — the first block is the VAR($p$) equation itself, and each subsequent block is just a shifted identity:

$$\begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{y}_t \\ \mathbf{y}_{t-1} \\ \mathbf{y}_{t-2} \\ \vdots \\ \mathbf{y}_{t-p+1} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{c} \\ \mathbf{0} \\ \mathbf{0} \\ \vdots \\ \mathbf{0} \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} \Phi_1 & \Phi_2 & \dots & \Phi_{p-1} & \Phi_p \\ I_k & 0 & \dots & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & I_k & \dots & 0 & 0 \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots & \vdots \\ 0 & 0 & \dots & I_k & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{y}_{t-1} \\ \mathbf{y}_{t-2} \\ \mathbf{y}_{t-3} \\ \vdots \\ \mathbf{y}_{t-p} \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} \boldsymbol{\epsilon}_t \\ \mathbf{0} \\ \mathbf{0} \\ \vdots \\ \mathbf{0} \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5.** Compact form:

$$\mathbf{z}_t = \mathbf{c}^* + \mathcal{F} \mathbf{z}_{t-1} + \boldsymbol{\eta}_t, \qquad \dim(\mathbf{z}_t) = kp, \quad \dim(\mathcal{F}) = kp \times kp \quad \blacksquare$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation C — Yule–Walker Equations for VAR

**Statement.** The autocovariance matrices of a stationary VAR($p$) satisfy $\Gamma_\ell = \sum_{i=1}^{p} \Phi_i \Gamma_{\ell-i}$ for $\ell \ge 1$.

**Step 1.** Subtract the mean equation from the original VAR — define the mean-adjusted series $\tilde{\mathbf{y}}_t = \mathbf{y}_t - \boldsymbol{\mu}$:

$$\tilde{\mathbf{y}}_t = \Phi_1 \tilde{\mathbf{y}}_{t-1} + \Phi_2 \tilde{\mathbf{y}}_{t-2} + \dots + \Phi_p \tilde{\mathbf{y}}_{t-p} + \boldsymbol{\epsilon}_t$$

**Step 2.** Compute the cross-moment $E(\tilde{\mathbf{y}}_{t-\ell} \tilde{\mathbf{y}}_t')$ for $\ell \ge 1$ by post-multiplying the equation by $\tilde{\mathbf{y}}_{t-\ell}'$ and taking expectations:

$$E(\tilde{\mathbf{y}}_{t-\ell} \tilde{\mathbf{y}}_t') = \sum_{i=1}^{p} \Phi_i E(\tilde{\mathbf{y}}_{t-\ell} \tilde{\mathbf{y}}_{t-i}') + E(\tilde{\mathbf{y}}_{t-\ell} \boldsymbol{\epsilon}_t')$$

**Step 3.** Recognise that $E(\tilde{\mathbf{y}}_{t-\ell} \boldsymbol{\epsilon}_t') = 0$ for $\ell \ge 1$ (shock $\boldsymbol{\epsilon}_t$ is orthogonal to all past $\tilde{\mathbf{y}}$'s). Also, $E(\tilde{\mathbf{y}}_{t-\ell} \tilde{\mathbf{y}}_{t-i}') = \Gamma_{\ell-i}$ (by stationarity, the lag difference is $\ell - i$).

**Step 4.** Therefore:

$$\Gamma_\ell = \sum_{i=1}^{p} \Phi_i \Gamma_{\ell-i} \quad \blacksquare$$

**Step 5 (Variance equation, $\ell = 0$).** Set $\ell = 0$ in the cross-moment computation. Now $E(\tilde{\mathbf{y}}_{t} \boldsymbol{\epsilon}_t') = \Sigma_\epsilon$ (the contemporaneous cross-moment, since the shock is *not* orthogonal to its own-date $\mathbf{y}$):

$$\Gamma_0 = \sum_{i=1}^{p} \Phi_i \Gamma_{-i} + \Sigma_\epsilon = \sum_{i=1}^{p} \Phi_i \Gamma_i' + \Sigma_\epsilon$$

This is a **discrete Lyapunov equation**, solvable for $\Gamma_0$ given $\Phi_1, \dots, \Phi_p$ and $\Sigma_\epsilon$.

---

## 3.4 Derivation D — Recursive $h$-Step Forecast

**Statement.** The minimum mean-square-error forecast is $\hat{\mathbf{y}}_{t+h} = \mathbf{c} + \sum_{i=1}^{p} \Phi_i \hat{\mathbf{y}}_{t+h-i}$ for $h \ge 1$.

**Step 1.** The $h$-step forecast is defined as $\hat{\mathbf{y}}_{t+h} = E(\mathbf{y}_{t+h} \mid \mathcal{F}_t)$, where $\mathcal{F}_t$ is the information set up to time $t$.

**Step 2.** Write the VAR($p$) equation at $t+h$ and take the conditional expectation given $\mathcal{F}_t$:

$$E(\mathbf{y}_{t+h} \mid \mathcal{F}_t) = \mathbf{c} + \sum_{i=1}^{p} \Phi_i E(\mathbf{y}_{t+h-i} \mid \mathcal{F}_t) + E(\boldsymbol{\epsilon}_{t+h} \mid \mathcal{F}_t)$$

**Step 3.** Since $\boldsymbol{\epsilon}_{t+h}$ is the *innovation at time $t+h$* and is independent of $\mathcal{F}_t$, we have $E(\boldsymbol{\epsilon}_{t+h} \mid \mathcal{F}_t) = \mathbf{0}$.

**Step 4.** For $h-i \le 0$, $E(\mathbf{y}_{t+h-i} \mid \mathcal{F}_t) = \mathbf{y}_{t+h-i}$ (observed value). For $h-i > 0$, $E(\mathbf{y}_{t+h-i} \mid \mathcal{F}_t) = \hat{\mathbf{y}}_{t+h-i}$ (recursive forecast). Combining:

$$\hat{\mathbf{y}}_{t+h} = \mathbf{c} + \sum_{i=1}^{p} \Phi_i \hat{\mathbf{y}}_{t+h-i} \quad \blacksquare$$

---

## 3.5 Derivation E — Stationarity via the Determinantal Condition

**Statement.** A VAR($p$) is stationary if and only if $\det(I_k - \Phi_1 z - \Phi_2 z^2 - \dots - \Phi_p z^p) \ne 0$ for all complex $z$ with $\vert z \vert \le 1$.

**Step 1.** In the companion form $\mathbf{z}_t = \mathbf{c}^* + \mathcal{F}\mathbf{z}_{t-1} + \boldsymbol{\eta}_t$, the characteristic polynomial of $\mathcal{F}$ is:

$$\det(\lambda I_{kp} - \mathcal{F}) = \lambda^{k(p-1)} \det\left(\lambda I_k - \Phi_1 - \frac{1}{\lambda}\Phi_2 - \dots - \frac{1}{\lambda^{p-1}}\Phi_p\right)$$

**Step 2.** Multiplying inside the determinant by $\lambda^{p-1}$ (a constant w.r.t. the determinant) gives:

$$\det(\lambda I_{kp} - \mathcal{F}) = \lambda^{k(p-1)} \cdot \frac{1}{\lambda^{p-1}} \det\left(\lambda^p I_k - \Phi_1 \lambda^{p-1} - \Phi_2 \lambda^{p-2} - \dots - \Phi_p\right)$$

$$= \lambda^{k(p-1)-(p-1)} \det\left(\lambda^p I_k - \sum_{i=1}^{p} \Phi_i \lambda^{p-i}\right)$$

**Step 3.** Substituting $z = 1/\lambda$ (so that $\vert \lambda \vert < 1 \iff \vert z \vert > 1$), the **eigenvalue condition** $\rho(\mathcal{F}) < 1$ becomes:

$$\det\left(I_k - \Phi_1 z - \Phi_2 z^2 - \dots - \Phi_p z^p\right) \ne 0 \quad \text{for } \vert z \vert \le 1 \quad \blacksquare$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Block Diagram of a VAR(p) Process

The following Mermaid block diagram traces how a VAR($p$) system ingests lagged observations, transforms them via the coefficient matrices, and emits both forecasts and the innovation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: Lagged Vector y sub t minus 1] --> B1[Phi sub 1 Matrix Multiply]
    A2[Input: Lagged Vector y sub t minus 2] --> B2[Phi sub 2 Matrix Multiply]
    A3[Input: Lagged Vector y sub t minus p] --> Bp[Phi sub p Matrix Multiply]
    C[Intercept Vector c] --> SumNode
    B1 --> SumNode
    B2 --> SumNode
    Bp --> SumNode
    Eps[White Noise Vector epsilon sub t, mean zero, Cov Sigma epsilon] --> SumNode
    SumNode[Matrix Sum] --> Out[Output: y sub t in R power k]
    Out --> F1[Compute Mean mu equals I minus sum Phi i inverse c]
    Out --> F2[Compute Autocovariance Gamma ell via Yule Walker]
    Out --> F3[Compute Forecast y hat sub t plus h recursively]
    Out --> F4[Compute Impulse Response Psi j from MA form]
    F1 --> Result[Stationarity Check: eigenvalues of companion F satisfy mod lambda less than 1]
    F2 --> Result
    F3 --> Result
    F4 --> Result
    Result --> Decision{Is the Process Stationary?}
    Decision -->|Yes| Done[Process is covariance stationary, forecasts are valid]
    Decision -->|No| Unstable[Reject or Difference the Series]
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture of a VAR(p) Forecasting Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    D1[Raw Multivariate Data y1 y2 yk] --> D2[Stationarity Test: ADF on each series]
    D2 --> D3{Differenced?}
    D3 -->|Yes| D4[Apply d-th order differencing]
    D3 -->|No| D5[Proceed with raw series]
    D4 --> D6[Determine VAR order p via AIC BIC]
    D5 --> D6
    D6 --> D7[OLS Estimation of Phi sub 1 to Phi sub p and c]
    D7 --> D8[Residual Diagnostics: Portmanteau and Normality]
    D8 --> D9[Check Companion Matrix Eigenvalues mod lambda less than 1]
    D9 --> D10[Deploy for h step Ahead Forecasting]
    D10 --> D11[Construct Impulse Response Functions Psi j]
    D10 --> D12[Perform Granger Causality Tests]
```

## 4.3 Companion-Form Schematic for VAR(2)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[State z sub t minus 1 contains y sub t minus 1 and y sub t minus 2] --> S1[Companion Matrix F of size 2k by 2k]
    S1 --> S2[Top Row: Phi sub 1 Phi sub 2]
    S1 --> S3[Sub-diagonal: I sub k and I sub k]
    S2 --> S4[Augmented y sub t equals Phi sub 1 y sub t minus 1 plus Phi sub 2 y sub t minus 2]
    S3 --> S5[Shift identities: y sub t minus 1 equals y sub t minus 1]
    S4 --> S6[Compute eigenvalues of F: spectral radius less than 1 for stationarity]
    S5 --> S6
    S6 --> S7[Output: confirms covariance stationarity of the VAR 2 system]
```
