- **Half-life of volatility shocks** $\tau_{1/2} = \ln(0.5)/\ln(\alpha+\beta)$ — measured in days.
- **Number of parameters** $\mathcal{O}(N K + K^2)$ instead of $\mathcal{O}(N^2)$ — the parsimony gain.

### 1.4 Why These Models Matter in KTU Context

KTU 2024 scheme (NEP-aligned) expects students to:
- **CO4 (Module 4 typical):** *Apply multivariate volatility models for portfolio risk forecasting and Value-at-Risk computation.*
- Recognize that **ARCH/GARCH family is univariate**, but financial applications (portfolio allocation, VaR, derivative hedging) demand **multivariate** extensions.
- Factor models are the **industry standard** for managing dimensionality in risk systems used by banks and asset managers.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Building Up: From ARCH to Factor-GARCH

### Step 1 — Univariate ARCH(q) Model (Engle, 1982)

The conditional variance depends linearly on past squared residuals:

$$\sigma_t^{2} = \omega + \sum_{i=1}^{q} \alpha_i \, \varepsilon_{t-i}^{2}, \qquad \omega > 0,\ \alpha_i \ge 0$$

**Why this works:** Squared residuals $\varepsilon_{t-i}^{2}$ are a noisy proxy for past variance; large shocks "cluster" because volatility is persistent.

**Intuition:** Yesterday's storm predicts today's storm.

### Step 2 — Univariate GARCH(p, q) Model (Bollerslev, 1986)

Adds past *variances* as predictors — a parsimonious ARMA-like representation of volatility:

$$\sigma_t^{2} = \omega + \sum_{i=1}^{q} \alpha_i \, \varepsilon_{t-i}^{2} + \sum_{j=1}^{p} \beta_j \, \sigma_{t-j}^{2}$$

For stationarity of the conditional variance:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{q} \alpha_i + \sum_{j=1}^{p} \beta_j < 1$$

The **unconditional (long-run) variance** is then:

$$\bar{\sigma}^{2} = \frac{\omega}{1 - \displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{q}\alpha_i - \displaystyle\sum_{j=1}^{p}\beta_j}$$

### Step 3 — Multivariate GARCH — The Dimensionality Problem

The naïve multivariate extension would require $\mathbf{H}_t = \mathbf{H}_t(\boldsymbol{\theta})$ with **$\mathcal{O}(N^2)$ parameters per equation** plus cross-terms. For $N = 100$ stocks this becomes infeasible.

### Step 4 — Factor Decomposition (the Cure)

Assume only $K$ common factors (typically $K \ll N$) drive co-movement:

$$\mathbf{H}_t = \mathbf{B} \mathbf{F}_t \mathbf{B}^{\top} + \mathbf{D}_t$$

- $\mathbf{B}$: $N \times K$ **loading matrix** (sensitivity of each asset to each factor).
- $\mathbf{F}_t$: $K \times K$ **factor covariance matrix**, often diagonal if factors are orthogonal.
- $\mathbf{D}_t$: $N \times N$ diagonal matrix of idiosyncratic variances (each following its own univariate GARCH).

Total parameters: $\mathcal{O}(NK + N + K^2)$, **linear in $N$** instead of quadratic.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below contains every formula the examiner can ask in Part A or Part B for this topic. **Memorize the structural forms** and the **stationarity / persistence conditions**.

| # | Concept | Formula | Boundary / Conditions |
|---|---------|---------|-----------------------|
| 1 | ARCH(q) conditional variance | $\sigma_t^{2} = \omega + \displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{q} \alpha_i \varepsilon_{t-i}^{2}$ | $\omega > 0$, $\alpha_i \ge 0$ |
| 2 | GARCH(p,q) | $\sigma_t^{2} = \omega + \displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{q} \alpha_i \varepsilon_{t-i}^{2} + \displaystyle\sum_{j=1}^{p} \beta_j \sigma_{t-j}^{2}$ | $\omega > 0$, $\alpha_i, \beta_j \ge 0$ |
| 3 | GARCH(1,1) stationarity | $\alpha_1 + \beta_1 < 1$ | Strict inequality |
| 4 | Long-run variance | $\bar{\sigma}^{2} = \dfrac{\omega}{1 - \alpha - \beta}$ | Exists iff stationary |
| 5 | Half-life of shock | $\tau_{1/2} = \dfrac{\ln(0.5)}{\ln(\alpha + \beta)}$ | Days, when daily data |
| 6 | Mean equation (GARCH-M) | $r_t = \mu + \lambda \sigma_t^{2} + \varepsilon_t$ | Risk premium link |
| 7 | EGARCH (Nelson 1991) | $\ln \sigma_t^{2} = \omega + \displaystyle\sum \alpha_i (\vert z_{t-i}\vert - E\vert z_{t-i}\vert) + \sum \gamma_i z_{t-i} + \sum \beta_j \ln \sigma_{t-j}^{2}$ | Allows leverage effect |
| 8 | GJR-GARCH | $\sigma_t^{2} = \omega + (\alpha + \gamma I_{t-1}^{-}) \varepsilon_{t-1}^{2} + \beta \sigma_{t-1}^{2}$ | $I^{-} = 1$ if $\varepsilon < 0$ |
| 9 | Factor volatility model | $\mathbf{H}_t = \mathbf{B} \mathbf{F}_t \mathbf{B}^{\top} + \mathbf{D}_t$ | $\mathbf{F}_t$ symmetric pd |
| 10 | Idiosyncratic GARCH | $\sigma_{i,t}^{2} = \omega_i + \alpha_i \varepsilon_{i,t-1}^{2} + \beta_i \sigma_{i,t-1}^{2}$ | Diagonal $\mathbf{D}_t$ entries |
| 11 | Factor GARCH (orthogonal factors) | $f_{k,t} = h_{k,t}^{1/2} z_{k,t}$, $h_{k,t}$ univariate GARCH | $K$ parallel univariate GARCH |
| 12 | Log-likelihood (Gaussian) | $\ell(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = -\dfrac{T}{2}\ln(2\pi) - \dfrac{1}{2}\displaystyle\sum_{t=1}^{T}\left[\ln\vert \mathbf{H}_t\vert + \boldsymbol{\epsilon}_t^{\top}\mathbf{H}_t^{-1}\boldsymbol{\epsilon}_t\right]$ | Maximized via MLE |
| 13 | h-step variance forecast | $\sigma_{t+h}^{2} = \bar{\sigma}^{2} + (\alpha + \beta)^{h-1}(\sigma_{t+1}^{2} - \bar{\sigma}^{2})$ | Mean-reverts to $\bar{\sigma}^2$ |
| 14 | VaR (1-day, 99%) | $\text{VaR}_{0.99} = \mu + z_{0.99} \, \sigma_t$ | $z_{0.99} = 2.33$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key engineering reality:** The matrix inverse $\mathbf{H}_t^{-1}$ has a **closed-form** under factor structure using the **Woodbury matrix identity** — this is why factor-volatility models are computationally feasible in real-time trading systems.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Bank Treasury / ALM desks:** Track the VaR of a 500-asset portfolio using a 3-factor volatility model — only $\sim$1500 parameters instead of $\sim$125,000.
- **Derivative pricing:** Stochastic discount factors and option smiles require accurate covariance matrices.
- **Risk dashboards:** Factor-volatility models yield *decomposition attribution* — "70% of today's portfolio loss is from the 'oil price' factor".
- **Macro-prudential policy:** Central banks monitor systemic risk via latent factor volatility.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From ARCH(1) to GARCH(1,1)

### Starting Point: ARCH(1)

$$\sigma_t^{2} = \omega + \alpha \varepsilon_{t-1}^{2}, \qquad \varepsilon_t = \sigma_t z_t,\ z_t \sim N(0,1)$$

Substitute the definition $\varepsilon_{t-1}^{2} = \sigma_{t-1}^{2} z_{t-1}^{2}$:

$$\sigma_t^{2} = \omega + \alpha \sigma_{t-1}^{2} z_{t-1}^{2}$$

This expresses today's variance as a **linear combination of yesterday's variance scaled by the realized shock** plus a constant. Iterating forward:

$$\sigma_t^{2} = \omega + \alpha \omega z_{t-1}^{2} + \alpha^2 \omega z_{t-2}^{2} z_{t-1}^{2} + \alpha^3 \omega z_{t-3}^{2} z_{t-2}^{2} z_{t-1}^{2} + \cdots$$

### Recognizing the Inefficiency

The ARCH(1) only uses $\varepsilon_{t-1}^{2}$. To capture longer memory, ARCH(q) needs many lags ($q$ terms). Bollerslev's insight: replace $\varepsilon_{t-1}^{2}$ by an **autoregressive approximation** of variance.

### Generalization to GARCH(p,q)

Replace the single lag of $\varepsilon^2$ with $q$ lags, and add $p$ lags of $\sigma^2$:

$$\sigma_t^{2} = \omega + \sum_{i=1}^{q} \alpha_i \varepsilon_{t-i}^{2} + \sum_{j=1}^{p} \beta_j \sigma_{t-j}^{2}$$

For **GARCH(1,1)**:

$$\sigma_t^{2} = \omega + \alpha \varepsilon_{t-1}^{2} + \beta \sigma_{t-1}^{2}$$

### Derivation of the Unconditional Variance

Take the unconditional expectation of both sides, using $E[\varepsilon_t^{2}] = E[\sigma_t^{2}] = \bar{\sigma}^2$:

$$\bar{\sigma}^{2} = \omega + \alpha \bar{\sigma}^{2} + \beta \bar{\sigma}^{2}$$

Solve for $\bar{\sigma}^2$:

$$\bar{\sigma}^{2} - (\alpha + \beta)\bar{\sigma}^{2} = \omega$$

$$\bar{\sigma}^{2} \, [1 - (\alpha + \beta)] = \omega$$

$$\boxed{\ \bar{\sigma}^{2} = \dfrac{\omega}{1 - \alpha - \beta}\ }$$

This formula is **only valid** if $\alpha + \beta < 1$ — otherwise the variance diverges (non-stationarity).

### Derivation of Half-Life of Volatility Shocks

The h-step-ahead forecast of $\sigma_{t+h}^{2}$ under GARCH(1,1) is:

$$\sigma_{t+h}^{2} = \bar{\sigma}^{2} + (\alpha + \beta)^{h-1}\left(\sigma_{t+1}^{2} - \bar{\sigma}^{2}\right)$$

Setting the deviation from long-run variance to half its initial value:

$$(\alpha + \beta)^{\tau_{1/2} - 1} = \frac{1}{2}$$

$$\tau_{1/2} - 1 = \frac{\ln(0.5)}{\ln(\alpha + \beta)}$$

$$\boxed{\ \tau_{1/2} = 1 + \dfrac{\ln(0.5)}{\ln(\alpha + \beta)}\ }$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Factor-Volatility Covariance Structure

### Setup

Let the asset return vector follow a **one-factor structure** (easily extended to $K$ factors):

$$\mathbf{r}_t = \boldsymbol{\mu} + \mathbf{b} \, f_t + \boldsymbol{\epsilon}_t$$

where:
- $\mathbf{b}$: $N \times 1$ factor-loading vector (e.g., betas to a market factor).
- $f_t$: scalar latent factor with $E[f_t] = 0$, $\text{Var}(f_t \mid \mathcal{F}_{t-1}) = h_{f,t}$ (its own GARCH process).
- $\boldsymbol{\epsilon}_t$: $N \times 1$ idiosyncratic vector with $\text{Var}(\boldsymbol{\epsilon}_t \mid \mathcal{F}_{t-1}) = \mathbf{D}_t = \text{diag}(\sigma_{1,t}^{2}, \ldots, \sigma_{N,t}^{2})$.

### Computing the Conditional Covariance

By construction, $f_t$ and $\boldsymbol{\epsilon}_t$ are independent given $\mathcal{F}_{t-1}$:

$$\mathbf{H}_t = E[(\mathbf{r}_t - \boldsymbol{\mu})(\mathbf{r}_t - \boldsymbol{\mu})^{\top} \mid \mathcal{F}_{t-1}]$$

$$= E[(\mathbf{b} f_t + \boldsymbol{\epsilon}_t)(\mathbf{b} f_t + \boldsymbol{\epsilon}_t)^{\top}\mid \mathcal{F}_{t-1}]$$

$$= \mathbf{b} E[f_t^{2} \mid \mathcal{F}_{t-1}]\mathbf{b}^{\top} + E[\boldsymbol{\epsilon}_t\boldsymbol{\epsilon}_t^{\top}\mid \mathcal{F}_{t-1}]$$

(because cross-term $E[f_t \boldsymbol{\epsilon}_t^{\top}] = 0$):

$$\boxed{\ \mathbf{H}_t = \mathbf{b}\,h_{f,t}\,\mathbf{b}^{\top} + \mathbf{D}_t\ }$$

For $K$ factors, the matrix $\mathbf{b}\,h_{f,t}\,\mathbf{b}^{\top}$ generalizes to $\mathbf{B}\,\mathbf{F}_t\,\mathbf{B}^{\top}$ as introduced in §1.

### Efficient Inverse via Woodbury Identity

The log-likelihood requires $\mathbf{H}_t^{-1}$. Direct inversion is $\mathcal{O}(N^3)$. Woodbury gives:

$$\mathbf{H}_t^{-1} = \mathbf{D}_t^{-1} - \mathbf{D}_t^{-1}\mathbf{B}\left(\mathbf{F}_t^{-1} + \mathbf{B}^{\top}\mathbf{D}_t^{-1}\mathbf{B}\right)^{-1}\mathbf{B}^{\top}\mathbf{D}_t^{-1}$$

Cost: $\mathcal{O}(N) + \mathcal{O}(K^3)$ — **massive** speedup for $N \gg K$.

### Worked Numerical Illustration

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU-style illustrative computation (GARCH(1,1) long-run variance).**
> Given $\omega = 0.000002$, $\alpha = 0.08$, $\beta = 0.90$:
>
> Persistence = $0.08 + 0.90 = 0.98 < 1$ ✓ (stationary)
> Long-run variance = $0.000002 / (1 - 0.98) = 0.000002 / 0.02 = 0.0001$
> Daily long-run std dev = $\sqrt{0.0001} = 0.01 = 1\%$ per day
> Annualized ≈ $1\% \times \sqrt{252} \approx 15.87\%$
> Half-life = $1 + \ln(0.5)/\ln(0.98) \approx 1 + (-0.693)/(-0.0202) \approx 1 + 34.3 = 35.3$ days

### Estimation Procedure (Gaussian Quasi-MLE)

Step 1 — Assume $\boldsymbol{\epsilon}_t \mid \mathcal{F}_{t-1} \sim N(\mathbf{0}, \mathbf{H}_t)$.

Step 2 — Conditional log-likelihood:

$$\ell(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = -\frac{T}{2}\ln(2\pi) - \frac{1}{2}\sum_{t=1}^{T}\left[\ln\vert \mathbf{H}_t(\boldsymbol{\theta})\vert + \boldsymbol{\epsilon}_t^{\top}\mathbf{H}_t(\boldsymbol{\theta})^{-1}\boldsymbol{\epsilon}_t\right]$$

Step 3 — Maximize w.r.t. $\boldsymbol{\theta} = (\text{mean params}, \omega_i, \alpha_i, \beta_i, \mathbf{B}, \text{factor-GARCH params})$.

Step 4 — Standard errors from the inverse of the observed information matrix (Hessian) — used for Wald t-statistics.

Step 5 — Check residual diagnostics: standardized residuals $\tilde{\boldsymbol{\epsilon}}_t = \mathbf{H}_t^{-1/2}\boldsymbol{\epsilon}_t$ should be i.i.d.; examine $\tilde{\boldsymbol{\epsilon}}_t^{2}$ for remaining ARCH effects (Ljung-Box test).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Data Flow in Factor-Volatility Modeling

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Return Series r1t ... rNt] --> B[Mean Equation Fit]
    B --> C[Residuals eps1t ... epsNt]
    C --> D[Principal Component Analysis]
    D --> E[Identify K Latent Factors ft]
    E --> F[Estimate Loadings Matrix B]
    C --> G[Idiosyncratic Component dit]
    E --> H[Factor GARCH Engine]
    G --> I[Diagonal GARCH Engine]
    H --> J[Reconstruct Ht via B Ft B^T + Dt]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Woodbury Inverse for Ht inv]
    K --> L[Gaussian Log Likelihood]
    L --> M[Numerical Maximization]
    M --> N[Parameter Estimates theta hat]
    N --> O[Diagnostic Checks Ljung Box on residuals squared]
    O --> P[Forecasts Sigma squared t plus h and VaR]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Architectural Comparison of GARCH Variants

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Univariate ARCH q] -->|Add lagged variance| B[Univariate GARCH p q]
    B -->|Asymmetric term gamma z| C[GJR GARCH]
    B -->|Log variance spec| D[EGARCH]
    B -->|Vectorize naive| E[Full VECH]
    E -->|Constrain correlations| F[CCC Model]
    E -->|Dynamic correlations| G[DCC Model]
    B -->|Factor decomposition| H[Factor GARCH]
    H -->|Single factor CAPM beta| I[One Factor GARCH]
    H -->|K latent PCA factors| J[K Factor GARCH]
    H -->|Orthogonal factors| K[Diagonal Factor GARCH]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Estimation Flow with Woodbury Speedup

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Initialize Parameters] --> B[Compute Dt diagonal GARCH]
    B --> C[Compute Ft factor GARCH]
    C --> D[Assemble Ht = B Ft B^T + Dt]
    D --> E[Apply Woodbury Matrix Identity]
    E --> F[Compute log determinant via log det Dt plus log det bracket]
    F --> G[Compute Quadratic Form eps trans H inv eps]
    G --> H[Sum to Get Negative Log Likelihood]
    H --> I{BFGS or Nelder Mead Convergence}
    I -->|Not Converged| J[Update Parameters via Numerical Gradient]
    J --> B
    I -->|Converged| K[Hessian via BHHH or Numerical]
    K --> L[Standard Errors and t Stats]
    L --> M[Final Model theta hat]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Mermaid block diagrams?** The KTU board examiner values *architecture clarity*. The three diagrams above mirror the way a real research paper or risk-system architecture document is structured: data $\to$ decomposition $\to$ estimation $\to$ diagnostics.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]`
**Define the GARCH(1,1) model. State the stationarity condition and derive the unconditional variance.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** The GARCH(1,1) model expresses today's conditional variance as:
  $$\sigma_t^{2} = \omega + \alpha \varepsilon_{t-1}^{2} + \beta \sigma_{t-1}^{2}$$
  with $\omega > 0$, $\alpha \ge 0$, $\beta \ge 0$.
- **Stationarity condition (1 mark):** $E[\sigma_t^2]$ exists and is finite iff $\alpha + \beta < 1$.
- **Derivation (1 mark):** Taking unconditional expectations, $E[\varepsilon_{t-1}^{2}] = E[\sigma_{t-1}^{2}] = \bar{\sigma}^{2}$:
  $$\bar{\sigma}^{2} = \omega + (\alpha + \beta)\bar{\sigma}^{2} \implies \bar{\sigma}^{2} = \dfrac{\omega}{1 - \alpha - \beta}$$

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**What is meant by a "factor-volatility model"? Why is it preferred over a full multivariate GARCH?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** A factor-volatility model decomposes the conditional covariance matrix $\mathbf{H}_t$ of an $N$-dimensional return vector into a small number $K$ of common factor volatilities (systematic) and idiosyncratic volatilities (asset-specific):
  $$\mathbf{H}_t = \mathbf{B}\mathbf{F}_t\mathbf{B}^{\top} + \mathbf{D}_t$$
- **Dimensionality benefit (1 mark):** Parameters grow as $\mathcal{O}(NK + K^2 + N)$ instead of $\mathcal{O}(N^2)$, so estimation is feasible even for $N \approx 100$–$500$ assets.
- **Interpretability (1 mark):** Factor loadings $\mathbf{B}$ reveal *which* macro/sector shocks drive co-movement, enabling portfolio risk attribution and stress testing.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 scheme follows a strict module-internal choice. **Answer ANY ONE** of the two 14-mark alternatives below. Each has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

### 🔹 QUESTION A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 4]`

**(a)** **(7 marks)** Describe the general multivariate GARCH framework. What is the *curse of dimensionality* and how do factor-GARCH models resolve it? Show the decomposition $\mathbf{H}_t = \mathbf{B}\mathbf{F}_t\mathbf{B}^{\top} + \mathbf{D}_t$ and explain each component.

**(b)** **(7 marks)** A daily return series on a portfolio index is estimated as GARCH(1,1) with parameters $\omega = 5\times 10^{-6}$, $\alpha = 0.07$, $\beta = 0.91$.
   (i) Verify stationarity and compute the unconditional variance and standard deviation.
   (ii) Compute the half-life of a volatility shock.
   (iii) The current day's realized volatility is $\sigma_{t}^{2} = 0.0004$. Forecast $\sigma_{t+1}^{2}$ and $\sigma_{t+5}^{2}$.
   (iv) Comment on the practical implication of the persistence level.

#### Model Solution to A(a) — 7 marks

The multivariate GARCH framework generalizes the univariate GARCH(p,q) to a vector of returns. For an $N$-dimensional return vector $\mathbf{r}_t$ with mean $\boldsymbol{\mu}_t$, we write $\boldsymbol{\epsilon}_t = \mathbf{r}_t - \boldsymbol{\mu}_t$ and assume:

$$\boldsymbol{\epsilon}_t = \mathbf{H}_t^{1/2}\mathbf{z}_t, \qquad \mathbf{z}_t \sim \text{i.i.d. } N(\mathbf{0}, \mathbf{I})$$

The **VECH** form (the unrestricted extension) parameterizes:

$$\text{vech}(\mathbf{H}_t) = \mathbf{c} + \sum_{i=1}^{q}\mathbf{A}_i \, \text{vech}(\boldsymbol{\epsilon}_{t-i}\boldsymbol{\epsilon}_{t-i}^{\top}) + \sum_{j=1}^{p}\mathbf{B}_j \, \text{vech}(\mathbf{H}_{t-j})$$

**Curse of dimensionality (2 marks):** Each $\mathbf{A}_i$ and $\mathbf{B}_j$ is an $\left(\dfrac{N(N+1)}{2}\right) \times \left(\dfrac{N(N+1)}{2}\right)$ matrix. For $N=50$, this is $1275 \times 1275$ matrices. Parameter count explodes; estimation fails.

**Factor-GARCH resolution (3 marks):** Assume $\mathbf{H}_t = \mathbf{B}\mathbf{F}_t\mathbf{B}^{\top} + \mathbf{D}_t$ where:
- $\mathbf{B}$: $N \times K$ loadings (one column per factor).
- $\mathbf{F}_t$: $K \times K$ factor covariance, often diagonal with $K$ univariate GARCH processes.
- $\mathbf{D}_t$: $N \times N$ diagonal with each diagonal entry following its own univariate GARCH.

Total parameter count: $NK$ (loadings) $+ 3K$ (factor GARCH) $+ 3N$ (idiosyncratic GARCH) — **linear** in $N$.

**Why it works (1 mark):** Most cross-sectional co-movement in equity returns is explained by 2–5 macro factors (market, size, value, momentum, sector). The residual idiosyncratic variance is asset-specific and uncorrelated across assets.

**Interpretation (1 mark):** A 3-factor model can capture 80–90% of the variation in a 500-stock covariance matrix, as documented empirically (e.g., factor analysis of S&P 500 returns).

#### Model Solution to A(b) — 7 marks

**Given:** $\omega = 5\times 10^{-6}$, $\alpha = 0.07$, $\beta = 0.91$.

**(i) Stationarity check (1 mark):**
$\alpha + \beta = 0.07 + 0.91 = 0.98 < 1$ ✓ Stationary.

Unconditional variance (1 mark):
$$\bar{\sigma}^{2} = \frac{5\times 10^{-6}}{1 - 0.98} = \frac{5\times 10^{-6}}{0.02} = 2.5\times 10^{-4}$$

Unconditional standard deviation (1 mark):
$$\bar{\sigma} = \sqrt{2.5\times 10^{-4}} = 0.01581 \approx 1.58\%\text{ per day}$$

Annualized: $0.01581 \times \sqrt{252} \approx 25.1\%$ per year — a typical equity index level.

**(ii) Half-life (1.5 marks):**
$$\tau_{1/2} = 1 + \frac{\ln(0.5)}{\ln(0.98)} = 1 + \frac{-0.6931}{-0.02020} \approx 1 + 34.31 = 35.31 \text{ days}$$

Interpretation: it takes roughly **5–6 weeks** for half of a volatility shock to dissipate.

**(iii) One-step forecast (1 mark):**
$$\sigma_{t+1}^{2} = \omega + \alpha \sigma_t^{2} + \beta \sigma_t^{2} = \omega + (\alpha+\beta)\sigma_t^{2}$$
$$= 5\times 10^{-6} + 0.98 \times 0.0004 = 5\times 10^{-6} + 0.000392 = 0.000397$$

Five-step forecast (1.5 marks): Using $\sigma_{t+h}^{2} = \bar{\sigma}^{2} + (\alpha+\beta)^{h-1}(\sigma_{t+1}^{2} - \bar{\sigma}^{2})$:
$$\sigma_{t+5}^{2} = 0.00025 + (0.98)^{4}(0.000397 - 0.00025)$$
$$= 0.00025 + (0.9224)(0.000147)$$
$$= 0.00025 + 0.0001356 = 0.0003856$$

So $\sigma_{t+5} \approx 1.96\%$ per day.

**(iv) Practical implication (1 mark):** With persistence $0.98$, volatility is *highly persistent* — volatility shocks from a 2008-type event would take many months to fully decay. Risk managers must use long-horizon VaR and stress-testing, not just 1-day VaR. Also, near-unit-root persistence suggests that EGARCH or FIGARCH may fit even better (fractional integration).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Loss (A(b)(iii)):**
> Students frequently confuse the formula — using $\sigma_{t+1}^{2} = (\alpha+\beta)^h \sigma_t^{2}$ which is **wrong**. The correct expression involves $\sigma_{t+1}^{2}$ on the right side, not $\sigma_t^{2}$. Always re-derive: $\sigma_{t+h}^{2} = \bar{\sigma}^2 + (\alpha+\beta)^{h-1}(\sigma_{t+1}^2 - \bar{\sigma}^2)$. **Lose 1 mark** if you write the wrong formula.

---

### 🔹 QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper]`

**(a)** **(7 marks)** Derive the conditional covariance matrix under a **one-factor GARCH model** with the decomposition $\mathbf{H}_t = \mathbf{b}\,h_{f,t}\,\mathbf{b}^{\top} + \mathbf{D}_t$. Show how the Woodbury matrix identity enables efficient inversion.

**(b)** **(7 marks)** Compare three multivariate volatility models: **Diagonal VECH**, **CCC (Constant Conditional Correlation)**, and **Factor-GARCH**. Tabulate their parameter counts, pros, and cons. For a portfolio of 30 stocks, recommend the most suitable model with justification.

#### Model Solution to B(a) — 7 marks

**Setup (2 marks):** Write the return vector with one common factor $f_t$:

$$\mathbf{r}_t = \boldsymbol{\mu} + \mathbf{b}\,f_t + \boldsymbol{\epsilon}_t$$

- $\mathbf{b} \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times 1}$ — loadings (e.g., CAPM betas).
- $f_t \sim (0, h_{f,t})$ — latent factor with its own GARCH(1,1): $h_{f,t} = \omega_f + \alpha_f \varepsilon_{f,t-1}^{2} + \beta_f h_{f,t-1}$.
- $\boldsymbol{\epsilon}_t \sim (\mathbf{0}, \mathbf{D}_t)$ — idiosyncratic vector, $\mathbf{D}_t = \text{diag}(\sigma_{1,t}^{2}, \ldots, \sigma_{N,t}^{2})$, each $\sigma_{i,t}^{2}$ univariate GARCH.

**Derivation (3 marks):** Conditioning on $\mathcal{F}_{t-1}$ and using independence of $f_t$ and $\boldsymbol{\epsilon}_t$:

$$\mathbf{H}_t = \text{Var}(\mathbf{r}_t \mid \mathcal{F}_{t-1}) = \mathbf{b}\,\text{Var}(f_t\mid \mathcal{F}_{t-1})\,\mathbf{b}^{\top} + \text{Var}(\boldsymbol{\epsilon}_t\mid \mathcal{F}_{t-1})$$

$$= \mathbf{b}\,h_{f,t}\,\mathbf{b}^{\top} + \mathbf{D}_t \qquad\blacksquare$$

The $(i,j)$-th element is:
$$h_{ij,t} = b_i b_j h_{f,t} + \delta_{ij} \sigma_{i,t}^{2}$$

So **all off-diagonal covariances** are perfectly correlated through the single factor, scaled by the factor's variance.

**Woodbury identity (2 marks):** For $\mathbf{H}_t = \mathbf{D}_t + \mathbf{b}\,h_{f,t}\,\mathbf{b}^{\top} = \mathbf{D}_t + \mathbf{U}\mathbf{C}\mathbf{V}^{\top}$ with $\mathbf{U} = \mathbf{b}$, $\mathbf{C} = h_{f,t}$, $\mathbf{V} = \mathbf{b}^{\top}$:

$$\mathbf{H}_t^{-1} = \mathbf{D}_t^{-1} - \mathbf{D}_t^{-1}\mathbf{b}\left(h_{f,t}^{-1} + \mathbf{b}^{\top}\mathbf{D}_t^{-1}\mathbf{b}\right)^{-1}\mathbf{b}^{\top}\mathbf{D}_t^{-1}$$

Since $\mathbf{D}_t^{-1}$ is diagonal (cheap, $\mathcal{O}(N)$) and the inner bracket is a scalar ($K=1$), the inverse costs $\mathcal{O}(N)$ — vs $\mathcal{O}(N^3)$ for a general matrix.

**Engineering utility:** This is the workhorse identity behind RiskMetrics-style systems used in real-time bank risk engines.

#### Model Solution to B(b) — 7 marks

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Feature | Diagonal VECH | CCC (Bollerslev 1990) | Factor-GARCH |
|---------|---------------|----------------------|--------------|
| Parameter count | $\mathcal{O}(N)$ | $N(N-1)/2$ + $3N$ | $NK + 3K + 3N$ |
| Correlation structure | Diagonal $\mathbf{H}_t$ (independence) | Constant $\mathbf{R}$ | Time-varying through factor $h_{f,t}$ |
| Captures dynamics? | No | Only variances vary; correlations static | Both variances and (implicit) correlations vary |
| Estimation complexity | Trivial | 2-step feasible | MLE / EM |
| Captures cross-asset shocks | No | Yes (constant) | Yes (dynamic) |
| Pros | Simple, fast | Parsimonious, robust | Realistic, interpretable |
| Cons | Ignores correlation | Unrealistic constant $\rho_{ij}$ | More parameters, harder estimation |

**Recommendation for 30-stock portfolio (3 marks):**

The **CCC model** is **recommended** as a baseline because:
- $N=30$ implies $435$ correlations — CCC estimates these robustly from standardized residuals.
- Constant correlations, while stylized, are a reasonable first-order approximation; for many equity portfolios, conditional correlations actually are *slowly* varying.
- Two-step estimation is feasible and stable.

However, if the application involves **crisis periods** (where correlations spike — a well-known empirical fact), then upgrade to **Factor-GARCH** with $K = 2$ or $3$ factors. The diagonal VECH is **not recommended** because it ignores cross-sectional risk, which defeats the purpose of portfolio diversification analysis.

**Conclusion:** Start with CCC; if residual diagnostics show remaining dynamics or the goal is VaR in stressed markets, switch to Factor-GARCH.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Loss (B(b)):**
> Students often confuse the parameter counts. Diagonal VECH has $\mathcal{O}(N)$ parameters (diagonal $\mathbf{H}_t$); CCC has $\mathcal{O}(N^2)$ correlations **plus** $\mathcal{O}(N)$ variances (so $N(N-1)/2 + 3N$ for a Gaussian quasi-likelihood). **Lose 1 mark** if you state CCC has only $\mathcal{O}(N)$ parameters. Always distinguish *correlation parameters* from *volatility parameters*.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Factor-Volatility Models (Module 4)**

- [ ] **ARCH(q):** Conditional variance = constant + linear combination of past $q$ squared residuals.
- [ ] **GARCH(p,q):** Adds $p$ lagged conditional variances — parsimonious ARMA-like representation of volatility.
- [ ] **Stationarity condition for GARCH(1,1):** $\alpha + \beta < 1$ (strict inequality).
- [ ] **Unconditional (long-run) variance:** $\bar{\sigma}^{2} = \dfrac{\omega}{1 - \alpha - \beta}$.
- [ ] **Persistence parameter:** $\alpha + \beta$ — high values (close to 1) imply very persistent volatility.
- [ ] **Half-life of volatility shock:** $\tau_{1/2} = 1 + \dfrac{\ln(0.5)}{\ln(\alpha + \beta)}$ — measured in days for daily data.
- [ ] **h-step variance forecast:** $\sigma_{t+h}^{2} = \bar{\sigma}^{2} + (\alpha+\beta)^{h-1}(\sigma_{t+1}^{2} - \bar{\sigma}^{2})$.
- [ ] **GJR-GARCH / EGARCH:** Capture **asymmetric leverage effect** — negative shocks raise volatility more than positive shocks.
- [ ] **Factor-volatility model:** $\mathbf{H}_t = \mathbf{B}\mathbf{F}_t\mathbf{B}^{\top} + \mathbf{D}_t$ — factor loading × factor variance + diagonal idiosyncratic variances.
- [ ] **Parsimony gain:** Parameters grow **linearly** in $N$ instead of quadratically.
- [ ] **Woodbury identity:** Enables $\mathcal{O}(N)$ inversion of $\mathbf{H}_t$ — the computational backbone of real-time risk systems.
- [ ] **Estimation:** Gaussian Quasi-MLE by maximizing $\ell(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = -\frac{1}{2}\sum [\ln \vert \mathbf{H}_t\vert + \boldsymbol{\epsilon}_t^{\top}\mathbf{H}_t^{-1}\boldsymbol{\epsilon}_t]$.
- [ ] **Diagnostics:** Standardized residuals should be free of ARCH effects; apply Ljung-Box test on $\tilde{\boldsymbol{\epsilon}}_t^{2}$.
- [ ] **CCC vs DCC vs Factor-GARCH:** CCC has constant correlations; DCC has dynamic correlations; Factor-GARCH induces dynamic correlations through a latent factor.
- [ ] **Industry relevance:** Banks use factor-volatility models for VaR, expected shortfall, capital adequacy (Basel III), and stress testing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-line board-exam mnemonic:** *Factor-volatility = latent factors (GARCH) + loadings + idiosyncratic (GARCH) — parsimonious, interpretable, real-time friendly.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Multivariate t Distribution
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET742 - FINANCIAL TIME SERIES ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Multivariate t Distribution

# Multivariate t Distribution: A Heavy-Tailed Generalization of the Multivariate Normal

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)**
> A random vector $\mathbf{X} = (X_1, X_2, \dots, X_p)^T \in \mathbb{R}^p$ is said to follow a **Multivariate Student's t-distribution** with location parameter $\boldsymbol{\mu} \in \mathbb{R}^p$, positive-definite scale matrix $\boldsymbol{\Sigma} \in \mathbb{R}^{p \times p}$, and degrees of freedom $\nu > 0$, written as $\mathbf{X} \sim t_p(\boldsymbol{\mu}, \boldsymbol{\Sigma}, \nu)$, if its joint probability density function is given by:
> $$f(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{\Gamma\left(\frac{\nu + p}{2}\right)}{\Gamma\left(\frac{\nu}{2}\right) \, \nu^{p/2} \pi^{p/2} \, \vert \boldsymbol{\Sigma} \vert^{1/2}} \left[ 1 + \frac{1}{\nu} (\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu})^T \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1} (\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}) \right]^{-\frac{\nu + p}{2}}$$
> for $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^p$. Here $\Gamma(\cdot)$ is the gamma function and $\vert \boldsymbol{\Sigma} \vert$ denotes the determinant of the scale matrix.

## 1.1 Intuitive Overview & Real-World Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Fatter Tail" Story — Why We Need It in Finance**
> The Multivariate Normal distribution is the workhorse of classical statistics, but it underestimates the probability of **extreme events**. In financial markets, crashes and rallies (e.g., the 2008 financial crisis, the 2020 COVID crash, the 2022 bond rout) occur far more often than a Gaussian model predicts. The **Multivariate t-distribution** was designed precisely to fix this flaw.

**Analogy — The Drunk Student and the Exam Grader:**
Imagine two students, **A** and **B**, taking the same exam.
- **Student A (Multivariate Normal):** Always scores close to the class average. A 5-sigma deviation is essentially impossible.
- **Student B (Multivariate t, low $\nu$):** Usually scores near average, but occasionally has a "wild day" and either aces the paper spectacularly or fails miserably. The probability of extreme scores is **non-negligible**.

In financial returns, **Student B represents reality.** Asset returns are "well-behaved" most of the time but exhibit **heavy tails** during crises. The Multivariate t-distribution captures this joint heavy-tailed behavior across multiple assets simultaneously.

## 1.2 Why Multivariate t and Not Just Univariate t?

In a portfolio of $p$ assets, returns are **correlated** — a crash in oil prices drags down energy stocks, airlines, and emerging-market currencies together. The **Multivariate t-distribution** models these **joint** heavy tails and **co-movements** in a single unified framework, making it the natural choice for Value-at-Risk (VaR), portfolio optimization under fat tails, and GARCH-type residual modeling.

> [!TIP]
> **Three Key Parameters to Remember**
> 1. **$\boldsymbol{\mu}$ (Location vector):** The center of mass (analogous to the mean, but the mean is *only equal* to $\boldsymbol{\mu}$ when $\nu > 1$).
> 2. **$\boldsymbol{\Sigma}$ (Scale matrix):** Governs the spread and the correlation structure between components.
> 3. **$\nu$ (Degrees of freedom):** Controls tail-heaviness. **Smaller $\nu$** $\Rightarrow$ **heavier tails**. As $\nu \to \infty$, the distribution collapses to $N_p(\boldsymbol{\mu}, \boldsymbol{\Sigma})$.

## 1.3 The Standard Multivariate t-Distribution

A **standard** multivariate t-vector $\mathbf{T} \sim t_p(\mathbf{0}, \mathbf{I}_p, \nu)$ has the simple density:

$$f(\mathbf{t}) = \frac{\Gamma\left(\frac{\nu + p}{2}\right)}{\Gamma\left(\frac{\nu}{2}\right) \, (\nu \pi)^{p/2}} \left( 1 + \frac{\mathbf{t}^T \mathbf{t}}{\nu} \right)^{-\frac{\nu + p}{2}}$$

Any $t_p(\boldsymbol{\mu}, \boldsymbol{\Sigma}, \nu)$ random vector can be generated as the **affine transformation**:

$$\mathbf{X} = \boldsymbol{\mu} + \mathbf{L} \mathbf{T}, \quad \text{where} \quad \boldsymbol{\Sigma} = \mathbf{L} \mathbf{L}^T \text{ (Cholesky factorization)}$$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis: Properties, Hierarchy & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical (Mixture) Representation — The "Birth Certificate" of the t-Distribution

The single most important structural fact about the Multivariate t-distribution is that it is a **continuous mixture of multivariate normals**. This representation is foundational for both theoretical proofs and computational simulation (Gibbs sampling in Bayesian finance).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theorem (Normal–Inverse-Gamma Mixture Representation)**
> Let $S \sim \text{Inverse-Gamma}(\nu/2, \nu/2)$, i.e., its density is $f(s) \propto s^{-\nu/2 - 1} \exp(-\nu / (2s))$ for $s > 0$. Conditional on $S = s$, suppose
> $$\mathbf{X} \mid S = s \;\sim\; N_p\!\left( \boldsymbol{\mu},\, \frac{1}{s} \boldsymbol{\Sigma} \right).$$
> Then marginally,
> $$\mathbf{X} \sim t_p(\boldsymbol{\mu}, \boldsymbol{\Sigma}, \nu).$$

**Interpretation in finance:** The latent variable $S$ acts as a **stochastic volatility scaler**. When $S$ is small, the conditional variance balloons, generating the heavy tails. When $S$ is large, the distribution tightens around $\boldsymbol{\mu}$. This is the **mathematical engine** behind GARCH-with-t-innovations models and multivariate stochastic volatility models used in industry.

## 2.2 Construction via Ratio of Independent Variates

A constructive definition (often used in textbooks and KTU derivations):

$$\mathbf{X} = \boldsymbol{\mu} + \frac{\mathbf{Z}}{\sqrt{W / \nu}}$$

where $\mathbf{Z} \sim N_p(\mathbf{0}, \boldsymbol{\Sigma})$ and $W \sim \chi^2_\nu$ are **independent**. Then $\mathbf{X} \sim t_p(\boldsymbol{\mu}, \boldsymbol{\Sigma}, \nu)$. This is the multivariate extension of the classical Student's t-construction.

## 2.3 Moments and Existence Conditions

| Moment | Formula | Existence Condition |
|---|---|---|
| **Mean** $E[\mathbf{X}]$ | $\boldsymbol{\mu}$ | $\nu > 1$ |
| **Covariance** $\text{Cov}(\mathbf{X})$ | $\dfrac{\nu}{\nu - 2} \boldsymbol{\Sigma}$ | $\nu > 2$ |
| **Skewness (standardized)** | $\mathbf{0}$ (symmetric) | All $\nu$ |
| **Kurtosis (excess, marginal)** | $\dfrac{6}{\nu - 4}$ | $\nu > 4$ |
| **Mode** | $\boldsymbol{\mu}$ | All $\nu$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **The Covariance Trap (Common KTU Mistake)**
> The matrix $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}$ in the density is **NOT** the covariance matrix of $\mathbf{X}$. It is the **scale matrix**. The true covariance is $\frac{\nu}{\nu - 2}\boldsymbol{\Sigma}$. When $\nu \le 2$, the variance is **infinite** — a hallmark of financial crises and a key reason the t-distribution captures them.

## 2.4 Marginal and Conditional Distributions

A beautiful **closure property** holds for the Multivariate t:

- **Marginal:** If $\mathbf{X} \sim t_p(\boldsymbol{\mu}, \boldsymbol{\Sigma}, \nu)$, then partitioning $\mathbf{X} = (\mathbf{X}_1^T, \mathbf{X}_2^T)^T$ with corresponding partition of $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}$, the marginal of $\mathbf{X}_1$ is $t_{p_1}(\boldsymbol{\mu}_1, \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{11}, \nu)$ — **same degrees of freedom**.
- **Conditional:** The conditional distribution of $\mathbf{X}_1 \mid \mathbf{X}_2 = \mathbf{x}_2$ is again multivariate t:
$$\mathbf{X}_1 \mid \mathbf{X}_2 = \mathbf{x}_2 \;\sim\; t_{p_1}\!\left( \boldsymbol{\mu}_1 + \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{12}\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{22}^{-1}(\mathbf{x}_2 - \boldsymbol{\mu}_2),\, \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{11\cdot 2},\, \nu + p_2 \right)$$
where $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{11\cdot 2} = \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{11} - \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{12}\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{22}^{-1}\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{21}$.

> [!NOTE]
> Notice the **degrees of freedom increases by $p_2$** under conditioning — observing more variables tightens the conditional distribution, exactly as intuition demands.

## 2.5 Distributional Properties (Probability-Theoretic)

1. **Elliptical symmetry:** The density depends on $\mathbf{x}$ only through the quadratic form $(\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu})^T \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1} (\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu})$. Hence level sets are **ellipsoids** centered at $\boldsymbol{\mu}$, just like the multivariate normal.
2. **Linear invariance:** If $\mathbf{X} \sim t_p(\boldsymbol{\mu}, \boldsymbol{\Sigma}, \nu)$ and $\mathbf{A} \in \mathbb{R}^{q \times p}$ is constant, then
$$\mathbf{A}\mathbf{X} + \mathbf{b} \sim t_q(\mathbf{A}\boldsymbol{\mu} + \mathbf{b}, \mathbf{A}\boldsymbol{\Sigma}\mathbf{A}^T, \nu).$$
3. **Asymptotic collapse:** $t_p(\boldsymbol{\mu}, \boldsymbol{\Sigma}, \nu) \xrightarrow{d} N_p(\boldsymbol{\mu}, \boldsymbol{\Sigma})$ as $\nu \to \infty$.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **Concept** | **Formula / Property** | **Condition** |
|---|---|---|
| Density of $t_p(\boldsymbol{\mu}, \boldsymbol{\Sigma}, \nu)$ | $f(\mathbf{x}) = \dfrac{\Gamma\!\left(\frac{\nu+p}{2}\right)}{\Gamma\!\left(\frac{\nu}{2}\right) (\nu\pi)^{p/2} \vert\boldsymbol{\Sigma}\vert^{1/2}} \left[ 1 + \frac{1}{\nu}(\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu})^T \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1}(\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu}) \right]^{-\frac{\nu+p}{2}}$ | $\nu > 0$, $\boldsymbol{\Sigma} \succ 0$ |
| Stochastic representation | $\mathbf{X} = \boldsymbol{\mu} + \mathbf{Z}/\sqrt{W/\nu}$, $\mathbf{Z} \sim N_p(\mathbf{0},\boldsymbol{\Sigma})$, $W \sim \chi^2_\nu$ | — |
| Mixture representation | $\mathbf{X} \mid S=s \sim N_p(\boldsymbol{\mu}, \boldsymbol{\Sigma}/s)$, $S \sim \text{Inv-Gamma}(\nu/2, \nu/2)$ | — |
| Mean | $E[\mathbf{X}] = \boldsymbol{\mu}$ | $\nu > 1$ |
| Covariance | $\text{Cov}(\mathbf{X}) = \dfrac{\nu}{\nu-2}\boldsymbol{\Sigma}$ | $\nu > 2$ |
| Marginal | $X_1 \sim t_{p_1}(\boldsymbol{\mu}_1, \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{11}, \nu)$ | Same $\nu$ |
| Conditional | $\mathbf{X}_1 \mid \mathbf{X}_2 = \mathbf{x}_2 \sim t_{p_1}(\boldsymbol{\mu}_{1\cdot 2}, \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{11\cdot 2}, \nu + p_2)$ | $\nu + p_2 > 1$ for mean |
| Linear transformation | $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{X}+\mathbf{b} \sim t_q(\mathbf{A}\boldsymbol{\mu}+\mathbf{b}, \mathbf{A}\boldsymbol{\Sigma}\mathbf{A}^T, \nu)$ | Rank($\mathbf{A}$) = $q$ |
| Mahalanobis distance | $D^2 = (\mathbf{X}-\boldsymbol{\mu})^T \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1}(\mathbf{X}-\boldsymbol{\mu}) \sim \dfrac{p\nu}{\chi^2_p}\cdot F$ | — |
| Asymptotic | $t_p(\boldsymbol{\mu}, \boldsymbol{\Sigma}, \nu) \to N_p(\boldsymbol{\mu}, \boldsymbol{\Sigma})$ | $\nu \to \infty$ |
| Excess kurtosis (marginal) | $6/(\nu-4)$ | $\nu > 4$ |
| MGF / CGF | **Does not exist** for any $\nu$ | — |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering & Finance Utility

- **Risk Management:** Computing portfolio VaR and Expected Shortfall (ES) under fat-tailed joint distributions.
- **Multivariate GARCH (DCC, BEKK, CCC-GARCH):** Residual innovations modeled as $t_p(\mathbf{0}, \mathbf{H}_t, \nu)$.
- **Bayesian VAR / VECM:** Fat-tailed likelihoods robust to outliers.
- **Copula Models:** The t-copula is the most popular elliptical copula for capturing **tail dependence** in financial markets.
- **High-Dimensional Statistics:** Robust estimation of covariance matrices, Hotelling's $T^2$ test, and discriminant analysis when normality is violated.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Multivariate t-Density via the Ratio Construction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Goal:** Starting from the stochastic representation $\mathbf{X} = \boldsymbol{\mu} + \mathbf{Z}/\sqrt{W/\nu}$ with $\mathbf{Z} \sim N_p(\mathbf{0}, \boldsymbol{\Sigma})$ and $W \sim \chi^2_\nu$ independent, derive the joint density of $\mathbf{X}$.

### Step 1: Set up the joint density of $(\mathbf{Z}, W)$

The joint density of the independent pair $(\mathbf{Z}, W)$ is:

$$f_{\mathbf{Z}, W}(\mathbf{z}, w) = f_{\mathbf{Z}}(\mathbf{z}) \cdot f_W(w) = \frac{1}{(2\pi)^{p/2} \vert\boldsymbol{\Sigma}\vert^{1/2}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{1}{2}\mathbf{z}^T \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1}\mathbf{z}\right) \cdot \frac{1}{2^{\nu/2} \Gamma(\nu/2)} w^{\nu/2 - 1} e^{-w/2}$$

### Step 2: Define the transformation $(\mathbf{z}, w) \mapsto (\mathbf{x}, w)$

We have $\mathbf{x} = \boldsymbol{\mu} + \mathbf{z} / \sqrt{w/\nu}$, so solving for $\mathbf{z}$:

$$\mathbf{z} = \sqrt{\frac{w}{\nu}}\, (\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}), \qquad w = w.$$

The Jacobian matrix of the map $(\mathbf{z}, w) \mapsto (\mathbf{x}, w)$ is block triangular:

$$\mathbf{J} = \begin{pmatrix} \dfrac{\partial \mathbf{x}}{\partial \mathbf{z}^T} & \dfrac{\partial \mathbf{x}}{\partial w} \\ \mathbf{0}^T & 1 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} \sqrt{w/\nu}\, \mathbf{I}_p & \dfrac{1}{2}\sqrt{\nu/w^3}\,(\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu}) \\ \mathbf{0}^T & 1 \end{pmatrix}$$

The absolute value of the determinant is:

$$\vert \det \mathbf{J} \vert = \left(\frac{w}{\nu}\right)^{p/2} \cdot 1 = \left(\frac{w}{\nu}\right)^{p/2}$$

### Step 3: Substitute into the joint density

$$f_{\mathbf{X}, W}(\mathbf{x}, w) = f_{\mathbf{Z}, W}\!\left(\sqrt{w/\nu}\,(\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu}), w\right) \cdot \left(\frac{w}{\nu}\right)^{p/2}$$

The exponential term simplifies as:

$$-\frac{1}{2}\mathbf{z}^T \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1} \mathbf{z} = -\frac{w}{2\nu} (\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu})^T \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1} (\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu})$$

The $w$-dependent prefactors from $f_{\mathbf{Z}}$ and the Jacobian combine as:

$$\frac{1}{(2\pi)^{p/2} \vert\boldsymbol{\Sigma}\vert^{1/2}} \cdot \left(\frac{w}{\nu}\right)^{p/2}$$

So:

$$f_{\mathbf{X}, W}(\mathbf{x}, w) = \frac{(w/\nu)^{p/2}}{(2\pi)^{p/2} \vert\boldsymbol{\Sigma}\vert^{1/2}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{w}{2\nu} D^2\right) \cdot \frac{w^{\nu/2-1} e^{-w/2}}{2^{\nu/2}\Gamma(\nu/2)}$$

where $D^2 = (\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu})^T \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1} (\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu})$ is the **Mahalanobis distance squared**.

### Step 4: Marginalize out $w$ to get the marginal density of $\mathbf{X}$

$$f_{\mathbf{X}}(\mathbf{x}) = \int_0^\infty f_{\mathbf{X}, W}(\mathbf{x}, w)\, dw$$

$$= \frac{1}{(2\pi\nu)^{p/2} \vert\boldsymbol{\Sigma}\vert^{1/2} \, 2^{\nu/2}\Gamma(\nu/2)} \int_0^\infty w^{(\nu+p)/2 - 1} \exp\!\left[-\frac{w}{2}\left(1 + \frac{D^2}{\nu}\right)\right] dw$$

### Step 5: Recognize the integral as a Gamma integral

The integrand is the kernel of a Gamma distribution. Using the identity:

$$\int_0^\infty w^{a-1} e^{-b w}\, dw = \frac{\Gamma(a)}{b^a}, \quad a, b > 0$$

we identify $a = \frac{\nu+p}{2}$ and $b = \frac{1}{2}\left(1 + \frac{D^2}{\nu}\right)$. Hence:

$$\int_0^\infty w^{(\nu+p)/2 - 1} \exp\!\left[-\frac{w}{2}\left(1 + \frac{D^2}{\nu}\right)\right] dw = \frac{\Gamma\!\left(\frac{\nu+p}{2}\right)}{\left[\frac{1}{2}\left(1 + \frac{D^2}{\nu}\right)\right]^{(\nu+p)/2}}$$

### Step 6: Assemble the final density

$$f_{\mathbf{X}}(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{1}{(2\pi\nu)^{p/2} \vert\boldsymbol{\Sigma}\vert^{1/2} \, 2^{\nu/2}\Gamma(\nu/2)} \cdot \frac{\Gamma\!\left(\frac{\nu+p}{2}\right) \, 2^{(\nu+p)/2}}{\left(1 + \frac{D^2}{\nu}\right)^{(\nu+p)/2}}$$

Simplifying the powers of 2: $(2\pi\nu)^{p/2} \cdot 2^{\nu/2} / 2^{(\nu+p)/2} = (2\pi)^{p/2} \nu^{p/2}$. Therefore:

$$\boxed{\,f_{\mathbf{X}}(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{\Gamma\!\left(\frac{\nu+p}{2}\right)}{\Gamma\!\left(\frac{\nu}{2}\right) \, (\nu\pi)^{p/2} \, \vert\boldsymbol{\Sigma}\vert^{1/2}} \left(1 + \frac{(\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu})^T \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1} (\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu})}{\nu}\right)^{-\frac{\nu+p}{2}}\,}$$

$\blacksquare$

## 3.2 Derivation of the Mean (for $\nu > 1$)

Using the mixture representation $\mathbf{X} \mid S = s \sim N_p(\boldsymbol{\mu}, \boldsymbol{\Sigma}/s)$ and $S \sim \text{Inv-Gamma}(\nu/2, \nu/2)$:

$$E[\mathbf{X}] = E\!\left[E[\mathbf{X} \mid S]\right] = E[\boldsymbol{\mu}] = \boldsymbol{\mu}$$

This works because $E[\mathbf{X} \mid S = s] = \boldsymbol{\mu}$ for all $s$. The condition $\nu > 1$ is required because for $0 < \nu \le 1$, the integral defining $E[\mathbf{X}]$ diverges (the density decays as $r^{-(p+\nu)}$ in the tails, which is non-integrable for $p + \nu \le p$ — i.e., always — relative to the first moment).

## 3.3 Derivation of the Covariance (for $\nu > 2$)

By the **law of total covariance**:

$$\text{Cov}(\mathbf{X}) = E\!\left[\text{Cov}(\mathbf{X} \mid S)\right] + \text{Cov}\!\left(E[\mathbf{X} \mid S]\right) = E\!\left[\frac{\boldsymbol{\Sigma}}{S}\right] + \text{Cov}(\boldsymbol{\mu}) = \boldsymbol{\Sigma}\, E\!\left[\frac{1}{S}\right]$$

For $S \sim \text{Inv-Gamma}(\nu/2, \nu/2)$, the moment $E[1/S] = \dfrac{\nu/2}{\nu/2 - 1} = \dfrac{\nu}{\nu - 2}$ (valid for $\nu > 2$).

Hence:

$$\boxed{\,\text{Cov}(\mathbf{X}) = \frac{\nu}{\nu - 2}\, \boldsymbol{\Sigma}\,}$$

## 3.4 Derivation of the Conditional Distribution

Let $\mathbf{X} \sim t_p(\boldsymbol{\mu}, \boldsymbol{\Sigma}, \nu)$ be partitioned. We use the **linear transformation** property. Define:

$$\mathbf{Y} = \begin{pmatrix} \mathbf{X}_1 \\ \mathbf{X}_2 \end{pmatrix} \quad \xrightarrow{\;\mathbf{A}\;} \quad \begin{pmatrix} \mathbf{X}_{1\cdot 2} \\ \mathbf{X}_2 \end{pmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{A} = \begin{pmatrix} \mathbf{I}_{p_1} & -\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{12}\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{22}^{-1} \\ \mathbf{0} & \mathbf{I}_{p_2} \end{pmatrix}$$

Then $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{Y} = (\mathbf{X}_1 - \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{12}\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{22}^{-1}\mathbf{X}_2, \mathbf{X}_2)^T$ and by the linear invariance property:

$$\mathbf{X}_1 - \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{12}\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{22}^{-1}\mathbf{X}_2 \;\sim\; t_{p_1}(\boldsymbol{\mu}_{1\cdot 2}, \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{11\cdot 2}, \nu) \quad \text{independently of } \mathbf{X}_2$$

Therefore, conditional on $\mathbf{X}_2 = \mathbf{x}_2$:

$$\mathbf{X}_1 \mid \mathbf{X}_2 = \mathbf{x}_2 \;\sim\; t_{p_1}\!\left( \boldsymbol{\mu}_1 + \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{12}\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{22}^{-1}(\mathbf{x}_2 - \boldsymbol{\mu}_2),\, \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{11\cdot 2},\, \nu \right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Refinement (Doornik's result):** A more careful treatment using the joint ratio representation shows the **conditional** degrees of freedom is $\nu + p_2$, not $\nu$. This is the version quoted in the formula sheet above and is the form appearing in modern KTU module content.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style 3-mark problem)

> **Problem:** Suppose $\mathbf{X} = (X_1, X_2)^T \sim t_2(\boldsymbol{\mu}, \boldsymbol{\Sigma}, 5)$ with
> $$\boldsymbol{\mu} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.02 \\ 0.01 \end{pmatrix}, \quad \boldsymbol{\Sigma} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.04 & 0.01 \\ 0.01 & 0.09 \end{pmatrix}.$$
> Find the mean vector and covariance matrix of $\mathbf{X}$.

**Solution:**

Since $\nu = 5 > 2$, the mean exists and equals $\boldsymbol{\mu}$:
$$E[\mathbf{X}] = \begin{pmatrix} 0.02 \\ 0.01 \end{pmatrix}.$$

The covariance is $\dfrac{\nu}{\nu - 2}\boldsymbol{\Sigma} = \dfrac{5}{3}\boldsymbol{\Sigma}$:

$$\text{Cov}(\mathbf{X}) = \frac{5}{3}\begin{pmatrix} 0.04 & 0.01 \\ 0.01 & 0.09 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.0\overline{6} & 0.01\overline{6} \\ 0.01\overline{6} & 0.15 \end{pmatrix}.$$

**Excess kurtosis** (each marginal): $\dfrac{6}{\nu - 4} = \dfrac{6}{1} = 6$ — i.e., the marginals have kurtosis $9$ (a Gaussian has kurtosis $3$). This quantifies the **fat tails** in a way any risk manager can appreciate.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Hierarchical Generation of a Multivariate t-Vector

This mermaid diagram shows the **operational architecture** of how a $t_p(\boldsymbol{\mu}, \boldsymbol{\Sigma}, \nu)$ random vector is generated in practice (used in Monte Carlo simulation, MCMC, and stress-testing engines).

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Chi-Squared Generator: W ~ chi-squared with nu degrees of freedom"] --> B["Latent Scale Variable: S = nu / W"]
    B --> C["Conditional Covariance: Sigma divided by S"]
    C --> D["Multivariate Normal Sampler: Z ~ N_p(0, Sigma / S)"]
    D --> E["Affine Shift: X = mu + Z"]
    E --> F["Output: X follows t_p with location mu, scale Sigma, and nu degrees of freedom"]
    G["Random Seed: Uniform(0,1)"] --> A
    G --> D
    H["Parameter Bank: mu, Sigma, nu"] --> E
    H --> C

    style A fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd
    style B fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107
    style C fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107
    style D fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754
    style E fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545
    style F fill:#e2e3e5,stroke:#6c757d
```

**Reading the diagram:** Start at the top. Sample $W$ from a chi-squared distribution with $\nu$ degrees of freedom. Convert to a precision/scale variable $S = \nu/W$. This $S$ scales the covariance matrix: large $S$ tightens the distribution (calm markets), small $S$ inflates it (crash days). Sample a multivariate normal with the **scaled** covariance, then add the mean. The result is a heavy-tailed multivariate t-observation.

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Properties & Closure Map of the Multivariate t-Distribution

This diagram shows the **algebraic closure properties** that make the Multivariate t analytically tractable and computationally useful.

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1["t_p with parameters mu, Sigma, nu"] --> P2["Marginal: t_{p1} with mu_1, Sigma_11, nu"]
    P1 --> P3["Conditional: t_{p1} with mean mu_1.2, scale Sigma_11.2, df nu + p2"]
    P1 --> P4["Linear Transform: A X + b gives t_q with A mu + b, A Sigma A transpose, nu"]
    P1 --> P5["Limit as nu grows large: t_p converges in distribution to N_p with mu, Sigma"]
    P1 --> P6["Mixture: t_p is a continuous mixture of N_p with random covariance Sigma / S"]
    P1 --> P7["Quadratic Form: Mahalanobis distance D squared has F scaled distribution"]

    style P1 fill:#0d6efd,stroke:#08308b,color:#fff
    style P2 fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd
    style P3 fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd
    style P4 fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd
    style P5 fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107
    style P6 fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754
    style P7 fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545
```

## 4.3 Tail-Behavior Comparison (Conceptual Sketch)

The following block diagram contrasts the **tail behavior** of Multivariate Normal and Multivariate t — the visual heart of why finance requires t.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Distance r from location mu"] --> B["Multivariate Normal: density decays as exp(-r squared / 2)"]
    A --> C["Multivariate t with small nu: density decays as r to the power -(nu + p)"]
    B --> D["Very low probability of extreme events"]
    C --> E["Significantly higher probability of extreme events: matches crash frequency"]

    style B fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd
    style C fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545
    style D fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd
    style E fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545
```

**Key takeaway from the diagram:** Gaussian tails decay **exponentially** (super-fast), while t-distribution tails decay as a **power law** (much slower). Power-law tails are the empirical signature of financial market crashes (Mandelbrot's 1963 observation).

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
>
> **Q1. Define the Multivariate Student's t-distribution. State its probability density function and the three parameters involved. When does it reduce to a multivariate normal distribution?**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> A $p$-dimensional random vector $\mathbf{X}$ is said to follow a Multivariate Student's t-distribution with location parameter $\boldsymbol{\mu} \in \mathbb{R}^p$, positive-definite scale matrix $\boldsymbol{\Sigma} \in \mathbb{R}^{p \times p}$, and degrees of freedom $\nu > 0$, written $\mathbf{X} \sim t_p(\boldsymbol{\mu}, \boldsymbol{\Sigma}, \nu)$, if its joint density is:
> $$f(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{\Gamma\!\left(\frac{\nu+p}{2}\right)}{\Gamma\!\left(\frac{\nu}{2}\right)(\nu\pi)^{p/2}\vert\boldsymbol{\Sigma}\vert^{1/2}} \left[1 + \frac{1}{\nu}(\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu})^T \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1}(\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu})\right]^{-(\nu+p)/2}$$
> The three parameters are:
> - **$\boldsymbol{\mu}$** — location (center)
> - **$\boldsymbol{\Sigma}$** — scale matrix (spread & correlation)
> - **$\nu$** — degrees of freedom (tail-heaviness)
>
> As $\nu \to \infty$, the t-distribution converges in distribution to the Multivariate Normal $N_p(\boldsymbol{\mu}, \boldsymbol{\Sigma})$. **(3 marks)**

---

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
>
> **Q2. State the stochastic representation (ratio construction) of the Multivariate t-distribution. Why is this representation useful in financial modeling?**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> If $\mathbf{Z} \sim N_p(\mathbf{0}, \boldsymbol{\Sigma})$ and $W \sim \chi^2_\nu$ are independent, then
> $$\mathbf{X} = \boldsymbol{\mu} + \frac{\mathbf{Z}}{\sqrt{W/\nu}} \sim t_p(\boldsymbol{\mu}, \boldsymbol{\Sigma}, \nu).$$
> This representation is useful because:
> 1. It provides an **easy simulation method** — sample a Normal and a chi-squared, combine them.
> 2. It shows that the t-distribution is a **scale mixture of normals**, which is the basis of stochastic-volatility models in finance where the latent $W/\nu$ represents the **inverse variance** (precision) of returns. **(3 marks)**

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - Module 4, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

### Question A (14 Marks)

**A(a)** Derive the probability density function of the Multivariate Student's t-distribution $t_p(\boldsymbol{\mu}, \boldsymbol{\Sigma}, \nu)$ using the ratio construction $\mathbf{X} = \boldsymbol{\mu} + \mathbf{Z}/\sqrt{W/\nu}$ with $\mathbf{Z} \sim N_p(\mathbf{0}, \boldsymbol{\Sigma})$ and $W \sim \chi^2_\nu$ independent. Show every step. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (A(a)):**

1. **Joint density of $(\mathbf{Z}, W)$ by independence** (1 mark):
$$f_{\mathbf{Z}, W}(\mathbf{z}, w) = \frac{\exp\!\left(-\tfrac{1}{2}\mathbf{z}^T \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1} \mathbf{z}\right)}{(2\pi)^{p/2}\vert\boldsymbol{\Sigma}\vert^{1/2}} \cdot \frac{w^{\nu/2-1} e^{-w/2}}{2^{\nu/2}\Gamma(\nu/2)}$$

2. **Inverse transformation and Jacobian** (2 marks):
$\mathbf{z} = \sqrt{w/\nu}(\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu})$, Jacobian absolute determinant $\vert\det\mathbf{J}\vert = (w/\nu)^{p/2}$.

3. **Substitute** (1 mark):
$$f_{\mathbf{X},W}(\mathbf{x},w) = \frac{(w/\nu)^{p/2}}{(2\pi)^{p/2}\vert\boldsymbol{\Sigma}\vert^{1/2} \cdot 2^{\nu/2}\Gamma(\nu/2)} \, w^{\nu/2-1} \exp\!\left[-\frac{w}{2}\!\left(1 + \tfrac{D^2}{\nu}\right)\right]$$
where $D^2 = (\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu})^T \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1}(\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu})$.

4. **Marginalize over $w \in (0,\infty)$** (1 mark): integral of $w^{(\nu+p)/2-1}\exp[-w(1+D^2/\nu)/2]$ is a Gamma integral yielding $\Gamma((\nu+p)/2) / [(1+D^2/\nu)/2]^{(\nu+p)/2}$.

5. **Simplify constants to obtain the final density** (2 marks):
$$f_{\mathbf{X}}(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{\Gamma\!\left(\frac{\nu+p}{2}\right)}{\Gamma\!\left(\frac{\nu}{2}\right)(\nu\pi)^{p/2}\vert\boldsymbol{\Sigma}\vert^{1/2}} \left(1 + \frac{D^2}{\nu}\right)^{-(\nu+p)/2}$$

**(Total: 7 marks)**

---

**A(b)** For $\mathbf{X} \sim t_p(\boldsymbol{\mu}, \boldsymbol{\Sigma}, \nu)$:
- (i) State the conditions under which the mean and covariance matrix exist, and write down their values.
- (ii) Show that the covariance matrix is $\dfrac{\nu}{\nu-2}\boldsymbol{\Sigma}$ using the mixture representation.
- (iii) Briefly explain why the t-distribution is preferred over the Normal in financial return modeling. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (A(b)):**

**(i)** The mean exists for $\nu > 1$ and equals $\boldsymbol{\mu}$. The covariance exists for $\nu > 2$ and equals $\dfrac{\nu}{\nu-2}\boldsymbol{\Sigma}$. **(2 marks)**

**(ii)** Mixture representation: $\mathbf{X} \mid S = s \sim N_p(\boldsymbol{\mu}, \boldsymbol{\Sigma}/s)$ with $S \sim \text{Inv-Gamma}(\nu/2, \nu/2)$. By the law of total covariance: $\text{Cov}(\mathbf{X}) = E[\text{Cov}(\mathbf{X}\mid S)] + \text{Cov}(E[\mathbf{X}\mid S]) = \boldsymbol{\Sigma} \cdot E[1/S] + 0$. For $S \sim \text{Inv-Gamma}(\nu/2, \nu/2)$, $E[1/S] = \nu/(\nu-2)$ (valid for $\nu>2$). Hence $\text{Cov}(\mathbf{X}) = \dfrac{\nu}{\nu-2}\boldsymbol{\Sigma}$. **(3 marks)**

**(iii)** Financial return distributions exhibit **excess kurtosis** (fat tails) and **volatility clustering** that the Gaussian cannot capture. The t-distribution accommodates this with its single parameter $\nu$: small $\nu$ produces heavy tails, matching the empirical frequency of market crashes. It also admits a scale-mixture interpretation that aligns naturally with stochastic-volatility models. **(2 marks)**

**(Total A(a) + A(b) = 14 marks)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**B(a)** Define the **Multivariate t-distribution** as a **scale mixture of Multivariate Normals**. State the theorem precisely and use it to derive the mean and variance of a univariate $t_\nu(\mu, \sigma^2)$ random variable. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (B(a)):**

**Theorem:** Let $S \sim \text{Inv-Gamma}(\nu/2, \nu/2)$ with density proportional to $s^{-\nu/2-1}\exp(-\nu/(2s))$. If conditional on $S=s$, $\mathbf{X} \mid S=s \sim N_p(\boldsymbol{\mu}, \boldsymbol{\Sigma}/s)$, then marginally $\mathbf{X} \sim t_p(\boldsymbol{\mu}, \boldsymbol{\Sigma}, \nu)$. **(2 marks)**

**Derivation for univariate case ($p=1$):**
$\mathbf{X} = X \sim t_\nu(\mu, \sigma^2)$ with $X \mid S = s \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2/s)$.

- **Mean:** $E[X] = E[E[X \mid S]] = E[\mu] = \mu$. (Valid since the inner conditional mean is constant; condition $\nu > 1$ for finiteness.) **(1.5 marks)**

- **Variance:** $\text{Var}(X) = E[\text{Var}(X\mid S)] + \text{Var}(E[X\mid S]) = \sigma^2 E[1/S] + 0$. Now $E[1/S]$ for $S \sim \text{Inv-Gamma}(\nu/2, \nu/2)$: if $S$ has density $f(s) \propto s^{-\nu/2-1}\exp(-\nu/(2s))$, then $E[1/S] = \nu/(\nu-2)$ for $\nu>2$. Hence $\text{Var}(X) = \dfrac{\nu}{\nu-2}\sigma^2$. **(3.5 marks)**

**(Total: 7 marks)**

---

**B(b)** Let $\mathbf{X} = (X_1, X_2, X_3)^T \sim t_3(\boldsymbol{\mu}, \boldsymbol{\Sigma}, 6)$ where
$$\boldsymbol{\mu} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 2 \\ 3 \end{pmatrix}, \quad \boldsymbol{\Sigma} = \begin{pmatrix} 4 & 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 9 & 2 \\ 0 & 2 & 16 \end{pmatrix}.$$
- (i) Find the mean vector and covariance matrix of $\mathbf{X}$.
- (ii) Find the marginal distribution of $(X_1, X_2)^T$.
- (iii) Find the conditional distribution of $X_1$ given $X_2 = 4$ and $X_3 = 5$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (B(b)):**

**(i)** Since $\nu = 6 > 2$, mean and covariance exist. **(1 mark)**
$$E[\mathbf{X}] = \boldsymbol{\mu} = (1, 2, 3)^T.$$
$$\text{Cov}(\mathbf{X}) = \frac{6}{6-2}\boldsymbol{\Sigma} = \frac{3}{2}\begin{pmatrix} 4 & 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 9 & 2 \\ 0 & 2 & 16 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 6 & 1.5 & 0 \\ 1.5 & 13.5 & 3 \\ 0 & 3 & 24 \end{pmatrix}.$$

**(ii)** By the marginal closure property, $(X_1, X_2)^T \sim t_2(\boldsymbol{\mu}_{12}, \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{12}, 6)$ where $\boldsymbol{\mu}_{12} = (1, 2)^T$ and $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{12} = \begin{pmatrix} 4 & 1 \\ 1 & 9 \end{pmatrix}$. **(2 marks)**

**(iii)** Using the conditional formula with $p_1 = 1$, $p_2 = 2$, $\mathbf{x}_2 = (4, 5)^T$:

- Conditional scale: $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{11\cdot 2} = \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{11} - \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{12}\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{22}^{-1}\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{21}$. With $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{12} = (1, 0)$ and $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{22} = \begin{pmatrix} 9 & 2 \\ 2 & 16 \end{pmatrix}$:

$$\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{22}^{-1} = \frac{1}{140}\begin{pmatrix} 16 & -2 \\ -2 & 9 \end{pmatrix}, \quad \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{12}\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{22}^{-1} = \frac{1}{140}(16, -2) = \left(\frac{8}{70}, -\frac{1}{70}\right).$$

$$\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{11\cdot 2} = 4 - \frac{16}{140} = 4 - \frac{8}{70} = \frac{280 - 8}{70} = \frac{272}{70} = \frac{136}{35}.$$

- Conditional location: $\mu_{1\cdot 2} = \mu_1 + \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{12}\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{22}^{-1}(\mathbf{x}_2 - \boldsymbol{\mu}_2) = 1 + \left(\frac{8}{70}, -\frac{1}{70}\right)\begin{pmatrix} 4-2 \\ 5-3 \end{pmatrix} = 1 + \frac{16 - 2}{70} = 1 + \frac{14}{70} = 1.2.$

- Conditional df: $\nu + p_2 = 6 + 2 = 8$.

$$X_1 \mid X_2=4, X_3=5 \;\sim\; t_1\!\left(1.2, \tfrac{136}{35}, 8\right). \quad \textbf{(4 marks)}$$

**(Total B(a) + B(b) = 14 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Confusing $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}$ with the covariance matrix.** The scale matrix in the density is **not** the covariance when $\nu \le \infty$. Always state $\text{Cov}(\mathbf{X}) = \frac{\nu}{\nu-2}\boldsymbol{\Sigma}$ explicitly.
> 2. **Forgetting the existence conditions.** Mean requires $\nu > 1$, covariance requires $\nu > 2$, fourth moment requires $\nu > 4$. State these in your answer.
> 3. **Jacobian errors in the PDF derivation.** The factor $(w/\nu)^{p/2}$ from the Jacobian is the most-skipped step. A 1-mark penalty is typical if missing.
> 4. **Mixing up $\nu$ in the conditional distribution.** The conditional df is $\nu + p_2$ (not $\nu$ alone) when derived from the joint ratio representation.
> 5. **Units in covariance:** When $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}$ has units of (return)$^2$, then $\text{Cov}(\mathbf{X})$ has the same units — multipliers like $\nu/(\nu-2)$ are **dimensionless**. Show this awareness.
> 6. **Not verifying positive-definiteness of $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}$.** KTU expects you to note that $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}$ must be positive-definite for the density to be valid.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** $\mathbf{X} \sim t_p(\boldsymbol{\mu}, \boldsymbol{\Sigma}, \nu)$ with three parameters — location $\boldsymbol{\mu}$, scale $\boldsymbol{\Sigma} \succ 0$, and degrees of freedom $\nu > 0$.
- **Two equivalent constructions:** (i) Ratio $\mathbf{X} = \boldsymbol{\mu} + \mathbf{Z}/\sqrt{W/\nu}$, $\mathbf{Z} \sim N_p(\mathbf{0}, \boldsymbol{\Sigma})$, $W \sim \chi^2_\nu$; (ii) Scale mixture of normals with $S \sim \text{Inv-Gamma}(\nu/2, \nu/2)$.
- **Density:** $f(\mathbf{x}) \propto \vert \boldsymbol{\Sigma} \vert^{-1/2} \left(1 + \tfrac{D^2}{\nu}\right)^{-(\nu+p)/2}$ with $D^2 = (\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu})^T \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1}(\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu})$.
- **Mean exists for** $\nu > 1$, **equal to** $\boldsymbol{\mu}$. **Covariance exists for** $\nu > 2$, **equal to** $\dfrac{\nu}{\nu-2}\boldsymbol{\Sigma}$.
- **Excess kurtosis (marginal):** $\dfrac{6}{\nu-4}$ for $\nu > 4$ — the **measure of tail-heaviness**.
- **Elliptical symmetry:** level sets are ellipsoids; only depends on $D^2$.
- **Linear invariance:** $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{X}+\mathbf{b} \sim t_q(\mathbf{A}\boldsymbol{\mu}+\mathbf{b}, \mathbf{A}\boldsymbol{\Sigma}\mathbf{A}^T, \nu)$.
- **Marginal closure:** any sub-vector is $t_{p_1}(\boldsymbol{\mu}_1, \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{11}, \nu)$ — **same** $\nu$.
- **Conditional distribution:** $t_{p_1}\!\left(\boldsymbol{\mu}_{1\cdot 2}, \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{11\cdot 2}, \nu + p_2\right)$ — df **increases** with conditioning.
- **Limiting case:** $t_p(\boldsymbol{\mu}, \boldsymbol{\Sigma}, \nu) \to N_p(\boldsymbol{\mu}, \boldsymbol{\Sigma})$ as $\nu \to \infty$.
- **No MGF/CGF exists** for any $\nu$ (a distinctive property used in proofs).
- **Financial use cases:** Multivariate GARCH residuals, VaR/ES, t-copula for tail dependence, robust Bayesian VAR.
- **Degrees-of-freedom rule of thumb in finance:** empirical estimates for daily returns typically give $\nu \in (3, 8)$ — a strong indictment of the Gaussian assumption.
- **The Cholesky route:** generate $t_p$ via $\mathbf{X} = \boldsymbol{\mu} + \mathbf{L}\mathbf{T}$ with $\mathbf{T} \sim t_p(\mathbf{0}, \mathbf{I}_p, \nu)$ and $\boldsymbol{\Sigma} = \mathbf{L}\mathbf{L}^T$.
